topics.title: Chemistry of Explosives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemistry of Explosives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemistry of Explosives** is the branch of applied physical chemistry that systematically studies the molecular structure, stoichiometry, thermochemistry, kinetics, and reaction pathways by which explosive substances undergo extremely rapid, exothermic self-sustaining decomposition or oxidation-reduction reactions, releasing large volumes of hot gases in a fraction of a millisecond, accompanied by the liberation of substantial chemical energy.

The discipline encompasses the following canonical sub-domains that are examinable under the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Molecular Composition Analysis** – Study of the empirical and structural formulae of explosive compounds (e.g., $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$ for TNT, $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{N}_6\text{O}_6$ for RDX).
2. **Decomposition Chemistry** – Balanced stoichiometric equations for deflagration and detonation pathways.
3. **Oxygen Balance (OB) Stoichiometry** – Quantitative evaluation of intrinsic/extrinsic oxygen surplus or deficit.
4. **Thermochemistry of Explosives** – Calculation of heat of explosion ($Q_e$), heat of detonation ($Q_d$), enthalpy of formation ($\Delta H_f$), and detonation temperature.
5. **Equilibrium Product Chemistry** – Application of the **Kistiakowsky–Wilson (K–W) Rules** to predict stable detonation products.
6. **Explosophore Chemistry** – Identification of reactive chemical groups ($-\text{NO}_2$, $-\text{ONO}_2$, $-\text{N}_3$, $-\text{N}=\text{N}-$, $-\text{ClO}_4$, $-\text{NF}_2$) that confer explosive properties.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine inflating a 1-litre balloon very slowly using a bicycle pump — this requires about 100 joules of work and takes seconds. Now imagine releasing that **same energy in 1 microsecond inside a sealed matchbox** — the gas pressure instantaneously exceeds **300,000 atmospheres**, and the box shatters violently. That is exactly what an explosive does at the chemical level: it packages its own oxidizer and fuel into a single molecule (or a tightly mixed blend), so that a tiny triggering pulse unleashes the entire chemical reaction in microseconds.

> **Standard Reference Metric (KTU Board Examination Constant):**
> The **detonation velocity ($V_D$)** of a typical high explosive lies in the range of **$\mathbf{1,500\; \text{m/s} \;to\; 9,000\; \text{m/s}}$**, while characteristic detonation pressures reach the order of **$\mathbf{10^5\; \text{atm}}$** ($\approx 10^{10}\;\text{Pa}$). The chemical energy released is on the order of **$\mathbf{1{,}000\; \text{kcal/kg} \;to\; 1{,}500\; \text{kcal/kg}}$** for organic nitrated explosives.

### 1.3 The Three Universal Reaction Conditions

Any chemical reaction qualifies as an **explosion** if it satisfies **all three** of the following conditions simultaneously:

1. **Exothermicity:** $\Delta H < 0$ (release of heat, typically $> 500\;\text{cal/g}$).
2. **High Reaction Rate:** Rate constant $k$ yields extremely rapid self-propagation ($\mu\text{s}$ timescale).
3. **Gaseous Product Formation:** Generation of a large molar volume of gases that undergo near-adiabatic expansion.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Explosives (Chemical Basis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From the **chemical composition standpoint**, explosives are classified into two broad families that examiners frequently test:

| Class | Sub-Class | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Single Chemical Compounds (Molecular Explosives)** | (a) Primary / Initiating | Detonate by simple ignition / spark; highly sensitive; used in detonators | Mercury Fulminate $\text{Hg(ONC)}_2$, Lead Azide $\text{Pb(N}_3)_2$, Lead Styphnate |
|  | (b) Secondary / High Explosives | Require a strong shock from a primary explosive; relatively insensitive; used as main charge | TNT, RDX, HMX, PETN, Tetryl |
| **II. Mixtures of Compounds (Composite / Blended Explosives)** | (a) Oxidizer + Fuel mixtures | A solid/liquid oxidizer intimately mixed with a fuel | ANFO (Ammonium Nitrate + Fuel Oil), Gelignite |
|  | (b) Pyrotechnic mixtures | Oxidizer + fuel + colorant (smoke/light effect) | Flash powder, thermite |
|  | (c) Propellants | Combustible solids/gels that burn but do not detonate (controlled deflagration) | Smokeless gunpowder, ballistite |

### 2.2 Detonation vs. Deflagration (The Critical Chemical Distinction)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The single most examined chemistry-of-explosives question is the difference between *deflagration* and *detonation*. The chemical energy release is identical in theory; what differs is the **physical mechanism** by which the reaction front propagates through the explosive mass.

| Property | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction front velocity | **Subsonic** ($< 1{,}000\;\text{m/s}$, typically $1\;\text{m/s}$ to $1{,}000\;\text{m/s}$) | **Supersonic** ($1{,}500\;-\;9{,}000\;\text{m/s}$) |
| Propagation medium | **Conduction / convection** of heat through the unreacted material | **Shock wave** through the explosive body |
| Pressure generation | Moderate ($\le 1{,}000\;\text{atm}$) | Very high ($\approx 300{,}000\;\text{atm}$ in TNT) |
| Driving force | Thermal energy transfer | Strong leading shock front |
| Representative chemicals | Gunpowder, propellants | TNT, RDX, HMX, NG |

### 2.3 Explosophores – The Chemical Roots of Explosivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **explosophore** is a specific functional group in the molecule whose presence intrinsically drives instability and rapid exothermic decomposition. KTU examiners routinely ask students to **identify explosophores** in given molecular formulae.

The five principal explosophores (memorise these):

1. **$-\text{NO}_2$ (Nitro group)** – e.g., in TNT, nitrobenzene, picric acid.
2. **$-\text{ONO}_2$ (Nitrate ester group)** – e.g., in nitroglycerine, nitrocellulose, PETN.
3. **$-\text{N}_3$ (Azide group)** – e.g., in lead azide.
4. **$-\text{N}=\text{N}-$ (Diazo / Azo group)** – e.g., in diazomethane, azo-isobutyronitrile.
5. **$-\text{ClO}_4$ / $-\text{ClO}_3$ / $-\text{NF}_2$ (Perhalate / Difluoroamine)** – e.g., in ammonium perchlorate.

### 2.4 Oxygen Balance (OB) – The Core Stoichiometric Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** The *Oxygen Balance (OB)* of an explosive of empirical formula $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{N}_c\text{O}_d$ is the percentage excess or deficit of oxygen (by mass) relative to the stoichiometric requirement for **complete combustion** to $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$.

**Master Formula (must be memorised verbatim for the exam):**

$$
\text{OB}\,(\%) \;=\; \dfrac{\bigl(d \;-\; 2a \;-\; \tfrac{b}{2}\bigr)\;\times\; 16}{\text{Molecular Mass}}\;\times\;100
$$

where the constants are:
- $a$ = number of carbon atoms,
- $b$ = number of hydrogen atoms,
- $c$ = number of nitrogen atoms (does **not** affect OB since $\text{N} \to \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{N}_2$),
- $d$ = number of oxygen atoms,
- **$16$** = molar mass of oxygen (g/mol).

**Three Canonical Oxygen-Balance Regimes:**

| Regime | OB Value | Behaviour | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero Oxygen Balance (ideal)** | $\text{OB} = 0\%$ | All C oxidises to $\text{CO}_2$; all H to $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | NG (Nitroglycerine, $\text{OB} = +3.5\%$; nearly ideal) |
| **Oxygen Positive** | $\text{OB} > 0\%$ | Excess oxygen; some $\text{O}_2$ released | PETN, NG, Nitroglycol |
| **Oxygen Negative** | $\text{OB} < 0\%$ | Oxygen-deficient; produces toxic CO, soot, $\text{H}_2$, C | TNT ($-74\%$), RDX ($-22\%$), Picric Acid ($-46\%$) |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> The following consolidated table lists every equation a KTU 2024 board examiner can legitimately ask on the *Chemistry of Explosives* topic. Note the deliberate replacement of the vertical-bar absolute-value symbol $\vert\cdot\vert$ with the LaTeX `\vert` macro to prevent markdown-table breakage.

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxygen Balance (per 100 g) | $\text{OB} \;=\; \dfrac{\bigl(d - 2a - \tfrac{b}{2}\bigr)\cdot 16}{M}\,\times\,100$ | Determines whether additional oxidiser must be blended with a fuel-deficient explosive to maximise energy |
| 2 | Heat of Explosion (Hugoniot–Kirchhoff) | $Q_e \;=\; \sum\Delta H_f(\text{products}) \;-\; \sum\Delta H_f(\text{reactants})$ | Predicts the work-potential of an explosive in a confined chamber |
| 3 | Heat of Detonation at Constant Volume | $Q_v \;=\; Q_p \;+\; \Delta n \cdot R \cdot T$ | The actual energy liberated in the detonation wave (since detonation is a constant-V process) |
| 4 | Detonation Temperature (approx.) | $T_{\det} \;\approx\; \dfrac{Q_v}{\sum \bar{C}_p^{(i)} \cdot n_i}$ | Used to estimate the luminous temperature and thermal hazard of an explosive |
| 5 | Berthelot–Petot Brisance Index | $B \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \cdot V_D^2}{g \cdot K}$ | Brisance (shattering ability) scales with $\rho$ and $V_D^2$ |
| 6 | Detonation Velocity (Chapman–Jouguet semi-empirical) | $V_D \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\sqrt{\gamma\,P_{\text{CJ}}\,\rho_{\text{CJ}}}$ | First-pass engineering estimate of $V_D$ |
| 7 | Specific Gas Volume (molar) | $V_0 \;=\; \dfrac{22.4 \cdot \sum n_i}{M_{\text{explosive}}}$ (L/kg) | Volume of gases produced per kg — drives the pneumatic work |
| 8 | Kistiakowsky–Wilson H$_2$O–CO rule | If OB $< 0$: $\text{H} \to \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$ first, then $\text{C} \to \text{CO}$, then $\text{CO} \to \tfrac{1}{2}\text{CO}_2$ | Systematically predicts detonation products |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Oxygen Balance for TNT ($\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked KTU-Type Long-Answer Problem:** Calculate the Oxygen Balance of TNT given its molecular formula is $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$ and molecular mass $M = 227.13\;\text{g/mol}$.

**Step 1 — Identify atomic counts.**

From the formula $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{N}_c\text{O}_d = \text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$:

$$
a = 7,\quad b = 5,\quad c = 3,\quad d = 6
$$

**Step 2 — Write the idealised combustion reaction.**

For *complete combustion* to $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and $\text{N}_2$, the oxygen required is:

$$
\text{O}_2^{\text{needed}} \;=\; 7\,\text{C} \to 7\,\text{CO}_2 \;\text{uses}\; 14\;\text{O atoms}
$$
$$
\text{O}_2^{\text{needed}} \;+\;\; 5\,\text{H} \to \tfrac{5}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\text{uses}\; \tfrac{5}{2}\;\text{O atoms}
$$

So total oxygen atoms required = $14 + \tfrac{5}{2} = \tfrac{33}{2} = 16.5$ atoms.

**Step 3 — Compute the oxygen deficit.**

Oxygen available in TNT = $d = 6$ atoms.
Oxygen deficit $= 6 - 16.5 = -10.5$ atoms of O per molecule.

**Step 4 — Convert to grams of oxygen per 100 g of TNT.**

Each atom of oxygen has mass $16\;\text{g/mol}$, and TNT has $M = 227.13\;\text{g/mol}$.

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{(d - 2a - \tfrac{b}{2})\;\cdot\;16}{M}\;\times\;100
$$

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{\bigl(6 - 2(7) - \tfrac{5}{2}\bigr)\cdot 16}{227.13}\times 100
$$

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{(-10.5)\cdot 16}{227.13}\times 100
$$

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{-168}{227.13}\times 100 \;\approx\; -73.97\;\%
$$

> **Final Exam-Ready Result:** $\boxed{\text{OB}_{\text{TNT}} \approx -74\%}$
>
> The **negative sign** indicates TNT is **strongly oxygen-deficient** — for every kg of TNT fired, $\approx 740\;\text{g}$ of additional external oxygen must be supplied (e.g., from an oxidising salt like $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ in the ANFO blend) for *complete* combustion.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Oxygen Balance for Nitroglycerine (NG, $\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9$)

**Step 1 — Atomic counts.**

$$
a = 3,\;\; b = 5,\;\; c = 3,\;\; d = 9,\;\; M = 227.09\;\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen required for complete combustion.**

$$
\text{O atoms needed} \;=\; 2a + \tfrac{b}{2} \;=\; 2(3) + \tfrac{5}{2} \;=\; 6 + 2.5 \;=\; 8.5
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the master formula.**

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{NG}} \;=\; \dfrac{\bigl(9 - 8.5\bigr)\cdot 16}{227.09}\times 100
$$

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{NG}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5 \cdot 16}{227.09}\times 100 \;\approx\; +3.5\;\%
$$

> **Final Exam-Ready Result:** $\boxed{\text{OB}_{\text{NG}} = +3.5\%}$

The slight oxygen surplus means NG burns almost ideally to $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, releasing maximum energy per gram.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Application of Kistiakowsky–Wilson (K–W) Rules to RDX ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{N}_6\text{O}_6$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The K–W Rules (1955) are universally used in KTU exams to predict the *stable gaseous products* of detonation for an oxygen-negative explosive. Memorise the three-step priority order.

**K–W Rules (Priority Sequence):**

1. **Hydrogen Priority:** All hydrogen preferentially forms $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
2. **Carbon Priority:** Carbon that remains after oxygen allocation first forms $\text{CO}$.
3. **Residual Oxygen:** Any oxygen left after producing $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and $\text{CO}$ then converts $\tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{CO} \to \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{CO}_2$.
4. **Residual Carbon:** Any carbon still remaining after (3) appears as solid **free C (soot)**.
5. **Nitrogen:** Always forms $\text{N}_2$.

**Step 1 — Determine oxygen availability versus demand.**

For RDX $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{N}_6\text{O}_6$ ($M = 222.12\;\text{g/mol}$):

O atoms available = $6$.
O atoms needed for ideal combustion = $2a + b/2 = 6 + 3 = 9$.
Net deficit = $-3$ atoms → oxygen-negative.

**Step 2 — Allocate O to H first (Rule 1).**

Six H atoms → need $3$ O atoms to form $3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$. (O remaining = $6 - 3 = 3$ atoms)

**Step 3 — Allocate O to C (Rule 2).**

Three O atoms remain → can convert $3$ C atoms to $3\,\text{CO}$ (1 O per CO). Carbon available = $3$, oxygen available = $3$ → exactly balanced here, producing $\text{CO}$.

So the balanced detonation equation is:

$$
\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{N}_6\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CO} \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; 3\,\text{N}_2
$$

> **Result:** RDX detonation produces **equal molar** $\text{CO}$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ — no $\text{CO}_2$, no soot, but unfortunately toxic CO is a hazard in underground blasting.

### 3.4 Comparative Stoichiometric Table (Most Tested Explosives)

| Explosive | Formula | $M$ (g/mol) | OB (%) | K–W Detonation Products |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TNT | $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$ | 227.13 | $-74.0$ | $5\,\text{C} + 3\,\text{CO} + 2\,\tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 1\tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{N}_2$ |
| RDX | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{N}_6\text{O}_6$ | 222.12 | $-21.6$ | $3\,\text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\,\text{N}_2$ |
| HMX | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{N}_8\text{O}_8$ | 296.16 | $-21.6$ | $4\,\text{CO} + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4\,\text{N}_2$ |
| PETN | $\text{C}_5\text{H}_8\text{N}_4\text{O}_{12}$ | 316.20 | $-10.1$ | $2\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{CO} + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\,\text{N}_2$ |
| Nitroglycerine | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9$ | 227.09 | $+3.5$ | $3\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 1\tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{N}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2$ |
| AN (Ammonium Nitrate) | $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ | 80.04 | $+20.0$ | $2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{N}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2$ |
| Picric Acid | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7$ | 229.11 | $-45.4$ | $\text{C} + 4\,\text{CO} + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 1\tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{N}_2$ |
| Lead Azide | $\text{Pb(N}_3)_2$ | 291.24 | $-5.6$ | $\text{Pb} + 3\,\text{N}_2$ |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Heat of Detonation ($Q_d$) Values (KTU Reference Data)

| Explosive | $-\Delta H_f$ (kcal/mol) | $Q_d$ (kcal/kg) | $V_0$ (L/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| TNT | 13.0 | **1080** | 730 |
| RDX | 14.7 | **1320** | 908 |
| HMX | 17.9 | **1360** | 782 |
| PETN | 110.6 | **1430** | 780 |
| Nitroglycerine | 88.6 | **1485** | 715 |
| AN | 87.4 | 345 | 980 |
| Lead Azide | — | 360 | 308 |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Hierarchical Classification of Explosives (Chemical Basis)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Explosive Substance] --> B[Single Chemical Compound]
    A --> C[Composite Mixture]

    B --> B1[Primary Initiating Explosives]
    B --> B2[Secondary High Explosives]

    B1 --> B1a[Mercury Fulminate Hg ONC 2]
    B1 --> B1b[Lead Azide Pb N3 2]
    B1 --> B1c[Lead Styphnate]

    B2 --> B2a[Nitroaromatics]
    B2 --> B2b[Nitramines]
    B2 --> B2c[Nitrate Esters]

    B2a --> B2a1[TNT]
    B2a --> B2a2[Picric Acid]
    B2a --> B2a3[Tetryl]

    B2b --> B2b1[RDX]
    B2b --> B2b2[HMX]
    B2b --> B2b3[CL 20]

    B2c --> B2c1[Nitroglycerine]
    B2c --> B2c2[PETN]
    B2c --> B2c3[Nitrocellulose]

    C --> C1[Oxidizer Fuel Blends]
    C --> C2[Pyrotechnic Mixtures]
    C --> C3[Propellants]

    C1 --> C1a[ANFO]
    C1 --> C1b[Gelignite]
    C1 --> C1c[Emulsion Explosives]

    C2 --> C2a[Flash Powder]
    C2 --> C2b[Thermite]

    C3 --> C3a[Smokeless Gunpowder]
    C3 --> C3b[Ballistite]
    C3 --> C3c[Composite Propellants]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Detonation Reaction Pathway (Chemistry of Energy Release)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Init[Initial Shock / Hot Spot] --> React[Exothermic Decomposition Reaction]
    React --> Gases[Hot High Pressure Gases]
    Gases --> Shock[Leading Detonation Shock Wave]
    Shock --> Propagate[Self Sustained Propagation Through Explosive Mass]
    Propagate --> Work[Mechanical Work / Fragmentation / Heave]

    React -.Heat Feedback.-> React
    Shock -.Pressure Feedback.-> Propagate
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Sequential Application of Kistiakowsky–Wilson Rules

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start: Empirical Formula Ca Hb Nc Od] --> S1[Compute Oxygen Balance]
    S1 --> S2{Is OB Zero?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Products: CO2 + H2O + N2]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is OB Negative?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Apply KW Priority]
    S5 --> S5a[Rule 1: H to H2O first]
    S5a --> S5b[Rule 2: Remaining O to C yields CO]
    S5b --> S5c[Rule 3: Any excess O converts CO to CO2]
    S5c --> S5d[Rule 4: Residual C becomes free soot]
    S5d --> S5e[Rule 5: N to N2]
    S5e --> S6[Balanced Detonation Equation]
    S4 -- No Positive --> S7[Excess O2 released as free O2 in products]
    S7 --> S6
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the term "Oxygen Balance" of an explosive. Explain its significance with two examples.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (Examiner-Expected Length: 3-4 lines):**
>
> **Definition:** Oxygen Balance (OB) is the percentage excess or deficiency of oxygen in an explosive relative to the stoichiometric requirement for complete combustion of carbon to $\text{CO}_2$ and hydrogen to $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
>
> **Significance:**
> (i) Determines the **energy-yield efficiency** of the explosive (a zero or slightly positive OB yields maximum energy).
> (ii) Governs the **nature of detonation products** — e.g., oxygen-deficient explosives yield toxic CO and soot; oxygen-balanced ones yield $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
>
> **Examples:** TNT has OB = $-74\%$ (oxygen-deficient); NG has OB = $+3.5\%$ (nearly balanced).

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between a primary and a secondary explosive with one example each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Primary (Initiating) | Secondary (High) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Sensitivity | Very high (sensitive to heat/spark/friction) | Relatively insensitive |
> | Initiation | Detonates by direct flame, spark, or impact | Requires a primary explosive shock |
> | Use | Detonator/Blasting cap charge | Main explosive charge |
> | Example | Lead azide $\text{Pb(N}_3)_2$ | TNT, RDX |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the chemical classification of explosives with a clear hierarchy. Write the molecular formulae and structures of any two nitroaromatic and two nitramine explosives.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the Oxygen Balance of **Picric Acid ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7$, $M = 229.11$ g/mol)**. Predict its detonation products using the **Kistiakowsky–Wilson rules**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE:**
> (i) Many students write the *combustion reaction* equation in (b) but **forget to compute OB numerically** using the formula — both steps carry marks.
> (ii) In K–W rule application, students often forget the **priority order**: H takes O *before* C. Always apply Rule 1 (H → $\text{H}_2\text{O}$) **first**.
> (iii) Do not confuse "oxygen positive" with "energy positive" — an oxygen-positive explosive can still be less powerful if it produces excess $\text{O}_2$ (which does no useful work).

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Classification (write in hierarchical form as below):**

- **Chemical explosives**
  - **Single chemical compounds (Molecular explosives)**
    - **Primary (Initiators):** Mercury fulminate $\text{Hg(ONC)}_2$, Lead azide $\text{Pb(N}_3)_2$, Lead styphnate $\text{C}_6\text{H}(\text{NO}_2)_3\text{O}_2\text{Pb}$.
    - **Secondary (High explosives):**
      - **Nitroaromatics** — benzene/toluene ring bearing $-\text{NO}_2$ groups.
      - **Nitramines** — nitrogen-bearing ring with $-\text{N–NO}_2$ linkage.
      - **Nitrate esters** — alcohols esterified with nitric acid ($-\text{O–NO}_2$).
  - **Composite (Blended) explosives** — physical mixtures of oxidiser + fuel (e.g., ANFO, slurry).

**Examples:**

- *Nitroaromatic 1:* **TNT (2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene)** — $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$. Structure: benzene ring with $-\text{CH}_3$ at position 1 and $-\text{NO}_2$ at positions 2, 4, 6.
- *Nitroaromatic 2:* **Picric Acid (2,4,6-Trinitrophenol)** — $\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7$. Structure: phenol ring with $-\text{NO}_2$ at 2, 4, 6 and $-\text{OH}$ at 1.
- *Nitramine 1:* **RDX (Cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine)** — $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{N}_6\text{O}_6$. Structure: six-membered ring of alternating $-\text{CH}_2-$ and $-\text{N}(\text{NO}_2)-$ groups.
- *Nitramine 2:* **HMX (Cyclotetramethylenetetranitramine)** — $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{N}_8\text{O}_8$. Structure: eight-membered ring with four $-\text{CH}_2-$ and four $-\text{N}(\text{NO}_2)-$ units.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Atomic counts.**

For Picric Acid $\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7$: $a = 6$, $b = 3$, $c = 3$, $d = 7$, $M = 229.11$.

**Step 2 — Apply the OB master formula.**

$$
\text{OB} \;=\; \dfrac{\bigl(7 - 2(6) - \tfrac{3}{2}\bigr)\cdot 16}{229.11}\times 100
$$

$$
\text{OB} \;=\; \dfrac{(7 - 12 - 1.5)\cdot 16}{229.11}\times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{-6.5 \cdot 16}{229.11}\times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{OB}_{\text{Picric Acid}} \;\approx\; -45.39\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply K–W Rules (oxygen-negative, so apply 5-step sequence):**

O available = $7$ atoms. O needed = $2(6) + \tfrac{3}{2} = 13.5$ atoms. Deficit = $-6.5$ atoms → strong oxygen-negative.

- *Rule 1 (H to $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ first):* $3\,\text{H}$ consumes $1.5$ O → produces $\tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$. O left = $7 - 1.5 = 5.5$.
- *Rule 2 (Remaining O converts C to CO):* $5.5$ O atoms convert $5.5$ C atoms to $5.5$ CO.
- *Rule 3 (Any extra O converts CO to $\text{CO}_2$):* No extra O remains.
- *Rule 4 (Residual C):* C remaining = $6 - 5.5 = 0.5$ C → produced as **free soot**.
- *Rule 5 ($\text{N} \to \text{N}_2$):** $3\,\text{N}$ → $1.5\,\text{N}_2$.

**Balanced Detonation Equation:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7 \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{C} \;+\; \tfrac{11}{2}\,\text{CO} \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{N}_2
$$

(Equivalently, multiply through by 2 for integer coefficients: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7 \to 1\,\text{C} + 11\,\text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\,\text{N}_2$ — but check atom balance: LHS has 6 C; RHS has 1 + 11 = 12 C. Re-balance: actually the correct balanced form is $2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7 \to \text{C} + 11\,\text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\,\text{N}_2$.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of suitable molecular formulae, explain the role of **explosophores** in conferring explosive properties to a compound. List any five explosophores with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **oxygen balance of Nitroglycerine ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9$, $M = 227.09$ g/mol)**? If 100 kg of an oxygen-negative explosive (TNT, OB = $-74\%$) is to be blended with Ammonium Nitrate ($\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$, OB = $+20\%$) to achieve an overall zero OB mixture, calculate the **mass ratio in which they must be blended**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE:**
> (i) In part (a), **draw or describe the chemical structure** of at least one example for each explosophore; simply listing the names is not enough.
> (ii) In part (b), students frequently forget to convert the percentages properly when mixing — the OB is a **mass-percentage**, so the additive rule is linear: $\text{OB}_{\text{mix}} = \sum w_i \cdot \text{OB}_i$. Do not use mole fractions.

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Concept:** An *explosophore* is a reactive chemical functional group whose presence in a molecule creates internal strain, weak bonds, and high internal-energy storage such that the molecule is predisposed to rapid, exothermic self-decomposition when initiated. They are the **chemical roots of explosivity**.

**Five Explosophores with Examples:**

1. **Nitro group ($-\text{NO}_2$):** Found in TNT ($\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$), Picric Acid ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7$).
2. **Nitrate ester group ($-\text{O–NO}_2$):** Found in Nitroglycerine ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_9$), PETN ($\text{C}_5\text{H}_8\text{N}_4\text{O}_{12}$).
3. **Azide group ($-\text{N}_3$):** Found in Lead Azide ($\text{Pb(N}_3)_2$).
4. **Diazo / Azo group ($-\text{N}=\text{N}-$):** Found in Azo-bis-isobutyronitrile (AIBN).
5. **Perchlorate group ($-\text{ClO}_4$):** Found in Ammonium Perchlorate ($\text{NH}_4\text{ClO}_4$), used in solid rocket propellants.

**Structural Illustrations (verbal descriptions):**
- In **TNT**, the C–$\text{NO}_2$ bond is a typical *C-nitro* linkage where nitrogen is bonded directly to the aromatic carbon.
- In **Nitroglycerine**, the O–$\text{NO}_2$ bond is an *O-nitro* linkage where oxygen of the glycerol ester binds to the nitro group. O–N bonds are weaker than C–N bonds, making NG more sensitive than TNT.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Oxygen Balance of NG.**

Using the master formula with $a=3$, $b=5$, $c=3$, $d=9$, $M = 227.09$:

$$
\text{OB}_{\text{NG}} \;=\; \dfrac{(9 - 6 - 2.5)\cdot 16}{227.09}\times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{0.5 \cdot 16}{227.09}\times 100 \approx +3.52\%
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\text{OB}_{\text{NG}} \approx +3.5\%}$

**Step 2 — Mass-Ratio Blending of TNT and AN for Zero OB.**

Let mass of TNT = $m_1$ kg with $\text{OB}_1 = -74\%$.
Let mass of AN = $m_2$ kg with $\text{OB}_2 = +20\%$.

For an additive mixture (oxygen balance is mass-weighted):

$$
w_1 \cdot \text{OB}_1 \;+\; w_2 \cdot \text{OB}_2 \;=\; 0
$$

where $w_1 = \dfrac{m_1}{m_1 + m_2}$ and $w_2 = \dfrac{m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$.

Equivalently: $\;m_1 \cdot \text{OB}_1 + m_2 \cdot \text{OB}_2 = 0$.

$$
m_1 \cdot (-74) \;+\; m_2 \cdot (+20) \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} \;=\; \dfrac{20}{74} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{37} \;\approx\; 0.270
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TNT} : \text{AN} \;\approx\; 10 : 37\;\;\text{(by mass)}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** For every $10$ kg of TNT, $37$ kg of Ammonium Nitrate must be blended to achieve complete combustion (zero OB). This is the basic principle behind the industrial **ANFO** formulation (94% AN + 6% Fuel Oil), which is calibrated to achieve near-zero OB for maximum blasting efficiency in quarrying and mining.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Board Exam, Last-Night Revision):**

- [x] **Three conditions for explosion:** Exothermic + High rate + Gaseous products.
- [x] **Detonation vs. Deflagration:** Supersonic shock-driven vs. subsonic heat-conduction.
- [x] **Five primary explosophores:** $-\text{NO}_2$, $-\text{ONO}_2$, $-\text{N}_3$, $-\text{N}=\text{N}-$, $-\text{ClO}_4$.
- [x] **Master OB Formula (memorise verbatim):** $\text{OB} = \dfrac{(d - 2a - b/2)\cdot 16}{M}\times 100$.
- [x] **Standard OB values to remember:**
  - TNT = $-74\%$
  - RDX = HMX = $-21.6\%$
  - PETN = $-10.1\%$
  - NG = $+3.5\%$
  - AN = $+20\%$
- [x] **Kistiakowsky–Wilson 5-step priority:** H→$\text{H}_2\text{O}$ first → C→CO → residual O converts CO→$\text{CO}_2$ → residual C = soot → N→$\text{N}_2$.
- [x] **Three classes of explosives chemically:** Molecular (primary/secondary), Composite, Pyrotechnics.
- [x] **Characteristic energies:** $Q_d \sim 1000{-}1500\;\text{kcal/kg}$; $V_D \sim 1500{-}9000\;\text{m/s}$; $P_{\text{CJ}} \sim 300{,}000\;\text{atm}$.
- [x] **Mixing rule:** Zero-OB blend = mass-weighted linear combination of individual OBs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemical Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Explosives Technology and Safety**, a **Chemical Reaction** refers to the rearrangement of atomic bonds between the constituent elements of an explosive material, its oxidizer, and the surrounding environment, resulting in the transformation of the reactant species into thermodynamically more stable product molecules. For explosives, these reactions are characteristically **highly exothermic**, **fast** (propagation speeds of mm/s for deflagration up to km/s for detonation), and **self-oxidizing**, meaning the explosive carries both the fuel and oxidizer within the same molecular framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT753, Module 1):**
> Chemical reactions in explosives technology include **redox reactions, decomposition reactions, combustion reactions, and detonation reactions**. They are the foundation for understanding heat of reaction, oxygen balance, gas evolution, and energy release — all critical performance and safety parameters.

### 1.2 Classification of Chemical Reactions Relevant to Explosives

| Reaction Type | Formal Definition | Example in Explosives |
|---|---|---|
| **Decomposition Reaction** | A single compound breaks into simpler products. | $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \rightarrow \text{N}_2\text{O} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| **Redox (Oxidation–Reduction) Reaction** | Simultaneous electron transfer; oxidizer gains e⁻, fuel loses e⁻. | $2\text{KNO}_3 + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2$ |
| **Combustion Reaction** | Rapid oxidation producing heat, light, and gases. | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| **Detonation Reaction** | Supersonic shock-driven decomposition of an explosive. | $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 7\text{CO} + \frac{5}{2}\text{H}_2 + \frac{3}{2}\text{N}_2$ |
| **Synthesis Reaction** | Two or more species combine to form a new product. | $\text{HNO}_3 + \text{C}_6\text{H}_2(\text{CH}_3)_3 \rightarrow \text{TNT} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an explosive molecule like a compressed spring held shut by a tiny latch (the activation energy barrier).**
> - **Redox** is like burning coal in a furnace — oxygen strips electrons away steadily.
> - **Combustion** is a slow opening of the spring — the latch slides off over a second.
> - **Detonation** is a hammer smashing the latch — the spring snaps open in microseconds, releasing all stored energy nearly instantaneously.
> - **Decomposition** is the spring unwinding by itself once the latch weakens (e.g., old ammonium nitrate caking and breaking down).

This is why an explosive reaction is not just "fast" — it is **self-sustaining**, **shock-driven**, and produces **permanent gases** that generate immense pressure.

### 1.4 Essential Stoichiometric & Thermodynamic Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used Throughout Explosives Chemistry:**
> - Standard molar gas volume, **$V_m = 22.414\ \text{L/mol}$ at STP (273.15 K, 1 atm)**
> - Universal gas constant, **$R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$**
> - Atomic oxygen mass used in oxygen balance, **$= 16\ \text{g/mol}$**
> - Standard reference state enthalpy, **$\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$** for elements in their standard state (O₂, N₂, H₂, C-graphite).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry Behind Explosive Performance

Every explosive reaction can be analyzed through four cardinal parameters that KTU examiners consistently test:

1. **Heat of Reaction (ΔH)** — How much thermal energy is released per mole/gram.
2. **Oxygen Balance (OB)** — Whether the explosive has too little, too much, or exactly enough oxygen.
3. **Gas Volume (V₀)** — How many moles of gaseous product are produced per gram.
4. **Reaction Velocity** — Deflagration vs. detonation (sub-sonic vs. supersonic).

### 2.2 Oxygen Balance (OB) — Step-by-Step Logic

The **Oxygen Balance** indicates the percentage of oxygen deficiency or excess when an explosive undergoes complete combustion to $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$, and $\text{SO}_2$ (if sulfur present).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OB matters:**
> - **Negative OB** → fuel-rich → produces toxic **CO** (carbon monoxide) → dangerous in confined spaces.
> - **Zero OB** → ideal, maximum energy + clean gases.
> - **Positive OB** → oxygen-rich → excess $\text{O}_2$ → reduced energy per gram.

For an explosive with molecular formula $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{N}_c\text{O}_d$:

**Step 1** — Compute oxygen required for complete combustion of C and H:

$$\text{O}_{\text{required}} = 2a + \frac{b}{2}\ \text{(in oxygen atoms)}$$

**Step 2** — Compare with oxygen available in the molecule:

$$\text{O}_{\text{available}} = d$$

**Step 3** — Convert difference to grams and express as % of molecular weight:

$$\text{OB}\,(\%) = \frac{16 \times \left(d - 2a - \frac{b}{2}\right)}{M_w} \times 100$$

### 2.3 Heat of Reaction (ΔH) — Hess's Law Application

Heat of reaction is computed from **standard heats of formation** ($\Delta H_f^\circ$) of reactants and products:

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum_{j} n_j \cdot \Delta H_{f,\text{products}}^\circ - \sum_{i} n_i \cdot \Delta H_{f,\text{reactants}}^\circ$$

For explosives, the products of **detonation** are typically taken as:
- $\text{H}_2\text{O (g)}$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{C (solid graphite)}$, $\text{CO}$ (depending on OB).

### 2.4 Gas Volume Produced per Gram of Explosive

Permanent gas volume is a measure of "work capability" — more gas + more heat = more pressure work done on surroundings.

$$V_0 = \frac{n_{\text{gas}} \times 22{,}414\ \text{mL}}{M_w} \quad \text{(in mL/g at STP)}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Explosive Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen Balance | $\text{OB}\,(\%) = \dfrac{16 \cdot (d - 2a - b/2)}{M_w} \times 100$ | % | TNT: −73.97 % ; NG: +3.5 % |
| Heat of Reaction | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum n_j \Delta H_{f,P}^\circ - \sum n_i \Delta H_{f,R}^\circ$ | kJ/mol | TNT ≈ −1130 kJ/mol |
| Heat per Gram | $Q = \dfrac{\vert \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ \vert}{M_w} \times 1000$ | J/g | TNT ≈ 4560 J/g |
| Specific Gas Volume | $V_0 = \dfrac{n_{\text{gas}} \times 22{,}414}{M_w}$ | mL/g | TNT ≈ 730 mL/g |
| Detonation Velocity (Chapman–Jouguet) | $D = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$ | m/s | TNT ≈ 6900 m/s |
| CJ Pressure | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$ | Pa | TNT ≈ 21 GPa |
| Thermal Explosion Criterion (Semenov) | $\text{critical radius}\ r_c = \sqrt{\delta_{\text{th}}\dfrac{\lambda}{Q \rho C_v}}$ | m | Used for cook-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These relationships feed directly into:
> - **Blast design** in mining & demolition (using OB + ΔH).
> - **Storage safety** (Semenov thermal explosion limit → cook-off prevention).
> - **Propellant selection** (gas volume → ballistic efficiency).
> - **Crater & fragmentation prediction** (CJ pressure → shock impulse).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Oxygen Balance of TNT (C₇H₅N₃O₆)

We will derive, from first principles, the oxygen balance of **2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene (TNT)** — the reference standard explosive in KTU examinations.

**Given:**
- Molecular formula: $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$
- Atomic counts: $a = 7,\ b = 5,\ c = 3,\ d = 6$

**Step 1 — Compute molecular weight:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_w &= (7 \times 12.011) + (5 \times 1.008) + (3 \times 14.007) + (6 \times 15.999) \\
    &= 84.077 + 5.040 + 42.021 + 95.994 \\
    &= 227.132\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute oxygen required for complete combustion to $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$:**

$$\text{O}_{\text{req}} = 2a + \frac{b}{2} = 2(7) + \frac{5}{2} = 14 + 2.5 = 16.5\ \text{atoms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute oxygen available in the molecule:**

$$\text{O}_{\text{avail}} = d = 6\ \text{atoms}$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen deficit (negative sign convention):**

$$\Delta \text{O} = d - \left(2a + \frac{b}{2}\right) = 6 - 16.5 = -10.5\ \text{atoms}$$

**Step 5 — Convert deficit to mass and normalize to molecular weight:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OB}\,(\%) &= \frac{16 \times (-10.5)}{227.132} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{-168}{227.132} \times 100 \\
&= -73.97\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** TNT is **oxygen-deficient by ~74 %**. This is why detonation products of TNT contain **CO (carbon monoxide)** and **solid carbon (soot)**, not pure $\text{CO}_2$ — visible as the black smoke of military TNT detonations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Heat of Detonation of TNT Using Hess's Law

We will derive the heat released when **1 mole of TNT detonates** using the **Kistiakowsky–Wilson rules** for explosive products.

**Reaction (oxygen-deficient case, typical detonation products):**

$$\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 1.5\ \text{CO}_2 + 5.5\ \text{CO} + 2.5\ \text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.5\ \text{N}_2 + 0.75\ \text{C (s)}$$

**Standard heats of formation used (from thermochemical tables):**

| Species | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (kJ/mol) |
|---|---|
| TNT (C₇H₅N₃O₆) | −54.4 |
| CO₂ (g) | −393.5 |
| CO (g) | −110.5 |
| H₂O (g) | −241.8 |
| N₂ (g) | 0.0 |
| C (graphite) | 0.0 |

**Step 1 — Sum heats of formation of products (each multiplied by stoichiometric coefficient):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum n_j \Delta H_{f,P}^\circ &= (1.5)(-393.5) + (5.5)(-110.5) + (2.5)(-241.8) \\
&\quad + (1.5)(0) + (0.75)(0) \\
&= -590.25 + (-607.75) + (-604.50) \\
&= -1802.5\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum heats of formation of reactants:**

$$\sum n_i \Delta H_{f,R}^\circ = 1 \times (-54.4) = -54.4\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hess's Law:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ &= \sum n_j \Delta H_{f,P}^\circ - \sum n_i \Delta H_{f,R}^\circ \\
&= (-1802.5) - (-54.4) \\
&= -1748.1\ \text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to heat per gram of TNT:**

$$Q = \frac{\vert \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ \vert}{M_w} = \frac{1748.1}{227.13} = 7.70\ \text{kJ/g} \approx 7700\ \text{J/g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The experimentally measured heat of detonation of TNT is **≈ 4500 kJ/kg (4.5 kJ/g)** at standard conditions. Our derived value is high because we assumed water in gaseous state and ideal product distribution. With $\text{H}_2\text{O (liq)}$ and full $\text{CO}_2$, the experimental value drops. This shows the importance of specifying product states in Hess's Law.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Gas Volume of TNT

Using the detonation reaction equation:

$$\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 1.5\ \text{CO}_2 + 5.5\ \text{CO} + 2.5\ \text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.5\ \text{N}_2 + 0.75\ \text{C}$$

**Step 1 — Count gaseous moles per mole of TNT (excluding solid C):**

$$n_{\text{gas}} = 1.5 + 5.5 + 2.5 + 1.5 = 11.0\ \text{mol gas/mol TNT}$$

**Step 2 — Apply STP molar volume:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= \frac{n_{\text{gas}} \times 22{,}414\ \text{mL/mol}}{M_w} \\
&= \frac{11.0 \times 22{,}414}{227.13} \\
&= \frac{246{,}554}{227.13} \\
&= 1085.5\ \text{mL/g at STP}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Observation:** The theoretical $V_0$ of TNT is ~1085 mL/g, but experimental value at detonation conditions (very high T, P) is **~730 mL/g at STP** after correcting for partial recombination of CO and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ into $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2$ at the CJ isentrope.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Explosives

| Explosive | Formula | OB (%) | Heat of Detonation (kJ/g) | $V_0$ (mL/g) | Detonation Velocity (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TNT | C₇H₅N₃O₆ | −73.97 | 4.50 | 730 | 6900 |
| RDX | C₃H₆N₆O₆ | −21.6 | 5.40 | 908 | 8750 |
| HMX | C₄H₈N₈O₈ | −21.6 | 5.50 | 927 | 9100 |
| PETN | C₅H₈N₄O₁₂ | −10.1 | 5.80 | 780 | 8400 |
| NG | C₃H₅N₃O₉ | +3.5 | 6.30 | 715 | 7700 |
| AN | NH₄NO₃ | +20.0 | 1.60 | 980 | 2500 |
| Lead Azide | PbN₆ | — | 1.54 | 308 | 5180 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Oxygen Balance. Calculate the Oxygen Balance of PETN (C₅H₈N₄O₁₂).**

**Model Answer:**

Oxygen Balance (OB) is the percentage of oxygen excess or deficit when an explosive undergoes complete combustion to its most oxidized gaseous products ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{SO}_2$).

For PETN: $\text{C}_5\text{H}_8\text{N}_4\text{O}_{12}$, $a = 5$, $b = 8$, $d = 12$.

$$M_w = (5)(12) + (8)(1) + (4)(14) + (12)(16) = 60 + 8 + 56 + 192 = 316\ \text{g/mol}$$

$$\text{OB}\,(\%) = \frac{16 \times \left(12 - 10 - \frac{8}{2}\right)}{316} \times 100 = \frac{16 \times (-2)}{316} \times 100 = -10.13\,\%$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Combustion (Deflagration) and Detonation reactions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Combustion / Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction velocity | Subsonic (mm/s to m/s) | Supersonic (km/s, 1500–9500 m/s) |
| Driving mechanism | Heat conduction | Shock wave |
| Pressure effect on velocity | Strongly increases | Marginal |
| Density of explosive effect | Sensitive | Sensitive |
| Products | Gases + heat | Gases + heat + extreme pressure |
| Example | Gunpowder burning | TNT, RDX detonation |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the Heat of Detonation of **RDX (C₃H₆N₆O₆)** using Hess's Law, given the following standard heats of formation:
RDX = +74.0 kJ/mol; $\text{CO}_2$ = −393.5 kJ/mol; $\text{CO}$ = −110.5 kJ/mol; $\text{H}_2\text{O (g)}$ = −241.8 kJ/mol; $\text{N}_2$ = 0 kJ/mol; C (graphite) = 0 kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mining engineer must select an explosive for **underground coal blasting** where **toxic CO production is critical**. Compare OB of **TNT, RDX, and ANFO (NH₄NO₃ + fuel oil)**. Recommend the best choice with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Using Kistiakowsky–Wilson rules for an oxygen-deficient explosive (OB = −21.6 % for RDX):

**Detonation reaction assumed:**
$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{N}_6\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 3\ \text{CO} + 3\ \text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\ \text{N}_2$$

**Step 1 — Sum product enthalpies:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum n_j \Delta H_{f,P}^\circ &= 3(-110.5) + 3(-241.8) + 3(0) \\
&= -331.5 + (-725.4) + 0 \\
&= -1056.9\ \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Reactant enthalpy:**

$$\sum n_i \Delta H_{f,R}^\circ = 1 \times (+74.0) = +74.0\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Hess's Law:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = -1056.9 - (+74.0) = -1130.9\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Heat per gram:**

$$Q = \frac{1130.9}{222.12} = 5.09\ \text{kJ/g}$$

**(b)** Oxygen Balance comparison:

| Explosive | Formula | OB (%) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| TNT | C₇H₅N₃O₆ | −73.97 | Heavy fuel-rich → **high CO** → unsafe underground |
| RDX | C₃H₆N₆O₆ | −21.6 | Moderate CO → needs ventilation |
| ANFO | NH₄NO₃ + diesel | ≈ 0 (tuned) | **Near zero OB → clean gases → safest** |

**Recommendation:** **ANFO (Ammonium Nitrate + Fuel Oil)** is the best choice because:
- Its OB can be **tuned to zero** by adjusting fuel oil ratio (≈ 5.7 % by mass).
- Produces minimum CO and toxic fumes — critical for **underground confined blasting**.
- Cheapest among the three — economical for large-scale mining.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**
**(a)** Explain the **Semenov Thermal Explosion Theory**. Derive the **critical condition for thermal ignition** in terms of heat generation rate and heat loss rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kg stockpile of ammonium nitrate is stored in a warehouse. If the surrounding temperature is 35 °C and the activation energy of decomposition is 110 kJ/mol, calculate whether thermal runaway will occur given that the **critical ambient temperature** is 38 °C. Justify your answer using Semenov's criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Semenov's Theory:**
Semenov models a reactive mass as a uniform-temperature system with two competing processes:

$$\text{Heat generation rate:}\ \dot{q}_g = Q \cdot k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot C^n\ (\text{exponential in } T)$$

$$\text{Heat loss rate:}\ \dot{q}_l = \chi \cdot S \cdot (T - T_0)\ (\text{linear in } T)$$

**Critical condition** — at the ignition point the two curves are tangent:

$$\dot{q}_g = \dot{q}_l \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d\dot{q}_g}{dT} = \frac{d\dot{q}_l}{dT}$$

Solving simultaneously yields:

$$T_{\text{crit}} = \frac{E_a}{2R} \quad \text{and}\quad \dot{q}_{g,\text{crit}} = \frac{E_a}{2eRT_{\text{crit}}^2}\ \dot{q}_l(T_{\text{crit}})$$

If $T_0$ (ambient) lies above the critical value, runaway occurs.

**(b) Calculation:**

Given: $T_0 = 35\,°\text{C} = 308.15\ \text{K}$, $E_a = 110{,}000\ \text{J/mol}$, critical ambient $T_{\text{crit,amb}} = 38\,°\text{C}$.

Since the **ambient temperature (35 °C) is below the critical ambient temperature (38 °C)**, Semenov's criterion predicts **NO thermal runaway** under steady-state storage conditions.

However, **localized hotspots (Semenov's Frank–Kamenetskii limit)**, contamination, or confinement could still trigger decomposition. Safety margin = 3 °C — **warehouse cooling/ventilation is mandatory**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify product state** (gaseous vs liquid $\text{H}_2\text{O}$) — examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Sign confusion** in Hess's Law — always compute (Products − Reactants), not vice versa.
> 3. **Unit errors** in oxygen balance — final answer must be in **%**.
> 4. **Wrong stoichiometric coefficient for N₂** — nitrogen atoms pair up as $\text{N}_2$, so divide by 2.
> 5. **Forgetting that OB requires atomic oxygen mass = 16 g/mol** — converting atom count to grams is a common error.
> 6. **Confusing $\Delta H_{\text{combustion}}$ with $\Delta H_{\text{det}}$** — detonation reactions follow different product rules (Kistiakowsky–Wilson / Kamlet) than combustion.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Oxygen Balance (OB):** indicator of oxygen surplus/deficit; computed as $\dfrac{16(d - 2a - b/2)}{M_w} \times 100$.
- **Hess's Law:** $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = \sum \Delta H_{f,P} - \sum \Delta H_{f,R}$ — always products minus reactants.
- **Negative OB** → fuel-rich → toxic CO + soot; **Positive OB** → oxygen-rich; **Zero OB** → cleanest + highest work.
- **Heat of detonation (TNT)** ≈ 4.5 kJ/g; **Detonation velocity (TNT)** ≈ 6.9 km/s; **CJ Pressure** ≈ 21 GPa.
- **Specific gas volume** $V_0$ is a direct measure of explosive's pressure work capability.
- **Deflagration vs. Detonation:** subsonic heat-driven vs. supersonic shock-driven.
- **Kistiakowsky–Wilson rules** govern product distribution for oxygen-deficient explosives during detonation.
- **Semenov's thermal explosion theory** is the foundation of **cook-off safety** in storage magazines.
- **ANFO** has tunable OB ≈ 0 → safest for **underground blasting**.
- Always **write balanced equations** with proper stoichiometric coefficients (fractions allowed for whole-molecule scaling).
- **Permanent gases** ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{CO}$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O (g)}$) at STP volume of **22.414 L/mol**.
- **KTU expects**: chemical equation + unit + numerical answer + 1–2 sentence engineering interpretation in every numerical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Categories of Explosives by Chemical Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Categories of Explosives by Chemical Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT753 – Explosives Technology and Safety)** syllabus under Module 1, **Explosives by Chemical Type** refers to the systematic classification of energetic materials based on their intrinsic molecular structure, the nature of bond cleavage they undergo during energy release, and the chemical family of their principal reactive constituents. This classification is foundational because the *chemical architecture* of an explosive determines its **detonation velocity (VOD)**, **sensitivity to initiation**, **oxygen balance**, **gas volume evolved per kilogram**, and its **classification for storage, transport, and licensing under the Indian Explosives Rules, 2008**.

The three broadest chemical categories are:

1. **Single-Compound (Molecular) Explosives** — Explosives in which the entire molecule is the explosive entity (e.g., TNT, RDX, HMX, PETN, Nitroglycerine).
2. **Mixed (Composite / Blended) Explosives** — Two or more chemically distinct components physically mixed to achieve desired properties (e.g., ANFO, dynamites, slurries, emulsions).
3. **Pyrotechnic Compositions** — Mixtures of fuels and oxidizers designed primarily for heat, light, smoke, or sound rather than brisant work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Core Concept):** The chemical-type classification is *orthogonal* to the low/high explosive or primary/secondary explosive classifications. A student must be able to map any given explosive (e.g., PETN) onto BOTH axes — chemical family AND explosive class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of explosives the way a **chef thinks of cooking ingredients**:
- **Single-compound explosives** are like a *ready-made curry paste* — the chemistry is already "cooked in" at the molecular level. Just apply a spark (heat/impact) and the entire molecule reacts.
- **Mixed explosives** are like a *trail-mix snack* — individual fuel (oil, nuts) and oxidizer (salt, spices) components are physically combined; the reaction happens *between* components at the grain boundaries.
- **Pyrotechnics** are like a *decorative fruitcake* — the goal is not destruction but controlled sensory output (light, smoke, sound).

Just as a chef chooses ingredients based on *flavor profile* (sweet, spicy, sour), a blasting engineer chooses explosives based on the **performance profile** (brisance, fume class, water resistance, sensitivity).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following metrics are universally applied across all chemical-type categories and must be memorized:

- **Detonation Velocity (VOD): typically $\mathbf{1{,}500 - 9{,}000\ m/s}$** (deflagrating materials: < $\mathbf{1{,}000\ m/s}$)
- **Loading Density ($\rho$): typically $\mathbf{0.7 - 1.78\ g/cm^3}$** for commercial explosives
- **Oxygen Balance (OB): theoretical range $\mathbf{-100\%\ to\ +60\%}$**
- **Standard Temperature of Explosion: $\mathbf{T_{exp} \approx 2{,}000 - 4{,}500\ ^\circ C}$**
- **Gas volume evolved: $\mathbf{600 - 1{,}100\ L/kg}$** of explosive
- **Universal gas constant (R) used in thermochemistry: $\mathbf{R = 8.314\ J/(mol\cdot K)}$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Oxygen Balance (OB):** The percentage of oxygen remaining or deficient after complete combustion of an explosive to products such as CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, etc. An OB = **0%** is ideal (zero excess, zero deficit). Negative OB → toxic **CO** is produced; positive OB → toxic **NOₓ** is produced.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification Logic

The classification proceeds in three logical tiers:

**Tier 1 — Mechanistic Tier (How energy propagates):**
- **Detonating (High) Explosives**: Supersonic shock-driven reaction. Reaction zone thickness: $\mathbf{0.1 - 1\ mm}$.
- **Deflagrating (Low) Explosives**: Subsonic combustion-driven reaction. Reaction zone thickness: $\mathbf{> 1\ mm}$, often centimeters.

**Tier 2 — Sensitivity Tier (How easily it is initiated):**
- **Primary (Initiator) Explosives**: Detonate from heat, spark, friction, or impact. Used in *blasting caps* and *detonators*. Example: Lead Azide (Pb(N₃)₂), Mercury Fulminate (Hg(ONC)₂), DDNP.
- **Secondary (Booster/Main Charge) Explosives**: Require a strong shock from a primary explosive. Used in *booster charges* and *column charges*. Example: PETN, RDX, HMX, TNT.

**Tier 3 — Chemical-Family Tier (What the molecule/compound is made of):**

| # | Chemical Family | Representative Explosive | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nitrate Esters (organic)** | Nitroglycerin (NG), Nitrocellulose (NC), PETN | Dynamites, propellants, detonating cord |
| 2 | **Nitramines (cyclic)** | RDX (Cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine), HMX | Military boosters, composite propellants |
| 3 | **Aromatic Nitro Compounds** | TNT (Trinitrotoluene), Picric Acid, Tetryl | Military shells, commercial boosters |
| 4 | **Aliphatic Nitro Compounds** | Nitromethane, Nitropropane | Specialty industrial blasting |
| 5 | **Ammonium Nitrate (Oxidizer Salt)** | AN (NH₄NO₃) — alone or with fuel oil | ANFO, heavy ANFO |
| 6 | **Chlorate / Perchlorate Salts** | KClO₃, KClO₄ + fuel | Historical "Cheddite", pyrotechnics |
| 7 | **Metal-Acid Explosives** | Lead Azide, Silver Azide, Lead Styphnate | Primary detonators, percussion caps |
| 8 | **Fulminates** | Mercury Fulminate Hg(ONC)₂ | Historical blasting caps |
| 9 | **Peroxides** | TATP, HMTD | Improvised / unstable, security concern |
| 10 | **Slurry / Water-Gel** | AN + water + sensitizer + fuel + crosslinker | Wet-hole blasting, bulk loading |
| 11 | **Emulsion Explosives** | AN/NN solution droplets in oil matrix | Bulk commercial blasting, quarrying |
| 12 | **Pyrotechnic Compositions** | Mg/PTFE/Viton (Thermite-like flash) | Military flares, signals |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of the vertical bar `|` in all table entries to prevent markdown parsing conflicts.

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen Balance (OB)** | $OB = \dfrac{1600}{M}\ \bigl(2n_{CO_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}n_{CO} + n_{H_2O} + n_{SO_2} - 2n_C - \tfrac{1}{2}n_H - 2n_O\bigr)$ | Percentage of excess/deficit oxygen relative to complete combustion; ideal = **0%** |
| **Heat of Explosion (Q)** | $Q = -\Delta H_{products - reactants}\ \text{[kJ/kg]}$ | Energy liberated per kg; typical range $\mathbf{2{,}000 - 6{,}000\ kJ/kg}$ |
| **Detonation Pressure (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot VOD^{2}}{4}$ | Brisance indicator; typical $\mathbf{5 - 40\ GPa}$ |
| **Detonation Velocity (Chapman–Jouguet)** | $VOD = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T_{CJ}}{M_{avg}}}$ | CJ-plane velocity; depends on $\gamma$ (ratio of specific heats) |
| **Gas Volume (V₀)** | $V_0 = \dfrac{22.4 \cdot n_{gas}}{M_{explosive}}\ \text{[L/kg]}$ | Work-producing gas volume at STP |
| **Loading Density (ρ)** | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}\ \text{[g/cm}^{3}\text{]}$ | Mass per unit charge volume; affects VOD |
| **Trauzl Lead Block Test** | $\Delta V = V_{after} - V_{before}\ \text{[cm}^{3}\text{]}$ | Relative strength test; standard sample **PETN = 500 cm³** |
| **Rate of Detonation (D)** | $D = \dfrac{L}{t}\ \text{[m/s]}$ | Measured VOD via Dautriche or continuous VOD probe |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for chemical families — "NACAPO"**: **N**itrates → **N**itramines → **A**romatic Nitro → **C**hlorates → **A**mmonium Salts → **P**eroxides → **O**xidizer/Fuel mixes.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of Chemical-Type Classification |
|---|---|
| **Mining & Quarrying** | Choosing ANFO vs emulsion vs dynamite based on rock hardness, water inflow, fume regulations |
| **Defence / Ordnance** | Selecting RDX/HMX/TNT formulations for warheads, shells, and shaped charges |
| **Civil Blasting** | Demolition of buildings, tunnels (NHAI tunneling uses bulk emulsion) |
| **Aerospace** | Composite modified double-base (CMDB) propellants for rockets use NC + NG + RDX |
| **Forensic Explosives Investigation** | Identifying post-blast residues (e.g., NG traces → dynamite; AN prills → ANFO) |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Indian Explosives Rules 2008 classify by hazard (Class 1.1, 1.2, …, 1.6 UN system) tied to chemical behavior |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Oxygen Balance for TNT (C₇H₅N₃O₆)

This is the **single most asked quantitative question** in KTU exams on this topic. We derive it line-by-line.

**Step 1: Write the molecular formula.**
TNT = C₇H₅N₃O₆. Molecular weight $M = 7(12) + 5(1) + 3(14) + 6(16) = 84 + 5 + 42 + 96 = 227\ g/mol$.

**Step 2: Write the ideal complete-combustion equation (oxygen balanced to CO₂, H₂O, N₂).**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_7 H_5 N_3 O_6 \longrightarrow\ & 7\ CO_2 + \tfrac{5}{2}\ H_2O + \tfrac{3}{2}\ N_2 \\
\text{Atomic O on RHS} &= 7(2) + \tfrac{5}{2}(1) = 14 + 2.5 = 16.5 \\
\text{Atomic O already in TNT} &= 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the deficit.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Deficit O atoms} &= 16.5 - 6 = 10.5 \\
\text{Deficit in grams per mole} &= 10.5 \times 16 = 168\ g \\
\text{Oxygen Balance} &= \dfrac{-168}{227} \times 100 = -73.99\ \% \approx \mathbf{-74\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**
TNT has a strongly **negative oxygen balance**, meaning combustion under confined blasting conditions will produce toxic **CO** instead of the desired CO₂. This is why TNT is rarely used alone in confined underground mines — fume-class issues.

### 3.2 Derivation: Detonation Pressure of PETN at Loading Density ρ = 1.7 g/cm³

Given: $VOD = 8{,}340\ m/s$ (literature value for confined PETN).

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{\rho \cdot VOD^{2}}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}700\ kg/m^{3} \cdot (8{,}340\ m/s)^{2}}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}700 \times 69{,}555{,}600}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{1.1824 \times 10^{11}}{4} \\
&= 2.956 \times 10^{10}\ Pa \\
&= \mathbf{29.56\ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** This extremely high pressure in the detonation front is what makes PETN an excellent booster explosive — it can fully initiate a less-sensitive secondary charge like ANFO or a cast TNT/Wax picrite charge.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Chemical Family vs. Property

| Chemical Family | Oxygen Balance (%) | Sensitivity (Figure of Insensitiveness) | Brisance (by Sand Test) | Water Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nitrate Esters (NG, NC)** | $+3.5$ (NG) | High (FI ≈ 10) | High | Poor (NG) |
| **Nitramines (RDX)** | $-21.6$ | Medium (FI ≈ 80) | Very High | Excellent |
| **Aromatic Nitro (TNT)** | $-74$ | Low (FI ≈ 200+) | Medium | Excellent |
| **AN (Oxidizer Salt)** | $+20$ (AN) to $-0.4$ (ANFO at 94/6) | Low (insensitive) | Low | Hygroscopic, dissolves |
| **Chlorates** | varies | Medium | Medium | Poor |
| **Lead Azide** | $-5.0$ (approx) | Very High (FI < 5) | Used as initiator | Slight |
| **Emulsions** | $\approx -2$ to $+2$ | Very Low (bulk transportable) | Medium | Excellent (water in oil) |
| **Slurries/Water-Gels** | $\approx -5$ to $-15$ | Low | Medium | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure of Insensitiveness (FI)** — The minimum height (in cm) of a 2-kg drop-weight that causes 50% probability of explosion. **Higher FI = Less sensitive (safer to handle).** Standard sample **RDX = 80 cm**.

### 3.4 Classification Algorithm (Step-by-Step Mapping)

When given a chemical name, follow this decision tree:

**Step 1** → Does the molecule contain the **-O-NO₂ group** (nitrate ester)? → **YES** ⇒ Nitrate Ester family (NG, NC, PETN, EGDN, DEGDN).
**Step 2** → Does the molecule contain the **N-NO₂ group in a ring**? → **YES** ⇒ Nitramine family (RDX, HMX, CL-20).
**Step 3** → Is the -NO₂ group attached directly to an **aromatic ring**? → **YES** ⇒ Aromatic Nitro family (TNT, picric acid, tetryl, DNT).
**Step 4** → Is the principal oxidizer **NH₄NO₃**? → **YES** ⇒ AN-based family (ANFO, heavy ANFO, slurries).
**Step 5** → Does the molecule contain a **heavy metal azide (-N₃)**? → **YES** ⇒ Metal azide family (primary).
**Step 6** → Is the mixture a **water-in-oil emulsion**? → **YES** ⇒ Emulsion explosive.
**Step 7** → None of the above, contains **-O-O- peroxide** ⇒ Peroxide explosive.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
