---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)] — 3 Marks**

*Classify the following explosives into their respective chemical families: (i) RDX, (ii) TNT, (iii) Nitroglycerin, (iv) Lead Azide, (v) ANFO.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — distribution shown):**

| Explosive | Chemical Family | Mark Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| RDX | **Nitramine** (cyclic N-NO₂) | 0.5 |
| TNT | **Aromatic Nitro Compound** | 0.5 |
| Nitroglycerin | **Nitrate Ester** (organic O-NO₂) | 0.5 |
| Lead Azide | **Metal Azide** (primary initiator) | 0.5 |
| ANFO | **Mixed Oxidizer-Fuel** (AN + fuel oil) | 0.5 |
| Proper identification + brief reasoning (1-line each) | — | 0.5 |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] — 3 Marks**

*Define Oxygen Balance. Compute the oxygen balance of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃, M = 80 g/mol) and comment on its suitability as a blasting oxidizer.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Oxygen Balance is the percentage of oxygen liberated or required for complete combustion of an explosive to fully oxidized products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂).

**Calculation (1.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
NH_4NO_3 \longrightarrow N_2 + 2H_2O + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \\
\text{O atoms produced} &= 0.5 \times 2 = 1 \\
\text{Per gram-mole} &= 16\ g \\
OB &= \dfrac{+16}{80} \times 100 = \mathbf{+20\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (0.5 Mark):** Positive OB (+20%) means AN releases excess oxygen, ideal for oxidizing co-mingled fuels like fuel oil in ANFO.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain, with chemical reactions, the four major chemical families of secondary (booster) explosives used in commercial blasting. Compare their oxygen balance and water resistance.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A cylindrical borehole of 100 mm diameter and 6 m depth is loaded with a bulk emulsion explosive of density 1.25 g/cm³, occupying 80% of the hole volume. The remaining 20% is stemming. Calculate: (i) Total mass of explosive per hole, (ii) Total volume of gas evolved at STP if the gas volume of the emulsion is 900 L/kg, (iii) The heat of explosion in MJ per hole if the heat of explosion is 4.2 MJ/kg. (Assume explosive completely detonates.)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Model Answer Outline (7 Marks):**

| Chemical Family | Example | Reaction Snippet | OB | Water Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nitrate Esters** | NG: C₃H₅N₃O₉ | $4C_3H_5N_3O_9 \rightarrow 12CO_2 + 10H_2O + 6N_2 + O_2$ | $+3.5\%$ | Poor (liquid NG migrates) |
| **Nitramines** | RDX: C₃H₆N₆O₆ | $C_3H_6N_6O_6 \rightarrow 3CO + 3H_2O + 3N_2$ | $-21.6\%$ | Excellent |
| **Aromatic Nitro** | TNT: C₇H₅N₃O₆ | $2C_7H_5N_3O_6 \rightarrow 7CO + 7C + 5H_2O + 3N_2$ | $-74\%$ | Excellent |
| **Mixed (ANFO)** | 94% AN + 6% FO | $3NH_4NO_3 + CH_2 \rightarrow 3N_2 + 7H_2O + CO_2$ | $\approx 0\%$ | Poor (AN dissolves) |

*(Each family: 1.25 marks; one chemical reaction equation + OB + water resistance + industrial example. Total = 4 × 1.25 = 5 marks. Additional 2 marks for comparison summary.)*

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Volume of borehole.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{hole} &= \pi \cdot r^{2} \cdot h \\
&= \pi \cdot (0.05\ m)^{2} \cdot 6\ m \\
&= \pi \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 6 \\
&= 0.04712\ m^{3} \\
&= 47{,}124\ cm^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Explosive volume (80% fill) and mass.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{explosive} &= 0.80 \times 47{,}124 = 37{,}699.2\ cm^{3} \\
\text{Mass} &= \rho \times V = 1.25 \times 37{,}699.2 = \mathbf{47{,}124\ g} = \mathbf{47.124\ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Gas volume at STP.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{gas} &= 47.124\ kg \times 900\ L/kg = \mathbf{42{,}411.6\ L} = \mathbf{42.41\ m^{3}}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Heat of explosion per hole.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 47.124\ kg \times 4.2\ MJ/kg = \mathbf{197.92\ MJ\ per\ hole}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5: Units & final answer statement (1 mark):**

Final answers:
- Mass of explosive per hole = **47.124 kg**
- Gas volume at STP = **42,411.6 L**
- Heat of explosion per hole = **197.92 MJ**

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the classification of explosives by chemical type with a detailed flowchart. Differentiate between primary and secondary explosives with at least three points. Why is RDX classified as a nitramine?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *The detonation velocity of an explosive is measured as 4,500 m/s at a loading density of 1.0 g/cm³. Calculate (i) the detonation pressure, (ii) the time taken for the detonation front to travel through a 50 cm long column of this explosive, (iii) the kinetic energy per kg of explosive in the detonation wave.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Model Answer Outline (7 Marks):**

1. **Flowchart Discussion (3 Marks):** Describe the three-tier classification (Mechanistic → Sensitivity → Chemical family) with reference to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above. Mention that chemical family classification is independent of primary/secondary status.
2. **Primary vs. Secondary Explosives Comparison Table (2.5 Marks):**

| Property | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | Very high (heat/spark) | Moderate (shock required) |
| Detonator function | YES, initiates | NO, needs booster |
| Quantity used | mg–g scale | kg–tonne scale |
| Examples | Lead Azide, DDNP | RDX, TNT, PETN |

3. **Why RDX is a nitramine (1.5 Marks):** RDX (C₃H₆N₆O₆) contains the **N-NO₂ functional group bonded within a triazine (six-membered N-rich) ring**. Each of the three ring nitrogens carries an NO₂ group as a substituent, making it a **cyclic nitramine**, not a nitrate ester or aromatic nitro compound.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $VOD = D = 4{,}500\ m/s$, $\rho = 1{,}000\ kg/m^{3}$, $L = 0.5\ m$.

**Step 1 (2.5 Marks):** Detonation pressure.

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{\rho \cdot D^{2}}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}000 \times (4{,}500)^{2}}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}000 \times 2.025 \times 10^{7}}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{2.025 \times 10^{10}}{4} \\
&= \mathbf{5.0625 \times 10^{9}\ Pa} \approx \mathbf{5.06\ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Time for detonation front to traverse 50 cm.

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{0.5\ m}{4{,}500\ m/s} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4}\ s = \mathbf{111.11\ \mu s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2.5 Marks):** Kinetic energy per kg of detonation products.

The detonation wave mass-flux carries kinetic energy equal to:

$$\begin{aligned}
KE &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot D^{2} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1{,}000 \cdot (4{,}500)^{2} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2.025 \times 10^{10} \\
&= \mathbf{1.0125 \times 10^{10}\ J/m^{3}}
\end{aligned}$$

Per kg, dividing by density: $KE/m = \tfrac{1}{2} D^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(4{,}500)^{2} = \mathbf{1.0125 \times 10^{7}\ J/kg = 10.125\ MJ/kg}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Chemical Type" with "Class of Explosive":** Students frequently list "primary/secondary" instead of "nitramine/aromatic nitro". Always classify by **molecular functional group** for chemical-type questions.
> 2. **Oxygen Balance Sign Errors:** A negative OB means *oxygen deficit* (produces CO), NOT a negative energy. Always re-check signs in the OB formula.
> 3. **Mixing units in VOD formula:** Density MUST be in **kg/m³** (not g/cm³) and VOD in **m/s** to get pressure in Pascals. A common error yields pressure in bars, leading to a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **PETN vs RDX confusion:** PETN is a *nitrate ester* (NOT a nitramine), even though both are high-energy secondary explosives.
> 5. **ANFO oxygen balance:** Students often quote ANFO OB as +20% (which is the OB of pure AN). For 94/6 ANFO, the actual OB is approximately **−0.4%** — a common 1-mark trap.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Chemical-type classification** is the third axis (after mechanism and sensitivity) on which explosives are categorized.
- [ ] The **four most-tested chemical families** are: **Nitrate Esters, Nitramines, Aromatic Nitro, and Ammonium Nitrate mixtures.**
- [ ] **Oxygen Balance (OB) formula** must be memorized with signs: negative ⇒ CO produced, positive ⇒ NOₓ produced, zero ⇒ ideal.
- [ ] **Detonation Pressure formula:** $P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot D^{2}}{4}$ — derive twice; appears in nearly every KTU Part B question.
- [ ] **PETN** = pentaerythritol tetranitrate — a **nitrate ester**, NOT a nitramine.
- [ ] **RDX** = cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine — the prototype **nitramine**.
- [ ] **TNT** OB = **−74%**, strongly oxygen-deficient.
- [ ] **NG** OB = **+3.5%**, near oxygen-balanced.
- [ ] **ANFO (94/6)** OB ≈ **−0.4%**, the practical gold standard for near-zero OB.
- [ ] **Lead Azide** — primary explosive, used in **detonators**, never as a bulk charge.
- [ ] **Figure of Insensitiveness (FI)** standard samples: RDX = 80 cm; PETN ≈ 30 cm; Lead Azide ≈ 5 cm.
- [ ] **Emulsion explosives** are water-in-oil matrix with AN/NN solution droplets — best water resistance for wet boreholes.
- [ ] **Mnemonic NACAPO**: Nitrate → Nitramine → Aromatic → Chlorate → Ammonium → Peroxide → Oxidizer-fuel.
- [ ] Always state **units** explicitly in KTU numerical answers; marks are deducted if units are missing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Forms of Explosives, Propellants, and Pyrotechnics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Use Forms of Explosives, Propellants, and Pyrotechnics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explosives** are reactive substances that contain a large amount of potential energy stored in chemical bonds, capable of undergoing extremely rapid, self-propagating decomposition (detonation or deflagration) producing a sudden release of heat, gas, and pressure. **Propellants** are energetic materials that burn in a controlled, subsonic manner (deflagration) to generate hot gases for thrust or impulse without requiring external oxygen. **Pyrotechnics** are compositions designed to produce a specific non-detonative effect such as heat, light, sound, smoke, colour, or motion through a self-sustaining redox reaction.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the **"Use Forms"** classification refers to the **physical state, chemical composition, and end-application class** into which these energetic materials are engineered and deployed in the field. Each class is tailored to a specific engineering purpose: shattering rock, propelling a rocket, illuminating a battlefield, or entertaining a crowd.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of energetic materials like a family of "controlled fire":

- **Explosives** are like a **hammer** — they deliver an instantaneous, shattering impact. They release energy in microseconds at supersonic speeds.
- **Propellants** are like a **pushing engine** — they burn steadily (milliseconds to minutes), pushing gases out of a nozzle to provide thrust, like a controlled steam engine in a bottle.
- **Pyrotechnics** are like a **stage performer** — they are designed not to destroy, but to *produce an effect*: brilliant light, coloured flame, smoke, whistle, or sparkle.

The chemistry is similar (all are based on **oxidation–reduction of fuel-oxidiser pairs**), but the **rate of reaction** and the **end product** are engineered differently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Classification Metric — Reaction Velocity (VOD):**
> - **High Explosives (Detonating):** VOD = **1500 m/s** to **9500 m/s** (supersonic, shattering).
> - **Low Explosives / Propellants (Deflagrating):** VOD = **1 m/s** to **1000 m/s** (subsonic, heaving/pushing).
> - **Pyrotechnics:** VOD = **1 m/s** to **~400 m/s** (very slow, effect-oriented).

The unifying law: All three obey the **Go/No-Go Thermodynamic Principle** — the material must release net energy $\Delta E_{out} > \Delta E_{in}$ when triggered, and the **rate of release** determines the form of use.

### 1.3 The Three Functional Forms at a Glance

| Form Class | Reaction Mode | VOD (m/s) | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| High Explosives (HE) | Detonation | **1500 – 9500** | Shattering, brisant work |
| Propellants | Deflagration | **1 – 1000** | Thrust generation |
| Pyrotechnics | Combustion | **1 – 400** | Effect generation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Use Forms of Explosives (High Explosives)

The engineering classification of explosives is based on **sensitivity**, **physical form**, and **end use**. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to identify the correct explosive for a given engineering application.

#### 2.1.1 Primary High Explosives (Initiators / Lead Compounds)
- **Function:** Initiate detonation in a secondary explosive charge. Extremely sensitive.
- **Examples:** Lead Azide $\text{Pb(N}_3)_2$, Mercury Fulminate $\text{Hg(ONC)}_2$, Lead Styphnate, Diazodinitrophenol (DDNP).
- **Use Form:** Pressed pellets in **detonator cups**, **blasting caps**, and **percussion primers**.
- **Key Property:** They reliably transition from **deflagration to detonation (DDT)** upon a small stimulus (heat, spark, friction, or shock).

#### 2.1.2 Secondary High Explosives (Main Charge / Base Explosives)
- **Function:** Provide the main brisant, shattering work. Less sensitive, hence safer to handle.
- **Examples:**
  * **TNT (Trinitrotoluene)** — standard military explosive, melting point **80.8 °C**, can be cast-loaded into shells.
  * **RDX (Cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine)** — VOD $\approx$ **8750 m/s**, used in Composition B, C-4.
  * **HMX (Cyclotetramethylenetetranitramine)** — VOD $\approx$ **9100 m/s**, most powerful military HE.
  * **PETN (Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate)** — VOD $\approx$ **8400 m/s**, used in detonating cord (Primacord).
  * **TATB** — extremely insensitive, used in nuclear weapons.

#### 2.1.3 Blasting / Commercial Explosives (Bulk Industrial Use)
- **ANFO (Ammonium Nitrate + Fuel Oil):** 94% AN + 6% FO, density $\approx$ **0.85 g/cm³**, VOD $\approx$ **4000 m/s** under confinement. Used in **open-pit mining, quarrying, road construction**.
- **Water Gels / Slurries:** AN + water + sensitiser (e.g., TNT, aluminium powder), used in **wet boreholes**.
- **Emulsions:** Water-in-oil emulsion of AN solution, VOD up to **5500 m/s**, excellent water resistance.
- **Bulk Emulsion Trucks** — pumped directly into boreholes for large-scale blasting.

#### 2.1.4 Plastic / Sheet / Putty Explosives
- **Plastic Explosive C-4:** 91% RDX + 9% plasticiser, mouldable like putty, odourless, used by **military combat engineers** for shaped demolition charges.
- **Detasheet (PETN-based):** Flexible, used in **metal cutting, breaching** and commercial quarrying.

#### 2.1.5 Liquid and Slurry Explosives
- **Nitroglycerin (NG):** Historical blasting liquid, $C_3H_5N_3O_9$, highly sensitive.
- **Nitromethane:** Race-fuel based sensitiser for **plaster shooting** and specialised industrial use.
- **Slurries (Tovex, Hydromex):** Pumpable, water-resistant, used in underwater blasting.

### 2.2 Use Forms of Propellants

Propellants are designed to burn at a **controlled, predictable rate**, not to detonate. Their use forms span from **firearms ammunition** to **space launch vehicles**.

#### 2.2.1 Solid Propellants
- **Single-Base:** Pure nitrocellulose (NC) — used in early smokeless gunpowders, low flame temperature.
- **Double-Base:** NC + NG (Nitroglycerin) — used in modern **ballistic propellants (spherical, tubular)**, increases energy density.
- **Triple-Base:** NC + NG + Nitroguanidine (NQ) — low flash signature, used in **tank ammunition** to avoid revealing position.
- **Composite Solid:** Polymer binder (HTPB) + oxidiser (Ammonium Perchlorate, AP) + metal fuel (Al) — used in **space launch vehicles (PSLV, GSLV boosters)** and **missile motors (Agni, Prithvi)**.

#### 2.2.2 Liquid Propellants
- **Cryogenic Bipropellant (LOX + LH₂):** Liquid Oxygen + Liquid Hydrogen. Highest specific impulse ($I_{sp}$). Used in **cryogenic upper stages of GSLV Mk-III**.
- **Storable Bipropellant (MMH + N₂O₄):** Monomethylhydrazine + Nitrogen Tetroxide. Hypergolic (ignite on contact), used in **satellite orbit-correction thrusters, Apollo LEM descent engine**.

#### 2.2.3 Hybrid Propellants
- **Solid Fuel + Liquid/Gaseous Oxidiser:** E.g., **HTPB fuel + N₂O oxidiser**. Throttleable, safer, used in **SpaceShipOne** and sounding rockets.

#### 2.2.4 Gel Propellants
- Liquid propellant gelled with a polymer — combines the **safety of solids** with the **performance/restartability of liquids**. Used in **advanced tactical missiles**.

### 2.3 Use Forms of Pyrotechnics

Pyrotechnics are categorised by the **sensory effect** they generate, not by energy content.

| Pyrotechnic Form | Active Ingredient | Effect | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flares (Illumination)** | Mg, Al, NaNO₃, Ba(NO₃)₂ | Intense white/colour light | Battlefield illumination, distress signals |
| **Tracers** | Mg/PTFE (Magnesium + Teflon/Viton) | Bright IR-visible light | Bullet tracking, rocket guidance |
| **Smoke generators** | Hexachloroethane + Zn, Red P | Coloured/obscurant smoke | Naval signalling, crowd control |
| **Incendiaries** | Thermite (Al + Fe₂O₃) | Sustained high heat (>2500 °C) | Military firebombs, welding |
| **Delays / Fuses** | BaCrO₄ + B (slow burning) | Time-controlled burn | Bomb fuzing, ejection seats |
| **Airbag Inflators** | NaN₃ → N₂ gas (70% in 30 ms) | Rapid gas generation | Automotive safety |
| **Display Fireworks** | KNO₃ + S + C, metal salts for colour | Visual + acoustic | Entertainment, celebration |
| **Whistling Compositions** | Potassium picrate / benzoate | Audible tone via gas oscillation | Aerial shells, alarm devices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Colour Generation Rule (KTU Favourite):**
> - **Red** → Strontium salts (SrCO₃)
> - **Green** → Barium salts (BaCl₂)
> - **Blue** → Copper salts (CuCl)
> - **Yellow** → Sodium salts (Na oxalate)
> - **White** → Magnesium / Aluminium metal
> - **Purple** → Strontium + Copper mixture

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_{loading} = \dfrac{m_{explosive}}{V_{borehole}}$ | Bulk density of charge in blast design |
| 2 | $VOD = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n^2 - 1) \cdot P}{\rho_0}}$ | Velocity of detonation from Chapman–Jouguet theory (ideal gas) |
| 3 | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 \cdot VOD^2}{(n+1)}$ | Chapman–Jouguet detonation pressure |
| 4 | $I_{sp} = \dfrac{V_e}{g_0}$ | Specific impulse of propellant (s) — Vₑ = exhaust velocity, $g_0$ = 9.806 m/s² |
| 5 | $\Delta V = g_0 \cdot I_{sp} \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{m_0}{m_f} \right)$ | Tsiolkovsky rocket equation — burn-up velocity |
| 6 | $Q_{explosion} = m \cdot \Delta H_{detonation}$ | Total heat released by explosive mass m |
| 7 | $PV = nRT$ (after gas release) | Adiabatic gas expansion driving propellant work |
| 8 | $t_{burn} = \dfrac{L_r}{r_{burn}}$ | Web-burn time of solid propellant grain |
| 9 | $A_{Nozzle} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{P_c} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{R T_c}{M \gamma}}$ | Throat area of propellant nozzle |
| 10 | $L_{detonation} \approx 3.5 \cdot d_{charge}$ | Detonation stabilisation length for cylindrical charge |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation for a Solid Propellant System

**Problem Setup:** A solid propellant rocket motor has the following parameters:
- Initial mass $m_0 = 2500$ kg
- Final (after burnout) mass $m_f = 500$ kg
- Specific impulse $I_{sp} = 260$ s (typical for HTPB-AP composite)
- Standard gravity $g_0 = 9.806$ m/s²

**Find:** The theoretical $\Delta V$ (velocity increment) the rocket gains in vacuum.

**Step 1 — Recall the Fundamental Equation:**

The Tsiolkovsky equation is derived from conservation of momentum for a variable-mass system:

$$m \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt} = -V_e \cdot \dfrac{dm}{dt}$$

where $V_e$ is the effective exhaust velocity and $v$ is rocket velocity.

**Step 2 — Rearrange the Variables:**

$$dv = -V_e \cdot \dfrac{dm}{m}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides from initial to final state:**

$$\int_{v_0}^{v_f} dv = -V_e \int_{m_0}^{m_f} \dfrac{dm}{m}$$

$$v_f - v_0 = V_e \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{m_0}{m_f} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Express the Velocity Increment:**

$$\Delta V = v_f - v_0 = V_e \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{m_0}{m_f} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Relate Exhaust Velocity to Specific Impulse:**

$$V_e = I_{sp} \cdot g_0$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta V = I_{sp} \cdot g_0 \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{m_0}{m_f} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Numerical Values:**

$$\Delta V = 260 \times 9.806 \times \ln \left( \dfrac{2500}{500} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Mass Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{m_0}{m_f} = \dfrac{2500}{500} = 5$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Natural Logarithm:**

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$

**Step 9 — Final Calculation:**

$$\Delta V = 260 \times 9.806 \times 1.6094$$

$$\Delta V = 2549.56 \times 1.6094$$

$$\Delta V \approx 4102.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 10 — Sanity Check & Engineering Interpretation:**

$$\Delta V \approx 4.1 \text{ km/s}$$

This is a typical value for a **single-stage sounding rocket** or a **suborbital missile**. For orbital insertion (~9.4 km/s), multi-staging or higher $I_{sp}$ (cryogenic propellant ~450 s) is required — directly justifying the use of cryogenic upper stages in GSLV Mk-III.

### 3.2 Derivation: Chapman–Jouguet Detonation Pressure for a TNT Charge

**Given:**
- Density of cast TNT $\rho_0 = 1650$ kg/m³
- Measured VOD = **6940 m/s**
- Polytropic index $n = 3$ (typical for condensed explosives)

**Step 1 — CJ Pressure Formula (high-yield KTU form):**

$$P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 \cdot VOD^2}{(n + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values:**

$$P_{CJ} = \dfrac{1650 \times (6940)^2}{(3 + 1)}$$

**Step 3 — Square the VOD:**

$$(6940)^2 = 48,163,600 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by Density:**

$$1650 \times 48,163,600 = 7.947 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa (numerator)}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $(n+1) = 4$:**

$$P_{CJ} = \dfrac{7.947 \times 10^{10}}{4}$$

$$P_{CJ} = 1.987 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to GPa (KTU standard unit for detonation pressure):**

$$P_{CJ} \approx 19.9 \text{ GPa} \approx 200 \text{ kbar}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is the **brisant pressure** responsible for crushing, shattering, and metal-cutting action of TNT — and is why TNT remains the **standard reference** for explosive relative effectiveness (RE-factor = 1.00).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Form

| Engineering Need | Recommended Form | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Blasting a granite quarry | **ANFO / Bulk Emulsion** | Low cost, high bulk energy, easily pumped into deep boreholes |
| Military bomb warhead | **Composition B (RDX/TNT)** | High VOD, high brisance, castable |
| Sabotage / field demolition | **C-4 Plastic Explosive** | Mouldable, water-resistant, insensitive to fire |
| Tank gun ammunition | **Triple-Base Propellant** | Low muzzle flash, stable burn |
| Satellite kick motor | **HTPB-AP Composite Solid** | Long storage, reliable, no leaks |
| Lunar descent engine | **MMH + N₂O₄ (Storable Hypergolic)** | Ignite on contact, restartable |
| Distress signal at sea | **Magnesium-based Flare** | Intense white light, ~7000 candela |
| Racing-car airbag | **Sodium Azide Inflator** | 70% inflation in 30 ms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Energetic Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENERGETIC MATERIALS]:::root --> B[EXPLOSIVES]
    A --> C[PROPELLANTS]
    A --> D[PYROTECHNICS]

    B --> B1[Primary HE]
    B --> B2[Secondary HE]
    B --> B3[Blasting Industrial]
    B --> B4[Plastic Sheet Liquid]

    B1 --> B1a[Lead Azide]
    B1 --> B1b[Mercury Fulminate]

    B2 --> B2a[TNT]
    B2 --> B2b[RDX]
    B2 --> B3c[HMX]
    B2 --> B3d[PETN]
    B2 --> B3e[TATB]

    B3 --> B3a[ANFO]
    B3 --> B3b[Slurry]
    B3 --> B3c[Emulsion]

    C --> C1[Solid Propellant]
    C --> C2[Liquid Propellant]
    C --> C3[Hybrid Propellant]
    C --> C4[Gel Propellant]

    C1 --> C1a[Single Base NC]
    C1 --> C1b[Double Base NC NG]
    C1 --> C1c[Triple Base NC NG NQ]
    C1 --> C1d[Composite HTPB AP Al]

    C2 --> C2a[Cryogenic LOX LH2]
    C2 --> C2b[Storable MMH N2O4]

    D --> D1[Flares and Tracers]
    D --> D2[Smoke Generators]
    D --> D3[Incendiaries]
    D --> D4[Delays and Fuses]
    D --> D5[Airbag Inflators]
    D --> D6[Display Fireworks]

    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#FFD700,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef boom fill:#7a1f1f,stroke:#ff5555,color:#ffffff
    classDef thrust fill:#1f4a3f,stroke:#55ffaa,color:#ffffff
    classDef pyro fill:#4a3f1f,stroke:#ffaa55,color:#ffffff

    class A root
    class B,B1,B2,B3,B4,B1a,B1b,B2a,B2b,B3c,B3d,B3e,B3a,B3b,B3c boom
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C1a,C1b,C1c,C1d,C2a,C2b thrust
    class D,D1,D2,D3,D4,D5,D6 pyro
```

### 4.2 Propellant Form — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IGN[Ignition Signal]:::in --> IGNT[Igniter Grain]
    IGNT --> GRA[Solid Propellant Grain HTPB plus AP plus Al]:::core
    GRA --> CHB[Combustion Chamber]:::core
    CHB --> NZL[Convergent Divergent Nozzle]:::core
    NZL --> THR[Thrust Vector]
    CHB --> REG[Pressure Regulator]:::ctrl
    REG --> GRA
    REG --> TVC[Thrust Vector Control Vanes]:::ctrl
    TVC --> THR

    classDef core fill:#7a1f1f,stroke:#ffaa00,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#55ccff,color:#ffffff
    classDef in fill:#333333,stroke:#aaaaaa,color:#ffffff
    class IGN,IGNT in
    class GRA,CHB,NZL,THR core
    class REG,TVC ctrl
```

### 4.3 Pyrotechnic Effect Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PYT[Pyrotechnic Composition]:::pyroot --> OXI[Oxidiser KNO3 BaNO3]:::comp
    PYT --> FUE[Fuel S C Mg Al]:::comp
    PYT --> BIN[Binder Resin Dextrin]:::comp
    PYT --> ADD[Specialty Additives]:::comp

    OXI --> EF1[Heat Light Effect]:::eff
    FUE --> EF2[Smoke Gas Effect]:::eff

    ADD --> EF4[Colour Sound Effect]:::eff

    EF1 --> USR1[Flares Tracers Incendiaries]
    EF2 --> USR2[Smoke Screens Distress Signals]

    classDef pyroot fill:#4a3f1f,stroke:#FFD700,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**"Differentiate between High Explosives, Propellants, and Pyrotechnics on the basis of VOD and end use."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Structure: Definition + VOD + Use):**

| Parameter | High Explosives | Propellants | Pyrotechnics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reaction Mode | Detonation (supersonic shock) | Deflagration (subsonic burn) | Combustion (very slow burn) |
| VOD (m/s) | **1500 – 9500** | **1 – 1000** | **1 – 400** |
| Primary Use | Shattering, blasting, military warhead | Thrust generation, gun propulsion | Light, smoke, heat, colour, sound effects |
| Typical Example | RDX, TNT, PETN | HTPB-AP composite, NC-NG ballistite | Mg flare, red P smoke, thermite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for each correct parameter (Reaction + VOD range + Example). Total = 3 marks. Students often lose a mark by mixing the VOD boundaries.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**"List four use forms of solid propellants and state one application of each."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Single-Base (NC):** Used in early smokeless gunpowder — small arms ammunition.
2. **Double-Base (NC + NG):** Used in modern ballistite — high-velocity tank and artillery ammunition.
3. **Triple-Base (NC + NG + NQ):** Used in low-flash tank rounds — minimises muzzle signature.
4. **Composite (HTPB + AP + Al):** Used in **Agni, Prithvi, PSLV boosters** — large space launch vehicles.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**
**(a)** Classify explosives based on use forms. Describe the composition, properties, and applications of **ANFO and Emulsion explosives** in mining engineering. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With neat labelled diagram, explain the working of a **solid propellant rocket motor** using a composite (HTPB-AP) grain. Derive the **Tsiolkovsky rocket equation** and compute the $\Delta V$ for a motor with $m_0 = 4000$ kg, $m_f = 800$ kg, $I_{sp} = 250$ s. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & Description of ANFO + Emulsion:**

**Classification of Explosives by Use Form (3 Marks):**
- Primary HE (initiators) — Lead Azide, Mercury Fulminate
- Secondary HE (main charge) — TNT, RDX, HMX, PETN
- Blasting/Commercial — ANFO, Slurries, Emulsions
- Plastic/Sheet/Liquid — C-4, Detasheet, NG

**ANFO (2 Marks):**
- Composition: 94.3% Ammonium Nitrate (prilled porous) + 5.7% Fuel Oil (diesel).
- Density: 0.85 g/cm³
- VOD: ~4000 m/s (when confined in 100 mm borehole)
- Application: Open-pit mining, quarrying, road construction; cannot be used in wet holes as AN is hygroscopic.

**Emulsion Explosives (2 Marks):**
- Composition: Supersaturated AN solution (oxidiser) dispersed as droplets in a hydrocarbon oil matrix (fuel), with a sensitiser (glass micro-balloons or gas bubbles).
- Density: 1.10–1.30 g/cm³
- VOD: 4500–5500 m/s
- Application: Wet boreholes, large-diameter blasting, underwater blasting, bulk truck loading.

**(b) Solid Propellant Motor + Tsiolkovsky Derivation + Calculation:**

**Working of HTPB-AP Solid Motor (Diagram) (2 Marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2. Key components: Igniter → Grain (HTPB + AP + Al) → Combustion Chamber → Nozzle → Thrust.

**Derivation of Tsiolkovsky Equation (3 Marks):**
Refer to Section 3.1 Steps 1–5.

**Numerical Computation (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta V = I_{sp} \cdot g_0 \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{m_0}{m_f} \right)$$

$$\Delta V = 250 \times 9.806 \times \ln \left( \dfrac{4000}{800} \right)$$

$$\Delta V = 250 \times 9.806 \times \ln(5)$$

$$\Delta V = 250 \times 9.806 \times 1.6094$$

$$\Delta V \approx 3946.5 \text{ m/s} \approx 3.95 \text{ km/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**(a)** Explain the use forms of **pyrotechnics** with a tabulated classification. Discuss the role of **metal salts in producing colours** in display fireworks and the chemical basis of the **airbag inflator (NaN₃)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **solid, liquid, and hybrid propellants** in a matrix form. Explain why **triple-base propellants** are preferred for tank ammunition. What is the role of **aluminium fuel** in composite solid propellants? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pyrotechnic Use Forms (4 Marks):**

| Form | Function | Active Ingredients |
|---|---|---|
| Flares (illumination) | Battlefield/signal light | Mg + NaNO₃ + binder |
| Tracers | Bullet tracking | Mg + PTFE (Viton) |
| Smoke generators | Visual obscurant | Red P, Zn + hexachloroethane |
| Incendiaries | Sustained burn > 2500 °C | Thermite (Al + Fe₂O₃) |
| Delays/Fuses | Time control | BaCrO₄ + B (slow pyrotechnic delay) |
| Airbag inflators | Rapid N₂ generation | NaN₃ → N₂ + Na |
| Fireworks | Entertainment display | KNO₃ + S + C + colour salts |

**Colour Generation by Metal Salts (1.5 Marks):** Refer to colour rule in Section 2.3 — Strontium = Red, Barium = Green, Copper = Blue, Sodium = Yellow, Mg/Al = White. Mechanism: Metal atoms get thermally excited in flame; electrons relax with emission of characteristic wavelength.

**Airbag Inflator — NaN₃ Chemistry (1.5 Marks):**
$$2 \, \text{NaN}_3 \rightarrow 2 \, \text{Na} + 3 \, \text{N}_2 \uparrow$$
The reaction is fast, releases nitrogen gas, ~67% inflates the bag within 30 ms. The metallic Na byproduct is converted to harmless silicates by reaction with Fe₂O₃ and SiO₂ scavengers:
$$6 \, \text{Na} + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3 \, \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow 3 \, \text{Na}_2\text{O} \cdot \text{SiO}_2 + 2 \, \text{Fe} \quad \text{(sodium silicate slag)}$$

**(b) Propellant Matrix (3 Marks):**

| Property | Solid | Liquid | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Impulse $I_{sp}$ (s) | 200–280 | 300–460 | 280–340 |
| Throttleability | No | Yes | Yes |
| Storability | Excellent | Cryogenic (poor) | Excellent |
| Restartability | No | Yes (storable) | Yes |
| Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Typical Use | PSLV boosters, missiles | GSLV cryogenic stage, satellites | SpaceShipOne |

**Triple-Base for Tank Ammunition (2 Marks):**
Triple-base contains **Nitroguanidine (NQ)** which produces **low flame temperature** and **low muzzle flash**, making the tank's position invisible to enemy IR/visual detection — a critical battlefield advantage. Additionally, NQ is **low-erosive** to gun barrels, extending barrel life.

**Role of Aluminium in Composite Propellants (2 Marks):**
- Aluminium powder (5–20%) raises **flame temperature** to ~3500 K and increases **density-specific impulse ($\rho \cdot I_{sp}$)**.
- Combustion: $2 \, \text{Al} + 1.5 \, \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, releasing ~1670 kJ/mol.
- However, condensed $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ smoke causes **secondary smoke trail** and **two-phase flow losses** — a current research area for **smokeless aluminised formulations**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **VOD Confusion Trap:** Students often quote VOD of propellants as 1000+ m/s. **Correct range for propellant deflagrations is 1–1000 m/s** (typical = 5–30 mm/s in solid grains). Values > 1000 m/s imply **detonation**, not deflagration.
> 2. **ANFO Composition Mistake:** Examiners expect **94:6 ratio** of AN:FO, not 50:50. Miswriting this ratio costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Tsiolkovsky Equation Form:** Always write in **logarithmic form** with $g_0$ explicitly — $\Delta V = g_0 I_{sp} \ln(m_0/m_f)$. Forgetting $g_0$ halves the numerical answer.
> 4. **Pyrotechnic Effect Mismatch:** Writing "sodium salts = green" instead of "yellow" is the most common colour error — students confuse flame-test mnemonics.
> 5. **Propellant vs Pyrotechnic Boundary:** Airbag inflator is a **pyrotechnic gas generator** (uses NaN₃), **not** a propellant. Students often misclassify this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Explosives** → Detonate (VOD > 1500 m/s), **Propellants** → Deflagrate (VOD 1–1000 m/s), **Pyrotechnics** → Combust (VOD 1–400 m/s). The **rate of energy release** defines the form.
- **Primary HE (Lead Azide, Mercury Fulminate)** are sensitive **initiators** — they bridge the gap between stimulus and the main charge. **Secondary HE (TNT, RDX, HMX, PETN)** do the **brisant work**.
- **ANFO** = 94% AN + 6% Fuel Oil → VOD ~4000 m/s, used in **dry open-pit mining**. Cannot be used in wet holes.
- **Emulsion explosives** → VOD 4500–5500 m/s, **water resistant**, ideal for wet boreholes and bulk loading.
- **C-4 plastic explosive** = 91% RDX + 9% plasticiser → mouldable, water-resistant, used by combat engineers.
- **Single-base** propellant = pure NC. **Double-base** = NC + NG. **Triple-base** = NC + NG + NQ (low flash, tank ammunition).
- **Composite solid** = HTPB binder + AP oxidiser + Al fuel → used in **PSLV, GSLV, Agni, Prithvi**.
- **LOX + LH₂** = highest $I_{sp}$ (~450 s) but cryogenic. **MMH + N₂O₄** = storable, hypergolic, restartable (Apollo LEM).
- **Tsiolkovsky equation:** $\Delta V = g_0 \cdot I_{sp} \cdot \ln(m_0/m_f)$ — the foundation of all rocket propulsion.
- **Pyrotechnic colours:** Sr = Red, Ba = Green, Cu = Blue, Na = Yellow, Mg/Al = White.
- **Airbag inflator:** $2 \text{NaN}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{Na} + 3\text{N}_2$; ~67% inflation in 30 ms.
- **Thermite (Al + Fe₂O₃):** Sustained burn > 2500 °C, used in incendiaries and rail welding.
- **Application rule:** Choose the form based on the **engineering need** (shatter, push, illuminate, signal, inflate, entertain).
- **CJ Pressure** for HE: $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 VOD^2 / (n+1)$ — typical values 15–40 GPa for military HE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Burning Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Burning Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burning Model:** A *Burning Model* is a simplified geometric and thermodynamic framework that mathematically describes the temporal evolution of the *reactive surface area* of a solid or granular explosive (or propellant) as it undergoes **deflagration** (subsonic combustion). It combines the linear burning rate law (typically **Vieille's Law**, $r = aP^{n}$) with a *geometry-dependent surface regression* equation (most commonly the **de Considère Equation**) to predict the chamber pressure–time history, $P(t)$, in a confined or vented system.

In the strict KTU parlance for **PESFT753**, a burning model links three physical layers:
1. **Microscale (Kinetics):** Thermal decomposition and flame front propagation.
2. **Mesoscale (Geometry):** The way the burning surface recedes with time (slab, cylinder, sphere, granular bed).
3. **Macroscale (Ballistics):** The resulting mass generation rate, $\dot{m}(t)$, that drives pressure in the surrounding gas volume.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Candle vs. The Firecracker

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine two burning objects on a kitchen table:**

**Object A — A Wax Candle (Slab / End-Burner):** The flame stays the same size as the wax melts down. The wick stays put. The burning surface area is *constant* with time. This is the **n = 0 (zero-index) burning model** — used for rocket propellant grains that burn on one face only.

**Object B — A Spherical Firecracker Burning from the Outside In:** As the outer crust burns away, the diameter shrinks, so the exposed hot surface becomes *smaller and smaller* with time. Conversely, if you light the firecracker from the *inside* (think of a hollow sphere with a central ignition), the burning surface actually *grows* as the flame eats outward. This is the **de Considère n = 2 (sphere) model** — the foundation of interior-ballistics calculations.

> **Key Takeaway:** A "burning model" is simply a mathematical statement of *how fast the active surface shrinks or grows* as combustion proceeds, coupled to *how fast the surface itself recedes* (the linear burn rate).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Linear Burn Rate ($r$):** Expressed in **mm/s**. For propellants, $r$ ranges from **1–25 mm/s** at chamber pressures of **1–10 MPa**.
- **Pressure Exponent ($n$ or $b$):** Dimensionless. **Low exponent ($n < 0.5$)** indicates *plateau-burning* propellant. **High exponent ($n > 0.8$)** indicates *progressive-burning* propellant (dangerous in confined spaces).
- **Burn Time ($t_b$):** Total time for complete consumption of the charge, $t_b = e_0 / r$ for a slab.
- **Web Thickness ($e_0$):** The initial half-thickness of the unburned charge — a critical design parameter in propellant grain geometry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Burning Model

A complete burning model is constructed from three orthogonal components:

### Pillar 1 — Linear Burn Rate Law (Vieille's Law, 1893)
This is an empirical, thermodynamically validated relationship between the local pressure and the rate at which the combustion wave recedes into the unreacted solid:

$$r = a \, P^{n}$$

- $a$ = burn rate coefficient (units depend on $n$),
- $n$ = pressure exponent (dimensionless),
- $P$ = local chamber pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** The exponent **$n$ classifies propellants**:
> - $n = 0$: Neutral burning (candle-like). Safe in mines.
> - $0 < n < 1$: Sub-linear. Civilian mining explosives (e.g., ANFO).
> - $n \geq 1$: *Super-rate* or *catastrophic* burning — leads to **deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT)**. Always rejected for storage and transport.

### Pillar 2 — Geometric Surface Regression (de Considère Equation)
As the charge burns, the geometry evolves. For a body of characteristic dimension $e_0$ (half-web), the instantaneous surface area $A(t)$ evolves as:

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_b}\right)^{\alpha}$$

The burning index $\alpha$ depends on the **shape and burn direction**:

| Geometry | Burn Direction | Burning Index $\alpha$ | Surface Behavior |
|:---------|:---------------|:---------------------:|:-----------------|
| Slab (rectangular plate) | Both sides | 0 | Constant ($A = A_0$) |
| Cylinder (rod) | Radial (outside-in) | 1 | Linearly decreasing |
| Sphere (ball) | Radial (outside-in) | 2 | Quadratically decreasing |
| Slab (long grain) | End-burning only | 0 (constant) | $A = A_0$ throughout |
| Cylinder (tube) | Inside-out | $-1$ | Linearly *increasing* |
| Sphere (hollow) | Inside-out | $-2$ | Quadratically *increasing* |

### Pillar 3 — Mass Generation & Chamber Pressure Coupling
The instantaneous mass burning rate is:

$$\dot{m}(t) = \rho_s \, r(t) \, A(t) = \rho_s \, a P^{n}(t) \, A(t)$$

This is substituted into the **isentropic energy equation** for a closed vessel (or vented vessel with appropriate loss terms) to solve for $P(t)$.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table for Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Typical Units | Validity / Notes |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:----------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| 1 | **Vieille's Burn Rate Law** | $r = aP^{n}$ | $a, n$ | $r$ in mm/s, $P$ in MPa | $n$ is dimensionless |
| 2 | **Burn Time (Slab)** | $t_b = e_0 / r$ | $e_0$ half-web, $r$ burn rate | mm, mm/s, s | Total consumption time |
| 3 | **Burn Time (Cylinder/Sphere)** | $t_b = r_0 / r$ | $r_0$ outer radius | mm, mm/s, s | Radial burn |
| 4 | **de Considère (General)** | $A(t)/A_0 = (1 - t/t_b)^{\alpha}$ | $\alpha = 0, 1, 2$ | dimensionless | $\alpha$ = burning index |
| 5 | **Mass Burning Rate** | $\dot{m} = \rho_s \, r \, A$ | $\rho_s$ solid density | kg/m³, mm/s, m² → kg/s | Couples kinetics to geometry |
| 6 | **Chamber Pressure Rise (Closed)** | $\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{R_s \, T_f}{V} \dot{m}$ | $R_s$ gas constant, $T_f$ flame T | Pa/s | From ideal-gas EOS |
| 7 | **St. Robert's Law (alternate form)** | $r = \beta P^{\nu}$ | $\beta, \nu$ | — | Older notation for Vieille's |
| 8 | **Q-R Parameter** | $Q = A_b / V$, $R = K A_v / V$ | $A_b$ burning area, $A_v$ vent area | m⁻¹ | Vented explosion model |
| 9 | **Granular Bed Burn Rate (Birks)** | $r_g = k P^{m} D_p^{n}$ | $D_p$ particle diameter | mm/s | For porous explosives |
| 10 | **Pyrolysis Number** | $Py = r^2 / \kappa$ | $\kappa$ thermal diffusivity | s | Indicates DDT tendency |

> **Important constraint for all KTU calculations:** All pressures must be in a *consistent unit system*. The examiner's preferred set in **SI** is **MPa for $P$**, **mm/s for $r$**, **mm for length**. In **CGS**, use **bar** and **cm/s**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rocket Motor Design:** Predicts $P(t)$ for grain stability. A *progressive* burn ($\alpha = -1$ tube) gives nearly constant thrust.
- **Vessel Explosion Protection (NFPA 68):** The **Q-R method** uses burning-model geometry to size rupture disks and vent panels for dust collectors and grain silos.
- **Ammonium Nitrate Safety:** High $n$ + granular geometry → *unconfined burning accelerates* → DDT → massive detonation. This is why **ANFO** storage regulations are strict.
- **Fireworks Manufacturing (KTU Kerala industrial context):** Pyrotechnic star burn time is sized using slab burn model: $t_b = e_0 / r$, then matched to desired visual effect duration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1: de Considère Equation for a Burning Sphere (Outside-In)

> **Problem Setup:** Consider a solid sphere of an explosive of initial radius $r_0$ and density $\rho_s$. The sphere is ignited uniformly over its entire outer surface at $t = 0$ and burns inward at a constant linear rate $r$ (m/s, assumed constant for now). Derive an expression for $A(t)/A_0$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometric State at Time $t$

After a time interval $t$, the unreacted sphere has a current radius $r(t)$. Since the surface is regressing *inward* at linear rate $r$:

$$r(t) = r_0 - r \cdot t$$

**Logic:** The flame front moves at speed $r$ into the charge. In time $t$, it has covered a distance $r \cdot t$ of the original radius. The remaining unburnt radius is therefore the original minus the burnt depth.

### Step 2 — Compute the Current Surface Area

Surface area of a sphere of radius $r(t)$ is:

$$A(t) = 4\pi \, r(t)^{2} = 4\pi \, (r_0 - r t)^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Initial Surface Area

At $t = 0$:

$$A_0 = 4\pi \, r_0^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Form the Dimensionless Ratio

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \frac{4\pi (r_0 - r t)^{2}}{4\pi r_0^{2}} = \left( \frac{r_0 - r t}{r_0} \right)^{2}$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the Burn Time

Define the **total burn time** as the time when $r(t) \to 0$:

$$t_b = \frac{r_0}{r}$$

Therefore $r = r_0 / t_b$, and substituting:

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \left( \frac{r_0 - \frac{r_0}{t_b} t}{r_0} \right)^{2} = \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_b} \right)^{2}$$

### Final Result for a Sphere (Outside-In):

$$\boxed{\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_b} \right)^{2}, \quad \alpha = 2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: de Considère Equation for a Cylinder (Radial, Outside-In)

### Step 1 — Geometry

Cylinder of initial outer radius $r_0$ and length $L$. Burn front moves inward radially at rate $r$.

$$r(t) = r_0 - r t$$

### Step 2 — Lateral Surface Area (ignoring end caps)

$$A(t) = 2\pi \, r(t) \, L = 2\pi \, L \, (r_0 - r t)$$

### Step 3 — Initial Area

$$A_0 = 2\pi \, L \, r_0$$

### Step 4 — Form the Ratio

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \frac{2\pi L (r_0 - r t)}{2\pi L r_0} = \frac{r_0 - r t}{r_0} = 1 - \frac{r t}{r_0}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $t_b = r_0 / r$

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = 1 - \frac{t}{t_b}$$

### Final Result for a Cylinder (Outside-In):

$$\boxed{\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = 1 - \frac{t}{t_b}, \quad \alpha = 1}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Generation Rate Coupled to Pressure (Closed Vessel)

### Step 1 — Mass Burned up to Time $t$

For a sphere burning outside-in, the volume of unburned material at time $t$ is:

$$V_s(t) = \frac{4}{3}\pi (r_0 - r t)^{3}$$

### Step 2 — Mass Burned

$$m_b(t) = \rho_s \left[ \frac{4}{3}\pi r_0^{3} - \frac{4}{3}\pi (r_0 - r t)^{3} \right] = \frac{4}{3}\pi \rho_s \left[ r_0^{3} - (r_0 - r t)^{3} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Mass Burn Rate by Differentiation

$$\dot{m}(t) = \frac{dm_b}{dt} = \frac{4}{3}\pi \rho_s \cdot 3(r_0 - r t)^{2} \cdot (-r) = -4\pi \rho_s r (r_0 - r t)^{2}$$

The negative sign indicates mass loss from the solid; we define the *positive generation rate* as:

$$\dot{m}(t) = 4\pi \rho_s \, r \, (r_0 - r t)^{2} = \rho_s \, r \, A(t)$$

This confirms the **general identity** $\dot{m} = \rho_s \, r \, A$ for any geometry.

### Step 4 — Pressure Evolution Equation

Applying the energy balance to the gas in a closed volume $V$ (assuming isothermal, ideal gas for simplicity — a KTU-acceptable approximation in Module 1):

$$P(t) V = m_b(t) R_s T_f$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} \cdot V = \dot{m}(t) \, R_s T_f$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{R_s T_f}{V} \, \rho_s \, r \, A(t) = \frac{R_s T_f}{V} \, \rho_s \, a P^{n}(t) \, A(t)$$

### Final Coupled ODE:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{\rho_s \, a \, R_s T_f}{V} \, P^{n}(t) \, A(t)}$$

This is the **master equation of interior ballistics**, integrable only after $A(t)$ is specified by the chosen *burning model* (slab / cylinder / sphere).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: de Considère for Inside-Out Burning (Hollow Sphere)

> **Why this matters:** A fireworks "shell" burst charge is approximately a hollow sphere. The ignition is at the center, and the flame front moves *outward*.

### Step 1 — Geometry

Inner ignition at $r = 0$. Burn front at radius $r(t) = r \cdot t$. The unburned material lies *outside* this front, between $r(t)$ and $r_0$.

### Step 2 — Burning Surface Area

The instantaneous surface is the inner spherical surface at $r(t)$:

$$A(t) = 4\pi (r t)^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Initial Burning Surface (a point, but treated as $\epsilon$ for ratio)

For the ratio form, the *initial* surface is the cavity wall (or the initial kernel area, $A_0$):

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \frac{4\pi (r t)^{2}}{A_0} \propto t^{2}$$

Expressed in burn-time-normalized form, letting $t_b = r_0 / r$:

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \left( \frac{t}{t_b} \right)^{2} \cdot (\text{geometric factor})$$

A more rigorous closed-form result using $r(t) = r_0 - r t_b + r t$ for an annular burn gives:

$$\boxed{\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \left( \frac{t}{t_b} \right)^{2} \cdot \text{const}, \quad \alpha = -2 \text{ (negative index, growth)}}$$

This is the **progressive-burning** regime — the surface area *increases* with time, leading to an *accelerating* mass generation rate and a *runaway* pressure rise.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Burn Geometry | Direction | $A(t)/A_0$ Form | Index $\alpha$ | Pressure Profile $P(t)$ |
|:--------------|:---------:|:-----------------|:--------------:|:------------------------|
| Slab (plate) | Both faces | $1$ | $0$ | $P \propto t^{1}$ (linear) |
| Cylinder (rod) | Radial, outside-in | $1 - t/t_b$ | $1$ | $P \propto t^{n+1}$ accelerating |
| Sphere (ball) | Radial, outside-in | $(1 - t/t_b)^{2}$ | $2$ | $P$ rises then falls (degressive) |
| Cylinder (tube) | Radial, inside-out | $1/(1 - t/t_b)$ | $-1$ | $P$ grows hyperbolically |
| Hollow sphere | Inside-out | $\propto t^{2}$ | $-2$ | $P$ grows super-linearly (DDT-prone) |
| Granular bed | Distributed | $A = A_0$ (approx.) | $0$ | $P \propto t$ (porous burn) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Complete Burning Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Block diagram** showing how the three pillars of the burning model interact in a closed-vessel pressure-prediction algorithm. This is the standard Mermaid block-flow used in KTU technical interviews and answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input: Charge Geometry e0 r0 shape] --> geomBlock
    inputB[Input: Burn Rate Law r equals aP to the n] --> rateBlock
    inputC[Input: Initial Conditions P0 T0 V] --> initBlock
    geomBlock[Geometry Module: de Consider e Equation A of t] --> multiplyBlock
    rateBlock[Kinetics Module: Linear Burn Rate Evaluation] --> multiplyBlock
    initBlock[Initial State Vector] --> integrateBlock
    multiplyBlock[Multiplier: m dot equals rho s times r times A of t] --> integrateBlock
    integrateBlock[Integrator: dP by dt equals Rg Tf by V times m dot] --> outputCheck{Convergence Check P convergence less than 1 percent}
    outputCheck -- yes --> finalP[Output: P of t and t burn]
    outputCheck -- no --> loopBack[Time Step Advance t equals t plus dt]
    loopBack --> integrateBlock
    rateBlock --> subCrit{Subsonic Check Mach number less than 0.7}
    subCrit -- yes --> deflagState[Deflagration Mode Valid]
    subCrit -- no --> ddtAlert[WARNING DDT Threshold Crossed Switch to Detonation Model]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Selecting the Burning Index $\alpha$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{Is the charge porous or granular?}
    startQ -- yes --> gran[A equals A0 approximately alpha equals 0]
    startQ -- no --> shapeQ{What is the macroscopic shape?}
    shapeQ -- slab --> dirQ1{End burn or both faces?}
    dirQ1 -- end --> endBurn[alpha equals 0]
    dirQ1 -- both --> bothBurn[alpha equals 0]
    shapeQ -- cylinder --> dirQ2{Inside out or outside in?}
    dirQ2 -- inside out --> tubeProg[alpha equals minus 1 progressive]
    dirQ2 -- outside in --> tubeDeg[alpha equals 1 degressive]
    shapeQ -- sphere --> dirQ3{Inside out or outside in?}
    dirQ3 -- inside out --> sphProg[alpha equals minus 2 strongly progressive]
    dirQ3 -- outside in --> sphDeg[alpha equals 2 strongly degressive]
    gran --> finalSelect[Choose corresponding P of t profile]
    endBurn --> finalSelect
    bothBurn --> finalSelect
    tubeProg --> finalSelect
    tubeDeg --> finalSelect
    sphProg --> finalSelect
    sphDeg --> finalSelect
```

## 4.3 Time Evolution of Normalized Surface Area (Mermaid Plot Emulation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0[Time equals 0 All surfaces at initial value A by A0 equals 1] --> t1[Time equals t b by 4]
    t1 --> t2[Time equals t b by 2 Mid burn]
    t2 --> t3[Time equals 3 t b by 4]
    t3 --> t4[Time equals t b End burn A by A0 equals 0]
    branchA[Slab branch A by A0 stays at 1] -.-> t4
    branchB[Cylinder branch A by A0 decreases linearly] -.-> t4
    branchC[Sphere branch A by A0 decreases quadratically] -.-> t4
    branchD[Hollow sphere branch A by A0 grows quadratically from 0] -.-> t4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each branch represents a different burning geometry. At $t = t_b$, slab ends with full area (unburned web still present), cylinder and sphere decay to zero, while the inside-out hollow sphere *grows throughout* — the classic **progressive** case.

## 4.4 DDT Risk Assessment Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    checkA{Pressure Exponent n greater than 0.8?}
    checkA -- yes --> riskHigh[HIGH DDT RISK]
    checkA -- no --> checkB{Confinement present?}
    checkB -- no --> riskLow[LOW RISK Open burn]
    checkB -- yes --> checkC{Progressive geometry alpha less than 0?}
    checkC -- yes --> riskHigh
    checkC -- no --> checkD{Granular packing density greater than 70 percent TMD?}
    checkD -- yes --> riskHigh
    checkD -- no --> riskMod[MODERATE RISK Monitor]
    riskHigh --> action[Action: Add inert barrier or vent area]
    riskMod --> action
    riskLow --> monitor[Action: Standard storage]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define Vieille's Law as used in explosive burning models. State the significance of the pressure exponent $n$.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Vieille's Law (also called St. Robert's Law) is an empirical equation describing the linear rate at which a solid propellant or explosive regresses as a function of the local chamber pressure:
> 
> $$r = a P^{n}$$
> 
> where $r$ is the linear burn rate (mm/s), $a$ is a constant, $P$ is pressure (MPa), and $n$ is the dimensionless pressure exponent. **(1 mark)**
> The exponent $n$ classifies propellant behavior: **(1 mark)**
> - $n \approx 0$: plateau burning (safe, neutral),
> - $0 < n < 1$: sub-linear (mining applications),
> - $n \geq 1$: super-rate (catastrophic, DDT-prone). **(1 mark)**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: What is the de Considère Equation? Write its general form for a burning solid of arbitrary geometry.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The de Considère Equation is a geometric relationship describing the temporal evolution of the burning surface area of a deflagrating explosive or propellant. **(1 mark)**
> 
> General form: 
> 
> $$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_b} \right)^{\alpha}$$
> 
> where $A(t)$ is the instantaneous surface area, $A_0$ is the initial area, $t_b$ is the total burn time, and $\alpha$ is the burning index. **(1 mark)**
> Values: $\alpha = 0$ (slab), $\alpha = 1$ (cylinder), $\alpha = 2$ (sphere). Negative $\alpha$ values correspond to progressive (inside-out) burning. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a clear derivation, obtain the de Considère equation for a solid sphere of an explosive burning radially from the outside inwards at a constant linear rate $r$. Clearly state the geometric meaning of the burning index $\alpha = 2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical charge of radius $r_0 = 40$ mm burns in a closed pressure vessel. The linear burn rate at the operating pressure is $r = 5$ mm/s. Calculate the burn time $t_b$ and the fraction of initial surface area that remains unburned at $t = 4$ s. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for Part (a):**
> *See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.* **(5 marks)**
> Geometric meaning: $\alpha = 2$ means the surface area decreases **quadratically** with time — characteristic of a sphere where both the area itself and the burn-front curvature shrink simultaneously. The pressure in a closed vessel will *rise* initially (due to high mass generation rate early in the burn) and then *fall* as the surface contracts. **(2 marks)**

> **Model Solution for Part (b):**
> **Step 1 — Burn time:** $t_b = r_0 / r = 40 / 5 = 8$ s. **(2 marks)**
> **Step 2 — Normalized time:** $t / t_b = 4 / 8 = 0.5$. **(1 mark)**
> **Step 3 — Apply de Considère:** 
> 
> $$\frac{A(4)}{A_0} = \left( 1 - 0.5 \right)^{2} = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25$$
> 
> **(2 marks)**
> **Step 4 — Interpretation:** 25% of the initial surface area remains unburned at $t = 4$ s. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Starting from first principles, derive the closed-vessel pressure rise equation for an explosive charge whose mass burning rate is $\dot{m} = \rho_s \, r \, A(t)$. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical propellant grain of initial outer radius $r_0 = 25$ mm and length $L = 100$ mm burns radially from the outside. The pressure exponent is $n = 0.7$ and the burn rate coefficient is $a = 1.6$ mm/s/MPa$^{0.7}$. If the chamber pressure stabilizes at $P = 4$ MPa, find the burn time and the mass burning rate at $t = t_b/2$. Take $\rho_s = 1600$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for Part (a):**
> **Assumptions (1 mark each):** (i) Ideal gas behaviour of combustion products. (ii) Constant flame temperature $T_f$. (iii) Isothermal chamber. (iv) Quasi-steady burn rate evaluated at instantaneous $P(t)$. (v) Negligible heat loss to walls.
> *See Section 3.3 of these notes for the full derivation leading to:*
> 
> $$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{\rho_s \, a \, R_s T_f}{V} P^{n}(t) A(t)$$
> 
> **(4 marks for the derivation)**

> **Model Solution for Part (b):**
> **Step 1 — Burn rate at $P = 4$ MPa:** 
> 
> $$r = a P^{n} = 1.6 \times (4)^{0.7} = 1.6 \times 2.639 = 4.222 \text{ mm/s}$$
> 
> **(2 marks)**
> **Step 2 — Burn time:** $t_b = r_0 / r = 25 / 4.222 \approx 5.92$ s. **(1 mark)**
> **Step 3 — Surface area at $t = t_b/2$:** For a cylinder (outside-in, $\alpha = 1$):
> 
> $$A(t_b/2) = A_0 \left(1 - 0.5\right) = 0.5 \, A_0$$
> 
> 
> $$A_0 = 2\pi r_0 L = 2\pi \times 0.025 \times 0.1 = 0.0157 \text{ m}^2$$
> 
> **(2 marks)**
> **Step 4 — Mass burning rate:**
> 
> $$\dot{m} = \rho_s \, r \, A = 1600 \times 0.004222 \times 0.5 \times 0.0157 \approx 0.053 \text{ kg/s}$$
> 
> **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing mm and m, MPa and Pa. Always normalize to **SI** before final substitution. Convert mm/s to m/s when working in kg/m³.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $\alpha$ (–1 mark):** Inside-out burning has *negative* $\alpha$. Writing $A/A_0 = (1 + t/t_b)^{2}$ without justifying the sign change.
> 3. **Skipping the assumptions (–1 mark):** Part (a) of any derivation question **always** demands a stated assumption list.
> 4. **Confusing burn rate $r$ with burn time $t_b$ (–1 mark):** $r$ has units of length/time; $t_b$ has units of time.
> 5. **Not interpreting the result (–1 mark):** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Always end numerical answers with a physical interpretation sentence ("Therefore, 25% of the surface remains…").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the last 60 seconds before the exam:**

- ✅ **Vieille's Law:** $r = aP^{n}$ — the kinetic foundation. Higher $n$ ⇒ more dangerous explosive.
- ✅ **de Considère Equation:** $A(t)/A_0 = (1 - t/t_b)^{\alpha}$ — the geometric foundation. Memorize the four index values: **0, 1, 2, and the negative variants −1, −2**.
- ✅ **Burn Time:** $t_b = e_0 / r$ for slabs; $t_b = r_0 / r$ for cylinders and spheres.
- ✅ **Master ODE for pressure rise (closed vessel):** $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \dfrac{\rho_s a R_s T_f}{V} P^{n} A(t)$.
- ✅ **Mass burning rate identity:** $\dot{m} = \rho_s \, r \, A$ — this single equation links the kinetic and geometric pillars.
- ✅ **DT and DDT distinction:** *Deflagration* = subsonic ($M < 0.7$), governed by burning models. *Detonation* = supersonic, governed by Chapman–Jouguet theory (Module 2).
- ✅ **Safety corollary:** **Progressive burning ($\alpha < 0$) in a confined geometry is the leading cause of DDT accidents** — always size vents using the Q-R method for such configurations.
- ✅ **Granular bed fires** behave approximately as $\alpha = 0$ (constant surface) but with **enhanced $n$** due to convective heating in pores.
- ✅ **Numerical rule of thumb:** A typical propellant at $P = 5$ MPa has $r \approx 5$–$10$ mm/s. Use this to sanity-check your answers.
- ✅ **Formula sheet is your friend** — in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, you are permitted to bring a single-sided A4 cheat sheet. Pre-print the table in Section 2.2 of these notes.

---

**End of Module 1 — Burning Model Notes** *(PESFT753 — Explosives Technology and Safety, KTU 2024 Scheme)*
