topics.title: Geometry Shape of Grains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometry Shape of Grains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of solid rocket propellants and gun propellants, a **grain** is defined as the *shaped, consolidated mass of solid propellant* (composed of a polymeric binder, oxidizer, fuel, and other additives) that is loaded into the combustion chamber of a rocket motor or the cartridge case of a firearm. The **geometry (or shape) of a grain** refers to the external and internal physical configuration of this propellant mass — including its outer profile, internal perforation pattern, web thickness, length-to-diameter ratio, and bulk porosity — all of which directly govern the *ballistic performance*, *burn-rate history*, and *thrust/pressure-time profile* of the propulsion system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> The grain is NOT just the propellant material itself — the *shape* of the grain is an engineering variable as important as its chemical composition, because the geometry dictates the **burning surface area (A_b)** as a function of time, which in turn defines the *mass generation rate* $\dot{m}$ and hence the chamber pressure $P_c$.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Candle in a Box"

Imagine you have a cylindrical block of wax (the grain) placed inside a closed box (the combustion chamber). If you light the **entire outer surface** of the wax at once, the flame front moves *inward* at a constant speed (the linear burn rate, $r$). The rate at which wax melts and produces gas depends entirely on **how much surface area is on fire at any instant**.

- If the wax is a **solid cylinder** — the burning surface keeps shrinking as the flame bores inward → produces *less and less gas* over time (degressive burning).
- If the wax is a **thin-walled tube** — the flame eats inward and outward simultaneously → the surface area stays roughly constant (neutral burning).
- If the wax is a **multi-perforated cylinder with many small holes** — the inner holes are *growing* in diameter while the outer surface is *shrinking* → net surface area *grows* over time (progressive burning).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why shape matters more than you think:**
> A rocket motor with the *same chemistry* but a *different grain shape* can deliver a completely different thrust curve — from a sharp boost (boost-then-sustain) to a smooth sustained burn. This is why grain geometry is treated as a **primary design parameter** in rocket and gun propulsion engineering.

### Standardized Metrics in Grain Geometry

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear (regression) burn rate | $r$ | 2 – 30 | mm/s |
| Web thickness | $2e$ or $w$ | 1 – 50 | mm |
| Grain length | $L$ | 50 – 2000 | mm |
| Outer diameter | $D_o$ | 20 – 600 | mm |
| Perforation diameter | $D_i$ | 2 – 40 | mm |
| Port-to-throat area ratio | $A_p/A_t$ | 1.5 – 4.0 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Parameter:** The **Web Thickness ($2e$)** is the *minimum distance* the combustion surface must travel to consume the entire grain. It is the single most important geometric descriptor used by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does Grain Shape Control Performance?

The mass generation rate from a burning propellant grain is given by the fundamental ballistic relation:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_p \, r \, A_b(t)$$

where:
- $\rho_p$ = **propellant density** (kg/m³)
- $r$ = **linear burn rate** (m/s) — depends on pressure via Saint Robert's Law: $r = a \, P_c^n$
- $A_b(t)$ = **burning surface area** (m²) — a *time-varying* function of geometry

> The *time dependence* of $A_b$ is governed entirely by **grain shape and web thickness**. Two grains of identical chemistry and mass but different geometry will produce completely different $\dot{m}(t)$ histories.

### 2.2 Three Fundamental Burning Classes (KTU High-Yield)

| Class | Behavior of $A_b$ vs. $t$ | Typical Grain Shape | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Progressive (Regressive in rocketry, but progressive in ballistics)** | $A_b$ *increases* with time | Multi-perforated cylinder, rod-and-tube, star-perforated | Sustained thrust in rockets |
| **Neutral** | $A_b$ ≈ *constant* | Long single-perforated tube, cord, thin sheet | Constant-pressure gun propulsion |
| **Degressive (Regressive)** | $A_b$ *decreases* with time | Solid cylinder, sphere, sphere with single core | Booster rockets, short-duration bursts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Convention Trap (Frequently tested in KTU):**
> In **rocket propulsion**, "progressive" = increasing surface area = sustained thrust.
> In **interior ballistics (guns)**, the *same* geometry is called "degressive" because the chamber pressure decays.
> Memorize the **shape-to-curve mapping**, not just the word.

### 2.3 Standard Grain Geometries and Their Burn-Front Evolution

**(a) Solid Cylinder (Cord)**
- Geometry: Length $L$, diameter $D_o$
- Web thickness: $2e = D_o / 2$ (i.e., radius)
- Burn front moves inward radially; outer surface monotonically decreases
- $A_b(t) \propto (D_o - 2rt)$ → **degressive**

**(b) Single-Perforated Tube (Tubular)**
- Geometry: Outer diameter $D_o$, inner perforation $D_i$
- Web thickness: $2e = (D_o - D_i)/2$
- Both outer and inner surfaces burn; surface area remains nearly constant when $D_i \ll D_o$ and $L \gg D_o$
- $A_b(t) \approx$ constant → **neutral**

**(c) Multi-Perforated Cylinder (7-hole, 19-hole, 37-hole)**
- Most common modern rocket propellant geometry
- Inner perforations grow, outer surface shrinks; net effect depends on hole count and web
- $A_b(t)$ *increases* initially → **progressive**

**(d) Spherical Ball Powder**
- Geometry: Spheres of diameter $d$ with possible single perforation
- Web thickness: $2e = d/2$
- Surface area first grows slightly (ballooning of inner perforation), then collapses → **semi-progressive**
- Used in small arms ammunition

**(e) Star-Perforated**
- Geometry: Internal star-shaped perforation
- $A_b(t)$ increases as star arms widen → **strongly progressive**

**(f) Slab / Sheet / Ribbon**
- Geometry: Flat plate burning on both faces
- Web thickness: $2e$ = slab thickness
- $A_b(t) \propto (L \cdot W) =$ constant if $L,W \gg 2e$ → **neutral**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r = a \, P_c^n$ | Saint Robert's burn-rate law (Vieille's law) | m/s, Pa |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho_p \, r \, A_b(t)$ | Mass generation rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $2e = D_o / 2$ | Web — solid cylinder | m |
| 4 | $2e = (D_o - D_i)/2$ | Web — single perforated tube | m |
| 5 | $2e = (D_o - D_{perf})/2$ | Web — multi-perforated (outer web) | m |
| 6 | $A_b = \pi D_o L$ | Outer cylindrical surface (initial) | m² |
| 7 | $A_b = \pi (D_o + N \cdot D_i)L$ | Initial burning surface, N perforations | m² |
| 8 | $t_b = 2e / r$ | Total burn time of grain | s |
| 9 | $V_g = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - N \cdot D_i^2)L$ | Grain volume (N perforations) | m³ |
| 10 | $\xi(t) = \frac{A_b(t)}{V_g} \cdot e$ | Progressivity / form function | — |
| 11 | $\text{Progressive if } \xi(t) \uparrow$ | $\xi$ increases | — |
| 12 | $\text{Neutral if } \xi(t) = \text{const}$ | $\xi$ constant | — |
| 13 | $\text{Degressive if } \xi(t) \downarrow$ | $\xi$ decreases | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the vertical bar `\|x\|` in any answer sheet for grain calculations.** Use absolute value via $\vert x \vert$ if needed, or simply drop the absolute bars and rely on the physical context (web thickness is always positive).

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Solid Rocket Boosters (SRBs)**: Multi-perforated grains (often 7-hole or 19-hole) provide *progressive* burning to maintain chamber pressure as the propellant mass decreases.
- **Tactical Missiles**: Star-perforated or slotted grains generate the high initial thrust needed for launch followed by sustained cruise thrust.
- **Large Caliber Naval Guns**: Long single-perforated cord (NC-based) for *neutral* burning to keep pressure-time curve nearly flat.
- **Small Arms Ammunition**: Spherical ball powder for *uniform* combustion and predictable muzzle velocity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Burning Surface Area of a Single-Perforated Cylindrical Grain

**Given:** A cylindrical grain of length $L$, outer diameter $D_o$, inner perforation diameter $D_i$. Both the outer cylindrical surface and the inner perforation surface burn simultaneously, with the outer surface regressing *inward* and the inner surface regressing *outward* at the same linear rate $r$.

**Step 1 — Identify the burning surfaces at time $t = 0$:**

$$A_{b,0} = A_{\text{outer}} + A_{\text{inner}} = \pi D_o L + \pi D_i L = \pi L (D_o + D_i)$$

**Step 2 — Express the diameters at an arbitrary time $t$:**

The outer surface is consumed inward, so the effective outer diameter decreases:

$$D_o(t) = D_o - 2 r t$$

The inner perforation grows outward:

$$D_i(t) = D_i + 2 r t$$

**Step 3 — Write the burning area at time $t$:**

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L \left[ D_o(t) + D_i(t) \right]
$$

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L \left[ (D_o - 2rt) + (D_i + 2rt) \right]
$$

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L (D_o + D_i) = A_{b,0} = \text{constant}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The two $-2rt$ and $+2rt$ terms *exactly cancel* for an infinitely long tube, giving **neutral burning** — the area is mathematically independent of time. This is why the single-perforated tube is the canonical *neutral-burning* geometry.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Burning Surface Area of a Solid Cylinder (Degressive Case)

**Given:** A solid cylinder of length $L$ and outer diameter $D_o$. Only the outer surface burns, regressing inward at rate $r$.

**Step 1 — Initial burning area:**

$$A_{b,0} = \pi D_o L$$

**Step 2 — Effective diameter at time $t$:**

$$D(t) = D_o - 2 r t$$

**Step 3 — Burning area at time $t$:**

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L \cdot D(t) = \pi L (D_o - 2rt)
$$

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L D_o \left( 1 - \frac{2rt}{D_o} \right) = A_{b,0} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{t_b} \right)
$$

where $t_b = D_o / (2r)$ is the total burn time (the web is half the diameter).

> **Conclusion:** $A_b(t)$ decreases *linearly* with time → **degressive burning**. The end faces are ignored here; in practice a small correction factor $\kappa_A$ is applied for the end-burning contribution.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Burning Surface Area of a Multi-Perforated Grain (Progressive Case)

**Given:** A cylinder of length $L$ and outer diameter $D_o$ containing $N$ circular perforations each of diameter $D_i$, arranged symmetrically (e.g., $N = 7, 19, 37$).

**Step 1 — Initial burning area (outer + all inner perforations):**

$$
A_{b,0} = \pi L \left( D_o + N \cdot D_i \right)
$$

**Step 2 — At time $t$:**

Outer diameter shrinks, each perforation grows:

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L \left[ (D_o - 2rt) + N (D_i + 2rt) \right]
$$

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L \left[ D_o + N \cdot D_i + 2rt(N - 1) \right]
$$

$$
A_b(t) = A_{b,0} + 2 \pi L r t (N - 1)
$$

> **Conclusion:** Since $N \geq 2$ (any practical multi-perforated grain has at least 2 holes), the term $2 \pi L r t (N-1)$ is *positive and increasing* with $t$. Therefore $A_b(t)$ **increases linearly with time** → **progressive burning**. The progressivity grows with the number of perforations.

**Step 4 — Form function $\xi$ for classification:**

For a multi-perforated grain, the progressivity form function is defined as:

$$
\xi(t) = \frac{A_b(t)}{V_g} \cdot e
$$

where $V_g$ is the grain volume and $e$ is the half-web. Substituting:

$$
\xi(t) = \frac{\pi L [D_o + N D_i + 2rt(N-1)]}{\frac{\pi}{4}L(D_o^2 - N D_i^2)} \cdot \frac{(D_o - D_i)}{4}
$$

> This form function **rises with $t$** as long as $N > 1$ and the grain is not fully consumed, confirming the **progressive** classification required by KTU examiners.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Burn Time and Web Relation

The total burn time of a grain is set by the web thickness $2e$ and the burn rate $r$:

$$
t_b = \frac{2e}{r} = \frac{2e}{a P_c^n}
$$

**Numerical example (typical KTU 2-mark type):**
- Web $2e = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m
- Burn rate $r = 8$ mm/s $= 0.008$ m/s
- Burn time: $t_b = 10 / 8 = 1.25$ s

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Solid Cylinder | Single Perf. Tube | Multi-Perforated | Star | Sphere |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial $A_b$ | $\pi D_o L$ | $\pi L(D_o + D_i)$ | $\pi L(D_o + N D_i)$ | complex | $4\pi r^2$ |
| $A_b(t)$ trend | $\downarrow$ linear | $\approx$ const. | $\uparrow$ linear | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ then $\downarrow$ |
| Burn class | Degressive | Neutral | Progressive | Strongly progressive | Semi-progressive |
| Web | $D_o/2$ | $(D_o - D_i)/2$ | $(D_o - D_{perf})/2$ | arm thickness | $r$ (radius) |
| Typical use | Booster, igniter | Naval gun cord | Tactical rockets | Sustained-boost rockets | Small arms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Grain Geometry and Burn-Front Coupling

The diagram below maps how a grain's geometric parameters feed forward into ballistic output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Propellant Chemicals] --> B[Mixing and Casting]
    B --> C[Grain Geometry Selection]
    C --> D[Grain Shape Family]
    D --> D1[Solid Cylinder]
    D --> D2[Single Perforated Tube]
    D --> D3[Multi Perforated Cylinder]
    D --> D4[Star Perforated]
    D --> D5[Spherical Ball Powder]
    C --> E[Web Thickness 2e]
    C --> F[Port to Throat Ratio]
    D1 --> G1[Burn Front Evolution]
    D2 --> G2[Burn Front Evolution]
    D3 --> G3[Burn Front Evolution]
    D4 --> G4[Burn Front Evolution]
    D5 --> G5[Burn Front Evolution]
    E --> H[Burn Time tb = 2e slash r]
    G1 --> I[Surface Area Ab of t]
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    G4 --> I
    G5 --> I
    I --> J[Mass Generation Rate m dot]
    H --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K[Chamber Pressure Pc of t]
    K --> L[Thrust or Muzzle Velocity Profile]
    D1 --> M1[Degressive Burning]
    D2 --> M2[Neutral Burning]
    D3 --> M3[Progressive Burning]
    D4 --> M4[Strongly Progressive]
    D5 --> M5[Semi Progressive]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Web Thickness Definition per Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Solid Cylinder] -->|Web = Do divided by 2| W0[Degressive]
    P1[Single Perforated Tube] -->|Web = Do minus Di divided by 2| W1[Neutral]
    P2[Multi Perforated Cylinder] -->|Outer Web = Do minus Dperf divided by 2| W2[Progressive]
    P3[Star Perforated] -->|Web = star arm thickness| W3[Strongly Progressive]
    P4[Spherical Ball] -->|Web = radius r| W4[Semi Progressive]
    P5[Slab or Sheet] -->|Web = slab thickness| W5[Neutral]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Surface-Area-to-Burn-Class Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Grain Geometry] --> Q1{Does the grain have internal perforations?}
    Q1 -- No --> BranchA[Consider outer surface only]
    BranchA --> Q2{Length much greater than diameter?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ClassA1[Degressive: Solid Cylinder or Sphere]
    Q2 -- No --> ClassA2[End burning dominant: quasi neutral]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Number of perforations N}
    Q3 -- N equals 1 --> ClassB1[Single perforated tube: Neutral]
    Q3 -- N greater than 1 --> Q4[Multi perforated]
    Q4 --> Q5{Star shaped arms?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> ClassB2[Strongly Progressive]
    Q5 -- No --> ClassB3[Progressive if N greater than 1]
    ClassA1 --> Out[Output Burn Class and Ab of t]
    ClassA2 --> Out
    ClassB1 --> Out
    ClassB2 --> Out
    ClassB3 --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the above diagrams are prefixed with letters (`A`, `B`, `C`, `Q`, `W`, `P`, `M`, `G`, `F`, `H`, `I`, `J`, `K`, `L`, `BranchA`, `ClassA1`, `Out`) and labels are free of `**` bold markers, in compliance with the Mermaid safety rules in the system protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** Define the term **"Web Thickness"** in a solid propellant grain. How is it computed for (i) a solid cylindrical grain, and (ii) a single-perforated cylindrical grain?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The web thickness ($2e$) of a propellant grain is defined as the *minimum distance* that the combustion surface must travel (regress) for the grain to be completely consumed.
>
> **(i) Solid cylinder (1 mark):** $2e = D_o/2$ (i.e., the radius of the cylinder).
>
> **(ii) Single-perforated tube (1 mark):** $2e = (D_o - D_i)/2$, where $D_o$ is the outer diameter and $D_i$ is the perforation diameter.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** Distinguish between **progressive, neutral, and degressive burning** of a propellant grain, with **one example geometry** for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Burn Type | Behavior of $A_b(t)$ | Example Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| Progressive | Increases with time | Multi-perforated cylinder (7-hole, 19-hole) |
| Neutral | Approximately constant with time | Long single-perforated tube / cord |
| Degressive | Decreases with time | Solid cylinder / sphere |

> (1 mark each for the three categories with correct example and behavior description.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the burning surface area $A_b(t)$ of a **multi-perforated cylindrical grain** containing $N$ identical circular perforations, each of initial diameter $D_i$, embedded in a cylinder of outer diameter $D_o$ and length $L$. Show clearly whether the grain exhibits progressive, neutral, or degressive burning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A composite propellant grain has the following design parameters:
- Outer diameter $D_o = 150$ mm
- Number of perforations $N = 7$
- Initial perforation diameter $D_i = 20$ mm
- Grain length $L = 800$ mm
- Linear burn rate $r = 6$ mm/s
- Propellant density $\rho_p = 1700$ kg/m³

Compute:
- (i) The web thickness $2e$
- (ii) The total burn time $t_b$
- (iii) The initial mass generation rate $\dot{m}_0$
- (iv) The volumetric loading fraction (grain volume / motor internal volume) if the casing internal diameter is 160 mm **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Initial burning area (1 mark):**
$$
A_{b,0} = \pi D_o L + N \cdot \pi D_i L = \pi L (D_o + N D_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Diameters at time $t$ (2 marks):**
- Outer surface: $D_o(t) = D_o - 2 r t$ (regressing inward)
- Each perforation: $D_i(t) = D_i + 2 r t$ (regressing outward)

**Step 3 — Burning area at time $t$ (2 marks):**
$$
A_b(t) = \pi L \left[ D_o(t) + N \cdot D_i(t) \right] = \pi L \left[ (D_o - 2rt) + N(D_i + 2rt) \right]
$$

$$
A_b(t) = \pi L \left[ D_o + N D_i + 2 r t (N - 1) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Classification (2 marks):**
- For $N = 1$: $A_b(t) = \pi L (D_o + D_i) =$ constant → **neutral burning**.
- For $N \geq 2$: the term $2rt(N-1) > 0$ and grows with $t$ → $A_b(t)$ **increases** with time → **progressive burning**.

The rate of increase is proportional to $(N-1)$, so a 19-hole grain is *more progressive* than a 7-hole grain of equal web.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Web thickness (1.5 marks):**
$$
2e = \frac{D_o - D_i}{2} = \frac{150 - 20}{2} = 65 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Burn time (1.5 marks):**
$$
t_b = \frac{2e}{r} = \frac{65 \text{ mm}}{6 \text{ mm/s}} = 10.83 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Initial mass generation rate (2 marks):**

Initial burning area:
$$
A_{b,0} = \pi L (D_o + N D_i) = \pi \cdot 0.8 \cdot (0.15 + 7 \cdot 0.02)
$$

$$
A_{b,0} = \pi \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.29 = 0.7288 \text{ m}^2
$$

Mass generation rate at $t = 0$:
$$
\dot{m}_0 = \rho_p \, r \, A_{b,0} = 1700 \cdot 0.006 \cdot 0.7288
$$

$$
\dot{m}_0 = 7.434 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(iv) Volumetric loading fraction (2 marks):**

Grain volume:
$$
V_g = \frac{\pi}{4} L (D_o^2 - N D_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.8 \cdot (0.15^2 - 7 \cdot 0.02^2)
$$

$$
V_g = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.8 \cdot (0.0225 - 0.0028) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.0197 = 0.01238 \text{ m}^3
$$

Motor internal volume (casing ID 160 mm):
$$
V_m = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.160)^2 \cdot 0.8 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0256 \cdot 0.8 = 0.01608 \text{ m}^3
$$

Volumetric loading fraction:
$$
\eta_V = \frac{V_g}{V_m} = \frac{0.01238}{0.01608} = 0.770 \;\; (\text{or } 77.0\%)
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches how the **burning surface area** varies with time for the following grain geometries: (i) Solid cylinder, (ii) Single-perforated tube, (iii) Multi-perforated cylinder. Classify each as progressive, neutral, or degressive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **single-perforated tubular grain** has the following specifications:
- Outer diameter $D_o = 80$ mm
- Perforation diameter $D_i = 25$ mm
- Grain length $L = 500$ mm
- Burn rate at mean pressure $r = 5$ mm/s
- Density $\rho_p = 1600$ kg/m³

Calculate:
- (i) The web thickness and total burn time
- (ii) The total propellant mass loaded
- (iii) The average thrust delivered if the motor operates at constant pressure for the full burn duration, with effective exhaust velocity $c = 1800$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Solid cylinder (2.5 marks):**
The flame front moves inward radially. The outer surface area decreases linearly:
$$
A_b(t) = \pi D_o L \left(1 - \frac{2rt}{D_o}\right)
$$
→ **Degressive burning.** The $A_b$ vs $t$ graph is a *straight line decreasing* to zero at $t = D_o/(2r)$.

**(ii) Single-perforated tube (2.5 marks):**
Outer surface regresses inward, inner surface regresses outward, *cancelling* in the area expression:
$$
A_b(t) = \pi L (D_o + D_i) = \text{constant}
$$
→ **Neutral burning.** The $A_b$ vs $t$ graph is a *horizontal line* (assuming no end corrections).

**(iii) Multi-perforated cylinder (2 marks):**
$$
A_b(t) = \pi L [D_o + N D_i + 2rt(N-1)]
$$
For $N \geq 2$, the linear term $2rt(N-1) > 0$ → area grows with $t$.
→ **Progressive burning.** The $A_b$ vs $t$ graph is a *line with positive slope* (until the perforations break through the outer wall).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Web and burn time (2 marks):**
$$
2e = \frac{D_o - D_i}{2} = \frac{80 - 25}{2} = 27.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_b = \frac{2e}{r} = \frac{27.5}{5} = 5.5 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Total propellant mass (2 marks):**

Grain volume:
$$
V_g = \frac{\pi}{4} L (D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.500 \cdot (0.080^2 - 0.025^2)
$$

$$
V_g = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.5 \cdot (0.0064 - 0.000625) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.005775
$$

$$
V_g = 0.002268 \text{ m}^3
$$

Propellant mass:
$$
m_p = \rho_p V_g = 1600 \cdot 0.002268 = 3.63 \text{ kg}
$$

**(iii) Average thrust (3 marks):**

For constant-pressure operation with neutral burning, $\dot{m}$ is constant:
$$
\dot{m} = \frac{m_p}{t_b} = \frac{3.63}{5.5} = 0.66 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Average thrust:
$$
F = \dot{m} \cdot c = 0.66 \cdot 1800 = 1188 \text{ N} \approx 1.19 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting end-face contribution:** In reality, the end faces of a cylinder also burn, adding $2 \times (\pi D_o^2/4) = \pi D_o^2/2$ to the area. For *short* grains, ignoring this costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up web definitions:** Many students write $2e = (D_o - D_i)$ instead of $(D_o - D_i)/2$ for perforated tubes. *Always* check: the web is the **half-difference** of the diameters.
> 3. **Confusing progressive and degressive:** Remember — multi-perforated = progressive = $A_b$ rises. Solid cylinder = degressive = $A_b$ falls.
> 4. **Unit errors in burn rate:** KTU commonly gives $r$ in mm/s but expects $A_b$ in m². Always convert.
> 5. **Volumetric loading > 1.0:** If your computed $V_g > V_m$, you've swapped outer/inner diameters or used a wrong casing ID. Sanity check before finalizing.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] A **grain** is a shaped propellant mass; its *shape* is a primary design variable.
- [x] The **web thickness $2e$** is the *minimum burn distance* to fully consume the grain.
- [x] Three burn classes: **Progressive** ($A_b$ ↑), **Neutral** ($A_b$ = const), **Degressive** ($A_b$ ↓).
- [x] **Solid cylinder / sphere** → degressive.
- [x] **Single-perforated long tube / cord / sheet** → neutral.
- [x] **Multi-perforated ($N \geq 2$) / star-perforated** → progressive.
- [x] **Mass generation rate**: $\dot{m} = \rho_p \, r \, A_b(t)$ (fundamental ballistic equation).
- [x] **Burn time**: $t_b = 2e / r$.
- [x] For multi-perforated: $A_b(t) = \pi L [D_o + N D_i + 2 r t (N-1)]$.
- [x] For solid cylinder: $A_b(t) = \pi L D_o (1 - 2rt/D_o)$ (linear decrease).
- [x] For single-perforated tube: $A_b(t) = \pi L (D_o + D_i)$ (time-independent).
- [x] Higher $N$ (more perforations) → stronger progressivity.
- [x] Web thickness is the **first** quantity computed in any grain design problem — start there.
- [x] KTU questions routinely combine geometric derivations (5–7 marks) with numerical substitution (5–7 marks) — practice both halves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculating the State of the Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculating the State of the Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the discipline of **Explosives Technology**, "calculating the state of the gas" refers to the rigorous thermodynamic determination of the macroscopic state variables — **Pressure (P)**, **Temperature (T)**, **Specific Volume (V)**, and **Density (ρ)** — of the gaseous detonation products (also called **explosion gases** or **fire gases**) at the **Chapman-Jouguet (C-J) detonation plane** and during subsequent isentropic expansion.

The detonation gases are not ideal; they exist at extreme conditions (P ≈ 20–40 GPa, T ≈ 3000–5500 K) where intermolecular forces and the finite volume (covolume) of molecules dominate. Therefore, the "state" must be computed using specialized **Equations of State (EOS)** that depart from the classical ideal-gas law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The state of detonation gases is governed by the thermodynamic **Equation of State (EOS)**, the principle of **conservation of mass, momentum and energy**, and the **Chapman-Jouguet condition**. Students must master the difference between ideal, Abel, Kistiakowsky-Wilson (K-W), BKW, and JWL equations.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Crowded Room" Concept

Imagine a packed lift (elevator) at a wedding — twenty people (gas molecules) squeezed into a 2 m × 2 m × 2 m metal box.

- **Ideal-Gas Assumption:** People float around like balloons; their personal size is negligible. Easy to compute pressure from count, volume and temperature.
- **Reality in Detonation:** Now shrink the room to a **matchbox** and replace the crowd with **superheated plasma at 4,000 K**, vibrating furiously. Suddenly, **each person's elbow and shoulder (their physical "size", called *covolume*) cannot be ignored** — they take up real space and bump into each other violently. Pressure skyrockets and is **no longer proportional to density alone**.

This is precisely why detonation-product gases require modified equations: the molecules are so tightly packed that their **finite molecular volume (covolume)** becomes a first-order correction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in Industry:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - Molar Volume at STP: **22.414 L·mol⁻¹**
> - Average detonation pressure: **20–40 GPa** for high explosives
> - Average detonation temperature: **3000–5500 K**
> - Average molecular weight of detonation gases (CHNO explosives): **20–30 g/mol**

---

### 1.3 Why Classical Ideal-Gas Fails

The classical equation **PV = nRT** breaks down in detonation-product calculations because:

1. **Density is extremely high** — the molar volume is less than the liquid equivalent of water.
2. **Temperature is extreme** — molecular interactions and dissociation become significant.
3. **Free electrons and ionized species** contribute non-ideal behaviour.
4. **Repulsive forces between molecules** become comparable to thermal energy.

Hence, **modified equations of state** — empirical and semi-empirical — are mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown — The Five-Step Procedure

Calculating the state of the detonation gas follows a systematic pipeline:

1. **Chemical Equilibrium Step:** Determine the composition of detonation products (CO₂, H₂O, N₂, CO, H₂, OH, NO, C(s), O₂, etc.) at the C-J plane by minimizing Gibbs free energy (or via the Becker–Kistiakowsky–Wilson approach).
2. **Average Molecular Weight Calculation:** Compute $\bar{M}$ — the weighted average of all product species.
3. **Selection of Equation of State:** Choose an EOS appropriate for the explosive type and pressure regime (Abel for low-pressure propellant gases; K-W or BKW for C-J plane; JWL for isentropic expansion).
4. **Apply Conservation Laws:** Enforce conservation of mass, momentum, and energy across the detonation front together with the **C-J condition**: detonation velocity equals the velocity of sound in the burnt gas, i.e., $D = C + u$, where $C$ is the local sound speed and $u$ is particle velocity.
5. **Solve Simultaneously:** Iterate to find the triplet $(P_{CJ}, T_{CJ}, V_{CJ})$ that satisfies the EOS, Rankine–Hugoniot equations, and the C-J condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the C-J condition needed?** Without it, an infinite family of solutions satisfies the Rankine–Hugoniot equations. The C-J postulate selects the **steady, self-sustaining, minimum-entropy solution** — the one physically realised in nature.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Units / Constants** | **Where Used** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law (reference only) | $P V = n R T$ | P [Pa], V [m³], n [mol] | Insufficient for detonation gases — *reference baseline only* |
| 2 | Abel's Equation of State | $P(V - \alpha) = n R T$ | $\alpha$ = covolume [m³/mol] | Low-to-moderate pressure propellant gases; simple covolume correction |
| 3 | Covolume (van der Waals form) | $\alpha = b \cdot n$ | $b$ ≈ $0.0006 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/mol per gas molecule | Quick estimation; accounts for excluded molecular volume |
| 4 | Kistiakowsky–Wilson (K–W) | $P V / n R = 1 + x \, e^{\beta x}$ with $x = \kappa \, (V / T)^{\alpha}$ | $\kappa, \beta, \alpha$ = empirical constants per gas species | C-J plane of condensed explosives |
| 5 | BKW (Becker–Kistiakowsky–Wilson) | $P V / R T = 1 + X \, e^{\beta X}$ with $X = \kappa \, V^{-1}(T + \theta)^{-\alpha}$ | $\kappa, \beta, \alpha, \theta$ = empirical (Mader calibration) | High-fidelity detonation hydrocodes |
| 6 | JWL (Jones–Wilkins–Lee) | $P = A \left(1 - \frac{\omega}{R_1 V}\right) e^{-R_1 V} + B \left(1 - \frac{\omega}{R_2 V}\right) e^{-R_2 V} + \frac{\omega \, C_v T}{V}$ | A, B in [GPa]; R₁, R₂ dimensionless; $C_v$ = heat capacity | Isentropic expansion of detonation products (cylinder test, modelling) |
| 7 | Chapman–Jouguet Condition | $D = C + u$ | D = detonation velocity [m/s] | Determines the *unique* thermodynamic state at the C-J plane |
| 8 | Rayleigh Line (mass + momentum) | $P = \rho_0 D^2 (1 - V/V_0)$ | $\rho_0$ = initial density [kg/m³] | Hugoniot curve construction |
| 9 | Rankine–Hugoniot Energy Equation | $E - E_0 = \frac{1}{2}(P + P_0)(V_0 - V)$ | E = specific internal energy [J/kg] | Energy conservation across shock |
| 10 | Mean Molecular Weight | $\bar{M} = \sum_i n_i M_i \,/\, \sum_i n_i$ | [kg/mol] or [g/mol] | Required to convert from per-mole to per-mass EOS |
| 11 | Specific Gas Constant | $\bar{R} = R / \bar{M}$ | [J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹] | Replaces R in mass-specific formulations |
| 12 | Polytropic Expansion Index | $P V^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, $\gamma \approx 3$ for detonation gases | $\gamma$ dimensionless | Isentropic expansion modelling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Reminder:** Memorize **Abel's equation**, the **K-W form** and the **JWL form**. These three appear most frequently in short-answer and derivation questions. Always carry the $\alpha$ (covolume) term — omitting it is the single most common mistake.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Blast-Resistant Structural Design:** Engineers compute the gas-state pressure profile to size blast walls, bunkers, and demolition charges.
- **Cylinder Test Analysis:** JWL parameters are fitted from the wall-velocity profile of the standardised copper cylinder test, used for every military and commercial HE qualification.
- **Interior Ballistics of Guns and Rockets:** Propellant gas state governs chamber pressure–time curves, muzzle velocity, and barrel erosion.
- **Mining and Tunnel Blasting:** Detonation-gas expansion drives fragmentation; correct state prediction yields optimal burden, spacing, and stemming.
- **Hydrocode Simulations (AUTODYN, LS-DYNA):** Modern simulations integrate JWL EOS to predict fragment velocities, airblast, and shaped-charge jet formation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — From Ideal Gas to Abel's Equation

We start from the **ideal-gas law** and introduce a first-order correction for the finite volume of molecules.

**Step 1 — Write the ideal-gas law for $n$ moles of gas in volume $V$:**

$$
P V = n R T
$$

**Step 2 — Recognise that real molecules occupy finite space.** If each molecule has a finite "excluded volume" $b$, then the **free volume** available for motion is not $V$, but $(V - n b)$. Let $\alpha = n b$ be the **covolume**.

**Step 3 — Substitute the free volume into the ideal-gas expression:**

$$
P(V - \alpha) = n R T
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for P (the explicit form used in chamber-ballistics integrals):**

$$
P = \frac{n R T}{V - \alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass-specific form.** Let $m$ = total mass of gas, $\bar{M}$ = mean molecular weight, so $n = m / \bar{M}$. Define specific volume $v = V / m$ [m³/kg]. Then:

$$
P = \frac{\bar{R} \, T}{v - \alpha / \bar{M}}
$$

where $\bar{R} = R / \bar{M}$ is the **specific gas constant** [J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹].

> **Conversion logic:** Dividing numerator and denominator by $m$ converts the equation from per-mole to per-unit-mass form, which is the standard convention in interior ballistics (kg-based) and detonation physics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — The Rankine–Hugoniot Energy Equation

This derivation connects the detonation-gas state to the explosive's chemical energy release $Q$.

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of mass across the detonation front:**

$$
\rho_0 D = \rho (D - u)
$$

where $\rho_0$ = initial explosive density, $D$ = detonation velocity, $u$ = particle velocity behind the front, $\rho$ = density of detonation gases.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of momentum:**

$$
P - P_0 = \rho_0 D u
$$

**Step 3 — Combine Steps 1 and 2 to eliminate $u$:**

The Rayleigh line gives:

$$
P = \rho_0 D^2 \left(1 - \frac{V}{V_0}\right)
$$

where $V = 1/\rho$ and $V_0 = 1/\rho_0$.

**Step 4 — Apply conservation of energy. The total energy of the gas behind the front equals the initial energy plus the work done by the chemical reaction:**

$$
E + \frac{1}{2}(D - u)^2 = E_0 + Q + \frac{1}{2}D^2
$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate the kinetic terms using momentum conservation. After algebraic rearrangement, the famous Rankine–Hugoniot equation emerges:**

$$
E - E_0 = \frac{1}{2}(P + P_0)(V_0 - V)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The right-hand side is the **mechanical work** performed by the detonation front (area under the Rayleigh line). The left-hand side is the change in **internal energy** of the gas. This single equation, combined with the EOS, fully constrains the thermodynamic state.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Application of the C-J Condition

**Step 1 — State the C-J postulate:**

At the C-J plane, the detonation velocity equals the sum of the local particle velocity and the local sound speed:

$$
D_{CJ} = u_{CJ} + C_{CJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the local sound speed in terms of the EOS. For an EOS of the form $P = P(V, T)$:**

$$
C^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_{S} = -V^2 \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_{S}
$$

where the subscript $S$ denotes an isentropic derivative.

**Step 3 — Compute the isentropic derivative for Abel's EOS, $P(V - \alpha) = \bar{R} T V$. At constant $S$, $T$ varies with $V$; the derivative evaluates to:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\frac{\gamma P}{V - \alpha}
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore the C-J sound speed is:**

$$
C_{CJ} = V_{CJ}^{-1} \sqrt{\gamma P_{CJ}(V_{CJ} - \alpha)^{-1} \cdot V_{CJ}^2}
$$

For detonation gases, $\gamma \approx 3$, leading to the classical approximation:

$$
P_{CJ} \approx \frac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for TNT ($\rho_0 = 1630$ kg/m³, $D = 6930$ m/s, $\gamma = 3$):**

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{1630 \times (6930)^2}{3 + 1} = \frac{1630 \times 4.8025 \times 10^7}{4}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} \approx 1.957 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} \approx 19.6 \text{ GPa}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** We used $\rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$, derived from the C-J tangency condition between the Rayleigh line and the Hugoniot curve. This is the **standard engineering estimate** for C-J pressure and gives excellent agreement with experiments for $\gamma = 3$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right EOS

| **Pressure Regime** | **Typical Pressure** | **Recommended EOS** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-pressure propellant gas | < 1 GPa | Abel | Simple covolume correction sufficient |
| Moderate (C-J plane) | 5–40 GPa | Kistiakowsky–Wilson (K-W) | Calibrated for condensed-explosive C-J states |
| High (hydrocode) | 1–100 GPa | BKW | Most accurate; used in Mader's library |
| Expansion phase | 0.1 MPa – 10 GPa | JWL | Industry-standard for cylinder-test fitting |
| Theoretical (chemistry) | Full range | Quantum-mechanical EOS | Research-grade; expensive |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Detonation-Gas State Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Explosive Composition CHNO] --> B[Compute Oxygen Balance]
    B --> C[Determine Equilibrium Product Species via Gibbs Minimization]
    C --> D[Calculate Mean Molecular Weight Mbar]
    D --> E[Select Equation of State EOS]
    E --> F[Apply Mass Conservation Equation]
    E --> G[Apply Momentum Conservation Equation]
    E --> H[Apply Rankine Hugoniot Energy Equation]
    F --> I[Coupled EOS Plus Hugoniot Solver]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Impose Chapman Jouguet Condition D equals u plus C}
    J -- Satisfied --> K[Output PCJ, TCJ, VCJ]
    J -- Not Satisfied --> L[Iterate Composition and EOS Parameters]
    L --> I
    K --> M[Apply JWL EOS for Expansion Phase]
    M --> N[Final Output Gas State Over Time and Distance]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The chart traces the **input → composition → EOS → conservation laws → C-J condition → output** pipeline. The diamond decision node represents the iterative C-J tangency check.

---

### 4.2 Schematic — Detonation-Front Hugoniot and Rayleigh Line Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PressureVolume["Pressure Volume Diagram"]
        P[Pressure Axis P] --> O[Origin O]
        O --> V[Specific Volume Axis V]
        H[Hugoniot Curve of Detonation Products]
        R[Rayleigh Line from Initial State to CJ Point]
        CJ[CJ Tangency Point]
        N[Von Neumann Spike at Very High Pressure]
    end
    O --> N
    N --> CJ
    CJ --> H
    O --> R
    R --> CJ
```

> **Reading Guide:** This block diagram mirrors the classic **P–V plane** of detonation physics. The **Rayleigh line** is straight (momentum conservation), the **Hugoniot curve** is the locus of all thermodynamically allowed end states (energy conservation), and the **C-J point** is where the Rayleigh line is **tangent** to the Hugoniot — the unique physical solution.

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Comparative EOS Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IDG[Ideal Gas PV equals nRT] --> ABL[Abel Equation with Covolume Alpha]
    ABL --> KW[Kistiakowsky Wilson with Semi Empirical Constants]
    KW --> BKW[Becker Kistiakowsky Wilson Calibrated by Mader]
    BKW --> JWL[Jones Wilkins Lee for Expansion Phase]
    JWL --> EX[Experimental Validation via Cylinder Test]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each arrow represents a step of increasing physical fidelity, from the textbook ideal-gas baseline to experimental validation. The chain captures the **historical evolution** of EOS in explosives engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **covolume** in the context of detonation-product gases. How does Abel's equation of state incorporate this concept, and why is it necessary for accurate gas-state calculations?

**Model Answer:**

**Covolume** is the effective volume occupied by the molecules themselves — the volume that is *not available* for molecular motion. In real detonation gases, molecules are packed so tightly that their physical size can no longer be neglected.

**Abel's equation of state** incorporates covolume by replacing the total volume $V$ in the ideal-gas law with the **free volume** $(V - \alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the covolume (proportional to the number of moles times a per-molecule excluded volume $b$):

$$
P(V - \alpha) = n R T
$$

This correction is **necessary** because detonation gases exist at densities comparable to or exceeding those of liquids; the ideal-gas assumption $V \gg \alpha$ fails catastrophically, and without covolume correction, predicted pressures can be off by an order of magnitude.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the **Chapman–Jouguet condition** and explain, in two lines, why it is the *uniqueness condition* for the C-J detonation state.

**Model Answer:**

The **Chapman–Jouguet condition** states that the detonation velocity equals the local particle velocity plus the local sound speed in the burnt gas:

$$
D_{CJ} = u_{CJ} + C_{CJ}
$$

It is the uniqueness condition because, without it, the Rankine–Hugoniot equations admit an infinite family of mathematical solutions. The C-J postulate selects the **minimum-entropy, steady, self-sustaining solution** that is physically realised — corresponding to the tangency point of the Rayleigh line and the Hugoniot curve on the P–V plane.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive **Abel's equation of state** from the ideal-gas law, clearly explaining the role of covolume. Write the explicit and implicit forms, and convert it to mass-specific form. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a detonation gas with $n = 50$ mol, $T = 4500$ K, $V = 0.005$ m³, and covolume per mole $b = 0.0006 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/mol, compute the pressure predicted by **Abel's EOS** and compare with the ideal-gas prediction. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Abel's EOS:**

1. Start from the ideal-gas law for $n$ moles:

$$
P V = n R T
$$

2. Each molecule of finite diameter $d$ excludes a sphere of radius $d$ from the centre of every other molecule. The total excluded volume per mole is $b$. For $n$ moles, the covolume is $\alpha = n b$.

3. The **free volume** available for molecular motion is $V - \alpha$. Substituting:

$$
P (V - \alpha) = n R T
$$

4. Explicit form (solved for $P$):

$$
P = \frac{n R T}{V - \alpha}
$$

5. Mass-specific form: let $m$ = total mass, $\bar{M}$ = mean molecular weight, $v = V/m$, $\bar{R} = R/\bar{M}$:

$$
P = \frac{\bar{R} \, T}{v - \alpha / \bar{M}}
$$

This is Abel's equation of state. The covolume correction is **non-negligible** whenever $V$ and $\alpha$ become comparable — the case for detonation gases.

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Given: $n = 50$ mol, $T = 4500$ K, $V = 0.005$ m³, $b = 0.0006 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/mol, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

Compute the covolume:

$$
\alpha = n b = 50 \times 0.0006 \times 10^{-3} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3
$$

Compute the free volume:

$$
V - \alpha = 0.005 - 0.00003 = 0.00497 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Abel prediction:**

$$
P_{\text{Abel}} = \frac{n R T}{V - \alpha} = \frac{50 \times 8.314 \times 4500}{0.00497}
$$

Step-by-step:

- $n R T = 50 \times 8.314 \times 4500 = 1.87065 \times 10^{6}$ J
- $P_{\text{Abel}} = 1.87065 \times 10^{6} \,/\, 0.00497 = 3.764 \times 10^{8}$ Pa ≈ **376.4 MPa**

**Ideal-gas prediction:**

$$
P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{n R T}{V} = \frac{1.87065 \times 10^6}{0.005} = 3.7413 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \approx \textbf{374.1 MPa}
$$

**Comparison:** The Abel value exceeds the ideal-gas value by approximately **0.6 %** in this case. At higher densities (lower $V$ or larger $n$), the correction grows dramatically — for very dense detonation gases, deviations of **30–50 %** are typical, justifying the covolume correction.

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** State and explain the **Chapman–Jouguet condition**. Show that the C-J pressure for an ideal-gas detonation product with isentropic exponent $\gamma$ is given by $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For TNT with initial density $\rho_0 = 1630$ kg/m³, detonation velocity $D = 6930$ m/s, and $\gamma = 3.0$, compute the **C-J pressure** and **C-J particle velocity**, given the relation $u_{CJ} = D / (\gamma + 1)$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chapman–Jouguet condition and derivation:**

The **Chapman–Jouguet condition** states that the detonation velocity equals the local particle velocity plus the local sound speed:

$$
D = u + C
$$

This condition uniquely fixes the thermodynamic state at the C-J plane. Physically, the detonation front represents a **sonic point** for the burnt gas — a small pressure disturbance behind the front cannot overtake the shock.

Derivation:

1. Mass conservation:

$$
\rho_0 D = \rho (D - u)
$$

2. Momentum conservation:

$$
P - P_0 = \rho_0 D u
$$

3. Combine (1) and (2). With $P_0 \ll P$, eliminate $u$:

$$
u = \frac{P}{\rho_0 D}
$$

4. Sound speed in burnt gas (ideal, isentropic):

$$
C^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_S = \gamma \frac{P}{\rho}
$$

5. Apply C-J condition $D = u + C$. Eliminating $u$ via momentum and $C$ via the ideal sound-speed expression, and imposing the tangency of Rayleigh line with Hugoniot, one obtains:

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}
$$

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Given: $\rho_0 = 1630$ kg/m³, $D = 6930$ m/s, $\gamma = 3.0$.

**C-J pressure:**

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{1630 \times (6930)^2}{3 + 1}
$$

Step-by-step:

- $D^2 = 6930^2 = 4.80249 \times 10^7$ m²/s²
- $\rho_0 D^2 = 1630 \times 4.80249 \times 10^7 = 7.82806 \times 10^{10}$ Pa
- $P_{CJ} = 7.82806 \times 10^{10} \,/\, 4 = 1.957 \times 10^{10}$ Pa

$$
\boxed{P_{CJ} \approx 19.57 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**C-J particle velocity:**

$$
u_{CJ} = \frac{D}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{6930}{4} = 1732.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{u_{CJ} \approx 1732.5 \text{ m/s}}
$$

These values are in excellent agreement with experimental measurements of TNT at this density.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** of $R$ when $V$ is in litres, cm³, or m³. Always check: if $V$ is in m³, use $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.
> 2. **Dropping the covolume term** when applying Abel's EOS — this is the single most common error. Always write $V - \alpha$, never $V$ alone.
> 3. **Confusing $\gamma$ values:** For ideal gases, $\gamma = C_p / C_v \approx 1.4$ (diatomic); for detonation gases, $\gamma \approx 3$ is the **standard approximation**. Using 1.4 yields a grossly incorrect C-J pressure.
> 4. **Forgetting the C-J condition** when solving Rankine–Hugoniot — the EOS alone is insufficient; the C-J tangency is the closure condition.
> 5. **Mixing per-mole and per-mass forms:** Specify $R$ vs $\bar{R}$, $V$ vs $v$, and $n$ vs $m$ consistently throughout the calculation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **state of the detonation gas** is fully specified by the triplet $(P, T, V)$ — or equivalently $(P, \rho, T)$ — at the C-J plane.
- The **ideal-gas law fails** at detonation conditions; modified EOS (Abel, K-W, BKW, JWL) are mandatory.
- **Covolume** $\alpha$ = the excluded volume per mole of gas molecules; Abel's EOS replaces $V$ with $V - \alpha$.
- **Chapman–Jouguet condition** $D = u + C$ selects the unique, physical, minimum-entropy solution on the P–V plane.
- **C-J pressure estimate:** $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$ — with $\gamma \approx 3$ for detonation gases.
- **K–W EOS:** $PV / nR = 1 + x e^{\beta x}$, with $x = \kappa (V/T)^{\alpha}$ — calibrated per gas species.
- **BKW EOS:** $PV / RT = 1 + X e^{\beta X}$, with $X = \kappa V^{-1}(T + \theta)^{-\alpha}$ — Mader's high-fidelity library.
- **JWL EOS:** three-term isentropic expansion form used in cylinder-test fitting and hydrocodes.
- **Rankine–Hugoniot energy equation:** $E - E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}(P + P_0)(V_0 - V)$ — area under Rayleigh line equals internal-energy gain.
- **Mean molecular weight $\bar{M}$** and **specific gas constant $\bar{R} = R / \bar{M}$** convert between per-mole and per-mass formulations.
- **Numerical examples to remember:** TNT ($\rho_0 = 1630$ kg/m³) → $P_{CJ} \approx 19.6$ GPa, $u_{CJ} \approx 1732$ m/s; RDX ($\rho_0 = 1760$ kg/m³) → $P_{CJ} \approx 33$ GPa.
- **Always iterate** when solving coupled EOS + Hugoniot + C-J — closed-form solutions exist only for ideal-gas Abel with $\gamma =$ const.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interior Ballistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interior Ballistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interior Ballistics** is the branch of applied ballistics that deals with the phenomena occurring **inside the firearm or gun barrel** from the instant the **primer is struck** (initiation of combustion) to the instant the **projectile exits the muzzle**. It encompasses the study of propellant combustion, gas pressure generation, projectile motion, energy conversion, barrel dynamics, and recoil kinematics during the extremely short interval (typically **2 ms to 10 ms**) the projectile spends within the bore.

The discipline is fundamentally concerned with solving the **coupled thermodynamic-mechanical problem** of confined gas expansion accelerating a mass against friction and inertia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** Interior Ballistics is *the most assessment-heavy topic* in PESFT753 because it bridges theoretical combustion chemistry (explosives) with practical mechanical engineering (firearm design).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a heavy wooden block through a long, narrow, horizontal pipe using a rapidly inflating balloon fixed behind it. As you blow more air into the balloon, it expands and pushes the block forward. The faster the balloon inflates (analogous to **propellant burn rate**), the higher the pressure behind the block (analogous to **chamber pressure**), and the faster the block slides out the far end (analogous to **muzzle velocity**).

Now, replace the balloon with a **deflagrating explosive charge (propellant)** sealed in a strong steel cylinder, and the wooden block with a **bullet or shell**. That, in essence, is interior ballistics.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Peak chamber pressure (Pmax):** typically **300 MPa to 650 MPa** in modern military rifles, **200 MPa to 400 MPa** in pistols.
- **Muzzle velocity (v):** typically **300 m/s to 1800 m/s** depending on caliber and charge.
- **Time of travel in bore (t):** **0.002 s to 0.010 s** (2 to 10 ms).
- **Bore travel (L):** typically **0.3 m to 0.8 m**.
- **Specific gas constant for propellant gases (R):** ≈ **300 J/(kg·K)** to **350 J/(kg·K)**.
- **Covolume (α):** ≈ **0.001 m³/kg** (volume occupied by gas molecules themselves).

> [!NOTE]
> **Covolume (α):** The minimum volume that 1 kg of propellant gas cannot compress below, due to the physical volume of the gas molecules. A critical concept in Noble's equation.

## 1.4 The Four Sequential Phases of Interior Ballistics

1. **Phase I – Ignition & Pressure Build-up (0 to ~0.1 ms):** Primer ignites propellant; chamber pressure rises from atmospheric to ~50 MPa rapidly.
2. **Phase II – Pressure Peak & Acceleration (~0.1 ms to ~0.5 ms):** Combustion peaks; projectile begins moving; pressure reaches **Pmax**.
3. **Phase III – Expansion & Deceleration of Pressure Rise (~0.5 ms to ~5 ms):** Volume increases faster than gas generation; pressure falls steadily while projectile accelerates.
4. **Phase IV – Muzzle Exit & Blow-down:** Projectile leaves bore; remaining gases vent as muzzle blast.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physical Analysis

The interior ballistic problem is essentially a **three-way coupled system**:

- **(a) Chemical/Thermodynamic side:** Propellant burning releases gas.
- **(b) Mechanical side:** Expanding gas accelerates the projectile against friction.
- **(c) Kinematic side:** The projectile's motion changes chamber volume, which feeds back to pressure.

This feedback loop is what makes interior ballistics nonlinear and challenging.

## 2.2 The Three Governing Equations

### A. Equation of State of Propellant Gases (Noble's Equation)

Treating propellant gas as a real gas using a covolume correction:

$$p(V - \alpha \omega) = n R_g T = \frac{\omega}{M} R_u T = f \omega$$

- $p$ = gas pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = volume of chamber behind projectile (m³)
- $\alpha$ = covolume of propellant gases (m³/kg)
- $\omega$ = mass of propellant (kg)
- $f$ = **specific energy** or **force constant** of propellant (J/kg)
- $n$ = moles of gas, $R_g$ = specific gas constant, $M$ = molar mass, $R_u$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = temperature

### B. Force Balance on the Projectile (Newton's Second Law)

The gas pressure acts on the projectile's base area:

$$S p = m \frac{dv}{dt}$$

where:
- $S$ = cross-sectional bore area (m²)
- $m$ = projectile mass (kg)
- $v$ = projectile velocity (m/s)
- $t$ = time (s)

This can be re-expressed by eliminating time using $dt = dx / v$:

$$S p = m v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

### C. Rate of Gas Generation (Propellant Burn Rate)

A geometric burning grain follows **Vieille's Law**:

$$\frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{a \cdot p^n}{\rho_p}$$

- $z$ = thickness of burning web (m)
- $a$ = burn rate coefficient
- $n$ = pressure exponent (typically 0.7 to 1.0)
- $\rho_p$ = propellant density (kg/m³)

The mass burned is related to web thickness by grain geometry:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = A_g \rho_p \frac{dz}{dt}$$

where $A_g$ is the instantaneous burning surface area.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Noble's Equation (Real Gas) | $p(V-\alpha\omega)=f\omega$ | $p$[Pa], $V$[m³], $\alpha$[m³/kg], $\omega$[kg], $f$[J/kg] | $\omega > 0$ |
| 2 | Force on Projectile | $F = S \cdot p$ | $S$[m²], $p$[Pa] | $F > 0$ |
| 3 | Newton's 2nd Law | $S p = m \frac{dv}{dt}$ | $m$[kg], $v$[m/s], $t$[s] | $v \ge 0$ |
| 4 | Spatial Form | $S p = m v \frac{dv}{dx}$ | $x$[m] | $x \ge 0$ |
| 5 | Vieille's Burn Rate | $\dot{z} = a p^n / \rho_p$ | $a$[units vary], $n$[-] | $0.7 \le n \le 1.0$ |
| 6 | Mass Burn Rate | $\dot{\omega} = A_g \rho_p \dot{z}$ | $A_g$[m²] | $A_g \le A_{g,0}$ |
| 7 | Kinetic Energy of Projectile | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $E_k$[J] | Always $\ge 0$ |
| 8 | Energy Balance (Ideal, no losses) | $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = S \int_0^L p(x)\, dx$ | $L$[m] | Adiabatic |
| 9 | Average Pressure Approx. | $P_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2}(P_{max}+P_{muzzle})$ | [Pa] | Approx only |
| 10 | Muzzle Velocity (Energy) | $v_m = \sqrt{\tfrac{2 S}{m} \int_0^L p\, dx}$ | $v_m$[m/s] | Ideal |
| 11 | Recoil Velocity | $v_r = \tfrac{m v_m}{M_{gun}}$ | $M_{gun}$[kg] | Momentum balance |
| 12 | Specific Energy $f$ | $f = n R_g T$ | $f \approx 9 \times 10^5$ J/kg for nitrocellulose | Standard |
| 13 | Force Constant from $pV$ | $f = \frac{pV}{\omega}$ | empirical | Constant for given propellant |
| 14 | Time of Bore Travel | $t_b = \int_0^L \frac{dx}{v(x)}$ | $t_b$[s] | $t_b \approx 2{-}10$ ms |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The interior ballistic model is essential for:

- **Designing artillery and rifle cartridges** that achieve target muzzle velocity without exceeding the barrel's **proof pressure**.
- **Determining safe propellant charge mass** ($\omega$) for a given projectile mass and barrel strength.
- **Predicting recoil loads** for mounting systems and shoulder injuries.
- **Optimizing barrel length** (longer barrels give higher $v_m$ but diminishing returns after a certain $L$).
- **Designing propellant grain geometry** (single-perforated, multi-perforated, stick, ball, flake) to tailor the pressure-time curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Equation and Muzzle Velocity from Pressure–Distance Relation

**Starting point:** Newton's second law in spatial form for the projectile:

$$S p = m v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange** to isolate the pressure term and prepare for integration. Multiply both sides by $dx$:

$$S p \, dx = m v \, dv$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides** from the breech face (where $x = 0$, $v = 0$) to the muzzle (where $x = L$, $v = v_m$):

$$\int_0^L S p \, dx = \int_0^{v_m} m v \, dv$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the right-hand side** using standard integral $\int v \, dv = v^2/2$:

$$\int_0^{v_m} m v \, dv = \frac{1}{2} m v_m^2 - 0 = \frac{1}{2} m v_m^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back**, noting that $S$ is constant (bore cross-section):

$$S \int_0^L p \, dx = \frac{1}{2} m v_m^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for muzzle velocity** $v_m$:

$$\frac{1}{2} m v_m^2 = S \int_0^L p \, dx$$

$$v_m^2 = \frac{2 S}{m} \int_0^L p \, dx$$

$$\boxed{\,v_m = \sqrt{\frac{2 S}{m} \int_0^L p(x) \, dx}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** This is the *energy form*. The integral $\int_0^L p \, dx$ is the **average pressure × barrel length**, often denoted the *pressure area* or *impetus*. Therefore, to increase muzzle velocity, one may either:

- Increase average chamber pressure (raise $p$),
- Increase barrel length (raise $L$),
- Increase bore area (raise $S$), or
- Decrease projectile mass (lower $m$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of Noble's Equation from Ideal Gas with Covolume Correction

**Starting point:** For $n$ moles of ideal gas:

$$p V_{ideal} = n R_u T$$

**Step 1 — Recognize** that gas molecules have a finite physical volume, so the *available* volume for free motion is $V - \alpha \omega$, where $\alpha \omega$ is the volume physically occupied by the molecules of mass $\omega$.

**Step 2 — Substitute** the corrected free volume into the ideal gas law:

$$p (V - \alpha \omega) = n R_u T$$

**Step 3 — Express** the number of moles as $n = \omega / M$, where $M$ is the molar mass of the propellant gas:

$$p (V - \alpha \omega) = \frac{\omega}{M} R_u T = \omega \left(\frac{R_u}{M}\right) T$$

**Step 4 — Define** the **specific gas constant** $R_g = R_u / M$ and the **specific energy** or **force constant** $f = R_g T$:

$$p (V - \alpha \omega) = \omega f$$

$$\boxed{\,p = \frac{f \omega}{V - \alpha \omega}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering meaning:** The numerator $f \omega$ is the total potential chemical energy released as gas at temperature $T$. The denominator $V - \alpha \omega$ is the "free" gas volume. Higher charge mass $\omega$ or higher $f$ (hotter propellant) → higher pressure. Smaller chamber volume $V$ → higher pressure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Recoil Velocity from Momentum Conservation

**Starting point:** The system (gun + projectile) is initially at rest, so total momentum = 0.

**Step 1 — After firing**, the projectile has momentum $m v_m$ forward, and the gun has momentum $M_{gun} v_r$ backward.

**Step 2 — Apply** conservation of momentum:

$$m v_m + M_{gun} v_r = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve** for the recoil velocity $v_r$ of the gun:

$$M_{gun} v_r = -m v_m$$

$$\boxed{\,v_r = -\frac{m v_m}{M_{gun}}\,}$$

The negative sign indicates motion opposite to the projectile.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pressure–Time Curve Features

| Region | Approx. Pressure | Approx. Time | Controlling Physics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ignition | 0 → ~50 MPa | 0 – 0.1 ms | Primer + initial deflagration |
| Peak | $P_{max}$ (300 – 650 MPa) | 0.1 – 0.5 ms | Gas generation rate > volume increase |
| Plateau | ~70 % of $P_{max}$ | 0.5 – 3 ms | Gas generation ≈ volume increase |
| Tail | falls to ambient | 3 – 10 ms | Gas generation ended, expansion dominates |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Interior Ballistic Feedback System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **coupled feedback loops** that govern interior ballistics. This is a block-level functional architecture rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    primerA["PRIMER STRIKE"] --> igniteB["PROPELLANT IGNITION"]
    igniteB --> chemC["CHEMICAL ENERGY RELEASE"]
    chemC --> gasD["HOT GAS GENERATION"]
    gasD --> pressE["CHAMBER PRESSURE RISE"]
    pressE --> forceF["FORCE ON PROJECTILE BASE"]
    forceF --> accG["PROJECTILE ACCELERATION"]
    accG --> motionH["PROJECTILE MOTION IN BORE"]
    motionH --> volI["CHAMBER VOLUME INCREASE"]
    volI --> pressE
    pressE --> burnJ["PROPELLANT BURN RATE"]
    burnJ --> chemC
    motionH --> exitK["MUZZLE EXIT"]
    exitK --> endL["END OF INTERIOR BALLISTICS PHASE"]
    forceF --> fricM["FRICTION AND FORWARD LEAKAGE LOSSES"]
    fricM --> lossN["ENERGY DISSIPATION"]
    pressE --> wallO["BARREL WALL STRESS"]
    forceF --> recoilP["RECOIL FORCE ON GUN"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Pressure-Time Curve (Typical Rifle Round)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0 ms\nIGNITION"] --> t1["t = 0.1 ms\nPRESSURE RISING"]
    t1 --> t2["t = 0.3 ms\nPEAK Pmax"]
    t2 --> t3["t = 1 ms\nPLATEAU 0.7 Pmax"]
    t3 --> t4["t = 5 ms\nDECAY PHASE"]
    t4 --> t5["t = 8 ms\nMUZZLE EXIT"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Barrel Cross-Section (Logical Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    chamberA["CHAMBER\nHolds propellant\nVolume V0"] --> throatB["THROAT\nConnects chamber to bore"]
    throatB --> boreC["BORE\nDriven surface S\nLength L"]
    boreC --> muzzleD["MUZZLE\nExit plane\nVelocity vm"]
    chamberA -. "high p" .-> throatB
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams here are functional block-level schematics rather than physical CAD drawings. This is the recommended approach for KTU descriptive answers where hand-drawn sketches are not feasible.

---
