<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define interior ballistics. List its four sequential phases with approximate time durations.**

**Model Answer:**
Interior ballistics is the study of all phenomena occurring inside a firearm barrel from the instant of primer ignition to the instant the projectile exits the muzzle. The four phases are:

1. **Ignition & Pressure Build-up** (0 to ~0.1 ms): primer fires, propellant begins deflagrating, pressure rises from atmospheric to ~50 MPa.
2. **Pressure Peak & Acceleration** (~0.1 to ~0.5 ms): combustion peaks, projectile begins motion, pressure reaches $P_{max}$ of 300–650 MPa.
3. **Expansion & Decay** (~0.5 to ~5 ms): gas generation rate falls below volume expansion rate, pressure drops steadily while projectile continues to accelerate.
4. **Muzzle Blow-down** (~5 to ~10 ms): remaining gases vent as muzzle flash.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is covolume in Noble's equation? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
Covolume ($\alpha$) is the minimum volume that a unit mass of propellant gas cannot be compressed below, owing to the physical volume occupied by the gas molecules themselves. For propellant gases, $\alpha \approx 0.001$ m³/kg.

It is necessary because propellant gases at the high pressures (300–650 MPa) generated in firearms deviate significantly from ideal gas behaviour. The "free" volume available for molecular motion is $V - \alpha \omega$, not $V$. Neglecting $\alpha$ leads to grossly overestimated pressures at high densities. Noble's corrected equation $p(V - \alpha \omega) = f \omega$ accounts for this real-gas effect.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A very common error students make in interior ballistic derivations is treating the bore cross-section $S$ as variable (it is constant for a non-tapered barrel). Other frequently lost marks come from forgetting to specify units, confusing $f$ (specific energy) with $F$ (force), and omitting the integral form in the energy derivation. Always state the form of Noble's equation with all variables defined before substituting.

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

#### (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified] — 7 Marks
**Starting from Newton's second law, derive the expression for the muzzle velocity of a projectile in terms of the chamber pressure and barrel length.**

**Model Solution:**

The force on the projectile's base is $F = S \cdot p$, where $S$ is the bore cross-sectional area.

By Newton's second law:
$$S p = m \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Using the chain rule $\frac{dv}{dt} = v \frac{dv}{dx}$ (since $x$ is monotonic in $t$ for forward motion):
$$S p = m v \frac{dv}{dx}$$

Rearranging and multiplying by $dx$:
$$S p \, dx = m v \, dv$$

Integrating from the breech ($x = 0, v = 0$) to the muzzle ($x = L, v = v_m$):
$$S \int_0^L p \, dx = m \int_0^{v_m} v \, dv$$

Evaluating:
$$S \int_0^L p \, dx = \frac{1}{2} m v_m^2$$

Solving for $v_m$:
$$\boxed{v_m = \sqrt{\frac{2 S}{m} \int_0^L p \, dx}}$$

This is the *energy form* of the muzzle velocity. If pressure were uniform at $p_{avg}$:
$$v_m \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 S L \, p_{avg}}{m}}$$

#### (b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] — 7 Marks
**A rifle has bore area $S = 7.85 \times 10^{-5}$ m² (caliber 10 mm), barrel length $L = 0.6$ m, projectile mass $m = 0.012$ kg, and average chamber pressure $p_{avg} = 2.5 \times 10^8$ Pa. Calculate the muzzle velocity and the kinetic energy of the projectile.**

**Model Solution:**

Using the uniform-pressure approximation:
$$v_m = \sqrt{\frac{2 S L \, p_{avg}}{m}}$$

Substituting:
$$v_m = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.6 \times 2.5 \times 10^8}{0.012}}$$

Numerator: $2 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.6 \times 2.5 \times 10^8$
$= 2 \times 7.85 \times 0.6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{(-5+8)}$
$= 2 \times 7.85 \times 1.5 \times 10^3$
$= 2 \times 11775$
$= 23550$

Therefore:
$$v_m = \sqrt{\frac{23550}{0.012}} = \sqrt{1.9625 \times 10^6} \approx 1400.9 \text{ m/s}$$

Kinetic energy:
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_m^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.012 \times (1400.9)^2$$
$$= 0.006 \times 1.9625 \times 10^6 \approx 11775 \text{ J} \approx 11.78 \text{ kJ}$$

**Answer:** $v_m \approx 1401$ m/s; $E_k \approx 11.78$ kJ.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

#### (a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified] — 7 Marks
**State and explain Noble's equation for propellant gases. Define all terms and discuss the role of the covolume.**

**Model Solution:**

Noble's equation for propellant gases is:
$$p(V - \alpha \omega) = f \omega$$

Or equivalently:
$$p = \frac{f \omega}{V - \alpha \omega}$$

**Definitions:**
- $p$ = pressure of gas (Pa)
- $V$ = total volume behind the projectile (m³)
- $\alpha$ = covolume (m³/kg) — the volume per unit mass intrinsically occupied by gas molecules
- $\omega$ = mass of propellant gas (kg)
- $f$ = force constant or specific energy (J/kg), given by $f = n R_g T = R_u T / M$

**Role of covolume:** At the very high pressures developed in firearms (300–650 MPa), the ideal gas law $pV = nRT$ underestimates pressure significantly because the molecules themselves occupy non-negligible volume. The "free" volume for molecular motion is the total volume minus the covolume term $\alpha \omega$. As $V \to \alpha \omega$, pressure $p \to \infty$, mimicking real-gas behaviour where gas becomes incompressible.

**Why covolume cannot be neglected:** For a typical rifle charge, $\alpha \omega$ can be 5–15 % of $V$. Ignoring it causes large errors in predicted $P_{max}$.

#### (b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 7 Marks
**A 12-gauge shotgun shell uses $\omega = 0.025$ kg of propellant with force constant $f = 9.5 \times 10^5$ J/kg and covolume $\alpha = 9.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/kg. The chamber-plus-bore volume behind the wad at peak pressure is $V = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m³. Calculate the peak pressure.**

**Model Solution:**

Using Noble's equation:
$$p = \frac{f \omega}{V - \alpha \omega}$$

Compute the covolume term:
$$\alpha \omega = 9.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 = 2.375 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

Free volume:
$$V - \alpha \omega = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} - 2.375 \times 10^{-5} = 1.625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

Numerator:
$$f \omega = 9.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.025 = 23750 \text{ J}$$

Pressure:
$$p = \frac{23750}{1.625 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.4615 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.46 \text{ GPa}$$

**Note:** This value exceeds safe chamber pressure (typical limit ~70 MPa). The result indicates the calculation is unrealistic for a 12-gauge shell — students should interpret by comparing with realistic charge masses; in practice only a fraction of $\omega$ is converted to gas at peak, and the calculation requires correction. **Always cross-check against realistic propellant charge data.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interior ballistics** spans only ~2 to ~10 ms but involves peak pressures of **300–650 MPa**.
- The four phases are: **Ignition → Peak → Plateau → Blow-down**.
- The three governing equations are:
  - **Noble's equation** (gas state): $p(V-\alpha\omega)=f\omega$
  - **Force balance:** $Sp = mv \, dv/dx$
  - **Burn rate (Vieille):** $\dot{z} = a p^n / \rho_p$
- **Covolume ($\alpha$)** is essential — never set it to zero in real problems.
- **Specific energy $f$** for typical nitrocellulose propellants: $\approx 9 \times 10^5$ to $1.1 \times 10^6$ J/kg.
- Muzzle velocity depends on $\sqrt{(SL/m)}$ — design lever.
- Recoil velocity: $v_r = -m v_m / M_{gun}$ (momentum balance, sign indicates opposite direction).
- **Burn rate exponent $n$**: $n < 1$ gives progressive burning (pressure falls as web burns — desirable).
- Grain geometry (single-perforated, multi-perforated, ball, flake) controls $A_g(t)$ and hence $p(t)$.
- Barrel length gives diminishing returns: doubling $L$ does NOT double $v_m$ because average $p$ decreases.
- Always **define variables, state units, and box final answers** in KTU answers.
- Expect KTU questions on **derivation of energy equation, Noble's equation, recoil, and numerical problems** involving $v_m$ or $P_{max}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound, Shock and Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sound, Shock and Detonation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔊 Sound, Shock and Detonation — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Sound — The Gentle Pressure Pulse

**Sound** is a mechanical, longitudinal pressure wave that propagates through an elastic medium by means of small, adiabatic compressions and rarefactions. In the context of explosives engineering, sound is the *subsonic* baseline against which all violent energy-release phenomena are measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A *sound wave* is a small-amplitude pressure disturbance in a fluid for which the fractional change in density ($\Delta\rho / \rho_0$) is much less than unity, and the propagation velocity equals the **local speed of sound** $C_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$ in that medium.

The **speed of sound in air at standard sea-level conditions** is a fixed physical constant:

$$C_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\gamma\,R\,T_0} \;\approx\; \mathbf{340.29 \; m/s}$$

For a typical rock medium (used in blasting engineering), longitudinal wave speeds lie in the range of **$3\,000$ – $6\,000 \; m/s$**, depending on density, porosity and jointing.

### 🌊 Conceptual Analogy — The Pebble in a Pond

Imagine tossing a small pebble into a still pond. The water surface oscillates gently; the wave spreads out radially, weakens with distance, and travels at the natural speed governed by gravity and surface tension. Now imagine *throwing a brick* into the same pond — you get a sharp, fast, leading edge of disturbed water (a *shock*), followed by a turbulent wake. **Sound is the pebble; shock is the brick.** A detonation is what happens when the brick is itself made of high explosive.

## 1.2 Shock Wave — The Supersonic Discontinuity

A **shock wave** is a propagating surface across which the thermodynamic state of the medium (pressure, density, temperature, particle velocity, entropy) changes *discontinuously*. By definition, the shock front travels at a speed **greater than the local speed of sound** ahead of it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Sound:**
> - *Sound*: continuous, reversible, isentropic, infinitesimal amplitude, travels at $C_0$.
> - *Shock*: discontinuous, irreversible (entropy rises), finite amplitude, travels at $U_s > C_0$.

The ratio $\mathbf{M} = U_s / C_0$ is called the **Mach number of the shock** and is the central dimensionless parameter used to classify disturbances in air, water or rock.

## 1.3 Detonation — Self-Sustained Supersonic Combustion with Shocks

A **detonation** is a self-propagating reactive shock wave in which the leading shock front compresses and heats the unreacted explosive to a temperature at which chemical reaction occurs within a reaction zone of finite thickness (typically $\sim 0.1 - 1.0 \; mm$). The energy released sustains the front without external input.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard Definition (PESFT753):**
> *Detonation is the rapid, self-sustaining, supersonic propagation of a reaction zone driven by a coupled shock front, in which the chemical reaction and the shock wave form a self-organising, hydrodynamically stable structure known as the ZND (Zel'dovich–von Neumann–Döring) wave.*

### 🔥 Detonation vs Deflagration — A Critical Contrast

| Property | Deflagration | Detonation |
|----------|--------------|------------|
| Reaction-front velocity | $10^{-3} - 10^{1} \; m/s$ (subsonic) | $1\,500 - 9\,500 \; m/s$ (supersonic) |
| Driving mechanism | Heat conduction & mass diffusion | Hydrodynamic shock compression |
| Pressure at front | Near atmospheric | $10 - 40 \; GPa$ |
| Density jump | Negligible | Discontinuous ($\rho_2 / \rho_1 \approx 1.4 - 1.7$) |
| Brisance (shattering) | Low | Extremely high |
| TNT equivalent typical | $0.1 - 0.3$ | $\mathbf{1.0}$ (reference) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brisance** is the shattering power of an explosive — directly proportional to detonation pressure $P_{CJ}$ — and is the single most important practical property for rock blasting, demolition and warhead design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mach Number — Universal Classifier of Disturbances

The **Mach number** is the most important dimensionless parameter in compressible-flow and explosion physics:

$$\mathbf{M} \;=\; \frac{U}{C_0} \;=\; \frac{\text{flow or front velocity}}{\text{local sound speed}}$$

The four Mach regimes form the conceptual backbone of the entire module:

| Regime | Mach Range | Physical Character | Example |
|--------|------------|--------------------|---------|
| **Subsonic** | $0 \le M < 1$ | Disturbances outrun the flow; pressure signals propagate upstream | Airflow around a slow car |
| **Sonic** | $M = 1$ exactly | Local flow reaches $C_0$ | Throat of converging nozzle |
| **Supersonic** | $1 < M < 5$ | Mach cone forms; shock is oblique | Jet fighter, rifle bullet |
| **Hypersonic** | $M > 5$ | Shock lies very close to body; intense heating | Re-entry vehicle, shaped-charge jet |

For a stationary observer, an explosion source is **supersonic** in air when its expansion velocity $U > 340 \; m/s$.

## 2.2 The Rankine–Hugoniot Jump Relations

Across any 1-D shock or detonation front, three conservation laws must hold — **mass, momentum, and energy** — leading to the **Rankine–Hugoniot (RH) relations**.

> [!NOTE]
> These relations are the *thermodynamic backbone* of detonation theory. Every practical equation (Chapman–Jouguet pressure, detonation velocity, TNT equivalent) is derived from them.

Let subscript $1$ denote the unshocked (upstream) state and $2$ the shocked (downstream) state. For a front moving at velocity $U_s$ into quiescent medium:

$$\rho_1\,(U_s - u_1) \;=\; \rho_2\,(U_s - u_2) \quad \text{(mass)}$$

$$P_1 + \rho_1(U_s - u_1)^2 \;=\; P_2 + \rho_2(U_s - u_2)^2 \quad \text{(momentum)}$$

$$h_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}(U_s - u_1)^2 \;=\; h_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(U_s - u_2)^2 \quad \text{(energy)}$$

These three equations, combined with the equation of state, fully determine the downstream state of a *non-reactive* shock.

## 2.3 The Rayleigh Line and the Hugoniot Curve

For a 1-D steady shock, eliminating $u_2$ between the mass and momentum equations yields the famous **Rayleigh line equation**:

$$P_2 - P_1 \;=\; -\rho_1^{\,2}\,(U_s - u_1)^2\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_2} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_1}\right)$$

This is a straight line in the $(P, 1/\rho)$ plane. The locus of all possible downstream states that satisfy conservation of mass and momentum is the **Hugoniot curve**, defined implicitly by:

$$h_2 - h_1 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,(P_2 - P_1)\,\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The intersection of the Rayleigh line and the Hugoniot curve gives the physically realised shock state.** For a reactive shock (detonation), an additional *detonation Hugoniot* shifted upward by the heat of reaction $Q$ replaces the inert Hugoniot.

## 2.4 Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) Condition

The CJ condition is the **stability criterion** that uniquely selects the operating point of a self-sustained detonation:

> [!NOTE]
> **CJ Condition:** At the sonic plane (end of reaction zone), the flow velocity relative to the front equals the local speed of sound, i.e. $\mathbf{M_{rel} = 1}$. Equivalently, the Rayleigh line is *tangent* to the detonation Hugoniot at the CJ point.

This tangency selects **one unique value** of detonation velocity $D_{CJ}$ for a given explosive composition — the value that is observed experimentally.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU problem in this module is solved from one of these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Speed of sound (ideal gas) | $C_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$ | m/s | $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats, $R$ = specific gas constant |
| 2 | Mach number | $M = U / C_0$ | dimensionless | Universal classifier |
| 3 | Particle velocity (behind strong shock) | $u_2 = \dfrac{2\,C_0}{\gamma+1}\left(M_s - \dfrac{1}{M_s}\right)$ | m/s | Useful for blast-damage analysis |
| 4 | Pressure ratio across shock | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{2\gamma M_s^{2} - (\gamma - 1)}{\gamma + 1}$ | dimensionless | Hugoniot relation for $\gamma$-law gas |
| 5 | Density ratio across shock | $\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \dfrac{(\gamma + 1) M_s^{2}}{(\gamma - 1) M_s^{2} + 2}$ | dimensionless | Maximum $= (\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)$ as $M_s \to \infty$ |
| 6 | Temperature ratio across shock | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{\left[2\gamma M_s^{2} - (\gamma-1)\right]\left[(\gamma-1)M_s^{2} + 2\right]}{(\gamma+1)^{2} M_s^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Used in blast-thermal analysis |
| 7 | CJ Detonation pressure | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0\, D_{CJ}^{\,2}}{\gamma + 1}$ | Pa | $D_{CJ}$ = CJ detonation velocity |
| 8 | CJ Particle velocity | $u_{CJ} = \dfrac{D_{CJ}}{\gamma + 1}$ | m/s | $u_{CJ} = C_0$ at CJ point |
| 9 | CJ Detonation velocity (simplified) | $D_{CJ} \approx \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2}-1)\,Q}$ | m/s | $Q$ = chemical energy per unit mass (J/kg) |
| 10 | TNT equivalent mass | $m_{TNT} = m_{exp} \cdot \dfrac{Q_{exp}}{Q_{TNT}}$ | kg | $Q_{TNT} \approx 4.184 \; MJ/kg$ |
| 11 | TNT equivalent (overpressure method) | $m_{TNT} = \dfrac{Z_{exp}}{Z_{TNT}} \cdot m_{exp}$ | kg | $Z$ = scaled distance $R / m^{1/3}$ |
| 12 | Hopkinson–Cranz scaled distance | $Z = R \,/\, m_{TNT}^{\,1/3}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | Kingery–Bulmash blast curves |
| 13 | Brisance (Brisanz value) | $B = \dfrac{P_{CJ} \cdot V_{CJ}}{2}$ | J | Indirectly $\propto D_{CJ}^{\,3}$ |
| 14 | Acoustic impedance (explosive–rock) | $Z_a = \rho_0 \, C_0$ | kg/(m²·s) | Determines shock transmission into rock |
| 15 | Polytropic gas constant | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | dimensionless | $\gamma_{products} \approx 1.2 - 1.4$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Brisance is *not* the same as total energy. It measures the **rate** of energy release, dominated by the **detonation pressure** $P_{CJ}$. A slower explosive with more total energy can have *less* brisance.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Civil blasting** uses $P_{CJ}$ and $D_{CJ}$ to predict rock fragmentation and ground vibration (PPV).
- **Tunnelling & mining** applies the Hopkinson–Cranz scaling to set safe charge-per-delay limits.
- **Demolition engineering** uses TNT-equivalent scaling to design safe stand-off distances.
- **Defence / warhead design** maximises $P_{CJ}$ for shaped-charge jets and hypervelocity impacts.
- **Process safety** uses acoustic-impedance matching ($Z_a = \rho_0 C_0$) to predict sympathetic detonation between adjacent explosive charges.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exhaustive**. No algebraic transition is omitted. Each line of mathematics is followed by a plain-English explanation of the *physical and logical reason* for that step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas

**Starting point:** Newton's second law applied to a small adiabatic compression of an ideal gas.

Consider a slab of gas of equilibrium density $\rho_0$, pressure $P_0$ and temperature $T_0$. A piston is moved inward by an infinitesimal amount $dx$, producing a compression $dV$. By Newton's second law, the restoring pressure force per unit area $dP$ accelerates the slab:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} \;=\; -\rho_0 \,\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$$

Because the disturbance is small, the process is **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic), so $dP = C_0^{2}\, d\rho$ where $C_0^{2} \equiv (\partial P / \partial \rho)_s$ is the square of the speed of sound by definition.

> **Reasoning:** For an infinitesimal sound wave, the entropy change is of second order and can be neglected; hence the *isentropic* derivative $(\partial P/\partial\rho)_s$ is the relevant compressibility.

Combined with the 1-D wave equation $\partial^{2}x/\partial t^{2} = C_0^{2}\, \partial^{2}x/\partial x^{2}$, the standard wave solution $x = f(x \pm C_0 t)$ confirms that disturbances propagate at speed $C_0$.

For an ideal gas undergoing isentropic change, $P V^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, hence $P \propto \rho^{\gamma}$ and $(\partial P / \partial \rho)_s = \gamma P / \rho$. Substituting $P = \rho R T$:

$$C_0^{\,2} \;=\; \gamma\,\frac{P_0}{\rho_0} \;=\; \gamma\,R\,T_0$$

$$\boxed{\,C_0 \;=\; \sqrt{\gamma\,R\,T_0}\,}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Sound travels faster in hot gases ($\propto \sqrt{T}$) and in gases with larger $\gamma$ (monoatomic $\gamma=1.67$ vs diatomic $\gamma=1.4$). At $T_0 = 288.15 \; K$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \; J/(kg\cdot K)$ for air, this gives $C_0 = \mathbf{340.3 \; m/s}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rankine–Hugoniot Relations from Control-Volume Analysis

**Setup:** A 1-D shock propagates at velocity $U_s$ into a fluid initially at rest ($u_1 = 0$). We attach a control volume moving with the shock so that the flow is *steady* in the shock frame.

In the shock frame, fluid approaches at velocity $(U_s - u_1) = U_s$ and leaves at $(U_s - u_2)$. Mass, momentum and energy are conserved across the (infinitely thin) front.

### Step 1 — Conservation of Mass

$$\rho_1\, U_s \;=\; \rho_2\,(U_s - u_2)$$

> **Reasoning:** Mass in = mass out. The mass flux entering per unit area is $\rho_1 U_s$, the mass flux leaving is $\rho_2 (U_s - u_2)$. Equating them gives a relation linking upstream and downstream densities with particle velocity.

Solving for $U_s$:

$$U_s \;=\; \frac{\rho_2\, u_2}{\rho_2 - \rho_1}$$

### Step 2 — Conservation of Momentum

Apply Newton's second law to the control volume: the net pressure force equals the rate of change of momentum flux:

$$P_1 - P_2 \;=\; \rho_1\, U_s^{2} - \rho_2\,(U_s - u_2)^{2}$$

> **Reasoning:** The pressure $P_1$ on the upstream face pushes fluid into the shock; the pressure $P_2$ on the downstream face pushes back. The net force accelerates fluid from velocity $U_s$ to $(U_s - u_2)$.

Using the mass-conservation relation to eliminate $U_s$ and $u_2$:

$$P_2 - P_1 \;=\; \rho_1\, U_s\, u_2 \;=\; \rho_2\,(U_s - u_2)\,u_2$$

$$\boxed{\,P_2 - P_1 \;=\; \rho_1\, U_s\, u_2\,}$$

This is the fundamental **shock momentum equation**.

### Step 3 — Conservation of Energy (steady-flow first law)

For a steady, adiabatic (no heat transfer across the shock — only dissipation inside it), the stagnation enthalpy is conserved:

$$h_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}U_s^{\,2} \;=\; h_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(U_s - u_2)^{2}$$

> **Reasoning:** A shock is adiabatic because the front is too thin for heat conduction to occur on the timescale of passage. Therefore the total specific enthalpy (sensible + kinetic) is the same upstream and downstream.

Expanding $(U_s - u_2)^{2} = U_s^{2} - 2 U_s u_2 + u_2^{2}$:

$$h_2 - h_1 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,U_s^{\,2} - \tfrac{1}{2}(U_s - u_2)^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,u_2\,(2 U_s - u_2) \;=\; U_s u_2 - \tfrac{1}{2}u_2^{2}$$

Eliminating $U_s u_2$ using the momentum equation $U_s u_2 = (P_2 - P_1)/\rho_1$:

$$h_2 - h_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho_1} - \frac{u_2^{2}}{2}$$

But from the mass equation, $u_2 = U_s (\rho_2 - \rho_1)/\rho_2 = (P_2 - P_1) (\rho_2 - \rho_1)/(\rho_1 \rho_2 U_s)$… A cleaner route is to eliminate $u_2$ using the identity derived from the Rayleigh line. The final symmetric form of the **Hugoniot energy equation** is:

$$\boxed{\,h_2 - h_1 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,(P_2 - P_1)\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}\right)\,}$$

> **Reasoning:** This equation contains *no velocities*. It links the thermodynamic states $(P_1, \rho_1)$ and $(P_2, \rho_2)$ through a constant-energy curve — the Hugoniot locus.

### Step 4 — Pressure and Density Ratios for a Calorically Perfect Gas

For an ideal gas with $h = c_p T = \gamma P/((\gamma-1)\rho)$, substituting into the Hugoniot energy equation and using the momentum equation yields, after algebraic reduction (see e.g. standard compressible-flow texts):

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \;=\; \frac{2\gamma\, M_s^{2} - (\gamma-1)}{\gamma + 1}$$

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} \;=\; \frac{(\gamma+1)\, M_s^{2}}{(\gamma-1)\, M_s^{2} + 2}$$

where $M_s = U_s/C_0$ is the shock Mach number.

> **Reasoning:** The pressure ratio increases without bound as $M_s \to \infty$, but the density ratio saturates at the *finite* limit $(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)$ (which equals 6 for $\gamma=1.4$). This is why strong shocks in air compress the gas to at most $\sim 6$ times ambient density — a counter-intuitive but experimentally verified result.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chapman–Jouguet Detonation Velocity

**Setup:** A detonation has, behind the leading shock, a reaction zone in which chemical energy $Q$ per unit mass is released. The detonation Hugoniot is the *shifted* inert Hugoniot:

$$h_2 - h_1 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,(P_2 - P_1)\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_2}\right) \;+\; Q$$

The CJ condition (Rayleigh line tangent to detonation Hugoniot at the CJ point) gives, via standard thermodynamic differentiation:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial (1/\rho)}\right)_{\!Hugoniot} \;=\; -\rho^{2}\,C_{CJ}^{\,2} \;=\; \text{slope of Rayleigh line}$$

This tangency condition, applied to a $\gamma$-law product gas, yields the famous **CJ similarity solution**:

$$u_{CJ} \;=\; \frac{D_{CJ}}{\gamma + 1}$$

$$P_{CJ} \;=\; \frac{\rho_0\,D_{CJ}^{\,2}}{\gamma + 1}$$

$$\boxed{\,D_{CJ} \;=\; \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2} - 1)\,Q}\,}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Higher heat of reaction $Q$ gives higher detonation velocity ($\propto \sqrt{Q}$). Heavier product molecules (lower $\gamma$) reduce $D_{CJ}$. These two opposing trends explain why aluminised explosives (high $Q$ but heavy Al$_2$O$_3$ product) do not always give the highest velocity, even though they release more total energy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — TNT Equivalent via Hopkinson–Cranz Scaling

**Setup:** Two explosions of different masses and energies produce the same overpressure at the same scaled distance $Z = R/m^{1/3}$.

**Step 1 — Define scaled distance:**

$$Z \;=\; \frac{R}{m_{TNT}^{\,1/3}} \quad [m/kg^{1/3}]$$

> **Reasoning:** The Hopkinson–Cranz (or Sachs) scaling law states that the blast wave from a *TNT* charge of mass $m$ at distance $R$ is geometrically and dynamically similar to a charge of $k^{3} m$ at distance $kR$. This is the empirical foundation of all standardised blast-curve predictions (Kingery–Bulmash, TM5-1300).

**Step 2 — Equate overpressure of the test explosive to that of TNT:**

For the same overpressure $\Delta P$ at the same scaled distance $Z$, the mass of TNT that produces the same blast is:

$$m_{TNT} \;=\; m_{exp} \cdot \frac{Q_{exp}}{Q_{TNT}}$$

where $Q_{TNT} = 4.184 \; MJ/kg$ is the standard reference value.

> **Reasoning:** Blast effects scale with the total thermodynamic work delivered to the surrounding atmosphere, which for a *fully-coupled* air blast is proportional to $m \cdot Q$. A charge with twice the energy per kg therefore has twice the TNT equivalent mass.

**Step 3 — Practical note on overpressure method:**

For a *partially-coupled* or *reflected* blast (e.g. blast at the ground surface, hemispherical charge), the formula becomes:

$$m_{TNT,\text{eff}} \;=\; \eta \cdot m_{exp} \cdot \frac{Q_{exp}}{Q_{TNT}}$$

where $\eta \approx 1.8$ is the standard hemispherical ground-burst efficiency factor.

$$\boxed{\,m_{TNT} \;=\; \eta \cdot m_{exp} \cdot \frac{Q_{exp}}{4.184 \; MJ/kg}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 50 kg cylindrical charge of a booster explosive (heat of reaction $Q = 5.20 \; MJ/kg$, $\gamma = 1.30$ for product gases, $\rho_0 = 1\,650 \; kg/m^{3}$) detonates in air. Find:

(a) Detonation velocity $D_{CJ}$
(b) Detonation pressure $P_{CJ}$
(c) TNT equivalent mass

**Solution:**

(a) Using $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2} - 1) Q}$:

$$D_{CJ} \;=\; \sqrt{2(1.30^{2} - 1)(5.20 \times 10^{6})}$$

$$D_{CJ} \;=\; \sqrt{2(0.69)(5.20 \times 10^{6})} \;=\; \sqrt{7.176 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 2\,679 \; m/s$$

(b) Using $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D_{CJ}^{\,2}/(\gamma + 1)$:

$$P_{CJ} \;=\; \frac{1\,650 \times (2\,679)^{2}}{2.30} \;=\; \frac{1\,650 \times 7.18 \times 10^{6}}{2.30}$$

$$P_{CJ} \;=\; \frac{1.184 \times 10^{10}}{2.30} \;=\; 5.15 \times 10^{9} \; Pa \;\approx\; 5.15 \; GPa$$

(c) TNT equivalent:

$$m_{TNT} \;=\; m_{exp} \cdot \frac{Q_{exp}}{Q_{TNT}} \;=\; 50 \times \frac{5.20}{4.184} \;=\; 62.1 \; kg \; TNT$$

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below use **Mermaid block-level functional flow** syntax. All node IDs are alphanumeric-only, labels contain raw uppercase text with no embedded markdown formatting, and the syntax is fully compatible with the standard Mermaid renderer.

## 4.1 Disturbance Classification by Mach Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Disturbance in Fluid Medium] --> B{Mach Number M}
    B -->|M is less than 1| C[SUBSONIC REGIME]
    B -->|M equals 1| D[SONIC REGIME]
    B -->|1 less than M less than 5| E[SUPERSONIC REGIME]
    B -->|M greater than 5| F[HYPERSONIC REGIME]

    C --> C1[Smooth pressure variations, signals travel upstream, no shock, example subsonic aircraft]
    D --> D1[Local Mach equals 1, choked flow, example nozzle throat]
    E --> E1[Mach cone forms, oblique or normal shock, example rifle bullet]
    F --> F1[Shock detached close to body, strong gas heating, example reentry vehicle]

    C1 --> G[Energy released as SOUND and HEAT]
    D1 --> H[Critical limit, transition to shock]
    E1 --> I[Energy released as SHOCK WAVE with entropy rise]
    F1 --> J[Strong shock with ionisation and radiation]

    G --> K[Injury and damage mode: auditory and thermal]
    H --> L[Deflagration detonation transition point]
    I --> M[Primary damage: brisant fracture and blast overpressure]
    J --> N[Extreme damage: hypervelocity impact and shaped charge jet]
```

## 4.2 Detonation Wave Structure — ZND Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Unreacted explosive at P0 rho0 u0] -->|Shock jump| B1[Von Neumann spike at PVNM rhoVNM uVNM]
    B1 -->|Chemical reaction zone, finite thickness| C1[Reaction zone, pressure falls, density rises]
    C1 -->|CJ plane, local Mach equals 1| D1[Chapman Jouguet state at PCJ rhoCJ uCJ]
    D1 -->|Taylor expansion fan| E1[Expansion flow to ambient P0 behind wave]
    E1 --> F1[Detonation products, subsonic in lab frame]

    A1 --> G1[Initial temperature T0]
    G1 --> H1[Shock compression heats to thousands of K]
    H1 --> I1[Ignition delay tau_ign]
    I1 --> J1[Exothermic reaction releases Q J per kg]
    J1 --> K1[Sustains shock and reaction zone]
    K1 --> L1[Stable self propagating ZND detonation]
```

## 4.3 Hugoniot Curve and CJ Operating Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State P1 rho1 upstream] --> B[Apply mass and momentum conservation]
    B --> C[Rayleigh Line, straight line in P and 1 over rho plane]
    B --> D[Hugoniot Curve, locus of all possible downstream states]

    C --> E[Slope depends on shock velocity Us]
    D --> F[Shape depends on equation of state and heat release Q]

    D --> G[Inert Hugoniot, Q equals 0]
    D --> H[Detonation Hugoniot, shifted upward by Q]

    H --> I[Rayleigh line intersects Hugoniot at TWO points]
    I --> J[Lower point: strong detonation, unstable]
    I --> K[Upper point: weak detonation, unstable]
    I --> L[CJ point: Rayleigh line TANGENT to Hugoniot, STABLE, unique]
    L --> M[This selects D_CJ uniquely, matches experiment]
    L --> N[Operating state: sonic in shock frame, subsonic in lab frame behind]
```

## 4.4 Sound versus Shock versus Detonation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Release Event] --> B{Magnitude of Pressure Jump}
    B -->|dP over P0 less than 0.01| C[SOUND WAVE]
    B -->|dP over P0 around 0.1 to 1.0| D[COMPRESSION WAVE]
    B -->|dP over P0 much greater than 1| E[SHOCK WAVE]
    B -->|Energy released as chemistry, sustained by shock| F[DETONATION]

    C --> C1[Isentropic, reversible, no entropy change, M less than 1.1]
    D --> D1[Weakly non linear, steepening, M around 1.0 to 1.5]
    E --> E1[Discontinuous front, entropy rises, M greater than 1.5, no reaction]
    F --> F1[Reactive shock, ZND structure, M greater than 5 typically]

    F1 --> G1[Deflagration to Detonation Transition DDT in confined geometry]
    G1 --> H1[Turbulent flame acceleration and shock formation]
    H1 --> I1[Run up distance of order 0.1 to 1 m in gases]
    I1 --> J1[Full detonation established]
    J1 --> K1[Industrial hazard, primary safety concern in process plants]
```

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions test direct recall and definition-level understanding. Answers should be 3–5 sentences with one relevant formula.

### Question 1. Define Mach number. Classify the four Mach regimes with one engineering example each.

**Model Answer:**

The **Mach number** is the dimensionless ratio of a flow or front velocity $U$ to the local speed of sound $C_0$ in the medium: $M = U / C_0$. It is the universal classifier of compressible disturbances.

| Regime | Range | Example |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Subsonic | $0 \le M < 1$ | Civilian aircraft cruising at $M \approx 0.8$ |
| Sonic | $M = 1$ | Throat of a converging–diverging rocket nozzle |
| Supersonic | $1 < M < 5$ | Rifle bullet, $M \approx 2-3$ |
| Hypersonic | $M > 5$ | Re-entry vehicle, $M \approx 15-25$ |

> **Examiner's tip:** Always state the defining formula $M = U/C_0$ first. Marks are split: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the regime table with examples.

### Question 2. Differentiate between detonation and deflagration. Why is this distinction critical for explosives safety?

**Model Answer:**

A **deflagration** is a subsonic reaction ($v \sim 0.001 - 10 \; m/s$) driven by heat and mass diffusion at near-atmospheric pressure. A **detonation** is a supersonic reactive shock ($v \sim 1\,500 - 9\,500 \; m/s$) driven by hydrodynamic compression, with reaction-zone pressures of $10 - 40 \; GPa$.

The distinction is **safety-critical** because:

1. A deflagration can transition to a detonation (DDT) in confined geometry, producing a sudden, order-of-magnitude increase in pressure and damage.
2. Storage, transport and handling regulations for deflagrating materials differ markedly from those for detonating explosives.
3. Blast-damage prediction (using TNT-equivalent scaling) is only valid in the detonation regime; deflagration requires separate thermal-radiation models.

> **Examiner's tip:** State at least one numerical contrast. Many students lose the third mark by giving only qualitative answers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has internal choice between (a) OR (b). We provide **two fully independent alternatives** below. Every sub-question has a complete model solution.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Theoretical Depth on Sound and Shock*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Rankine–Hugoniot relations for a 1-D steady shock wave propagating into a fluid at rest, starting from the conservation of mass, momentum and energy. State the final forms of the three equations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a strong shock in air ($\gamma = 1.4$) with shock Mach number $M_s = 3.0$, calculate the post-shock (i) pressure ratio, (ii) density ratio, (iii) temperature ratio, and (iv) particle velocity. Take ambient conditions as $P_1 = 101.325 \; kPa$, $T_1 = 288.15 \; K$, $C_0 = 340.3 \; m/s$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.2** above. The expected 7-mark model answer should contain:

- **Statement of control volume** (1 mark): "A 1-D shock is steady in its own reference frame; mass, momentum and energy fluxes are conserved across the infinitely thin front."
- **Mass equation** (1 mark): $\rho_1 U_s = \rho_2 (U_s - u_2)$.
- **Momentum equation** (2 marks): $P_1 + \rho_1 U_s^{2} = P_2 + \rho_2 (U_s - u_2)^{2}$, then reduction to $P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 U_s u_2$.
- **Energy equation** (2 marks): $h_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}U_s^{2} = h_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(U_s - u_2)^{2}$, then reduction to $h_2 - h_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(P_2 - P_1)(1/\rho_1 + 1/\rho_2)$.
- **Final boxed forms** (1 mark): All three Rankine–Hugoniot equations presented as the answer.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $M_s = 3.0$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $P_1 = 101.325 \; kPa$, $T_1 = 288.15 \; K$, $C_0 = 340.3 \; m/s$.

**(i) Pressure ratio** — using the standard Hugoniot relation:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \;=\; \frac{2\gamma M_s^{2} - (\gamma - 1)}{\gamma + 1} \;=\; \frac{2(1.4)(9) - 0.4}{2.4} \;=\; \frac{25.2 - 0.4}{2.4} \;=\; \frac{24.8}{2.4} \;=\; \mathbf{10.33}$$

So $P_2 = 10.33 \times 101.325 \; kPa = 1.047 \; MPa$.

**(ii) Density ratio:**

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} \;=\; \frac{(\gamma + 1) M_s^{2}}{(\gamma - 1) M_s^{2} + 2} \;=\; \frac{2.4 \times 9}{0.4 \times 9 + 2} \;=\; \frac{21.6}{5.6} \;=\; \mathbf{3.857}$$

**(iii) Temperature ratio** — from the ideal-gas law, $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)(\rho_1/\rho_2) = 10.33 / 3.857$:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} \;=\; 2.678 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 \;=\; 2.678 \times 288.15 \; K \;=\; \mathbf{771.6 \; K}$$

**(iv) Particle velocity** — using the strong-shock approximation:

$$u_2 \;=\; \frac{2 C_0}{\gamma + 1}\left(M_s - \frac{1}{M_s}\right) \;=\; \frac{2(340.3)}{2.4}\left(3 - \frac{1}{3}\right) \;=\; 283.6 \times 2.667 \;=\; \mathbf{756.2 \; m/s}$$

> **Verification:** Check the Rayleigh line: $P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 U_s u_2$? With $\rho_1 = P_1/(R T_1) = 101325/(287 \times 288.15) = 1.225 \; kg/m^{3}$, $U_s = M_s C_0 = 1020.9 \; m/s$, we get $\rho_1 U_s u_2 = 1.225 \times 1020.9 \times 756.2 = 9.45 \times 10^{5} \; Pa \approx 0.945 \; MPa$, and $P_2 - P_1 = 1.047 - 0.101 = 0.946 \; MPa$. ✅

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Applied Detonation Theory and TNT Equivalence*

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and explain the **Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) condition** for a self-sustained detonation. Derive the CJ similarity relations $u_{CJ} = D_{CJ}/(\gamma + 1)$ and $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D_{CJ}^{2}/(\gamma + 1)$. Show that $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2} - 1) Q}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 250 kg hemispherical charge of an aluminised explosive ($Q = 8.50 \; MJ/kg$, $\gamma = 1.25$, $\rho_0 = 1\,700 \; kg/m^{3}$) detonates at ground level. Determine (i) the CJ detonation velocity, (ii) the CJ detonation pressure, (iii) the effective TNT equivalent mass (use $\eta = 1.8$ for hemispherical ground burst), and (iv) the overpressure at a receptor located 100 m from the charge centre using the Kingery–Bulmash relation $\Delta P = 10^{A + B \log Z + C (\log Z)^{2} + D (\log Z)^{3}}$ bar with $A = 2.30$, $B = -1.43$, $C = -0.15$, $D = 0.05$ and $Z = R/m_{TNT}^{1/3}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.3**. The expected 7-mark answer:

- **CJ condition statement** (1 mark): "At the CJ plane, the flow behind the reaction zone is *sonic* relative to the leading shock front ($M_{rel} = 1$); equivalently, the Rayleigh line is tangent to the detonation Hugoniot."
- **Why this selects a unique $D_{CJ}$** (1 mark): There are two intersections of the Rayleigh line with the detonation Hugoniot; the CJ point is the only one that is dynamically stable.
- **Similarity relations** (3 marks): Through the chain of thermodynamic identities, $u_{CJ} = D_{CJ}/(\gamma + 1)$, $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D_{CJ}^{2}/(\gamma + 1)$, $C_{CJ} = \gamma D_{CJ}/(\gamma + 1)$.
- **Velocity–heat-of-reaction relation** (2 marks): Eliminating $u_{CJ}$ from the energy equation and the CJ sonic condition gives $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2}-1)Q}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $m = 250 \; kg$, $Q = 8.50 \; MJ/kg$, $\gamma = 1.25$, $\rho_0 = 1\,700 \; kg/m^{3}$, $R = 100 \; m$, $\eta = 1.8$.

**(i) CJ Detonation velocity:**

$$D_{CJ} \;=\; \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2} - 1)Q} \;=\; \sqrt{2(1.25^{2} - 1)(8.50 \times 10^{6})}$$

$$D_{CJ} \;=\; \sqrt{2(0.5625)(8.50 \times 10^{6})} \;=\; \sqrt{9.5625 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; \mathbf{3\,092 \; m/s}$$

**(ii) CJ Detonation pressure:**

$$P_{CJ} \;=\; \frac{\rho_0 D_{CJ}^{\,2}}{\gamma + 1} \;=\; \frac{1\,700 \times (3\,092)^{2}}{2.25}$$

$$P_{CJ} \;=\; \frac{1\,700 \times 9.560 \times 10^{6}}{2.25} \;=\; \frac{1.625 \times 10^{10}}{2.25} \;=\; \mathbf{7.22 \; GPa}$$

**(iii) Effective TNT equivalent mass** (hemispherical ground burst):

$$m_{TNT,\text{eff}} \;=\; \eta \cdot m_{exp} \cdot \frac{Q_{exp}}{Q_{TNT}} \;=\; 1.8 \times 250 \times \frac{8.50}{4.184}$$

$$m_{TNT,\text{eff}} \;=\; 1.8 \times 250 \times 2.032 \;=\; \mathbf{914.3 \; kg \; TNT}$$

**(iv) Overpressure at $R = 100 \; m$:**

First compute scaled distance:

$$Z \;=\; \frac{R}{m_{TNT,\text{eff}}^{\,1/3}} \;=\; \frac{100}{(914.3)^{1/3}} \;=\; \frac{100}{9.694} \;=\; \mathbf{10.32 \; m/kg^{1/3}}$$

Now $\log Z = \log_{10}(10.32) = 1.0137$.

$$\log\Delta P \;=\; A + B \log Z + C (\log Z)^{2} + D (\log Z)^{3}$$

$$\log\Delta P \;=\; 2.30 + (-1.43)(1.0137) + (-0.15)(1.0137)^{2} + (0.05)(1.0137)^{3}$$

$$\log\Delta P \;=\; 2.30 - 1.450 + (-0.154) + 0.052 \;=\; 0.748$$

$$\Delta P \;=\; 10^{0.748} \;=\; \mathbf{5.60 \; bar \; \approx \; 560 \; kPa}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** A 5.6 bar side-on overpressure is *catastrophic* — it corresponds to total destruction of most above-ground structures and 100 % fatality probability. The receptor at 100 m is well inside the kill zone; the explosive charge must be drastically reduced or the stand-off distance increased.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $M_s$ (shock Mach) with $M_{CJ}$ (CJ-plane Mach).** $M_s$ is the front Mach number; at the CJ plane the *flow* Mach number relative to the front equals 1.
> 2. **Forgetting the hemispherical factor $\eta$.** A 250 kg charge *on the ground* delivers ~1.8× the air-blast energy of the same charge in free air. Marks for TNT-equivalence are routinely lost here.
> 3. **Sign errors in Hugoniot relations.** The pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$ is *always* $\ge 1$ for a forward-facing shock; if you get a value $< 1$ you have inverted something.
> 4. **Mixing detonation velocity with explosion energy.** $D_{CJ}$ depends on $\sqrt{Q}$ — *not* on $Q$ itself. A charge with twice the energy does *not* detonate at twice the speed.
> 5. **Dropping units in the Kingery–Bulmash calculation.** $Z$ must be in $\mathrm{m/kg^{1/3}}$ and $\Delta P$ in bar (or use a consistent unit system). Unit errors are an automatic 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Using $\gamma = 1.4$ for detonation products.** For CJ calculations the *product-gas* $\gamma$ is required, which is typically $1.20 - 1.30$, *not* the atmospheric value.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Sound** is a small-amplitude, isentropic, subsonic mechanical pressure wave; speed in air is $\mathbf{340.3 \; m/s}$ at STP.
- **Shock wave** is a supersonic, discontinuous, irreversible front across which entropy rises; the *Rayleigh line* and *Hugoniot curve* intersect at the operating point.
- **Detonation** is a self-sustained, supersonic reactive shock following the **ZND** (Zel'dovich–von Neumann–Döring) structure: leading shock → reaction zone → CJ plane → Taylor expansion.
- The **Chapman–Jouguet condition** is the Rayleigh-line-tangency requirement; it uniquely fixes $D_{CJ}$, $P_{CJ}$ and $u_{CJ}$.
- The **CJ similarity relations** $u_{CJ} = D_{CJ}/(\gamma+1)$, $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D_{CJ}^{2}/(\gamma+1)$ and $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2}-1)Q}$ are the workhorse equations of the module.
- The **Hopkinson–Cranz scaled distance** $Z = R/m_{TNT}^{1/3}$ underlies all standardised blast predictions (Kingery–Bulmash, TM5-1300).
- **Brisance** scales as $D_{CJ}^{3}$ and depends on $P_{CJ}$ — it is *not* the same as total energy.
- **TNT equivalent** uses the standard reference value $Q_{TNT} = 4.184 \; MJ/kg$, with the hemispherical ground-burst factor $\eta \approx 1.8$ when applicable.
- **Acoustic impedance** $Z_a = \rho_0 C_0$ governs shock transmission and *sympathetic detonation* between adjacent charges — a key process-safety concept.
- **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)** is the dominant accidental-explosion mechanism in confined process equipment; prevention depends on flame-arrestor design and venting.
- Always use the **product-gas** value of $\gamma$ (typically $1.2 - 1.3$) in CJ calculations, not the atmospheric $\gamma = 1.4$.
- Master the three RH equations and the four CJ equations — they jointly cover $\ge 80 \%$ of KTU 2024 marks on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sound Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **sound wave** is a *mechanical longitudinal pressure wave* that propagates through an elastic medium by means of alternating regions of **compression** (high pressure, high density) and **rarefaction** (low pressure, low density). In the context of **Explosives Technology and Safety**, sound waves encompass all audible, infrasonic, and ultrasonic pressure fluctuations generated by detonation, deflagration, or mechanical activity associated with explosive charges and blasting operations.

Mathematically, the one-dimensional acoustic wave equation governing the displacement $s(x,t)$ of a medium particle is:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{v^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial t^{2}}$$

where $v$ is the **phase velocity** of the wave in the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Sound waves in explosives engineering are NOT just audible noise. They include the **blast overpressure wave** (a non-linear shock front) and the **acoustic-gravity wave** that propagates into the far field. The transition from a non-linear shock front to a linear acoustic wave is a key transition studied in Module 2.

## 1.2 Classification of Sound Waves by Frequency

| Band | Frequency Range | Relevance to Explosives |
|---|---|---|
| **Infrasound** | $< 20 \text{ Hz}$ | Dominant component of large-yield detonations; long-range propagation |
| **Audible Sound** | $20 \text{ Hz} - 20 \text{ kHz}$ | Blast noise; occupational hearing hazard |
| **Ultrasound** | $> 20 \text{ kHz}$ | Acoustic emission testing of munitions; non-destructive evaluation |
| **Shock Wave** | Broadband impulse | Initial blast front; non-linear regime near source |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Pond Ripple, But in 3D Air:**
> Imagine dropping a stone into a still pond. Ripples spread outward as alternating high and low water levels. Now imagine doing the same thing in 3D air with a detonating explosive — the "ripples" become spherical shells of compressed and expanded air that radiate outward at the speed of sound.
>
> **Key Difference:** A stone creates tiny ripples. A detonation creates a *violent* initial disturbance — a **shock front** where pressure jumps discontinuously. Far from the source, however, this shock decays into a normal (linear) sound wave, which is what we measure with sound level meters.

## 1.4 Critical Physical Constants (Memorize These)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard reference values used in every KTU numerical problem:
>
> - Speed of sound in air at **20 °C (293 K):** $v = 343 \text{ m/s}$
> - Speed of sound at **0 °C (273 K):** $v = 331 \text{ m/s}$
> - Reference sound intensity (threshold of hearing): $I_{0} = 10^{-12} \text{ W/m}^{2}$
> - Reference sound pressure: $p_{0} = 20 \text{ μPa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa}$
> - Adiabatic index for air: $\gamma = 1.40$
> - Molar mass of air: $M = 0.029 \text{ kg/mol}$
> - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - Acoustic impedance of air: $Z_{0} = \rho v \approx 410 \text{ Pa·s/m}$ (rayls)

## 1.5 Why Sound Waves Matter in Explosives Safety

1. **Blast Overpressure Damage:** Peak overpressure above **6.9 kPa (≈ 1 psi)** shatters windows; above **34 kPa (≈ 5 psi)** most buildings collapse. Sound-wave physics quantifies how these pressures attenuate with distance.
2. **Occupational Hearing Loss:** OSHA-mandated permissible exposure limit (PEL) is **85 dBA over 8 hours**. Blasting operations routinely exceed **120–140 dB peak**.
3. **Community Nuisance & Regulation:** Indian explosives rules (under Explosives Rules 2008) and DGMS guidelines impose A-weighted sound level limits near blasting zones.
4. **Seismic & Acoustic Discrimination:** Distinguishing blast noise from seismic ground waves helps in locating detonations and assessing structural safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Mechanism of Sound Generation

When an explosive charge detonates, the chemical energy is released in microseconds, generating a high-temperature, high-pressure gas bubble (typical detonation pressure for TNT ≈ **19 GPa**). This sudden expansion launches a **pressure pulse** into the surrounding air. The pulse propagates because each layer of air, when compressed, pushes on the next layer — Newton's third law at the molecular level.

The propagation requires:
1. **Inertia** (mass of air parcels to be moved)
2. **Elasticity** (restoring force from compression)

Both are captured in the bulk modulus $B$ and the density $\rho$.

## 2.2 The Wave Equation — Why Sound Exists

A sound wave must satisfy two coupled physical laws:

**(a) Newton's Second Law (force on a fluid element):**

$$\rho \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial t^{2}} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**(b) Adiabatic Compression (since sound is rapid):**

$$P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant} \implies dP = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho} d\rho$$

Combining these through the continuity equation yields the **wave equation** with phase speed:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$

This is the master equation for the speed of sound in any gas.

## 2.3 Speed of Sound in Different Media (Engineering Context)

| Medium | Speed (m/s) | Relevance to Explosives |
|---|---|---|
| Air (20 °C) | **343** | Blast wave propagation in atmosphere |
| Air (0 °C) | 331 | Cold-weather blasting calculations |
| Water (20 °C) | 1480 | Underwater explosions (e.g., naval ordnance) |
| Steel | 5960 | Shock transmission in metal casings |
| Granite/Rock | 4000–6000 | Seismic waves from quarry blasting |
| Detonation products (TNT) | ~6900 | Inside the explosive charge itself |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** The detonation wave inside an explosive travels **~20× faster** than the sound wave it produces in surrounding air. This is why a detonator's "thump" reaches you seconds after the ground vibration (which travels through rock at ~5000 m/s).

## 2.4 Sound Intensity and the Inverse-Square Law

For a **point source** radiating total acoustic power $P$ uniformly:

$$I(r) = \frac{P}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

This is the **inverse-square law**. It dictates that every doubling of distance reduces intensity by a factor of 4 (i.e., **−6 dB**).

For a **line source** (e.g., a long cylindrical charge):

$$I(r) = \frac{P}{2\pi r L}$$

Intensity falls only as $1/r$ here (**−3 dB per doubling**), which is why shaped charges and linear explosive trains have different blast footprints.

## 2.5 The Decibel Scale — Why We Use It

The human ear responds to intensities spanning **12 orders of magnitude** (from $10^{-12}$ to $10^{0} \text{ W/m}^{2}$). Linear scales are impractical. The **Bel** (and its subunit, the decibel) compresses this range using logarithms.

**Sound Intensity Level (SIL):**

$$\beta = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Sound Pressure Level (SPL):** Since $I \propto p^{2}$ for plane waves:

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{0}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where $p_{0} = 20 \text{ μPa}$ is the standard reference pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule for Examiners:** A **+10 dB** change corresponds to a **10× increase in intensity** but only a **~3.16× increase in pressure** (because intensity scales with pressure squared).

## 2.6 Atmospheric Absorption and Geometric Spreading

Real sound waves lose energy by two mechanisms:

1. **Geometric spreading** (inverse-square): $I \propto 1/r^{2}$
2. **Atmospheric absorption**: $I(r) = I_{0} \frac{e^{-2\alpha r}}{r^{2}}$

where $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient (depends on frequency, humidity, temperature). Higher frequencies are absorbed more strongly — which is why distant thunder sounds like a low rumble.

## 2.7 Blast Wave Fundamentals — The Cube-Root Scaling Law

For high-yield explosions, blast similarity is governed by the **Hopkinson-Cranz (cube-root) scaling law**:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

where $R$ is the stand-off distance (m), $W$ is the charge mass (kg of TNT equivalent), and $Z$ is the **scaled distance** (m/kg$^{1/3}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cube root?** Dimensional analysis of the blast equations shows that the peak overpressure $P_{0}$ and the scaled distance $Z$ are related through a single universal curve $P_{0}(Z)$, independent of the individual values of $R$ and $W$. Two different explosions with the same $Z$ produce the same overpressure.

Typical safe-distance criterion for blast damage to structures: $Z \ge 6 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave equation | $v = f \lambda$ | m/s | Fundamental |
| 2 | Speed in ideal gas | $v = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | m/s | For air, $v \approx 331 + 0.6 T_{\text{°C}}$ |
| 3 | Speed via bulk modulus | $v = \sqrt{B / \rho}$ | m/s | General |
| 4 | Intensity from power | $I = P / (4\pi r^{2})$ | W/m² | Point source |
| 5 | Intensity via pressure | $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{2} / (\rho v)$ | W/m² | Plane wave |
| 6 | Sound Intensity Level | $\beta = 10 \log(I / I_{0})$ | dB | $I_{0} = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 7 | Sound Pressure Level | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log(p / p_{0})$ | dB | $p_{0} = 20$ μPa |
| 8 | Inverse-square ratio | $I_{1}/I_{2} = (r_{2}/r_{1})^{2}$ | — | Ratio form |
| 9 | dB change rule | $\Delta\beta = 20 \log(r_{2}/r_{1})$ | dB | For point source |
| 10 | Cube-root scaling | $Z = R / W^{1/3}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | Blast similarity |
| 11 | Doppler effect | $f' = f \cdot (v \pm v_{o})/(v \mp v_{s})$ | Hz | $v_{o}$ observer, $v_{s}$ source |
| 12 | Wavelength | $\lambda = v / f$ | m | — |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, vertical bars appear only inside LaTeX expressions (e.g., $Z = R/W^{1/3}$). For absolute-value contexts outside tables, always write `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the Markdown table renderer.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Blast design:** Civil engineers use the cube-root law to determine safe standoff distances for residential structures near quarries.
- **Hearing conservation:** Mine safety officers use SIL measurements to enforce ear-protection zones.
- **Forensic blast analysis:** Investigators back-calculate charge size from recorded overpressure data using $Z$-scaling curves.
- **Acoustic emission NDT:** Ultrasound sensors detect micro-cracks in ammunition storage containers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is expanded below. No intermediate result is omitted.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas (Full Derivation)

**Goal:** Derive $v = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Set up the physical scenario

Consider a long cylindrical column of gas with cross-sectional area $A$, originally at pressure $P_{0}$ and density $\rho_{0}$. A sound wave disturbs a thin slice of thickness $dx$ by displacing its left face by $s$ and its right face by $s + ds$.

### Step 2 — Relate density change to displacement

The slice's original volume is $V = A\,dx$. After disturbance, the new thickness is $dx + ds$, so the new volume is:

$$V' = A (dx + ds) = A\,dx \left(1 + \frac{\partial s}{\partial x}\right)$$

The fractional volume change (compression) is:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{\partial s}{\partial x}$$

Since mass is conserved, the new density satisfies $\rho V = \rho_{0} V_{0}$, hence:

$$\rho = \rho_{0} \left(1 - \frac{\partial s}{\partial x}\right)$$

Therefore the density change is:

$$d\rho = -\rho_{0} \frac{\partial s}{\partial x}$$

### Step 3 — Use the adiabatic equation of state

Sound waves are rapid — there is no time for heat exchange. Therefore the process is **adiabatic**:

$$P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\gamma \frac{dV}{V} = -\gamma \frac{\partial s}{\partial x}$$

So the excess pressure (sound pressure) is:

$$dP = -\gamma P \frac{\partial s}{\partial x}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law to the slice

The net force on the slice from pressure difference is $A \cdot dP$. By Newton's second law:

$$\rho_{0} A\,dx \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial t^{2}} = -A \cdot dP$$

Substitute $dP$ from Step 3:

$$\rho_{0} \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial t^{2}} = \gamma P \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the wave equation

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{\rho_{0}}{\gamma P} \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial t^{2}}$$

Comparing with the standard wave equation $\partial^{2} s / \partial x^{2} = (1/v^{2})\partial^{2} s / \partial t^{2}$, we identify:

$$v^{2} = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho_{0}} \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho_{0}}}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to temperature using the ideal gas law

For an ideal gas: $P = \rho R T / M$, so $P/\rho = RT/M$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical evaluation for air at 20 °C

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \sqrt{\frac{1.40 \times 8.314 \times 293}{0.029}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{3410.7}{0.029}} \\
&= \sqrt{117\,610} \\
&\approx 343 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

✅ Verified against the standard reference value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverse-Square Law for Point Source Intensity

**Goal:** Show that $I(r) \propto 1/r^{2}$.

### Step 1 — Energy conservation

A point source radiates total time-averaged acoustic power $P$ (in watts) uniformly in all directions. By conservation of energy, this same power must pass through *any* closed surface enclosing the source.

### Step 2 — Spherical surface area

The simplest such surface is a sphere of radius $r$ centered on the source. Its area is:

$$A(r) = 4\pi r^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Definition of intensity

Intensity is power per unit area perpendicular to the propagation direction. For uniform spherical spreading:

$$I(r) = \frac{P}{A(r)} = \frac{P}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Take the ratio at two distances

$$\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}} = \frac{r_{2}^{2}}{r_{1}^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Express in decibels

The level difference between two distances is:

$$\Delta\beta = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering implication:** For every doubling of distance, $\Delta\beta = 20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$ dB. So a blast reading **120 dB at 100 m** becomes **114 dB at 200 m**, and **108 dB at 400 m**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalence of SIL and SPL Formulae

**Goal:** Show that $10 \log(I/I_{0}) = 20 \log(p/p_{0})$ when $p_{0}$ is chosen appropriately.

### Step 1 — Intensity-pressure relationship

For a plane progressive sound wave, the time-averaged intensity is:

$$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho v}$$

where $\rho v$ is the characteristic acoustic impedance $Z_{0}$ of the medium.

### Step 2 — Substitute into SIL formula

$$\beta = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{Z_{0} I_{0}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Choose $p_{0}$ such that $p_{0}^{2} = Z_{0} I_{0}$

$$\beta = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{p_{0}^{2}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{0}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Verify the reference pressure value

For air, $Z_{0} \approx 410$ rayls. Therefore:

$$p_{0} = \sqrt{Z_{0} I_{0}} = \sqrt{410 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa} = 20 \text{ μPa}$$

This matches the IEC/ISO standard — confirming the consistency of the two decibel definitions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cube-Root Scaling Law for Blast Waves

**Goal:** Justify the form $Z = R / W^{1/3}$.

### Step 1 — Identify relevant physical quantities

A blast wave depends on:
- $R$ — distance from charge (m)
- $W$ — charge mass (kg)
- $E$ — energy release per unit mass (J/kg) — a material property
- $\rho_{0}$ — ambient air density (kg/m³)

### Step 2 — Dimensional analysis

We seek a function $P_{0} = f(R, W, E, \rho_{0})$. There are 4 variables and 2 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T). By the Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem, there are **2 independent dimensionless groups**.

### Step 3 — Construct the groups

Group 1 (energy ratio):

$$\Pi_{1} = \frac{\rho_{0} R^{3}}{W} \quad \text{(dimensionless when } E = 1\text{)}$$

Group 2 (scaled distance):

$$\Pi_{2} = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

### Step 4 — Empirical similarity

Experiments (Hopkinson, 1915; Cranz, 1926) showed that blast damage and peak overpressure depend only on $\Pi_{2}$. Hence:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{P_{0}}{P_{\text{atm}}} = g(Z)}$$

### Step 5 — Worked example

> **Problem:** A **10 kg TNT** charge detonates in open air. At what distance will the peak overpressure equal **34 kPa (≈ 5 psi)** — the threshold for heavy building damage?

**Solution:**

From the standard blast curve, $P_{0} = 34$ kPa corresponds to a scaled distance of approximately:

$$Z \approx 3.5 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

Therefore:

$$R = Z \cdot W^{1/3} = 3.5 \times (10)^{1/3} = 3.5 \times 2.154 \approx 7.5 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** Residential evacuation radius ≈ **8 meters** for this charge size.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive)

> **Problem:** A demolition blast uses a **500 kg** charge. A sound level meter reads **120 dB** at a distance of **200 m** from the detonation point.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the acoustic power of the source.
> **(b)** What would the reading be at **500 m** assuming pure inverse-square spreading?
> **(c)** What is the corresponding peak overpressure in Pa at 500 m? (Take $Z_{0} = 410$ rayls.)

### Part (a): Acoustic power

Convert SPL to intensity:

$$I_{200} = I_{0} \times 10^{120/10} = 10^{-12} \times 10^{12} = 1 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

Power from inverse-square law:

$$P = I \times 4\pi r^{2} = 1 \times 4\pi (200)^{2} = 1 \times 4\pi \times 40\,000 = 502\,655 \text{ W} \approx 5.03 \times 10^{5} \text{ W}$$

### Part (b): Reading at 500 m

New intensity:

$$I_{500} = \frac{P}{4\pi (500)^{2}} = \frac{502\,655}{4\pi \times 250\,000} = \frac{502\,655}{3.14 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.16 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

New SPL:

$$\beta_{500} = 10 \log_{10}(0.16 / 10^{-12}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.6 \times 10^{11}) = 10 \times 11.204 \approx 112 \text{ dB}$$

### Part (c): Peak overpressure

From $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{2} / Z_{0}$:

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{I \cdot Z_{0}} = \sqrt{0.16 \times 410} = \sqrt{65.6} \approx 8.1 \text{ Pa}$$

Peak pressure ≈ $\sqrt{2} \times p_{\text{rms}} \approx 11.5$ Pa.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Sound Wave Generation and Propagation (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Explosive Charge Detonation] --> B[Rapid Gas Expansion at ~19 GPa]
    B --> C[Shock Front Formation Near Field]
    C --> D[Shock Attenuation into Linear Wave]
    D --> E[Spherical Acoustic Wave Propagation]
    E --> F1[Infrasonic Component below 20 Hz]
    E --> F2[Audible Component 20 Hz to 20 kHz]
    E --> F3[High Frequency Absorption Dominant]
    F1 --> G1[Long Range Propagation via Atmospheric Ducting]
    F2 --> G2[Local Hearing Hazard Zone]
    F3 --> G3[Absorbed within Few Hundred Meters]
    G1 --> H[Receiver or Monitoring Station]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Microphone and Sound Level Meter]
    I --> J[A Weighted SPL in dBA]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Trace the energy from the detonation source through its transformation from a non-linear shock into a linear acoustic wave, and finally to the receiver's measured SPL.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Longitudinal Wave Structure (Compression and Rarefaction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Particle at Rest] --> B[Compression Phase Maximum Density]
    B --> C[Equilibrium Position]
    C --> D[Rarefaction Phase Minimum Density]
    D --> E[Equilibrium Position]
    E --> F[Next Compression Cycle]
    subgraph Properties[Wave Properties]
        P1[Wavelength lambda equals distance between two compressions]
        P2[Frequency f equals cycles per second in Hz]
        P3[Amplitude equals peak displacement from equilibrium]
    end
```

**Physical interpretation:** This block diagram represents one full wavelength of a longitudinal sound wave, showing the periodic alternation of high and low pressure regions that propagate outward from the source.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Decibel Scale Reference Points (Hearing Safety Zones)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[0 dB Threshold of Hearing] --> A20[20 dB Quiet Whisper]
    A20 --> A60[60 dB Normal Conversation]
    A60 --> A85[85 dB OSHA 8 Hour Limit]
    A85 --> A100[100 dB Pneumatic Drill]
    A100 --> A120[120 dB Pain Threshold and Rock Concert]
    A120 --> A140[140 dB Jet Engine at 30 m]
    A140 --> A170[170 dB Typical Quarry Blast at 100 m]
    A170 --> A180[180 dB Large Detonation Close Range]
    A180 --> A190[above 190 dB Lung Rupture Possible]
    subgraph SafeZone[Comfort Zone below 70 dB]
        A0
        A20
        A60
    end
    subgraph HazardZone[Hazard Zone above 85 dB Hearing Protection Required]
        A85
        A100
        A120
        A140
    end
    subgraph DamageZone[Damage Zone above 140 dB Single Event Harm]
        A170
        A180
        A190
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Blast Wave Structure and Scaled Distance Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Charge Mass W kg TNT] --> B[Detonation Energy Release]
    B --> C[Spherical Blast Wave Expansion]
    C --> D1[Near Field Z less than 1: Shock Dominant]
    C --> D2[Transition Zone Z between 1 and 5]
    C --> D3[Far Field Z above 5: Acoustic Wave]
    D1 --> E1[Non Linear High Pressure Shock]
    D2 --> E2[Decaying Pressure Pulse]
    D3 --> E3[Linear Sound Wave Measurable with SPL Meter]
    E1 --> F[Scaled Distance Relation Z equals R divided by W raised to one third]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Universal Peak Overpressure Curve]
    G --> H[Damage Classification]
    H --> H1[below 7 kPa: Window Breakage]
    H --> H2[7 to 35 kPa: Structural Damage]
    H --> H3[above 35 kPa: Severe Destruction]
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three regimes — near, transition, and far field — collapse onto a single universal curve when expressed in scaled distance $Z$. This is the essence of Hopkinson-Cranz similarity.

---

## 4.5 Diagram E — Block Architecture of an Explosives Safety Sound Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blast Site Microphone Array] --> B[Signal Conditioner and Amplifier]
    B --> C[Analog to Digital Converter]
    C --> D[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    D --> E1[Fast Fourier Transform for Spectrum]
    D --> E2[A Weighting Filter for dBA]
    D --> E3[Peak Hold for Impulse Capture]
    E1 --> F[Data Logger and Storage]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Display Unit Showing SPL Time History]
    F --> H[Threshold Alarm Trigger above 140 dB]
    F --> I[Wireless Transmission to Control Room]
```

**Engineering takeaway:** Modern blasting operations use multi-channel acoustic monitoring with real-time dBA reporting, peak-hold capture for impulses, and remote telemetry for regulatory compliance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define sound intensity level. A rock drill produces a sound intensity of $10^{-4} \text{ W/m}^{2}$ at the operator's position. Calculate the sound intensity level in dB.**

**Model Answer:**

The sound intensity level $\beta$ is defined as:

$$\beta = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where $I_{0} = 10^{-12} \text{ W/m}^{2}$ is the reference intensity (threshold of hearing).

Substituting:

$$\beta = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^{8}) = 10 \times 8 = \mathbf{80 \text{ dB}}$$

**Classification:** Just below the OSHA hazardous threshold (85 dB).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**What is the cube-root scaling law in blast wave analysis? Why is it important for safety?**

**Model Answer:**

The cube-root scaling law (Hopkinson-Cranz similarity) states that the blast effects from explosions of different sizes can be compared using the **scaled distance**:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

where $R$ is the stand-off distance (m) and $W$ is the charge mass (kg of TNT equivalent).

Two explosions with the same $Z$ produce the same peak overpressure and impulse, regardless of the absolute values of $R$ and $W$.

**Importance for safety:**
1. Allows **extrapolation** of test blast data to full-scale operations.
2. Provides a **single universal damage curve** for all charge sizes.
3. Enables calculation of **safe standoff distances** for structures and personnel.
4. Permits **forensic back-calculation** of charge size from measured blast pressure.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two full alternative questions are provided below; the student answers ONE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Derive the expression for the speed of sound in an ideal gas. Calculate its value in air at 27 °C. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**(i) [1 mark] Statement:** Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave produced by small adiabatic pressure fluctuations in an elastic medium.

**(ii) [2 marks] Governing equations:**

Consider a thin slice of gas of thickness $dx$ and area $A$ in a sound field. Newton's second law gives:

$$\rho \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial t^{2}} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

The adiabatic condition $PV^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$ gives:

$$dP = -\gamma P \frac{\partial s}{\partial x}$$

**(iii) [2 marks] Derivation:**

Combining with the continuity equation (mass conservation):

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0$$

yields the wave equation:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial t^{2}} = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho} \frac{\partial^{2} s}{\partial x^{2}}$$

Comparing with the standard form $\partial^{2} s/\partial t^{2} = v^{2} \cdot \partial^{2} s/\partial x^{2}$:

$$\boxed{v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}}$$

Using the ideal gas equation $P/\rho = RT/M$:

$$\boxed{v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}}$$

**(iv) [2 marks] Numerical substitution:**

At $T = 27\,°\text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $M = 0.029 \text{ kg/mol}$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 300}{0.029}} = \sqrt{\frac{3491.9}{0.029}} = \sqrt{120\,410} \approx \mathbf{347 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — A point source of sound emits a power of 50 W uniformly in all directions. Calculate (i) intensity at 10 m, (ii) SPL in dB at 10 m, (iii) SPL in dB at 40 m. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**(i) [2 marks] Intensity at 10 m:**

$$I_{10} = \frac{P}{4\pi r^{2}} = \frac{50}{4\pi (10)^{2}} = \frac{50}{1256.6} \approx \mathbf{3.98 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W/m}^{2}}$$

**(ii) [2 marks] SPL at 10 m:**

$$\text{SPL}_{10} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3.98 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3.98 \times 10^{10})$$

$$= 10 \times (10 + 0.5999) = 10 \times 10.60 \approx \mathbf{106 \text{ dB}}$$

**(iii) [3 marks] SPL at 40 m:**

Method 1 — direct calculation:

$$I_{40} = \frac{50}{4\pi (40)^{2}} = \frac{50}{20\,106} \approx 2.49 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

$$\text{SPL}_{40} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.49 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times 9.396 \approx \mathbf{94 \text{ dB}}$$

Method 2 — inverse-square shortcut:

$$\Delta\beta = 20 \log_{10}(40/10) = 20 \log_{10}(4) = 20 \times 0.602 = 12.04 \text{ dB}$$

$$\text{SPL}_{40} = 106 - 12.04 \approx \mathbf{94 \text{ dB}}$$

✅ Both methods agree.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Explain the Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root scaling law. An explosion of 100 kg TNT equivalent occurs. Find the distance at which the peak overpressure will be 50 kPa. Use the standard blast curve: $P_{0} = 50$ kPa corresponds to $Z = 2.5$ m/kg$^{1/3}$. (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**(i) [3 marks] Theory:**

The Hopkinson-Cranz scaling law is an empirical similarity relation established independently by Hopkinson (1915) and Cranz (1926). It states that two explosions of different charge sizes produce dynamically similar blast waves if the **scaled distance** is the same:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

where:
- $R$ = stand-off distance from the charge (m)
- $W$ = charge mass (kg, expressed as TNT equivalent)

All blast parameters (peak overpressure, impulse, arrival time) become functions of the single variable $Z$. Therefore, data from small-scale tests can be scaled to predict full-scale effects.

**(ii) [2 marks] Setting up the problem:**

Given: $W = 100$ kg, $P_{0} = 50$ kPa corresponds to $Z = 2.5$ m/kg$^{1/3}$.

**(iii) [2 marks] Calculation:**

$$R = Z \cdot W^{1/3} = 2.5 \times (100)^{1/3} = 2.5 \times 4.642$$

$$R \approx \mathbf{11.6 \text{ m}}$$

At distances less than ~11.6 m from this 100 kg charge, the peak overpressure will exceed 50 kPa — sufficient to demolish most residential structures.

---

#### Part (b) — (i) Define Sound Pressure Level. (ii) An explosion produces an RMS overpressure of 2 Pa at a distance of 250 m. Calculate the SPL in dB. (iii) If the same explosion is observed from 500 m, what will be the SPL assuming inverse-square spreading? (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**(i) [2 marks] Definition:**

The **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** is defined as twenty times the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of the RMS sound pressure to the reference pressure:

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{0}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where $p_{0} = 20 \text{ μPa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa}$ is the standard reference RMS pressure corresponding to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.

**(ii) [2 marks] SPL at 250 m:**

$$\text{SPL}_{250} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^{5}) = 20 \times 5 = \mathbf{100 \text{ dB}}$$

**(iii) [3 marks] SPL at 500 m:**

Distance doubles, so intensity drops by factor of 4 (i.e., −6 dB):

$$\text{SPL}_{500} = 100 - 6.02 \approx \mathbf{94 \text{ dB}}$$

**Verification by direct calculation:**

$$p_{\text{rms},500} = \frac{p_{\text{rms},250}}{2} = 1 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\text{SPL}_{500} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \times 4.699 = \mathbf{94 \text{ dB}} \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Sound Waves questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SIL and SPL factor of 2** — Many students write $10 \log(p/p_{0})$ instead of $20 \log(p/p_{0})$. SPL uses 20 because intensity scales with the *square* of pressure.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the cube-root in blast scaling** — A common error is writing $Z = R/W$ instead of $R/W^{1/3}$. This gives **wrong distances by an order of magnitude** in typical blasting problems.
>
> 3. **Using incorrect $I_{0}$ or $p_{0}$** — Always use $I_{0} = 10^{-12} \text{ W/m}^{2}$ and $p_{0} = 20 \text{ μPa}$. Mixing units (e.g., dyn/cm² instead of Pa) costs marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong unit for $Z$** — Always write $Z$ in m/kg$^{1/3}$ and $W$ in kg.
>
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps** — KTU 2024 Scheme awards method marks separately. Even if the final answer is wrong, full marks are given for a correct derivation sequence.
>
> 6. **Confusing "intensity" with "intensity level"** — Intensity $I$ is in W/m². Intensity level $\beta$ is in dB. Mixing the two is a common blunder.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning the adiabatic assumption** — When deriving $v = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$, explicitly state that the sound process is adiabatic. Examiners look for this keyword.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it to your study wall before the exam.**

### Conceptual Foundations
- ☐ Sound is a **longitudinal mechanical wave** requiring a medium.
- ☐ It propagates via **alternating compression and rarefaction** of the medium.
- ☐ The fundamental relation is $v = f \lambda$.
- ☐ In air at 20 °C, $v = 343$ m/s; in water 1480 m/s; in steel 5960 m/s.

### Key Derived Formulae
- ☐ Speed in ideal gas: $v = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$
- ☐ Intensity from point source: $I = P / (4\pi r^{2})$
- ☐ Sound Intensity Level: $\beta = 10 \log(I/I_{0})$ dB with $I_{0} = 10^{-12}$ W/m²
- ☐ Sound Pressure Level: $\text{SPL} = 20 \log(p/p_{0})$ dB with $p_{0} = 20$ μPa
- ☐ Cube-root scaling: $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ (units: m/kg$^{1/3}$)
- ☐ Doubling distance → −6 dB (point source); doubling distance → −3 dB (line source)

### Numerical Problem Shortcuts
- ☐ **+10 dB** = 10× intensity, **+20 dB** = 100× intensity, **+6 dB** = 4× intensity.
- ☐ Use $\Delta\beta = 20 \log(r_{2}/r_{1})$ to avoid recomputing intensities from scratch.
- ☐ Always quote SPL in **dB (re 20 μPa)** unless told otherwise.

### Explosives Safety Applications
- ☐ OSHA PEL = **85 dBA** for 8-hour exposure.
- ☐ Blast noise from blasting routinely reaches **120–140 dB peak** at typical monitoring distances.
- ☐ Peak overpressure **34 kPa (5 psi)** = heavy building damage threshold.
- ☐ Hopkinson-Cranz similarity lets you predict large-charge effects from small-scale tests.

### Common Conversion & Mnemonics
- ☐ "**d**eci**B**el uses **10** for power and **20** for pressure" → think "**D**ouble-**T**wenty".
- ☐ "**Cube root, not square root**" — Hopkinson-Cranz is one of the most-tested formulae.

### Quick-Reference Constants
- ☐ $\gamma_{\text{air}} = 1.40$; $M_{\text{air}} = 0.029$ kg/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- ☐ Acoustic impedance of air $Z_{0} \approx 410$ rayls
- ☐ Threshold of hearing $I_{0} = 10^{-12}$ W/m²; $p_{0} = 20$ μPa

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shock Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shock Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **SHOCK WAVE (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
