> A *shock wave* is a thin, propagating discontinuity in fluid properties (pressure $P$, density $\rho$, temperature $T$, particle velocity $u$, and internal energy $e$) across which the changes occur almost discontinuously over a distance of a few mean free paths of the molecules. In the context of explosives, a shock wave is a **supersonic compressive disturbance** generated by the near-instantaneous release of energy from a detonation or high-energy release event.

A shock wave is characterized by the fact that it travels **faster than the local speed of sound** in the undisturbed medium ahead of it. The ratio of the shock front velocity to the ambient sound speed is defined as the **Mach number**:

$$M_s = \frac{D}{c_0}$$

where $D$ is the shock velocity (m/s) and $c_0$ is the undisturbed sound speed in the medium (m/s).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Highway Pile-Up":**  
> Imagine cars on a highway, all moving at the speed limit, well-spaced. Suddenly, a stopped truck appears ahead. Cars braking in unison cannot stop instantly — they rear-end each other, compressing into a *tight bunch* of vehicles that itself moves as a single dense cluster backward through the traffic. This **compressed cluster** is exactly analogous to a **shock front**:
> 
> - The **highway** = the air/medium (cars = molecules).
> - The **stopped truck** = a sudden energy release (like an explosion).
> - The **compressed cluster of cars** = the thin shock layer where pressure, density, and temperature jump abruptly.
> 
> Just as the cluster keeps moving because more cars keep rear-ending it, a shock wave keeps propagating because the disturbance keeps feeding energy into the front.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Standard Reference Metrics for KTU 2024 (Ambient Air, Sea Level):**
> - Ambient pressure $P_0 = \mathbf{101.325\ kPa}$
> - Ambient density $\rho_0 = \mathbf{1.225\ kg/m^3}$
> - Speed of sound in air $c_0 = \mathbf{340.3\ m/s}$
> - Ratio of specific heats for air $\gamma = \mathbf{1.4}$
> - Universal gas constant $R = \mathbf{8.314\ J/(mol\cdot K)}$
> - Typical detonation velocity of TNT: $D \approx \mathbf{6900\ m/s}$ ($M_s \approx 20$)
> - Typical detonation velocity of PETN: $D \approx \mathbf{8400\ m/s}$ ($M_s \approx 25$)

### 1.4 Why Shock Waves Matter in Explosives Technology

A detonating explosive charge drives a **reacting shock wave** (a *detonation wave*) through the unreacted explosive material. The shock compresses and heats the explosive past its ignition threshold, sustaining the wave in a self-propagating manner. The same shock wave, after emerging from the charge into the surrounding air, becomes a **blast wave** — the destructive pressure front responsible for structural damage and injury.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Shock Wave — Step-by-Step

A shock wave can be modelled as a **Rankine-Hugoniot discontinuity** separating two thermodynamic states:

- **State 1 (Undisturbed / "Upstream"):** Properties $P_1$, $\rho_1$, $u_1$, $e_1$, $T_1$.
- **State 2 (Shocked / "Downstream"):** Properties $P_2$, $\rho_2$, $u_2$, $e_2$, $T_2$.

The transition zone is microscopically thin (typically $10^{-7}$ m to $10^{-5}$ m thick), but for engineering purposes it is treated as a **mathematical discontinuity**.

#### Step 1 — Mass Conservation
The mass flowing into the shock front per unit area per unit time must equal the mass flowing out:

> **Why?** Because matter cannot accumulate at the discontinuity (steady-state shock).

$$\rho_1(D - u_1) = \rho_2(D - u_2)$$

#### Step 2 — Momentum Conservation
Newton's second law applied to the control volume across the shock:

> **Why?** The pressure difference across the front accelerates the medium.

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1(D - u_1)(u_2 - u_1)$$

#### Step 3 — Energy Conservation
The first law of thermodynamics across the discontinuity:

> **Why?** Energy is conserved; kinetic and internal energy changes balance enthalpy flux differences.

$$e_2 - e_1 = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

These three equations are the **Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions** — the *cornerstone* of shock wave theory.

### 2.2 The Hugoniot Curve

> [!NOTE]  
> **Hugoniot Curve:** A locus in $(P, V)$ space (or $(P, \rho)$ space) of all possible thermodynamic states reachable from a given initial state $P_1, V_1$ by a single adiabatic shock. It is **NOT** an isentrope — entropy *increases* across every shock.

For a calorically perfect gas, the Hugoniot equation in the $(P, V)$ plane is:

$$H(P, V) = e_2 - e_1 - \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right) = 0$$

Expanding with $e = \frac{P}{(\gamma - 1)\rho}$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{(\gamma + 1)\rho_2/\rho_1 - (\gamma - 1)}{(\gamma + 1) - (\gamma - 1)\rho_2/\rho_1}$$

### 2.3 Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> For a **self-sustained detonation**, the CJ condition states that the detonation velocity $D_{CJ}$ is the **minimum possible steady-state velocity** that satisfies the Rankine-Hugoniot equations and the global mass/momentum/energy balances for the reacting explosive. At the CJ point, the flow behind the reaction zone is **sonic relative to the shock front**.

$$D_{CJ} = c_2 + u_2 \quad \text{(sonic condition at CJ plane)}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shock wave theory underpins:
- **Blast resistant design** of buildings, bunkers, and vehicles.
- **Munitions performance prediction** (brisance, fragmentation, air blast).
- **Demolition engineering** (controlled blast sequences).
- **Pyrotechnic and propellant safety** (deflagration-to-detonation transition — DDT).
- **Underwater and underground explosion analysis** (mining, oil & gas).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Mach number of shock | $M_s = D / c_0$ | $D$ = shock velocity, $c_0$ = ambient sound speed | dimensionless |
| 2 | Mass conservation (R-H) | $\rho_1(D - u_1) = \rho_2(D - u_2)$ | $\rho$ = density, $u$ = particle velocity | $\text{kg/m}^3$, m/s |
| 3 | Momentum conservation (R-H) | $P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1(D - u_1)(u_2 - u_1)$ | $P$ = pressure | Pa |
| 4 | Energy conservation (R-H) | $e_2 - e_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)(\tfrac{1}{\rho_1} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_2})$ | $e$ = specific internal energy | J/kg |
| 5 | Density ratio (strong shock) | $\rho_2 / \rho_1 = (\gamma + 1)/(\gamma - 1)$ | $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats | dimensionless |
| 6 | Pressure ratio (strong shock) | $P_2 / P_1 = \tfrac{2\gamma M_s^2 - (\gamma - 1)}{\gamma + 1}$ | $M_s$ = shock Mach number | dimensionless |
| 7 | Temperature ratio | $T_2 / T_1 = \tfrac{[2\gamma M_s^2 - (\gamma - 1)][(\gamma - 1)M_s^2 + 2]}{(\gamma + 1)^2 M_s^2}$ | $T$ = absolute temperature | dimensionless |
| 8 | Particle velocity | $u_2 = \tfrac{2 c_0 (M_s^2 - 1)}{(\gamma + 1) M_s}$ | post-shock particle velocity | m/s |
| 9 | CJ detonation velocity | $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)Q}$ | $Q$ = heat of detonation per unit mass | m/s |
| 10 | CJ pressure | $P_{CJ} = \tfrac{\rho_0 D_{CJ}^2}{\gamma + 1}$ | $\rho_0$ = initial density of explosive | Pa |
| 11 | CJ particle velocity | $u_{CJ} = D_{CJ} / (\gamma + 1)$ | post-CJ particle velocity | m/s |
| 12 | Sonic condition at CJ | $D_{CJ} = c_2 + u_2$ | $c_2$ = sound speed in reaction products | m/s |
| 13 | Sound speed in gas | $c_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $R$ = specific gas constant, $T$ = temperature | m/s |
| 14 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho_0 c_0$ | resistance to wave propagation | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rankine–Hugoniot Jump Conditions

> **Setup:** Consider a stationary shock wave moving to the right with velocity $D$ in a laboratory frame. To simplify, transform to a **shock-attached frame** moving with velocity $D$. In this frame, the gas flows *into* the shock from the left with velocity $w_1 = D - u_1$ and *exits* to the right with velocity $w_2 = D - u_2$.

#### Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Mass

A control volume enclosing the shock front has steady inflow and outflow:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 w_1 A = \rho_2 w_2 A$$

Dividing by area $A$ and writing $w_i = D - u_i$:

$$\rho_1 (D - u_1) = \rho_2 (D - u_2)$$

> **Logic:** Whatever mass per unit time enters must leave, since the shock is in steady state and matter cannot pile up at an infinitely thin interface.

Rearranging to extract the shock velocity $D$:

$$D = \frac{\rho_1 u_1 - \rho_2 u_2}{\rho_1 - \rho_2}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Momentum

Newton's second law for the control volume: net pressure force equals rate of change of momentum flux:

$$(P_1 - P_2) A = \dot{m} (w_2 - w_1)$$

Substituting $\dot{m}/A = \rho_1 w_1 = \rho_1 (D - u_1)$:

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 (D - u_1)(u_2 - u_1)$$

> **Logic:** The pressure jump across the shock must accelerate the gas from $u_1$ to $u_2$. This is the shock's "engine" — pressure pushing gas through the discontinuity.

#### Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Energy

First law for steady, adiabatic flow with no shaft work (the shock is a pure discontinuity):

$$h_1 + \frac{1}{2} w_1^2 = h_2 + \frac{1}{2} w_2^2$$

where $h = e + P/\rho$ is the specific enthalpy. Subtracting common terms and using $h = e + P/\rho$:

$$e_2 - e_1 = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

> **Logic:** The kinetic energy gained by the gas through the shock comes from the loss of internal energy due to compression. This is the famous **Hugoniot energy equation**.

This completes the three Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hugoniot Equation for a Calorically Perfect Gas

Starting with the energy equation, substitute the perfect-gas internal energy:

$$e = \frac{P}{(\gamma - 1)\rho}$$

Then:

$$e_2 - e_1 = \frac{P_2}{(\gamma - 1)\rho_2} - \frac{P_1}{(\gamma - 1)\rho_1}$$

Equating to the energy jump:

$$\frac{P_2}{(\gamma - 1)\rho_2} - \frac{P_1}{(\gamma - 1)\rho_1} = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

Multiply both sides by $2(\gamma - 1)$:

$$\frac{2P_2}{\rho_2} - \frac{2P_1}{\rho_1} = (P_1 + P_2)(\gamma - 1)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

Define the density ratio $\eta = \rho_2 / \rho_1$. Dividing through by $P_1 / \rho_1$:

$$\frac{2P_2}{P_1 \eta} - 2 = (\gamma - 1)\left(\frac{P_1 + P_2}{P_1}\right)\left(1 - \eta\right)$$

Let $\pi = P_2 / P_1$. After algebra:

$$\frac{2\pi}{\eta} - 2 = (\gamma - 1)(1 + \pi)(1 - \eta)$$

Solving for $\pi$ as a function of $\eta$:

$$\pi = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{(\gamma + 1)\eta - (\gamma - 1)}{(\gamma + 1) - (\gamma - 1)\eta}$$

> **Logic:** This is the **Rayleigh line / Hugoniot relation**. It tells us that for any prescribed density ratio $\eta$, the pressure ratio across the shock is *uniquely determined* for a perfect gas. The curve $\pi(\eta)$ plotted in $(P, V)$ space is the **Hugoniot curve**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Strong-Shock Limit

As the shock becomes very strong ($M_s \to \infty$), the upstream pressure $P_1$ becomes negligible compared to $P_2$. The Hugoniot relation reduces to:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \to \frac{2\gamma M_s^2}{\gamma + 1} \quad \text{as } M_s \to \infty$$

The density ratio approaches a finite ceiling:

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} \to \frac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma - 1}$$

For air ($\gamma = 1.4$): $\rho_2 / \rho_1 \to 6$ — air can be compressed at most by a factor of **6** in an infinitely strong shock.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A planar shock wave propagates through air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $P_1 = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$, $\rho_1 = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $T_1 = 288\ \text{K}$) with Mach number $M_s = 3$. Compute the post-shock pressure, density, temperature, and particle velocity.

**Step 1 — Pressure ratio:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{2\gamma M_s^2 - (\gamma - 1)}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{2(1.4)(9) - 0.4}{2.4} = \frac{25.2 - 0.4}{2.4} = \frac{24.8}{2.4} \approx 10.33$$

Therefore $P_2 = 10.33 \times 101.325\ \text{kPa} \approx \mathbf{1.047\ MPa}$.

**Step 2 — Density ratio:**

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{(\gamma + 1)M_s^2}{(\gamma - 1)M_s^2 + 2} = \frac{(2.4)(9)}{(0.4)(9) + 2} = \frac{21.6}{5.6} \approx 3.857$$

Therefore $\rho_2 = 3.857 \times 1.225 \approx \mathbf{4.725\ kg/m^3}$.

**Step 3 — Temperature ratio:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2/\rho_2}{P_1/\rho_1} = \frac{10.33}{3.857} \approx 2.678$$

Therefore $T_2 = 2.678 \times 288\ \text{K} \approx \mathbf{771.4\ K}$.

**Step 4 — Particle velocity:**

$$c_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_1} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 288} \approx 340.2\ \text{m/s}$$

$$D = M_s \cdot c_0 = 3 \times 340.2 = 1020.6\ \text{m/s}$$

$$u_2 = \frac{2 c_0 (M_s^2 - 1)}{(\gamma + 1) M_s} = \frac{2 \times 340.2 \times (9 - 1)}{2.4 \times 3} = \frac{5443.2}{7.2} \approx \mathbf{756.0\ m/s}$$

> **Sanity check:** $u_2 < D$ ✓ (downstream gas moves slower than shock), and $u_2 > 0$ ✓ (gas is pushed forward).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Zonal Structure of a Detonation-Driven Shock Wave

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["Unreacted Explosive Region 0"] --> A1["Leading Shock Front State 1"]
    A1 --> A2["Reaction Zone State 2 Reaction in Progress"]
    A2 --> A3["Chapman Jouguet Plane State CJ Sonic Condition"]
    A3 --> A4["Taylor Expansion Fan Rarefaction Region"]
    A4 --> A5["Fully Expanded Detonation Products State 3 Ambient Pressure"]

    A0 -.- note0["P0 rho0 T0 u0"]
    A1 -.- note1["P1 rho1 T1 u1 D velocity"]
    A2 -.- note2["Chemical energy release Heat of reaction Q"]
    A3 -.- note3["DCJ c2 plus u2 Sonic Relative to Front"]
    A4 -.- note4["Prandtl Meyer Expansion"]
    A5 -.- note5["P equals P0 Bursting into atmosphere"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Gas flows from left (unreacted) to right (products). The shock front compresses and ignites the explosive, the reaction zone liberates energy over a finite thickness, and the CJ plane marks the sonic point that fixes the detonation velocity. Beyond CJ, a rarefaction fan expands the products toward ambient pressure.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pressure–Volume Hugoniot Curve with Rayleigh Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUG["P versus V Hugoniot Plot"]
        O0["Initial State P1 V1 Origin of Shock"]
        O2["Final State P2 V2 Behind Shock"]
        RLINE["Rayleigh Line Straight Line from State 1 to State 2 Slope equals minus D squared times rho1 squared"]
        ISEN["Isentrope Curve through State 1 Subsonic Compression"]
        HUGC["Hugoniot Curve Locus of All Possible Shock States"]

        O0 --- RLINE
        O0 --- HUGC
        O2 --- RLINE
        O2 --- HUGC
        ISEN --- O0
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Rayleigh line** is a straight chord from initial state to shocked state, whose slope is $-D^2\rho_1^2$. The **Hugoniot curve** is the locus of all thermodynamic states reachable by a single shock from state 1. The **intersection** of the Rayleigh line with the Hugoniot gives the actual shock state. The isentrope lies *below* the Hugoniot for compression (entropy increases across a shock).

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Flow from Detonation to Blast Damage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["Explosive Charge Detonation Initiation"] --> SW["Reacting Shock Wave Travels Through Charge"]
    SW --> EM["Emergence into Surrounding Air as Blast Wave"]
    EM --> PS["Peak Overpressure Region P2 greater than P0"]
    PS --> EXP["Exponential Pressure Decay with Distance"]
    EXP --> IMP["Impulse Transfer to Structures and Personnel"]
    IMP --> DMG["Damage Assessment Cratering Fragmentation Injury"]
```

> **Engineering takeaway:** The same shock wave changes its character — from a *reacting* shock inside the charge to a *non-reacting* blast wave outside — but the Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions govern both. Damage correlates with both **peak overpressure** and **impulse** (the time-integrated pressure load).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define a shock wave. Explain why a shock wave is not an isentropic process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **shock wave** is a thin, propagating discontinuity in fluid properties (pressure, density, temperature, particle velocity) across which there is an *abrupt* jump occurring over a distance of a few molecular mean free paths. It travels at a velocity **greater than the local speed of sound** in the undisturbed medium ahead.

> A shock wave is **not isentropic** because:
> 1. Across the discontinuity, the process is **adiabatic but irreversible** — viscous dissipation and thermal conduction within the shock front produce entropy.
> 2. The Rankine–Hugoniot energy equation can be shown (using the second law) to imply $\Delta s > 0$ for any real shock with $P_2 > P_1$.
> 3. Mathematically, the Hugoniot curve lies *above* the isentrope in the $(P, V)$ plane for compression, confirming the entropy increase.

**(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for shock thickness/irreversibility, 1 mark for entropy reasoning.)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**State the Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions. What physical conservation laws do they represent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions for a shock wave moving with velocity $D$ are:
> 
> 1. **Mass (Continuity):** $\rho_1 (D - u_1) = \rho_2 (D - u_2)$ — *Conservation of mass.*
> 2. **Momentum:** $P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 (D - u_1)(u_2 - u_1)$ — *Conservation of momentum (Newton's second law).*
> 3. **Energy:** $e_2 - e_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)(\tfrac{1}{\rho_1} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_2})$ — *Conservation of energy (First Law of Thermodynamics).*

> These three relations govern the jump in fluid state across a moving discontinuity and are the foundation of all steady-state shock analysis in explosives and compressible flow.

**(Award: 1.5 marks for writing the three equations correctly, 1.5 marks for naming the corresponding conservation laws.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** Derive the Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions for a one-dimensional steady shock wave. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. One-dimensional, planar shock.
2. Steady state in shock-attached frame.
3. Adiabatic, no heat addition across front.
4. Inviscid flow (viscous effects confined to thin shock layer).
5. No mass sources or chemical reaction at the front (pure mechanical shock).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

*Step 1 — Mass Conservation:*
Consider a control volume fixed to the shock. In the shock-attached frame, the inflow velocity is $w_1 = D - u_1$ and the outflow velocity is $w_2 = D - u_2$. Steady mass flux balance gives:

$$\rho_1 w_1 = \rho_2 w_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \rho_1(D - u_1) = \rho_2(D - u_2)$$

*Step 2 — Momentum Conservation:*
Net pressure force on the CV equals the rate of change of momentum flux:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_2 w_2^2 - \rho_1 w_1^2$$

Using $\rho_1 w_1 = \rho_2 w_2 = \dot{m}/A$ and rearranging:

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1(D - u_1)(u_2 - u_1)$$

*Step 3 — Energy Conservation:*
First law for steady, adiabatic, no-work flow: total enthalpy is conserved across the shock:

$$h_1 + \frac{w_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{w_2^2}{2}$$

Expanding $h = e + P/\rho$ and simplifying:

$$e_2 - e_1 = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

These three relations are the **Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions**.

---

**(b)** A strong shock wave propagates through air ($\gamma = 1.4$) at a Mach number of $M_s = 2.5$. The ambient conditions are $P_1 = 100\ \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$. Compute the post-shock pressure, density, temperature, particle velocity, and the shock velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute ambient sound speed and shock velocity (1 mark):**

$$c_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_1} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 300} = \sqrt{120540} \approx 347.2\ \text{m/s}$$

$$D = M_s \times c_0 = 2.5 \times 347.2 = 868.0\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Post-shock pressure (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{2\gamma M_s^2 - (\gamma - 1)}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{2(1.4)(6.25) - 0.4}{2.4} = \frac{17.5 - 0.4}{2.4} = \frac{17.1}{2.4} = 7.125$$

$$P_2 = 7.125 \times 100\ \text{kPa} = \mathbf{712.5\ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Post-shock density (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{(\gamma + 1) M_s^2}{(\gamma - 1) M_s^2 + 2} = \frac{2.4 \times 6.25}{0.4 \times 6.25 + 2} = \frac{15.0}{4.5} = 3.333$$

With $\rho_1 = P_1 / (R T_1) = 100000 / (287 \times 300) = 1.161\ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$\rho_2 = 3.333 \times 1.161 = \mathbf{3.871\ kg/m^3}$$

**Step 4 — Post-shock temperature (1 mark):**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2/\rho_2}{P_1/\rho_1} = \frac{7.125}{3.333} = 2.1375$$

$$T_2 = 2.1375 \times 300 = \mathbf{641.3\ K}$$

**Step 5 — Post-shock particle velocity (1 mark):**

$$u_2 = \frac{2 c_0 (M_s^2 - 1)}{(\gamma + 1) M_s} = \frac{2 \times 347.2 \times (6.25 - 1)}{2.4 \times 2.5} = \frac{2 \times 347.2 \times 5.25}{6.0} = \frac{3645.6}{6.0} = \mathbf{607.6\ m/s}$$

> **Final answer summary:** $D = 868.0\ \text{m/s}$, $P_2 = 712.5\ \text{kPa}$, $\rho_2 = 3.871\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $T_2 = 641.3\ \text{K}$, $u_2 = 607.6\ \text{m/s}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** What is the **Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) condition**? Explain its physical significance in the theory of detonation waves. Derive the expression for the CJ detonation velocity in terms of the heat of detonation $Q$ and the polytropic index $\gamma$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of CJ Condition (2 marks):**

> The **Chapman–Jouguet condition** states that for a *self-sustained steady detonation*, the detonation velocity $D_{CJ}$ is the **minimum velocity** at which the Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions, combined with the heat release $Q$ from the chemical reaction, can be satisfied. Equivalently, at the CJ plane (the rear boundary of the reaction zone), the flow velocity relative to the shock front is **exactly sonic**:
> 
> $$D_{CJ} = c_2 + u_2$$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**

> - The sonic condition at CJ **decouples** the reaction zone from the rear boundary: rarefaction waves from the back of the charge **cannot** overtake the front, ensuring the detonation runs at a *fixed, well-defined velocity*.
> - This explains why the detonation velocity of a given explosive at a given density is a *reproducible material property* — independent of charge size, confinement, or initiation strength (above a threshold).
> - It also explains why detonation is *self-sustaining*: the shock provides the activation energy for the next layer of explosive, which in turn supplies the energy to drive the shock.

**Derivation of $D_{CJ}$ (3 marks):**

The total specific energy of the detonation products is $e_2 = e_1 + Q + \frac{1}{2}(u_1^2 - u_2^2) + \frac{P_1}{\rho_1} - \frac{P_2}{\rho_2}$ (using steady-state energy equation with chemical heat release $Q$). For an unreacted explosive initially at rest ($u_1 = 0$) and ambient pressure ($P_1 \approx 0$), the energy equation simplifies to:

$$e_2 - e_1 = Q - \frac{D_{CJ}^2}{2}\left(1 - \frac{\rho_1^2}{\rho_2^2}\right) + \frac{P_2}{\rho_2} - \frac{P_1}{\rho_1}$$

Imposing the CJ sonic condition and the perfect-gas equation of state, and combining with the momentum and mass equations, one obtains after algebra (see standard texts):

$$D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)Q}$$

> **Physical meaning:** $D_{CJ}$ scales with the *square root* of the heat of detonation $Q$ — a higher energy release produces a faster, more powerful detonation.

---

**(b)** A column of TNT ($\rho_0 = 1630\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $Q = 4.5 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}$, $\gamma = 3.0$ for detonation products) is detonated. Calculate (i) the CJ detonation velocity, (ii) the CJ pressure, and (iii) the CJ particle velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CJ Detonation Velocity (2.5 marks):**

$$D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)Q} = \sqrt{2(9 - 1)(4.5 \times 10^6)} = \sqrt{2 \times 8 \times 4.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$= \sqrt{72 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^7} \approx \mathbf{8485\ m/s}$$

(This matches the known TNT detonation velocity of ~6900 m/s using a more accurate $\gamma \approx 2.7$; the discrepancy is due to the simplified $\gamma$ used in the textbook formula.)

**Step 2 — CJ Pressure (2.5 marks):**

$$P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 D_{CJ}^2}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{1630 \times (8485)^2}{3.0 + 1} = \frac{1630 \times 7.2 \times 10^7}{4}$$

$$= \frac{1.1736 \times 10^{11}}{4} \approx 2.93 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Pa} \approx \mathbf{29.3\ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — CJ Particle Velocity (2 marks):**

$$u_{CJ} = \frac{D_{CJ}}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{8485}{4} \approx \mathbf{2121\ m/s}$$

> **Sanity check:** $u_{CJ} < D_{CJ}$ ✓. The particle velocity is roughly $\sim$25% of the detonation velocity, consistent with textbook CJ values for condensed explosives.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Examiner's Advisory:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to use the shock-attached frame** in the Rankine–Hugoniot derivation → loses 2–3 marks immediately. Always begin by stating $w_i = D - u_i$.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $u_2$ (particle velocity) with $D$ (shock velocity).** The particle velocity is the *velocity of the gas* behind the shock; the shock velocity is the *velocity of the discontinuity itself*. They are **not equal** except in the CJ sonic condition.
> 
> 3. **Using $P_1 \approx 0$ for air shocks** without justification. Only valid in the *strong-shock* limit. For $M_s < 3$, the ambient pressure contribution is non-negligible.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up the Hugoniot curve with the isentrope.** Hugoniot locus = *all possible shock end-states* from a given initial state; isentrope = *reversible adiabatic* path. The Rankine–Hugoniot energy equation is for the Hugoniot, not the isentrope.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing or incorrect SI units. Always state units in the boxed final answer.
> 
> 6. **Sign errors in momentum equation.** The standard form is $P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 (D - u_1)(u_2 - u_1)$. Verify that this yields $P_2 > P_1$ for a forward shock (compression).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Recall Bullets:**
> 
> - **Shock wave** = thin, supersonic, compressive discontinuity in fluid properties.
> - **Mach number** $M_s = D / c_0$ — must exceed **1** for a true shock.
> - **Rankine–Hugoniot conditions** = mass, momentum, and energy conservation across the front — *three equations, three unknowns* ($P_2, \rho_2, u_2$) for given $D$.
> - **Hugoniot curve** ≠ **Isentrope**; entropy strictly *increases* across a real shock.
> - **Strong-shock limit:** $P_2 / P_1 \to 2\gamma M_s^2 / (\gamma + 1)$; $\rho_2 / \rho_1 \to (\gamma + 1)/(\gamma - 1) = 6$ for air.
> - **CJ condition:** $D_{CJ} = c_2 + u_2$ — sonic at CJ plane; fixes *unique* detonation velocity.
> - **CJ velocity formula:** $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)Q}$.
> - **CJ pressure:** $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D_{CJ}^2 / (\gamma + 1)$.
> - **CJ particle velocity:** $u_{CJ} = D_{CJ} / (\gamma + 1)$.
> - **Detonation** is a *reacting* shock (self-sustaining); **blast wave** is the *non-reacting* shock emerging from the charge.
> - **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho_0 c_0$ governs shock transmission/reflection at material interfaces.
> - Typical TNT: $D \approx 6900\ \text{m/s}$, $P_{CJ} \approx 21\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_0 = 1630\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
> - Typical PETN: $D \approx 8400\ \text{m/s}$, $P_{CJ} \approx 33\ \text{GPa}$.
> - For air at STP: $c_0 \approx 340\ \text{m/s}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\rho_0 = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
> - Always use the **shock-attached frame** for derivations; always state the **assumptions** explicitly.
> - Always check the **signs** of jumps ($P_2 > P_1$ for compression shocks) and the **ordering** ($u_2 < D$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detonation Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Detonation Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **detonation wave** is a self-sustaining, supersonic shock front propagating through an explosive medium, behind which an extremely rapid exothermic chemical reaction occurs within a very thin reaction zone. The energy released by this reaction continuously reinforces the leading shock, allowing the wave to travel at a steady, characteristic velocity (the **detonation velocity**, typically **1,500 m/s – 9,000 m/s**) without any external energy input.

Mathematically, a detonation wave is described as a coupled **reactive shock discontinuity** that satisfies the Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions plus the species continuity equation, together with the equation of state of the explosive and its detonation products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A detonation wave is fundamentally distinguished from a deflagration (subsonic combustion) by:
> 1. **Supersonic propagation** (Mach number relative to unreacted gas $M > 1$).
> 2. **Pressure-supported shock front** where the leading shock compresses the explosive before reaction occurs.
> 3. **Self-sustaining nature** driven by the chemistry within the reaction zone, not by heat conduction or diffusion.

---

## 1.2 The ZND Model (Zel'dovich – von Neumann – Döring)

The modern, accepted physical picture of a one-dimensional steady detonation wave is captured by the **ZND Model**:

| Zone | Physical State | Key Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 1 — Unreacted Explosive** | Ambient $P_0$, $\rho_0$, $T_0$ | Initial state |
| **Zone 2 — Leading Shock (von Neumann Spike)** | Compressed, unreacted | $P_{VN}$ — peak pressure |
| **Zone 3 — Reaction Zone** | Chemical energy released | Finite width $\Delta$ |
| **Zone 4 — Detonation Products (CJ Plane)** | Thermodynamic equilibrium | $P_{CJ}$, $V_{CJ}$, $T_{CJ}$ |

The **Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) plane** is defined at the sonic point where the flow velocity relative to the wave equals the local speed of sound in the products: $u_{CJ} = c_{CJ}$.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Snowplough Analogy for Detonation Waves:**
> Imagine a **massive snowplough truck** (the leading shock) pushing into a deep field of dry, compacted sawdust (the explosive medium). The truck compresses the sawdust instantly (von Neumann spike). Behind the truck, an enormous friction-driven fire ignites from the heat of compression (reaction zone), converting sawdust into expanding hot gas (detonation products). The expanding gas pushes the truck forward, which in turn compresses more sawdust — creating a self-driving, self-sustaining machine.
>
> - The **truck cannot stop** without losing its pushing force.
> - The **fire cannot expand forward of the truck** because the truck is supersonic relative to the sawdust.
> - The system reaches a **steady cruising speed** — this is the detonation velocity.

This is why detonations are so destructive: the entire explosive energy is liberated in a *thin zone* travelling at *kilometres per second*, producing pressures of **10–40 GPa** in the reaction zone.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Characteristic Ranges

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (for condensed explosives) |
|---|---|---|
| Detonation velocity | $D$ | **5,500 – 9,000 m/s** (TNT ≈ 6,900 m/s, PETN ≈ 8,400 m/s) |
| Detonation (CJ) pressure | $P_{CJ}$ | **10 – 40 GPa** |
| von Neumann spike pressure | $P_{VN}$ | **20 – 60 GPa** (typically 1.5 – 2× $P_{CJ}$) |
| Reaction zone thickness | $\Delta$ | **0.1 – 10 mm** |
| Detonation temperature | $T_{CJ}$ | **2,500 – 4,500 K** |
| Detonation (CJ) density | $\rho_{CJ}$ | $\approx 1.4 - 1.7 \rho_0$ |

---

## 1.5 Detonation vs. Deflagration — The Critical Contrast

> [!WARNING]
> This distinction is a **favourite KTU question**. Memorise the comparison.

| Property | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Propagation speed | Subsonic ($M < 1$) | Supersonic ($M > 1$) |
| Driving mechanism | Heat conduction, diffusion | Shock compression + chemistry |
| Pressure front | Near ambient | Elevated (GPa range) |
| Damage mechanism | Quasi-static pressure, thermal | Brisant, shattering, hydrodynamic |
| Typical velocity | mm/s to m/s | km/s |
| Reaction zone | Thick (mm–cm) | Very thin ($\mu$m–mm) |
| Example | Gunpowder burning in open | High explosive charge initiation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Conservation Laws Across the Detonation Front

For a steady, planar, one-dimensional detonation moving at velocity $D$ into stationary unreacted explosive, mass, momentum, and energy must all be conserved. Let subscript $0$ denote the unreacted state and subscript $1$ denote the reacted (CJ) state.

### Step 2.1.1 — Mass Conservation (Continuity)

$$
\rho_0 \, D = \rho_1 \, (D - u_1)
$$

where $u_1$ is the particle velocity of the products in the laboratory frame.

### Step 2.1.2 — Momentum Conservation

$$
P_0 + \rho_0 D^2 = P_1 + \rho_1 (D - u_1)^2
$$

### Step 2.1.3 — Energy Conservation (Rankine–Hugoniot Energy Equation)

$$
e_1 - e_0 = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_0)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_0} - \frac{1}{\rho_1}\right) + Q
$$

where $e$ is the specific internal energy and $Q$ is the chemical energy released per unit mass.

---

## 2.2 The Rayleigh Line & Hugoniot Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hugoniot curve** is the locus of all thermodynamically permissible end states satisfying the three conservation laws for a given initial state and heat release $Q$.
>
> **The Rayleigh line** is the locus of states satisfying mass + momentum conservation alone (a straight line in $P$–$V$ space of slope $-\rho_0^2 D^2$).

A valid detonation solution occurs at the intersection of the Rayleigh line with the Hugoniot curve. For a given $D$, the line has a specific slope; the CJ condition uniquely fixes $D$.

---

## 2.3 Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CJ Condition:** The detonation velocity is the *minimum* supersonic velocity for which a steady solution exists on the strong detonation branch. This minimum corresponds to the point where the Rayleigh line is **tangent** to the Hugoniot curve of the reaction products.

Mathematically, the CJ condition states that the flow at the CJ plane is **sonic** relative to the wave:

$$
D = u_1 + c_1 \Big|_{CJ}
$$

where $c_1 = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s}$ is the local speed of sound in the products.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D^2 = V_0^2 \dfrac{(P_1 - P_0)}{(V_0 - V_1)}$ | Rayleigh line equation (square of detonation velocity) |
| 2 | $e_1 - e_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_0)(V_0 - V_1) + Q$ | Hugoniot energy equation; $V = 1/\rho$ |
| 3 | $P_{CJ} \approx \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$ | CJ pressure for ideal gas with constant $\gamma$ |
| 4 | $\rho_{CJ} \approx \dfrac{\rho_0(\gamma + 1)}{\gamma}$ | CJ density |
| 5 | $u_{CJ} = \dfrac{D}{\gamma + 1}$ | CJ particle velocity |
| 6 | $T_{CJ} = \dfrac{2Q(\gamma^2 - 1)}{(\gamma + 1)^2 \, c_v}$ | CJ temperature (ideal-gas approximation) |
| 7 | $P_{VN} \approx \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}\left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma^2 - 1}{\gamma}\right)$ | von Neumann peak pressure |
| 8 | $E_{min} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{2(\gamma + 1)}$ | Minimum (chemical + kinetic) energy density at CJ plane |
| 9 | $M_{CJ} = \dfrac{D}{c_0}$ | Mach number of detonation relative to unreacted medium |
| 10 | $\Delta t \approx \dfrac{a}{D}$ | Reaction zone residence time; $a$ = reaction zone length |
| 11 | $L = \dfrac{a}{c_v T_{CJ}}$ | Dimensionless reaction zone parameter |

> **Engineering Note on Units:** $P$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $D$ in m/s, $Q$ in J/kg. For condensed explosives use **GPa** ($1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$). $\gamma = C_P/C_V$ is the ratio of specific heats of detonation products (typically $\gamma \approx 2.5 - 3.0$ for condensed explosives).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Blasting Engineering (Mining & Quarrying):** Detonation velocity $D$ directly determines the **brisance** (shattering effect) and the rock fragmentation pattern. Engineers choose explosives by matching $D$ to the rock impedance.
2. **Munitions Design:** Shaped-charge jets and detonation wave shaping rely on controlled $D$ gradients (e.g., wave-shaper liners producing explosive lenses).
3. **Safety Engineering:** Critical diameter $d_c$ and failure diameter $d_f$ depend on $D$ and reaction zone length — these determine safe charge geometries and booster requirements.
4. **Defence & Propulsion:** Pulse detonation engines (PDE) exploit the cyclic nature of detonation waves for thrust generation.
5. **Forensic Investigation:** Detonation residue patterns, crater dimensions, and shock damage signatures are back-calculated using the ZND model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: From Conservation Laws to CJ Pressure

### Step 1 — Combine Mass and Momentum Equations

Starting from:

$$
\rho_0 D = \rho_1 (D - u_1)
$$

Solve for $(D - u_1)$:

$$
(D - u_1) = \dfrac{\rho_0 D}{\rho_1}
$$

Substitute into the momentum equation:

$$
P_0 + \rho_0 D^2 = P_1 + \rho_1 \left(\dfrac{\rho_0 D}{\rho_1}\right)^2
$$

Simplify the second term on the RHS:

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{\rho_0^2 D^2}{\rho_1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_1 - P_0 = \rho_0 D^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_1}\right)
$$

Using specific volumes $V = 1/\rho$, so $1 - \dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_1} = 1 - \dfrac{V_1}{V_0} = \dfrac{V_0 - V_1}{V_0}$:

$$
P_1 - P_0 = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2 (V_0 - V_1)}{V_0}
$$

Multiplying by $V_0$:

$$
(P_1 - P_0) V_0 = \rho_0 D^2 (V_0 - V_1)
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $D^2$

$$
D^2 = V_0^2 \, \dfrac{(P_1 - P_0)}{(V_0 - V_1)}
$$

This is the **Rayleigh line equation** — a linear relationship between $P_1$ and $V_1$ for a given $D$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CJ Pressure (Ideal-Gas Polytropic Approximation)

For detonation products behaving as a polytropic gas with $P V^\gamma = \text{const}$, the speed of sound is:

$$
c_1^2 = \gamma \dfrac{P_1}{\rho_1} = \gamma P_1 V_1
$$

The CJ condition $D = u_1 + c_1$ combined with the continuity equation $u_1 = D(1 - \rho_0/\rho_1) = D(1 - V_1/V_0)$ gives:

$$
c_1 = D - u_1 = D \dfrac{V_1}{V_0}
$$

Square both sides:

$$
c_1^2 = D^2 \dfrac{V_1^2}{V_0^2}
$$

Equating with the sound speed expression:

$$
\gamma P_1 V_1 = D^2 \dfrac{V_1^2}{V_0^2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\gamma P_1 = D^2 \dfrac{V_1}{V_0^2}
$$

Now use the Rayleigh line from §3.1, $D^2 = V_0^2 (P_1 - P_0)/(V_0 - V_1)$, and substitute (neglecting $P_0 \ll P_1$):

$$
\gamma P_1 = \dfrac{V_0^2 (P_1 - P_0)}{(V_0 - V_1)} \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{V_0^2}
$$

$$
\gamma P_1 = \dfrac{V_1 (P_1 - P_0)}{(V_0 - V_1)}
$$

For a strong detonation, $P_0 \ll P_1$:

$$
\gamma \approx \dfrac{V_1}{V_0 - V_1}
$$

Solving for $V_1/V_0$:

$$
V_1 = V_0 \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma + 1}
$$

Since $\rho_1 / \rho_0 = V_0 / V_1$:

$$
\rho_{CJ} = \rho_0 \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma}
$$

Substituting back into $P_1 = D^2 V_1 / (\gamma V_0)$:

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{D^2}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{V_0} = \dfrac{D^2}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma + 1} = \dfrac{D^2}{\gamma + 1}
$$

Multiplying by $\rho_0$ to restore dimensional units:

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}
$$

This is the **detonation (CJ) pressure** — one of the most heavily used formulas in KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CJ Particle Velocity and Temperature

### CJ Particle Velocity

From continuity:

$$
u_{CJ} = D\left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_{CJ}}\right) = D\left(1 - \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma + 1}\right) = \dfrac{D}{\gamma + 1}
$$

### CJ Temperature

For an ideal gas, $e = c_v T$ and the Hugoniot energy equation becomes:

$$
c_v(T_{CJ} - T_0) = \tfrac{1}{2}(P_{CJ} + P_0)(V_0 - V_{CJ}) + Q
$$

Using $P_{CJ} V_{CJ} = R T_{CJ} / M$ (where $R/M = c_v(\gamma - 1)$) and neglecting $P_0$ and $T_0$:

$$
T_{CJ} = \dfrac{2Q(\gamma^2 - 1)}{(\gamma + 1)^2 \, c_v}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: von Neumann Spike Pressure

The von Neumann state is the peak pressure experienced by the explosive just behind the leading shock but before significant reaction has occurred. It lies on the **unreacted Hugoniot** (the Hugoniot curve for $Q = 0$).

For an unreacted polytropic gas with the same $\gamma$, using the Rankine–Hugoniot relations for a simple shock:

$$
P_{VN} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}\left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma^2 - 1}{\gamma}\right)
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
P_{VN} = P_{CJ} \cdot \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma + 1}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** For condensed explosives with $\gamma \approx 3$, this gives $P_{VN} \approx 1.5 \, P_{CJ}$, confirming the typical ratio quoted in Section 1.4.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A column of TNT has initial density $\rho_0 = 1,630 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and detonation velocity $D = 6,900 \text{ m/s}$. Assuming the detonation products obey a polytropic law with $\gamma = 3.0$, calculate:
(a) CJ pressure, (b) CJ density, (c) CJ particle velocity, (d) von Neumann spike pressure.

### (a) CJ Pressure

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1} = \dfrac{1630 \times (6900)^2}{4.0}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{1630 \times 4.761 \times 10^7}{4} = \dfrac{7.7604 \times 10^{10}}{4}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = 1.9401 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} \approx \mathbf{19.4 \text{ GPa}}
$$

### (b) CJ Density

$$
\rho_{CJ} = \rho_0 \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma} = 1630 \times \dfrac{4}{3} = \mathbf{2,173.3 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

### (c) CJ Particle Velocity

$$
u_{CJ} = \dfrac{D}{\gamma + 1} = \dfrac{6900}{4} = \mathbf{1,725 \text{ m/s}}
$$

### (d) von Neumann Spike Pressure

$$
P_{VN} = P_{CJ} \cdot \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma + 1} = 19.4 \times \dfrac{6}{4} = 19.4 \times 1.5 = \mathbf{29.1 \text{ GPa}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always **state the formula first**, then substitute numerical values, and **carry units** throughout the calculation. Final answer must be in SI units (Pa) or convenient submultiples (GPa).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Rayleigh Line, Hugoniot Curves, and CJ Point

```mermaid

        Y -->|Upper Branch: Detonation| L2[Strong Detonation Solutions]
        L2 -->|Minimum Slope: CJ Tangency| CJ[CJ Point: Unique Stable Detonation]
        CJ -->|Downstream: Taylor Expansion| L3[Expansion Fan]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define a **detonation wave**. State **any two** distinguishing features between detonation and deflagration.

**Model Answer:**

A detonation wave is a self-sustaining shock wave propagating at supersonic speed through an explosive medium, behind which an exothermic chemical reaction occurs within a thin reaction zone. The energy released continuously reinforces the leading shock front.

Two distinguishing features:

| Feature | Detonation | Deflagration |
|---|---|---|
| Propagation | Supersonic ($M > 1$, $D \approx 1.5$–$9 \text{ km/s}$) | Subsonic ($D < 1 \text{ m/s typically}$) |
| Driving mechanism | Hydrodynamic shock compression | Heat conduction and mass diffusion |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two features)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is the **Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) condition**? Why is it important in detonation physics?

**Model Answer:**

The Chapman–Jouguet condition states that at the reaction-zone terminus, the flow of detonation products relative to the wave is **sonic**, i.e.:

$$
D = u_{CJ} + c_{CJ}
$$

**Importance:**
1. It uniquely determines the steady-state detonation velocity $D$ (the minimum supersonic solution tangent to the Hugoniot).
2. It fixes all downstream thermodynamic state variables ($P_{CJ}$, $\rho_{CJ}$, $T_{CJ}$, $u_{CJ}$).
3. It defines the locus where the leading shock can no longer be overtaken by rearward disturbances, ensuring **self-sustained, steady propagation**.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for sonic condition, 1 for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B.A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Choice 1

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **ZND (Zel'dovich–von Neumann–Döring) model** of a steady one-dimensional detonation wave. Identify and explain the significance of the **von Neumann spike** and the **Chapman–Jouguet plane**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**ZND Model Description:**
The ZND model is a one-dimensional, steady-state, plane-wave model of detonation consisting of four distinct zones:

1. **Unreacted explosive zone** — ahead of the wave, the explosive is at ambient conditions ($P_0$, $\rho_0$, $T_0$).
2. **Leading shock (von Neumann spike)** — an instantaneous, non-reactive jump that compresses the explosive to peak pressure $P_{VN}$ (typically 1.5–2× $P_{CJ}$). At this point the medium is hot and dense but **unreacted**.
3. **Reaction zone** — of finite width $\Delta$ (typically 0.1–10 mm for condensed explosives). Chemistry proceeds rapidly, releasing energy $Q$, while pressure decays from $P_{VN}$ to $P_{CJ}$.
4. **Detonation products beyond CJ plane** — Taylor rarefaction wave expands products into the surroundings.

**Significance of von Neumann spike:** Marks the *peak pressure* experienced by the explosive and represents the **initial state from which chemical reaction proceeds**. Its value is critical for **initiation sensitivity** calculations and reaction-zone modelling.

**Significance of CJ plane:** The sonic terminus of the reaction zone. Defines the **steady-state, self-sustaining** detonation velocity $D$ via the CJ tangency condition. All downstream flow is governed by this plane as the reference state.

*(Marks distribution: 3 for sketch, 2 for zone description, 1 for VN significance, 1 for CJ significance)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Starting from the **conservation equations**, derive the expression for the **detonation (CJ) pressure** $P_{CJ}$ in terms of initial density $\rho_0$, detonation velocity $D$, and the polytropic exponent $\gamma$ of the products.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Write conservation laws across the detonation front:**

Mass:
$$
\rho_0 D = \rho_1 (D - u_1)
$$

Momentum:
$$
P_0 + \rho_0 D^2 = P_1 + \rho_1 (D - u_1)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Combine mass and momentum to get the Rayleigh line:**

Eliminate $(D - u_1) = \rho_0 D / \rho_1$:

$$
(P_1 - P_0) = \rho_0 D^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho_1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CJ sonic condition:**

$$
D = u_1 + c_1, \quad c_1^2 = \gamma P_1 / \rho_1
$$

Using continuity, $u_1 = D(1 - \rho_0/\rho_1)$, so $c_1 = D \rho_0/\rho_1$:

$$
\gamma \dfrac{P_1}{\rho_1} = D^2 \dfrac{\rho_0^2}{\rho_1^2}
$$

$$
\gamma P_1 = D^2 \dfrac{\rho_0^2}{\rho_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\rho_1$ from Rayleigh and substitute:**

For strong detonation $P_0 \ll P_1$:

$$
\rho_1 = \rho_0 \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain $P_{CJ}$:**

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}
$$

*(Marks distribution: 2 for writing conservation laws, 2 for combining them, 2 for applying CJ condition, 1 for final result with units)*

---

### Question B.B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice 2

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **Rayleigh line** and the **Hugoniot curve** for a reactive shock. Using a $P$–$V$ diagram, explain how the **Chapman–Jouguet condition** selects the unique steady-state detonation solution.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Rayleigh Line:** The locus of all $(P_1, V_1)$ states that satisfy mass and momentum conservation across a shock of given strength, plotted in $P$–$V$ space as a straight line:

$$
P_1 - P_0 = -\rho_0^2 D^2 (V_1 - V_0)
$$

**Hugoniot Curve:** The locus of all $(P_1, V_1)$ states satisfying mass, momentum, and energy conservation for a given heat release $Q$:

$$
e_1 - e_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_0)(V_0 - V_1) + Q
$$

For $Q = 0$, this is the **unreacted Hugoniot**; for $Q > 0$, it is the **reacted Hugoniot** (shifted upward).

**Geometric Argument:**

A valid detonation requires the Rayleigh line to intersect the reacted Hugoniot curve. Among the family of Rayleigh lines (parametrised by $D$), only one specific slope produces a single point of **tangency** — this is the **CJ point**. Mathematically, this tangency implies:

$$
\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_{Rayleigh} = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_{Hugoniot, \, products}
$$

This tangency condition is equivalent to the sonic condition $D = u_1 + c_1$.

For Rayleigh lines **steeper** than the CJ line, two intersections exist (over-driven detonation, unstable). For **shallower** lines, no real solution exists — the detonation fails. Hence the CJ condition uniquely fixes the minimum stable detonation velocity.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for definitions, 2 for P-V diagram description, 3 for CJ tangency argument)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A charge of PETN ($\rho_0 = 1,770 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $D = 8,400 \text{ m/s}$) detonates steadily. Assuming detonation products behave as a polytropic gas with $\gamma = 2.8$, calculate:
(i) CJ pressure, (ii) CJ density, (iii) CJ particle velocity, (iv) CJ temperature, given $c_v = 1,500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ and heat of detonation $Q = 5.8 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**(i) CJ Pressure:**

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1} = \dfrac{1770 \times (8400)^2}{3.8}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{1770 \times 7.056 \times 10^7}{3.8} = \dfrac{1.2489 \times 10^{11}}{3.8}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = 3.286 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} \approx \mathbf{32.9 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**(ii) CJ Density:**

$$
\rho_{CJ} = \rho_0 \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma} = 1770 \times \dfrac{3.8}{2.8} = \mathbf{2,402.1 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

**(iii) CJ Particle Velocity:**

$$
u_{CJ} = \dfrac{D}{\gamma + 1} = \dfrac{8400}{3.8} = \mathbf{2,210.5 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(iv) CJ Temperature:**

$$
T_{CJ} = \dfrac{2Q(\gamma^2 - 1)}{(\gamma + 1)^2 \, c_v}
$$

$$
T_{CJ} = \dfrac{2 \times 5.8 \times 10^6 \times (2.8^2 - 1)}{(3.8)^2 \times 1500}
$$

$$
T_{CJ} = \dfrac{2 \times 5.8 \times 10^6 \times 6.84}{14.44 \times 1500} = \dfrac{7.9344 \times 10^7}{21,660}
$$

$$
T_{CJ} \approx \mathbf{3,663 \text{ K}}
$$

*(Marks distribution: ~1.5 each sub-part + 0.5 for units consistency)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Detonation Wave Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing detonation velocity $D$ with particle velocity $u_{CJ}$.** Many students use $D$ as both. Remember: $u_{CJ} = D/(\gamma + 1)$, which is much smaller than $D$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that $\gamma$ refers to the detonation products, NOT the unreacted explosive.** The polytropic exponent of the products is typically $\gamma \approx 2.5 - 3.0$.
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency.** $P$ must come out in **Pascals (Pa)** from $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$. Converting to GPa requires division by $10^9$. A 19-GPa answer written as $1.9 \times 10^{10}$ Pa is correct but conversion is expected.
>
> 4. **Neglecting the CJ sonic condition.** Writing $P_1 = \rho_0 D^2 V_1 / (\gamma V_0)$ without invoking $D = u_1 + c_1$ leads to a wrong $\rho_1$.
>
> 5. **In sketches, omitting the rarefaction (Taylor) wave** beyond the CJ plane. This is a frequent omission in ZND model diagrams.
>
> 6. **Treating detonation as analogous to combustion.** Detonation is a *hydrodynamic* phenomenon driven by shock chemistry, not a thermal diffusion process.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Detonation Waves**

- [ ] A **detonation wave** is a *self-sustaining, supersonic reactive shock* — propagate without external input at velocity $D \approx 1.5$–$9 \text{ km/s}$.
- [ ] The **ZND model** comprises 4 zones: Unreacted → von Neumann Spike → Reaction Zone → CJ Plane → Rarefaction.
- [ ] The **von Neumann spike** is the *peak pressure* (unreacted, compressed) — typically $P_{VN} \approx 1.5 \, P_{CJ}$.
- [ ] The **CJ plane** is the *sonic terminus* of the reaction zone, where $D = u_{CJ} + c_{CJ}$.
- [ ] The **CJ condition** is the **minimum-velocity, tangent-to-Hugoniot** solution — it uniquely fixes $D$.
- [ ] **Rayleigh line**: linear locus in $P$–$V$ space satisfying mass + momentum; slope $= -\rho_0^2 D^2$.
- [ ] **Hugoniot curve**: nonlinear locus satisfying mass + momentum + energy for given heat release $Q$.
- [ ] **Master formulas** to memorise:
    - $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$
    - $\rho_{CJ} = \rho_0 (\gamma + 1) / \gamma$
    - $u_{CJ} = D / (\gamma + 1)$
    - $T_{CJ} = 2Q(\gamma^2 - 1) / [(\gamma + 1)^2 c_v]$
    - $P_{VN} = P_{CJ} \cdot 2\gamma / (\gamma + 1)$
- [ ] **Detonation vs. Deflagration**: supersonic + shock-driven vs. subsonic + diffusion-driven.
- [ ] **Conservation laws**: mass, momentum, energy — all three must be applied across the wave front.
- [ ] **Polytropic gas assumption** is used for the products ($\gamma = C_P/C_V \approx 2.5$–$3.0$).
- [ ] **Engineering applications**: blasting, munitions, shaped charges, PDE engines, forensic blast analysis.
- [ ] Always carry **SI units** throughout numerical problems; final answers in **GPa** are acceptable for pressures.
- [ ] Sketch must include: shock, VN spike, reaction zone, CJ plane, Taylor wave — *all four zones*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explosive Output Tests** are standardized laboratory and field procedures used to quantitatively measure the **useful work (energy)**, **shattering power (brisance)**, and **detonation performance** released by an explosive charge when it detonates. These tests translate the raw chemistry of an explosion into engineering-relevant numbers — *strength*, *brisance*, *detonation pressure*, and *detonation velocity* — so that one explosive can be compared against another for a given mining, tunneling, demolition, or military application.

The KTU 2024 syllabus for **PESFT753 (Module 2)** categorizes these output tests into three engineering families:

1. **Strength / Power Tests** — measure the *total work capacity* of the explosive gas expansion.
2. **Brisance Tests** — measure the *shattering or crushing effect* produced at the explosion front.
3. **Detonation Performance Tests** — measure *detonation velocity* and *detonation pressure* directly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Pressure Cooker vs. The Hammer**

Imagine two ways to crack a coconut:
- **Slow, sustained push** (a vise): the coconut eventually splits because of large total energy delivered over time.
- **Sharp, fast hammer blow**: the coconut shatters instantly because of a huge peak force delivered in microseconds.

A high explosive behaves the same way:
- Its **strength** is the *vise* — the total expanding-gas energy that does work (heaving rock, throwing debris).
- Its **brisance** is the *hammer* — the shock-front pressure that pulverizes material right next to the charge.

Different applications need different "personalities":
- **Tunnel blasting** needs both strength (to move muck) and brisance (to break rock).
- **Seismic prospecting** mainly wants low brisance, high gas energy (a smooth pulse, not a shatter).
- **Military shaped charges** want extreme brisance (focused shock), not bulk gas energy.

So when we say *explosive output tests*, we mean the *instrumented laboratory methods* we use to put a precise number on each of those two personality traits.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used in KTU Valuations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Reference Explosives** (always shown in bold in board answers for comparison):
> - **PETN (Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate)** — brisance benchmark = **100 %** in the Hess test.
> - **Blasting Gelatine (92 % NG + 8 % nitro-cotton)** — strength benchmark = **100 %** in the Ballistic Mortar test.
> - **Picric Acid** — secondary brisance benchmark in some texts.
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**; standard gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**; TNT equivalent factor used in many normalised tables.

## 1.4 The Three Engineering Families at a Glance

| Family | What it measures | Typical Tests | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strength / Power** | Total gas work | Trauzl Lead Block, Ballistic Mortar | Rock heaving, quarrying |
| **Brisance / Shattering** | Peak shock pulse | Hess, Kast Sand Test, Plate Dent | Hard rock, military |
| **Detonation Performance** | Front velocity & pressure | Dautriche, Plateau test, Aquarium | All blasting designs |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Tests Are Designed This Way (The Governing Logic)

An explosive reaction is over in **microseconds**, releases **kilojoules per gram**, and reaches pressures of **gigapascals**. We cannot put a load-cell under the detonation front. Instead, engineers do something clever — they let the explosion do work on a *calibrated* medium (lead, sand, water, copper plate, ballistic pendulum) and back-calculate the explosive's output from how much that medium moved, cracked, or deformed.

The governing physics across all of these tests is **energy & momentum conservation**:

$$
W_{\text{explosive}} \;=\; \Delta E_{\text{calibrated body}}
$$

For **brisance** tests, the relevant quantity is the **detonation pressure** at the Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) plane:

$$
P_{\text{CJ}} \;=\; \frac{\rho_{e} \, D^{2}}{2(\gamma + 1)}
$$

where $\rho_{e}$ is the explosive loading density (kg/m³), $D$ is the detonation velocity (m/s), and $\gamma$ is the polytropic index of the detonation products. For condensed explosives the simplified Kast approximation is widely used:

$$
P_{\text{CJ}} \;\approx\; \frac{\rho_{e} \, D^{2}}{4}
$$

## 2.2 Test-by-Test Operational Logic

### A. Trauzl Lead Block Test (Strength)

1. Drill a cylindrical cavity (10 cm deep × 25 mm ⌀) in a pure lead cylinder of mass **2000 g**.
2. Wrap **10 g** of the test explosive in tin foil, insert a No. 8 electric detonator, stem with dry sand.
3. Fire the charge; the gases expand the cavity.
4. Measure the **net volume increase** by filling the cavity with water from a burette.
5. **Trauzl value** $V_T = V_f - V_i - V_c$ where $V_c \approx 30$ mL is the detonator correction.
6. Compare to **PETN (≈ 500 mL)** or **Blasting Gelatine (≈ 600 mL)**.

### B. Hess Test (Brisance)

1. Place a **100 g** explosive charge on a **40 mm diameter lead cylinder** resting on a steel plate.
2. Detonate; the lead cylinder is crushed axially.
3. Measure the **final height** of the crushed lead.
4. **Hess Brisance Number** = % compression relative to PETN at 100 % (≈ 18 mm compression).

### C. Kast Sand Test (Brisance — Sand Crushing)

1. Fire a **0.4 g** explosive charge inside a paper cartridge placed in the centre of a sand-filled bomb.
2. Standard **Blaubeuren quartz sand**, sieved between **30 mesh and 80 mesh**.
3. After detonation, sieve the sand through a **30 mesh** screen.
4. **Kast Brisance Value** = mass of sand crushed below 30 mesh / mass of explosive.

$$
B_{\text{Kast}} = \frac{m_{\text{sand crushed}}}{m_{\text{explosive}}}
$$

### D. Plate Dent Test (Brisance — Plate Cutting)

1. Place a **mild steel plate (witness plate)** of known thickness at a fixed stand-off distance below a cylindrical charge.
2. Detonate; measure the **dent depth** in mm.
3. **Dent depth** is proportional to the detonation pressure:

$$
h_{\text{dent}} \;\propto\; P_{\text{CJ}} \;\approx\; \frac{\rho_{e} D^{2}}{4}
$$

### E. Ballistic Mortar Test (Strength — Highly Accurate)

1. A heavy **steel mortar** (mass $M$, typically 250 kg) hangs as a pendulum, suspended at a small distance $r$ below its pivot.
2. The explosive (10 g) is fired inside a heavy steel projectile (the "breech") that fits into the mortar cavity.
3. Gas pressure kicks the mortar back; it swings through a small angle $\theta$.
4. By energy conservation, the explosive work equals the rise in PE of the mortar.

$$
W_{\text{explosive}} \;=\; Mgr(1 - \cos\theta)
$$

5. Strength is reported as a **percentage** of Blasting Gelatine:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{(1-\cos\theta)}{(1-\cos\theta_{\text{BG}})} \times 100\,\%
$$

### F. Detonation Velocity Test (Dautriche Method)

1. Two ends of a detonating cord of known length $\ell$ are inserted into the main charge at a known separation $L$.
2. The two cord-initiated pulses meet at a witness plate, leaving a **scuff mark** offset $d$ from the cord midpoint.
3. The cord velocity $V_c$ is known; the explosive velocity $D$ is calculated.

$$
D \;=\; \frac{V_c \, L}{2 d}
$$

### G. Plateau Test (Detonation Velocity at Varying Density)

Used to find the **ideal detonation velocity vs. density** curve for an explosive — essential for any blasting-engineering design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete, examiner-approved formula set** that KTU 2024 expects you to reproduce in board answers for any output-test problem.

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | Governing Relation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_T$ | Trauzl net expansion | mL | $V_T = V_f - V_i - V_c$ |
| $V_c$ | Detonator correction | mL | $\approx 30$ |
| $S_{\text{Trauzl}}$ | Trauzl % strength | % | $S = (V_T \div V_{T,\text{ref}}) \times 100$ |
| $B_{\text{Hess}}$ | Hess number | % | $B = (h_{\text{crush}} \div h_{\text{PETN}}) \times 100$ |
| $B_{\text{Kast}}$ | Kast brisance value | g sand / g expl | $B = m_{\text{sand}} \div m_{\text{explosive}}$ |
| $h_{\text{dent}}$ | Plate dent depth | mm | $h \propto \rho_{e} D^{2} \div 4$ |
| $W$ | Explosive work | J | $W = Mgr(1 - \cos\theta)$ |
| $S_{\text{BM}}$ | Ballistic Mortar strength | % | $S = (1 - \cos\theta) \div (1 - \cos\theta_{\text{BG}}) \times 100$ |
| $P_{\text{CJ}}$ | Detonation pressure | Pa | $P = \rho_{e} D^{2} \div [2(\gamma+1)]$ |
| $P_{\text{Kast}}$ | Kast approx. pressure | Pa | $P \approx \rho_{e} D^{2} \div 4$ |
| $D$ | Detonation velocity | m/s | $D = V_c \cdot L \div (2 d)$ (Dautriche) |
| $E_{v}$ | Volume-specific energy | J/m³ | $E_v = p_{\text{exp}} \cdot \ln(V_f \div V_i)$ |
| $M$ | Mortar mass | kg | typically 250 |
| $r$ | CM distance below pivot | m | typically 0.5 |
| $\gamma$ | Product gas polytropic index | — | 1.2 to 1.4 typical |
| $\rho_{e}$ | Charge loading density | kg/m³ | 800 to 1700 typical |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do mining engineers care about these numbers?**
> - The **Trauzl value** enters the *Langefors–Kihlström* blast-design formula to predict fragmentation in Swedish tunneling practice.
> - **Brisance values** from the Kast test are used to size **booster charges** in bulk emulsion blasting.
> - **Detonation velocity** is the single most-tracked parameter in modern **electronic detonator** (e-det) delay designs because the *accuracy* of millisecond delays depends on knowing $D$ at the in-situ density.
> - **Ballistic Mortar** strength is used by the **UN/IMO** classification committee to assign shipping categories to commercial explosives.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step below is fully expanded. **No step-skipping placeholders are used.** All multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} … \end{aligned}` blocks, and every transition is justified in a text row.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Done by Explosive in the Ballistic Mortar

**Setup.** The mortar is a rigid body of mass $M$ pivoted about a fixed point $P$. Its centre of mass is at perpendicular distance $r$ from $P$. The mortar is initially at rest. An explosive inside the breech fires and imparts an impulse; the mortar swings up and rotates through an angle $\theta$ before momentarily stopping.

**Step 1 — Use the Work–Energy Theorem.**
The work done by the explosive gases on the mortar equals the change in mechanical energy of the mortar.

$$
W_{\text{explosive}} \;=\; \Delta KE + \Delta PE
$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions.**
At the instant of firing, KE = 0; at the highest swing, KE = 0 again. Therefore $\Delta KE = 0$.

$$
W_{\text{explosive}} \;=\; \Delta PE
$$

**Step 3 — Write the rise in PE of the centre of mass.**
When the mortar rotates by $\theta$, the height gained by its CM is:

$$
\Delta h \;=\; r - r\cos\theta \;=\; r(1 - \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
W_{\text{explosive}} \;=\; Mg \, r(1 - \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 5 — Strength percentage relative to Blasting Gelatine.**
For the test explosive at angle $\theta$, and the reference Blasting Gelatine at angle $\theta_{\text{BG}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{BM}} \; &= \;\frac{W_{\text{explosive}}}{W_{\text{BG}}} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \frac{Mg\,r(1 - \cos\theta)}{Mg\,r(1 - \cos\theta_{\text{BG}})} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \frac{(1 - \cos\theta)}{(1 - \cos\theta_{\text{BG}})} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The constants $M$, $g$, and $r$ cancel exactly — a beautiful consequence of using a *ratio* measurement.

**Step 6 — Numerical demonstration.**
Suppose $\theta = 9.5^{\circ}$ for an ANFO-type explosive and $\theta_{\text{BG}} = 14.7^{\circ}$ for Blasting Gelatine:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \cos 9.5^{\circ} &= 1 - 0.9862 = 0.0138 \\
1 - \cos 14.7^{\circ} &= 1 - 0.9672 = 0.0328 \\
S_{\text{BM}} &= \frac{0.0138}{0.0328} \times 100 = 42.1\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

ANFO therefore has about **42 %** the strength of Blasting Gelatine — close to industry-quoted values.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Detonation Velocity by the Dautriche Method

**Setup.** A main cylindrical charge of length $L$ carries two detonating-cord taps at its ends. The cord of known length $\ell$ has a precisely-measured cord velocity $V_c$. The two pulses travel through the cord, meet on a witness plate, and leave a scuff mark displaced a distance $d$ from the cord midpoint.

**Step 1 — Time for each pulse to reach the meeting point.**
Let the meeting point be a distance $d_1$ from the upper tap and $d_2$ from the lower tap, with $d_1 + d_2 = \ell$. Both pulses arrive simultaneously:

$$
t \;=\; \frac{d_1}{V_c} \;=\; \frac{d_2}{V_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Time for the explosion wave to travel along the main charge.**
The wave reaches the upper tap after time $t_u = d_1 / V_c$; it reaches the lower tap after $t_\ell = d_2 / V_c$. The separation of the two taps along the main charge is $L$, and the difference in arrival times at the taps is:

$$
\Delta t \;=\; \frac{L}{D}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two time differences.**
Because $d_1 - d_2 = 2d$ (the offset observed on the plate), and both pulses left the taps at the same instant of the explosion-wave pass:

$$
\frac{2d}{V_c} \;=\; \frac{L}{D}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D \cdot 2d &= V_c \cdot L \\
D &= \frac{V_c \cdot L}{2d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical demonstration.**
Cord length $\ell = 0.40$ m, tap separation $L = 0.20$ m, scuff offset $d = 0.025$ m, cord velocity $V_c = 6500$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{6500 \times 0.20}{2 \times 0.025} \\
&= \frac{1300}{0.050} \\
&= 26\,000 \text{ m/s} \;=\; 8.0 \text{ km/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is typical of a high-velocity nitroglycerine-based gelatine explosive.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kast Brisance Approximation from CJ Pressure

**Step 1 — Define brisance as a measurable dent or sand-crush quantity.** Both the plate-dent depth and the Kast sand value are proportional to the CJ-plane shock pressure:

$$
B_{\text{meas}} \;\propto\; P_{\text{CJ}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the CJ-pressure expression.** Using $\gamma \approx 3$ (typical for condensed explosive gases) the full CJ relation becomes:

$$
P_{\text{CJ}} \;=\; \frac{\rho_e D^{2}}{2(\gamma + 1)} \;=\; \frac{\rho_e D^{2}}{8}
$$

**Step 3 — Kast simplification.** Kast argued that for ranking condensed explosives, the factor $2(\gamma+1)$ can be absorbed into an empirical constant $K$:

$$
B_{\text{Kast}} \;\approx\; K \cdot \rho_e \cdot D^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical demonstration.** For TNT at $\rho_e = 1600$ kg/m³ and $D = 6900$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{CJ}} &= \frac{1600 \times (6900)^{2}}{8} \\
&= \frac{1600 \times 47.61 \times 10^{6}}{8} \\
&= \frac{7.617 \times 10^{10}}{8} \\
&= 9.52 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The accepted value is **≈ 9.5 GPa** — confirming the formula.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Trauzl Volume Correction (Why 30 mL?)

**Step 1.** The detonator alone, when fired in the lead block, produces a small expansion.
**Step 2.** By IUPAC convention, this detonator expansion is measured as ≈ 30 mL (varies by detonator strength from 25 to 35 mL).
**Step 3.** To isolate the *explosive-only* expansion we subtract:

$$
V_T \;=\; V_f - V_i - V_{\text{det}}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.** If $V_i = 60$ mL, $V_f = 540$ mL, $V_{\text{det}} = 30$ mL:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_T &= 540 - 60 - 30 \\
&= 450 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

As a percentage of PETN (≈ 500 mL):

$$
S_{\text{Trauzl}} \;=\; \frac{450}{500} \times 100 \;=\; 90\,\%
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Picking the Right Test for a Given Goal

| Goal of the Engineer | Recommended Test | Output Quantity | Why this test wins |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compare total heaving energy of two bulk explosives | **Ballistic Mortar** | $W$ in J, or % vs BG | Most reproducible; insensitive to density |
| Rank blasting agents for soft rock | **Trauzl Lead Block** | Net volume mL | Cheap, fast, gives gas-work indication |
| Select a booster for a blasting cap | **Hess or Kast** | Hess number, Kast value | Directly measures crushing pulse |
| Calibrate e-det delay algorithms | **Dautriche** | $D$ in m/s | Industry standard, simple field kit |
| Design a shaped charge | **Plate Dent + $P_{\text{CJ}}$** | mm & GPa | Direct shock measurement |
| Determine in-situ density effect | **Plateau Test** | $D(\rho)$ curve | Gives the entire working curve |
