The estimator $\bar{i}$ weights trials by their position in the staircase, correcting for the asymmetric nature of the responses. > [!NOTE] > This derivation appears **verbatim** in UN Test Manual STANAG 4489 and forms a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE papers. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Thermal Ignition Time For thermal sensitivity (Koenen test or DSC), the time-to-ignition $t$ is governed by Arrhenius kinetics. **Step 1 — Write the basic Arrhenius form.** The rate constant $k$ of the decomposition reaction is: $$k = A \, e^{-E_a / R T}$$ **Step 2 — Define induction time.** The induction period $\tau$ is inversely proportional to $k$: $$\tau \approx \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{A} \, e^{E_a / R T}$$ **Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm to linearize.** Both sides become: $$\ln(\tau) = \ln\left(\frac{1}{A}\right) + \frac{E_a}{R T}$$ $$\ln(\tau) = -\ln A + \frac{E_a}{R T}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the slope.** Plotting $\ln(\tau)$ vs $1/T$ gives a straight line. The slope equals $E_a / R$, and the intercept equals $-\ln A$. **Step 5 — Solve for activation energy.** Rearranging the slope: $$E_a = R \cdot \text{slope} = R \cdot \frac{\Delta \ln(\tau)}{\Delta (1/T)}$$ For PETN (pentaerythritol tetranitrate), experimental values yield $E_a \approx 150$ kJ/mol, confirming rapid thermal decomposition at moderate temperatures. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ESD Stored Energy **Step 1 — A capacitor stores electrical energy as:** $$E = \frac{Q^2}{2 C} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} Q V$$ **Step 2 — Substitute standard HBM parameters:** For the standard human-body model: $C = 100\,\text{pF} = 100 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F}$. **Step 3 — Evaluate at typical voltages:** At $V = 1000\,\text{V}$: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (1000)^2$$ $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{6}$$ $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 10^{-4}$$ $$E = 5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{J} = 0.05\,\text{mJ}$$ At $V = 10\,000\,\text{V}$: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (10^4)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 10^8 = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{J} = 5\,\text{mJ}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Insight:** Lead azide ($\text{Pb(N}_3\text{)}_2$) has an **ESD ignition threshold of ~7 mJ**, making it dangerously sensitive. A static spark from a human (≈ 10–25 mJ at 10 kV) is more than enough to detonate it — this is why **anti-static footwear** is mandatory in munitions plants. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Comparison of Common Explosives | Explosive | Impact H₅₀ (cm, 2 kg) | Friction Limit (N) | ESD (mJ) | Card Gap (mm) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Lead Azide | 4 | 0.1 | 7 | < 5 | | PETN | 12 | 60 | 200 | 60 | | RDX | 22 | 120 | 250 | 80 | | HMX | 28 | 150 | 300 | 90 | | TNT | 100 | 350 | 1000 | > 200 | | ANFO | > 200 | > 360 | > 5000 | > 250 | | TATB | > 320 | > 360 | > 2000 | > 250 | > [!NOTE] > **Reading the matrix:** Lower values = more sensitive (more dangerous to handle). Higher values = more insensitive. TATB is the gold-standard insensitive high explosive. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BAM Fall Hammer — Functional Architecture The following block diagram illustrates the energy flow and signal logic of the BAM Fall Hammer test apparatus. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sample Loading Bay] --> B[Anvil Confinement
40 mm³ charge] B --> C[Drop Weight
m = 1 or 2 kg] C --> D[Variable Height
h = 10 to 100 cm] D --> E[Impact Event
t = 2 to 4 ms] E --> F{Outcome
Detection} F -->|Explosion X| G[Bruceton
Reduce h] F -->|No Reaction O| H[Bruceton
Increase h] G --> I[Staircase
Convergence] H --> I I --> J[H50 Estimate
and Std Dev S] J --> K[Classification
UN Hazard Class] ``` ## 4.2 Card Gap Test — Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Detonator
and Booster] --> P2[Donor Charge
Main Explosive] P2 --> P3[PMMA Cards
Attenuator Stack] P3 --> P4[Acceptor Charge
Witness Plate Below] P4 --> P5{Detonation
Propagation} P5 -->|Yes X| P6[Increase Gap
Add Card] P5 -->|No O| P7[Decrease Gap
Remove Card] P6 --> P8[Limiting Gap
50 percent Point] P7 --> P8 ``` ## 4.3 ESD Tester — Equivalent Circuit Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[High Voltage DC
Source 0 to 25 kV] --> Q2[Charging Capacitor
C = 100 pF] Q2 --> Q3[Discharge Resistor
R = 1.5 kiloOhm] Q3 --> Q4[Spark Gap
Trigger Pin] Q4 --> Q5[Explosive Sample
Holding Fixture] Q5 --> Q6[Energy Meter
E half CV squared] Q6 --> Q7[Go NoGo
Statistical Analysis] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Every block is a physical or functional component. Arrows represent the **direction of energy or signal flow**, not physical wiring. Test outcomes (X / O) drive the staircase iteration that converges to the threshold. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — Definition **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Initiation Sensitivity** of an explosive. Mention any two standardized tests used to evaluate it. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Initiation Sensitivity** of an explosive is the quantitative measure of the minimum stimulus (mechanical, thermal, electrical or shock) required to initiate the explosive decomposition or detonation of the substance. It defines the boundary between safe handling and accidental ignition. Two standardized tests are: **(i) BAM Fall Hammer Test** for impact sensitivity (H₅₀), and **(ii) BAM Friction Test** for friction sensitivity (limiting normal load in N). *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two tests = 3 marks total.)* --- ### Question 2 — Concept **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Explain the **Hot Spot Theory** in the context of impact sensitivity of explosives. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Hot Spot Theory** (Bowden & Yoffe, 1952) explains how mechanical impact triggers detonation. When an explosive is impacted, the kinetic energy is not absorbed uniformly — it concentrates at microscopic sites such as voids, crystal defects, or inter-particle friction points. These "hot spots" reach temperatures of **$10^3$–$10^4$ K** for microseconds, causing localized exothermic decomposition. The heat released then propagates through the bulk, leading to full detonation. The theory justifies why **porous, granular, or contaminated** explosives are far more impact-sensitive than pure, void-free crystals. *(Award 1 mark each for: localized energy concentration, temperature range, propagation to detonation = 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per **KTU ESE Module-Internal Choice pattern**, TWO complete alternative questions are provided. Either **Question A** OR **Question B** will appear in the exam. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Describe the **BAM Fall Hammer Test** in detail, including the apparatus, sample preparation, test procedure, and statistical method for computing H₅₀. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** An explosive sample is tested using the Bruceton staircase method with a 2 kg drop weight. The lowest trial level is 15 cm. The trial outcomes (in cm) are: 18, 15, 18, 21, 18, 15, 18, 15, 18, 21, 18, 18, 15, 18, 21, 18, 15, 18, 21, 18. Calculate the **H₅₀** value and the **standard deviation S** of the impact energy. Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$. **\[7 marks\]** --- **Model Solution (a) — \[7 marks\]:** 1. **Apparatus (2 marks):** The BAM Fall Hammer consists of a vertically guided steel mass (1 kg or 2 kg), a striking anvil with a defined hardness, a sample-confining ring, and an adjustable height scale (10–100 cm). The striker has a polished steel tip. 2. **Sample Preparation (1 mark):** Approximately $40\,\text{mm}^3$ of explosive is placed between two steel cylinders (anvils) and confined by a collar ring. 3. **Procedure (2 marks):** The drop weight is raised to a pre-set height and released. Outcomes are classified as "explosion" (audible report, smoke, or visible decomposition) or "no reaction". Heights are varied per the **Bruceton Up-Down staircase method** until at least 6 reversals occur. 4. **H₅₀ Calculation (2 marks):** The H₅₀ is calculated using the Bruceton estimator: $$H_{50} = h_0 + \Delta h \cdot \left(\bar{i} + \frac{N_{\text{exp}}}{N} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$ where $\bar{i}$ is the mean trial index and $N_{\text{exp}}$ is the count of explosions. --- **Model Solution (b) — \[7 marks\]:** **Step 1 — Organize the data (1 mark):** Sorted trial levels (cm): 15, 15, 15, 15, 15, 15, 18, 18, 18, 18, 18, 18, 18, 18, 18, 21, 21, 21, 21. **Step 2 — Re-index around the lowest level (2 marks):** Let $h_0 = 15$ cm, step size $\Delta h = 3$ cm. - Level $i = 0$ corresponds to 15 cm (count $n_0 = 6$). - Level $i = 1$ corresponds to 18 cm (count $n_1 = 9$). - Level $i = 2$ corresponds to 21 cm (count $n_2 = 4$). **Step 3 — Compute the weighted sum (1 mark):** $$\sum i \, n_i = 0 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 9 + 2 \cdot 4 = 0 + 9 + 8 = 17$$ $$N = 6 + 9 + 4 = 19$$ $$\bar{i} = \frac{17}{19} \approx 0.895$$ **Step 4 — Compute H₅₀ (1 mark):** $$H_{50} = 15 + 3 \cdot \left(0.895 + \frac{?}{19} - 0.5\right)$$ Assume all "X" trials approximate to total explosions $N_{\text{exp}} = 13$ (levels 18 and 21): $$H_{50} = 15 + 3 \cdot (0.895 + 0.684 - 0.5) = 15 + 3 \cdot 1.079 = 15 + 3.24 = 18.24\,\text{cm}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the standard deviation (1 mark):** $$\sum i^2 n_i = 0 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 9 + 4 \cdot 4 = 25$$ $$S = 3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{25 - 19 \cdot (0.895)^2}{18}} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{25 - 15.22}{18}} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{0.543} = 3 \cdot 0.737 \approx 2.21\,\text{cm}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to impact energy (1 mark):** $$E_{50} = m \cdot g \cdot H_{50} = 2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1824 \approx 3.58\,\text{J}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > - Forgetting to **convert cm → m** before multiplying by $g$ (penalty: 1 mark). > - Using total trials (19) instead of $N-1$ in the standard deviation denominator (penalty: 1 mark). > - Omitting units (cm, J) in the final answer (penalty: 0.5 mark each). > - Confusing $H_{50}$ (cm) with $E_{50}$ (J). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **Card Gap Test** for shock sensitivity determination. Draw a labeled schematic and discuss how the limiting gap value is interpreted. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** The thermal decomposition of an explosive follows Arrhenius behavior. The time-to-ignition is measured as: - At $T_1 = 500\,\text{K}$: $t_1 = 50\,\text{s}$ - At $T_2 = 550\,\text{K}$: $t_2 = 5\,\text{s}$ Calculate the **apparent activation energy** $E_a$. Use $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$. **\[7 marks\]** --- **Model Solution (a) — \[7 marks\]:** 1. **Principle (1 mark):** The Card Gap Test determines the ability of an explosive to propagate detonation after attenuation through an inert barrier. It directly measures shock sensitivity. 2. **Setup (2 marks):** A donor charge (test explosive) is separated from an acceptor charge (same explosive) by a stack of PMMA ("lucite") cards of variable thickness. A witness plate (steel or lead) is placed below the acceptor. A detonator initiates the donor. 3. **Procedure (1 mark):** The number of cards is varied (typically 0 to 100+ cards, each 1.6 mm thick). The test outcome is binary: detonation propagates (clean hole in witness plate = "go") or fails (no hole = "no-go"). 4. **Interpretation (2 marks):** The **limiting gap** is the card thickness at which the detonation fails to propagate in 50% of trials (the 50% point). A larger limiting gap means the explosive is **less shock-sensitive** (more robust). E.g., PETN has a gap of ~60 mm; ANFO has > 250 mm. 5. **Schematic (1 mark):** (Refer to the Card Gap architecture flow diagram in Section 4.2 above.) --- **Model Solution (b) — \[7 marks\]:** **Step 1 — Recall the linearized Arrhenius form (1 mark):** $$\ln(t) = -\ln A + \frac{E_a}{R T}$$ **Step 2 — Write the equations for the two temperatures (2 marks):** At $T_1 = 500\,\text{K}$: $$\ln(50) = -\ln A + \frac{E_a}{R \cdot 500}$$ $$3.912 = -\ln A + \frac{E_a}{4157}$$ At $T_2 = 550\,\text{K}$: $$\ln(5) = -\ln A + \frac{E_a}{R \cdot 550}$$ $$1.609 = -\ln A + \frac{E_a}{4572.7}$$ **Step 3 — Subtract to eliminate $A$ (2 marks):** $$3.912 - 1.609 = \frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{550}\right)$$ $$2.303 = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{550 - 500}{500 \cdot 550}\right)$$ $$2.303 = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{50}{275000}\right)$$ $$2.303 = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot 1.818 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $E_a$ (2 marks):** $$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{2.303}{1.818 \times 10^{-4}} = 12{,}668\,\text{K}$$ $$E_a = 12{,}668 \cdot 8.314 \approx 1.053 \times 10^5\,\text{J/mol}$$ $$E_a \approx 105.3\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > - Forgetting that $E_a / R$ yields a temperature (in K), not an energy — must multiply by $R$ last. > - Mismatching units: temperatures **must be in Kelvin** (penalty: 1 mark if °C used). > - Sign errors when subtracting equations: $\ln(t_1) - \ln(t_2)$ with $T_2 > T_1$ gives $T_1 - T_2$ in the bracket (penalty: 1 mark). > - Final answer **must include units** (kJ/mol); bare numerical answers lose 0.5 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Initiation Sensitivity** = minimum stimulus to initiate an explosive. - **Five standard stimuli:** Impact, Friction, Heat, Shock, ESD. - **H₅₀** is the gold standard for impact sensitivity; computed via **Bruceton staircase**. - **BAM Fall Hammer** uses a 1 kg or 2 kg drop weight; impact energy $E = mgh$. - **BAM Friction Test** reports a **limiting normal load in N**. - **Card Gap Test** uses PMMA cards; larger gap = less sensitive. - **ESD energy** for the Human Body Model: $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ with $C = 100\,\text{pF}$. - **Arrhenius equation** governs thermal ignition: $\ln(t) = -\ln A + E_a/RT$. - **Hot Spot Theory (Bowden–Yoffe):** explains impact initiation via localized $10^3$–$10^4$ K hot spots. - **Bruceton estimator:** $H_{50} = h_0 + \Delta h(\bar{i} + N_{\text{exp}}/N - 1/2)$. - **UN Classification Tests:** Series 3(a) impact, 3(b) Koenen thermal, 3(c) friction, 3(d) card gap. - **Standard test conditions:** 20 ± 2 °C, 50 ± 5% RH, atmospheric pressure. - **Order of decreasing sensitivity:** Lead Azide > PETN > RDX > HMX > TNT > ANFO > TATB. - **Always include units** in final answers: H₅₀ in cm or J, ESD in mJ, $E_a$ in kJ/mol. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 2 Take-home:** Sensitivity testing is **not** a measure of explosive power — it is a measure of **handling safety**. A highly sensitive explosive (e.g., lead azide) is **dangerous to handle** but may have less brisance than an insensitive one (e.g., ANFO in bulk form). ---

topics.title: Non Electric Initiators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLORIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non Electric Initiators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# NON-ELECTRIC INITIATORS – CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DEFINITION:** Non-Electric Initiators (NEIs) are defined as **explosive initiation devices and accessory systems that transmit, propagate, or trigger a detonation impulse through a low-energy train—predominantly using the detonation products of a core high-explosive, the propagation of a low-velocity shock wave through a reactive granular coating, or the timed burning of a pyrotechnic delay composition—WITHOUT the use of an electric current, electric fuse-head, or capacitor discharge.**

The classical classification under KTU Module-2 places NEIs into **four functional families**:

1. **Plain Detonators (PDs)** combined with a **Safety Fuse (Bickford type)**.
2. **Detonating Cords (DC)** — `Primacord`, `Detacord`, `Cordtex`, `Austin Black Powder Cord`.
3. **Shock-Tube Initiating Systems (NONEL / Non-Electric Detonator / Dyno Nobel NONEL)**.
4. **Pyrotechnic Delay & Gas-Phase Initiation Systems** (in-bore hole delay elements, gas generators).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Electric"?** In an era of mechanized mining and tunnel-blasting, the single largest accidental initiation cause in the 20th century was **stray current, galvanic action, radio-frequency (RF) pickup, and lightning**. NEIs eliminate this entire failure mode by replacing the electrical firing pulse with a **chemical-detonation pulse**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you want to light 50 birthday candles placed across a large room **simultaneously**, but you are terrified that the slightest static spark will set them off accidentally. What do you do?

- **Electrical approach:** Run a copper wire to each candle, hold a battery, hope no one walks by with a Van-de-Graaff generator. *Risky.*
- **Non-Electric approach (DETONATING CORD):** Lay a thin "firecracker string" on the floor, knot it under each candle. Light one end — the chemical burn races along the string at **7,000 m/s**, popping every candle. *No electricity involved.*
- **Non-Electric approach (SHOCK TUBE):** Instead of a firecracker, lay a thin plastic straw whose inside walls are painted with a paper-thin layer of reactive dust. A starter pistol fires a small cap that creates a **silent, 2,000 m/s pressure puff** inside the tube, which propagates to a small detonator at every candle. *No fire, no flame, no electricity, no sparks, no stray-current risk.*

The **"string"** is the initiator. The **"puff"** is the impulse. The **"candle"** is the detonator/delay element. The system is non-electric.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants & reference values for Module-2:**

- Velocity of Detonation (VOD) of standard **Primacord (PETN core, 10 g/m)**: **≈ 7,000 m/s** (range 6,800–7,200 m/s).
- VOD of low-energy **Detacord (1.5 g/m PETN)**: **≈ 5,500 m/s**.
- VOD of high-strength DC (40 g/m, 80 g/m): **≈ 7,200 m/s**.
- Shock-tube propagation velocity (NONEL, 3 mm LDPE tube with HMX/Al reactive coating): **≈ 1,950–2,050 m/s**.
- Detonation pressure of a 10 g/m PETN cord (ρ ≈ 1,540 kg/m³): **≈ 18.9 GPa**.
- Standard delay intervals in pyrotechnic delays: **25 ms, 50 ms, 100 ms steps** (commonly used in commercial blasting).
- Delay scatter (jitter) of pyrotechnic elements: **± 1–2 %** of nominal delay.
- Safety-fuse burning rate: **120 ± 20 s/m** (regulated, slow-pyrotype).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall – VOD Confusion:** A common exam trap is to confuse the **propagation velocity of a shock tube (~2,000 m/s)** with the **detonation velocity of PETN (~7,000 m/s)**. The shock tube is **deflagration-to-shock**, not high-order detonation of PETN. Remember: shock tube = **low-order shock**, not high-order detonation.

---

## 1.4 Position of Non-Electric Initiators in the Blasting Circuit Hierarchy

$$
\text{Initiation Energy Hierarchy (Low → High):}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Safety Fuse (120 s/m)}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Shock Tube (~2,000 m/s)}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Detonating Cord (~7,000 m/s)}} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Electric Detonator (instant)}}
$$

This hierarchy tells us that **detonating cord** sits at the **top of non-electric initiation power** and is used as a trunk line for bulk blasting; **shock tube** is the **modern safe workhorse** for down-the-hole initiation; and **safety fuse + plain detonator** is the **manual / historical** method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Family 1 — Detonating Cord (DC) / Primacord / Detacord

### 2.1.1 Construction (Layered Architecture)

A detonating cord is essentially a **flexible, rope-like explosive transmission line**. From core to sheath:

1. **Core**: Continuous filament of **PETN (Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate)** crystals, dry-loaded.
   - Loading: **1.5 g/m** (Detacord, low), **10 g/m** (Primacord, standard), **40 g/m** (Reinforced), **80 g/m** (Surface line).
2. **Inner textile wrap**: Cotton or polyester yarn, counter-wound, **prevents extrusion** and contains the core.
3. **Waterproofing layer**: Asphaltic/bitumen + plastic tape (optional, for "A" or "AW" grade).
4. **Outer textile counter-wrap**: Polyester or polypropylene yarns in the opposite helix — gives **tensile strength** (≥ 100 N, typically 200 N).
5. **Optional outer plastic jacket**: PVC/PE for abrasive environments (e.g., quarry blasting).

### 2.1.2 Detonation Mechanism – Step-by-Step

1. **Initiation** of the cord is achieved by a **detonator** (No. 6 or No. 8 strength, base charge ≥ 0.5 g PETN) crimped to the end.
2. The detonator's base charge generates a **shock wave of pressure > 10 GPa** in the PETN core.
3. PETN, being a **secondary high explosive** with a critical diameter of **~0.3 mm**, sustains a steady-state **detonation wave** travelling at the VOD.
4. The reaction zone is approximately **0.5–1.0 mm thick** (steady Chapman-Jouguet zone).
5. The **detonation products expand radially** through the textile wrap, ensuring the outer sheath does **not rupture** the cord (designed to contain > 95 % of energy in the axial direction).

### 2.1.3 Detonating Cord KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas in this table are required reading for the KTU Module-2 ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | VOD (PETN, ideal) | $D = \sqrt{2(\gamma^{2}-1)\,Q}$ | 7,000 m/s (10 g/m cord) | Steady-state CJ detonation |
| 2 | Detonation Pressure | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_{0}\,D^{2}}{\gamma+1}$ | ≈ 18.9 GPa (PETN) | One-dimensional steady detonation |
| 3 | Detonation Pressure (alt) | $P_{CJ} \approx \dfrac{\rho_{0}\,D^{2}}{4}$ | 18.9 GPa | Approximation for γ ≈ 3 |
| 4 | Energy per metre | $E/L = \rho_{0}\,A\,Q$ | 10 g/m × 5,800 kJ/kg = 58 kJ/m | A = cross-section |
| 5 | Critical Diameter | $d_c$ (PETN) ≈ 0.3 mm | Use $d > 5\,d_c$ for safety | Below $d_c$: failure |
| 6 | Detonation Failure Diameter | $d_f \approx 10\,d_c$ | ≈ 3 mm | Above $d_f$: reliable |
| 7 | Detonator cord initiation pressure required | $P_{init} \geq 8$ GPa | At the cord end | Below: misfire |
| 8 | Shock-to-detonation time in PETN | $t_{SDT} = \dfrac{x\,\rho_0\,\mu\,\dots}{P-P_{CJ}}$ | < 50 ns (10 g/m cord) | Run-up length < 5 mm |

> **Engineering utility:** The 7,000 m/s VOD of detonating cord is the reason it is used as a **trunk line** in multi-hole blasts — one end-initiated trunk can fire 100+ holes in **< 14 ms**, ensuring the trunk detonation wave reaches every branch before any branch's pyrotechnic delay has elapsed significantly (limiting sympathetic detonation risk).

---

## 2.2 Family 2 — Shock-Tube Initiating System (NONEL)

### 2.2.1 Construction

A NONEL shock tube is a **3 mm outer-diameter, 1 mm inner-diameter Low-Density PolyEthylene (LDPE) tube**, whose **inner surface is dust-coated with a sub-milligram-per-metre layer of reactive powder**, typically:

- **HMX** (Cyclotetramethylene-tetranitramine), **0.5–2.0 mg/m**, **OR**
- **HMX + aluminium** mixture, **0.5–5 mg/m**,
- Bonded with the polymer during extrusion.

### 2.2.2 Initiation Mechanism

1. A **low-energy starter** (percussion-actuated, like a 9 mm rimfire cap, OR a remote gas-pyrotechnic starter) delivers a **mechanical impulse** at one end of the tube.
2. This generates a **subsonic-to-sonic compression wave** in the gas column inside the tube.
3. The compression wave **passes over the reactive dust** on the inner wall.
4. The dust ignites and burns in a **fast deflagration-to-shock transition**, sustaining a **shock front** travelling at **~2,000 m/s** (NOT a true high-order detonation, but a **low-order stable shock**).
5. At the **downhole end**, the shock enters a small **delay element + base charge detonator** which then initiates the main charge.

### 2.2.3 NONEL Shock-Tube KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value | Note |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Shock propagation velocity | $V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma\,R\,T}{M}}$ | 1,950–2,050 m/s | For He/Ar gas at 300 K |
| 2 | Shock front pressure | $P_s = P_0\left(\dfrac{2\,\gamma\,M_s^{2}-(\gamma-1)}{\gamma+1}\right)$ | ≈ 0.3–0.5 MPa | Low-pressure (NOT detonation) |
| 3 | Energy content per metre | $E/L$ | 0.5–2.0 mg/m × 5,500 kJ/kg (HMX) | ≈ 2.75–11 J/m |
| 4 | Tube burst pressure (LDPE) | $P_{burst}$ | ≥ 7 MPa | Safety margin |
| 5 | Reaction-zone thickness | $\delta$ | ≈ 1–2 mm | Dust-deflagration |
| 6 | Detonator strength downstream | No. 6 / No. 8 | ≥ 0.5 g PETN base | Standard |

> **Engineering utility:** Shock tubes are **immune to electricity, RF, stray current, and static**. They produce **no flame, no spark, no hot gas jet at the tube mouth**, and are **safe in methane/dust atmospheres** (permissible explosives standard). They are the **dominant initiation technology in modern hard-rock metal mines** (e.g., Kiruna, LKAB Sweden; Kolar Gold Fields; Indian iron-ore mines).

---

## 2.3 Family 3 — Safety Fuse + Plain Detonator (Historical, but in KTU syllabus)

### 2.3.1 Construction
- A **safety fuse (Bickford)** is a **cord of black powder (75 % KNO₃ + 15 % C + 10 % S)** wrapped in jute and bitumen.
- **Burning rate:** 120 ± 20 s/m (regulated).
- It is crimped to a **plain detonator (No. 6 or No. 8)** containing 0.5 g PETN or RDX base charge.

### 2.3.2 Mechanism
- The black powder burns at a controlled rate, delivering a thermal impulse to the detonator.
- The detonator's primary explosive (e.g., lead azide, 0.2 g) ignites → flashes → sets off the secondary base charge (PETN/RDX, 0.5 g) → high-order detonation → main charge.

### 2.3.3 Limitations
- **Slow initiation** (60–120 s/m).
- **Vulnerable to moisture, kinking, abrasion damage.**
- **Burning rate varies with pressure, temperature, humidity** (jitter 5–10 %).
- Replaced almost entirely by shock tube in modern industry.

---

## 2.4 Family 4 — Pyrotechnic Delay Elements

A **pyrotechnic delay** is a small pressed pellet of **slow-burning composition** (typically barium chromate + boron + Pb₃O₄, OR Zr/Ni + KClO₄) housed in a delay element body. It provides a **precise time gap** (e.g., 25 ms, 50 ms) between successive holes in a blast pattern.

$$
\text{Total Delay} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \pm 1\%\text{ jitter}
$$

These are **integral parts** of both NONEL delay detonators and surface delay connectors for detonating cord.

---

## 2.5 Why Non-Electric? — Comparative Engineering Analysis

| Failure Mode | Electric Initiator | Non-Electric Initiator |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Lightning strike | **Catastrophic** | Safe (no conductor) |
| Stray current from motors | Risky | Safe |
| RF / Mobile phone | Risky | Safe |
| Static electricity | Risky | Safe |
| Galvanic action | Possible | Impossible |
| Misfire due to wire break | Possible | Very rare |
| Inherently safe in gassy mines | No | **Yes** (permissible) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The single most important reason for the **industrial shift from electric to non-electric initiators** is **elimination of inadvertent initiation by extraneous energy sources** — primarily **lightning** (highest fatality contributor in mining initiation accidents globally).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Detonation Pressure in Detonating Cord (CJ Theory)

The **Chapman-Jouguet (CJ) condition** is the cornerstone of steady-state detonation physics. For a 1-D planar steady detonation:

**Step 1: Continuity (mass conservation)**

$$
\rho_0 \, D = \rho \, (D - u)
$$

where:
- $\rho_0$ = initial density of unreacted explosive (kg/m³),
- $D$ = VOD (m/s),
- $\rho$ = density at CJ plane (kg/m³),
- $u$ = particle velocity at CJ plane (m/s).

**Step 2: Momentum (Rankine-Hugoniot momentum equation)**

$$
P_{CJ} - P_0 = \rho_0 \, D \, u
$$

For $P_0 \approx 0$ (atmospheric), this simplifies to:

$$
P_{CJ} = \rho_0 \, D \, u
$$

**Step 3: Energy (Rankine-Hugoniot energy equation)**

$$
E - E_0 = \dfrac{(P_0 + P_{CJ})}{2}\,\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_0} - \dfrac{1}{\rho}\right)
$$

With the ideal gas equation of state for the detonation products at the CJ plane and the **CJ sonic condition** $D - u = c_{CJ}$ (detonation products move at the speed of sound relative to the CJ plane), the closed-form CJ solution yields:

$$
u = \dfrac{D}{\gamma + 1}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the momentum equation**

$$
P_{CJ} = \rho_0 \, D \cdot \dfrac{D}{\gamma + 1} = \dfrac{\rho_0 \, D^{2}}{\gamma + 1}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute PETN values**

For standard Primacord (PETN core, 10 g/m):
- $\rho_0 \approx 1{,}540$ kg/m³,
- $D \approx 7{,}000$ m/s,
- $\gamma \approx 3$ (typical for PETN detonation products).

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{1{,}540 \times (7{,}000)^{2}}{3 + 1} = \dfrac{1{,}540 \times 49 \times 10^{6}}{4}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{7.546 \times 10^{10}}{4} = 1.8865 \times 10^{10} \;\text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{CJ} \approx 18.9 \;\text{GPa}}
$$

This is the **detonation pressure of standard Primacord**, and it is the value the KTU board examiner expects you to quote.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity of Detonation (VOD) from Thermochemistry

The classical CJ VOD formula in terms of **heat of detonation Q** and **polytropic index γ** is:

$$
D = \sqrt{2\,(\gamma^{2} - 1)\,Q}
$$

**Step 1: For PETN**
- $Q$ (heat of detonation) ≈ 5,800 kJ/kg = $5.8 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg,
- $\gamma$ ≈ 3.

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$
D = \sqrt{2 \times (3^{2} - 1) \times 5.8 \times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{2 \times 8 \times 5.8 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{92.8 \times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{9.28 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
\boxed{D \approx 9{,}633 \;\text{m/s}}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with experimental value**

The textbook value for PETN at $\rho_0 = 1{,}540$ kg/m³ is **8,300 m/s** for a *confined* charge; the lower value (7,000 m/s) for *unconfined cord* is due to **lateral expansion losses** in the textile wrap, which the simple 1-D CJ formula does not capture. The 1-D formula is an **upper bound**; the actual cord value is **70–80 %** of this ideal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked for the VOD of "Primacord" or "detonating cord" in KTU 2024, give the **practical cord value (≈ 7,000 m/s)**, not the **theoretical PETN VOD (≈ 8,300 m/s)**. The examiner expects the cord value, which is **lower** than free PETN because of radial expansion losses in the textile.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Per Metre of Detonating Cord

For a detonating cord with **linear charge density λ (g/m)**, the **chemical energy per unit length** is:

$$
\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{\lambda}{1000} \cdot Q
$$

**Step 1: For 10 g/m Primacord**

$$
\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{10}{1000} \times 5{,}800 = 58\;\text{kJ/m}
$$

**Step 2: For 40 g/m reinforced cord**

$$
\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{40}{1000} \times 5{,}800 = 232\;\text{kJ/m}
$$

**Step 3: For 80 g/m heavy cord**

$$
\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{80}{1000} \times 5{,}800 = 464\;\text{kJ/m}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation**

> An 80 g/m cord, used as a **trunk line** in open-pit blasting, carries **0.46 kJ/mm** of chemical energy. This is **plenty** to initiate a **booster (250–500 g cast pentolite)** in a fraction of a millisecond. The trunk line is **not a small piece of cord** — it is a **substantial detonation channel**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Shock Tube Propagation Velocity (1-D Acoustic Model)

A shock tube in its simplest model is a **gas-filled pipe with a propagating pressure discontinuity**. The propagation velocity for the **incident shock** in the low-pressure gas is:

$$
V_s = M_s \cdot a_0
$$

where $a_0$ is the **acoustic speed** in the undisturbed gas:

$$
a_0 = \sqrt{\gamma\,R\,T}
$$

For the gas mixture (typically helium or argon) inside a NONEL tube at **T = 300 K**:
- $\gamma$ (monatomic gas) = 5/3,
- $R$ (specific gas constant) = 2077 J/(kg·K) for He,
- $T$ = 300 K.

$$
a_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{5}{3} \times 2077 \times 300} = \sqrt{1{,}038{,}500} \approx 1019 \;\text{m/s}
$$

For a typical shock Mach number $M_s \approx 2$:

$$
V_s = 2 \times 1019 \approx 2{,}038 \;\text{m/s}
$$

This matches the **experimental value of ~2,000 m/s** quoted for the NONEL shock tube.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of NEI Families

| Parameter | Safety Fuse | Shock Tube (NONEL) | Detonating Cord | Electric |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|------------------|----------|
| Initiation energy | Thermal (flame) | Mechanical shock | Detonation | Electrical |
| VOD / Burn rate | 120 s/m | ~2,000 m/s | ~7,000 m/s | Instant |
| Energy/m | ~4 kJ/m (BP) | ~5 J/m | 58 kJ/m (10 g/m) | n/a |
| RF immunity | Yes | **Yes** | **Yes** | NO |
| Lightning immunity | Yes | **Yes** | **Yes** | NO |
| Delay precision | ±5 % | ±1 % (pyrotechnic) | ±1 % (delay det) | ±0.1 % (electronic) |
| Water resistance | Poor | **Excellent** | Excellent (AW grade) | Poor (wires) |
| Cost (per hole) | Lowest | Medium | High (cord + det) | Medium-high |
| Modern adoption | **Abolished** | **Dominant** | Common (trunk) | Declining |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Detonating Cord Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Outer Plastic Jacket Optional PVC or PE] --> nodeB[Outer Polyester Yarn Wrap Counter Helix Tensile 200N]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Asphaltic Bitumen Waterproofing Layer AW Grade Only]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Inner Cotton Yarn Wrap Contains PETN Core]
    nodeD --> nodeE[PETN Core Linear Mass 1.5 to 80 g per metre]
    nodeE --> nodeF[No 6 or No 8 Electric or Non Electric Starter Detonator Crimped]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Detonation Wave VOD approximately 7000 m per second]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Steady State CJ Detonation Pressure 18.9 GPa]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Radial Expansion Through Yarn 95 percent axial retention]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Output Shock Initiates Booster Pentolite 250g]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram: NONEL Shock Tube System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Starter Initiator Percussion 9mm or Gas Pyrotechnic] --> nodeB[Subsonic Compression Wave in LDPE Tube ID 1mm]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Reactive Dust Layer on Inner Wall HMX 0.5 to 2 mg per metre]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Deflagration to Shock Transition]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Steady Shock Front Velocity approximately 2000 m per second]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Shock Pressure 0.3 to 0.5 MPa at Tube Mouth]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Pyrotechnic Delay Element 25 to 500 ms selectable]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Lead Azide Primary Charge 0.2g]
    nodeH --> nodeI[PETN Secondary Base Charge 0.5g]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Output Detonation Wave to Main Charge ANFO or Emulsion]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Comparative Failure-Mode Safety Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Extraneous Energy Sources] --> nodeB[Lightning Strike]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Stray Current from Motors]
    nodeA --> nodeD[RF from Walkie Talkies]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Static Electricity]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Galvanic Action]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Electric Detonator Vulnerable]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH[Non Electric Initiator Immune]
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI[Catastrophic Premature Detonation]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Safe Energy Path]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Functional Initiation Energy Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Lowest Energy Safety Fuse 4 kJ per metre 120 s per metre] --> nodeB[Shock Tube 5 J per metre 2000 m per second]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Detonating Cord 58 kJ per metre 7000 m per second]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Electric Detonator Instantaneous]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Highest Energy Electronic Detonator 1 us precision]
```

> **Interpretation:** Each step in the hierarchy brings **higher energy density, faster delivery, and more precise timing**, but **also reintroduces some electrical sensitivity**. The art of blasting engineering is to **match the initiation energy to the application** — small-scale, gassy mines prefer safety fuse; large open pits use a combination of NONEL + cord + electronic detonators.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "non-electric initiator". List any four advantages of non-electric initiation systems over electric initiation systems in commercial blasting.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-electric initiator:** An explosive initiation device that transmits or triggers a detonation impulse using a chemical detonation train, a shock wave, or a thermal/pyrotechnic pulse — **without the use of an electrical current** in the firing circuit.

**Four advantages (any four required):**

1. **Immunity to extraneous electrical energy**: Safe against lightning, stray currents, RF, static, and galvanic action.
2. **Permissible in gassy/dusty mines**: NONEL shock tubes produce no flame or spark, satisfying intrinsic-safety standards (e.g., IS 6609, DGMS India).
3. **Reduced misfire risk**: No wire break, no continuity failure, no short-circuit.
4. **Simpler field logistics**: A single trunk line can fire many holes; delay timing is built into the delay element, not requiring complex circuit calculations.
5. **Better fragmentation control**: Pyrotechnic delays provide 25–50 ms steps, allowing precise burden-relief sequencing.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of a detonating cord in a multi-hole blast? Why is its velocity of detonation (≈ 7,000 m/s) important for trunk-line applications?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of detonating cord in a multi-hole blast:** A detonating cord (Primacord/Detacord) is used as the **trunk line** that carries the detonation wave from the **initiator** (one end) to **all the branch lines** tied into individual blast holes. The branch lines then initiate **booster charges** (cast pentolite/TNT) in each hole, which in turn initiate the **main charge** (ANFO/emulsion). The cord also functions as a **delay propagation path** when surface delay connectors are used.

> **Why VOD ≈ 7,000 m/s is important:** The trunk-line detonation wave must reach **every branch tie-point in a time much shorter than the longest in-hole delay** (typically 500–1000 ms). At 7,000 m/s, a 100 m trunk line is traversed in **~14 ms** — orders of magnitude shorter than typical pyrotechnic delays — ensuring **no sympathetic detonation** of neighbouring holes before the intended delay sequence completes. This guarantees the **correct delay timing** of the blast pattern.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS) — ESE MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has TWO sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **construction and working of a non-electric shock tube initiator (NONEL system)**. Explain the role of the reactive HMX coating and state the typical shock propagation velocity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **detonation pressure in a standard Primacord (10 g/m PETN core, VOD = 7,000 m/s)** using the Chapman-Jouguet equations, clearly listing all assumptions. Compute the energy per metre of the cord. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) – 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Construction:**
A NONEL shock tube is a 3 mm outer diameter, 1 mm inner diameter LDPE tube. The **inner wall is coated with a sub-milligram-per-metre layer of reactive HMX powder** (0.5–2 mg/m), sometimes mixed with aluminium. The tube is filled with a **light gas (He/Ar)** at ambient pressure and sealed at both ends. A **pyrotechnic starter** (percussion cap, 9 mm) is at the uphole end, and a **delay element + base charge** is at the downhole end.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Working — initiation sequence:**
The starter delivers a mechanical impulse → a **compression wave** forms in the gas column → the wave passes over the HMX dust on the wall → dust **deflagrates to a stable low-order shock** → shock travels at **~2,000 m/s** → shock enters the delay element → delay elapses → shock initiates **lead azide (primary, 0.2 g)** → lead azide flashes to PETN (secondary base, 0.5 g) → high-order detonation output to main charge.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Role of HMX coating:**
HMX is a **secondary high explosive** with a **deflagration-to-detonation transition** capability. The thin dust layer, when ignited by the compression wave, releases ~5,500 kJ/kg of energy — **enough to sustain the shock front** in the gas column. The amount is **too small to detonate the cord** (no risk of fragmentation) but **sufficient to drive the shock** to the downhole detonator.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Typical velocity:**
The shock propagation velocity is **1,950–2,050 m/s** (typically quoted as **2,000 m/s**).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) – 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Assumptions:**
(i) One-dimensional, steady-state planar detonation. (ii) Polytropic detonation products with $\gamma = 3$. (iii) CJ condition: $D - u = c_{CJ}$. (iv) $P_0 \approx 0$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — CJ derivation:**
From mass, momentum, energy and CJ sonic condition, the closed-form expressions are:

$$
u = \dfrac{D}{\gamma + 1} \qquad\qquad P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0\,D^{2}}{\gamma + 1}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Numerical substitution:**
Given $\rho_0 = 1{,}540$ kg/m³, $D = 7{,}000$ m/s, $\gamma = 3$:

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{1{,}540 \times 49 \times 10^{6}}{4} = 1.8865 \times 10^{10}\;\text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{CJ} \approx 18.9\;\text{GPa}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Energy per metre:**
For $\lambda = 10$ g/m, $Q = 5{,}800$ kJ/kg:

$$
\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{10}{1000} \times 5{,}800 = 58\;\text{kJ/m}
$$

**Engineering comment:** This energy density is enough to reliably initiate a 250 g cast pentolite booster in < 1 ms.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**(a)** Compare the **construction, working principle, and energy content of detonating cord and shock tube initiators**. Why is the shock tube considered safer in gassy coal mines? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blast pattern has 25 holes tied to a 150 m trunk line of 10 g/m Primacord. The trunk is initiated at one end. The longest in-hole delay is 500 ms. Determine:
   (i) The time taken by the trunk detonation wave to reach the last branch tie-point. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The total chemical energy stored in the trunk line. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The detonation pressure at the CJ plane, given $\rho_0 = 1{,}540$ kg/m³ and $\gamma = 3$. **(2 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) – 7 marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Detonating Cord | Shock Tube (NONEL) |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Construction** | PETN core (1.5–80 g/m), textile wraps, optional plastic jacket | LDPE tube (1 mm ID) with HMX dust on inner wall, gas-filled |
| **Working** | Sustained high-order detonation of PETN | Low-order shock (deflagration-to-shock) in gas |
| **VOD** | 7,000 m/s | 2,000 m/s |
| **Energy/m** | 58 kJ/m (10 g/m cord) | ~5 J/m |
| **Output** | Direct high-pressure detonation to booster | Low-pressure shock to delay → detonator |
| **Side-effect** | Cord is consumed (no residue) | Tube remains intact, no flame |
| **Cost** | Higher (per metre) | Lower (per metre) |

**Why shock tube is safer in gassy coal mines (2 marks):**
1. The shock tube **produces no flame, no spark, and no hot gas jet at the tube mouth** — it cannot ignite methane/air mixtures.
2. The reactive dust is **0.5–2 mg/m** — even complete combustion cannot raise the surrounding gas temperature to the **methane ignition temperature (537 °C)** at any distance.
3. It is **certified as "permissible"** under international standards (MSHA, DGMS, ATEX), allowing use in **gassy/dusty underground coal mines** where electric detonators are prohibited.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) – 7 marks

**Given:**
- Trunk length $L = 150$ m
- VOD $D = 7{,}000$ m/s
- Linear charge $\lambda = 10$ g/m
- $\rho_0 = 1{,}540$ kg/m³
- $\gamma = 3$
- $Q = 5{,}800$ kJ/kg

**(i) Trunk wave time (3 marks):**

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{150}{7{,}000} = 0.0214\;\text{s} = 21.4\;\text{ms}
$$

This is **far less** than 500 ms — confirming safe sequencing.

**(ii) Total trunk energy (2 marks):**

$$
E = \dfrac{\lambda}{1000} \cdot Q \cdot L = \dfrac{10}{1000} \times 5{,}800 \times 150
$$

$$
E = 0.01 \times 5{,}800 \times 150 = 8{,}700\;\text{kJ}
$$

**(iii) Detonation pressure (2 marks):**

$$
P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0\,D^{2}}{\gamma + 1} = \dfrac{1{,}540 \times 49 \times 10^{6}}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{CJ} = 18.87\;\text{GPa}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALLS — Where students lose marks in Module-2 NEI questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing VOD of detonating cord (7,000 m/s) with VOD of shock tube (2,000 m/s).** Examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if these are swapped. Memorize: **cord = detonation, tube = shock**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert g/m to kg/m** in energy-per-metre calculations. 10 g/m = 0.01 kg/m. Common student error is to write $E = 10 \times 5800 = 58{,}000$ kJ/m (WRONG by 1000×).
> 
> 3. **Mixing up γ = 3 vs γ + 1 = 4** in the CJ pressure formula. The formula is $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$, **NOT** $\rho_0 D^2 / \gamma$. Examiner will deduct full marks.
> 
> 4. **Claiming shock tube is a "detonation".** It is a **stable low-order shock**, not a CJ detonation. Writing "shock tube detonates at 2000 m/s" will lose you the conceptual mark.
> 
> 5. **Not listing assumptions** in derivation questions. Always state: "1-D steady, CJ condition, $P_0 \approx 0$, polytropic γ". 1 mark reserved for assumptions in every derivation.
> 
> 6. **Confusing "permissible" with "permissive"** — the correct mining-engineering term is **"permissible explosive / permissible initiator"**, not "permissive".

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Non-Electric Initiators**

- ✅ **Definition:** NEI = initiation WITHOUT electrical current.
- ✅ **Four families:** (i) Safety fuse + plain detonator, (ii) Detonating cord, (iii) Shock tube / NONEL, (iv) Pyrotechnic delay.
- ✅ **Detonating cord core:** PETN; **VOD = 7,000 m/s**; **energy = 58 kJ/m** (10 g/m cord).
- ✅ **Shock tube core:** HMX dust on LDPE; **shock velocity = 2,000 m/s**; energy ~ 5 J/m.
- ✅ **CJ detonation pressure formula:** $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$.
- ✅ **For Primacord:** $P_{CJ} \approx 18.9$ GPa.
- ✅ **VOD formula:** $D = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)Q}$ — upper bound (1-D ideal).
- ✅ **Why NEI?** Lightning, stray current, RF, static immunity.
- ✅ **Shock tube safety in gassy mines:** No flame, no spark, no hot gas.
- ✅ **Pyrotechnic delay jitter:** ±1–2 % of nominal (25, 50, 100 ms standard steps).
- ✅ **Critical diameter of PETN:** ~0.3 mm; failure diameter ~3 mm.
- ✅ **Safety fuse burn rate:** 120 ± 20 s/m — historical method, now obsolete.
- ✅ **KTU numbers to memorize:** $\rho_0$ (PETN) = 1,540 kg/m³; $\gamma$ (PETN products) = 3; $Q$ (PETN) = 5,800 kJ/kg; $D$ (PETN, theoretical) ≈ 8,300 m/s; $D$ (cord, practical) ≈ 7,000 m/s.
- ✅ **VOD Hierarchy:** Safety fuse (120 s/m) < Shock tube (2,000 m/s) < Detonating cord (7,000 m/s) < Electronic det (instant, with 1 µs precision).
- ✅ **Standard detonator strength for cord initiation:** No. 6 / No. 8, base charge ≥ 0.5 g PETN.
- ✅ **Energy of 10 g/m cord = 58 kJ/m** — sufficient to initiate 250 g cast pentolite booster.
- ✅ **Primary explosive in NEI detonators:** Lead azide, 0.2 g.
- ✅ **Secondary base charge:** PETN or RDX, 0.5 g.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot-Wire Initiators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hot-Wire Initiators # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **Hot-Wire Initiator** (also termed a **bridgewire initiator** or **electrical detonator**) is an electro-explosive device (EED) that functions on the principle of **resistive (Joule) heating**. When a controlled electrical current is passed through a fine, high-resistivity metallic wire embedded in (or in intimate contact with) a primary explosive, pyrotechnic composition, or explosive charge, the resulting **I²R thermal energy** raises the temperature of the explosive to its **ignition temperature (T_ig)** or **activation energy threshold (E_a)**, thereby triggering a self-sustaining deflagration or detonation. $$P_{dissipated} = I^2 R_{bw}$$ Where: - $I$ = firing current (Amperes) - $R_{bw}$ = bridgewire resistance (Ohms) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hot-Wire Initiator ≠ Exploding Bridge-Wire (EBW) Initiator.** > A hot-wire initiator relies on **slow thermal heating** (millisecond regime) of a primary explosive, whereas an EBW uses a **fast shock wave** generated by vaporization of the bridgewire (microsecond regime). This is a high-frequency exam distinction in KTU ESE papers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Electric Kettle and Matchstick** > Imagine a thin **nichrome wire** (the same alloy used in your room heater or electric kettle). When current flows, the wire glows red-hot. Now, instead of heating water, place the **head of a matchstick (containing red phosphorus / potassium chlorate)** in contact with this glowing wire. The match head **ignites** and bursts into flame. A hot-wire initiator is essentially this exact principle, miniaturized, sealed, and loaded with a much more reactive primary explosive (such as **lead azide, Pb(N₃)₂**) instead of match-head composition. The wire heats up; the explosive reaches its ignition point; the reaction becomes self-sustaining. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Typical Firing Current (All-Fire)**: **0.5 A – 3.0 A** for civilian blasting; up to **5 A** for military detonators. - **No-Fire Current (Safety Threshold)**: **0.1 A – 0.25 A** held for **5 minutes** without initiation (MIL-STD-1316 standard). - **Bridgewire Resistance**: **0.5 Ω – 2.0 Ω** (commonly around **1.0 Ω ± 10%**). - **Bridgewire Diameter**: **25 µm – 75 µm** (typically **~40 µm**). - **Primary Explosive Used**: Lead Azide **Pb(N₃)₂** with ignition temperature ~**315 °C**; or Lead Styphnate ~**282 °C**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Stepwise Logic 1. **Electrical Input Phase**: A capacitor bank or constant-current source delivers a precisely controlled current pulse (typically **DC**, but AC and capacitor-discharge pulses are also used) through the lead wires to the bridgewire. 2. **Joule Heating Phase**: The bridgewire, made of a high-resistivity alloy (Nichrome, Constantan, or Platinum-Iridium), converts electrical energy into thermal energy per the **Joule-Lenz Law**: $$Q = I^2 R_{bw} \, t = V \, I \, t = \frac{V^2 t}{R_{bw}}$$ 3. **Heat Transfer Phase**: Heat conducts from the bridgewire into the surrounding explosive charge. The rate of heat transfer depends on: - Thermal conductivity of the explosive (k, W/m·K) - Contact area (A, m²) - Temperature gradient (ΔT = T_wire − T_explosive) 4. **Thermal Ignition Phase**: When the explosive at the wire-explosive interface reaches its **critical ignition temperature T_c**, exothermic decomposition reactions accelerate faster than heat is lost to the surroundings. The reaction becomes **self-sustaining** (thermal runaway, governed by **Frank-Kamenetskii thermal explosion theory**). 5. **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)**: In initiating explosives, the deflagration rapidly transitions to a stable detonation wave in the base charge (usually **PETN** or **RDX**). ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Hot-Wire Design | Design Criterion | Engineering Rationale | |------------------|----------------------| | **High resistivity material** | Maximizes I²R heat for a given current | | **Small diameter wire** | Low thermal mass → fast temperature rise | | **Intimate explosive contact** | Minimizes thermal resistance at interface | | **Primary explosive loading** | Low activation energy (~150–315 °C) ensures reliable ignition | | **Hermetic sealing** | Prevents moisture ingress, ESD damage, and accidental initiation | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these equations — they constitute ~70% of KTU ESE numerical questions on this topic.** | # | Formula / Parameter | LaTeX Expression | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Power dissipated in bridgewire | $P = I^2 R_{bw}$ | Watts (W) | Instantaneous heating power | | 2 | Total heat (energy) generated | $Q = I^2 R_{bw} \, t$ | Joules (J) | Cumulative thermal energy | | 3 | Bridgewire resistance | $R_{bw} = \rho \, L / A$ | Ohms (Ω) | Geometry + material property | | 4 | Resistivity temperature dependence | $\rho_T = \rho_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ | Ω·m | Resistance increases with temperature | | 5 | Critical ignition energy (empirical) | $E_{ig} = C_v \cdot m \cdot (T_{ig} - T_{amb})$ | Joules (J) | Minimum energy to raise explosive to T_ig | | 6 | Bridgewire thermal time constant | $\tau = \rho_m \, c_p \, V_{bw} / (h \, A_s)$ | seconds (s) | Response time of wire heating | | 7 | All-Fire / No-Fire safety factor | $SF = I_{all-fire} / I_{no-fire}$ | dimensionless | Should be ≥ 1.5 (MIL-STD) | | 8 | Frank-Kamenetskii parameter | $\delta = \frac{Q_{rxn} \, E_a \, r^2 \, C_0 \, e^{-E_a/RT_a}}{\kappa \, R \, T_a^2}$ | dimensionless | Predicts thermal runaway | > **Notation note:** $\rho$ = resistivity, $L$ = wire length, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $\rho_m$ = mass density, $c_p$ = specific heat, $V_{bw}$ = bridgewire volume, $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient, $A_s$ = surface area. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mining & Quarrying**: Sequential blasting of boreholes in surface and underground mines. - **Demolition**: Controlled structural collapse of buildings and bridges. - **Military**: Detonation of warheads, shaped charges, and missile arming systems. - **Aerospace**: Stage separation bolts, parachute mortars, and solid rocket motor ignition. - **Pyrotechnics**: Flash powder initiation in display fireworks. - **Seismic Exploration**: Land and marine seismic source detonation. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bridgewire Resistance from First Principles The resistance of a uniform metallic wire is derived from **Ohm's law** applied at the differential level: $$R_{bw} = \int_0^L \frac{\rho \, dx}{A(x)}$$ For a wire of uniform cross-section (A = constant) and uniform resistivity: $$R_{bw} = \frac{\rho \, L}{A}$$ **Step 1**: Express cross-sectional area in terms of wire diameter *d*: $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ **Step 2**: Substitute into resistance formula: $$R_{bw} = \frac{\rho \, L}{\pi d^2 / 4} = \frac{4\rho L}{\pi d^2}$$ **Step 3**: Numerical example (typical Nichrome bridgewire): - $\rho$ (Nichrome) = $1.10 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω·m - $L = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $d = 40$ µm $= 40 \times 10^{-6}$ m $$R_{bw} = \frac{4 \times (1.10 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3})}{\pi \times (40 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$ $$R_{bw} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-8}}{5.027 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R_{bw} \approx 4.38 \; \Omega$$ > **Engineering note**: To achieve the standard ~1 Ω bridgewire, designers either reduce wire length (~1.1 mm) or increase diameter. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Firing Energy (Energy Balance Method) We treat the bridgewire + a thin shell of explosive (mass *m*, specific heat *c_v*) as a lumped thermal system. For ignition: $$Q_{generated} \geq Q_{required}$$ **Step 1**: Heat required to raise the explosive shell from ambient ($T_{amb}$) to ignition temperature ($T_{ig}$): $$Q_{req} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_{ig} - T_{amb})$$ **Step 2**: Heat supplied by Joule heating during pulse duration *t*: $$Q_{sup} = I^2 R_{bw} \, t$$ **Step 3**: Equate for the **critical (minimum firing) condition**: $$I^2 R_{bw} \, t_{crit} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_{ig} - T_{amb})$$ **Step 4**: Solve for minimum firing current at a fixed pulse duration: $$I_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{m \, c_v \, (T_{ig} - T_{amb})}{R_{bw} \, t}}$$ **Step 5**: Alternatively, solve for minimum firing energy: $$E_{crit} = I^2 R_{bw} \, t_{crit} = m \, c_v \, (T_{ig} - T_{amb})$$ **Numerical evaluation** (Lead Azide initiator): - $m = 50$ mg $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ kg - $c_v = 500$ J/(kg·K) (approx.) - $T_{ig} = 315 °C = 588$ K - $T_{amb} = 25 °C = 298$ K - $R_{bw} = 1$ Ω - $t = 10$ ms $= 0.01$ s $$Q_{req} = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 500 \times (588 - 298) = (2.5 \times 10^{-2}) \times 290$$ $$Q_{req} = 7.25 \; \text{J}$$ $$I_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{7.25}{1 \times 0.01}} = \sqrt{725} \approx 26.9 \; \text{A}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Note on real-world values**: In practice, the contact area is much smaller than the full mass, so the *effective* heated mass is ~1–5 mg. Actual firing currents are ~**0.5 – 3 A**. Always check the effective heated mass in your problem statement. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frank-Kamenetskii Thermal Explosion Criterion For a reactive explosive with first-order Arrhenius kinetics, the steady-state energy balance in a sphere of radius *r* gives the **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter**: $$\delta = \frac{Q_{rxn} \, E_a \, r^2 \, C_0 \, e^{-E_a / R_u T_a}}{\kappa \, R_u \, T_a^2}$$ **Step 1**: Identify critical condition — thermal explosion occurs when: $$\delta \geq \delta_{crit}$$ For an infinite slab $\delta_{crit} = 0.878$; for a sphere $\delta_{crit} = 3.32$. **Step 2**: Solve for the **critical ambient temperature** $T_{a,crit}$ that triggers runaway. This requires implicit numerical solution, but the conceptual limit is: $$T_{a,crit} \approx \frac{E_a}{R_u \ln\left(\dfrac{Q_{rxn} \, E_a \, r^2 \, C_0}{\kappa \, R_u \, T_a^2 \, \delta_{crit}}\right)}$$ **Step 3**: Design implication — to prevent accidental ignition: - Reduce *r* (smaller charge volume) → lowers $\delta$ - Increase $\kappa$ (better heat dissipation) - Use explosives with higher $E_a$ (higher activation barrier) --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hot-Wire vs EBW vs SCB Initiators | Parameter | **Hot-Wire Initiator** | **Exploding Bridge-Wire (EBW)** | **Semiconductor Bridge (SCB)** | |---|---|---|---| | Initiation mechanism | **Thermal (slow Joule heating)** | **Shock / plasma (wire vaporization)** | **Plasma + thermal** | | Bridgewire material | Nichrome / Constantan / Pt-Ir | Gold / Platinum | Doped polysilicon | | Typical firing energy | **10 – 100 mJ** | **50 – 500 mJ** | **1 – 5 mJ** | | Firing time | **1 – 10 ms** | **< 1 µs** | **< 10 µs** | | Explosive required | Primary (e.g., Pb(N₃)₂) | Secondary (e.g., PETN, RDX) | Secondary | | ESD sensitivity | Moderate | Low | Very low | | Cost | Low | High | Moderate | | Primary use | Mining, demolition | Military, aerospace | Advanced fuzing, smart munitions | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Internal Construction of a Hot-Wire Detonator (Cross-Sectional Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB node1[LEAD WIRES - Copper insulated
carries firing current]:::lead node2[ELECTRICAL HEADER
hermetic glass metal seal
prevents moisture and ESD]:::header node3[BRIDGEWIRE - Nichrome 40 micron
resistive heating element R equals 1 ohm]:::wire node4[IGNITION CHARGE - Lead Styphnate
first fire sensitive primary explosive]:::ign node5[INITIATING CHARGE - Lead Azide PbN3
detonating primary explosive]:::init node6[BASE CHARGE - PETN or RDX
secondary high explosive output]:::base node7[DETONATION WAVE OUTPUT
velocity 7000 to 8500 m per s]:::output node8[SHELL - Aluminum or Steel cartridge
sealed outer body diameter 7 mm]:::shell node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node8 -.encloses.-> node3 node8 -.encloses.-> node4 node8 -.encloses.-> node5 node8 -.encloses.-> node6 classDef lead fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000 classDef header fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,color:#000 classDef wire fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,color:#fff classDef ign fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 classDef init fill:#ff4500,stroke:#8b0000,color:#fff classDef base fill:#dc143c,stroke:#8b0000,color:#fff classDef output fill:#00ced1,stroke:#008b8b,color:#000 classDef shell fill:#a9a9a9,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Operational Flow (Heat-to-Detonation Sequence) ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Power Source: Capacitor or
Constant Current Supply]:::src stepB[Step 1: DC Current Applied
I through lead wires]:::step stepC[Step 2: Joule Heating in
Bridgewire P equals I squared R]:::step stepD[Step 3: Conductive Heat
Transfer to Explosive]:::step stepE[Step 4: Explosive Temperature
reaches T ignition]:::step stepF[Step 5: Exothermic Decomposition
exceeds Heat Loss]:::step stepG[Step 6: Thermal Runaway
self sustaining reaction]:::step stepH[Step 7: Deflagration to
Detonation Transition DDT]:::step stepI[Step 8: Steady Detonation Wave
in Base Charge output]:::out stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI classDef src fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#00008b,color:#000 classDef step fill:#fafad2,stroke:#daa520,color:#000 classDef out fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Electro-Explosive Device (EED) Selection ```mermaid flowchart TB q1{Application Type?}:::q a1[Civilian Mining or
Quarrying]:::leaf a2[Military Warhead or
Aerospace Stage Separation]:::leaf a3[Sensitive ESD Prone
Environment]:::leaf q2{Required Response Time?}:::q b1[Greater than 1 ms
use Hot Wire]:::leaf b2[Less than 1 microsecond
use EBW or SCB]:::leaf q3{Safety Priority?}:::q c1[High ESD immunity
required SCB preferred]:::leaf c2[Low cost acceptable
Hot Wire preferred]:::leaf q1 -->|Low energy civil| a1 q1 -->|High reliability| a2 q1 -->|Static sensitive| a3 a1 --> q2 a2 --> q2 a3 --> q3 q2 --> b1 q2 --> b2 q3 --> c1 q3 --> c2 classDef q fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#483d8b,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set A]** **Define the term "All-Fire Current" for a hot-wire initiator and state its typical relationship with the "No-Fire Current" as per MIL-STD-1316.** **Model Answer** (3 marks): - **All-Fire Current** is defined as the minimum current that, when applied to the bridgewire for a specified duration (typically 50 ms or longer), **initiates the explosive charge with 99.9% probability at a 95% confidence level**. **(1.5 marks)** - **No-Fire Current** is the maximum current that can be applied for a specified duration (commonly **5 minutes**) **without initiating the explosive charge with 99.9% probability at 95% confidence**. **(1 mark)** - **Relationship**: As per MIL-STD-1316, the ratio of all-fire to no-fire current should satisfy a **safety factor of at least 1.5**, i.e., $SF = I_{all-fire} / I_{no-fire} \geq 1.5$. For example, typical values are $I_{no-fire} = 0.2$ A and $I_{all-fire} = 0.5$ A. **(0.5 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** **Differentiate between a primary explosive and a secondary explosive in the context of hot-wire initiator design. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer** (3 marks): - **Primary explosives**: Highly sensitive, easily initiated by heat, friction, spark, or impact. Used in **direct contact with the bridgewire** because they require very low activation energy (~150–315 °C). Example: **Lead Azide, Pb(N₃)₂** or **Lead Styphnate**. **(1.5 marks)** - **Secondary explosives**: Much less sensitive, requiring a strong shock wave or detonation to initiate. Used in the **base charge** to deliver high detonation pressure. Example: **PETN, RDX, or HMX**. **(1.5 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE — Dec 2024 Expected Style]** **Answer the following:** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** *With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working principle of a hot-wire electric detonator. Explain the role of each component.* **Model Solution**: 1. **Construction (3.5 marks)**: - **Lead wires**: Copper-insulated, carry firing current from the blasting machine to the bridgewire. - **Header (plug)**: A hermetic seal (typically glass-to-metal or polymer) that prevents moisture ingress and provides electrical isolation. - **Bridgewire**: A short length (1–5 mm) of **Nichrome (Ni 80%, Cr 20%)** or **Constantan** wire, ~**40 µm diameter**, with resistance ~**1 Ω**. - **Ignition charge**: A small quantity (~**30 mg**) of **Lead Styphnate** pressed against the bridgewire; provides first-fire ignition. - **Initiating charge**: A slightly larger mass (~**100 mg**) of **Lead Azide**; undergoes rapid deflagration-to-detonation transition. - **Base charge**: **PETN** or **RDX** (~**500 mg–1 g**); delivers the steady detonation wave. - **Shell**: Aluminum or gilding-metal tube (diameter **7.0 mm or 7.5 mm**, length 50–80 mm), sealed at one end. 2. **Working Principle (3.5 marks)**: - Firing current (**~0.5–3 A DC**) is applied through the lead wires. - The bridgewire dissipates energy per **Joule's Law**: $P = I^2 R_{bw}$. - Heat conducts into the Lead Styphnate; once it reaches ~**282 °C**, deflagration begins. - This hot particle jet initiates the Lead Azide, which transitions to **detonation** in microseconds. - The detonation wave transfers to the PETN base charge, producing the final **output detonation** at ~**8000 m/s**. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** *A hot-wire initiator has a bridgewire of Nichrome with diameter 50 µm, length 3 mm, and resistivity 1.1 × 10⁻⁶ Ω·m. If a current of 1.5 A is applied for 8 ms, calculate:* *(i) The bridgewire resistance.* *(ii) The total heat energy dissipated in the bridgewire.* **Model Solution**: **Given**: $d = 50$ µm $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m; $L = 3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $\rho = 1.1 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω·m; $I = 1.5$ A; $t = 8$ ms $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ s. **(i) Bridgewire resistance (3 marks)**: $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{4}$$ $$A = 1.963 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}^2$$ $$R_{bw} = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R_{bw} = \frac{3.3 \times 10^{-9}}{1.963 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.681 \; \Omega$$ **(ii) Total heat dissipated (4 marks)**: $$Q = I^2 R_{bw} \, t = (1.5)^2 \times 1.681 \times (8 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$Q = 2.25 \times 1.681 \times 0.008 = 0.0303 \; \text{J} \approx 30.3 \; \text{mJ}$$ > **Verification**: This value (30.3 mJ) falls well within the typical hot-wire firing energy range of 10–100 mJ, indicating reliable ignition is achieved. ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE — June 2024 Expected Style, Alternative Choice]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** *Explain the Frank-Kamenetskii thermal explosion theory as applied to hot-wire initiators. Discuss how it relates to the no-fire and all-fire current specifications.* **Model Solution**: 1. **Theoretical Foundation (3.5 marks)**: - Frank-Kamenetskii theory models a reactive explosive as a body in which exothermic decomposition follows **Arrhenius kinetics**: rate $= Z e^{-E_a/RT}$. - The theory establishes a **critical dimensionless parameter** $\delta_{crit}$ below which the system is thermally stable and above which thermal runaway occurs: $$\delta = \frac{Q_{rxn} \, E_a \, r^2 \, C_0 \, e^{-E_a / R_u T_a}}{\kappa \, R_u \, T_a^2}$$ - For a sphere, $\delta_{crit} = 3.32$; for an infinite slab, $\delta_{crit} = 0.878$. 2. **Application to Hot-Wire Design (3.5 marks)**: - The **no-fire current** corresponds to the maximum electrical input below which the bridgewire-explosive system remains in the sub-critical regime ($\delta < \delta_{crit}$) indefinitely. Heat generation rate equals heat loss rate; the system reaches steady state without runaway. - The **all-fire current** corresponds to the input that drives the system past $\delta_{crit}$, where the temperature at the wire-explosive interface rises until $T_{ig}$ is reached. - The ratio of these currents defines the **safety factor**, and is mathematically tied to the Frank-Kamenetskii critical temperature $T_{a,crit}$. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** *A blast designer specifies that the all-fire current shall be 1.0 A and the no-fire current shall be 0.2 A (both at 100 ms pulse duration). Determine the safety factor and comment on whether the design meets MIL-STD-1316 specifications. Also list TWO design modifications to improve the safety margin.** **Model Solution**: 1. **Safety Factor Calculation (3 marks)**: $$SF = \frac{I_{all-fire}}{I_{no-fire}} = \frac{1.0 \; \text{A}}{0.2 \; \text{A}} = 5.0$$ 2. **Compliance Check (2 marks)**: Since $SF = 5.0 \geq 1.5$, the design **satisfies** the MIL-STD-1316 safety requirement comfortably. In fact, an SF of 5.0 is considered **highly conservative** and is typical of military-grade detonators. 3. **Two Design Modifications to Improve Safety Margin (2 marks)**: - **Reduce bridgewire resistance** variability by tightening manufacturing tolerances on wire diameter (±5%) and using higher-purity Nichrome alloy. - **Improve thermal dissipation** at the header by adding a small **heat-sink fin** or using a more thermally conductive ceramic header (e.g., **alumina Al₂O₃** instead of plastic), allowing heat to escape faster at low currents. - *Alternative*: Use a **secondary safety barrier** such as a thin polymer film between bridgewire and explosive that melts only at high currents. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "no-fire current" with "leakage current"** — No-fire current is a probabilistic specification (statistical), not an electrical leakage phenomenon. **(−1 mark)** > 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — Wire diameter is in **µm**, length in **mm**, time in **ms**; convert to SI (m, s) before calculation. **(−2 marks typical)** > 3. **Mixing up hot-wire and EBW initiators** — A hot-wire initiator uses **slow thermal heating** and primary explosives; EBW uses **wire vaporization shock** and secondary explosives. Examiners frequently test this distinction. **(−2 marks)** > 4. **Omitting the role of ignition charge (Lead Styphnate)** — Many students jump directly to Lead Azide, missing the intermediate low-ignition-temperature charge that ensures reliable first-fire. **(−1 mark)** > 5. **Not specifying the time duration** when stating all-fire / no-fire currents — Always quote "**0.5 A for 100 ms**" not just "**0.5 A**". --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Bulletins for Last-Minute KTU Revision:** - ✅ Hot-wire initiator = **electro-explosive device** based on **I²R Joule heating** of a resistive bridgewire (typically Nichrome, ~1 Ω). - ✅ Working formula: **$P = I^2 R_{bw}$**; Energy = **$Q = I^2 R_{bw} t$**. - ✅ Bridgewire resistance: **$R_{bw} = \rho L / A = 4\rho L / (\pi d^2)$**. - ✅ Five functional components in order: **Lead wires → Header → Bridgewire → Ignition charge (Lead Styphnate) → Initiating charge (Lead Azide) → Base charge (PETN/RDX)**. - ✅ **All-fire current** = minimum current for 99.9% ignition; **No-fire current** = maximum current for 99.9% non-ignition (5 min exposure). - ✅ MIL-STD safety factor: **SF = I_all-fire / I_no-fire ≥ 1.5**. - ✅ Typical firing energy range: **10 – 100 mJ**; typical firing time: **1 – 10 ms**. - ✅ **NOT the same as EBW initiator**: Hot-wire = thermal, slow, primary explosive; EBW = shock/plasma, fast, secondary explosive. - ✅ **NOT the same as SCB initiator**: Hot-wire = metallic wire; SCB = doped polysilicon, plasma-assisted. - ✅ Frank-Kamenetskii criterion governs thermal runaway: $\delta \geq \delta_{crit}$ triggers ignition. - ✅ Activation energy $E_a$ and ignition temperature $T_{ig}$ are material-specific; Lead Azide $T_{ig} \approx$ **315 °C**. - ✅ Safety design priorities: low thermal mass bridgewire + intimate explosive contact + hermetic seal + conservative safety factor. - ✅ For KTU exam: Always **draw a labelled diagram**, **show unit conversions**, and **comment on safety factor compliance**.

topics.title: Exploding Bridge Wire Detonators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exploding Bridge Wire Detonators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Exploding Bridge Wire (EBW) Detonator** is a precision electro-explosive initiating device in which a thin metallic bridge wire, typically **gold** or **platinum** of diameter ~**0.04 mm**, is explosively vaporized by a sudden, high-current capacitive discharge. The resulting plasma burst generates a strong shock wave that directly initiates a high-density secondary explosive (donor charge), without the intermediate deflagration stage found in conventional hot-wire detonators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EBW detonators belong to the class of *shock-initiated* or *non-primary* detonators. They are fundamentally different from conventional bridge-wire (hot-wire) detonators because initiation occurs via **shock**, not **thermal heating**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine lighting a firecracker. In a normal electrical detonator, the bridge wire glows red-hot like a tiny toaster coil, and the heat slowly cooks the chemical until it pops. In an **EBW detonator**, the wire does *not* melt — it **explodes**. Think of it like a microscopic lightning strike: a capacitor dumps tens of thousands of amps through a hair-thin wire in less than a microsecond. The wire doesn't get a chance to melt; it instantly vaporizes into a tiny fireball of plasma, which punches a shock wave into the explosive pellet. The whole event takes about **1–2 microseconds (µs)**.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Metrics / Physical Constants (Industry-Standard Values):**
> - **Bridge wire material:** Gold (Au) — preferred for predictable action integral
> - **Bridge wire diameter:** ~**0.038–0.040 mm** (≈ **38–40 µm**)
> - **Capacitance (C):** **1 µF to 10 µF**
> - **Charging voltage (V):** **1000 V to 3000 V DC**
> - **Peak current:** up to **~1000 A**
> - **Burst time:** typically **< 2 µs**
> - **No-Fire Energy (NFE):** ≈ **0.05 A²·s** (gold wire) — known as the *action integral*
> - **All-Fire Energy (AFE):** ≈ **0.10–0.20 A²·s**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Energy Storage Phase** — A high-voltage DC supply charges a low-inductance energy-storage capacitor (typically oil-filled or ceramic) to a precise set voltage.
2. **Trigger Command** — A fast switch (trigatron spark gap, krytron, or thyratron) is fired by a low-energy control pulse.
3. **Capacitive Discharge** — The capacitor dumps its energy through the bridge wire of the detonator in a damped oscillatory pulse.
4. **Resistive Heating (Sub-microsecond)** — The bridge wire's resistance rises sharply as it heats. Energy delivered at this stage: $P = I^2 R$.
5. **Explosive Vaporization** — When the deposited energy crosses the *action integral* threshold, the wire does not melt — it **explodes** (silvery burst) into a high-pressure, high-temperature plasma (~**10⁴ K**, ~**10⁹ Pa**).
6. **Shock Wave Generation** — The plasma expansion drives a strong shock wave into the adjacent **donor charge** (typically PETN pressed to ~**1.6 g/cm³**).
7. **Detonation Transfer** — The shock initiates the donor, which then detonates the **acceptor charge** (RDX/HMX at higher density), delivering a high-order detonation output.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- *Why gold?* — Gold has a well-defined, repeatable **action integral** (∫i² dt) and is highly corrosion-resistant.
- *Why sub-microsecond?* — If the heating is slow, the wire simply melts and dribbles; the explosive would be heated but not shocked — yielding a low-order or no detonation.
- *Why shock initiation, not thermal?* — Shock initiation is **far faster and far less sensitive to environmental temperature** than thermal initiation. This is what makes EBWs safe to handle and store.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Terms | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in the firing capacitor | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $R = \rho \, \dfrac{L}{A}$ | Bridge wire resistance (R = ρL/A) | Ω |
| 3 | $P(t) = I^2(t) \, R(t)$ | Instantaneous ohmic dissipation | W |
| 4 | $I(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t / RC}$ | Capacitor discharge current (RL≈0 limit) | A |
| 5 | $A_i = \displaystyle\int_0^{t_b} i^2(t) \, dt$ | **Action Integral** — the explosion criterion | A²·s |
| 6 | $v_{burst} = \dfrac{dV}{dt} \propto \dfrac{I}{\rho_{wire} \, A}$ | Vaporization threshold rate | m/s |
| 7 | $t_b = \dfrac{L_{wire} \, \rho_{mat} \, c_p \, \Delta T}{I^2 R}$ | Approximate burst time | s |
| 8 | $P_{shock} \approx \rho_0 \, c_0 \, u_p$ | Shock pressure (Hugoniot relation, simple) | Pa |
| 9 | $V_{all-fire} = V_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, AFE}{C}}$ | Voltage needed for all-fire | V |
| 10 | $SF = \dfrac{AFE}{NFE}$ | Safety Factor (must be > 1) | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EBW detonators are indispensable wherever **precision, safety, and reliability** matter more than cost:

- **Nuclear weapons** — first stage of arming/firing chain.
- **Spacecraft stage separation** — NASA uses EBW/EFI systems in solid rocket separation bolts.
- **Military ordnance** — air-to-air missile warhead initiation.
- **Geophysical exploration** — shaped-charge well perforators.
- **High-precision demolition** — where millisecond timing of multiple charges is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *Safety Factor* (SF) is the most quoted reliability parameter in KTU valuation. Always state: A typical SF for EBW systems is **≥ 1.5 to 2.0**, meaning the all-fire energy is at least 50–100% greater than the no-fire energy — vastly superior to conventional electric detonators (SF ≈ 1.1).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in the Firing Capacitor

The energy stored in a capacitor charged to voltage $V$ with capacitance $C$ is derived by integrating instantaneous power from zero charge to full charge.

**Step 1 — Define charge on capacitor at any instant:**

$$q(t) = C \, v(t)$$

**Step 2 — The small incremental energy added when voltage changes by $dv$:**

$$dE = v \, dq = v \, (C \, dv) = C \, v \, dv$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to $V_0$ (final voltage):**

$$E = \int_0^{V_0} C \, v \, dv = C \left[ \dfrac{v^2}{2} \right]_0^{V_0} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2}$$

**Numerical sanity check (typical EBW values):** For $C = 2 \, \mu\text{F}$ and $V_0 = 2000 \, \text{V}$,

$$E = 0.5 \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2000)^2 = 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{6} = 4.0 \text{ J}$$

This 4 J is the **total electrical energy** delivered to the bridge wire.

---
