## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discharge Current Through the Bridge Wire

For a charged capacitor discharging through a (nearly) pure resistance R (the bridge wire, with circuit inductance L very small), apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$V_C(t) = I(t) \, R$$

Since $I = -C \, \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$ (current leaving the capacitor is the negative rate of charge change),

$$V_C = -RC \, \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to standard exponential form:**

$$\dfrac{dV_C}{V_C} = -\dfrac{dt}{RC}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides:**

$$\ln V_C - \ln V_0 = -\dfrac{t}{RC}$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate:**

$$V_C(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 4 — Use Ohm's law to get the current:**

$$I(t) = \dfrac{V_C(t)}{R} = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t/RC}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{I(t) = I_0 \, e^{-t/RC}, \quad \text{where} \quad I_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{R}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Action Integral (Bridge-Wire Explosion Criterion)

The **action integral** $A_i$ is the energy criterion that determines whether the bridge wire will explode. It is empirically and theoretically defined as:

$$A_i = \int_0^{t_b} i^2(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the discharge current from Derivation 2:**

$$A_i = \int_0^{t_b} \left( \dfrac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t/RC} \right)^2 dt = \dfrac{V_0^2}{R^2} \int_0^{t_b} e^{-2t/RC} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_0^{t_b} e^{-2t/RC} \, dt = \left[ -\dfrac{RC}{2} \, e^{-2t/RC} \right]_0^{t_b} = \dfrac{RC}{2} \left( 1 - e^{-2t_b/RC} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$A_i = \dfrac{V_0^2 \, C}{2R} \left( 1 - e^{-2t_b/RC} \right)$$

**Step 4 — In the limit where the burst time is small compared to the time constant ($t_b \ll RC$, the capacitor barely discharges), $e^{-2t_b/RC} \approx 1$:**

**Final boxed result (for a fast burst):**

$$\boxed{A_i \approx \dfrac{V_0^2 \, C}{2R} = \dfrac{E}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} \quad \text{(no, more precisely: } A_i = \dfrac{E}{R}\text{)}}$$

Wait — let us cleanly re-state:

Since $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2$, then $V_0^2 C = 2E$, so:

$$A_i = \dfrac{2E}{2R}\left(1 - e^{-2t_b/RC}\right) = \dfrac{E}{R}\left(1 - e^{-2t_b/RC}\right)$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{A_i = \dfrac{E}{R}\left(1 - e^{-2t_b/RC}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical values used in KTU board problems:**
> - For **gold** bridge wire: NFE action integral ≈ **0.05 A²·s**, AFE ≈ **0.20 A²·s**
> - For **platinum**: slightly higher thresholds.
> - The bridge explodes if $A_i \geq A_i^{critical}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — All-Fire Voltage from the All-Fire Energy

Given an all-fire energy $E_{AF}$ and a known capacitance C, the all-fire charging voltage is obtained by inverting the energy equation.

**Step 1 — Start with the energy formula:**

$$E_{AF} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_{AF}^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_{AF}$:**

$$V_{AF}^2 = \dfrac{2 E_{AF}}{C}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{V_{AF} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, E_{AF}}{C}}}$$

**Numerical check:** For $E_{AF} = 0.20$ J and $C = 2 \, \mu\text{F} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ F,

$$V_{AF} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.20}{2 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{5}} = \sqrt{200000} \approx 447 \text{ V}$$

(In practice, real EBW systems are charged to **1000–3000 V** because circuit and contact losses reduce delivered energy.)

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Problem Style)

**Problem:** An EBW detonator uses a gold bridge wire of resistance **0.5 Ω** connected to a **2 µF** capacitor charged to **1500 V**. Compute:
1. The stored energy.
2. The peak discharge current.
3. The action integral, assuming burst occurs in 1 µs (use the fast-burst approximation).

**Solution:**

**(a) Stored Energy:**

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} (2 \times 10^{-6}) (1500)^2 = 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.25 \times 10^{6} = 2.25 \text{ J}$$

**(b) Peak Current (assume ideal discharge, R dominates):**

$$I_0 = \dfrac{V}{R} = \dfrac{1500}{0.5} = 3000 \text{ A}$$

**(c) Action Integral (fast burst, $t_b \ll RC$):**

$$RC = 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}, \quad t_b = 1 \, \mu\text{s} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

Since $t_b = RC$, $e^{-2t_b/RC} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$.

$$A_i = \dfrac{V_0^2 C}{2R}\left(1 - e^{-2}\right) = \dfrac{(1500)^2 (2 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 0.5} (1 - 0.1353)$$

$$A_i = \dfrac{2.25 \times 10^{6} \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}{1} (0.8647) = 4.5 \times 0.8647 \approx 3.89 \text{ A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$

Since $3.89 \text{ A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s} \gg 0.20 \text{ A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$ (AFE for gold), the wire will definitely explode — the design is well above all-fire.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EBW vs. Conventional Electric Detonator

| Parameter | Conventional (Hot-Wire) Detonator | EBW Detonator |
|---|---|---|
| Initiation Mode | Thermal (deflagration) | Shock (plasma-driven) |
| Bridge wire material | Nichrome / Constantan | Gold or Platinum |
| Bridge wire diameter | ~0.05 mm | ~0.04 mm |
| Energy required | 10–50 mJ | 200–500 mJ |
| Rise time of firing pulse | ~milliseconds | ~microseconds |
| Safety Factor (AFE/NFE) | ~1.1 | ~1.5–2.0 |
| Sensitivity to RF / static | High | Very low |
| Storage life | 5–10 yrs | 15–25 yrs |
| Cost | Low | High (10–50×) |
| Use case | Civil blasting, mining | Military, nuclear, space |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — EBW Detonator Construction (Cross-Sectional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    capA[High Voltage DC Supply 1000 to 3000 V] --> capB[Low Inductance Capacitor 1 to 10 microFarad]
    capB --> swA[Trigger Switch Trigatron or Krytron]
    swA --> wireA[Bridge Wire Gold 0.04 mm Diameter]
    wireA --> plasmaA[Explosive Vaporization Plasma 10 to the power 4 K]
    plasmaA --> donorA[Donor Charge PETN 1.6 g per cm cube]
    donorA --> acceptA[Acceptor Charge RDX or HMX]
    acceptA --> outA[Detonation Output 7000 to 9000 m per s]

    swA -.Control Pulse Low Energy.-> ctrlA[Firing Control Unit]
    capA -.Charges.-> capB
    wireA -.Action Integral 0.05 A2s.-> plasmaA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Sequential Operational Phases of an EBW Firing Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Phase 1 Capacitor Charging DC Supply to Capacitor] --> s2[Phase 2 Trigger Firing Command to Switch]
    s2 --> s3[Phase 3 Rapid Discharge Current through Bridge Wire]
    s3 --> s4[Phase 4 Resistive Heating Sub Microsecond]
    s4 --> s5[Phase 5 Explosive Vaporization Plasma Formation]
    s5 --> s6[Phase 6 Shock Wave into Donor Charge]
    s6 --> s7[Phase 7 Donor Detonates Acceptor]
    s7 --> s8[Phase 8 High Order Detonation Output]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Firing Circuit Architecture (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    psuA[HV Power Supply 0 to 3000 V DC] --> capC[Storage Capacitor C]
    capC --> trigA[Trigger Switch Krytron or Thyratron]
    trigA --> ebwA[EBW Detonator Bridge Wire plus Explosive Train]
    ebwA -.Burst Feedback.-> monA[Monitor and Safety Interlock]
    psuA --> monA
    trigA --> monA
    capC --> monA

    ctrlB[Logic Controller Microcontroller or FPGA] --> trigA
    ctrlB --> psuA
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparative Tree — Hot-Wire vs. EBW Detonator Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Electric Detonators] --> bA[Conventional Hot Wire]
    rootA --> bB[EBW Exploding Bridge Wire]
    rootA --> bC[EFI Exploding Foil Initiator]

    bA --> cA[Initiation by Thermal Deflagration]
    bA --> cB[Low Energy 10 to 50 mJ]

    bB --> dA[Initiation by Shock Wave]
    bB --> dB[High Energy 200 to 500 mJ]
    bB --> dC[Gold or Platinum Bridge Wire]

    bC --> eA[Initiation by Flyer Plate]
    bC --> eB[No Bridge Wire Uses Foil]
    bC --> eC[Ultra Precise Sub Microsecond]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Students:** In the cross-sectional architecture above, observe the *order* of the components in the explosive train: **Bridge wire → Plasma burst → Donor charge → Acceptor charge → Output.** This is the canonical *explosive train* structure you must reproduce in KTU descriptive answers for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]
> **Define an Exploding Bridge Wire (EBW) detonator. How does it differ from a conventional hot-wire electric detonator in terms of initiation mechanism?**

**Model Answer:**
An EBW detonator is an electro-explosive device in which a thin gold or platinum bridge wire is **explosively vaporized** by a high-current capacitive discharge to generate a shock wave that directly initiates a high-density secondary explosive.
**Key difference:** A conventional detonator initiates by **thermal heating (deflagration)** of a primary explosive, whereas an EBW initiates by **shock from plasma vaporization** of the wire, with no primary explosive and no thermal stage. This makes EBWs far safer (higher safety factor) and immune to most forms of accidental thermal or RF initiation. *(3 marks)*

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]
> **What is the *action integral* in the context of EBW detonators? State its typical value for a gold bridge wire.**

**Model Answer:**
The action integral $A_i = \int_0^{t_b} i^2(t) \, dt$ is the **electrical energy criterion** that determines whether a bridge wire will explode rather than merely melt. It is the time-integral of the squared current delivered to the wire, measured in **A²·s**.
For a **gold** bridge wire, the typical no-fire action integral (NFE) is approximately **0.05 A²·s**, and the all-fire action integral (AFE) is approximately **0.20 A²·s**. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question 3A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2 Pattern]
> **(a)** Describe, with the help of a neat sketch, the **construction and working** of an Exploding Bridge Wire (EBW) detonator. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the **energy stored in the firing capacitor** and the **peak discharge current** through the bridge wire of an EBW system. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — 3A(a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**
An EBW detonator consists of the following components stacked axially inside a sealed metallic housing:

1. **Electrical leads / pins** — two insulated pins for the high-voltage input.
2. **Bridge wire** — a short length (~1 mm) of **gold** wire, ~0.04 mm diameter, spot-welded between the pin tips.
3. **Donor charge** — a pellet of **PETN** (pentaerythritol tetranitrate) pressed against the bridge wire at a density of **~1.6 g/cm³**. This is the *first* explosive to detonate.
4. **Acceptor charge** — a pellet of **RDX** or **HMX** (cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine / cyclotetramethylenetetranitramine) at higher density (~1.7 g/cm³), placed above the donor.
5. **Output end** — sealed with a thin metal foil that ruptures upon detonation of the acceptor, producing the high-order detonation output (~8000 m/s).
6. **Sealed metal shell** — provides mechanical strength, water resistance, and electrostatic shielding.

**Working (3 marks):**
A high-voltage DC supply charges a low-inductance capacitor to a preset voltage (typically 1000–3000 V). Upon receiving a firing command, a fast electronic switch (krytron or thyratron) closes the circuit, dumping the capacitor's energy through the bridge wire in about **1–2 microseconds**. The current density is enormous (~10⁶ A/mm²), so the wire does **not melt** — it **vaporizes explosively** into a high-temperature (~10⁴ K), high-pressure (~10⁹ Pa) plasma. The plasma expansion drives a strong shock wave into the PETN donor pellet, which **detonates** (not deflagrates) and transfers the detonation to the RDX/HMX acceptor, producing a high-order detonation output. The total time from trigger to output detonation is on the order of **5–10 µs**.

### Model Solution — 3A(b) — Energy and Current Derivations (7 marks)

**Energy stored in the capacitor (3.5 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.1 above. The full derivation is reproduced here:

$$E = \int_0^{V_0} C v \, dv \implies \boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2}$$

**Peak discharge current (3.5 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.2. With the bridge wire treated as a pure resistance R, the discharge current decays exponentially:

$$\boxed{I(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \, e^{-t/RC}}$$

The peak current occurs at $t = 0$:

$$\boxed{I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0}{R}}$$

**Numerical:** If $V_0 = 2000$ V and $R = 0.5$ Ω, then $I_{peak} = 4000$ A.

---

> **Question 3B.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2 Pattern — Alternative Choice]
> **(a)** Explain the concept of the **action integral** for an EBW bridge wire. Show that for a fast-burst event ($t_b \ll RC$), the action integral is given by $A_i = E / R$. (7 marks)
> **(b)** An EBW detonator has a gold bridge wire of resistance **0.8 Ω** and is fired by a **5 µF** capacitor charged to **2500 V**. Compute the stored energy, the peak current, and verify whether the action integral exceeds the all-fire threshold of **0.20 A²·s** for gold. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — 3B(a) — Action Integral Derivation (7 marks)

**Conceptual Introduction (2 marks):**
The action integral $A_i = \int_0^{t_b} i^2(t) \, dt$ is the most reliable **bridge-wire explosion criterion**. It depends on the *rate* of energy delivery, not just the total energy. Two pulses of the same total energy can produce very different results if delivered at different rates.

**Derivation (5 marks):** From Section 3.3:

$$A_i = \int_0^{t_b} \left( \dfrac{V_0}{R} \right)^2 e^{-2t/RC} \, dt = \dfrac{V_0^2}{R^2} \cdot \dfrac{RC}{2}\left(1 - e^{-2t_b/RC}\right) = \dfrac{V_0^2 \, C}{2R}\left(1 - e^{-2t_b/RC}\right)$$

Using $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2$, so $V_0^2 C = 2E$:

$$A_i = \dfrac{2E}{2R}\left(1 - e^{-2t_b/RC}\right) = \dfrac{E}{R}\left(1 - e^{-2t_b/RC}\right)$$

**Fast-burst limit:** When $t_b \ll RC$, the exponential $e^{-2t_b/RC} \to 0$, and so:

$$\boxed{A_i \approx \dfrac{E}{R}}$$

> This compact relation is the **KTU-board-favorite** for quick numerical work.

### Model Solution — 3B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $R = 0.8$ Ω, $C = 5 \, \mu\text{F} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ F, $V_0 = 2500$ V.

**Step 1 — Stored Energy (2 marks):**

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2500)^2 = 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.25 \times 10^{6} = 15.625 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Peak Current (2 marks):**

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0}{R} = \dfrac{2500}{0.8} = 3125 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Action Integral (2 marks):**

Time constant $\tau = RC = 0.8 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ s = 4 µs.

For gold wire, the burst time $t_b \approx 1 \, \mu\text{s} \ll \tau = 4 \, \mu\text{s}$, so the fast-burst approximation is valid:

$$A_i = \dfrac{E}{R} = \dfrac{15.625}{0.8} = 19.53 \text{ A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (1 mark):**

$$A_i = 19.53 \text{ A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s} \gg 0.20 \text{ A}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s (AFE for gold)}$$

**Conclusion:** The action integral is **nearly 100× the all-fire threshold**. The system will fire reliably with a very large safety margin. The design is conservatively safe for the *no-fire* criterion (since $A_i \gg 0.05$ A²·s NFE, no spurious energy source could cause firing — meaning the user must use the genuine firing set; an accidental 110 V mains or 9 V battery cannot possibly deliver this much energy).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *melt* with *explode*.** Many students describe the wire as "melting" and the explosive being "heated". This is **wrong** and costs full conceptual marks. The wire **explodes/vaporizes** and the explosive is **shocked**, not heated.
> 2. **Forgetting the explosive train.** Marks are deducted when students describe only the bridge wire and skip the donor → acceptor transfer.
> 3. **Units in action integral.** Always quote the action integral in **A²·s**, not A² or J. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **No-fire vs. All-fire confusion.** NFE (≈ 0.05 A²·s) is the *maximum* energy that **will not** fire the detonator; AFE (≈ 0.20 A²·s) is the *minimum* energy that **will always** fire it. Reversing these is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the exponential decay derivation.** Many students write the energy formula but not the current-time relation. The current decay $I(t) = (V_0/R) e^{-t/RC}$ is a **mandatory KTU board expectation** for full marks.
> 6. **Neglecting units in numericals.** Forgetting to convert µF to F in $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ leads to a 1000× error — a classic 14-mark-zero pitfall.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EBW = Shock Initiation**, not thermal. The wire **explodes** into plasma, generating a shock wave that detonates the donor charge.
- **Bridge wire material = Gold** (sometimes Pt) for predictable action integral.
- **Bridge wire diameter ≈ 0.04 mm** (≈ 38–40 µm).
- **Action Integral** $A_i = \int i^2 dt$ — explosion criterion, in **A²·s**.
- **NFE ≈ 0.05 A²·s**, **AFE ≈ 0.20 A²·s** for gold.
- **Energy stored in firing capacitor:** $\boxed{E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2}$ (J)
- **Peak discharge current:** $\boxed{I_0 = V_0 / R}$ (A)
- **Discharge current decay:** $\boxed{I(t) = I_0 \, e^{-t/RC}}$
- **Action integral in fast-burst limit:** $\boxed{A_i \approx E / R}$
- **All-fire voltage formula:** $\boxed{V_{AF} = \sqrt{2 E_{AF} / C}}$
- **Safety Factor (SF) = AFE / NFE** ≥ 1.5 to 2.0 — far better than conventional detonators (~1.1).
- **Explosive train order:** Bridge wire → Plasma → **PETN donor** (1.6 g/cm³) → **RDX/HMX acceptor** (1.7 g/cm³) → Output.
- **Applications:** Nuclear weapons, spacecraft stage separation, military ordnance, precision demolition.
- **Burst time:** **~1–2 µs** — sub-microsecond rise time is essential for shock rather than thermal initiation.
- **EBW vs. EFI:** EBW uses a wire; the modern **Exploding Foil Initiator (EFI)** uses a thin metallic foil that bursts to drive a *flyer plate* across a gap, producing an even more powerful and precise shock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question on EBW detonators, structure your answer in **three clear blocks** — (i) **Construction** with components list, (ii) **Working** as a numbered sequence, (iii) **Mathematical analysis** with the relevant formula(s) and, if numerical, a fully converted SI calculation. This is the exact pattern followed in the KTU model answer key and yields full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

# Module 2 — Slapper Detonators (Exploding Foil Initiators)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Slapper Detonator**, technically classified as an **Exploding Foil Initiator (EFI)** or **Flying Plate Detonator (FPD)**, is an *electrically-initiated, shock-wave-generating* detonator in which a thin metallic foil is rapidly vaporized by a high-current pulse, accelerating a dielectric flyer ("slapper") across a precisely machined barrel to impact and shock-initiate a secondary explosive charge. It contains **no sensitive primary explosive**, making it intrinsically safe against electrostatic discharge (ESD), radio-frequency (RF) interference, and accidental mechanical stimuli.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightning-Powered Hammer"

Imagine you have a microscopic hammer housed inside a tiny cylindrical barrel. A thin, fragile plastic film acts as the "hammer head" floating just above a microscopic bridge of aluminum foil. The moment an enormous burst of electrical current (think of a tightly controlled lightning bolt) is forced through the foil, the foil **instantly explodes into a puff of superheated plasma**. The expanding plasma acts like an invisible piston that **catapults the plastic film down the barrel at speeds of 2 to 6 kilometers per second** — roughly 5 to 15 times the speed of sound. This high-velocity plastic projectile slams into a bed of explosive powder, creating a shock wave so intense that the explosive detonates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Insight (Board-Exam Favorite)**
> The slapper detonator represents a paradigm shift: it converts **electrical energy → plasma expansion → kinetic energy of flyer → shock initiation of explosive**. There is **no primary explosive** in the system; initiation is purely a *shock physics* phenomenon, not a thermal/chemical one.

### 1.2 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally accepted in EFI design:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Charging Voltage (V₀) | **1.0 – 4.5** | kV |
| Capacitance (C) | **0.1 – 8.0** | µF |
| Stored Energy (E = ½CV²) | **0.05 – 50** | J |
| Peak Current (I_peak) | **2 – 50** | kA |
| Foil Thickness | **4 – 25** | µm |
| Flyer Material | **Kapton / Parylene / Polyimide** | — |
| Flyer Thickness | **10 – 200** | µm |
| Flyer Mass | **0.05 – 5** | mg |
| Barrel Diameter | **0.2 – 1.5** | mm |
| Barrel Length | **0.1 – 0.5** | mm |
| Flyer Velocity | **2 – 6** | km/s |
| Booster Explosive | **HNS, PETN, CL-20** | — |
| Function Time | **< 1** | µs |
| Jitter | **< 50** | ns |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why this matters**
> Slapper detonators are the *only* practical detonator technology capable of functioning reliably after exposure to extreme environments (nuclear weapons, missile warheads, space re-entry) where conventional primary explosives would be destroyed or become insensitive.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture and Component Hierarchy

A slapper detonator is a layered, coaxial assembly. The five functional subsystems are:

1. **Header Assembly** — Carries the two electrical pins, provides hermetic sealing, and acts as the dielectric insulator between pins.
2. **Foil Bridge (Burst Element)** — A narrow constriction (typically 0.4 mm × 0.4 mm) of thin metal foil (copper or aluminum) connecting the two pins.
3. **Flyer (Slapper) Film** — A small disc of dielectric polymer (most commonly Kapton® polyimide) bonded directly over the foil bridge.
4. **Barrel** — A precisely machined cylindrical hole in a rigid insulating substrate (ceramic, glass-filled plastic) directly above the flyer.
5. **Explosive Powder Column** — A small pellet of high-density, shock-sensitive secondary explosive (HNS-II is the industry standard) pressed into the barrel at its far end.

> [!NOTE]
> The electrical energy is never in direct contact with the explosive. The flyer is the *only* physical medium that transmits energy from the electrical circuit to the explosive charge. This is the foundation of EFI safety.

## 2.2 Operational Sequence — The Five-Stage Process

### Stage 1 — Capacitor Charging
A high-voltage power supply charges a low-inductance energy-storage capacitor (typically a film or ceramic type) to the operating voltage V₀. An *EXPLOSIVE SAFETY SWITCH* (a triggered spark gap, an SCR, or a krytron) isolates the capacitor from the bridge until commanded.

### Stage 2 — Triggered Discharge
Upon receiving a fire command, the switch closes, connecting the capacitor to the bridge foil through a low-inductance transmission path. A critically-damped or underdamped L-C-R pulse is delivered.

### Stage 3 — Foil Burst (Electrical Explosion)
The current density J through the foil bridge reaches values of **10⁸ – 10⁹ A/cm²** within tens of nanoseconds. The foil undergoes:
- **Ohmic heating** (rapid temperature rise)
- **Melting** (loss of mechanical integrity)
- **Vaporization** (phase transition to gas)
- **Ionization** (formation of high-pressure, high-temperature plasma)

This entire transformation is called the **"burst"** and occurs in **< 1 µs**.

### Stage 4 — Flyer Acceleration
The expanding plasma exerts enormous pressure on the dielectric film. The film shears free from its backing and is accelerated down the barrel. The flyer reaches terminal velocity in **< 0.5 µs**.

### Stage 5 — Shock Initiation of Booster
The flyer impacts the explosive pellet at **2 – 6 km/s**. The impact creates a **shock pressure of several GPa** at the impact face. The shock wave propagates through the pellet, compressing voids and heating the explosive along grain boundaries until **hot-spot ignition** occurs, leading to **detonation** within nanoseconds.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *complete* set of equations you will need for any KTU numerical on slapper detonators. Memorize the boundary conditions.

| Symbol | Equation / Definition | Physical Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| E_C | $E_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2$ | Energy stored in the firing capacitor | Joules (J) |
| I(t) | $I(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{\omega L} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega t)$ | Underdamped discharge current (time-domain) | Amperes (A) |
| $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ | Damping coefficient of the firing circuit | s⁻¹ |
| $\omega$ | $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{LC} - \alpha^2}$ | Natural angular frequency of the RLC circuit | rad/s |
| I_peak | $I_{peak} \approx V_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ | Peak discharge current (critically/under-damped) | Amperes (A) |
| g | $g = \displaystyle\int_0^{t_b} J^2(t)\, dt$ | Specific action integral driving the burst | A²s·m⁻⁴ |
| g_c | $g \ge g_c$ (material constant) | Burst criterion (foil vaporization threshold) | A²s·m⁻⁴ |
| E_vap | $E_{vap} = m \left( c_s \Delta T + L_f + L_v \right)$ | Total energy to vaporize a foil mass m | Joules (J) |
| v_f | $v_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \eta E_C}{m_f}}$ | Flyer velocity from capacitor energy (with efficiency $\eta$) | m/s |
| P_shock | $P \approx \rho_f v_f u_p$ | Shock pressure from flyer impact (Hugoniot) | Pa (GPa) |
| u_p | $u_p = \dfrac{v_f}{2}$ (approx., symmetric impact) | Particle velocity behind shock front | m/s |
| $\rho_f$ | Material density of flyer | Density of Kapton ≈ **1420 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| m_f | $m_f = \rho_f \cdot A_{barrel} \cdot t_f$ | Mass of the accelerated flyer disc | kg |
| t_b | $t_b \approx \dfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{LC}$ (under-damped) | Approximate burst time | s |

> [!TIP]
> **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ when writing absolute values in your answer sheet.** Example: $\vert v_f \vert$ is *not* the same as `|v_f|` in raw text. This prevents table syntax breakage during PDF conversion.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Slapper detonators are deployed in environments where *failure is not an option* and where *conventional detonators are unusable*:

- **Nuclear Weapons Stockpile** — The only detonator qualified for thermonuclear primaries and secondaries.
- **Strategic Missile Re-entry Vehicles** — Survives 100 g acceleration, 300 °C re-entry heating, and 30+ years of dormant storage.
- **Crew Escape Systems** — Space Launch System (SLS) and Apollo/Legacy launch escape towers.
- **Pyro-actuated Aircraft Systems** — Canopy severance, fire suppression bottles, fuel-cutoff actuators.
- **Demolition Sequencers** — Multi-point initiation of large structure demolitions with sub-microsecond timing precision.
- **Oil/Gas Perforating Guns** — Shaped-charge initiation downhole at extreme temperatures and pressures.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> The derivations below are the **full, unabridged, step-by-step KTU board-style analytical solutions**. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. **No skipped steps. No abbreviations.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flyer Velocity from Capacitor Discharge (Work-Energy Method)

**Statement:** A slapper detonator is fired using a 2.5 µF capacitor charged to 3.0 kV. The flyer disc is a Kapton cylinder of diameter 0.8 mm and thickness 50 µm. The energy transfer efficiency from capacitor to flyer kinetic energy is 8 %. Determine the flyer velocity.

**Given:**
- $C = 2.5 \, \mu F = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \, F$
- $V_0 = 3.0 \, kV = 3.0 \times 10^3 \, V$
- $D_{barrel} = 0.8 \, mm \Rightarrow A = \pi (0.4 \times 10^{-3})^2 \, m^2$
- $t_f = 50 \, \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- $\rho_{Kapton} = 1420 \, kg/m^3$
- $\eta = 0.08$ (8 %)

**Step 1 — Capacitor stored energy:**

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2$$

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} \times (2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.0 \times 10^3)^2$$

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.0 \times 10^6$$

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} \times 22.5$$

$$E_C = 11.25 \, J$$

This is the **total stored electrical energy** available to drive the system.

**Step 2 — Flyer mass calculation:**

The flyer is a cylindrical disc of diameter equal to the barrel diameter and thickness equal to the film thickness.

$$A_{barrel} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.8 \times 10^{-3})^2$$

$$A_{barrel} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$A_{barrel} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2$$

The flyer volume is:

$$V_f = A_{barrel} \times t_f = (5.0265 \times 10^{-7}) \times (50 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$V_f = 2.5133 \times 10^{-11} \, m^3$$

The flyer mass is:

$$m_f = \rho_{Kapton} \times V_f = 1420 \times 2.5133 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$m_f = 3.569 \times 10^{-8} \, kg = 35.69 \, ng$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy delivered to flyer:**

$$E_k = \eta \times E_C = 0.08 \times 11.25$$

$$E_k = 0.9 \, J$$

**Step 4 — Equate kinetic energy to work expression:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_f v_f^2$$

Solve for v_f:

$$v_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_k}{m_f}}$$

$$v_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.9}{3.569 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

$$v_f = \sqrt{\frac{1.8}{3.569 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

$$v_f = \sqrt{5.0434 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{v_f \approx 7102 \, m/s \approx 7.10 \, km/s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This flyer velocity is at the *high end* of the operational range (typical 2–6 km/s). The result confirms that the chosen parameters will reliably initiate HNS-II. If the velocity were below ~2 km/s, the flyer would *thump* rather than shock-initiate the explosive, and the detonator would fail.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Burst Time from RLC Circuit Dynamics

**Statement:** A slapper detonator's firing circuit has $C = 0.5 \, \mu F$, $L = 50 \, nH$, and $R = 0.05 \, \Omega$. Determine the damping regime, the natural frequency, and the approximate burst time of an aluminum foil bridge.

**Given:**
- $C = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \, F$
- $L = 50 \times 10^{-9} \, H$
- $R = 0.05 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Check the damping regime:**

For a series RLC, the criterion is the comparison between $R^2$ and $4L/C$.

$$\frac{4L}{C} = \frac{4 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-7}}{5.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.4$$

$$R^2 = (0.05)^2 = 0.0025$$

Since $R^2 = 0.0025 \ll 0.4 = 4L/C$, the circuit is **strongly underdamped** (oscillatory discharge).

**Step 2 — Compute damping coefficient:**

$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{0.05}{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\alpha = 5.0 \times 10^{5} \, s^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute natural angular frequency:**

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{1}{LC} - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{(50 \times 10^{-9})(0.5 \times 10^{-6})} - (5.0 \times 10^5)^2}$$

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-14}} - 2.5 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\omega = \sqrt{4.0 \times 10^{13} - 2.5 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\omega = \sqrt{3.975 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\omega \approx 6.304 \times 10^{6} \, rad/s$$

**Step 4 — Approximate burst time (first half-cycle of current):**

$$t_b \approx \frac{\pi}{2\omega} = \frac{\pi}{2 \times 6.304 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$t_b \approx 2.49 \times 10^{-7} \, s = 249 \, ns$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The foil must vaporize within this 249 ns window. For aluminum with $g_c \approx 1.4 \times 10^9 \, A^2 s / m^4$, the current density must build up rapidly during this interval. Any impedance mismatch (e.g., poor solder joints, parasitic resistance) will lengthen $t_b$ and may cause the foil to fail in the *solid-state* regime (no vaporization, no flyer).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Slapper vs. Conventional Detonator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important comparison table for KTU Part-B questions. Memorize the qualitative direction of every row.

| Performance Parameter | Conventional (Bridgewire + Primary) | Slapper (EFI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensitive Primary Explosive Required | **Yes** (Lead Azide / Lead Styphnate) | **No** — only secondary explosive (HNS) |
| ESD Sensitivity | High (failure possible at $\sim$ 0.1 mJ) | Extremely Low (requires kV + kA pulse) |
| RF Susceptibility | Moderate to High | Negligible (no primary to ignite) |
| Function Time | 1 – 100 µs | **< 1 µs** (sub-microsecond) |
| Jitter | 1 – 10 µs | **< 50 ns** |
| Shelf Life (Vacuum Sealed) | 10 – 20 years | **25 – 30+ years** |
| Temperature Tolerance | -55 °C to + 200 °C | **-55 °C to + 300 °C** |
| Radiation Hardness | Poor (primary explosive decomposes) | **Excellent** (no radiation-sensitive chemistry) |
| Cost per Unit | Low | **High** (precision-machined assembly) |
| Fabrication Complexity | Low | Very High (cleanroom, photolithography) |
| Mass and Volume | Larger | **Smaller** (miniaturizable to chip-scale) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain in brief why a slapper detonator does not contain any primary explosive, and list two safety advantages that result from this design choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A slapper detonator achieves initiation purely through the **shock wave generated by hypervelocity flyer impact** on a secondary explosive (HNS-II). Since the initiation mechanism is *mechanical shock* and not thermal/chemical decomposition of a sensitive species, no primary explosive (e.g., lead azide) is required. Two resulting safety advantages are:
1. **Extreme ESD/RF immunity** — there is no low-decomposition-temperature species to ignite from stray electromagnetic energy.
2. **Long shelf life and environmental tolerance** — secondary explosives like HNS do not degrade significantly over decades, and there is no desensitization from primary-explosive migration or recrystallization. **(3 marks — 1 for explanation, 1 each for two advantages)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the "specific action integral" of a burst foil and state the typical critical value for aluminum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The specific action integral *g* is the time-integrated square of the current density flowing through the foil, given by:
$$g = \int_0^{t_b} J^2(t)\, dt$$
The foil **bursts (vaporizes)** when *g* reaches or exceeds a critical value *g_c*, which is a material property. For aluminum, $g_c \approx 1.4 \times 10^9 \, A^2 s/m^4$. For copper, $g_c \approx 2.4 \times 10^9 \, A^2 s/m^4$. **(3 marks — 1 for definition with units, 1 for the criterion, 1 for the numerical value with units)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Construction (7 Marks)
**With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a slapper detonator (Exploding Foil Initiator). Clearly identify the five functional subsystems and explain the energy transformation at each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

A Slapper Detonator (EFI) is an electrically fired detonator that initiates a secondary explosive by the impact of a high-velocity dielectric flyer. The five subsystems are:

1. **Header / Pins** — Two electrical pins carry the firing pulse; they are isolated by a glass-ceramic insulator.
2. **Foil Bridge** — A 4–25 µm thick copper or aluminum bridge connects the two pins.
3. **Flyer (Slapper) Film** — A 25–200 µm Kapton polyimide disc bonded over the bridge.
4. **Barrel and Booster** — A precisely machined insulating barrel (0.2–1.5 mm diameter, 0.1–0.5 mm long) terminates in a pellet of HNS-II.
5. **Firing Circuit** — High-voltage capacitor (0.1–8 µF), charging supply, and triggered safety switch.

**Energy transformations (stage-wise):**
- **Stage 1:** Electrical energy stored in capacitor: $E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2$ (typically 0.1–50 J).
- **Stage 2:** Capacitor discharges through bridge. Current density reaches $10^8 - 10^9 \, A/cm^2$ in < 1 µs. The foil undergoes ohmic heating → melting → vaporization → ionization, forming a high-pressure plasma.
- **Stage 3:** Plasma expansion accelerates the Kapton flyer down the barrel. Flyer velocity = 2–6 km/s. Only ~5–15 % of the capacitor energy is converted to flyer kinetic energy.
- **Stage 4:** Flyer impacts HNS-II pellet. The shock pressure (GPa-range) compresses voids in the explosive bed, creating **hot spots** > 700 K that grow into full detonation within nanoseconds.
- **Stage 5:** Detonation wave propagates into the main output charge (RDX/HMX). Function time < 1 µs; jitter < 50 ns.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram/listing, 3 for stage explanation, 2 for numerical/typical values)*

#### Part (b) — Energy Calculation (7 Marks)
**A 1.5 µF capacitor is charged to 3.5 kV and discharged through a 75 µm diameter copper bridge into a Kapton flyer of 60 µm thickness. The barrel diameter is 0.75 mm and the energy transfer efficiency is 10 %. Calculate: (i) stored energy, (ii) flyer mass, (iii) flyer velocity, and (iv) the kinetic energy delivered to the flyer. (Given: $\rho_{Kapton} = 1420 \, kg/m^3$)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stored Energy:**

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times (3.5 \times 10^3)^2$$

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.225 \times 10^7$$

$$\boxed{E_C = 9.1875 \, J}$$

**(ii) Flyer Mass:**

The flyer diameter = barrel diameter = 0.75 mm.

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2$$

$$V_f = A \times t_f = 4.418 \times 10^{-7} \times 60 \times 10^{-6} = 2.651 \times 10^{-11} \, m^3$$

$$m_f = \rho_{Kapton} \times V_f = 1420 \times 2.651 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$\boxed{m_f = 3.764 \times 10^{-8} \, kg = 37.64 \, ng}$$

**(iii) Flyer Velocity:**

$$v_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \eta E_C}{m_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.10 \times 9.1875}{3.764 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

$$v_f = \sqrt{\frac{1.8375}{3.764 \times 10^{-8}}} = \sqrt{4.882 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{v_f \approx 6987 \, m/s \approx 6.99 \, km/s}$$

**(iv) Kinetic Energy Delivered:**

$$E_k = \eta \times E_C = 0.10 \times 9.1875$$

$$\boxed{E_k = 0.919 \, J}$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 for (i), 2 for (ii), 2 for (iii), 1.5 for (iv). Unit consistency carries 0.5 mark each step.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Comparison with Conventional Detonator (7 Marks)
**Compare and contrast the slapper detonator with a conventional bridgewire detonator in terms of: (i) initiation mechanism, (ii) safety against ESD, (iii) function time, (iv) shelf life, (v) radiation tolerance, (vi) jitter, and (vii) cost. Tabulate your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Bridgewire + Primary (Conventional) | Slapper (EFI) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) | Initiation Mechanism | Hot bridgewire → ignites primary explosive → deflagration → detonation | Electrical burst → flyer kinetic energy → shock initiation of secondary |
| (ii) | ESD Safety | Poor — fires at < 1 mJ | Excellent — requires kV + kA |
| (iii) | Function Time | 1 – 100 µs | < 1 µs |
| (iv) | Shelf Life | 10 – 20 years | 25 – 30+ years |
| (v) | Radiation Tolerance | Poor — primary decomposes | Excellent — no sensitive chemistry |
| (vi) | Jitter | 1 – 10 µs | < 50 ns |
| (vii) | Cost | Low (mass produced) | High (precision assembly) |

**Conclusion:** The slapper detonator dominates every performance metric except cost, justifying its use in mission-critical applications such as nuclear weapons, missiles, and crew-escape systems. *(7 marks: 1 mark per row)*

#### Part (b) — Materials Selection Rationale (7 Marks)
**Explain the rationale for selecting (i) Kapton as the flyer material, (ii) HNS-II as the booster explosive, and (iii) copper or aluminum as the bridge foil. Discuss the alternatives where applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Kapton (Polyimide) Flyer:** Kapton is chosen because it has (a) **high tensile strength** to survive the explosive launch, (b) **high density** (1420 kg/m³) for momentum delivery, (c) **excellent dielectric strength** to insulate the explosive from the electrical circuit, (d) **thermal stability** up to 400 °C, and (e) **uniform mechanical properties** when produced as thin films. Alternatives: Parylene, Mylar. Parylene has a smoother surface finish, while Mylar is cheaper but less thermally stable.

**(ii) HNS-II Booster Explosive:** HNS-II (Hexanitrostilbene, Type II) is selected because (a) it is a **secondary explosive** (insensitive to friction/impact/ESD), (b) it has a **low shock initiation threshold** (~2 GPa), making it ideal for flyer impact, (c) it has **exceptional thermal stability** up to 320 °C, and (d) it has a **long vacuum-shelf life** (> 30 years). Alternatives: PETN (more sensitive), CL-20 (higher performance but newer/less proven), TATB (overly insensitive for this application).

**(iii) Copper/Aluminum Bridge Foil:** Both metals offer (a) **low resistivity** for rapid ohmic heating, (b) **low vaporization energy** for fast burst, and (c) **compatibility with photolithographic patterning**. **Aluminum** is preferred in micro-scale EFIs because $g_c$ is lower ($1.4 \times 10^9$ vs $2.4 \times 10^9$ for Cu), meaning it bursts with less energy. **Copper** is preferred in legacy designs because of better solderability to the pins. Alternatives: Gold (too expensive), Silver (excellent but expensive), Tantalum (high $g_c$).

*(7 marks: 2 + 2.5 + 2.5 distributed as above.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Slapper Detonator questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the initiation mechanism** — Many students write that the *foil* directly initiates the explosive. The correct statement is: the *foil* vaporizes, the *plasma* accelerates the *flyer*, and the *flyer* impacts the explosive. Energy crosses **three distinct physical domains** before reaching the explosive. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting the specific action integral** — When asked "why does the foil burst," the answer is *not* "because of high current" but "because $\int J^2 dt \ge g_c$." **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Mixing up materials** — Students often confuse HNS (booster) with HMX/RDX (main charge) or with PETN. Memorize: **HNS-II = booster**, **HMX/RDX = output/main charge**, **Kapton = flyer**, **Cu/Al = bridge**. **(–1 mark each error)**
> 4. **Unit errors in $\eta E_C / m_f$** — The unit of $v_f$ is m/s, *not* km/s. Convert at the end. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Drawing a poor diagram** — Always include the *five* functional zones in a vertical cross-section: pins → bridge → flyer → barrel → explosive. **(–1 mark)**
> 6. **Omitting jitter** — A common KTU question asks for "sub-microsecond performance." The answer is *function time < 1 µs AND jitter < 50 ns*. The distinction matters. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- Slapper Detonator = Exploding Foil Initiator (EFI) = Flying Plate Detonator (FPD). All three terms are synonymous in KTU syllabus.
- **No primary explosive** is used. The booster is always a **secondary explosive** (typically HNS-II).
- The **five functional subsystems** are: Header, Foil Bridge, Flyer Film, Barrel, Booster Pellet. Memorize this exact order.
- The **five-stage operational sequence** is: Charge → Discharge → Foil Burst → Flyer Acceleration → Shock Initiation.
- **Stored energy formula:** $E_C = \frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$. Typical range 0.1 – 50 J.
- **Flyer velocity formula:** $v_f = \sqrt{2 \eta E_C / m_f}$. Typical range 2 – 6 km/s.
- **Burst criterion:** $g = \int_0^{t_b} J^2 dt \ge g_c$. For Al: $g_c \approx 1.4 \times 10^9 \, A^2 s/m^4$.
- **Capacitor peak current:** $I_{peak} \approx V_0 \sqrt{C/L}$. Typical 2 – 50 kA.
- **Function time:** < 1 µs. **Jitter:** < 50 ns. **Shelf life:** 25 – 30+ years.
- **ESD immunity:** Requires kV + kA pulse — no accidental firing from stray electrostatic discharge.
- **Capacitor values:** 0.1 – 8.0 µF. **Charging voltages:** 1.0 – 4.5 kV.
- **Primary applications:** Nuclear weapons, missile re-entry vehicles, crew escape systems, shaped-charge oil-well perforators.
- **Key advantage:** Survives extreme thermal, mechanical, and radiation environments where conventional detonators fail.
- **Key disadvantage:** High cost and complex fabrication (cleanroom + photolithography required).
- **Diagrams to practice:** Cross-sectional block diagram showing all 5 zones; energy transformation flowchart; firing circuit schematic.
- **Common exam trap:** Students forget to include the *flyer* in their explanation and jump directly from "foil vaporizes" to "explosive detonates." Always include the flyer as the *third stage* — the kinetic-energy carrier between the plasma and the explosive.
- **The "M" in $\eta E_C$ formula** stands for *flyer mass*, not capacitor mass, not foil mass. Calculate $m_f = \rho_{Kapton} \times A_{barrel} \times t_f$.

> **End of Module 2 — Slapper Detonators Notes (PESFT753, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling in Design and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scaling in Design and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Scaling in Design and Analysis** is a fundamental engineering methodology in explosives technology that allows the prediction of blast, fragmentation, ground shock, and thermal effects produced by one explosive charge (typically a prototype or full-scale detonation) using data obtained from a smaller charge (the model). It relies on the principle of **geometric similarity** and **dimensional analysis** to relate physical effects across different charge masses or geometries while preserving the underlying physical phenomenon.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PESFT753, Module 3), scaling is treated as the mathematical bridge that connects laboratory/model blast tests with real-world full-scale explosive operations such as mining blasts, demolition operations, and military applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Scaling is not just a mathematical convenience — it is the *legal and operational basis* for determining safety distances, blast zones, and structural vulnerability charts used by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, **Directorate General of Mines Safety (DGMS)**, and **Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO)**.

## 1.2 Key Scaling Laws Used in Explosives Engineering

| Law | Functional Form | Primary Application |
|-----|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Hopkinson's Cube Root Law** | $Z = R / W^{1/3}$ | Air blast, ground vibration, cratering |
| **Square Root Law** | $Z = R / W^{1/2}$ | Underwater explosion bubble pulsations |
| **Sachs Scaling** | $Z = R / W^{1/3}$ with impulse-based adjustment | Air blast impulse, fragment distribution |
| **Geometric (Dimensional) Scaling** | $R_2 / R_1 = (W_2 / W_1)^{1/3}$ | All effects for similar explosive geometries |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honeycomb Analogy"

Imagine you are baking a single honeycomb-shaped cake in your kitchen. The recipe is for a small cake, but you need to feed 100 people. Instead of inventing a new recipe, you **scale up** the ingredients proportionally — double the diameter, multiply flour by 8, eggs by 4, sugar by 8, and so on. The cake looks, feels, and tastes the same.

In explosives engineering, scaling works on the *same logical principle*, except instead of flour and eggs, we are scaling **energy release, shock wave geometry, and impulse transfer**. The constant that must remain invariant across scales is called the **scaled distance (Z)** — measured in $\text{m}/\text{kg}^{1/3}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $\mathbf{g = 9.81\ m/s^2}$
> - Density of air (sea level): $\mathbf{\rho_{air} = 1.225\ kg/m^3}$
> - Speed of sound in air: $\mathbf{c = 343\ m/s}$
> - TNT equivalent factor for most commercial explosives: $\mathbf{F \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.0}$ (TNT-equivalent basis)

## 1.4 Why Scaling Is Indispensable

1. **Cost Reduction** — Conducting full-scale explosive tests is prohibitively expensive. Models are cheaper.
2. **Safety** — Modeling prevents injuries from premature or accidental detonations during trial tests.
3. **Predictive Capability** — Predicts effects of charges that have *not yet been detonated*.
4. **Legal Compliance** — Required by statutory bodies for quarry permissions, demolition permits, and ammunition storage.
5. **Design Iteration** — Allows designers to refine charges, geometries, and initiation sequences without repeated field trials.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Basis — Dimensional Homogeneity

A physical phenomenon can be meaningfully scaled only when the governing equations are **dimensionally homogeneous**. A phenomenon (e.g., air blast overpressure) is considered *scalable* when the same functional relationship between dimensionless groups holds at model and prototype scales.

For air blast, the relevant dimensional groups are:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{P}{\rho \cdot c^2}, \quad \Pi_2 = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

The requirement that $\Pi_1$ and $\Pi_2$ be conserved at both scales is what gives rise to Hopkinson's Law.

## 2.2 Hopkinson's Cube Root Law (1885)

This is the **most important scaling relation** in explosives engineering. Sir Bertram Hopkinson (1885) experimentally demonstrated that two charges of the same explosive, geometrically similar but of different mass, produce the **same overpressure** at distances proportional to the cube root of their masses.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

Where:
- $Z$ = **Scaled Distance** in $\text{m/kg}^{1/3}$
- $R$ = Actual distance from the charge in metres (m)
- $W$ = Mass of the explosive charge in kilograms (kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Principle:** *Two geometrically similar charges of the same explosive will produce identical blast effects at distances where the scaled distance $Z$ is identical.*

**Derivation Logic Step-by-Step:**

1. Start with the energy released by the charge: $E = W \cdot Q$, where $Q$ is the heat of detonation (J/kg).
2. The overpressure $P$ at distance $R$ is generated by a shock wave carrying this energy through a hemispherical surface of area $2\pi R^2$.
3. By dimensional analysis, $P$ must be a function of energy density: $P \propto \sqrt{E/R^3}$
4. Substituting $E = WQ$: $P \propto \sqrt{WQ/R^3} = \sqrt{Q} \cdot (W)^{1/2} / R^{3/2}$
5. Reorganising: $P$ depends on $W/R^3$, which yields the cube root form $Z = R/W^{1/3}$.

## 2.3 Sachs Scaling (Impulse-Based Extension)

Where Hopkinson scaling addresses peak overpressure, **Sachs Scaling** is preferred for **impulse** (the time-integrated pressure). Sachs showed that the impulse scaling maintains similarity in the *positive phase duration* of the blast wave.

**Sachs Scaled Distance:**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{E^{1/3}}$$

Where $E = W \cdot Q$ is the total released energy. For TNT, $Q_{TNT} \approx 4.52 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}$.

For convenience, this is often written as:

$$Z_{Sachs} = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}} \quad \text{(TNT-equivalent form)}$$

## 2.4 Square Root Scaling (Underwater Explosions)

For underwater explosions, the bubble pulsation energy scales differently because of the **incompressibility of water**. The scaling takes the form:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/2}}$$

This is observed in deep-water conditions where the bubble radius $R_b$ scales as:

$$R_b \propto W^{1/3} \quad \text{(maximum bubble radius)}$$

but the bubble period $T_b$ follows:

$$T_b \propto W^{1/3} \quad \text{(for surface-proximate charges)}$$

## 2.5 Geometric Scaling for Charge Dimensions

If two charges of the same explosive are geometrically similar, then:

$$\frac{L_2}{L_1} = \left(\frac{W_2}{W_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

Where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are any linear dimension (length, diameter, radius) of the model and prototype charges.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ | Hopkinson Scaled Distance | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |
| 2 | $R_2 = R_1 \cdot (W_2/W_1)^{1/3}$ | Distance Equivalence Law | m |
| 3 | $W_2 = W_1 \cdot (R_2/R_1)^3$ | Charge Equivalence Law | kg |
| 4 | $Z_{Sachs} = R/W^{1/3}$ | Sachs Scaled Distance (TNT-eq.) | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |
| 5 | $Z_{sw} = R/W^{1/2}$ | Square Root Scaled Distance | m/kg$^{1/2}$ |
| 6 | $L_2/L_1 = (W_2/W_1)^{1/3}$ | Geometric Similarity Ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_s = f(Z)$ | Peak Side-on Overpressure | kPa |
| 8 | $i_s = f(Z)$ | Specific Impulse | kPa·ms |
| 9 | $P_s / P_a = \phi(Z)$ | Dimensionless Overpressure | - |
| 10 | $v_v = f(Z)$ | Peak Particle Velocity (ground) | mm/s |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quarry & Mining Safety:** Determining blast clearance zones before excavation blasts.
- **Structural Engineering:** Predicting damage to nearby buildings during demolition.
- **Defence Engineering:** Estimating weapon effects for stockpile planning.
- **Pyrotechnics Display Design:** Determining spectator safe distances.
- **Tunnelling & Underground Excavation:** Predicting ground vibration impacts on adjacent structures.
- **Ammunition Storage:** BIS mandated compliance uses Hopkinson scaling for igloo-to-igloo separation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hopkinson's Cube Root Law

### Step 1 — Begin with Energy Released by the Detonation

When an explosive charge of mass $W$ detonates, it releases total energy:

$$E = W \cdot Q$$

Where $Q$ is the specific heat of detonation (J/kg). For TNT, $Q \approx 4.52 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}$.

### Step 2 — Geometric Spreading of Energy

Assuming the blast wave propagates as a hemispherical shock front (typical for surface or near-surface bursts), the energy density at radius $R$ is:

$$e_{density} = \frac{E}{2\pi R^2}$$

### Step 3 — Dimensional Analysis for Overpressure

By Buckingham's $\Pi$ Theorem, the overpressure $P$ must be expressible as a function of the dimensional variables:

$$P = f(E,\ \rho_{air},\ R)$$

where $\rho_{air}$ is the density of air.

The only dimensionally consistent form is:

$$P = K \cdot \rho_{air}^{a} \cdot E^{b} \cdot R^{c}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Dimensional Homogeneity

Equating exponents of M, L, T:

- $\Pi_M$: $0 = a + b$
- $\Pi_L$: $-1 = -3a + 3b - c$  
- $\Pi_T$: $-2 = -2a - 2b - 2c$

Solving this system:

$$a = \frac{1}{3}, \quad b = -\frac{1}{3}, \quad c = -1$$

So:

$$P = K \cdot \rho_{air}^{1/3} \cdot E^{-1/3} \cdot R^{-1}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Energy $E = WQ$

$$P = K \cdot \rho_{air}^{1/3} \cdot (WQ)^{-1/3} \cdot R^{-1}$$

Grouping all charge-dependent terms:

$$P = K \cdot \rho_{air}^{1/3} \cdot Q^{-1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{R}{W^{1/3}}\right)^{-1}$$

### Step 6 — Identify the Scaled Distance

Recognising that $\dfrac{R}{W^{1/3}}$ is the **scaled distance $Z$**, we obtain the final scaling form:

$$P = K' \cdot \left(\frac{R}{W^{1/3}}\right)^{-1} = K' \cdot Z^{-1}$$

This is the **inverse-power Hopkinson relation**, confirming that $P$ depends only on $Z$, not on $R$ and $W$ independently.

$$\boxed{\therefore Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}} = \text{constant for identical blast effects}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Functional Behaviour of Scaled Distance

| Scaled Distance Range $Z$ (m/kg$^{1/3}$) | Predicted Phenomenon | Engineering Implication |
|------------------------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| $Z < 0.5$ | Crater formation | Charge-on-ground contact zone |
| $0.5 \le Z < 2.0$ | Severe structural damage | Demolition zone |
| $2.0 \le Z < 5.5$ | Moderate building damage | Mandatory evacuation zone |
| $5.5 \le Z < 15$ | Window breakage, minor injuries | Cordon perimeter |
| $15 \le Z < 40$ | Sound level hazard | Audible range boundary |
| $Z \ge 40$ | Negligible effect | Free movement allowed |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A blasting operation uses **100 kg** of TNT-equivalent charge. Determine the distance at which the same overpressure will be experienced as a **1 kg** test charge produces at **10 m**.

**Given:**
- $W_1 = 1\ \text{kg}$, $R_1 = 10\ \text{m}$
- $W_2 = 100\ \text{kg}$, $R_2 = ?$

**Step 1 — Apply Hopkinson's Equivalence Law:**

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \left(\frac{W_2}{W_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{R_2}{10} = \left(\frac{100}{1}\right)^{1/3} = (100)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate cube root:**

$$(100)^{1/3} = 4.6416$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_2$:**

$$R_2 = 10 \times 4.6416 = 46.42\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with scaled distance:**

$$Z_1 = \frac{10}{(1)^{1/3}} = 10\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{46.42}{(100)^{1/3}} = \frac{46.42}{4.6416} = 10\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}\ \checkmark$$

**Answer:** The same blast effect will be observed at **46.42 m** for the 100 kg charge.

## 3.4 Second Worked Example — Reverse Charge Calculation

**Problem:** A safety officer needs a blast pressure of $P = 50\ \text{kPa}$ at a site located **200 m** from the planned detonation. Test data shows that the same pressure is produced by a **0.5 kg** charge at **5 m**. Find the required charge mass.

**Step 1 — Compute scaled distance of reference test:**

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}} = \frac{5}{(0.5)^{1/3}} = \frac{5}{0.7937} = 6.30\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Solve charge equation:**

$$W_2 = W_1 \cdot \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^3 = 0.5 \cdot \left(\frac{200}{5}\right)^3 = 0.5 \cdot (40)^3$$

$$W_2 = 0.5 \cdot 64000 = 32000\ \text{kg} = 32\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check via scaled distance:**

$$Z = \frac{200}{(32000)^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{31.75} = 6.30\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}\ \checkmark$$

**Answer:** A **32-tonne** charge is required to achieve the desired pressure at 200 m. *(In practice, this would be far above safe limits and rejected — illustrating why safety distances scale as $W^{1/3}$.)*

## 3.5 Derivation Summary — The Three Cardinal Scaling Equations

$$\text{(I) Scaled Distance Definition:} \quad Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

$$\text{(II) Distance Equivalence:} \quad R_2 = R_1 \left(\frac{W_2}{W_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$\text{(III) Charge Equivalence:} \quad W_2 = W_1 \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{3}$$

These three equations form the **analytical backbone** of every KTU question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Scaling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Blast Phenomenon] --> B{Choose Scaling Law}
    B -->|Air Blast| C[Hopkinson Cube Root]
    B -->|Underwater| D[Square Root Law]
    B -->|Impulse| E[Sachs Scaling]
    C --> F[Compute Scaled Distance Z]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Reference Data Available?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Apply Equivalence Equations]
    G -->|No| I[Use Empirical Charts - TM5 1300]
    H --> J[Predict Peak Overpressure P]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Validate Against Limits?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Finalise Design]
    K -->|No| M[Iterate Charge or Distance]
    M --> F
    L --> N[Generate Safety Document]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Scaling Methodology Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Block]
        A1[Charge Mass W1]
        A2[Reference Distance R1]
        A3[Explosive Type Q]
    end
    subgraph SCALING[Scaling Engine]
        B1[Hopkinson Law]
        B2[Geometric Similarity]
        B3[Equivalence Mapper]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Block]
        C1[Required Charge W2]
        C2[Equivalent Distance R2]
        C3[Safety Boundary]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Geometric Similarity Across Scales

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Small Charge W1] -->|Geometric Similarity Factor s| B[Large Charge W2]
    B -->|Cube Root Relation| C[s equals W2/W1 to power 1/3]
    C --> D[Linear dimensions scale by s]
    D --> E[Surface area scales by s squared]
    E --> F[Volume scales by s cubed]
    F --> G[Mass scales by s cubed]
    G --> H[Energy scales by s cubed]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Scaling Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B{Medium?}
    B -->|Air| C[Use Hopkinson Z = R over W cube root]
    B -->|Water - Deep| D[Use Square Root Z = R over W square root]
    B -->|Soil or Rock| E[Use Hopkinson with ground correction]
    B -->|Composite| F[Use Sachs with TNT equivalence]
    C --> G[End]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Hopkinson's Cube Root Law. Explain its significance in determining safety distances for explosive storage.**

**Model Answer:**

Hopkinson's Cube Root Law states that two geometrically similar charges of the same explosive produce identical blast effects (overpressure, impulse) at distances where the scaled distance $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ is the same.

Mathematically:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}\ \ \text{(m/kg}^{1/3}\text{)}$$

**Significance in Safety Distance Determination:**
1. Enables prediction of blast effects of large charges from small-scale test data.
2. Forms the statutory basis for igloo-to-igloo separation distances in ammunition depots (BIS: IS 4544).
3. Allows authorities to define evacuation perimeters before blasting operations.
4. Reduces trial-and-error testing, saving cost and preventing casualties.
5. Provides a *quantitative legal benchmark* for prosecution under explosives regulations.

**(3 marks — full marks expected.)**

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Hopkinson Scaling and Sachs Scaling. State two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hopkinson Scaling | Sachs Scaling |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| Primary variable | Peak overpressure $P$ | Blast impulse $i$ |
| Functional form | $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ | $Z = R/E^{1/3}$ |
| Reference framework | Charge mass $W$ | Total energy $E = WQ$ |
| Better for | Pressure-sensitive targets | Impulse-sensitive targets |

**Applications of Hopkinson Scaling:**
- Air blast overpressure prediction.
- Crater volume estimation.
- Ground vibration modelling in mining.

**Applications of Sachs Scaling:**
- Fragment velocity prediction.
- Building damage by total impulse.
- Wall and cladding response analysis.

**(3 marks — comparative table expected.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive Hopkinson's Cube Root Law starting from dimensional analysis. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quarry operator proposes to blast **500 kg** of ANFO (TNT-equivalent factor 0.85) at a quarry. From previous trials, it was found that **2 kg** of ANFO produced a peak overpressure of **$P = 25\ \text{kPa}$** at a distance of **15 m**. Estimate:
   1. The scaled distance $Z$.
   2. The distance at which the same overpressure of 25 kPa will occur for the proposed 500 kg blast. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
The overpressure $P$ depends on charge energy $E$, ambient air density $\rho_{air}$, and distance $R$.

$$P = f(E,\ \rho_{air},\ R)$$

**Step 2 — Form the dimensionless group via Buckingham Pi:**
Each variable has dimensions:
- $[P] = \text{M L}^{-1}\text{T}^{-2}$
- $[E] = \text{M L}^{2}\text{T}^{-2}$
- $[\rho] = \text{M L}^{-3}$
- $[R] = \text{L}$

Number of variables: 4; Number of fundamental dimensions: 3.
Therefore number of $\Pi$ groups: $4 - 3 = 1$.

**Step 3 — Choose repeating variables** $E$ and $R$, then form:

$$\Pi = \frac{P \cdot R^3}{E}$$

By the theorem, this dimensionless group is constant. Substituting $E = WQ$:

$$\Pi = \frac{P \cdot R^3}{W \cdot Q} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for P:**

$$P = \text{constant} \cdot \frac{W \cdot Q}{R^3}$$

Reorganising:

$$P \cdot R^3 / W = \text{constant} \cdot Q$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{R}{W^{1/3}} = Z = \text{constant for similar effects}$$

**Step 5 — State Assumptions:**
1. Same explosive type (identical $Q$).
2. Geometric similarity between charges.
3. Same atmospheric conditions (no wind, same altitude).
4. Hemispherical surface burst geometry.
5. Energy propagates without significant dissipation other than geometric spreading.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Part 1 — Compute scaled distance Z:**

Given: $W_1 = 2\ \text{kg}$, $R_1 = 15\ \text{m}$.

$$Z = \frac{R_1}{W_1^{1/3}} = \frac{15}{(2)^{1/3}} = \frac{15}{1.2599} = 11.91\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Part 2 — Apply Hopkinson equivalence for $W_2 = 500\ \text{kg}$:**

Since overpressure is identical, the scaled distance must also be identical:

$$Z = 11.91\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

So:

$$R_2 = Z \cdot W_2^{1/3} = 11.91 \cdot (500)^{1/3}$$

$$R_2 = 11.91 \cdot 7.937 = 94.53\ \text{m}$$

**Verification:**

$$Z_2 = \frac{94.53}{(500)^{1/3}} = \frac{94.53}{7.937} = 11.91\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}\ \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $Z = 11.91\ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$
2. $R_2 \approx 94.5\ \text{m}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take cube root of charge mass** — Writing $Z = 15/2 = 7.5$ instead of $15/1.26 = 11.91$.
> 2. **Squaring instead of cubing** in charge equivalence — Using $W_2 = W_1 (R_2/R_1)^2$ is the most common error.
> 3. **Omitting units** of $Z$ — Examiners expect $\text{m/kg}^{1/3}$ explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring TNT-equivalent factor** when given — The factor 0.85 affects $W_{TNT-eq}$, not raw $W$. For Hopkinson, scaling uses the actual charge mass scaled by the equivalence factor.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of geometric similarity in scaling. How does it lead to the cube root scaling rule for mass and linear dimensions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A demolition charge of **10 kg** placed on the ground produces a crater volume of **0.2 m³**. Using cube root scaling, calculate:
   1. The crater volume expected for a **640 kg** charge.
   2. The TNT-equivalent charge mass required to produce a peak overpressure of **$P = 100\ \text{kPa}$** at **50 m**, given that this pressure is achieved by **1 kg** at **$R = 4.5\ \text{m}$**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Concept of Geometric Similarity:**
Two explosive charges are *geometrically similar* if their shapes are identical and all corresponding linear dimensions are in the same ratio $s$. That is:

$$\frac{L_2}{L_1} = s$$

**Implications for Area and Volume:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = s^2, \quad \frac{V_2}{V_1} = s^3$$

**Mass Relation:**

$$\frac{W_2}{W_1} = \frac{\rho V_2}{\rho V_1} = s^3$$

Therefore:

$$s = \left(\frac{W_2}{W_1}\right)^{1/3} = \frac{L_2}{L_1}$$

**Connection to Cube Root Scaling:**
Since any linear dimension scales as $s$, distances also scale as $s$. Combining:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = s = \left(\frac{W_2}{W_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

This is the **cube root scaling rule** — it is a direct consequence of geometric similarity of charges and three-dimensional energy spreading.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Part 1 — Crater Volume:**

Crater volume scales with mass to the power 1 (since both mass and volume are three-dimensional). For geometrically similar charges:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{W_2}{W_1}$$

Therefore:

$$V_2 = 0.2 \times \frac{640}{10} = 0.2 \times 64 = 12.8\ \text{m}^3$$

**Part 2 — Charge Equivalence:**

Given: $W_1 = 1\ \text{kg}$ produces 100 kPa at $R_1 = 4.5\ \text{m}$.

Required: $W_2$ to produce same 100 kPa at $R_2 = 50\ \text{m}$.

Since overpressure is identical, scaled distance must be identical:

$$Z_1 = Z_2$$

$$\frac{R_1}{W_1^{1/3}} = \frac{R_2}{W_2^{1/3}}$$

Rearranging:

$$W_2^{1/3} = W_1^{1/3} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

$$W_2^{1/3} = (1)^{1/3} \cdot \frac{50}{4.5} = 11.111$$

$$W_2 = (11.111)^3 = 1371.7\ \text{kg} \approx 1.37\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Final Answers:**
1. Crater volume for 640 kg charge: $\mathbf{12.8\ m^3}$
2. Required TNT-equivalent charge: $\mathbf{\approx 1372\ kg}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing crater scaling exponent** — Volume scales *linearly* with mass (for geometric similarity), NOT with $W^{1/3}$. Students wrongly apply cube root scaling to crater volume.
> 2. **Mixing $W_1^{1/3}$ with $W_1$** in cubing operations — A classic algebraic slip.
> 3. **Rounding errors** — $(11.111)^3 = 1371.7$, not 1370 or 1400. Show intermediate steps.
> 4. **No verification step** — Always recheck by computing $Z$ for both charges; both should equal the same value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Must Remember for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Hopkinson's Cube Root Law:** $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ — *the single most important equation*.
- **Sachs Scaling:** Same form but energy-based ($E$ instead of $W$); used for impulse calculations.
- **Square Root Law:** $Z = R/W^{1/2}$ — *only for underwater / deep-water* scenarios.
- **Geometric Similarity Rule:** All linear dimensions scale as $W^{1/3}$.
- **Three Cardinal Equations:**
  - $R_2 = R_1 (W_2/W_1)^{1/3}$
  - $W_2 = W_1 (R_2/R_1)^3$
  - $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ (invariant for identical effects)
- **TNT-equivalent factor:** Multiply explosive mass by factor to get equivalent TNT mass when using Hopkinson empirically derived charts.
- **Crater volume** scales linearly with charge mass (NOT cube root).
- **Peak overpressure** is a function only of scaled distance $Z$.
- **Standard Scaled Distance Bands** (must memorise): 0.5, 2.0, 5.5, 15, 40 m/kg$^{1/3}$.
- **Atmospheric constants** to remember: $\rho_{air} = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_{air} = 343\ \text{m/s}$.
- **Application domains:** Mining, demolition, ammunition storage, military, tunnelling, quarrying.
- **Always verify** by computing $Z$ for both model and prototype — they must match.
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting cube root, or confusing crater scaling (linear) with overpressure scaling ($W^{1/3}$).
- **Legal relevance:** BIS, DGMS, and DRDO all use Hopkinson-derived charts for safety distance norms.
- **Worked answer format expected in exam:** Always show substitution → algebraic manipulation → numerical evaluation → verification with $Z$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geometric Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Explosives Technology and Rock Blasting**, **Geometric Similarity** is defined as the principle that two blast geometries (prototype and model, or original and scaled design) are geometrically similar when every corresponding linear dimension — such as **burden (B)**, **spacing (S)**, **hole diameter (D)**, **bench height (H)**, **stemming length (T)**, **subgrade drilling (J)**, and **charge length (L)** — maintains a constant, dimensionless ratio of proportionality.

Mathematically, if two blast patterns are geometrically similar with a linear scale factor **n**, then for every linear dimension *L₁* in Pattern 1, the corresponding dimension *L₂* in Pattern 2 satisfies:

$$\frac{L_1}{L_2} = n = \text{constant}$$

All linear dimensions, areas, and volumes scale according to:

$$\text{Linear Dimension} \propto n, \quad \text{Area} \propto n^2, \quad \text{Volume} \propto n^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PESFT753, Module 3):** Geometric similarity is a foundational pre-requisite for deriving the *cube-root scaling law* (Hopkinson's Law) and the *square-root scaling law* used in blast vibration prediction, crater blasting, and permissible explosives testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two systems are geometrically similar only if (a) they have the same shape, (b) all corresponding linear dimensions are proportional by a single constant, and (c) all corresponding angles are identical.** Material properties (density, VOD, rock strength) and explosive properties (detonation pressure, energy density) are NOT part of geometric similarity — they belong to *kinematic* and *dynamic similarity*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a photograph of a blast pattern printed at the size of a postage stamp. You now blow up the same photograph to the size of a cinema screen. Every line in the small picture is enlarged by the *same* factor — the burden line, spacing line, stemming length, hole diameter — everything grows uniformly. **That is geometric similarity.**

Now think of a toy car and a real car. If the toy car were a *perfect* scaled replica, then the wheel diameter, chassis length, and window height would all be 1/40th of the original, and the body shape would be identical. In blasting, we apply the same idea: if a 50 mm hole pattern worked at a small quarry, a 250 mm pattern at a large open-pit mine would be its geometrically similar counterpart at a scale factor of **n = 5**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Geometric similarity allows blasting engineers to (1) transfer proven blast designs from one mine to another, (2) extrapolate small-scale test blasts (e.g., crater tests) to full-scale production blasts, and (3) compare experimental blasts conducted at different sites on a like-for-like basis.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Burden | **B** | m | Distance from blast hole to nearest free face |
| Spacing | **S** | m | Distance between adjacent blast holes in same row |
| Hole Diameter | **D** | mm | Diameter of the blast hole |
| Bench Height | **H** | m | Vertical height of the bench being blasted |
| Stemming Length | **T** | m | Length of inert material (stemming) at top of hole |
| Subgrade Drilling | **J** | m | Portion of hole drilled below the bench floor (grade line) |
| Charge Length | **L** | m | Length of explosive column in the hole |
| Linear Scale Factor | **n** | dimensionless | Ratio of any corresponding linear dimension |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for Geometric Similarity

For two blast geometries (a "model" blast and a "prototype" blast) to qualify as geometrically similar, the following conditions MUST be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Shape Identity:** Both patterns must have the same *shape* (e.g., square pattern, staggered pattern, echelon pattern).
2. **Constant Linear Scale:** All linear dimensions must be scaled by a single dimensionless factor *n*. If burden scales by *n*, then spacing, stemming, and hole depth must also scale by exactly the *same n*.
3. **Angle Preservation:** All angles between corresponding features must remain unchanged (e.g., hole inclination angle, burden-to-spacing angle).
4. **Pattern Type Preservation:** A square pattern must remain a square pattern (S/B = 1); a staggered pattern (S/B = √3/2 ≈ 0.87) must remain staggered.

If any one condition is violated, the geometries are NOT geometrically similar, and scale-up using cube-root or square-root laws becomes mathematically invalid.

### 2.2 Derivation of Scaling Laws from Geometric Similarity

#### A. Cube-Root Scaling Law (Hopkinson's Law)

This law governs the scaling of **explosive charge weight** required to produce a similar blast effect (e.g., similar crater volume, similar fragmentation size) when geometry is preserved.

If we keep the explosive charge density (ρₑ) and rock properties constant, then:

- Volume of explosive ∝ (Linear dimension)³ ∝ n³
- Therefore: Weight of charge W ∝ n³

This leads directly to the famous cube-root scaling:

$$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^3$$

where *d* = characteristic linear dimension (e.g., depth of burial, crater radius), *r* = distance from charge.

#### B. Square-Root Scaling Law (for Ground Vibration)

Peak Particle Velocity (PPV) at a given distance from a blast follows:

$$PPV = K \left(\frac{\sqrt{W}}{R}\right)^n$$

where *W* is the maximum charge per delay (kg) and *R* is the distance (m). The square-root dependence emerges from empirical vibration propagation studies (USBM RI 8507 by David E. Siskind et al., 1980).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever you see a problem asking "what charge weight produces a similar blast at 2× the depth?" — the answer is *always 8× the charge*, because of cube-root scaling. Memorize this.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formatting note:** All absolute value or conditional notations use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{L_1}{L_2} = n$ | L = linear dimension, n = scale factor | Basic similarity condition |
| 2 | $A \propto n^2$ | A = area (m²) | Area scaling |
| 3 | $V \propto n^3$ | V = volume (m³) | Volume / charge weight scaling |
| 4 | $\dfrac{W_1}{W_2} = \left(\dfrac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^3$ | W = charge weight, d = linear dim | Hopkinson's cube-root law |
| 5 | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$ | R = distance, W = charge weight | Scaled distance for similar effect |
| 6 | $PPV = K\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{W}}{R}\right)^{\alpha}$ | K, α = site constants | Blast vibration prediction |
| 7 | $S/B = \text{constant}$ | S = spacing, B = burden | Pattern type preservation |
| 8 | $Z = \dfrac{R}{\sqrt{W}}$ | Z = scaled distance | USBM scaled distance formula |
| 9 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \dfrac{B_1}{B_2} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = n$ | All linear dimensions | Full geometric similarity test |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mine-to-Mine Design Transfer:** A blast engineer at Mine A (small holes) can scale a successful design to Mine B (large holes) by maintaining B, S, T, H, J in constant proportion.
- **Permissible Explosives Testing:** Regulatory bodies (DGMS India, MSHA USA) use geometrically similar test geometries to evaluate explosives in galleries of different cross-sections.
- **Crater Blasting Tests:** A small-scale crater test at depth *d* predicts the charge needed at full scale depth *n·d*.
- **Blast Simulation Software:** Codes like JKSimBlast, i-Blast, and WipFrag implicitly assume geometric similarity when scaling fragmentation models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear, Area, and Volume Scaling from Geometric Similarity

**Given:** Two geometrically similar blast patterns with scale factor *n*.

**Step 1 — Definition of scale factor.**
By definition of geometric similarity, for any linear dimension L:

$$\frac{L_{\text{model}}}{L_{\text{prototype}}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Let us denote any linear dimension (say burden B) as:
