$$B_{\text{model}} = \frac{B_{\text{prototype}}}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Area scaling.**
Area is a product of two linear dimensions. For example, the cross-sectional area of the stemming column is:

$$A_{\text{stem}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

Since diameter D also scales by 1/n in the model:

$$A_{\text{stem, model}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(\frac{D}{n}\right)^2 = \frac{A_{\text{stem, prototype}}}{n^2}$$

Therefore, in general:

$$A_{\text{model}} = \frac{A_{\text{prototype}}}{n^2}$$

**Step 3 — Volume scaling.**
Volume is a product of three linear dimensions. For example, the explosive charge volume is:

$$V_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$$

Since both D and L scale by 1/n in the model:

$$V_{\text{exp, model}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left(\frac{D}{n}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{L}{n}\right) = \frac{V_{\text{exp, prototype}}}{n^3}$$

Therefore, in general:

$$V_{\text{model}} = \frac{V_{\text{prototype}}}{n^3}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
Since charge weight W = ρ × V (where ρ = explosive density), and ρ is held constant under geometric similarity:

$$\frac{W_{\text{model}}}{W_{\text{prototype}}} = \frac{\rho \cdot V_{\text{model}}}{\rho \cdot V_{\text{prototype}}} = \frac{V_{\text{model}}}{V_{\text{prototype}}} = \frac{1}{n^3}$$

This is the foundation of cube-root scaling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hopkinson's Cube-Root Law

**Given:** Two geometrically similar blast craters, one produced by charge W₁ at depth d₁, and another by charge W₂ at depth d₂, in the same rock and using the same explosive.

**Step 1 — Assume geometric similarity.**
Therefore, all linear dimensions (including the depth of burial d) scale linearly:

$$\frac{d_1}{d_2} = n$$

**Step 2 — Volume of explosive is the only source of energy.**
Assuming energy density (energy per unit volume of explosive) is constant, the total energy released is proportional to the volume of explosive. Therefore energy scales as n³:

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = n^3$$

**Step 3 — Energy is also proportional to charge weight.**
Since W = ρV and ρ is constant:

$$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = n^3$$

**Step 4 — Substitute n = d₁/d₂:**

$$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^3$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to express scaled distance:**

$$\frac{d_1}{d_2} = \left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$$

Or equivalently, the *scaled depth* d/W^(1/3) is constant for similar blast effects:

$$\frac{d_1}{W_1^{1/3}} = \frac{d_2}{W_2^{1/3}} = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Result:** If you want to double the depth of a crater blast (n = 2), you must multiply the charge weight by 2³ = **8**. This is the single most tested fact in KTU exams on this topic.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A small-scale crater test in limestone used 2.5 kg of explosive at an optimum depth of 1.2 m. A full-scale production blast must be carried out at an optimum depth of 3.6 m in the same limestone using the same explosive. Assuming geometric similarity holds, calculate the required charge weight for the production blast.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the linear scale factor:

$$n = \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{3.6 \text{ m}}{1.2 \text{ m}} = 3$$

Step 2 — Apply Hopkinson's cube-root law:

$$\frac{W_2}{W_1} = n^3 = 3^3 = 27$$

Step 3 — Compute W₂:

$$W_2 = W_1 \times 27 = 2.5 \text{ kg} \times 27 = 67.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer:** The production blast requires **W₂ = 67.5 kg** of explosive.

**Verification by alternate path:**
The scaled depth ratio d/W^(1/3) must remain constant:

$$\frac{d_1}{W_1^{1/3}} = \frac{1.2}{(2.5)^{1/3}} = \frac{1.2}{1.3572} = 0.8841$$

$$\frac{d_2}{W_2^{1/3}} = \frac{3.6}{(67.5)^{1/3}} = \frac{3.6}{4.0617} = 0.8864$$

The slight discrepancy (≈ 0.26%) is due to rounding — confirms the answer.

---

### 3.4 Quick Reference: Scale-Up Cheat Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below uses `\vert` for absolute value notations to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| Scale Factor n | Burden Ratio | Spacing Ratio | Stemming Ratio | Volume Ratio | Charge Weight Ratio |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1.5 | 1.5× | 1.5× | 1.5× | 3.375× | 3.375× |
| 2.0 | 2.0× | 2.0× | 2.0× | 8.000× | 8.000× |
| 3.0 | 3.0× | 3.0× | 3.0× | 27.000× | 27.000× |
| 4.0 | 4.0× | 4.0× | 4.0× | 64.000× | 64.000× |
| 5.0 | 5.0× | 5.0× | 5.0× | 125.000× | 125.000× |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Geometric Similarity Decision Framework

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the decision logic an engineer follows to verify geometric similarity between two blast designs:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Compare Blast Pattern X and Y]) --> check1{Is shape identical?}
    check1 -- No --> failA[NOT Geometrically Similar: Different shape]
    check1 -- Yes --> check2{Are all linear dimensions scaled by a single constant n?}
    check2 -- No --> failB[NOT Geometrically Similar: Multiple scale ratios]
    check2 -- Yes --> check3{Are all corresponding angles preserved?}
    check3 -- No --> failC[NOT Geometrically Similar: Angles distorted]
    check3 -- Yes --> check4{Is pattern type preserved? eg S slash B constant}
    check4 -- No --> failD[NOT Geometrically Similar: Pattern type changed]
    check4 -- Yes --> passA[GEOMETRICALLY SIMILAR]
    passA --> applyLaw{Which scaling law to apply?}
    applyLaw --> cubeLaw[Cube Root Scaling: Charge Weight scales as n cubed]
    applyLaw --> sqrtLaw[Square Root Scaling: Scaled Distance Z equals R over sqrt W]
    cubeLaw --> endA([End: Design Transfer Validated])
    sqrtLaw --> endA
```

### 4.2 Geometric Similarity Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Geometric Parameters]
        iB[B Burden m]
        iS[S Spacing m]
        iD[D Hole Diameter mm]
        iH[H Bench Height m]
        iT[T Stemming m]
        iJ[J Subgrade Drilling m]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Geometric Similarity Engine]
        validator[Dimensionless Ratio Checker]
        scaleComp[Scale Factor n Calculator]
        cubeRoot[Charge Weight Cube Root Predictor]
        sqrtRoot[Vibration Square Root Predictor]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Scaled Design Output]
        oB[B scaled equals n times B]
        oS[S scaled equals n times S]
        oV[V scaled equals n cubed times V]
        oW[W scaled equals n cubed times W]
    end

    iB --> validator
    iS --> validator
    iD --> validator
    iH --> validator
    iT --> validator
    iJ --> validator

    validator --> scaleComp
    scaleComp --> cubeRoot
    scaleComp --> sqrtRoot

    cubeRoot --> oB
    cubeRoot --> oS
    cubeRoot --> oV
    cubeRoot --> oW
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Confirmation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compliance Note:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric (e.g., `startA`, `check1`, `failA`, `iB`, `validator`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. All quoted labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold, italics, or HTML markup inside double-quoted labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**Define geometric similarity as applied to blast design. State the conditions that must be satisfied for two blast patterns to be geometrically similar.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Geometric similarity between two blast patterns means that every corresponding linear dimension — burden, spacing, hole diameter, stemming length, bench height — is in the same constant ratio, and all corresponding angles are identical.

**Conditions (2 marks):**
1. The two patterns must have the **same shape**.
2. A **single constant scale factor n** must relate all corresponding linear dimensions.
3. All corresponding **angles must be preserved**.
4. The **pattern type** (square, staggered, rectangular) must be preserved, meaning ratios like S/B must remain constant.

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**State Hopkinson's cube-root law. A model crater test uses 1.5 kg of explosive at 0.8 m depth. Find the charge required for a geometrically similar blast at 2.4 m depth in the same rock.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Law (1 mark):** Hopkinson's cube-root law states that under geometric similarity, the charge weight W scales as the cube of the linear scale factor:

$$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^3$$

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{2.4}{0.8} = 3$$

$$W_2 = W_1 \times n^3 = 1.5 \times 27 = \mathbf{40.5 \text{ kg}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) Derive Hopkinson's cube-root scaling law from the principles of geometric similarity. Show every algebraic step clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Under geometric similarity, the scale factor between corresponding linear dimensions is constant:

$$\frac{d_1}{d_2} = n$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Energy released by an explosive charge is proportional to its volume V, and volume scales as the cube of linear dimensions:

$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = n^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{E_1}{E_2} = n^3$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Since W = ρV and explosive density ρ is unchanged:

$$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = n^3$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substituting n = d₁/d₂:

$$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^3$$

This is Hopkinson's cube-root law. The "scaled depth" d/W^(1/3) is constant for similar blast effects.

**Final Result (1 mark):** Equivalently:

$$\frac{d_1}{W_1^{1/3}} = \frac{d_2}{W_2^{1/3}}$$

---

**(b) A prototype blast hole has burden 2.5 m, spacing 3.0 m, stemming 2.0 m, and charge weight 25 kg. A geometrically similar model blast is to be designed at 1/5th linear scale using the same explosive. Determine (i) all linear dimensions of the model, (ii) the required charge weight, and (iii) the ratio of bench heights if the prototype bench is 8 m. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Prototype: B₁ = 2.5 m, S₁ = 3.0 m, T₁ = 2.0 m, W₁ = 25 kg, H₁ = 8 m
- Linear scale factor: n = 5 (prototype is 5× the model)

(i) **Linear dimensions of model (3 marks):**

$$B_2 = \frac{B_1}{n} = \frac{2.5}{5} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

$$S_2 = \frac{S_1}{n} = \frac{3.0}{5} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{n} = \frac{2.0}{5} = 0.4 \text{ m}$$

(ii) **Charge weight of model (3 marks):**

$$W_2 = \frac{W_1}{n^3} = \frac{25}{125} = 0.2 \text{ kg}$$

(iii) **Bench height ratio (1 mark):**

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_2 = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6 \text{ m}$$

---

#### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) Explain the difference between geometric similarity and dynamic similarity in blasting. Why is geometric similarity a necessary (but not sufficient) condition for dynamic similarity? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Geometric Similarity (3 marks):** Two blast geometries are geometrically similar when all corresponding linear dimensions are in a constant ratio *n* and all angles are preserved. It concerns ONLY shape and size proportions — burden, spacing, stemming, hole diameter, bench height — but does NOT specify what materials or energy densities are used.

**Dynamic Similarity (2 marks):** Two blast geometries are dynamically similar when, in addition to being geometrically similar, the ratios of all relevant forces (inertial, gravitational, explosive pressure, rock strength) are also preserved. Dynamic similarity requires geometric similarity PLUS preservation of dimensionless groups like the Froude number, Cauchy number, and energy density ratio.

**Geometric is necessary but not sufficient (2 marks):** Without identical shape proportions, force ratios cannot meaningfully be compared because the geometric "scaffold" upon which forces act differs. Hence every dynamically similar system MUST first be geometrically similar. However, two geometrically similar systems can have vastly different rock strengths or explosive energies — making them geometrically similar but NOT dynamically similar.

---

**(b) The scaled distance Z = R/√W is used to predict blast-induced ground vibration PPV using the equation PPV = K·Z^(-α). For a quarry blast with K = 1200, α = 1.6, R = 200 m, and W = 500 kg, calculate (i) the scaled distance, (ii) the predicted PPV, and (iii) the PPV if the same blast is geometrically scaled up by n = 2 (charge weight becomes n³ times). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Scaled distance (2 marks):**

$$Z = \frac{R}{\sqrt{W}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{500}} = \frac{200}{22.361} = 8.944 \text{ m/kg}^{1/2}$$

(ii) **Predicted PPV (2 marks):**

$$PPV = K \cdot Z^{-\alpha} = 1200 \times (8.944)^{-1.6}$$

Compute exponent:
$$(8.944)^{-1.6} = e^{-1.6 \ln(8.944)} = e^{-1.6 \times 2.1914} = e^{-3.506} = 0.03008$$

Therefore:
$$PPV = 1200 \times 0.03008 = 36.1 \text{ mm/s}$$

(iii) **PPV after geometric scale-up by n = 2 (3 marks):**

New charge weight:
$$W' = n^3 \times W = 8 \times 500 = 4000 \text{ kg}$$

New distance (scaled linearly):
$$R' = n \times R = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ m}$$

New scaled distance:
$$Z' = \frac{R'}{\sqrt{W'}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{4000}} = \frac{400}{63.246} = 6.325 \text{ m/kg}^{1/2}$$

New PPV:
$$PPV' = 1200 \times (6.325)^{-1.6}$$

$$(6.325)^{-1.6} = e^{-1.6 \ln(6.325)} = e^{-1.6 \times 1.8452} = e^{-2.9523} = 0.05232$$

$$PPV' = 1200 \times 0.05232 = 62.8 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Interpretation:** Geometric scale-up by n = 2 increases PPV from 36.1 mm/s to 62.8 mm/s — a **74% increase**, illustrating that scaling laws do NOT keep vibration constant; charge weight grows faster than distance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Exam**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cube:** The most common error is computing W₂ = n × W₁ (linear scaling) instead of W₂ = n³ × W₁. This alone costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up distance and charge weight:** Some students incorrectly write d₂ = n × W₁. Remember: distance scales linearly, but charge weight scales as the cube.
> 3. **Ignoring pattern type:** Two patterns with different S/B ratios are NOT geometrically similar, even if individual dimensions scale uniformly. Always check S/B constancy.
> 4. **Forgetting unit consistency:** Hole diameters are usually in mm, but burden/spacing in m. Convert before computing scale factor.
> 5. **Square-root vs Cube-root confusion:** Use cube-root for **charge weight scaling (Hopkinson)**; use square-root for **vibration scaled distance (USBM)**. Don't mix them.
> 6. **Assuming density changes:** Under geometric similarity, ρ (explosive density) and rock properties are assumed constant. If the problem says "different explosive", you must redo the energy analysis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Geometric Similarity**

- **Geometric similarity** = constant linear scale factor *n* + identical shape + preserved angles + preserved pattern type (S/B constant).
- **Linear dimensions scale as n**, **areas as n²**, **volumes as n³**.
- **Charge weight** (volume × density) **scales as n³** because W ∝ V.
- **Hopkinson's cube-root law:** W₁/W₂ = (d₁/d₂)³ — applied to **crater blasting, charge scaling, model-to-prototype transfer**.
- **Scaled depth d/W^(1/3)** is constant for similar blast effects.
- **Square-root scaling** is for **blast vibration** (PPV vs R/√W), NOT for charge weight.
- **To double the depth, octuple the charge.** (2³ = 8)
- **To triple the depth, multiply charge by 27.** (3³ = 27)
- Geometric similarity is a **necessary but not sufficient** condition for dynamic similarity.
- Always verify **S/B constancy**, **shape identity**, and **angle preservation** before applying cube-root scaling.
- USBM vibration formula: PPV = K(R/√W)^(-α) — typically K = 500-1500, α = 1.5-1.8 for Indian rock conditions.
- The scale factor n is **dimensionless** — always cancel units before computing.
- For a model at 1/n scale, **model charge = prototype charge / n³**.
- Geometric similarity is the *theoretical* foundation of permissible explosives gallery testing (Indian Standard IS: 6609, Part I).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accelerating Metal with Explosives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLORIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Accelerating Metal with Explosives # Module 3 — Accelerating Metal with Explosives ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Accelerating metal with explosives** is the controlled application of detonation energy to impart very high velocities (typically **300 m/s to 9,000 m/s**) to a metallic workpiece (plate, tube, conical liner, etc.). The detonation products, expanding at velocities of the order of the detonation velocity, exert an impulsive pressure on the metal surface, converting the chemical energy of the explosive into **kinetic energy of the metal**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gurney's Definition (1943):** The terminal velocity of an accelerated metal plate driven by the detonation of an adjacent explosive charge is governed by the ratio of the charge mass ($C$) to the metal mass ($M$) and the **Gurney specific energy** ($\sqrt{2E}$) of the explosive. The characteristic velocity $\sqrt{2E}$ is the velocity a metal fragment would attain if all the explosive energy were concentrated in it. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of it as the world's most violent "push". Imagine a stretched balloon pressed against a skateboard. When you pop the balloon, the rushing air (here, detonation gases) accelerates the skateboard. Now scale this up: - The "balloon" is the **detonation products** (hot, dense gas at $\sim$3,000–4,000 K and **20–40 GPa** pressure). - The "skateboard" is a **steel plate** of mass $M$. - The velocity of the "air rush" is the **detonation velocity** $D$ (6,000–9,000 m/s for most military explosives). > [!NOTE] > The plate never "feels" the full detonation velocity — it moves at a fraction of $D$ because momentum and energy must be **shared** between the gas and the plate according to Gurney's energy partition. ### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |----------|:------:|:-------------:|:----:| | Detonation velocity | $D$ | 5,000 – 9,500 | m/s | | Detonation (CJ) pressure | $P_{CJ}$ | 15 – 40 | GPa | | Gurney characteristic velocity | $\sqrt{2E}$ | 2.0 – 3.0 | km/s | | Plate terminal velocity | $V$ | 300 – 9,000 | m/s | | Mass ratio (charge/metal) | $C/M$ | 0.05 – 10.0 | — | | Impulse duration | $t_{imp}$ | 1 – 10 | $\mu$s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters at KTU:** "Accelerating metal with explosives" is the **parent concept** for three industrial/military applications that appear repeatedly in PESFT753 — (i) **Explosive Forming**, (ii) **Explosive Welding/Cladding**, and (iii) **Shaped-Charge / EFP Liners**. Mastering Gurney's equation is therefore non-negotiable. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Picture — Step by Step When a detonation wave sweeps across an explosive charge in contact with a metal plate, four sequential events occur: 1. **Shock Loading (0 – 0.5 µs):** The Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) detonation front strikes the metal surface. The CJ pressure $P_{CJ}$ is transmitted as a shock into the metal. 2. **Momentum Transfer (0.5 – 5 µs):** The high-pressure detonation products (a dense, hot plasma-like gas) expand and exert a quasi-steady pressure on the trailing face of the metal. This is the **Taylor wave** region. 3. **Coasting Phase (5 – 50 µs):** Once the Taylor rarefaction overtakes the plate, pressure drops to near-zero. The plate now moves at its **terminal velocity** $V$. 4. **Energy Equipartition:** Total chemical energy $C \cdot E$ (where $E$ is Gurney specific energy in J/kg) partitions between: - Kinetic energy of the **metal**: $\tfrac{1}{2} M V^2$ - Kinetic energy of the **detonation gases** (modelled as a linearly expanding slab). ## 2.2 Key Assumptions of Gurney's Model - Detonation products behave as an **ideal gas** with linear velocity profile. - All chemical energy is converted to **kinetic energy** (no heat losses to radiation, etc.). - Geometry is **1-D planar** (slab), **cylindrical**, or **spherical** with corresponding profile constants. - Metal is treated as a **rigid mass** (no internal deformation) at the moment of velocity assessment. > [!NOTE] > Gurney's equation is an **engineering model** — not a first-principles hydrodynamic simulation. It is extraordinarily accurate (within 5–10 %) for engineering design of flyers, fragments, and EFP liners, which is why it remains the KTU board examiner's favourite question. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Configuration | Gurney Velocity Equation | Notes | |:-:|:--------------|:-------------------------|:------| | 1 | **Open sandwich (asymmetric)** — explosive against a single plate of mass $M$ on a rigid anvil | $V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \dfrac{M}{C} + \dfrac{1}{3} \right)^{-1/2}$ | $\sqrt{2E}$ in m/s; $M,C$ in kg | | 2 | **Symmetric sandwich** — explosive of mass $C$ between two equal plates of total mass $M_{tot}$ | $V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \dfrac{M_{tot}}{2C} + \dfrac{1}{3} \right)^{-1/2}$ | Plates fly apart with equal and opposite $V$ | | 3 | **Cylindrical charge inside cylindrical tube** | $V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \dfrac{M}{C} + \dfrac{1}{2} \right)^{-1/2}$ | Used for fragmentation warheads | | 4 | **Cylindrical charge around cylindrical core (EFP/liner)** | $V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \dfrac{M}{C} + \dfrac{1}{2} \right)^{-1/2}$ | Same form; $M$ = liner mass | | 5 | **Spherical charge in spherical shell** | $V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \dfrac{M}{C} + \dfrac{3}{5} \right)^{-1/2}$ | Used for spherical fragmentation | | 6 | **Gurney characteristic velocity (definition)** | $\sqrt{2E} = D \left( \dfrac{1}{2(\gamma^2 - 1)} \right)^{1/2}$ | Links to detonation theory; $\gamma$ ≈ 3 for typical CHNO explosives | | 7 | **Effective charge height for parallel-plate flyer** | $h_{eff} = \dfrac{C}{\rho_{ex} \cdot A}$ | $A$ = plate area | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Substitutions for the KTU Board Exam:** If a question gives you the detonation velocity $D$ and the polytropic index $\gamma$, use equation (6) to first compute $\sqrt{2E}$, then substitute into (1), (2), (3) or (5) depending on geometry. **Marks are split 50:50** between these two steps. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | Plate Velocity Required | |:------|:------------|:----------------------:| | Aerospace | Explosive forming of large titanium panels | 200 – 500 m/s | | Petrochemical | Explosive welding of bimetallic clad plates (e.g., SS/CS) | 250 – 600 m/s (collision angle critical) | | Defence | EFP (Explosively Formed Penetrator) liners | 1,500 – 2,500 m/s | | Defence | Shaped-charge jet tip velocity | 6,000 – 9,000 m/s | | Mining/Demolition | Linear cutting charges | 2,000 – 4,000 m/s | | Research | Hypervelocity impact (flyer-plate gas-gun alternative) | 1,000 – 8,000 m/s | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of Gurney's Equation (Symmetric Sandwich) > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the **single most-tested analytical question** in PESFT753 Module 3. The KTU board examiner typically awards 7 marks out of 14 for a clean derivation with proper assumptions stated. ## 3.1 Problem Setup Consider a **symmetric sandwich** configuration: - An explosive slab of thickness $h$ (or mass $C$ per unit area $\rho_{ex} h$) - Sandwiched between two identical metal plates, each of mass $M$ per unit area $\rho_{m} t$ (where $t$ is plate thickness) - Detonation is initiated simultaneously along the free face of the charge - The two plates fly apart symmetrically with terminal velocity $V$ ``` M (plate) | C (charge) | M (plate) ↑ ←——— V ——— → D (detonation) | ↑ ——— V ———→ ``` ## 3.2 Assumption — Linear Velocity Profile (Gurney's "Tipped" Profile) In the expanding gas slab of current thickness $L(t) > h$, the velocity of gas particles varies **linearly** from $-V$ at the left plate to $+V$ at the right plate: $$ v_g(x,t) = V \left( \frac{2x}{L} - 1 \right), \quad 0 \le x \le L $$ This is the **Gurney assumption** — the average gas velocity is zero (centre-of-mass frame), and velocity magnitude is maximum at the metal interface. ## 3.3 Kinetic Energy of the Detonation Gases The mass of gas per unit area is $\rho_{ex} h = C/A$ (constant in planar geometry since the gas only stretches laterally, not transversally). The kinetic energy per unit area is: $$ \text{KE}_{gas} = \int_0^L \frac{1}{2} \rho_{ex} \, v_g^2(x) \, dx $$ Substitute $v_g$: $$ \text{KE}_{gas} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{ex} V^2 \int_0^L \left( \frac{2x}{L} - 1 \right)^2 dx $$ Let $u = 2x/L - 1$, so $du = (2/L) dx$, i.e., $dx = (L/2) du$. When $x = 0$, $u = -1$; when $x = L$, $u = +1$: $$ \text{KE}_{gas} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{ex} V^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} \int_{-1}^{+1} u^2 \, du $$ The integral evaluates to: $$ \int_{-1}^{+1} u^2 \, du = \left[ \frac{u^3}{3} \right]_{-1}^{+1} = \frac{2}{3} $$ Therefore: $$ \text{KE}_{gas} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{ex} V^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{6} \rho_{ex} L V^2 $$ ## 3.4 Terminal-Velocity Limit (Gurney's Key Simplification) At terminal velocity, the gas has expanded sufficiently that $L \gg h$. In this limit, the original slab thickness $h$ is negligible compared to $L$, so we may write: $$ \frac{L}{h} \rightarrow \infty \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L \cdot V \approx \text{const (mass continuity with negligible original thickness)} $$ The mass per unit area of gas is $\rho_{ex} h$, and **Gurney takes the limit** $L/h \to \infty$ in the gas KE expression in a specific way. The standard engineering result is that, after the Taylor wave has fully unloaded, the effective gas KE per unit charge mass is: $$ \text{KE}_{gas} = \frac{1}{6} C V^2 $$ where $C = \rho_{ex} h A$ is the total charge mass. > [!NOTE] > **Why $\tfrac{1}{6}$ and not $\tfrac{1}{3}$?** The factor $\tfrac{1}{6}$ comes from taking the **terminal (asymptotic) limit** where the gas has fully rarefied. At intermediate times, the factor is larger ($\tfrac{1}{3}$ when $L = h$), but at terminal velocity it asymptotes to $\tfrac{1}{6}$. This subtlety is sometimes asked as a 2-mark "explain" question. ## 3.5 Energy Conservation The total chemical energy released per unit area is $h \cdot \rho_{ex} \cdot E = C \cdot E / A$. Equating chemical energy to kinetic energy of gas + both plates (each of mass $M$): $$ C \cdot E = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} M V^2 + \frac{1}{6} C V^2 $$ Simplify: $$ C \cdot E = M V^2 + \frac{1}{6} C V^2 $$ Divide both sides by $C$: $$ E = \frac{M}{C} V^2 + \frac{1}{6} V^2 $$ Multiply both sides by 2: $$ 2E = V^2 \left( \frac{M}{C} + \frac{1}{3} \right) $$ ## 3.6 Final Gurney Equation (Symmetric Sandwich, $M$ = total plate mass) $$ \boxed{ \,\, V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \frac{M}{C} + \frac{1}{3} \right)^{-1/2} \,\, } $$ This is the **Gurney equation for the symmetric sandwich configuration**, identical in form to the open-sandwich case if $M$ denotes the total plate mass. (For the open-sandwich case, the gas can expand on one side only, doubling its KE contribution to $\tfrac{1}{3} C V^2$, which yields the **same form** — a remarkable feature of Gurney's model.) ## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A 5 mm thick steel plate (density $\rho_{steel}$ = **7,850 kg/m³**, area 0.05 m²) is accelerated by a 20 mm thick slab of Composition B explosive (density **1,700 kg/m³**, $\sqrt{2E}$ = **2,830 m/s**) in a symmetric sandwich. Calculate the terminal plate velocity. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute masses.** $$ M_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot t_{steel} \cdot A = 7850 \times 0.005 \times 0.05 = 1.9625 \text{ kg} $$ $$ C = \rho_{ex} \cdot h_{ex} \cdot A = 1700 \times 0.020 \times 0.05 = 1.700 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 2 — Mass ratio.** Total plate mass in symmetric sandwich is $M_{tot} = 2 M_{steel} = 3.925$ kg. $$ \frac{M_{tot}}{2C} = \frac{3.925}{2 \times 1.700} = \frac{3.925}{3.400} = 1.154 $$ **Step 3 — Apply Gurney's equation.** $$ V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \frac{M_{tot}}{2C} + \frac{1}{3} \right)^{-1/2} $$ $$ V = 2830 \times \left( 1.154 + 0.333 \right)^{-1/2} $$ $$ V = 2830 \times \left( 1.487 \right)^{-1/2} = 2830 \times 0.820 = 2321 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 4 — Result with units and physical check.** $$ \boxed{ \,\, V \approx 2.32 \text{ km/s} \,\, } $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** A typical shaped-charge jet tip moves at 6–9 km/s with much smaller $M/C$ ratios. Our $M/C \approx 1.15$ is on the heavy-plate side, hence the moderate velocity. This is in the **explosive forming** regime — perfect for plate forming or welding applications. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of an Explosive Metal-Acceleration System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Detonator
Input Trigger] --> B[Booster Charge
Initiates Main Charge] B --> C[Main Explosive Charge
Detonation Wave at Velocity D] C --> D[CJ Detonation Front
Pressure 15 to 40 GPa] D --> E[Detonation Products
Hot Dense Gas, T approx 3000 to 4000 K] E --> F[Taylor Rarefaction Wave
Pressure Decay Phase] F --> G[Metal Plate or Liner
Receives Impulse] G --> H[Acceleration Phase
Plate Gains Velocity V] H --> I[Terminal Velocity
V from Gurney Equation] I --> J{End Use} J --> K[Explosive Forming
Dies and Shapes Panel] J --> L[Explosive Welding
Clads Two Metals] J --> M[Shaped Charge Liner
Forms Hypervelocity Jet] J --> N[Fragmentation
Warhead Case] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Energy Partition Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Chemical Energy of Explosive
C times E units J] --> B[Detonation Products
KE of Gas] A --> C[Metal Mass
KE of Plate] B --> B1[Linear Velocity Profile
KE Gas equals one sixth C V squared] C --> C1[Translational Motion
KE Plate equals one half M V squared] B1 --> D[Total KE equals C times E] C1 --> D D --> E[Gurney Equation
V equals sqrt 2E times Function of M over C] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Configuration Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Sym[Symmetric Sandwich] S1[Plate M] --> S2[Charge C] --> S3[Plate M] S2 -. Detonation .- S2b[Gurney Form V equals sqrt 2E times M over 2C plus 1 third power minus half] end subgraph Open[Open Sandwich] O1[Anvil Rigid] --> O2[Charge C] --> O3[Plate M] O2 -. Detonation .- O2b[Gurney Form V equals sqrt 2E times M over C plus 1 third power minus half] end subgraph Cyl[Cylindrical] C1[Core M] --> C2[Charge C] --> C3[Outer Case M] C2 -. Detonation .- C2b[Gurney Form V equals sqrt 2E times M over C plus 1 half power minus half] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The annotations on the right of each block give the **Gurney velocity formula** in the form expected by the KTU examiner. Memorise the geometric factors $\tfrac{1}{3}$ (planar), $\tfrac{1}{2}$ (cylindrical), $\tfrac{3}{5}$ (spherical) — they are the heart of every KTU problem on this topic. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **State Gurney's law for the velocity of a metal plate accelerated by a slab explosive charge. What is the significance of the term $\sqrt{2E}$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Gurney's law states that the terminal velocity $V$ of a metal plate of mass $M$ accelerated by an adjacent explosive charge of mass $C$ is given by: $$ V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \frac{M}{C} + k \right)^{-1/2} $$ where $k = \tfrac{1}{3}$ for planar/sandwich geometry, $k = \tfrac{1}{2}$ for cylindrical, and $k = \tfrac{3}{5}$ for spherical configurations. The term $\sqrt{2E}$ is the **Gurney characteristic velocity** — a property of the explosive equal to the velocity a fragment would attain if **all** the explosive chemical energy were concentrated in it. For typical military explosives, $\sqrt{2E}$ lies between **2.0 and 3.0 km/s** (e.g., RDX ≈ 2.83 km/s, TNT ≈ 2.37 km/s, HMX ≈ 2.97 km/s). ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Distinguish between explosive forming and explosive welding. Why does the plate velocity range differ for the two applications?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Explosive Forming | Explosive Welding | |:----------|:----------------:|:----------------:| | Plate velocity | 200 – 500 m/s | 250 – 600 m/s | | Stand-off distance | 0.1 – 0.5 m | 0.05 – 0.2 m | | Collision angle | Not critical (single plate) | **Must be 5°–25°** for jetting | | Underwater setup | Common | Common | | Application | Dome/panel shaping | Bimetallic cladding | The velocity range differs because **explosive forming** only requires the plate to undergo large plastic deformation against a die (governed by material flow stress), while **explosive welding** requires a **specific collision-angle–velocity window** to produce a jet of surface material that ensures metallurgical bonding without melting. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Internal choice** is mandatory. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each part below carries 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] A steel plate of thickness 4 mm and area $0.10 \text{ m}^2$ is to be accelerated using a Composition B (Comp-B) explosive slab of thickness 25 mm in a symmetric sandwich configuration. **Given:** $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{CompB} = 1715 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\sqrt{2E}_{CompB} = 2.83 \text{ km/s}$. #### (a) Derive the Gurney equation for the symmetric sandwich configuration starting from energy conservation. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The detonation products obey a **linear velocity profile** (Gurney's tipped profile) across the expanding gas slab. 2. All chemical energy of the explosive is converted to **kinetic energy** of gas and metal (no losses). 3. The metal plate is treated as a **rigid mass**; its internal deformation energy is neglected at the moment of terminal velocity. 4. The geometry is **1-D planar**; the gas slab thickness becomes much larger than the original charge thickness $h$ at terminal velocity (asymptotic limit). 5. Detonation is instantaneous and simultaneous along the charge face. **Energy conservation (2 marks):** $$ C \cdot E = 2 \left( \tfrac{1}{2} M V^2 \right) + \tfrac{1}{6} C V^2 $$ where the factor 2 accounts for the **two plates** flying apart with equal speed $V$, and the gas KE term $\tfrac{1}{6} C V^2$ arises from the **linear velocity profile** assumption in the asymptotic limit (derived by integrating $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2$ over a slab with $v$ varying linearly from $-V$ to $+V$). **Algebraic reduction (2 marks):** $$ 2E = V^2 \left( \frac{M}{C} + \frac{1}{3} \right) $$ $$ \boxed{ \,\, V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \frac{M}{C} + \frac{1}{3} \right)^{-1/2} \,\, } $$ where $M$ is the **total mass of both plates** in the symmetric configuration. (1 mark for stating the final form and the geometric constant.) --- #### (b) Calculate the terminal velocity of each steel plate. If the same setup is repeated with a one-sided (open sandwich) configuration on a rigid anvil, what is the new plate velocity? Comment on the result. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute masses (1 mark):** $$ M_{one} = \rho_{steel} \cdot t \cdot A = 7850 \times 0.004 \times 0.10 = 3.140 \text{ kg} $$ $$ C = \rho_{ex} \cdot h \cdot A = 1715 \times 0.025 \times 0.10 = 4.2875 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 2 — Symmetric sandwich velocity (2 marks):** Total plate mass $M_{tot} = 2 \times 3.140 = 6.280$ kg. $$ \frac{M_{tot}}{2C} = \frac{6.280}{2 \times 4.2875} = \frac{6.280}{8.575} = 0.7324 $$ $$ V_{sym} = 2830 \times \left( 0.7324 + 0.3333 \right)^{-1/2} = 2830 \times (1.0657)^{-1/2} $$ $$ V_{sym} = 2830 \times 0.9687 = 2742 \text{ m/s} \approx 2.74 \text{ km/s} $$ **Step 3 — Open sandwich velocity (2 marks):** In the open sandwich, the gas expands only on one side. The Gurney equation has the same form but $M$ = mass of the single plate: $$ \frac{M_{one}}{C} = \frac{3.140}{4.2875} = 0.7324 $$ Note: the **mass ratio is identical** to the symmetric case (since $M_{tot}/2C$ in the symmetric case equals $M_{one}/C$ in the open case). Therefore: $$ V_{open} = 2830 \times (0.7324 + 0.3333)^{-1/2} = 2742 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 4 — Comment (2 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Counter-intuitive result:** Both configurations give the **same velocity** when the mass ratio $M/C$ is matched. This is because in Gurney's model, the gas kinetic energy in the open-sandwich case is **twice** that in the symmetric case (gas expands on one side only, doubling the average $v^2$ from $\tfrac{1}{6}CV^2$ to $\tfrac{1}{3}CV^2$), and this is **exactly balanced** by the halving of metal kinetic energy (only one plate instead of two). Hence, velocity is preserved. In practice, the open sandwich is preferred for explosive forming because the rigid anvil reflects the shock, doubling the impulse duration and giving slightly higher real-world velocities (≈ 5–10 % above Gurney prediction). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] Discuss the role of **Gurney's characteristic velocity** $\sqrt{2E}$ in the design of explosively accelerated metal systems. #### (a) Explain the physical significance of $\sqrt{2E}$ and outline how it can be determined from detonation physics. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Physical significance (3 marks):** The Gurney characteristic velocity $\sqrt{2E}$ is a **figure of merit for the explosive** that quantifies the maximum kinetic energy deliverable per unit mass of explosive. It has units of velocity (m/s) and represents the velocity that a negligibly small metal mass would acquire if all the explosive's chemical energy were converted into its kinetic energy: $$ \frac{1}{2} m V^2 = m E \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V = \sqrt{2E} $$ For real systems with finite $M/C$, the actual plate velocity is always **less than** $\sqrt{2E}$ because energy is shared with the detonation products. **Determination from detonation physics (4 marks):** Gurney empirically correlated $\sqrt{2E}$ with the detonation velocity $D$ and the polytropic index $\gamma$ of the detonation products: $$ \sqrt{2E} = D \left[ \frac{1}{2(\gamma^2 - 1)} \right]^{1/2} $$ For typical CHNO explosives, $\gamma \approx 3$, giving: $$ \sqrt{2E} \approx \frac{D}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{D}{4} $$ Thus for a Comp-B charge with $D = 7,980$ m/s: $$ \sqrt{2E} \approx \frac{7980}{4} = 1995 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(lower bound)} $$ The **experimental** value is $2,830$ m/s, indicating $\gamma_{eff} \approx 1.6$ — reflecting the more complex multi-species equation of state. The experimental $\sqrt{2E}$ is therefore determined either by **cylinder test** (copper cylinder expansion at $R/R_0 = 6$) or by direct **flyer-plate velocimetry** (VISAR, PDV, or streak photography). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board tip:** KTU examiners expect you to mention that $\sqrt{2E}$ is **empirically corrected** using the cylinder test. The standard value used in Indian defence literature for **Comp-B = 2.83 km/s, TNT = 2.37 km/s, RDX = 2.83 km/s, HMX = 2.97 km/s**. --- #### (b) For a Comp-B charge with $\sqrt{2E} = 2.83$ km/s driving a 6 mm thick copper flyer plate ($\rho_{Cu} = 8,960$ kg/m³, area $A = 0.05$ m²) in a symmetric sandwich, find the charge thickness required to achieve a flyer velocity of 3,500 m/s. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Set up the Gurney equation (1 mark):** $$ V = \sqrt{2E} \left( \frac{M}{C} + \frac{1}{3} \right)^{-1/2} $$ Solve for the mass ratio: $$ \frac{V}{\sqrt{2E}} = \left( \frac{M}{C} + \frac{1}{3} \right)^{-1/2} $$ $$ \left( \frac{V}{\sqrt{2E}} \right)^{-2} = \frac{M}{C} + \frac{1}{3} $$ $$ \frac{M}{C} = \left( \frac{\sqrt{2E}}{V} \right)^2 - \frac{1}{3} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (2 marks):** $$ \frac{\sqrt{2E}}{V} = \frac{2830}{3500} = 0.8086 $$ $$ \left( \frac{\sqrt{2E}}{V} \right)^2 = (0.8086)^2 = 0.6538 $$ $$ \frac{M}{C} = 0.6538 - 0.3333 = 0.3205 $$ **Step 3 — Compute plate mass (1 mark):** $$ M = 2 \times \rho_{Cu} \times t_{Cu} \times A = 2 \times 8960 \times 0.006 \times 0.05 $$ $$ M = 2 \times 8960 \times 0.0003 = 5.376 \text{ kg} $$ (The factor 2 is for both plates in the symmetric sandwich.) **Step 4 — Solve for charge mass $C$ (1 mark):** $$ C = \frac{M}{0.3205} = \frac{5.376}{0.3205} = 16.77 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to charge thickness (2 marks):** $$ h = \frac{C}{\rho_{ex} A} = \frac{16.77}{1715 \times 0.05} = \frac{16.77}{85.75} = 0.1956 \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{ \,\, h \approx 196 \text{ mm of Comp-B} \,\, } $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering sanity check:** $C/M \approx 3.12$ is a typical flyer-plate gas-gun configuration. The flyer velocity of 3.5 km/s is in the **hypervelocity impact** regime. Real flyer-plate gas-gun systems use a compressed-gas driver to achieve the same velocity with much less explosive — this problem illustrates the energy density advantage of chemical explosives. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in symmetric sandwich.** $M$ in the Gurney equation is the **total mass of both plates**, not one plate. Losing this factor costs **3 marks** in a 14-mark problem. > 2. **Wrong geometric constant.** $k = \tfrac{1}{3}$ for planar, $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for cylindrical, $\tfrac{3}{5}$ for spherical. Using $\tfrac{1}{3}$ for a cylindrical problem is a **favourite trap** by KTU examiners. > 3. **Mixing up $\sqrt{2E}$ with $D$.** The detonation velocity $D$ is the velocity of the **reaction zone**; $\sqrt{2E}$ is the **Gurney characteristic velocity** of the explosive. For RDX, $D \approx 8,750$ m/s but $\sqrt{2E} \approx 2,830$ m/s. These are not the same quantity. > 4. **Sign error in $C \cdot E$ equation.** Detonation products carry kinetic energy **opposite** to the plate, but energy is a scalar — both terms are **positive** in the conservation equation. > 5. **Unit inconsistency.** $\sqrt{2E}$ in km/s vs. $M/C$ dimensionless — the result $V$ is naturally in km/s. Convert at the end. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Definition:** Accelerating metal with explosives = converting chemical energy of detonation into kinetic energy of a metal workpiece via Taylor-wave pressure. - [x] **Master equation:** $V = \sqrt{2E} \left( M/C + k \right)^{-1/2}$ - [x] **Geometric constants:** $k_{planar} = \tfrac{1}{3}$, $k_{cylindrical} = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $k_{spherical} = \tfrac{3}{5}$ - [x] **$\sqrt{2E}$ values (memorise):** TNT = 2.37, Comp-B = 2.83, RDX = 2.83, HMX = 2.97, PETN = 2.93, ANFO = 1.85 (all in km/s). - [x] **$M$ in symmetric sandwich = total of both plates** (most common KTU trap). - [x] **Asymptotic limit:** $L/h \to \infty$ gives gas KE factor $\tfrac{1}{6}$; intermediate $L = h$ gives $\tfrac{1}{3}$. - [x] **Assumptions:** linear velocity profile, 1-D geometry, all chemical energy → KE, rigid plate, instantaneous detonation. - [x] **Energy partition:** $C \cdot E = M V^2 + \tfrac{1}{6} C V^2$ (symmetric sandwich). - [x] **Application matrix:** | Application | $V$ range (m/s) | Geometry | |:------------|:---------------:|:--------:| | Explosive forming | 200 – 500 | Symmetric/open | | Explosive welding | 250 – 600 | Inclined flyer | | EFP liner | 1,500 – 2,500 | Cylindrical/conical | | Shaped charge jet | 6,000 – 9,000 | Conical liner collapse | | Hypervelocity flyer | 1,000 – 8,000 | Planar sandwich | - [x] **Cylinder test** is the standard experimental method to determine $\sqrt{2E}$ from copper-tube expansion velocity. - [x] **Open sandwich** with rigid anvil gives the same $V$ as symmetric sandwich with same $M/C$ — counterintuitive but a classic KTU result. - [x] **Energy scaling:** $V$ depends only on the **mass ratio** $M/C$, not on the absolute area of the plate (in 1-D model). - [x] **Industrial relevance:** PESFT753 students should be able to identify this as the parent concept for Module 4 topics — shaped charges, EFPs, and explosive welding systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, the examiner awards **7 marks for derivation** (assumptions + energy balance + algebra) and **7 marks for numerical application** (correct geometric constant + correct $M$ definition + correct $\sqrt{2E}$ value + final answer with units). Skipping any of these four sub-steps in the numerical half costs at least 1.5 marks.

topics.title: Shock Waves in Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shock Waves in Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **shock wave** is a propagating, nearly discontinuous disturbance (a traveling wave of abrupt change in pressure, temperature, density, and particle velocity) that propagates through a medium at a speed greater than the local speed of sound. In the context of air and explosive detonations, it is a **non-linear compressive wave** across which thermodynamic state variables change abruptly over a distance of only a few mean free paths of the gas molecules (typically on the order of microns).

Mathematically, the shock front is treated as a mathematical **surface of discontinuity** (zero thickness idealization), across which the **Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions** — derived from conservation of mass, momentum, and energy — must be satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** A shock wave is NOT merely a loud sound wave. It is a *supersonic* discontinuity that permanently alters the medium it traverses. Behind the front, the air is heated, compressed, and set into motion in the direction of propagation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine standing at the edge of a still lake and flicking a small pebble versus heaving a large boulder into the water:

- **Pebble (weak disturbance):** Produces small, smooth, circular ripples. Each ripple moves outward at the natural wave speed (analogous to a normal sound wave in air).
- **Boulder (strong disturbance):** Pushes a huge wall of water ahead of it. The crest becomes a sharp, towering, almost vertical wall — this is a **hydraulic bore**, the water-analogue of a shock wave.

When a high explosive (like TNT or RDX) detonates, it does the same thing to the surrounding air in three dimensions: it hurls an enormous amount of energy outward as a thin, violently compressed shell of air — the **blast shock front** — traveling faster than sound.

**Everyday Examples:**
- **Lightning → Thunder:** The lightning channel superheats air to ~30,000 K in microseconds, driving an outward blast shock that we hear as thunder.
- **Sonic boom from a supersonic jet:** A nearly conical shock attached to the aircraft, sweeping across the ground.
- **Crack of a whip:** The tip accelerates past the speed of sound, generating a tiny shock — that is the *crack*.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value (Standard Air) |
|---|---|---|
| Ratio of specific heats | $\gamma$ | **1.40** |
| Specific gas constant | $R$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| Universal gas constant | $\bar{R}$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Molar mass of air | $M$ | **28.97 g/mol** |
| Speed of sound at 15 °C | $a_0$ | **340.3 m/s** |
| Standard sea-level pressure | $p_0$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Standard sea-level density | $\rho_0$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |

The **local speed of sound** at any temperature $T$ (in Kelvin) is given by:

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At TNT-equivalent scaling distance $Z = R / W^{1/3} = 1 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$, the peak overpressure is roughly **1000 kPa** — about **10 atmospheres** above ambient. This extreme pressure differential is what makes blast shock waves so destructive to structures and personnel.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Mechanism — Step-by-Step

1. **Energy Release at Source:** Detonation of a high explosive releases energy $E$ in microseconds ($\sim 10^{-6}$ s), converting solid explosive into high-pressure, high-temperature gaseous products (typical detonation pressure $p_{CJ} \approx 20\text{–}30$ GPa).
2. **Piston Effect on Surrounding Air:** The expanding detonation gases act like a high-speed piston, compressing the adjacent air layer.
3. **Catching-up of Disturbances:** The piston velocity vastly exceeds the local sound speed, so every successive compression wave outruns the one ahead of it.
4. **Wave Steepening → Shock Formation:** Because faster waves catch slower ones (sound speed increases in compressed air), the leading edge steepens progressively until it becomes a near-vertical discontinuity — the **shock front**.
5. **Propagation:** The shock propagates outward at a velocity $U_s$ that is a function of its strength (peak overpressure). Strong shocks travel faster than weak ones.
6. **Decay:** As the spherical front expands over radius $R$, the peak overpressure decays roughly as $R^{-1}$ to $R^{-3}$, eventually weakening into an ordinary acoustic wave.

---

### 2.2 Reference Frames & Mach Number

- **Lab (Stationary) Frame:** The undisturbed air ahead is at rest; the shock moves at velocity $U_s$.
- **Shock-Attached (Moving) Frame:** Steady flow, where upstream gas approaches the shock at Mach number $M_1 = u_1 / a_1 > 1$, and leaves at $M_2 < 1$ (subsonic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A defining property of a normal shock in air:** upstream flow is **supersonic** ($M_1 > 1$), downstream flow is **subsonic** ($M_2 < 1$). This is the entropy-generating, irreversible step that distinguishes shocks from ordinary sound waves.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **all rankine-hugoniot relations for a normal shock in a calorically perfect gas (air)**. These are the only formulas you need for any KTU board problem on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed of sound | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | m/s |
| 2 | Mach number | $M = u / a$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Pressure ratio** | $\dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\left(M_1^2 - 1\right)$ | across normal shock |
| 4 | **Density ratio** | $\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \dfrac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}$ | also $= \dfrac{u_1}{u_2}$ |
| 5 | **Temperature ratio** | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{\left[1 + \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2-1)\right]\left[(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2\right]}{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}$ | derived from $p/\rho = RT$ |
| 6 | **Downstream Mach** | $M_2^2 = \dfrac{1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2}{\gamma M_1^2 - \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}}$ | always $< 1$ if $M_1 > 1$ |
| 7 | Strong-shock pressure limit | $p_2 / p_1 \approx \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\, M_1^2$ | for $M_1 \gg 1$ |
| 8 | Strong-shock density limit | $\rho_2 / \rho_1 \to (\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1)$ | $= 6$ for air |
| 9 | Strong-shock temperature | $T_2 / T_1 \approx \dfrac{2\gamma(\gamma-1)}{(\gamma+1)^2} M_1^2$ | for $M_1 \gg 1$ |
| 10 | TNT scaled distance | $Z = R / W^{1/3}$ | m / kg$^{1/3}$ |
| 11 | Hopkinson–Cranz scaling | $\Delta p = f(Z)$ | cube-root law |
| 12 | Shock velocity | $U_s = M_1 \sqrt{\gamma R T_1}$ | m/s, lab frame |

*Note: Subscript 1 = upstream (undisturbed), Subscript 2 = downstream (behind shock).*

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Blast-Resistant Structural Design:** Nuclear weapon test data (e.g., from the U.S. Defense Nuclear Agency) have been compiled into TNT-equivalent blast curves used to design bunkers, ammunition storage magazines, and petrochemical facilities.
- **Underground / Surface Mine Safety:** Predicting flyrock range, airblast overpressure, and ground vibration from blasting operations.
- **Demolition Engineering:** Choosing charge weight, delay timing, and orientation to control fragmentation and direction of collapse.
- **Accident Investigation:** Determining TNT equivalence of vapor-cloud explosions (e.g., the 2005 Buncefield incident) by matching measured peak overpressure to scaled-distance curves.
- **Aerospace:** Re-entry vehicle ablation, scramjet inlet design — all governed by normal/oblique shock relations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Shock Jump Conditions

Consider a stationary normal shock in a duct. Let state **1** = upstream (left), state **2** = downstream (right). Apply **steady, 1-D conservation laws** across the control volume enclosing the shock:

---

**Step 1 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity)**

Mass entering per unit area per unit time on the left must equal mass leaving on the right:

$$\rho_1 u_1 = \rho_2 u_2$$

This is because the shock surface itself stores no mass. This defines the **mass flux** $G \equiv \rho_1 u_1 = \rho_2 u_2$ (a constant through the shock).

---

**Step 2 — Conservation of Momentum**

Apply Newton's second law to the fluid crossing the shock. The net force per unit area equals the momentum flux change:

$$p_1 - p_2 = \rho_2 u_2^2 - \rho_1 u_1^2$$

Rearranging using the mass flux $G = \rho u$:

$$p_1 + G u_1 = p_2 + G u_2$$

This is the **momentum equation of Hugoniot**.

---

**Step 3 — Conservation of Energy (Steady Flow Energy Equation)**

For an adiabatic shock with no work/heat transfer, total (stagnation) enthalpy is conserved:

$$h_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}u_1^2 = h_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}u_2^2$$

For a calorically perfect gas, $h = c_p T = \dfrac{\gamma R T}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{a^2}{\gamma - 1}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{a_1^2}{\gamma-1} + \frac{u_1^2}{2} = \frac{a_2^2}{\gamma-1} + \frac{u_2^2}{2}$$

This is the **Hugoniot energy equation**.

---

**Step 4 — Eliminate $u_2$ Using Mass Conservation**

From continuity:

$$u_2 = \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\, u_1$$

Substitute into the momentum equation:

$$p_1 - p_2 = \rho_1 u_1^2 \left(\frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2} - 1\right)$$

Let $r = \rho_2 / \rho_1$. Then $u_2 = u_1 / r$, and:

$$p_1 - p_2 = \rho_1 u_1^2 \left(\frac{1}{r} - 1\right)$$

Solving for $r$:

$$r = \frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{\rho_1 u_1^2}{p_1 - p_2 + \rho_1 u_1^2} = \frac{G^2}{G^2 + (p_2 - p_1)}\cdot\frac{1}{1}$$

Wait — let me clean this up. Divide numerator and denominator by $p_1$, introduce $M_1^2 = u_1^2/(\gamma p_1/\rho_1) = \rho_1 u_1^2/(\gamma p_1)$:

$$r = \frac{\gamma M_1^2}{\gamma M_1^2 - (p_2/p_1 - 1)}$$

Invert and solve for $p_2/p_1$:

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{\gamma M_1^2 (1 - r)}{r}$$

---

**Step 5 — Use Energy Equation to Eliminate $a_2$**

Rewrite the energy equation using $a^2 = \gamma R T = \gamma p / \rho$ and continuity:

$$\frac{\gamma p_1}{(\gamma-1)\rho_1} + \frac{u_1^2}{2} = \frac{\gamma p_2}{(\gamma-1)\rho_2} + \frac{u_2^2}{2}$$

After algebraic substitution of $u_2 = u_1/r$ and clearing denominators, one obtains:

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}\, r - 1}{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1} - r} \cdot \frac{1}{r}$$

Wait — let me re-derive cleanly. The standard result combines momentum and energy into:

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{(\gamma+1)r - (\gamma-1)}{(\gamma+1) - (\gamma-1)r}$$

---

**Step 6 — Eliminate the Density Ratio**

Equating the two expressions for $p_2/p_1$ from Steps 4 and 5, after considerable algebra:

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{(\gamma+1) M_1^2}{(\gamma-1) M_1^2 + 2}$$

**This is the canonical Rankine–Hugoniot density relation.**

---

**Step 7 — Back-Substitute to Get Pressure and Temperature**

Pressure ratio:

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\left(M_1^2 - 1\right)$$

Temperature ratio (using $p/\rho = RT$):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{p_2}{p_1} \cdot \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2} = \frac{\left[2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)\right]\left[(\gamma-1) M_1^2 + 2\right]}{(\gamma+1)^2 M_1^2}$$

---

**Step 8 — Downstream Mach Number**

From the continuity and downstream conditions, $M_2^2 = u_2^2/a_2^2$. Substituting all the above relations:

$$M_2^2 = \frac{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M_1^2}{\gamma M_1^2 - \frac{\gamma-1}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For $M_1 \to 1^{+}$, $M_2 \to 1$ (no shock — just a sound wave). For $M_1 \to \infty$, $M_2 \to \sqrt{(\gamma-1)/(2\gamma)} = \sqrt{0.4/2.8} = 0.378$ for air. So no matter how strong the shock, downstream Mach never exceeds ~0.378 for air — always subsonic, as required.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Behavior

| Regime | $M_1$ | $p_2/p_1$ | $\rho_2/\rho_1$ | $T_2/T_1$ | $M_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acoustic limit | $1.001$ | $\approx 1.0014$ | $\approx 1.0007$ | $\approx 1.0007$ | $\approx 1$ |
| Weak shock | $1.5$ | $2.05$ | $1.59$ | $1.29$ | $0.70$ |
| Moderate shock | $3.0$ | $10.33$ | $3.00$ | $3.44$ | $0.49$ |
| Strong shock | $5.0$ | $29.00$ | $4.50$ | $6.44$ | $0.42$ |
| Very strong | $10$ | $116.7$ | $5.71$ | $20.4$ | $0.39$ |
| Infinite Mach | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $6.00$ | $\infty$ | $0.378$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exam problems involving explosives:** A detonation typically drives air into $M_1 \approx 5\text{–}15$ (very strong shock regime). The density ratio never exceeds **6** for air — this is a *physical maximum compression* across a single normal shock, a fact students must remember.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A blast wave from a surface explosion arrives at a target 50 m away with $M_1 = 3.0$. Calculate the post-shock pressure, density, and temperature. Ambient: $p_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2(1.4)}{2.4}(9 - 1) = 1 + \frac{2.8}{2.4}(8) = 1 + 9.333 = 10.33$$

$$p_2 = 100 \times 10.33 = 1033 \text{ kPa} \approx 10.2 \text{ atm}$$

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{2.4 \times 9}{0.4 \times 9 + 2} = \frac{21.6}{5.6} = 3.857$$

$$\rho_1 = \frac{p_1}{R T_1} = \frac{100{,}000}{287 \times 300} = 1.161 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_2 = 1.161 \times 3.857 = 4.48 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{p_2/\rho_2}{p_1/\rho_1} = \frac{10.33}{3.857} = 2.68$$

$$T_2 = 300 \times 2.68 = 803 \text{ K}$$

$$M_2^2 = \frac{1 + 0.2 \times 9}{1.4 \times 9 - 0.2} = \frac{2.8}{12.4} = 0.2258$$

$$M_2 = 0.475$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The 50 m away target sees a peak overpressure of **933 kPa (≈9.3 atm gauge)** — sufficient to destroy typical residential masonry and inflict severe lung injury to exposed personnel.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Shock Formation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Detonation of High Explosive"] --> B["Sudden Energy Release in Microseconds"]
    B --> C["Detonation Gases at pCJ approx 20 to 30 GPa"]
    C --> D["Expansion Acts as High Speed Piston on Air"]
    D --> E["Series of Compression Waves Emitted"]
    E --> F["Sound Speed Higher in Compressed Air"]
    F --> G["Trailing Waves Catch Leading Waves"]
    G --> H["Wave Front Steepens Progressively"]
    H --> I["Formation of Sharp Discontinuity"]
    I --> J["Mature Shock Front Traveling Supersonically"]
    J --> K["Spherical Expansion and Decay"]
    K --> L["Eventually Weakens to Acoustic Wave"]
    J --> M["Engineering Damage Zone High Overpressure"]
    M --> N["Structural Collapse and Personnel Injury"]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Blast Wave Parameter Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT CONDITIONS"]
        A1["Charge Mass W kg"]
        A2["Ambient Pressure p1 kPa"]
        A3["Ambient Temperature T1 K"]
        A4["Stand-off Distance R m"]
    end

    subgraph Scaling["SCALING LAWS"]
        B1["Scaled Distance Z equal R over W cube root"]
        B2["Cube Root Hopkinson Cranz Law"]
    end

    subgraph Shock["SHOCK PARAMETERS"]
        C1["Shock Velocity Us m per s"]
        C2["Peak Overpressure delta p"]
        C3["Mach Number M1"]
        C4["Dynamic Pressure q"]
        C5["Positive Phase Duration t+"]
    end

    subgraph Rankine["RANKINE HUGONIOT RELATIONS"]
        D1["Pressure Ratio p2 by p1"]
        D2["Density Ratio rho2 by rho1"]
        D3["Temperature Ratio T2 by T1"]
        D4["Downstream Mach M2"]
    end

    subgraph Output["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"]
        E1["Structural Damage Criterion"]
        E2["Personnel Injury Threshold"]
        E3["Fragment Velocity"]
        E4["Required Standoff for Safety"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    A2 --> C2
    A3 --> C3
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C5
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D4
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
    C2 --> E1
    C2 --> E2
    C4 --> E3
    C5 --> E1
    C2 --> E4
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Description — Normal Shock Profile

A normal shock in the shock-attached frame looks like:

```
    U1 > a1 (supersonic)            U2 < a2 (subsonic)
         ─────►                          ────►
┌────────┐  ┌─────┐    ║    ┌─────┐  ┌────────┐
│  p1    │  │     │    ║    │     │  │   p2   │
│  T1    │  │     │ ◄══╬══► │     │  │   T2   │
│  ρ1    │  │     │    ║    │     │  │   ρ2   │
│  u1    │  │     │    ║    │     │  │   u2   │
└────────┘  └─────┘    ║    └─────┘  └────────┘
   STATE 1  APPROACH   SHOCK   POST-SHOCK    STATE 2
             REGION    FRONT     REGION
                       │
                       └─ p2 > p1
                          T2 > T1
                          ρ2 > ρ1
                          u2 < u1
                          s2 > s1 (entropy ↑)
```

The shock front (vertical bar ║) is treated as infinitesimally thin for engineering analysis. Across it, **all four thermodynamic variables jump discontinuously**, but **mass, momentum, and energy are exactly conserved**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define a shock wave. Explain why a shock wave in air is irreversible, while a sound wave is reversible.**

**Model Answer:**

A shock wave is a supersonic compressive disturbance across which pressure, density, temperature, and particle velocity change discontinuously over a near-zero distance (a few mean free paths of the gas molecules).

A sound wave is reversible because it is an infinitesimally small-amplitude disturbance governed by the linear, isentropic acoustic equation — every particle returns to its original state after the wave passes, with no net change in entropy.

In contrast, a shock wave involves finite-amplitude, non-linear compression. Across the front, the gas is heated to high temperature, and a portion of the mechanical energy is converted irreversibly into internal (thermal) energy via viscous dissipation and thermal conduction within the very thin shock layer. Consequently:

- Entropy strictly **increases** across a real shock: $\Delta s > 0$.
- The downstream state cannot spontaneously return to the upstream state.
- For this reason, **shocks are dissipative and irreversible**, while sound waves are conservative and reversible.

*(Examiner's note: Students often confuse "lossless" with "no entropy change". Emphasize that shocks are adiabatic overall but internally dissipative within the thin front.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State the Rankine–Hugoniot relations for a normal shock in air and explain the physical significance of each.**

**Model Answer:**

For a normal shock in air ($\gamma = 1.4$) with upstream Mach number $M_1$:

1. **Mass (continuity):** $\rho_1 u_1 = \rho_2 u_2$ — Mass flux is continuous; no mass is created or destroyed at the shock.

2. **Momentum:** $p_1 + \rho_1 u_1^2 = p_2 + \rho_2 u_2^2$ — The net pressure force on the shock balances the change in momentum flux.

3. **Energy:** $h_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}u_1^2 = h_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}u_2^2$ — Stagnation enthalpy is conserved across the (adiabatic, no-work) shock.

These three equations, combined with the **equation of state** $p = \rho R T$, yield explicit jump conditions for pressure, density, and temperature ratios. Their significance: they uniquely determine the post-shock thermodynamic state given only the pre-shock state and Mach number $M_1$, without needing to resolve the microscopic shock structure.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the Rankine–Hugoniot pressure and density ratios for a normal shock wave in a perfect gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kg TNT charge detonates on the ground. Using the **Hopkinson–Cranz cube-root scaling law**, estimate the peak overpressure at a distance of 100 m. Assume ambient conditions $p_0 = 101.3$ kPa, and use the following empirical curve (typical blast curve): at $Z = 1.0$, $\Delta p \approx 1000$ kPa; at $Z = 3.0$, $\Delta p \approx 250$ kPa; at $Z = 5.0$, $\Delta p \approx 90$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

Apply conservation of mass, momentum, and energy across the steady, one-dimensional normal shock:

**Continuity:**

$$\rho_1 u_1 = \rho_2 u_2 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Momentum:**

$$p_1 + \rho_1 u_1^2 = p_2 + \rho_2 u_2^2 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Energy (adiabatic, no work):**

$$h_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}u_1^2 = h_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}u_2^2 \quad \text{...(3)}$$

For a perfect gas: $h = c_p T = \dfrac{\gamma R T}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{a^2}{\gamma - 1}$, and $a^2 = \gamma R T = \gamma p / \rho$.

**Eliminating $u_2$:** From (1), $u_2 = (\rho_1/\rho_2) u_1 = u_1/r$ where $r = \rho_2/\rho_1$.

Substitute in (2):

$$p_1 + \rho_1 u_1^2 = p_2 + \rho_2 \frac{u_1^2}{r^2}$$

Dividing by $p_1$ and using $M_1^2 = \rho_1 u_1^2 / (\gamma p_1)$:

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \gamma M_1^2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right) \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Eliminating $a_2$:** Rewrite (3) using $a^2 = \gamma p/\rho$:

$$\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\frac{p_1}{\rho_1} + \frac{u_1^2}{2} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\frac{p_2}{\rho_2} + \frac{u_1^2}{2 r^2}$$

Divide through by $a_1^2 = \gamma p_1/\rho_1$ and use $M_1^2 = u_1^2/a_1^2$:

$$\frac{1}{\gamma-1} + \frac{M_1^2}{2} = \frac{1}{\gamma-1}\frac{p_2}{p_1}\frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2} + \frac{M_1^2}{2 r^2} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

Solving (4) and (5) simultaneously for $r$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}}$$

Back-substituting into (4):

$$\boxed{\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2 - 1)}$$

---

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

The Hopkinson–Cranz cube-root scaling law states that similar blast effects are produced at geometrically similar distances when the scaled distance is equal:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}}$$

**Step 1:** Compute scaled distance for the given scenario.

$$W = 100 \text{ kg TNT}, \quad R = 100 \text{ m}$$

$$Z = \frac{100}{(100)^{1/3}} = \frac{100}{4.642} = 21.55 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 2:** Interpolate from the given curve. The data points are:

| Z (m/kg$^{1/3}$) | $\Delta p$ (kPa) |
|---|---|
| 1.0 | 1000 |
| 3.0 | 250 |
| 5.0 | 90 |

At $Z = 21.55$, we are well outside the tabulated range. **Extrapolate** using the empirical asymptotic decay $\Delta p \propto Z^{-n}$ between $Z = 3$ and $Z = 5$:

$$n = \frac{\ln(250/90)}{\ln(5/3)} = \frac{\ln(2.778)}{\ln(1.667)} = \frac{1.022}{0.511} = 2.00$$

So at large $Z$, $\Delta p \approx A Z^{-2}$. Determining $A$ from $Z = 5$:

$$A = 90 \times 5^2 = 2250$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate at $Z = 21.55$:

$$\Delta p = \frac{2250}{(21.55)^2} = \frac{2250}{464.4} = 4.85 \text{ kPa}$$

**Result:** Peak overpressure at 100 m ≈ **4.85 kPa** (≈ 0.05 atm, or about 4.8% above ambient).

**Engineering interpretation:** This overpressure corresponds to approximately **minor structural damage** — window breakage in some structures, but well below the ~35 kPa threshold for residential collapse and well below the ~15 kPa threshold for serious personnel injury.

---

#### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the formation mechanism of a shock wave in air from an explosive detonation. Why is the shock front treated as a discontinuity in engineering analysis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An observer at 25 m from a detonation measures a shock velocity of $U_s = 680$ m/s. Calculate the upstream Mach number, peak overpressure, density ratio, and temperature ratio. Assume $T_1 = 288$ K, $p_1 = 101.3$ kPa, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

**Formation Mechanism:**

1. **Detonation releases energy in microseconds** ($\sim 10^{-6}$ s), converting the solid explosive into high-pressure detonation products at $p_{CJ} \approx 20\text{–}30$ GPa and temperature $T_{CJ} \approx 3000\text{–}5000$ K.

2. **The hot gaseous products expand violently** into the surrounding ambient air, behaving like an accelerating piston.

3. **A series of compression waves** is continuously emitted into the surrounding air. Each successive wave is emitted from gas that has already been heated and accelerated by the previous wave.

4. **Non-linear wave-steepening:** In a compressible fluid, the local sound speed $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ *increases* with temperature. Therefore, the trailing portions of a compression wave (in the hot, post-compressed gas) travel *faster* than the leading portions (in the cool, undisturbed air).

5. **Wave-crest catching:** Trailing waves overtake leading waves, causing the leading edge of the wave train to steepen progressively until the slope becomes nearly vertical — this is the **mature shock front**.

6. **Steady propagation:** Once formed, the shock propagates outward at supersonic speed, with the Rankine–Hugoniot relations governing the jump conditions.

**Why Treat as Discontinuity:**

- **Physical:** The actual shock thickness is only a few mean-free-paths ($\sim 10^{-7}$ m in air), negligibly small compared to the macroscopic length scales of interest (meters to kilometers).
- **Mathematical:** Treating it as a true discontinuity (zero thickness) yields the Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions, which are exact conservation laws and produce excellent engineering accuracy.
- **Computational:** Resolving the shock thickness in CFD would require impossibly fine grids; the discontinuity approximation with appropriate numerical shock-capturing schemes (e.g., Godunov, Roe) is the standard practice.

---

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Local speed of sound in undisturbed air:**

$$a_1 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_1} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 288} = \sqrt{115{,}718} = 340.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Upstream Mach number:**

$$M_1 = \frac{U_s}{a_1} = \frac{680}{340.2} = 1.999 \approx 2.0$$

**Step 3 — Peak overpressure:**

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2(1.4)}{2.4}(2^2 - 1) = 1 + \frac{2.8}{2.4}(3) = 1 + 3.5 = 4.5$$

$$\Delta p = p_2 - p_1 = 4.5 \times 101.3 - 101.3 = 354.55 \text{ kPa} \approx 355 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

**Step 4 — Density ratio:**

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{(2.4)(4)}{0.4 \times 4 + 2} = \frac{9.6}{3.6} = 2.667$$

**Step 5 — Temperature ratio:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{p_2}{p_1} \cdot \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2} = \frac{4.5}{2.667} = 1.688$$

$$T_2 = 288 \times 1.688 = 486.1 \text{ K} \; (\approx 213^\circ\text{C})$$

**Verification — downstream Mach:**

$$M_2^2 = \frac{1 + 0.2 \times 4}{1.4 \times 4 - 0.2} = \frac{1.8}{5.4} = 0.333$$

$$M_2 = 0.577 \quad (< 1, \text{ subsonic} — \text{validates shock})$$

**Final Results Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Upstream Mach $M_1$ | **2.00** |
| Peak overpressure $\Delta p$ | **355 kPa (gauge)** |
| Density ratio $\rho_2/\rho_1$ | **2.67** |
| Temperature ratio $T_2/T_1$ | **1.69** |
| Post-shock temperature $T_2$ | **486 K** |
| Downstream Mach $M_2$ | **0.577** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge vs absolute pressure.** If the question asks for "overpressure," always report the *gauge* value ($p_2 - p_1$). Reporting $p_2 = 456$ kPa without subtracting ambient loses a mark.
> 2. **Using the wrong speed of sound.** Always compute $a_1 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_1}$ from the given ambient temperature — never assume $a = 340$ m/s unless $T = 288$ K is explicitly stated.
> 3. **Mixing up $\gamma = c_p/c_v$ with $\gamma = c_v/c_p$.** For air, $\gamma = 1.4$ (greater than 1). Writing $\gamma = 0.714$ will produce nonsense.
> 4. **Reporting $M_2 > 1$.** If you ever compute $M_2 > 1$, you have either made an algebra error or are not dealing with a valid normal shock. Cross-check immediately.
> 5. **Neglecting the strong-shock density limit of 6.** For "infinite Mach" problems, students often write $\rho_2/\rho_1 \to \infty$. The correct limit is $(\gamma+1)/(\gamma-1) = 6$ for air.
> 6. **Skipping the energy equation.** A common KTU trap is to derive only mass + momentum and forget that the **three** conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy) are all required. The energy equation is what makes the shock relations *unique*.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Shock wave definition:** A *supersonic, discontinuous* compressive wave in a medium; treated as a surface of zero thickness across which the Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions hold.
- **Three conservation laws:** Mass, momentum, energy (all three are needed — do not drop any).
- **Always subsonic downstream:** $M_2 < 1$ for any shock with $M_1 > 1$ in air.
- **Maximum density ratio (air):** $\rho_2/\rho_1 \to 6$ as $M_1 \to \infty$. *Memorize this.*
- **Maximum downstream Mach (air):** $M_2 \to \sqrt{(\gamma-1)/(2\gamma)} = 0.378$ for $M_1 \to \infty$.
- **Speed of sound:** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$; depends on local temperature only.
- **Pressure ratio formula (memorize):**
  $$\dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2 - 1)$$
- **Density ratio formula (memorize):**
  $$\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \dfrac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}$$
- **Strong-shock limit:** Pressure and temperature grow unboundedly with $M_1$; density saturates at 6.
- **Hopkinson–Cranz cube-root scaling:** $Z = R/W^{1/3}$; used to predict blast effects from charges of different sizes.
- **Engineering values:** $\gamma_{\text{air}} = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $p_0 = 101.3$ kPa, $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level.
- **Irreversibility:** Shock waves are dissipative — entropy strictly increases across the front. Sound waves are isentropic and reversible.
- **Real-world engineering relevance:** Blast-resistant design, mine safety, demolition engineering, accident investigation, and aerospace applications all rely on these relations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shock Waves in Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shock Waves in Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shock Wave in Water:** A shock wave in water is a discontinuity surface propagating at **supersonic speed** through a fluid medium, generated by the rapid release of energy from an underwater explosion. It is characterized by an abrupt, nearly discontinuous jump in pressure, density, temperature, and particle velocity across a thin wavefront of the order of a few molecular mean free paths thick.

When an explosive charge detonates underwater, the chemical reaction occurs on a timescale of microseconds. The detonation products, at temperatures of **3000–5000 K** and pressures exceeding **10 GPa**, violently displace the surrounding water. Because water is essentially incompressible but the expansion is so rapid, the disturbance propagates as a **steep-fronted pressure pulse** — the shock wave — moving outward radially at speeds greater than the local sound speed in water (≈ **1500 m/s**).

Mathematically, the shock satisfies the **Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions**, which are derived from the conservation of mass, momentum, and energy across the discontinuous front.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Stone in a Pond (but on Steroids):**
> Imagine dropping a stone into a still pond. The water surface ripples outward in concentric circles — these are surface gravity waves, gentle and continuous. Now imagine replacing the stone with a tiny but enormously powerful bomb that detonates in **microseconds**. The water molecules don't have time to "swoosh" out of the way gradually; they are **slammed** outward. This violent shove creates a single, sharp, near-vertical wall of compressed water racing outward — that wall is the **shock wave in water**.

A second slower phenomenon follows: the hot gas bubble (detonation products) expands and contracts, sending out **secondary pressure waves** (bubble pulses). So an underwater explosion gives **two distinct signatures**:
1. The **primary shock wave** (fast, sharp, high amplitude, short duration).
2. The **secondary bubble pulsation** (slower, lower amplitude, longer duration).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants for Underwater Shock Analysis:**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_0 \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Speed of sound in water:** $c_0 \approx 1500 \text{ m/s}$ (≈ 1460 m/s freshwater, ≈ 1500 m/s seawater)
> - **Acoustic impedance of water:** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0 \approx 1.54 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$
> - **Adiabatic index for water (shocked):** $\gamma \approx 7.15$ (Cole's value, for the equation of state of water)
> - **TNT equivalent reference energy:** $Q_{TNT} = 4.184 \times 10^9 \text{ J/tonne}$
> - **Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$:** $P_h = P_{atm} + \rho_0 g h$

## 1.4 Why Study Shock Waves in Water?

The subject is not academic — it has **direct engineering applications**:
- **Naval and submarine warfare:** shock hardening of hulls
- **Underwater blasting** for harbor construction, demolition of shipwrecks
- **Pipeline and wellhead severance** in offshore oil & gas
- **Diver safety and swimmer protection** during controlled blasting
- **Seismic exploration** (understanding underwater acoustic sources)
- **Underwater welding and cutting** with explosives

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly addresses **Course Outcome CO3** of PESFT753 — *"Analyze the propagation and effects of shock waves in different media."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Phase Phenomenology of an Underwater Explosion

An underwater explosion is a **two-stage event**. The total energy $E_0$ released by the charge is partitioned as:

$$E_0 = E_s + E_b$$

where:
- $E_s$ = energy radiated as the **primary shock wave** (typically 40–60 % of total).
- $E_b$ = energy retained in the **gas bubble pulsation** (typically 40–60 % of total).

> [!NOTE]
> The exact partition depends on the explosive type, depth, and the **gas equation of state**. For TNT, the shock fraction is typically ≈ **53 %** and the bubble fraction ≈ **47 %**.

## 2.2 The Pressure–Time History at a Fixed Field Point

A transducer placed at distance $R$ from a spherical charge of weight $W$ records a characteristic waveform:
