1. **Shock front arrival** at $t = t_a = R/c_0$ — an abrupt, almost instantaneous pressure jump from ambient $P_0$ to the **peak pressure** $P_m$.
2. **Exponential decay** of pressure behind the front with a characteristic decay time $\theta$.
3. **Negative pressure phase** as the water rebounds, possibly going below ambient.
4. **Bubble pulses** arriving later, as the gas bubble oscillates.

The classical **Cole equation** for the pressure time-history is:

$$P(t) = P_m \, e^{-(t - t_a)/\theta}, \quad \text{for } t_a \le t \le t_a + \theta^*$$

where $\theta^*$ is a cutoff (typically a few times $\theta$).

## 2.3 Peak Pressure, Decay Constant, and Energy — Cole's Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Master Equations of R.H. Cole (1948):**

| # | Parameter | Cole's Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Peak Pressure** $P_m$ | $P_m = K_p \left(\dfrac{W^{1/3}}{R}\right)^{\alpha_p}$ | Pa | Maximum overpressure at distance $R$ |
| 2 | **Time Constant** $\theta$ | $\theta = K_\theta \, W^{1/3} \left(\dfrac{W^{1/3}}{R}\right)^{\alpha_\theta}$ | s | Exponential decay time of the shock |
| 3 | **Energy Flux** $E_s$ | $E_s = K_e \left(\dfrac{W^{1/3}}{R}\right)^{\alpha_e}$ | J/m² | Energy per unit area in the shock front |

> [!NOTE]
> The exponents $\alpha_p, \alpha_\theta, \alpha_e$ are slightly greater than **1**, reflecting the weak compressibility of water. The cube-root scaling $W^{1/3}$ arises from geometric similarity — all shock parameters for a spherical charge depend on the **charge radius** $r_0 = (3W/4\pi\rho_{expl})^{1/3}$.

## 2.4 Numerical Values of Cole's Constants (for TNT in water at infinite depth)

| Constant | Value (SI) |
|----------|------------|
| $K_p$ | $5.21 \times 10^7$ |
| $\alpha_p$ | $1.13$ |
| $K_\theta$ | $8.39 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| $\alpha_\theta$ | $-0.23$ |
| $K_e$ | $8.39 \times 10^3$ |
| $\alpha_e$ | $2.04$ |

> [!WARNING]
> These constants assume $W$ in **kg of TNT** and $R$ in **meters**. Mixing units is the #1 source of calculation errors in KTU answer sheets.

## 2.5 The Specific Shock Impulse

The **impulse** is the time-integral of the pressure pulse. For a single exponential decay:

$$I = \int_{t_a}^{\infty} P(t) \, dt = P_m \, \theta$$

This is the most useful **damage parameter** for brittle structures (ship hulls, concrete) because it represents the total momentum delivered per unit area.

## 2.6 Bubble Period and Bubble Radius (Rayleigh–Willis Formula)

After the primary shock has radiated away, the gas bubble continues to expand, reaches a maximum radius $R_b$, then collapses. The **first bubble period** $T_b$ is given by the **Willis formula**:

$$T_b = K_b \, \frac{W^{1/3}}{(h + 10)^{5/6}}$$

where $h$ is the charge depth in meters, $W$ in kg, and $K_b \approx 2.11$ (Willis's empirical constant).

## 2.7 Damage Criteria (Operational Engineering Rules)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU-Recognized Damage Thresholds (Peak Pressure at the Target):**
> - **Human lethality (lungs):** $P_m > 2 \times 10^5$ Pa (≈ 2 bar) → 50 % mortality
> - **Eardrum rupture:** $P_m > 1.6 \times 10^5$ Pa
> - **Wooden boat hull rupture:** $P_m > 5 \times 10^5$ Pa
> - **Concrete (20 cm) cracking:** $P_m > 2 \times 10^6$ Pa
> - **Steel plate (1 cm) perforation:** $P_m > 1 \times 10^7$ Pa

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Where Used |
|---|---------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | $P_m = K_p \left(\dfrac{W^{1/3}}{R}\right)^{\alpha_p}$ | $W$=kg TNT, $R$=m | Peak pressure |
| 2 | $\theta = K_\theta W^{1/3} \left(\dfrac{W^{1/3}}{R}\right)^{\alpha_\theta}$ | $\theta$ in s | Decay time |
| 3 | $I = P_m \, \theta$ | $I$ in Pa·s | Specific impulse |
| 4 | $E_s = K_e \left(\dfrac{W^{1/3}}{R}\right)^{\alpha_e}$ | $E_s$ in J/m² | Shock energy flux |
| 5 | $T_b = K_b \dfrac{W^{1/3}}{(h+10)^{5/6}}$ | $h$ in m | Bubble period |
| 6 | $E_0 = E_s + E_b$ | — | Energy partition |
| 7 | $c_0 = \sqrt{B/\rho_0} \approx 1500$ m/s | $B$=bulk modulus | Sound speed |
| 8 | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0$ | — | Acoustic impedance |
| 9 | $v = \dfrac{P_m - P_0}{\rho_0 c_0}$ | $v$ = particle velocity | Behind shock |
| 10 | $R_{safe} = K_p^{1/\alpha_p} W^{1/3} P_{crit}^{-1/\alpha_p}$ | $P_{crit}$ = safe limit | Safety distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Every one of these formulas is used by naval architects (hull shock design), offshore engineers (wellhead severance), and demolitions experts (safe blasting radius calculation). The cube-root scaling $W^{1/3}$ is the cornerstone of "scaling laws" — a 1000 kg charge is **equivalent in shock effect** to a 1 kg charge placed at 10× the distance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rankine–Hugoniot Jump Conditions

We start with a **stationary shock front** (we move into the reference frame of the wave). Let subscript 1 denote the undisturbed water in front of the shock, and subscript 2 denote the shocked water behind the front. The shock propagates with speed $U_s$ into the undisturbed medium.

### Step 1: Continuity (Mass Conservation)

In the shock frame, mass flux in = mass flux out:

$$\rho_1 U_s = \rho_2 (U_s - v_2)$$

Rearranging:

$$U_s = \frac{\rho_2 \, v_2}{\rho_2 - \rho_1}$$

**Logic:** The undisturbed water sees the shock approaching at $U_s$; the shocked water behind it moves at particle velocity $v_2$ in the laboratory frame, so relative to the shock, it recedes at $(U_s - v_2)$. Mass flux must balance.

### Step 2: Momentum Conservation

The pressure jump accelerates the water:

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 U_s v_2$$

**Logic:** The pressure difference $(P_2 - P_1)$ across the shock front times the unit area equals the rate of momentum flux, $\rho_1 U_s \times v_2$.

### Step 3: Energy Conservation (Rankine–Hugoniot Energy Equation)

Across the shock, the specific internal energy jumps:

$$e_2 - e_1 = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$$

**Logic:** This is the Hugoniot (shock adiabat) energy equation. It replaces the isentropic relation $de = T ds + P dv$ — note there is **no entropy term** because the shock is adiabatic but irreversible. The arithmetic mean pressure $(P_1 + P_2)/2$ times the specific volume change gives the work done.

### Step 4: Combining the Three

Eliminating $U_s$ and $v_2$ from the three jump equations and using the Tait equation of state for water:

$$P = B \left[\left(\frac{\rho}{\rho_0}\right)^n - 1\right] + P_0, \quad n \approx 7.15$$

yields the **shock speed** as a function of particle velocity:

$$U_s = c_0 + s v_2, \quad s \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \text{ for water}$$

This is the **acoustic approximation** valid up to a few GPa, and is the basis for all subsequent engineering formulas.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cube-Root Scaling Law (Geometric Similarity)

Consider two spherical charges of the same explosive with weights $W_1$ and $W_2$ detonating at depths such that hydrostatic conditions are similar. By **Buckingham's Pi Theorem**, with the relevant variables:

- Energy: $E_0$ (J)
- Charge weight: $W$ (kg TNT equivalent)
- Distance: $R$ (m)
- Water density: $\rho_0$ (kg/m³)
- Sound speed: $c_0$ (m/s)

There is only one dimensionless group. Hence **peak pressure** must be a function of a single combination:

$$\frac{P_m}{\rho_0 c_0^2} = f\!\left(\frac{R}{W^{1/3}}\right)$$

Reformulating:

$$P_m = \rho_0 c_0^2 \, f\!\left(\frac{R}{W^{1/3}}\right) = \rho_0 c_0^2 \, F\!\left(\frac{W^{1/3}}{R}\right)$$

This is the **theoretical origin of cube-root scaling**. It states: two charges of different weights $W_1$ and $W_2$ produce the **same peak pressure** at distances $R_1$ and $R_2$ provided:

$$\frac{W_1^{1/3}}{R_1} = \frac{W_2^{1/3}}{R_2}$$

## 3.3 Full Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (Model Solution for KTU Board Style):**

**Problem:** A **500 kg TNT charge** is detonated underwater at sufficient depth. Calculate the **peak pressure**, **time constant**, **specific impulse**, and **shock energy flux** at a distance of **50 m** from the charge. Use Cole's equations.

**Given:** $W = 500$ kg, $R = 50$ m

**Step 1 — Compute the scaled distance $\lambda$:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{W^{1/3}}{R} = \frac{500^{1/3}}{50} = \frac{7.937}{50} = 0.1587 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Logic:** Cube-root scaling is the universal reduced variable for underwater explosions.

**Step 2 — Peak Pressure:**

$$
P_m = K_p \cdot \lambda^{\alpha_p} = (5.21 \times 10^7) \cdot (0.1587)^{1.13}
$$

Compute $(0.1587)^{1.13}$:

$$
\ln(0.1587) = -1.841, \quad 1.13 \times (-1.841) = -2.080, \quad e^{-2.080} = 0.1247
$$

$$
P_m = 5.21 \times 10^7 \times 0.1247 = 6.50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Time Constant:**

$$
\theta = K_\theta W^{1/3} \lambda^{\alpha_\theta} = (8.39 \times 10^{-3})(7.937)(0.1587)^{-0.23}
$$

Compute $(0.1587)^{-0.23}$:

$$
-0.23 \times \ln(0.1587) = -0.23 \times (-1.841) = 0.4234, \quad e^{0.4234} = 1.527
$$

$$
\theta = 8.39 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.937 \times 1.527 = 1.017 \times 10^{-1} \text{ s} \approx 102 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Specific Impulse:**

$$
I = P_m \cdot \theta = 6.50 \times 10^6 \times 1.02 \times 10^{-1} = 6.63 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy Flux:**

$$
E_s = K_e \cdot \lambda^{\alpha_e} = 8.39 \times 10^3 \times (0.1587)^{2.04}
$$

Compute $(0.1587)^{2.04}$:

$$
2.04 \times \ln(0.1587) = 2.04 \times (-1.841) = -3.755, \quad e^{-3.755} = 0.02343
$$

$$
E_s = 8.39 \times 10^3 \times 0.02343 = 196.6 \text{ J/m}^2
$$

**Final Summary (boxed for examiner's clarity):**

$$
\boxed{P_m = 6.5 \text{ MPa}, \quad \theta = 102 \text{ ms}, \quad I = 6.63 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa·s}, \quad E_s = 196.6 \text{ J/m}^2}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Behavior with Distance

| Quantity | Functional Form | Behavior with Increasing $R$ | Physical Reason |
|----------|-----------------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| $P_m$ | $\propto R^{-1.13}$ | Decreases steeply | Geometric spreading + dissipation |
| $\theta$ | $\propto R^{0.23}$ | Increases slowly | Shock broadens as it travels |
| $I = P_m \theta$ | $\propto R^{-0.90}$ | Decreases | Most momentum radiated early |
| $E_s$ | $\propto R^{-2.04}$ | Decreases steeply | Energy density falls as $1/R^2$ on a spherical front |
| Bubble period $T_b$ | Independent of $R$ | Constant | Set by gas dynamics, not observer position |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Underwater Explosion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Detonation["Detonation Event"] --> Compression["Hot Gas Fireball T equal 3000-5000 K"]
    Compression --> Shock1["Primary Shock Wave"]
    Compression --> Bubble1["Gas Bubble Formation"]
    Shock1 --> Radiate["Radiation Through Water"]
    Radiate --> Attenuate["Attenuation by Geometric Spreading"]
    Attenuate --> Attenuate2["Attenuation by Viscous and Thermal Dissipation"]
    Attenuate2 --> Target1["Arrival at Target or Sensor"]
    Target1 --> Record["Pressure Time History P of t"]
    Record --> Decompose["Decompose into Peak Pressure, Decay Constant, Impulse"]
    Bubble1 --> Expand["First Bubble Expansion"]
    Expand --> MaxR["Maximum Bubble Radius R sub b"]
    MaxR --> Collapse["First Bubble Collapse"]
    Collapse --> Pulse1["First Rebound Pressure Pulse"]
    Collapse --> Recur["Successive Pulsations with Decreasing Amplitude"]
    Pulse1 --> Target2["Arrival at Target with Delay T sub b"]
    Target2 --> Add["Add to Total Loading"]
    Decompose --> Add
    Add --> Damage["Damage Assessment"]
    Damage --> Hull["Hull Rupture"]
    Damage --> Concrete["Concrete Cracking"]
    Damage --> Human["Human Injury"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pressure–Time History at a Fixed Gauge Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time t sub a: Shock Arrival"] --> B["Pressure jumps to Peak P sub m"]
    B --> C["Exponential Decay with Time Constant theta"]
    C --> D["Pressure falls below Ambient at about 1 to 2 theta"]
    D --> E["Negative Phase for about 5 to 10 theta"]
    E --> F["Equilibration toward Hydrostatic P sub 0"]
    F --> G["At t sub b: First Bubble Pulse Arrives"]
    G --> H["Secondary Peak of Amplitude about 10 to 20 percent of P sub m"]
    H --> I["Subsequent Pulses with Decreasing Amplitude"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cube-Root Scaling Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ChargeW1["Charge Weight W sub 1"] --> Ratio1["Scaled Distance Lambda sub 1 equal W sub 1 to the 1/3 over R sub 1"]
    ChargeW2["Charge Weight W sub 2"] --> Ratio2["Scaled Distance Lambda sub 2 equal W sub 2 to the 1/3 over R sub 2"]
    Ratio1 --> Equate["If Lambda sub 1 equal Lambda sub 2 then Peak Pressures are Identical"]
    Ratio2 --> Equate
    Equate --> Use["Engineering Use: Predict Effects of Large Charges from Small Charge Tests"]
    Use --> Example["1 kg at 10 m gives same P sub m as 1000 kg at 100 m"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Energy Partition in an Underwater Explosion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E0["Total Chemical Energy E sub 0"] --> Split["Energy Partition based on Explosive Type and Depth"]
    Split --> Es["Shock Wave Energy E sub s — about 40 to 60 percent"]
    Split --> Eb["Bubble Energy E sub b — about 40 to 60 percent"]
    Es --> EsUse["Radiated as Primary Shock — does damage at early times"]
    Eb --> EbUse["Stored in Gas Bubble — does damage via bubble pulses and heave"]
    EsUse --> Damage1["Brittle Fracture, Hull Rupture, Eardrum Damage"]
    EbUse --> Damage2["Ship Lift, Mooring Line Snap, Sustained Loading"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a shock wave in water. How does it differ from an ordinary acoustic wave?**

**Model Answer:**

> A shock wave in water is a **steep-fronted pressure discontinuity** that propagates at supersonic speed, across which there is an **abrupt jump in pressure, density, temperature, and particle velocity**. It is produced by the sudden release of energy from an underwater explosion.

> **Differences from an ordinary acoustic wave:**
> - Acoustic waves are **infinitesimal** disturbances obeying linear equations; shock waves are **finite-amplitude** and obey the **Rankine–Hugoniot** non-linear jump conditions.
> - Shock waves travel **faster than the local sound speed**; acoustic waves travel **at** the local sound speed.
> - Behind a shock, entropy increases; acoustic propagation is isentropic (reversible).
> - Shock waves have a **finite rise time** (of order microseconds) but the front is effectively a discontinuity; acoustic waves are smooth sinusoids.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the cube-root scaling law for underwater explosions. What is its physical origin?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** Two explosive charges of weights $W_1$ and $W_2$ produce the same peak pressure at distances $R_1$ and $R_2$ provided

$$
\frac{W_1^{1/3}}{R_1} = \frac{W_2^{1/3}}{R_2}
$$

> **Physical origin:** It is a direct consequence of **geometric similarity** for spherical charges. Two similar explosions of the same explosive at hydrostatically similar conditions are dynamically similar if their **linear scales are in the same ratio**. The charge's linear dimension scales as $W^{1/3}$, so distances must also scale as $W^{1/3}$ to preserve similarity. This is rigorously derived from **Buckingham's Pi Theorem** of dimensional analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Option A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, describe the **pressure–time history** recorded at a fixed point during an underwater explosion. Mark clearly the **peak pressure $P_m$**, the **time constant $\theta$**, the **negative phase**, and the **first bubble pulse**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **100 kg TNT charge** is detonated underwater. Using Cole's equations, determine the **peak pressure**, **time constant**, and **specific impulse** at a distance of **30 m** from the charge. State any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

1. A typical hydrophone or pressure gauge at distance $R$ records a characteristic waveform with the following features in chronological order:
   - **Arrival time** $t_a = R/c_0$: the moment the shock front passes the gauge.
   - **Peak pressure** $P_m$: instantaneous jump from ambient $P_0$ to a maximum (typically several MPa to tens of MPa).
   - **Exponential decay** with characteristic time $\theta$: the pressure behind the front falls as $P(t) = P_m \exp(-(t-t_a)/\theta)$.
   - **Negative phase** (between roughly $1.5\theta$ and $5\theta$): pressure may drop below ambient as water rebounds.
   - **Bubble pulse** at $t = t_b$: a secondary pressure rise due to first collapse of the gas bubble, typically 10–20 % of $P_m$ in amplitude.

2. The two scales on the time axis are the **shock duration** (order of $\theta$, milliseconds) and the **bubble period** (order of $T_b$, hundreds of milliseconds to seconds).

3. **Significance:** The shock wave is responsible for **brittle fracture** damage; the bubble pulse causes **ductile deformation** and may cause **mooring snap or ship lift**.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Given:** $W = 100$ kg, $R = 30$ m. Cole's constants (TNT in water, infinite depth): $K_p = 5.21 \times 10^7$, $\alpha_p = 1.13$, $K_\theta = 8.39 \times 10^{-3}$, $\alpha_\theta = -0.23$.

**Step 1:** Cube-root of charge weight:

$$
W^{1/3} = 100^{1/3} = 4.642 \text{ kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2:** Scaled distance:

$$
\lambda = \frac{W^{1/3}}{R} = \frac{4.642}{30} = 0.1547 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 3:** Peak pressure:

$$
P_m = 5.21 \times 10^7 \times (0.1547)^{1.13}
$$

Computing the power:

$$
1.13 \times \ln(0.1547) = 1.13 \times (-1.866) = -2.109, \quad e^{-2.109} = 0.1214
$$

$$
P_m = 5.21 \times 10^7 \times 0.1214 = 6.32 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.32 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Time constant:

$$
\theta = 8.39 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.642 \times (0.1547)^{-0.23}
$$

Computing:

$$
-0.23 \times \ln(0.1547) = -0.23 \times (-1.866) = 0.4292, \quad e^{0.4292} = 1.536
$$

$$
\theta = 8.39 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.642 \times 1.536 = 5.98 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} \approx 60 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5:** Specific impulse:

$$
I = P_m \cdot \theta = 6.32 \times 10^6 \times 5.98 \times 10^{-2} = 3.78 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Assumptions stated:**
- Charge is TNT, spherical, at sufficient depth to neglect free-surface reflection.
- The receiver is in the **far field** ($\lambda < 0.4$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ — satisfied).
- Cole's constants for infinite depth apply.

**Final boxed results:**

$$
\boxed{P_m = 6.32 \text{ MPa}, \quad \theta = 60 \text{ ms}, \quad I = 3.78 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa·s}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Option B

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions** for a normal shock in water. State clearly the assumptions and the physical meaning of each equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **1000 kg TNT charge** is detonated at a depth of **25 m**. Estimate the **first bubble period** using the **Willis formula** and comment on the **safe distance** for a moored ship of hull yield strength equivalent to $P_{crit} = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. The examiner expects the following three equations and a clear physical interpretation of each.)

1. **Continuity:** $\rho_1 U_s = \rho_2 (U_s - v_2)$ — Mass flux in = Mass flux out across the moving front.

2. **Momentum:** $P_2 - P_1 = \rho_1 U_s v_2$ — Net pressure force = rate of momentum transfer.

3. **Energy (Hugoniot):** $e_2 - e_1 = \frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_1} - \frac{1}{\rho_2}\right)$ — Adiabatic but irreversible; replaces the isentropic $de = T ds - P dv$ since $ds > 0$ across the shock.

**Assumptions:**
- One-dimensional, planar shock (locally, near the front, the spherical shock is approximated as planar).
- Steady shock in its own reference frame.
- No mass, momentum, or energy sources/sinks across the front (the shock is a true discontinuity).
- Fluid is inviscid and non-heat-conducting across the front.
- Closed-system energy balance: no radiation, no chemical reaction across the front.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1:** Compute the first bubble period using the Willis formula:

$$
T_b = K_b \cdot \frac{W^{1/3}}{(h + 10)^{5/6}}
$$

With $K_b = 2.11$, $W = 1000$ kg, $h = 25$ m:

$$
W^{1/3} = 10, \quad h + 10 = 35, \quad 35^{5/6} = ?
$$

$$
\ln 35 = 3.555, \quad (5/6) \times 3.555 = 2.963, \quad e^{2.963} = 19.34
$$

$$
T_b = 2.11 \times \frac{10}{19.34} = 1.091 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the safe distance for a hull rated at $P_{crit} = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa. Using the inverse of Cole's peak pressure formula:

$$
P_{crit} = K_p \left(\frac{W^{1/3}}{R_{safe}}\right)^{\alpha_p}
$$

Solve for $R_{safe}$:

$$
R_{safe} = W^{1/3} \left(\frac{K_p}{P_{crit}}\right)^{1/\alpha_p}
$$

Substituting:

$$
R_{safe} = 10 \times \left(\frac{5.21 \times 10^7}{5 \times 10^5}\right)^{1/1.13} = 10 \times (104.2)^{0.885}
$$

Computing the power:

$$
0.885 \times \ln(104.2) = 0.885 \times 4.646 = 4.112, \quad e^{4.112} = 61.16
$$

$$
R_{safe} = 10 \times 61.16 = 611.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Comment:** A moored ship with hull yield strength of $5 \times 10^5$ Pa must be at least **~612 m** from a 1-tonne underwater detonation to avoid rupture. This is consistent with naval safety manuals.

**Final boxed results:**

$$
\boxed{T_b = 1.09 \text{ s}, \quad R_{safe} = 612 \text{ m}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Unit confusion in Cole's equations** — failing to convert $W$ to kg and $R$ to meters. **(-2 marks typically).**
> 2. **Confusing shock impulse $I$ with momentum $I = m v$** — remember $I$ in this context is $\int P \, dt$, not $\int F \, dt$ for a mass. **(-1 mark).**
> 3. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** (spherical charge, infinite depth, far-field, TNT equivalent) in numerical problems. **(-1 to -2 marks).**
> 4. **Sign error in $\alpha_\theta$** — it is *negative* (-0.23), so the time constant **grows** with distance, not shrinks. **(-1 mark).**
> 5. **Using $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ instead of 1025 kg/m³** for seawater. The textbook answer is 1025 for ocean conditions; for freshwater, use 1000. **(-1 mark).**
> 6. **In derivations, omitting the entropy term explanation** — examiners want a sentence on why $ds > 0$ across a shock. **(-1 mark).**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 3, Topic: Shock Waves in Water**

- ⭐ A **shock wave in water** is a finite-amplitude pressure discontinuity traveling at supersonic speed, governed by the **Rankine–Hugoniot** jump conditions.
- ⭐ The phenomenon has **two distinct signatures**: a sharp **primary shock** (microseconds–milliseconds) and slower **bubble pulsations** (hundreds of milliseconds–seconds).
- ⭐ Total explosion energy is partitioned as $E_0 = E_s + E_b$ (shock + bubble), typically a roughly 50/50 split for TNT.
- ⭐ **Cole's three master equations** give $P_m$, $\theta$, and $E_s$ — all are functions of the **scaled distance** $\lambda = W^{1/3}/R$.
- ⭐ **Cube-root scaling** $W^{1/3}$ is a consequence of **geometric similarity** of spherical charges — two charges with equal $W^{1/3}/R$ produce identical peak pressures.
- ⭐ **Specific impulse** $I = P_m \theta$ is the **damage parameter** of choice for brittle structures (hulls, concrete).
- ⭐ **Willis formula** for bubble period: $T_b = K_b W^{1/3} / (h + 10)^{5/6}$ — depends on charge depth, not observer distance.
- ⭐ The **Tait equation of state** $P = B[(\rho/\rho_0)^n - 1] + P_0$ with $n \approx 7.15$ is the standard model for water under shock.
- ⭐ The **acoustic impedance** of water is $Z_0 = \rho_0 c_0 \approx 1.54 \times 10^6$ kg/(m²·s) — this is what makes water transmit shocks so efficiently compared to air.
- ⭐ Standard damage thresholds to memorize: lungs (2 bar), eardrums (1.6 bar), concrete (20 bar), steel plate (100 bar).
- ⭐ When the problem says "TNT equivalent", use Cole's constants for TNT directly. For other explosives, first compute the **TNT-equivalent weight** $W_{TNT} = W \times (Q_{expl}/Q_{TNT})$.
- ⭐ Always state **five assumptions** in numerical problems: spherical charge, far-field, TNT, infinite depth, hydrostatic ambient.
- ⭐ The **Rankine–Hugoniot** energy equation has the term $\frac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)(1/\rho_1 - 1/\rho_2)$ — the arithmetic mean pressure times volume change — which is the **work done by the mean pressure during compression**. Memorize this form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Craters from Explosives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Craters from Explosives # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **crater from explosives** is a depression, cavity, or hollow produced on the surface of the ground (soil, rock, or other medium) as a direct consequence of the detonation of a confined, semi-confined, or unconfined explosive charge placed at or near the surface. The geometry, volume, and morphology of the resulting crater depend primarily on the **mass and energy yield of the explosive**, the **depth of burial (DOB)**, the **stemming conditions**, and the **mechanical properties of the surrounding medium**. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, cratering is studied as a fundamental outcome of **near-surface explosion mechanics** and forms the analytical basis for civil blasting, mining bench blasts, military breaching operations, and safety-zone design. > [!NOTE] > **Cratering** is technically defined as the process of rock/soil displacement outward from the charge axis, generating a measurable depression whose boundaries are defined by the **apparent crater rim**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview Imagine pressing a tennis ball (the explosive charge) into a flat tray of wet sand (the ground medium) and then detonating it. The blast energy pushes the sand radially outward, throwing some of it into the air (ejecta) while compressing the surrounding sand. What remains is a bowl-shaped hollow — that hollow is the **crater**. Three intuitive insights follow: 1. **Bigger charge → bigger crater.** A tennis ball pushed in makes a small dimple; a basketball makes a deep bowl. Charge energy scales the crater dimensions. 2. **Deeper placement → different crater shapes.** A ball just resting on the surface (unconfined) makes a shallow splatter, while a ball buried halfway makes a clean bowl. This is exactly why **depth of burial (DOB)** is the single most influential design parameter. 3. **Stronger medium → smaller crater.** Pushing into wet sand (weak, loose) produces a bigger hole than pushing into compacted clay (strong). Rock strength and density govern crater resistance. > [!IMPORTANT] > The standard unit of explosive energy used in crater engineering is **kilograms of TNT equivalent (kg TNT-eq)**, with the **standard energy release of 4.184 × 10⁶ J/kg** as the reference benchmark. ## 1.3 Standard Crater Metrics (KTU-Listed Parameters) | Metric | Symbol | Description | |---|---|---| | Apparent Crater Radius | $R_a$ | Radius measured at the original ground surface | | True Crater Radius | $R_t$ | Radius measured at the actual rupture boundary below surface | | Apparent Depth | $D_a$ | Vertical distance from original surface to crater bottom | | True Depth | $D_t$ | Vertical distance from rupture boundary to crater bottom | | Crater Volume | $V_c$ | Total volume of material displaced | | Depth of Burial | $DOB$ | Vertical distance from explosive charge center to free surface | | Optimum Depth of Burial | $ODB$ | Depth producing maximum crater volume | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Mechanism of Crater Formation When an explosive charge detonates at or near a free surface, the resulting crater forms through three sequential energy-transfer phases. Each phase contributes a distinct portion of the observed crater geometry. ### Phase 1 — Stress-Wave (Shock) Phase - The detonation shock propagates outward as a hemispherical stress wave. - On reaching the free surface, it reflects as a **tensile (rarefaction) wave**. - Rock fails in **spalling** (tensile fracture) parallel to the surface — initiating the crater boundary. ### Phase 2 — Gas-Pressure (Expansion) Phase - Hot detonation gases (typically 3000–4000 K, several GPa pressure) expand violently. - These gases perform **thermodynamic work** on the fractured medium, accelerating the broken rock outward. - This phase produces the **ejecta** and the dominant crater volume. ### Phase 3 — Vent / Ejection Phase - Gases vent to atmosphere through the developing crater throat. - Final crater geometry stabilizes as pressure equilibrates. > [!TIP] > In **KTU board examinations**, examiners expect students to clearly delineate these three phases. A common mistake is to merge Phases 1 and 2 — which loses marks in the "explain the mechanism" 7-mark sub-parts. ## 2.2 Classification of Craters by Geometry ``` Crater Classification Tree │ ├── By Morphology │ ├── True Crater (full broken zone boundary) │ ├── Apparent Crater (visible above-ground depression) │ └── False / Boulder Crater (formed by ejected rock impact) │ ├── By Depth of Burial │ ├── Sub-ODB → "Blowout" (charge too shallow; excess ejecta, small bowl) │ ├── At ODB → "Normal" crater (maximum volume — design optimum) │ └── Super-ODB → "Deep" or "Camouflet" (no surface breach) │ └── By Application ├── Mining bench blast crater ├── Military breaching crater ├── Civil excavation crater └── Ditch / trench blast crater ``` ## 2.3 Hopkinson's Cube-Root Scaling — The Foundation The cornerstone principle of cratering physics is **geometric similarity scaling**, formalized by **Berthelot (1885)** and refined by **Hopkinson (1915)**. It states: > If two charges of the **same explosive type** detonate at **geometrically similar depths** in the **same medium**, then all corresponding linear crater dimensions scale with the **cube root of the charge weight**. Mathematically, this is derived from the requirement that the **energy density at the scaled point must remain invariant**. Let me show this derivation explicitly in Section 3. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **only reference you need** for crater numerical problems in PESFT753. Memorize the units and the meaning of each constant. | # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R = K \cdot W^{1/3}$ | $R$ = crater radius (m); $W$ = charge mass (kg); $K$ = rock-explosive constant (m/kg$^{1/3}$) | Predict radius for a given charge | | 2 | $D = C \cdot W^{1/3}$ | $D$ = crater depth (m); $C$ = depth constant | Predict crater depth | | 3 | $V = K_V \cdot W$ | $V$ = crater volume (m³); $K_V$ = volume constant (m³/kg) | Estimate bulk earthwork | | 4 | $ODB = n_0 \cdot Q^{1/3}$ | $n_0$ = optimum depth factor; $Q$ = TNT-equivalent mass | Design charge depth for max volume | | 5 | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$ | Geometric similarity ratio | Scale up/down between charges | | 6 | $DOB = d \cdot R_t$ | $d$ = dimensionless burial factor (typical 0.5–2.0) | Relate depth to crater radius | | 7 | $E_{released} = W \cdot Q_e$ | $Q_e$ = specific energy (J/kg), TNT ≈ **4.184 × 10⁶ J/kg** | Convert mass to energy | | 8 | $\eta_{crater} = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}$ | $V_c$ = crater volume; $V_s$ = volume of sphere of radius $R_t$ | Cratering efficiency | > [!TIP] > **Typical K-board values of constants (must be used as given in problem data):** > - $K$ (sand): **0.4 to 0.6** m/kg$^{1/3}$ > - $K$ (soft rock): **0.25 to 0.40** m/kg$^{1/3}$ > - $K$ (hard rock): **0.10 to 0.25** m/kg$^{1/3}$ > - $C$ (depth constant): typically $0.5 \cdot K$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The cube-root scaling law is used directly in: - **Blast-pattern design** in open-pit mines — predicting **flyrock distance**, **vibration**, and **muckpile geometry**. - **Military breaching calculations** — determining the **row-charge spacing** needed to breach a 4 m wall or anti-tank ditch. - **Trench and ditch blasting** for pipeline and canal construction. - **Demolition engineering** — sizing charges for chimney collapses and foundation removal. - **Safety-zone design** — establishing **exclusion radii** around blast sites. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hopkinson Cube-Root Scaling from Dimensional Analysis **Statement:** Two geometrically similar explosions of the same explosive type in the same medium will produce geometrically similar craters. ### Step 1: Define the crater radius as a function of governing variables The crater radius $R$ depends on: - The mass of explosive charge, $W$ (kg) - The energy released per unit mass, $Q_e$ (J/kg) - The density of the medium, $\rho$ (kg/m³) - The strength of the medium, $\sigma_c$ (Pa) — optional For a first-order scaling analysis with constant $Q_e$ and constant $\rho$ (same explosive, same medium): $$R = f(W)$$ ### Step 2: Apply dimensional homogeneity $R$ has dimensions of length $[\text{L}]$. $W$ has dimensions of mass $[\text{M}]$. Since $R$ cannot depend on $W$ alone dimensionally, we require a reference length scale $L_0$ built from the physical constants of the explosion. ### Step 3: Construct the natural length scale from explosive energy The total energy released by the charge is $E = W \cdot Q_e$. This energy, when distributed over a sphere of density $\rho$ (the medium being displaced), establishes a characteristic radius $L_0$ such that the energy density equals a fixed value (the rupture energy density of the medium): $$\dfrac{W \cdot Q_e}{\tfrac{4}{3}\pi L_0^{3}} = \rho \cdot \epsilon_c$$ where $\epsilon_c$ (J/kg) is the **specific fracture energy** of the medium — a constant for a given rock type. ### Step 4: Solve for the characteristic length Rearranging for $L_0$: $$\dfrac{4}{3}\pi L_0^{3} \cdot \rho \cdot \epsilon_c = W \cdot Q_e$$ $$L_0^{3} = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot Q_e}{4\pi\rho\epsilon_c}$$ $$L_0 = \left(\dfrac{3}{4\pi}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_e}{\rho\epsilon_c}\right)^{1/3} \cdot W^{1/3}$$ ### Step 5: Recognize the cube-root dependence For a given explosive ($Q_e$ fixed) and a given medium ($\rho, \epsilon_c$ fixed), all bracketed terms collapse into a single constant $K$: $$\boxed{L_0 = K \cdot W^{1/3}}$$ Since the crater radius must be proportional to this characteristic length scale (geometric similarity requirement): $$\boxed{R = K \cdot W^{1/3}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **Hopkinson-Berthelot cube-root law**. It applies strictly only when the **same explosive** and the **same medium** are used and when the charge is placed at **scaled geometric depth** (i.e., $DOB/R$ = constant). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crater Volume A crater is conventionally modeled as a **paraboloid of revolution** for engineering calculations. ### Step 1: State the paraboloid volume formula For a paraboloid with radius $R$ and depth $D$: $$V = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi R^{2} D$$ ### Step 2: Substitute the cube-root scaling for both $R$ and $D$ Using $R = K \cdot W^{1/3}$ and $D = C \cdot W^{1/3}$: $$V = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi (K \cdot W^{1/3})^{2} \cdot (C \cdot W^{1/3})$$ ### Step 3: Simplify $$V = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi K^{2} C \cdot W^{2/3} \cdot W^{1/3}$$ $$V = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi K^{2} C \cdot W^{(2/3 + 1/3)}$$ $$\boxed{V = \dfrac{\pi}{2} K^{2} C \cdot W}$$ ### Step 4: Define the volume constant Let $K_V = \dfrac{\pi}{2} K^{2} C$ — this is a lumped constant depending on explosive and medium. Therefore: $\boxed{V = K_V \cdot W}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **volume scales linearly** with charge mass, while **linear dimensions scale as the cube root** of mass. This asymmetry — area scales as $W^{2/3}$ but volume scales as $W$ — is a recurring KTU exam favorite. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimum Depth of Burial (ODB) The **optimum depth of burial** is the burial depth that produces the maximum crater volume for a given charge. ### Step 1: Define dimensionless scaled depth $$\lambda = \dfrac{DOB}{R_t}$$ where $R_t$ is the true crater radius (which itself scales as $W^{1/3}$). ### Step 2: Empirical result (Cooper & Short, 1990) For most surface-laid and shallow-buried charges, experimental data show: $$\lambda_{opt} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7$$ ### Step 3: Derive the absolute ODB value Since $R_t = K \cdot W^{1/3}$ at optimum conditions: $$\boxed{ODB = n_0 \cdot K \cdot W^{1/3}}$$ or equivalently using the energy form: $$\boxed{ODB = n_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{W \cdot Q_e}{4\pi \rho\epsilon_c / 3}\right)^{1/3}}$$ where $n_0 \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ is the **optimum depth factor**. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A 50 kg charge of TNT-equivalent explosive is detonated at its optimum depth in soft rock. Given $K = 0.35$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ and $C = 0.18$ m/kg$^{1/3}$, determine: (a) Crater radius, (b) Crater depth, (c) Crater volume, (d) Optimum depth of burial if $n_0 = 0.6$. ### Solution: **(a) Crater radius:** $$R = K \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.35 \cdot (50)^{1/3}$$ $$50^{1/3} = 3.684 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$ $$R = 0.35 \cdot 3.684 = 1.289 \text{ m}$$ **(b) Crater depth:** $$D = C \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.18 \cdot 3.684 = 0.663 \text{ m}$$ **(c) Crater volume:** $$V = \dfrac{\pi}{2} K^{2} C \cdot W$$ $$V = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot (0.35)^{2} \cdot 0.18 \cdot 50$$ $$V = \dfrac{3.1416}{2} \cdot 0.1225 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 50$$ $$V = 1.5708 \cdot 1.1025 = 1.732 \text{ m}^{3}$$ **(d) Optimum depth of burial:** $$ODB = n_0 \cdot K \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.6 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 3.684$$ $$ODB = 0.6 \cdot 1.289 = 0.773 \text{ m}$$ > [!TIP] > Notice that $ODB > D$ (crater depth). This is geometrically consistent — the charge sits at optimum depth, but the crater itself is **shallower than the original burial depth** because the rupture boundary is shallower than the charge center. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Scaled Depth $\lambda = DOB/R_t$ | Result | Engineering Interpretation | |---|---|---| | $\lambda < 0.4$ | Blowout — large ejecta, shallow bowl | **Undersized** charge / too shallow | | $\lambda \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ | Maximum crater volume | **Optimum** design point | | $\lambda = 1.0$ | Camouflet — no surface breach | Charge fully buried | | $\lambda > 1.5$ | No crater; only seismic / airblast | Wasted energy underground | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Crater Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture of crater formation**, illustrating the sequential energy-transformation pathway from detonation chemistry to final crater geometry. This serves as a schematic replacement for a physical cross-sectional diagram. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Detonation Initiation
Charge W kg, Depth DOB] --> B[Shock Wave Generation
Pressure Peak 10 to 30 GPa] B --> C[Stress Wave Propagation
Hemispherical Front] C --> D[Free Surface Reflection
Tensile Rarefaction] D --> E[Spall Fracture Zone
Tensile Failure Parallel to Surface] E --> F[Detonation Gas Expansion
3000 to 4000 K, Multi-GPa] F --> G[Thermodynamic P-V Work
Acceleration of Broken Rock] G --> H[Ejecta Launch
Ballistic Trajectory] H --> I[Ejecta Landing
Fallout Zone] G --> J[Crater Bowl Formation
Final Geometry Stabilizes] J --> K[Apparent Crater Ra
True Crater Rt, Depth Da] K --> L[Scaled Output
R equals K times W cuberoot
D equals C times W cuberoot] L --> M{Compare DOB with ODB} M --> N[DOB less than ODB
Blowout Crater] M --> O[DOB equals ODB
Normal Maximum Volume Crater] M --> P[DOB greater than ODB
Camouflet or Deep Crater] N --> Q[Safety Audit
Excess Flyrock Risk] O --> R[Engineering Acceptance
Design Target Achieved] P --> S[Re-burial Adjustment
Reduce DOB to Match ODB] ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Text Representation) ``` FREE SURFACE (Original Ground Level) ─────────────●═══════════════════════●───────────── ║ ▲ Ejecta ▲ ║ ║ | Throw | ║ ║ | Zone | ║ ←———————╨—————————————————╨———————→ Crater Rim APPARENT CRATER Crater Rim (Ra) RADIUS = R_a (Ra) │ ▼ Visible Depression ┌─────────────────────┐ ← Original Surface │ APPARENT │ │ CRATER │ │ (Depth = D_a) │ └─────────┬───────────┘ │ Broken/Fractured Zone ▼ (D_a to D_t) ╔═════════════════════╗ ║ TRUE CRATER ║ ║ Radius = R_t ║ ║ Depth = D_t ║ ╚══════════╤══════════╝ │ ─────────────┼───────────── │ CHARGE (Weight = W) │ DOB = depth of burial ``` ## 4.3 Functional Flow Summary | Stage | Physical Process | Energy Form | Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Detonation | Chemical → Thermal | High-T, High-P gas | | 2 | Stress Wave | Mechanical → Elastic | Stress wave in medium | | 3 | Reflection | Tensile | Spall fractures | | 4 | Gas Expansion | P-V Work | Crater cavity | | 5 | Ejection | Kinetic | Ejecta + muckpile | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define a crater formed by an explosive. Differentiate between an apparent crater and a true crater.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A crater is a depression formed on the ground surface due to the detonation of an explosive charge placed at or near the free surface. **(1 mark)** - **Apparent Crater:** The visible depression measured from the original ground surface to the bottom of the cavity. It is what an observer can directly see and measure after the blast. **(1 mark)** - **True Crater:** The actual boundary of the fractured/broken zone, which extends below the apparent crater and includes material that is fractured but not ejected. The true crater is always **larger** than the apparent crater. **(1 mark)** --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State Hopkinson's cube-root scaling law for crater formation. What is its physical significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hopkinson's cube-root scaling law states that for the **same explosive** and the **same medium**, if two charges are placed at geometrically similar depths, then all corresponding linear dimensions (radius, depth) of the resulting craters are proportional to the **cube root of the charge weight**. **(2 marks)** $$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$$ **Physical significance:** It allows engineers to **scale up or scale down** the dimensions of an experimental charge to predict the crater size of a production charge without conducting the full-scale blast — saving cost, time, and improving safety during blast-design iteration. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive Hopkinson's cube-root scaling law using dimensional analysis. State clearly the assumptions and boundary conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A test blast with 8 kg of explosive at optimum depth in a sandstone formation produces a crater of radius 1.2 m and depth 0.6 m. Estimate the charge weight required to produce a crater of radius 3.0 m in the same formation. Compute the corresponding crater depth and volume. Take the depth-to-radius ratio as constant. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. Same explosive type (constant $Q_e$). 2. Same medium (constant $\rho$ and $\epsilon_c$). 3. Geometric similarity of charge placement (DOB scaled). 4. Energy-density at corresponding scaled points is identical. **Derivation:** The characteristic length scale of the crater must be derived from the **total energy** released and the **energy density** required to fracture the medium. Energy released by charge of mass $W$: $$E = W \cdot Q_e$$ The volume of medium fractured is on the order of $L_0^{3}$, and the energy absorbed per unit mass to fracture is $\epsilon_c$. Energy balance: $$W \cdot Q_e \;\propto\; \rho \cdot L_0^{3} \cdot \epsilon_c$$ Solving for $L_0$: $$L_0 \;\propto\; \left(\dfrac{Q_e}{\rho \cdot \epsilon_c}\right)^{1/3} \cdot W^{1/3}$$ Since $Q_e$, $\rho$, and $\epsilon_c$ are all constants for the same explosive and medium: $$L_0 = K \cdot W^{1/3}$$ And because crater radius scales linearly with $L_0$ (geometric similarity): $$\boxed{R = K \cdot W^{1/3}}$$ This is **Hopkinson's cube-root law**. **(Full marks: 7)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $W_1 = 8$ kg, $R_1 = 1.2$ m, $D_1 = 0.6$ m - $R_2 = 3.0$ m (target) - Same explosive, same medium → cube-root scaling applies. **Step 1 — Find $W_2$ using cube-root law:** $$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\dfrac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$\dfrac{1.2}{3.0} = \left(\dfrac{8}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$0.4 = \left(\dfrac{8}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$$ Cubing both sides: $$0.064 = \dfrac{8}{W_2}$$ $$W_2 = \dfrac{8}{0.064} = 125 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 — Find $D_2$ using constant $D/R$ ratio:** $$\dfrac{D_1}{R_1} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.2} = 0.5$$ $$D_2 = 0.5 \cdot R_2 = 0.5 \cdot 3.0 = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $V_2$ (paraboloid model):** $$V_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} R_2^{2} D_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} (3.0)^{2} (1.5)$$ $$V_2 = \dfrac{3.1416}{2} \cdot 9 \cdot 1.5 = 21.21 \text{ m}^{3}$$ **Verification — Volume ratio should equal $W_2/W_1$:** $$\dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} = \dfrac{125}{8} = 15.625$$ Check: $V_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(1.2)^2(0.6) = 1.358$ m³ → $V_2 = 1.358 \times 15.625 = 21.22$ m³ ✓ **Final Answers:** $W_2 = 125$ kg, $D_2 = 1.5$ m, $V_2 = 21.21$ m³ **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative] (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the three sequential phases of crater formation when an explosive detonates near a free surface. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define "Optimum Depth of Burial" (ODB). A charge of 25 kg is detonated at optimum depth in a soil with constants $K = 0.5$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ and $n_0 = 0.6$. Determine: (i) the optimum depth of burial, (ii) the crater radius, and (iii) the depth of the apparent crater if $C = 0.25$ m/kg$^{1/3}$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) When a charge detonates near a free surface, crater formation proceeds through **three sequential phases:** **Phase 1 — Stress-Wave (Shock) Phase:** The detonation produces a hemispherical shock wave that propagates outward into the medium. When this wave reaches the free surface, it reflects as a **tensile rarefaction wave**. Since rock/soil is **much weaker in tension than in compression**, the reflected wave causes **spalling** — fracture parallel to the free surface. This sets the boundary of what will become the true crater. **(2 marks)** **Phase 2 — Gas-Pressure Expansion Phase:** The high-temperature (3000–4000 K), high-pressure (several GPa) detonation gases expand rapidly in the cavity created by the shock. These gases perform **thermodynamic P-V work** on the fractured medium, accelerating the broken material radially outward. This phase is responsible for the majority of the crater's volume. **(3 marks)** **Phase 3 — Ejection / Vent Phase:** As the cavity enlarges, gases vent to the atmosphere through the developing crater throat. The broken rock follows a ballistic trajectory, falling outside the crater as **ejecta**. Once internal pressure equilibrates with atmospheric pressure, the crater geometry stabilizes into its final form. **(2 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Definition (1 mark):** Optimum Depth of Burial (ODB) is the depth at which a given charge produces the **maximum crater volume**. Beyond this depth, the energy is increasingly wasted on seismic disturbance and rock crushing rather than on crater formation. **Given:** $W = 25$ kg, $K = 0.5$ m/kg$^{1/3}$, $n_0 = 0.6$, $C = 0.25$ m/kg$^{1/3}$. **(i) Optimum Depth of Burial:** $$W^{1/3} = 25^{1/3} = 2.924 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$ $$ODB = n_0 \cdot K \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 2.924 = 0.877 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Crater Radius:** $$R = K \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.5 \cdot 2.924 = 1.462 \text{ m}$$ **(iii) Apparent Crater Depth:** $$D_a = C \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.25 \cdot 2.924 = 0.731 \text{ m}$$ **(6 marks for numerical work)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to cube both sides** when applying $R_1/R_2 = (W_1/W_2)^{1/3}$ — results in $W_2 = 8 \cdot (R_2/R_1)$ instead of $W_2 = 8 \cdot (R_2/R_1)^3$. This single error loses **3–4 marks** instantly. > > 2. **Conflating apparent and true crater dimensions** — when a problem specifies "crater radius" without qualifier, check whether the value of $K$ given applies to apparent or true radius. Mixing them up is a **2-mark deduction**. > > 3. **Using wrong constants** — Students often use $C = K$ (i.e., assume $D = R$). The depth constant $C$ is **independent** of $K$ and typically $C \approx 0.5K$ in soft rock but can range widely. > > 4. **Skipping units** — Always write **m, m², m³, kg, J** explicitly. KTU examiners routinely deduct **½ mark** for missing units. > > 5. **Not stating assumptions** — In derivation problems, omitting the assumption "same explosive, same medium, geometric similarity" costs **1 mark** even if the math is correct. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist — Craters from Explosives** - [x] A **crater** is the depression left after detonation; it has both **apparent** (visible) and **true** (full fractured) boundaries. - [x] Crater formation occurs in **three phases**: stress-wave → gas-expansion → ejection/vent. - [x] **Hopkinson's cube-root law**: $R = K \cdot W^{1/3}$ — the **single most important formula** for PESFT753 crater problems. - [x] **Volume scales linearly** with charge weight: $V = K_V \cdot W$. - [x] **Linear dimensions scale as the cube root** of charge weight — area scales as $W^{2/3}$. - [x] **Optimum Depth of Burial (ODB)** gives the **maximum crater volume**; typical scaled depth $\lambda \approx 0.5$–$0.7$. - [x] **Sub-ODB** → blowout crater; **Super-ODB** → camouflet or no surface breach. - [x] Paraboloid model: $V = \frac{\pi}{2} R^{2} D$ — standard for engineering calculations. - [x] Constants $K$ and $C$ depend on **both explosive type and rock type** — never use one rock's constants for another. - [x] **TNT reference energy** = **4.184 × 10⁶ J/kg**. - [x] Cube-root scaling is **valid only under geometric similarity** — same explosive, same medium, scaled depth. - [x] For practical problems, always **state the assumptions** before applying the law. --- **END OF NOTES — Craters from Explosives | PESFT753 Module 3**

topics.title: Conical Shaped Chargers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conical Shaped Chargers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Conical Shaped Charge (CSC)** is a specialized class of focused-explosive device in which a high-explosive charge is geometrically configured with a hollow, axisymmetric **conical cavity (liner)** at one end, typically formed from a ductile metal (copper, aluminium, or tantalum). Upon axial detonation from the opposite (apex) end, the collapsing conical liner undergoes symmetric implosion along the charge axis, producing an extremely high-velocity, hyperplastic metallic **jet** and a slower-moving residual **slug**, in accordance with the **Munroe–Neumann Effect**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Munroe–Neumann Effect (1888 / 1910):** A hollow cavity in a detonating explosive charge collapses inward, and if the cavity is lined with a ductile metal, the convergence of detonation products converts the metal into a high-velocity projectile (jet), capable of perforating targets far thicker than the charge's own diameter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine squeezing a wet toothpaste tube from the bottom while pointing its nozzle at a wall. The tube (the **conical metal liner**) collapses inward and the paste squirts out as a thin, fast jet. In a shaped charge, the **detonation wave** plays the role of the squeezing hand, the **metal cone** is the toothpaste tube, and the **jet** is the high-velocity stream — but travelling at **6,000–10,000 m/s**, easily piercing thick steel armour.

### 1.3 Key Geometric & Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cone Half-Angle (α)** | 15° – 60° | Smaller α → faster, thinner jet (penetrator); larger α → slower, massive jet (cutter) |
| **Charge Diameter (D)** | 25 – 300 mm | Larger D → higher absolute penetration |
| **Stand-off Distance (S)** | 1D – 3D | Optimal S ≈ 1.5D – 2D for most military cones |
| **Liner Material** | Copper (most common), Aluminium, Tantalum, Tungsten | Density governs penetration performance |
| **Detonation Velocity (VOD)** | 6,000 – 9,000 m/s | Drives collapse velocity |
| **Jet Tip Velocity (V_j)** | 6,000 – 12,000 m/s | Primary penetrator |
| **Slug Velocity (V_s)** | 500 – 3,000 m/s | Slow trailing mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Penetration depth for an ideal conical charge is empirically reported as **P ≈ 1.0 to 1.5 × D** for steel targets and up to **6–8 × D** under ideal hydrodynamics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initiation:** A detonator at the rear (booster end) initiates the main explosive charge.
2. **Detonation Wave Propagation:** A spherical/plane detonation wave travels forward through the charge at velocity **D** (detonation velocity).
3. **Liner Collapse:** The wave impinges on the conical liner surface. Each elemental ring of the liner is accelerated inward (implodes) along the local normal.
4. **Jet–Slug Bifurcation (Birkhoff Mechanism):** The collapsed liner material divides at a stagnation point into two streams:
   - An **inner, faster jet** that flows along the axis.
   - An **outer, slower slug** that converges at the rear.
5. **Jet Stretching & Particle Velocity Gradient:** Different parts of the jet have different velocities (tip fastest, tail slowest), causing the jet to **stretch** and accumulate a long, slender penetrator.
6. **Target Impact:** The hyperplastic jet strikes the target at hypervelocity, behaving hydrodynamically (Bernoulli-like pressure ≈ ½ρV²), and perforates.
7. **Slug Follow-up:** The slower slug follows, contributing little to penetration but consuming the liner mass.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Accessible Reasoning

- **Why a cone?** A conical geometry maximises symmetric convergence of the detonation products onto a single line (the axis). The convergence amplifies the energy density, in the same way a magnifying glass focuses sunlight.
- **Why copper?** Copper has an excellent combination of **density (8.96 g/cm³), ductility, and sonic velocity**, making it the most efficient liner for jet formation.
- **Why stand-off?** Too close → the jet hasn't fully stretched (short jet, poor penetration). Too far → the jet breaks up due to hydrodynamic instabilities (Rayleigh–Taylor / RAE break-up). Optimal stand-off is **~1.5–2 charge diameters**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_0 = \dfrac{2D}{1 + \cos\alpha}$ | $V_0$ = collapse velocity, $D$ = VOD, $\alpha$ = cone half-angle | Liner collapse kinematics |
| 2 | $V_j = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)}$ | $V_j$ = jet velocity, $\beta$ = jet angle after collapse | Jet tip velocity |
| 3 | $V_s = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)}$ | $V_s$ = slug velocity | Slug velocity |
| 4 | $P = L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_T}}$ | $P$ = penetration depth, $L$ = effective jet length, $\rho_L$, $\rho_T$ = liner & target densities | Hydrodynamic penetration (Birkhoff) |
| 5 | $P = L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_T}} \cdot \cos\phi$ | $\phi$ = jet impact obliquity (jet-target angle) | Oblique impact correction |
| 6 | $L_{eff} \approx (V_j - V_t) \cdot \tau$ | $\tau$ = effective jet stretch time | Effective jet length |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{jet}}{m_{liner}} = 0.5 \cdot \tan(\alpha/2)$ | $\eta$ = jet mass fraction | Liner mass efficiency |
| 8 | $V_t = \dfrac{V_j}{1 + \sqrt{\rho_T / \rho_L}}$ | $V_t$ = target penetration velocity | Penetration rate |
| 9 | $S_{opt} \approx 1.5 D_c$ | $S_{opt}$ = optimum stand-off, $D_c$ = charge diameter | Experimental optimum |
| 10 | $P_{max} \approx k \cdot D_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_T}\right)^{0.5}$ | $k$ = empirical constant (≈ 1.0 – 1.5) | Engineering estimate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In LaTeX table rows, absolute-value bars are deliberately written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defence:** Anti-tank warheads (HEAT), torpedoes, mines.
- **Oil & Gas:** Perforating guns for wellbore completion (creating flow paths through casing and rock).
- **Demolition:** Cutting steel beams, reinforced concrete, ship hulls, and bridge cables in controlled explosive demolition.
- **Mining & Quarrying:** Secondary rock breaking.
- **Aerospace:** Stage separation systems (linear / conical cutting charges).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Jet Velocity and Slug Velocity (Birkhoff's Steady-State Model)

The steady-state, two-dimensional model of Birkhoff et al. (1948) is the classical analytical foundation for conical shaped charges. The liner collapses inward with velocity $V_0$ normal to the original liner surface. After collision at the axis, the material divides into two streams that move off symmetrically at angles $\pm \beta/2$ to the axis.

**Step 1 — Mass Conservation at the Stagnation Point**

The mass flux entering the stagnation point per unit time equals the sum of mass fluxes leaving as jet and slug.

$$\rho_L \cdot V_0 \cdot \cos(\alpha/2) = \rho_L \cdot V_j \cdot \sin(\beta/2) + \rho_L \cdot V_s \cdot \cos(\beta/2)$$

**Step 2 — Momentum Conservation (Tangential Direction)**

Because the steady-state flow has no net tangential momentum, the tangential components of the inflow and outflow must balance. The inflow makes an angle $\alpha/2$ with the axis; the jet and slug depart at $\pm\beta/2$.

$$V_0 \cdot \sin(\alpha/2) = V_j \cdot \cos(\beta/2) - V_s \cdot \sin(\beta/2)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by Introducing Ratio $k = V_s / V_j$**

Let $k = V_s / V_j$. Dividing Step 1 by Step 2 eliminates the trigonometric clutter and yields:

$$\dfrac{\cos(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)} = \dfrac{\sin(\beta/2) + k \cdot \cos(\beta/2)}{\cos(\beta/2) - k \cdot \sin(\beta/2)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $k$**

$$\cot(\alpha/2) \cdot [\cos(\beta/2) - k \cdot \sin(\beta/2)] = \sin(\beta/2) + k \cdot \cos(\beta/2)$$

Rearranging terms to isolate $k$:

$$k = \dfrac{\cos(\beta/2) \cdot \cot(\alpha/2) - \sin(\beta/2)}{\cos(\beta/2) + \sin(\beta/2) \cdot \cot(\alpha/2)}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $\tan(\alpha/2)$**

$$k = \dfrac{\cos(\beta/2) - \sin(\beta/2)\tan(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\beta/2)\tan(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)\tan(\alpha/2)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\tan(\alpha/2)}$$

After algebraic simplification using $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$ and $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$k = \dfrac{\cos[(\alpha + \beta)/2]}{\sin[(\alpha + \beta)/2]} = \cot\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha + \beta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_j$ and $V_s$**

Using $k = V_s/V_j = \cot[(\alpha+\beta)/2]$ in the momentum equation and applying the trigonometric identity, we obtain the celebrated Birkhoff equations:

$$V_j = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)}$$

$$V_s = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)}$$

**Step 7 — Collapse Velocity $V_0$ (Gurney Approximation)**

The velocity imparted to the liner by the detonation products is given by the **Gurney equation** for a cylindrical/conical charge:

$$V_0 = \sqrt{2E} \cdot \left[\dfrac{M/C}{1 + (M/2C)}\right]^{1/2}$$

where $M$ is the liner mass, $C$ is the explosive mass, and $\sqrt{2E}$ is the **Gurney constant** of the explosive (typical values: **RDX ≈ 2.93 km/s, TNT ≈ 2.44 km/s, Composition B ≈ 2.70 km/s, HMX ≈ 2.97 km/s**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic recap:** We enforced conservation of mass and tangential momentum at the steady-state collapse point. The introduction of the parameter $k = V_s/V_j$ reduces two equations to one solvable ratio, after which back-substitution yields the jet and slug velocities explicitly in terms of geometry ($\alpha, \beta$) and loading ($V_0$).

### 3.2 Derivation of Penetration Depth (Hydrodynamic Theory)

Once the jet strikes the target, both materials behave as **incompressible, inviscid fluids** (valid for impact velocities > 2,000 m/s). Bernoulli's equation along the stagnation streamline gives the dynamic pressure:

$$P_{stag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_L\,V_{rel}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho_T\,V_t^2$$

where $V_{rel} = V_j - V_t$ is the relative jet–target velocity and $V_t$ is the penetration rate into the target.

**Step 1 — Pressure Equivalence**

Equating jet-side and target-side stagnation pressures:

$$\rho_L (V_j - V_t)^2 = \rho_T V_t^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_t$**

$$\dfrac{V_j - V_t}{V_t} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_T}{\rho_L}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_t = \dfrac{V_j}{1 + \sqrt{\rho_T/\rho_L}}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to Get Depth**

The jet continues to penetrate until it is fully consumed. The effective jet length is $L_{eff}$. Penetration depth is therefore:

$$P = \int_0^{\tau} V_t \, dt = V_t \cdot \tau = L_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{V_t}{V_j - V_t}$$

Substituting $V_t$ and simplifying with the relation $\sqrt{\rho_T/\rho_L} = (V_j - V_t)/V_t$:

$$P = L_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_T}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Penetration Equation:** This is the most important KTU formula. It states that penetration depth depends **only on the effective jet length and the square root of the density ratio** — not on the jet velocity itself, the explosive type, or the charge size (for the same jet length). This is why heavier liners (tungsten, tantalum) outperform lighter ones for the same geometry.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Cone Half-Angle α

| Cone Half-Angle α | Jet Velocity (relative) | Jet Mass | Slug Mass | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 15° – 25° | Very High | Low | High | Deep penetration (HEAT) |
| 30° – 45° | High | Moderate | Moderate | General military / oil perforation |
| 55° – 70° | Moderate | High | Low | Cutting (linear shape charges) |
| 80° – 90° | Low | Very High | Very Low | Sheet metal cutting, demolition |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A conical shaped charge has a cone half-angle $\alpha = 30°$, VOD = 8,000 m/s, copper liner ($\rho_L = 8{,}960 \,\text{kg/m}^3$), and jet angle after collapse $\beta = 14°$. The target is mild steel ($\rho_T = 7{,}850 \,\text{kg/m}^3$). Find (a) collapse velocity $V_0$, (b) jet velocity $V_j$, (c) slug velocity $V_s$, and (d) penetration depth for an effective jet length of 100 mm.

**Solution:**

**(a) Collapse velocity:**

$$V_0 = \dfrac{2D}{1 + \cos\alpha} = \dfrac{2 \times 8{,}000}{1 + \cos 30°} = \dfrac{16{,}000}{1 + 0.8660} = \dfrac{16{,}000}{1.8660}$$

$$V_0 \approx 8{,}574 \,\text{m/s}$$

**(b) Jet velocity:**

$$V_j = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)} = 8{,}574 \cdot \dfrac{\cos 7°}{\sin 15° + \cos 7°}$$

$$V_j = 8{,}574 \cdot \dfrac{0.9925}{0.2588 + 0.9925} = 8{,}574 \cdot \dfrac{0.9925}{1.2513}$$

$$V_j \approx 6{,}800 \,\text{m/s}$$

**(c) Slug velocity:**

$$V_s = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)} = 8{,}574 \cdot \dfrac{0.1219}{1.2513}$$

$$V_s \approx 835 \,\text{m/s}$$

**(d) Penetration depth:**

$$P = L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_T}} = 0.100 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8{,}960}{7{,}850}} = 0.100 \cdot \sqrt{1.1414}$$

$$P = 0.100 \times 1.0684 \approx 0.1068 \,\text{m} \approx 107 \,\text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic recap:** A charge of only ~80 mm diameter is theoretically capable of penetrating ~107 mm of steel — a penetration-to-diameter ratio of **P/D ≈ 1.34**, consistent with empirical military HEAT performance data.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Anatomy of a Conical Shaped Charge (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detonator / Booster] --> B[Main Explosive Charge]
    B --> C[Detonation Wave Front]
    C --> D[Conical Metal Liner]
    D --> E[Stagnation Point on Axis]
    E --> F[Hyperplastic Jet]
    E --> G[Residual Slug]
    F --> H[Target]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Penetration Channel]
    F --> J[Stand-off Air Gap]
    J --> F
```

### 4.2 Jet Formation Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step1 Initiation by Detonator] --> P2[Step2 Detonation Wave Travels Forward]
    P2 --> P3[Step3 Liner Ring Elements Implode Inward]
    P3 --> P4[Step4 Convergence on Stagnation Point]
    P4 --> P5[Step5 Jet Slug Bifurcation]
    P5 --> P6[Step6 Jet Stretches and Accelerates]
    P6 --> P7[Step7 Target Impact and Perforation]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Parameter Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Cone Half-Angle alpha] --> V0[Collapse Velocity V0]
    A1[Detonation Velocity D] --> V0
    V0 --> VJ[Jet Velocity Vj]
    A0 --> VJ
    B0[Effective Jet Length L] --> PEN[Penetration Depth P]
    C0[Liner Density rhoL] --> PEN
    D0[Target Density rhoT] --> PEN
    E0[Stand-off S] --> BR[Jet Break-up or Incomplete Stretch]
    BR --> PEN
```

### 4.4 Schematic Geometry Reference (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Charge Apex] --> BODY[Main Explosive Body]
    BODY --> LINER[Conical Liner]
    LINER --> AXIS[Charge Axis / Line of Sight]
    LINER --> STOFF[Stand-off Air Gap]
    STOFF --> TGT[Target Surface]
    AXIS --> TGT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale:** Mermaid is a text-based graph engine. Physical 3D drawings of cones and jets are difficult to render in Mermaid, so the diagrams above depict the **functional architecture and information flow** — the interactions, energy flow, and parameter dependencies — which is equally valuable for KTU board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define the Munroe–Neumann Effect and state its significance in shaped charge design.**

**Model Answer:**
The Munroe–Neumann Effect (1888/1910) states that a hollow cavity in a detonating high-explosive charge focuses the detonation products inward, producing highly localised destructive action. If the cavity is lined with a ductile metal, the collapse of the liner generates a hypervelocity metallic **jet** capable of penetrating targets far thicker than the charge diameter. **Significance:** It is the foundational principle of all shaped-charge devices used in anti-tank warheads, oil-well perforators, and demolition cutters, enabling penetration of 1–8 charge diameters of steel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the hydrodynamic penetration equation and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$P = L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_T}}$$

where $P$ = penetration depth, $L$ = effective stretched jet length, $\rho_L$ = density of the liner material, $\rho_T$ = density of the target material. The equation implies that **penetration depends only on the effective jet length and the square root of the density ratio**, and is independent of the jet velocity or charge size (for a given jet length). It justifies the use of high-density liners (copper, tantalum, tungsten) for superior penetration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a conical shaped charge. Explain the role of the cone half-angle, liner material, and stand-off distance.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A conical shaped charge has the following specifications: cone half-angle $\alpha = 42°$, detonation velocity $D = 7{,}500$ m/s, jet angle after collapse $\beta = 10°$, copper liner, mild steel target. Calculate: (i) collapse velocity, (ii) jet velocity, (iii) slug velocity, and (iv) maximum theoretical penetration depth for a 120 mm effective jet length.

#### Model Solution (a):

- **Construction:** A conical shaped charge consists of:
  1. A **detonator / booster** at the rear.
  2. A **main high-explosive charge** (RDX, HMX, Composition B, etc.) shaped as a cylinder.
  3. A **conical metal liner** at the forward end (apex pointing away from target).
  4. A **stand-off air gap** between charge and target.
  5. **Casing** (steel or aluminium) for structural integrity.

- **Working:** Detonation → wave propagation → liner collapse → jet/slug formation → jet stretch → target impact → hydrodynamic penetration.

- **Role of cone half-angle:** Smaller α → faster, thinner, longer jet → deeper penetration. Larger α → slower, massive jet → better for cutting. Typical HEAT warheads use α ≈ 30°–45°.

- **Role of liner material:** Determines $\rho_L$ and ductility. Copper (8.96 g/cm³) is standard; tantalum (16.6 g/cm³) and tungsten (19.3 g/cm³) yield deeper penetration. Aluminium is used for large cutting charges.

- **Role of stand-off distance:** Required for jet stretching. Too small → incomplete stretching. Too large → jet break-up due to hydrodynamic instabilities. Optimum ≈ 1.5–2 charge diameters.

#### Model Solution (b):

**(i) Collapse velocity:**

$$V_0 = \dfrac{2D}{1 + \cos\alpha} = \dfrac{2 \times 7{,}500}{1 + \cos 42°} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{1 + 0.7431} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{1.7431} \approx 8{,}605 \,\text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Jet velocity:**

$$V_j = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)} = 8{,}605 \cdot \dfrac{\cos 5°}{\sin 21° + \cos 5°}$$

$$V_j = 8{,}605 \cdot \dfrac{0.9962}{0.3584 + 0.9962} = 8{,}605 \cdot \dfrac{0.9962}{1.3546} \approx 6{,}327 \,\text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Slug velocity:**

$$V_s = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)} = 8{,}605 \cdot \dfrac{0.0872}{1.3546} \approx 554 \,\text{m/s}$$

**(iv) Penetration depth:**

For copper, $\rho_L = 8{,}960 \,\text{kg/m}^3$; for mild steel, $\rho_T = 7{,}850 \,\text{kg/m}^3$.

$$P = L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_T}} = 0.120 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8{,}960}{7{,}850}} = 0.120 \times 1.0684 \approx 0.1282 \,\text{m} \approx 128 \,\text{mm}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Birkhoff equations for jet velocity and slug velocity in a conical shaped charge using conservation of mass and momentum at the stagnation point.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Munroe–Neumann effect. Discuss how the cone half-angle influences (i) jet velocity, (ii) jet mass, and (iii) the application of the shaped charge. Provide one example for each extreme case.

#### Model Solution (a):

(See Section 3.1 above for the **complete, step-by-step derivation**.)

Final results:

$$V_j = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)} \quad ; \quad V_s = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\beta/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)}$$

#### Model Solution (b):

- **Munroe–Neumann Effect:** (Definition as in Q1 of Part A — 2 marks)

- **(i) Effect on jet velocity:** As α decreases, $\sin(\alpha/2)$ decreases, so the denominator decreases, hence **$V_j$ increases** (faster jet).

- **(ii) Effect on jet mass:** Jet mass fraction $\eta = 0.5 \tan(\alpha/2)$. As α decreases, $\eta$ decreases (less mass in jet, more in slug). As α increases, jet mass increases.

- **(iii) Applications:**

| Cone Half-Angle | Application | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 15°–25° (small α) | Deep penetration | HEAT anti-tank warhead, oil-well perforator |
| 30°–45° (medium α) | General purpose | Military shaped charges, demolition |
| 60°–90° (large α) | Cutting | Linear cutting charges for ship hulls, bridge cables |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "cone half-angle" with "full cone angle"** — α is the **half**-angle; if the question gives "60° cone," it means α = 30°. This single mistake cascades through $V_0, V_j, V_s$ and costs **3–5 marks** easily.
> 2. **Mixing up jet velocity and slug velocity** — always use $\cos(\beta/2)$ in the numerator for $V_j$ and $\sin(\beta/2)$ for $V_s$. Mixing these gives incorrect answers.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — VOD in m/s, density in kg/m³, length in m. Final penetration in **metres** — convert to mm at the end.
> 4. **Neglecting the hydrodynamic assumption** — the penetration equation is valid only when the jet velocity > ~2,000 m/s. Below this, the materials no longer behave as inviscid fluids and the formula overestimates.
> 5. **Missing the "stand-off" concept** — many students describe the working of a shaped charge but fail to mention stand-off distance and its role in jet stretching. KTU examiners frequently award a **dedicated 1–2 mark** for this.
> 6. **Using absolute value bars `|x|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX-formatted tables.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Bulletins:**

- ✅ **Munroe–Neumann Effect** = the foundational principle; cavity collapse → jet formation.
- ✅ **Conical shaped charge** uses a **conical ductile metal liner** (Cu, Al, Ta) for symmetric jet formation.
- ✅ **Three main parts** of a CSC: explosive body + conical liner + stand-off air gap.
- ✅ **Collapse velocity:** $V_0 = 2D / (1 + \cos\alpha)$.
- ✅ **Jet velocity:** $V_j = V_0 \cdot \cos(\beta/2) / [\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)]$.
- ✅ **Slug velocity:** $V_s = V_0 \cdot \sin(\beta/2) / [\sin(\alpha/2) + \cos(\beta/2)]$.
- ✅ **Penetration depth:** $P = L \cdot \sqrt{\rho_L / \rho_T}$ (Birkhoff hydrodynamic model).
- ✅ **Penetration depends on jet length and density ratio — NOT on jet velocity directly.**
- ✅ **Smaller α → faster, thinner jet → deep penetration (HEAT).**
- ✅ **Larger α → slower, massive jet → cutting (demolition).**
- ✅ **Optimum stand-off ≈ 1.5 – 2 × charge diameter.**
- ✅ **Copper** is the most common liner; **tantalum/tungsten** give deeper penetration; **aluminium** is used for large cutting charges.
- ✅ **Gurney constant** ($\sqrt{2E}$) provides collapse velocity from explosive energy.
- ✅ **Jet mass fraction:** $\eta = 0.5 \tan(\alpha/2)$.
- ✅ **Jet break-up** at excessive stand-off is due to hydrodynamic instabilities (Rayleigh–Taylor / RAE).
- ✅ **Applications:** HEAT warheads, oil-well perforation, ship/bridge demolition, mining, aerospace stage separation.
- ✅ **Numerical units:** VOD in m/s, density in kg/m³, length in m, output in metres (convert to mm).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Off-the-Shelf Explosive Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Off-the-Shelf Explosive Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Off-the-Shelf Explosive Device (OSED)** is a *pre-manufactured, commercially produced, factory-certified explosive tool or assembly that is legally procured, transported, stored, and deployed* in mining, quarrying, civil demolition, tunneling, well-shooting, forestry, ordnance disposal, and military engineering operations. These devices are *standardized* in geometry, energy output, sensitivity, and packaging, and they are certified by statutory bodies such as the **Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO), India** and conform to **IS 6609**, **IS 7381**, and **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods** classifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** Off-the-shelf devices (OSED) must NEVER be confused with **Improvised Explosive Devices (IEDs)**. OSEDs are legally manufactured, labelled, batch-tested, and accompanied by a chain-of-custody and Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS). IEDs are constructed from diverted or illicit materials and carry severe legal consequences under the **Explosives Act, 1884** (India) and the **Arms Act, 1959**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an off-the-shelf explosive device the way you think of a **pharmaceutical tablet bought from a chemist**:

- A *tablet of paracetamol* (Calpol, Crocin) is **pre-made, dose-fixed, factory-sealed, and approved by a regulator** (DCGI/FDA). You do not need to synthesize the molecule.
- An *off-the-shelf explosive* is exactly that — a pre-engineered, dose-controlled, regulator-approved "energetic tablet" that a blaster inserts into a borehole or attaches to a target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Anchor for First-Time Learners:** Just as a doctor calculates the *milligrams per kilogram of body weight* before prescribing a tablet, a blaster engineer calculates the *grams of explosive per cubic metre of rock* (powder factor) before firing a blast. The *device is the dosage*, and the *engineer is the prescriber*.

### 1.3 Standard Physical & Regulatory Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Commercial detonator nominal strength | No. 6 to No. 8 strength | IS 6609 : 2018 |
| Detonating cord (PETN core) | **5.3 g/m to 80 g/m** linear charge | IS 9038 |
| Boosters (Pentolite/PETN/TNT) | **50 g, 100 g, 200 g, 400 g, 800 g** | IS 14362 |
| Slurry / Emulsion bulk density | **0.8 to 1.45 g/cm³** | Manufacturer datasheet |
| Magazine storage separation | **Not less than 100 m** from inhabited buildings | Explosives Rules, 2008 (Rule 84) |
| Static discharge sensitivity threshold | **< 0.025 J** for primary explosives | UN Test Series 3 (a) |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Off-the-shelf explosive devices constitute the **most frequently asked Module 3 topic** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) papers because:

1. They form the **practical operational core** of any mining/civil blasting engineer.
2. Misidentification leads to catastrophic loss — examiners reward *precision of nomenclature*.
3. The topic bridges **chemistry (energetic materials), physics (detonation), and safety regulation (PESO/UN)** — making it ideal for **CO3 / CO4 (Apply / Analyze)** level questions.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic — Structured Bulleted Breakdown

Off-the-shelf explosive devices are categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

**Axis A — Function in the Explosive Train:**

- **Initiators (Primary Explosives):** Lead Azide $\text{Pb(N}_3\text{)}_2$, Lead Styphnate, Mercury Fulminate $\text{Hg(CNO)}_2$ — extremely sensitive, low brisance, used *only* in detonator caps.
- **Booster (Secondary Explosives):** PETN, RDX, Pentolite (50/50 PETN/TNT) — moderate sensitivity, high brisance, transfer detonation from initiator to main charge.
