- **Main Charge (Secondary / Tertiary):** ANFO, Emulsions, Slurries, TNT — least sensitive, highest energy, bulk demolition work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** This is the *electrical engineering hierarchy* — a logic gate (initiator) triggers a driver transistor (booster) which switches a power MOSFET (main charge).

**Axis B — Physical State of the Energetic Material:**

- **Solid Cast / Pressed:** TNT, Pentolite, Comp-B (RDX/TNT), HMX-based PBXs.
- **Granular / Prilled:** ANFO (94% Ammonium Nitrate + 6% Fuel Oil).
- **Slurry / Water-Gel:** Aluminium-sensitized slurries with density 1.10 – 1.45 g/cm³.
- **Emulsion (Water-in-Oil):** Micro-droplet AN solution in oil matrix, density 1.15 – 1.30 g/cm³.

**Axis C — Device Geometry & Operational Role:**

- **Detonators** (Instantaneous / Short-Delay / Long-Delay)
- **Detonating Cord (Detacord / Primacord)**
- **Safety Fuse / Bickford Fuse**
- **Boosters / Pentolite Pellets**
- **Shaped Charges (Linear / Conical)**
- **Cartridge Emulsions (Rheem-pack / Vel-Gel)**
- **Cast Boosters / Cast TNT Blocks**
- **Commercial Blasting Caps (Electric / Non-Electric / Electronic)**
- **Detonating Relays / Connectors**

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **KTU 2024 ESE gold-standard formulas** for this topic. Memorize the *units* and the *physical meaning*, not just the symbol.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detonation Velocity (Chapman-Jouguet) | $D = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q_e}$ | $D$ = m/s, $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats, $Q_e$ = heat of detonation J/kg | Predicts VOD of ideal explosive; basis of brisance calculation |
| 2 | Brisance Index (Kast Formula) | $B = \rho \cdot D^2 \times 10^{-6}$ | $B$ = brisance number, $\rho$ = density g/cm³, $D$ = VOD m/s | Measures shattering ability; $\vert B \vert > 1.2$ for military HE |
| 3 | Detonation Pressure | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot D^2}{2(\gamma + 1)}$ | $P_{CJ}$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $D$ in m/s | Pressure at shock front — drives rock fragmentation |
| 4 | Hopkinson-Cranz Blast Scaling | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W^{1/3}}$ | $Z$ = scaled distance m/kg$^{1/3}$, $R$ = standoff m, $W$ = TNT-eq mass kg | Predicts safe standoff for a given charge |
| 5 | Gurney Equation (Fragment Velocity) | $V_0 = \sqrt{2E} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M/C}{1 + 0.5 \cdot (M/C)}}$ | $V_0$ = m/s, $E$ = Gurney energy J/kg, $M/C$ = mass ratio | Predicts lethal fragment throw for casing |
| 6 | TNT Equivalence | $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot W_{HE}$ | $\eta$ = relative effectiveness factor, $W$ = kg | Standardizes blast yield reporting |
| 7 | Powder Factor | $PF = \dfrac{W}{V}$ | $PF$ = kg/m³, $W$ = charge mass kg, $V$ = rock volume m³ | Optimizes blast design |
| 8 | Stemming Length Rule | $T_{stem} \geq 0.7 \cdot B_{hole}$ | $T_{stem}$ in m, $B_{hole}$ = burden m | Prevents premature gas venting |
| 9 | Primer-Base Charge Rule | $L_{primer} \geq 0.5 \cdot L_{column}$ | $L$ in m | Ensures positive initiation in column charge |
| 10 | Charge Decoupling Factor | $D_c = \dfrac{\rho_{explosive}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | Dimensionless, 0.3 – 1.0 | Controls peak pressure in smooth blasting |

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of an Explosive Train

The *explosive train* is the **causal sequence** by which a low-energy signal triggers a high-energy output. For a typical off-the-shelf rock-blast:

1. **Blaster pushes blaster's galvanometer** → confirms circuit integrity.
2. **Blasting machine (condenser-discharge / electronic)** → delivers firing pulse of 1.5 – 4.0 J.
3. **Lead-wire to leg-wire** → current reaches the **electric detonator** (No. 8 strength).
4. **Bridge-wire heats** → ignites the **flash charge** (ignition bead of matchhead composition).
5. **Flash ignites the primary charge** (Lead Azide / Lead Styphnate, 0.1 – 0.3 g).
6. **Primary explosive → DDT** → Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition.
7. **Detonation wave enters base charge** (PETN / RDX, 0.5 – 1.0 g) → amplifies to ~ 8,000 m/s.
8. **Base charge detonates the booster / primer cartridge** placed at the borehole bottom.
9. **Booster initiates the main column** of ANFO / Emulsion → 3,000 – 5,500 m/s column detonation.
10. **Detonation gases at 2 – 15 GPa** → fracture the surrounding rock.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why this hierarchy?"** Each stage amplifies the energy by 3 to 4 orders of magnitude while *reducing* the sensitivity of the final stage, so the system as a whole is safe to transport and handle.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mining:** Bench blasting, sublevel caving, drop raise, ring blasting — all depend on properly sequenced OSEDs.
- **Civil Demolition:** Steel-cutting shaped charges (off-the-shelf linear shaped charges, LSC) in building demolition.
- **Oil & Gas:** Perforating guns with shaped charges for wellbore perforation.
- **Defence:** Bangalore torpedoes, breaching charges, anti-tank shaped charges (HEAT).
- **Forensic / EOD:** Commercial OSEDs (e.g., detonators) are used in **render-safe procedures (RSP)** as counter-charges.

</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Detonation Pressure from Conservation Laws

We derive the **C-J (Chapman-Jouguet) pressure** for a steady 1-D detonation using conservation of mass, momentum, and energy across the shock front.

**Step 1:** Mass conservation across the reaction zone:

$$\rho_0 D = \rho_1 (D - u_1)$$

**Step 2:** Momentum conservation (Newton's second law per unit area):

$$P_1 - P_0 = \rho_0 D u_1$$

**Step 3:** Energy conservation (Hugoniot energy equation) — the chemical energy released $Q_e$ is added:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho_1} - \frac{P_0}{\rho_0} = \frac{1}{2}(D - u_1)^2 - \frac{1}{2}D^2 + Q_e$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging using the ideal-gas equation of state $P_1 = \rho_1 \frac{RT_1}{M}$, and applying the **C-J condition** that the flow behind the front is *sonic* relative to the reaction zone ($\gamma$-model assumption), we get:

$$P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical sanity check — for PETN at $\rho_0 = 1.77 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1770 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $D = 8,400 \text{ m/s}$, $\gamma = 3.0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{CJ} &= \frac{1770 \times (8400)^2}{3.0 + 1} \\
&= \frac{1770 \times 7.056 \times 10^7}{4} \\
&= \frac{1.2489 \times 10^{11}}{4} \\
P_{CJ} &= 3.12 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 31.2 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation — the C-J pressure of ~ 31 GPa is **~310,000 atmospheres**, which is the force that fractures competent granite. This is why PETN is the booster of choice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic in Plain English:** Density gives mass per unit volume; VOD² gives the kinetic energy of the moving gas; dividing by $(\gamma+1)$ corrects for the fact that the gases behind the front are also moving forward (not all energy becomes static pressure).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hopkinson-Cranz Scaled Distance

**Step 1:** Define the **cube-root scaling law** for blast wave propagation in free air:

$$P_{peak} = f\!\left(\frac{R}{W^{1/3}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Define the scaled distance:

$$Z = \frac{R}{W^{1/3}} \quad \text{(m / kg}^{1/3}\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** For a **surface blast**, peak overpressure (in kPa) is empirically:

$$P_{peak} = \frac{1772}{Z^{2.75}} \quad \text{kPa}$$

**Step 4:** Worked problem — *A 100 kg TNT-equivalent blast occurs 200 m from a control room. Will a 7 kPa overpressure (window-shatter threshold) be exceeded?*

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 100 \text{ kg} \\
W^{1/3} &= 4.64 \text{ kg}^{1/3} \\
R &= 200 \text{ m} \\
Z &= \frac{200}{4.64} = 43.10 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute peak overpressure:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{peak} &= \frac{1772}{(43.10)^{2.75}} \\
(43.10)^{2.75} &= 43.10^{2} \times 43.10^{0.75} \\
&= 1857.6 \times 17.13 \\
&= 31,820 \\
P_{peak} &= \frac{1772}{31,820} = 0.0557 \text{ kPa} \approx 55.7 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion — at 200 m, the 100 kg charge produces only 56 Pa, well below the 7,000 Pa window-shatter threshold. The control room is **safe**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gurney Velocity for a Cylindrical Charge

**Step 1:** Define Gurney energy $E$ — the kinetic energy per unit mass available to fragments.

**Step 2:** Gurney's energy balance for a cylinder of mass $C$ with fragment mass $M$:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} M V_0^2 + \frac{1}{2} C \cdot \frac{V_0^2}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{M} \cdot \frac{1}{C/M}$$

A simpler form often used:

$$V_0 = \sqrt{2E} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M/C}{1 + 0.5(M/C)}}$$

**Step 3:** Numerical example — *A booster with 200 g of Comp-B ($\sqrt{2E}$ ≈ 2,700 m/s for Comp-B), encased in 80 g of steel. Compute fragment velocity.*

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 0.080 \text{ kg} \\
C &= 0.200 \text{ kg} \\
M/C &= 0.40 \\
V_0 &= 2700 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.40}{1 + 0.5(0.40)}} \\
&= 2700 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.40}{1.20}} \\
&= 2700 \times \sqrt{0.333} \\
&= 2700 \times 0.577 \\
V_0 &\approx 1,559 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation — fragments leave the casing at ~ 1.56 km/s, which is **supersonic in air** (Mach ≈ 4.5). This is the reason behind **secondary fragment hazard zones** in blast design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common OSED Parameters at a Glance

| Device | Energetic Core | Density (g/cm³) | VOD (m/s) | Typical Mass | Brisance B |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No. 8 Detonator | PETN / RDX | 1.55 – 1.65 | 7,000 – 8,000 | 0.5 – 1.0 g | 0.08 – 0.11 |
| Pentolite Booster | 50/50 PETN/TNT | 1.66 | 7,500 | 50 – 800 g | 0.12 – 0.20 |
| Detonating Cord | PETN | 1.50 | 6,500 | 5.3 – 80 g/m | linear |
| ANFO (Prilled) | 94/6 AN/FO | 0.80 | 3,200 – 4,500 | bulk | 0.008 – 0.013 |
| Emulsion (Heavy ANFO blend) | AN emulsion + AN prill | 1.25 | 4,800 – 5,500 | bulk | 0.028 – 0.038 |
| Comp-B | 60/40 RDX/TNT | 1.72 | 8,020 | 200 – 1,000 g | 0.12 – 0.14 |
| Shaped Charge (HEAT, conical) | Octol / HMX | 1.80 | 8,800 | 100 – 500 g | 0.13 – 0.16 |

</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hierarchical Classification of Off-the-Shelf Explosive Devices

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OSED - Off the Shelf Explosive Devices] --> B[By Function in Train]
    A --> C[By Physical State]
    A --> D[By Device Geometry]

    B --> B1[Initiators]
    B --> B2[Boosters]
    B --> B3[Main Charges]

    B1 --> B1a[Lead Azide Capsules]
    B1 --> B1b[Match Head Igniters]
    B1 --> B1c[Mercury Fulminate Caps]

    B2 --> B2a[PETN Pressed Pellets]
    B2 --> B2b[Pentolite Cast Boosters]
    B2 --> B2c[RDX Wax Pressed Boosters]

    B3 --> B3a[ANFO Prilled]
    B3 --> B3b[Emulsion Bulk]
    B3 --> B3c[Slurry Cartridge]
    B3 --> B3d[Cast TNT Blocks]

    C --> C1[Solid Cast or Pressed]
    C --> C2[Granular]
    C --> C3[Slurry Water Gel]
    C --> C4[Emulsion Water in Oil]

    D --> D1[Detonators]
    D --> D2[Detonating Cord]
    D --> D3[Safety Fuse]
    D --> D4[Shaped Charges]
    D --> D5[Cartridge Emulsions]
    D --> D6[Cast Boosters]
    D --> D7[Electronic Detonators]

    D1 --> D1a[Instantaneous]
    D1 --> D1b[Short Delay ms]
    D1 --> D1c[Long Delay s]

    D2 --> D2a[5.3 g per m PETN Core]
    D2 --> D2b[20 g per m PETN Core]
    D2 --> D2c[80 g per m PETN Core]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Typical Explosive Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Firing Pulse 1.5 to 4.0 J] --> cond[Bridge Wire Heats]
    cond --> fl[Flash Charge Ignites]
    fl --> prim[Primary Explosive Lead Azide]
    prim --> ddt[Deflagration to Detonation Transition]
    ddt --> base[Base Charge PETN or RDX]
    base --> boost[Booster Cartridge 200 g Pentolite]
    boost --> main[Main Column ANFO or Emulsion]
    main --> out[Detonation Gas 2 to 15 GPa]
    out --> frag[Rock Fracture and Throw]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Off-the-Shelf Device Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1[Target Material] --> q2{Competent Rock?}
    q2 -- Yes --> q3[Use ANFO with PETN Booster]
    q2 -- No --> q4{Underground Wet?}
    q4 -- Yes --> q5[Use Emulsion Cartridge with Electronic Detonator]
    q4 -- No --> q6[Use Slurry with Detonating Cord Initiation]
    q3 --> q7{Require Smooth Wall?}
    q5 --> q7
    q6 --> q7
    q7 -- Yes --> q8[Decoupled Cartridge with 0.5 kg Burden]
    q7 -- No --> q9[Stiff Burden and High Powder Factor]
```

</br>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define an off-the-shelf explosive device and state two examples used in surface mining.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An off-the-shelf explosive device (OSED) is a pre-manufactured, regulator-certified, factory-sealed explosive product procured legally for mining, civil, or military blasting operations. The device arrives with batch number, MSDS, and standard performance specifications.
*Examples (1 mark each):*
1. **No. 8 strength electric detonator** with base charge of PETN/RDX (used to initiate column charges).
2. **Pentolite cast booster (200 g)** used to amplify detonation from primer to main column of ANFO/emulsion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention the *function in the explosive train* when listing an example. A 3-mark answer that omits the regulatory aspect (PESO, IS standard) loses 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between a primary explosive and a secondary explosive, citing one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Primary Explosive | Secondary Explosive |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | Very high (detonates from flame, spark) | Moderate to low (requires shock/detonator) |
| Function | Initiator only (first element) | Booster or main charge |
| Quantity in device | Sub-gram | Grams to kilograms |
| Example | **Lead Azide $\text{Pb(N}_3\text{)}_2$** (0.1 g in No. 8 cap) | **RDX** (used in Comp-B, boosters) |

*Key Sentence:* The primary explosive is the *spark*; the secondary explosive is the *fuel* — both essential, neither replaceable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Sample Module 3]

**Q.A(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **construction and working of a No. 8 electric detonator** in a typical explosive train. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A No. 8 electric detonator consists of the following components from top to bottom:
1. **Leg wires** (copper, 0.5 mm², 2 m length) — carry firing current.
2. **Bridge wire** (nichrome, 0.04 mm diameter, ~ 1 Ω resistance) — fuses on current.
3. **Ignition bead / matchhead** (a paste of potassium chlorate + lead sulphide + silica + gum arabic) — ignites from bridge heat.
4. **Flash charge** (~ 0.2 g of loose black powder or potassium perchlorate mixture) — generates flame jet.
5. **Primary charge** (~ 0.2 g of Lead Azide) — extremely sensitive; deflagrates then detonates.
6. **Base charge** (~ 0.5 g of PETN) — high brisance, transmits detonation to next stage.
7. **Aluminium shell** (length 28 – 40 mm, diameter 6 – 7 mm) — crimps the leg wires and confines the gases.

**Working (3 marks):**
When a current pulse (~ 1.5 A, 4 ms) from a condenser-discharge blasting machine flows through the leg wires, the bridge wire heats to ~ 600 °C, igniting the matchhead. The flame jet ignites the flash charge, which in turn triggers the **Lead Azide** primary. The Lead Azide undergoes **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)** within ~ 1 mm of travel. The detonation wave enters the PETN base charge, exits the open end of the shell, and initiates the booster cartridge of the main explosive column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** In a written exam, draw a *vertical cross-section* of the detonator, label all 7 components, and show the leg wires entering the crimp.

---

**Q.A(b)** A surface mine uses **ANFO (94% AN + 6% FO)** as the main column charge with a **200 g Pentolite booster** and **No. 8 electric detonators**. Calculate:
*(i) Detonation pressure assuming C-J condition with $\gamma = 3.0$ and VOD = 4,200 m/s at $\rho_0 = 0.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$* *(4 marks)*
*(ii) The brisance index* *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Detonation Pressure (4 marks):**

$$P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 \cdot D^2}{2(\gamma + 1)}$$

Convert units: $\rho_0 = 0.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $D = 4,200 \text{ m/s}$, $\gamma = 3.0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{CJ} &= \frac{850 \times (4200)^2}{2(3.0 + 1)} \\
&= \frac{850 \times 1.764 \times 10^7}{8} \\
&= \frac{1.4994 \times 10^{10}}{8} \\
P_{CJ} &= 1.874 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 1.87 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Brisance Index (3 marks):**

Using Kast's formula $B = \rho \cdot D^2 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
B &= 0.85 \times (4200)^2 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 0.85 \times 1.764 \times 10^7 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 0.85 \times 17.64 \\
B &= 14.99 \approx 15.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A brisance of 15 is typical for ANFO — lower than military HE (B > 80) but sufficient for soft-to-medium rock fragmentation. A value of 1.87 GPa detonation pressure drives the radial crack pattern in the surrounding rock.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Sample Module 3]

**Q.B(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and applications of shaped charges** as off-the-shelf devices in the demolition of hardened structures. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
A shaped charge exploits the **Munroe effect** (discovered by Charles E. Munroe, 1888). When a high-explosive charge is detonated with a *conical or linear cavity* (liner) facing the target, the detonation wave collapses the liner, accelerating a portion of its mass to a hyper-velocity jet (7,000 – 10,000 m/s). The jet produces a localized pressure of **200 – 300 GPa**, which exceeds the yield strength of any known material, enabling penetration of steel, concrete, or armour.

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Main charge:** Conical geometry of HMX, Octol, or Comp-B (180 – 250 g).
- **Liner:** Copper, aluminium, or tantalum, conical, 0.5 – 1.0 mm thick.
- **Stand-off:** 1.5 – 3 charge-diameters distance from target — critical for jet formation.
- **Booster:** 50 g Pentolite pellet at the apex of the cone.
- **Casing:** Plastic or aluminium body, factory-sealed.

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Well perforation** in oil/gas wells (perforating guns with shaped charges).
2. **EOD & military breaching** — door breaching charges, anti-tank HEAT rounds.
3. **Demolition of bridge piers, concrete columns, and reinforced steel structures**.
4. **Linear Shaped Charges (LSC)** for cutting steel girders, ship's hulls, and pipelines.

---

**Q.B(b)** A 500 g Pentolite booster ($\sqrt{2E}$ = 2,800 m/s) is enclosed in a 200 g steel casing. Using the Gurney equation, calculate the **fragment velocity** and comment on the **secondary fragment hazard zone** radius. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Gurney Velocity Calculation (5 marks):**

$$V_0 = \sqrt{2E} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M/C}{1 + 0.5(M/C)}}$$

Given: $M = 0.200 \text{ kg}$, $C = 0.500 \text{ kg}$, $\sqrt{2E} = 2,800 \text{ m/s}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
M/C &= 0.200/0.500 = 0.40 \\
V_0 &= 2800 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.40}{1 + 0.5(0.40)}} \\
&= 2800 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.40}{1.20}} \\
&= 2800 \times \sqrt{0.3333} \\
&= 2800 \times 0.5774 \\
V_0 &= 1,617 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Secondary Fragment Hazard Zone (2 marks):**
Empirically, the safe throw radius for steel-cased boosters is given by:

$$R_{safe} = 100 \times \sqrt{C_{kg}} \quad \text{m}$$

For $C = 0.5 \text{ kg}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{safe} &= 100 \times \sqrt{0.5} \\
&= 100 \times 0.707 \\
&= 70.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** Personnel must be evacuated to **at least 70 – 80 m** from the booster initiation point. Fragment velocities of 1,617 m/s are supersonic and will penetrate standard 6 mm steel plate at 50 m range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** In detonation pressure derivations, failing to convert $\text{g/cm}^3 \to \text{kg/m}^3$ costs 2 marks outright.
> 2. **Confusing Brisance with Detonation Pressure:** Brisance is a *dimensionless index*; pressure is in Pa. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit confusion.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why":** Pure algebraic substitution without explanation of physical significance (DDT, Munroe effect) is penalized — KTU 2024 ESE rubric reserves 1–2 marks per sub-question for conceptual clarity.
> 4. **Fabricating Standards:** Do not invent IS numbers. The known standards in this module are IS 6609, IS 7381, IS 14362, IS 9038.
> 5. **Mermaid in exam copy:** Never include Mermaid diagrams in a written answer; convert them to *hand-drawn labelled sketches*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for OSED Topic (Module 3):**

- **OSED** = pre-manufactured, regulator-certified, factory-sealed explosive device. **NOT** the same as an IED.
- **Explosive Train** = a 5-to-10 stage amplification chain: firing pulse → bridge wire → flash charge → primary → base charge → booster → main charge → rock.
- **Primary Explosives** (Lead Azide, Mercury Fulminate): milligram quantities, detonate from spark/heat.
- **Secondary Explosives** (PETN, RDX, HMX, TNT, ANFO, Emulsions): gram-to-tonne scale, need shock to initiate.
- **DDT** (Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition) occurs in < 1 mm of primary explosive travel; critical for detonator reliability.
- **No. 8 Detonator** is the KTU/JEE standard: 0.5 g PETN base charge, 7,000 – 8,000 m/s VOD.
- **Brisance** = $\rho \cdot D^2 \times 10^{-6}$; *higher* brisance = *more* shattering.
- **C-J Pressure** = $\dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$ — for ANFO ≈ 1.87 GPa; for PETN ≈ 31 GPa.
- **Gurney Equation** gives fragment throw velocity; safe radius = $100\sqrt{C_{kg}}$ metres for steel-cased boosters.
- **Hopkinson-Cranz Cube-Root Law** governs blast overpressure scaling: $Z = R/W^{1/3}$.
- **Shaped Charges (Munroe Effect):** cone-liner collapse → hyper-velocity jet → 200+ GPa localized pressure.
- **Detonating Cord (Detacord):** continuous PETN core, 5.3 – 80 g/m, used for simultaneous initiation of multiple holes.
- **Indian Regulatory Bodies:** PESO, DGMS (mining), CCOE (Chief Controller of Explosives). Standards: IS 6609, IS 7381, IS 14362, IS 9038, Explosives Rules 2008.
- **Golden Rule for Blasting:** *Stemming length $\geq 0.7 \times$ burden*; *primer length $\geq 0.5 \times$ column length*.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Off-the-Shelf Explosive Devices**
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Explosive Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Explosive Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Explosive Products** are flexible, ribbon-like or cord-like explosive configurations in which the active (explosive) phase is confined to a single geometric axis (linear) or arranged in a sheet form, producing a **directed, self-sustaining detonation front** that propagates along the principal longitudinal axis at a characteristic Velocity of Detonation (VOD), typically ranging from **5,000 m/s to 9,000 m/s** depending on core composition and confinement.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the term encompasses a heterogeneous family of engineered products whose defining geometric parameter is the **aspect ratio** (length ≫ cross-sectional dimension). The principal sub-classes are:

| Sub-Class | Geometry | Typical Core | Application |
|-----------|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Detonating Cord (DETACORD / PETN Cord)** | Circular cross-section, textile/plastic sheath | **PETN (Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate)** | Demolition, seismic, initiation |
| **Mild Detonating Cord (MDC)** | Thin textile-wrapped cord | **PETN / RDX (reduced core load)** | Oil-well perforation, mild initiation |
| **Linear Shaped Charge (LSC)** | V-shaped / half-round metal extrusion | **RDX / HMX / Octol** | Metal cutting, demolition, aerospace |
| **Flexible Sheet / Ribbon Explosive** | Planar / rectangular sheet | **PETN / RDX bonded with elastomer** | Cutting irregular profiles, welding |
| **Detonating Tube (Nonel / Shock Tube)** | Hollow plastic tube (coated internally) | **HMX / Aluminum powder** (trace) | Gasless initiation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Highway of Detonation"**
> Imagine a high-speed bullet train track. The track is the **sheath**, the rails are the **reflective confinement**, and the explosive core is the train itself — once the engine (booster/primer) starts the train moving, the entire length of track carries the detonation at a fixed velocity, just as train cars travel together along a fixed rail. The detonation is **continuous**, **directional**, and **self-sustaining**.

A more technical analogy: consider a **fuse wire carrying a flame** — but instead of slow combustion (~1 cm/s), the flame front moves at supersonic velocity (~7,000 m/s) with sufficient brisance to detonate adjacent booster charges.

## 1.3 Critical Design Metrics

The following physical constants and design parameters govern every linear explosive product:

- **Core Load (CL):** mass of explosive per unit length, expressed in **grains/foot (gr/ft)** or **g/m**. Standard DETACORD has CL ≈ **50 gr/ft (≈ 10.6 g/m)**.
- **Velocity of Detonation (VOD, D):** steady-state shock velocity in the explosive core, typically **6,000–7,000 m/s** for PETN-based cord.
- **Detonation Pressure (P_CJ):** Chapman–Jouguet pressure of the core, typically **15–30 GPa** for PETN.
- **Standoff Distance (S):** gap between LSC liner apex and target surface, optimized for **maximum penetration**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Linear Explosive Products

### A. Detonating Cord (DETACORD)

The most elementary linear explosive product. Construction is layered:

1. **Explosive Core** — A continuous extrusion of powdered **PETN** or **RDX** bound with a polymer binder (typically ~1–3% by mass).
2. **Inner Textile Wrap** — A helically wound fibrous sheath (rayon, polyester, or nylon) that mechanically consolidates the core.
3. **Waterproofing Layer** — A bitumen or wax coating to protect against moisture ingress.
4. **Outer Reinforcing Braid** — A woven textile or polymeric braid that provides tensile strength and abrasion resistance.
5. **Optional Metal Sheath** — For high-pressure well applications (MDC in oil/gas), a lead or aluminum jacket is added.

### B. Linear Shaped Charge (LSC)

A directed-energy cutter — engineering's scalpel. Its construction is fundamentally different from DETACORD:

- A **metal liner** (typically **Oxygen-Free High-Conductivity (OFHC) copper**, **lead**, or **zinc**) is shaped into a **V-groove**, **half-moon**, or **conical wedge**.
- The liner is backed by a **main explosive charge** (RDX/HMX composite).
- An outer **metal or plastic casing** provides mechanical rigidity.
- Detonation collapses the liner via the **Munroe Effect (Neumann Spike Effect)**, forming a continuous **slugs and jets** that penetrate the target.

### C. Munroe Effect — The Physical Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Munroe's Hypothesis (1888):** When a lined cavity is initiated on its convex side, the detonation wave converges on the liner, projecting it as a high-velocity jet — energy is concentrated along the liner's central axis, producing a focused cutting stream.

The collapse angle (liner angle, 2α) determines jet characteristics:

- **Small α** (≤ 30°) → long, thin, fast jet — excellent penetration
- **Large α** (≥ 60°) → short, thick, slow slug — good cutting depth with mass

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Velocity of Detonation (PETN core, untamped) | $D \approx 6{,}000 \pm 200$ | **m/s** |
| 2 | Velocity of Detonation (RDX core) | $D \approx 8{,}000 \pm 250$ | **m/s** |
| 3 | Detonation Pressure (Chapman–Jouguet) | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 \, D^2}{\gamma + 1}$ | **GPa**; $\rho_0$ = core density, $\gamma$ = polytropic index |
| 4 | Core Load (linear density) | $CL = \rho_0 \cdot A_c$ | **g/m**; $A_c$ = core cross-sectional area |
| 5 | Mass per Foot Conversion | $1 \text{ gr/ft} = 0.0648 \text{ g/0.3048 m}$ | **g/m** |
| 6 | Munroe Penetration Depth (Birkhoff's model) | $P_t = L \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_j}{\rho_t}}$ | **m**; $\rho_j$ = jet density, $\rho_t$ = target density |
| 7 | LSC Stand-Off Optimization | $S_{opt} = 0.5 \, \times \, \text{LSC diameter}$ | **m**; empirical rule for hollow jets |
| 8 | Gurney Fragment Velocity (linear driver) | $V_0 = \sqrt{2E}\sqrt{\dfrac{C/M}{1 + 0.5\,(C/M)}}$ | **m/s**; $C/M$ = core-to-mass ratio |
| 9 | Jet Tip Velocity (typical Cu liner) | $V_j \approx 4{,}000 - 10{,}000$ | **m/s** |
| 10 | Run Distance before Jet Break-up | $L_{break} \approx 5 \times \text{liner thickness}$ | **m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** All formulas above assume the linear product is initiated by a booster of sufficient brisance (minimum **0.4 g PETN equivalent** for DETACORD) and that the **core density is ≥ 95% of theoretical maximum density (TMD)**. A loose or aerated core will deflagrate rather than detonate, **silently failing** the initiation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Demolition:** Falling-cut LSCs sever reinforced concrete columns in millisecond timescale, enabling directional collapse.
- **Oil & Gas (Perforating):** MDC delivered downhole creates jet-paths through casing and rock to establish hydrocarbon flow.
- **Aerospace:** Mild LSC separates solid rocket motor stages and deploys payloads (NASA Standard Initiator heritage).
- **Mining:** Cutting roof bolts in underground roadways to allow controlled strata movement.
- **Ordnance Disposal (EOD):** Disrupting improvised explosive devices without sympathetic detonation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Chapman–Jouguet Detonation Pressure for Linear Core

We derive the steady-state detonation pressure inside the core of a detonating cord. Starting from the **Rankine–Hugoniot conservation equations** for a one-dimensional steady detonation front:

### Step 1 — Mass Conservation
The mass flux across the detonation front must be continuous:

$$
\rho_0 \, D = \rho \, (D - u)
$$

where:
- $\rho_0$ = undisturbed core density (kg/m³)
- $D$ = detonation velocity (m/s)
- $\rho$ = density behind the front (kg/m³)
- $u$ = particle velocity behind the front (m/s)

### Step 2 — Momentum Conservation
The pressure jump across the shock equals the momentum flux:

$$
P - P_0 = \rho_0 \, D \, u
$$

For a high-explosive core, ambient pressure $P_0 \approx 0$, so:

$$
P = \rho_0 \, D \, u
$$

### Step 3 — Energy Conservation (Rankine–Hugoniot Energy Equation)
The total specific energy is conserved:

$$
e - e_0 = \frac{1}{2}(P + P_0)\left(\frac{1}{\rho_0} - \frac{1}{\rho}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) Sonic Condition
At the CJ point, the flow behind the front is **sonic relative to the front**. This gives the additional constraint:

$$
D - u = c
$$

where $c$ is the local sound speed behind the front. For a polytropic gas, $c^2 = \gamma P / \rho$.

### Step 5 — Eliminate Particle Velocity $u$
From the momentum equation:

$$
u = \frac{P}{\rho_0 \, D}
$$

Substitute into the CJ sonic condition:

$$
D - \frac{P}{\rho_0 \, D} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $P$ in Closed Form
Squaring and simplifying (with $\rho = \rho_0 (\gamma + 1)/\gamma$ at CJ point):

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 \, D^2}{\gamma + 1}
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Application (PETN Core Example)
Given:
- $\rho_0 = 1{,}760 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $D = 7{,}000 \text{ m/s}$
- $\gamma \approx 2.8$ (for condensed explosives)

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{1760 \times (7000)^2}{2.8 + 1} = \frac{8.624 \times 10^{10}}{3.8}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} = 22.7 \text{ GPa}
$$

This matches the experimentally observed value (~21 GPa), validating the model.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Penetration Depth of Linear Shaped Charge (Birkhoff Model)

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup
Consider a linear shaped charge with a wedge-shaped (V-groove) liner of half-angle $\alpha$, thickness $\delta$, and density $\rho_j$. The detonation wave collapses the liner symmetrically, forming a jet of density $\rho_j$ moving at velocity $V_j$ toward a target of density $\rho_t$.

### Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation Across the Collapse
For incompressible liner collapse, applying Bernoulli's equation across the liner (neglecting liner strength):

$$
\frac{V_j^2}{2} = \frac{V_0^2}{2}\,(1 - \cos \beta)
$$

where $\beta = 2\alpha$ is the full collapse angle and $V_0$ is the initial velocity imparted by detonation pressure.

### Step 3 — Steady-State Jet Mass Flow
For a continuous jet of cross-sectional area $A_j$ and slug cross-section $A_s$, conservation of momentum gives the jet velocity:

$$
V_j = \frac{V_0 \cos\alpha + V_0}{1 + \cos\beta} \cdot 2 \cos\alpha
$$

For small angles ($\alpha \to 0$), this reduces to the classical result:

$$
V_j \approx \frac{V_0}{1 - \cos\beta}
$$

### Step 4 — Penetration Depth (Birkhoff's Hydrodynamic Theory)
Treating both jet and target as incompressible fluids, the steady-state penetration depth is:

$$
P_t = L_j \sqrt{\frac{\rho_j}{\rho_t}}
$$

where:
- $L_j$ = effective jet length (≈ $5\delta$ before break-up)
- $\rho_j$ = jet (liner) density
- $\rho_t$ = target density

### Step 5 — Application: OFHC Copper Liner on Mild Steel
Given:
- $\rho_j (\text{Cu}) = 8{,}960 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_t (\text{steel}) = 7{,}850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $L_j = 5 \times 0.8 \text{ mm} = 4.0 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$$
P_t = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{8960}{7850}}
$$

$$
P_t = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{1.1414} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0683
$$

$$
P_t \approx 4.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 4.27 \text{ mm}
$$

This is the **theoretical maximum penetration** for an ideal jet — practical values are typically 60–85% of this theoretical limit due to jet break-up, standoff losses, and target inhomogeneity.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Linear Products

| Parameter | DETACORD (50 gr/ft) | MDC (25 gr/ft) | LSC (100 gr/ft Cu-lined) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Core | PETN | PETN | RDX/HMX |
| VOD (m/s) | 7,000 | 6,500 | 8,200 |
| $P_{CJ}$ (GPa) | ~22 | ~18 | ~30 |
| Output Mode | Symmetric shock | Mild pulse | Directed jet |
| Typical Use | Demolition initiation | Oil-well perforation | Cutting metal/concrete |
| Initiation Threshold | 0.4 g PETN | 0.2 g PETN | Booster required |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a "linear explosive product" with two industrial examples. State the standard unit of "core load" and give one typical value for commercial detonating cord.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A linear explosive product is an explosive configuration in which the active charge is confined along a single elongated geometric axis, producing a self-sustaining detonation that propagates axially at high velocity. *(1 mark)* Industrial examples: **Detonating Cord (DETACORD)** and **Linear Shaped Charge (LSC)**. *(1 mark)* Core load is measured in **grains per foot (gr/ft)** or g/m; commercial DETACORD has a core load of approximately **50 gr/ft (~10.6 g/m)**. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Munroe Effect? How does the collapse angle α of a shaped charge liner affect the jet characteristics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Munroe Effect is the phenomenon where a lined cavity, when initiated on the convex side, projects the liner as a focused high-velocity jet — energy concentrates along the liner axis. *(1.5 marks)* A **small collapse angle (α ≤ 30°)** produces a long, thin, fast jet with high penetration depth; a **large angle (α ≥ 60°)** produces a short, thick, slow slug with greater cutting volume but lower penetration. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Describe the construction of a **Detonating Cord** with a neat labeled sketch. Explain the role of each layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 50 gr/ft PETN detonating cord has a core density of 1,760 kg/m³ and a core diameter of 4.0 mm. Calculate the **core cross-sectional area, linear mass density in g/m, and the theoretical Chapman–Jouguet detonation pressure** given $\gamma = 2.8$ and VOD = 7,000 m/s. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Construction of Detonating Cord (7 marks):**

A commercial detonating cord consists of **five concentric layers**, produced by a continuous extrusion and braiding process:

1. **Explosive Core (1.5 marks):** A continuous extrusion of fine crystalline **PETN (Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate)**, often blended with a small percentage (~1%) of polymeric binder to enhance mechanical cohesion. The PETN powder is compressed to **≥ 95% TMD** under radial pressure. The core diameter is typically 3–5 mm for commercial 50 gr/ft cord.

2. **Inner Textile Wrap (1.5 marks):** A **helically wound fibrous yarn** (rayon, polyester, or nylon) that applies radial compression to consolidate the core and prevent powder migration. This layer also provides **flexibility** while maintaining the cylindrical geometry.

3. **Waterproofing Layer (1 mark):** A coating of **bitumen, wax, or polymer emulsion** that protects the PETN from moisture and humidity. PETN is hygroscopic to a small degree, and water ingress can cause **desensitization** or **dead-pressing**.

4. **Outer Reinforcing Braid (1.5 marks):** A woven outer textile or polymer braid (typically **yellow, red, or orange** for visual identification) that provides **tensile strength**, abrasion resistance, and protection against mechanical damage during handling and laying.

5. **Optional Metal Sheath (1.5 marks):** For high-pressure oil-well applications (Mild Detonating Cord), a **lead or aluminum jacket** is added externally to resist hydrostatic collapse at depths exceeding 5,000 m.

**Role Summary:** Each layer is engineered for a specific function — explosive energy delivery, mechanical integrity, environmental protection, and operational safety.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given Data:**
- Core load = 50 gr/ft
- $\rho_0 = 1{,}760 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Core diameter $d = 4.0 \text{ mm} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $\gamma = 2.8$
- $D = 7{,}000 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1 — Core Cross-Sectional Area (2 marks):**

$$
A_c = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (4.0 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_c = \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Linear Mass Density in g/m (2 marks):**

$$
\mu = \rho_0 \cdot A_c = 1760 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\mu = 0.02212 \text{ kg/m} = 22.12 \text{ g/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to gr/ft Verification (1 mark):**

$$
\mu = 22.12 \text{ g/m} \times \frac{1 \text{ gr}}{0.0648 \text{ g}} \times \frac{0.3048 \text{ m}}{1 \text{ ft}}
$$

$$
\mu \approx 104 \text{ gr/ft}
$$

(Note: Standard commercial 50 gr/ft cord has a smaller core ~3.0 mm; the values here correspond to a heavy-duty ~100 gr/ft cord.)

**Step 4 — Chapman–Jouguet Detonation Pressure (2 marks):**

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 \, D^2}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{1760 \times (7000)^2}{2.8 + 1}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = \frac{1760 \times 4.9 \times 10^7}{3.8} = \frac{8.624 \times 10^{10}}{3.8}
$$

$$
P_{CJ} = 2.27 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} = 22.7 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** $A_c = 1.257 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$; $\mu = 22.12 \text{ g/m}$; $P_{CJ} = 22.7 \text{ GPa}$

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Munroe Effect** with a labeled diagram. Describe how the **liner collapse angle (α)** and **standoff distance (S)** jointly govern the penetration depth of a Linear Shaped Charge. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An LSC with an **OFHC copper liner** of wedge half-angle $\alpha = 25°$ and liner thickness $\delta = 0.8 \text{ mm}$ is used to perforate a **mild steel target**. Compute the **jet break-up length, theoretical penetration depth (Birkhoff model)**, and comment on the practical optimization of standoff distance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Munroe Effect Explanation (3.5 marks):**
When a lined cavity (V-shaped or conical) is subjected to a detonation wave from the convex (outer) side, the wave converges along the liner's central axis. By **conservation of momentum**, the liner material is projected inward and forward as a continuous, high-velocity jet. The jet tip travels at **4,000–10,000 m/s**, while the slower slug trails behind. The energy is **focused** along the axis — this is the Munroe Effect (1888, Charles E. Munroe).

**Governing Parameters (3.5 marks):**

- **Liner collapse angle (α):** A smaller α gives a longer, thinner, faster jet → **higher penetration per unit mass**. Larger α gives shorter, slower, but mass-heavier jets → **better for bulk cutting**.
- **Standoff distance (S):** Too small → jet has not formed (slug-mode penetration, low efficiency). Too large → jet breaks up into discrete particles ("carrot break-up"). The empirical optimum is:
$$
S_{opt} \approx 0.5 \times \text{LSC diameter}
$$
At this distance, the jet is **continuous, coherent, and fully formed**.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- $\alpha = 25°$ → full collapse angle $\beta = 50°$
- $\delta = 0.8 \text{ mm} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $\rho_j (\text{OFHC Cu}) = 8{,}960 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_t (\text{mild steel}) = 7{,}850 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Jet Break-up Length (2 marks):**

$$
L_{break} = 5 \delta = 5 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Penetration Depth (Birkhoff Model) (3 marks):**

$$
P_t = L_j \sqrt{\frac{\rho_j}{\rho_t}} = L_{break} \sqrt{\frac{8960}{7850}}
$$

$$
P_t = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{1.1414} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0683
$$

$$
P_t = 4.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 4.27 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Standoff Optimization Discussion (2 marks):**
The empirical optimum standoff for hollow copper jets is:
$$
S_{opt} = 0.5 \times D_{LSC} \quad (\text{typically } 6\text{–}10 \text{ mm for industrial LSC})
$$
At $S = S_{opt}$, the jet is fully stretched and coherent, giving **~80%** of theoretical penetration. Below this value, premature jet formation reduces efficiency; above this value, jet break-up degrades penetration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently confuse **gr/ft** with g/m. Always convert: **1 gr/ft ≈ 0.212 g/m**.
> 2. **Standoff Misconception:** Many students write "more standoff = more penetration." This is FALSE — beyond $S_{opt}$, penetration **decreases sharply** due to jet break-up.
> 3. **Forgetting $\rho_t$:** The Birkhoff penetration formula requires the **target density**, not the explosive density.
> 4. **Confusing Munroe Effect with Shaped Charge Effect:** They are synonymous — the Munroe Effect is the *original* historical term; *shaped charge* is the modern engineering term.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linear explosive products** are characterized by **length ≫ cross-section**, with continuous detonation propagation along the longitudinal axis.
- **Detonating Cord (DETACORD):** PETN core, textile sheath, **core load ~50 gr/ft**, VOD ~7,000 m/s — used for initiation and demolition.
- **Linear Shaped Charge (LSC):** Metal-lined (Cu/Pb/Zn), RDX/HMX main charge, produces directed jet via **Munroe Effect** — used for cutting.
- **Mild Detonating Cord (MDC):** Lower core load, used in oil-well perforation where sympathetic detonation must be avoided.
- **Core Load** is the single most important design metric, measured in **gr/ft** or **g/m**.
- **Chapman–Jouguet Pressure:** $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$ — directly proportional to $D^2$.
- **Munroe Effect:** Convergence of detonation wave focuses liner material into a hypervelocity jet (up to 10 km/s).
- **Birkhoff Penetration:** $P_t = L_j \sqrt{\rho_j / \rho_t}$ — depends on jet length and density ratio.
- **Standoff Optimization:** $S_{opt} \approx 0.5 \times D_{LSC}$ for maximum penetration.
- **Small collapse angle** → thin fast jet (deep penetration); **large collapse angle** → thick slow slug (volume cutting).
- **Always convert gr/ft to g/m** using $1 \text{ gr/ft} = 0.212 \text{ g/m}$ before KTU numerical problems.
- **Practical penetration is 60–85% of Birkhoff theoretical maximum** due to jet break-up and standoff losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical/Explosive Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical/Explosive Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Explosives Technology and Safety**, **Mechanical/Explosive Devices** (more precisely, *initiating/explosive accessories* or *blasting accessories*) are defined as the **non-bulk engineered components** that are used to **initiate, propagate, transmit, boost, or delay** the detonation wave of a main explosive charge in a controlled, predictable, and safe manner. They do not perform the primary rock-breaking work themselves but act as the *control layer* between the human operator (or machine controller) and the bulk explosive column inside the blast hole.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT753 Module 3), this classification broadly covers:

- **Detonators** — Electric, Non-Electric (Nonel/Detaline), Electronic (E*Star, i-kon, DaveyTronic), and Shock-tube based initiators.
- **Detonating Cord (Detacord / CDC / Primacord)** — A flexible linear explosive with a PETN/PENT core (~5 to 100 g/m) used to transmit detonation along a borehole column or surface trunkline.
- **Boosters / Pentolite Boosters** — A secondary high-explosive charge (typically 70% RDX + 30% TNT, or cast Pentolite 50/50) used to amplify the detonator output to a stable, high-velocity, high-pressure shock capable of reliably initiating the main charge (especially ANFO / emulsion blends).
- **Safety Fuse (Bickford Fuse)** — A slow-burning (typically **30 s/m to 100 s/m**, most commonly **90 seconds/foot ≈ 100 s/m**) textile-wrapped pyrotechnic train used in older mining.
- **Detonating Relay Connectors / Surface Delay Connectors** — Plastic-bodied miniature detonating assemblies inserted in a detonating cord line to introduce precise millisecond delays between holes.
- **Blasting Cap / Cap-sensitive Booster** — The miniature primary/secondary explosive charge housed inside a detonator.
- **Primers / Primer assemblies** — A booster coupled to a detonator or detonating cord downline, lowered into the borehole as the initiating unit of the main charge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In the 2024 scheme, the examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between the *initiating device* (detonator), the *propagating/connecting device* (detonating cord), and the *amplifying device* (booster). Confusing these three roles is the most common conceptual error.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fireworks display** on a stage. You, the operator, stand safely behind a control panel. You press a button — that button is the **detonator**. Inside the button is a tiny but very sensitive explosive (like a cap) that, when activated, sends a hot, sharp flame or shock wave through a *fuse wire* or *detonating cord* — analogous to a **long string of firecrackers connected in a line**. When this small flame reaches the **main firecracker barrel** (the bulk ANFO/emulsion charge in the borehole), it is often too weak to light it. So we tie a small, powerful firecracker to the side — this is the **booster** — which explodes with enough force to set off the main charge.

So in plain English:

> **Detonator = the match / button. Cord = the fuse wire. Booster = the amplifier. Main charge = the workhorse.**

The **mechanical** part comes in because these devices also have to *withstand the rough handling* of a mine — water, mud, falling rocks, compression — so their *casing, crimping, and waterproofing* are mechanical engineering problems as much as chemical ones.

### 1.3 Standard Units & Metrics Used in This Topic

- **Detonation Velocity (VOD)** of detonating cord: typically **6,000 to 7,000 m/s** for PETN-cored cord (often quoted as **~6,500 m/s**).
- **Safety Fuse Burn Rate:** **90 seconds/foot** is the international standard (≈ **100 s/m** or **10 mm/s**).
- **Cap Sensitivity Threshold:** Detonators must initiate reliably with **0.20 g to 0.30 g of mercury fulminate** equivalent or modern lead azide / DDNP mixes.
- **Booster Density:** Cast Pentolite density **~1.65 g/cm³**.
- **Detonator Delay Timing:** Standard MS delays range from **25 ms to 1,000 ms** in **25 ms** increments; LP (long period) delays extend to **6,000 ms**.
- **Initiation Energy for Electric Detonators:** No-fire current **0.18 A for 5 minutes**; All-fire current **0.45 A for 50 ms** (industry standard for copper-domed Cu/EtN).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Hierarchy of Initiation

To understand these devices, one must grasp the **three-tier initiation hierarchy** that governs every commercial blast:

1. **Tier 1 — Initiation Energy Source:** A small, precise, energy-controlled input.
   - Examples: Electric pulse (heating a bridge wire), shock tube gas ignition (Nonel), pyrotechnic safety fuse spark, electronic logic chip + capacitor discharge.

2. **Tier 2 — Primary Explosive (the "cap"):** A tiny amount of an extremely sensitive explosive that **deflagrates-to-detonates (DDT)** upon receiving Tier-1 energy.
   - Examples: Lead Azide (Pb(N₃)₂ — **~250 g of PETN equivalent output per 1 g**), Mercury Fulminate (Hg(ONC)₂ — historic), DDNP (Diazodinitrophenol — modern non-mercuric).

3. **Tier 3 — Secondary Explosive Base Charge:** A larger, less sensitive, high-VOD charge that delivers a stable detonation wave to the booster or directly to the main charge.
   - Examples: PETN (Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate), RDX (Cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine), HMX, Tetryl.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy?** Pure primary explosives are too sensitive to handle or transport safely. Pure secondary explosives need too much energy to initiate directly. The hierarchy lets you use a *tiny bit of dangerous stuff* to safely trigger a *much bigger but safer charge*. This is the **cardinal rule of commercial explosives engineering**.

### 2.2 Device-by-Device Operational Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Electric Detonator (Instantaneous / Delay)

A typical **Cu/EtN (Copper / Ethylene-Nitrated)** electric detonator consists of:

- **Plastic or Aluminum Shell** (length ~50–80 mm, diameter ~7 mm).
- **Bridge Wire** (resistance **0.8 to 2.0 Ω** typical, nichrome or platinum-iridium).
- **Ignition Head / Match Head** — pyrotechnic composition (typically lead thiocyanate + lead dioxide + antimony sulfide) that flares when the bridge wire heats.
- **Primary Charge** — Lead Azide (~0.20 g) or DDNP.
- **Base Charge** — PETN (~0.50 g).
- **Delay Element** (in MS detonators) — a pressed column of **lead peroxide / silicon / antimony sulfide / delay powder** that burns at a controlled rate, with **25 ms increments**.
- **Sealing Plug / Sulfur-Aluminum plug** — waterproofs the shell.

**Operating Logic:** Current flows → Bridge wire heats (Joule's Law: $Q = I^2 R t$) → Ignition head flashes → Delay element burns (if any) → Primary explosive DDT → Base charge detonates → Detonation wave exits through the open end at **~7,000 m/s**.

#### 2.2.2 Non-Electric Detonator (Nonel / Shock-Tube Detonator)

- **Shock Tube:** A three-layer plastic tube (inner: reactive powder ~20 mg/m of HMX/Al, mid: polyester, outer: polyethylene) with an inner diameter of ~1.2 mm.
- **Initiation:** A starter gun or a relay ignites a small propellant charge inside the tube, sending a **low-energy shock wave (not detonation)** at ~2,000 m/s along the tube's inner surface.
- **At the detonator end:** The shock wave enters a closed metal shell containing a **millisecond delay element** + **base charge (RDX)**.
- **Why it's safer:** Immune to stray electrical currents, radio frequency, and static electricity — major advantage in gassy/coal mines.

#### 2.2.3 Electronic Detonator

- Contains a **microprocessor chip**, **storage capacitor**, and **delay capacitor**.
- Programmable in **1 ms increments** with **0.1 ms accuracy** (vs. ±25 ms for pyrotechnic).
- Powered by an **external blasting machine** that authenticates, downloads delay timings via RFID or wired link, and fires the capacitor discharge.
- Used for **precision tunneling, contour blasting, and seismic wave control**.

#### 2.2.4 Detonating Cord (Detacord / Primacord)

- **Construction:** A textile / plastic fiber outer sheath with multiple counter-laid yarns, inside which lies a **PETN core** (linear charge density **5 g/m to 100 g/m**, common values are **5.3 g/m, 10 g/m, 20 g/m, 40 g/m, 100 g/m**).
- **Detonation Velocity:** **~6,000–7,000 m/s**.
- **Tensile Strength:** Typically **>50 kg minimum breaking strength** for standard cord.
- **Function:**
  - **Trunkline (surface) delay system** — laid out between holes to transmit initiation.
  - **Down-the-hole (DTH) initiation** — cord lowered into the borehole to initiate the bottom charge.
  - **Deck loading** — used to initiate charges separated by stemming.
  - **Secondary blasting / mud-capping** — used to tie onto boulders.

#### 2.2.5 Booster

- **Cast Pentolite (50/50 PETN/TNT)** — density **~1.65 g/cm³**, VOD ~7,500 m/s.
- **Pressed RDX/Wax (95/5)** — density **~1.55 g/cm³**, VOD ~8,000 m/s.
- **Common Masses:** **50 g, 100 g, 150 g, 200 g, 400 g, 800 g** (India standard 150 g is very common).
- **Why needed:** ANFO has a critical diameter of **~25–50 mm** in steel confinement and is **not cap-sensitive**. Without a booster, the detonator's output would simply **fizzle** in ANFO. A booster provides the **shock pressure (P_{cj})** required to initiate the larger charge.

#### 2.2.6 Safety Fuse

- A **textile-wrapped, water-proofed black powder train**.
- **Burn rate:** **90 seconds per foot = 100 seconds per meter = 10 mm/s**.
- Used in **plaster/mud-cap blasting** and small-scale secondary blasting.
- **Major drawback:** Cannot be used in gassy or dusty mines (open flame hazard).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat generated in bridge wire | $Q \;=\; I^{2}\,R\,t$ | Energy delivered to ignition head | Q in Joules, I in Amps, R in Ω, t in seconds |
| Detonation Pressure (Chapman–Jouguet) | $P_{cj} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho\, V_{D}^{\,2}}{2\,\left(\gamma + 1\right)}$ | Pressure at reaction zone driving rock fracture | **~10 to 30 GPa** for commercial explosives |
| VOD vs. Density (empirical) | $V_{D} \;=\; A \,+\, B \cdot \rho$ | Detonation velocity rises with bulk density | A, B are explosive-specific constants |
| Critical Diameter | $d_{c}$ | Minimum charge diameter for stable detonation | ANFO ~ 25–50 mm; Emulsion ~ 12–16 mm |
| Booster Shock Pressure | $P_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{b}\, V_{D,b}^{\,2}}{4}$ | Pressure transferred to main charge | $\sim$ 5–15 GPa at booster-main interface |
| Safety Fuse Burn Time | $T \;=\; \dfrac{L}{\dot{r}}$ | Time from lighting to cap detonation | $\dot{r}$ ≈ 10 mm/s |
| Detonating Cord Mass per Hole | $m_{cord} \;=\; \mu \cdot \left( L_{hole} \,+\, L_{tie} \right)$ | Required cord for one blast hole | $\mu$ = 5–100 g/m |
| Delay Timing (Pyrotechnic MS) | $t_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{\ell_{delay}}{\dot{v}_{pyro}}$ | Time delay from ignition to detonator base | $\dot{v}_{pyro}$ controlled by composition |
| Newton's Detonation Energy | $E \;=\; \dfrac{P_{cj}\,(V_{0}\,-\,V_{1})}{\gamma - 1}$ | Available work from detonation | Joules per gram |
| Bridge-wire Resistance (at T) | $R_T \;=\; R_0\, \bigl(1 \,+\, \alpha\,\Delta T\bigr)$ | Resistance change during firing | $\alpha$ = temp. coefficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The single most testable formula in this module is the **Joule heating equation $Q = I^2 R t$** for electric detonators, and the **Chapman–Jouguet pressure $P_{cj} = \rho V_D^2 / (2(\gamma+1))$** for boosters. Memorize both with units and physical meaning.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surface Mining (Iron Ore, Coal, Limestone):** Detonating cord trunkline + MS delay electric detonators are the industry standard for cost-effective, large-pattern blasts.
- **Underground Metal Mines:** Electronic detonators (e.g., I-kon, DaveyTronic) for precise timing, reducing vibration complaints from neighboring communities.
- **Tunneling / Civil Excavation:** Electronic detonators + smooth-wall blasting with precise contour delays for minimal overbreak.
- **Quarrying:** Nonel-based systems for safety against RF interference from mobile phones on site.
- **Seismic Exploration:** Electronic detonators provide sub-millisecond synchronization for high-resolution seismic imaging.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bridge-Wire Heat Equation (Joule's Law for Electric Detonators)

The electric detonator is, at its core, an application of **Joule's First Law**. The bridge wire, when current flows, dissipates electrical energy as heat. We derive the relationship.

**Step 1 — Define the system.**
A bridge wire of resistance $R$ (Ohms) carries a steady current $I$ (Amperes) for a duration $t$ (seconds). The energy dissipated is defined by the power law:

$$
P \;=\; V \cdot I
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law.**
Substituting $V = I \cdot R$ (Ohm's Law), we obtain the instantaneous power:

$$
P \;=\; (I \cdot R) \cdot I \;=\; I^{2}\,R
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over time.**
For a steady current, the energy (heat) released over time $t$ is:

$$
Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} P\, d\tau \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} I^{2}\,R\, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**
Since $I$ and $R$ are approximately constant over the firing pulse:

$$
Q \;=\; I^{2}\,R \int_{0}^{t} d\tau \;=\; I^{2}\,R\,t
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; I^{2}\,R\,t\,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** To *fire* a detonator reliably (i.e., heat the match head above its ignition temperature, typically **400–500 °C**), the value of $Q$ must exceed the **all-fire energy threshold**, which is industry-standardized at:

$$
Q_{\text{all-fire}} \;\geq\; 5.0 \;\text{mJ (millijoules)}
$$

For a typical bridge-wire resistance of **$R = 1.0\,\Omega$** and a pulse of $t = 5\,\text{ms}$:

$$
I_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{\text{all-fire}}}{R\,t}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}} \;=\; \sqrt{1} \;=\; 1.0\,\text{A (theoretical minimum)}
$$

In practice, the **all-fire current** is set higher (**0.45 A for 50 ms** by Indian CMR 2018 regulations) to provide a safety margin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Chapman–Jouguet Detonation Pressure for a Booster

The booster must deliver sufficient shock pressure into the main charge to drive its reaction zone to a stable detonation. This pressure is given by the **Chapman–Jouguet (C-J) model**.

**Step 1 — Energy conservation across the detonation front.**
For a steady one-dimensional detonation wave moving into an undisturbed explosive at density $\rho_0$ with velocity $V_D$, and a reaction product moving at velocity $u$ with density $\rho_1$:

$$
P_{cj} \,-\, P_{0} \;=\; \rho_0\, V_D\, (V_D \,-\, u)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the C-J sonic condition.**
The C-J hypothesis states that the reaction zone is a sonic surface relative to the burnt gas. This yields the classical C-J pressure:

$$
P_{cj} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_0\, V_D^{\,2}}{(\gamma \,+\, 1)}
$$

For most commercial explosives (where the pressure spike is roughly half the planar value):

$$
\boxed{\,P_{cj} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\rho_0\, V_D^{\,2}}{2\,(\gamma \,+\, 1)}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example for a 150 g Pentolite Booster.**
Given: $\rho_0 = 1{,}650 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $V_D = 7{,}500 \;\text{m/s}$, $\gamma \approx 3$:

$$
P_{cj} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1{,}650 \times (7{,}500)^2}{2\,(3 \,+\, 1)}
$$

Breaking this down:

$$
(7{,}500)^{2} \;=\; 5.625 \times 10^{7} \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
1{,}650 \times 5.625 \times 10^{7} \;=\; 9.281 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
2\,(3 \,+\, 1) \;=\; 8
$$

$$
P_{cj} \;\approx\; \dfrac{9.281 \times 10^{10}}{8} \;\approx\; 1.16 \times 10^{10} \;\text{Pa}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{cj} \;\approx\; 11.6 \;\text{GPa}\,}
$$

This pressure far exceeds the **failure pressure of ANFO** (~1 GPa), confirming why a booster is mandatory to initiate ANFO columns reliably.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Safety Fuse Burn-Time Formula

**Step 1 — Define variables.**
Let $L$ = length of safety fuse (m), $\dot{r}$ = linear burn rate (m/s). The burn time $T$ is:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{L}{\dot{r}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply standard rate.**
For standard Bickford fuse: $\dot{r} = 10 \;\text{mm/s} = 0.01 \;\text{m/s}$.

**Step 3 — Example.**
For a 1.2 m fuse:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{1.2}{0.01} \;=\; 120 \;\text{seconds}
$$

This is the minimum time the operator has to **retreat to safety** before the cap fires — a fundamental safety design constraint.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Comparison Table)

| Feature | Electric Detonator | Non-Electric (Nonel) | Electronic | Detonating Cord | Booster |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initiation Energy | Electrical (I²Rt) | Shock tube gas pulse | Capacitor discharge | Detonation wave | None (passive) |
| Sensitivity to Stray Current | High | Nil | Nil | Nil | Nil |
| Delay Accuracy | ± 5–10 ms | ± 5–10 ms | ± 0.1 ms | N/A | N/A |
| Cost per Hole (relative) | 1.0× | 1.5–2× | 5–10× | 0.3× | 0.5× |
| Use in Gassy Mines | Restricted / Forbidden | Permitted | Permitted | Permitted | Permitted |
| Mass of Explosive | < 1 g | < 1 g | < 1 g | 5–100 g/m | 50–800 g |
| Primary Explosive Inside | Lead Azide / DDNP | None in tube; RDX in cap | Lead Azide | None | None |
| VOD (if applicable) | ~ 7,000 m/s | ~ 7,000 m/s | ~ 7,000 m/s | 6,000–7,000 m/s | 7,000–8,000 m/s |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Commercial Blasting Circuit

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional flow* of energy from the operator's control device all the way to the rock fragmentation in the borehole. This is the architecture that students are expected to reproduce in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Blasting Machine / Shotshell Starter] -->|Electrical pulse OR gas pulse OR shock wave| B[Initiator Device]
    B --> C{Selection of Initiator Type}
    C -->|Electric| D1[Electric Detonator]
    C -->|Non-Electric| D2[Nonel Shock-Tube Detonator]
    C -->|Electronic| D3[Electronic Detonator with Microprocessor]
    D1 --> E[Bridge Wire Heating and Match Head Ignition]
    D2 --> F[Shock Wave Travels Along Tube Wall]
    D3 --> G[Capacitor Discharge to Bridge Wire]
    E --> H[Delay Element MS Powder Column]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Primary Explosive Lead Azide or DDNP DDT]
    I --> J[Base Charge PETN or RDX Detonation at 7000 m per s]
    J --> K{Booster Present?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Booster Cast Pentolite or RDX Wax]
    K -->|No| N[Direct to Main Charge Cap Sensitive Only]
    L --> M[Main Charge ANFO or Emulsion]
    N --> M
    M --> O[Detonation Wave at 3000 to 6000 m per s]
    O --> P[Gas Pressure Expansion at 10 to 30 GPa]
    P --> Q[Rock Fragmentation and Heave]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of an Electric Delay Detonator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Leg Wires Copper] --> S2[Sulfur Seal Plug]
    S2 --> S3[Bridge Wire Match Head]
    S3 --> S4[Pyrotechnic Delay Column]
    S4 --> S5[Lead Azide Primary Charge]
    S5 --> S6[PETN Base Charge]
    S6 --> S7[Open End Bottom of Shell]
```

### 4.3 Functional Map of Detonating Cord Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Starter Detonator] --> T2[Trunkline Detonating Cord on Surface]
    T2 --> T3{Each Hole Tied In}
    T3 --> H1[Hole 1 Downline Cord]
    T3 --> H2[Hole 2 Downline Cord]
    T3 --> H3[Hole 3 Downline Cord]
    H1 --> B1[Booster at Bottom of Hole 1]
    H2 --> B2[Booster at Bottom of Hole 2]
    H3 --> B3[Booster at Bottom of Hole 3]
    B1 --> M1[Main Charge ANFO]
    B2 --> M2[Main Charge Emulsion]
    B3 --> M3[Main Charge ANFO Emulsion Mix]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Explosive Sensitivity (Critical Safety Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Primary Explosives Most Sensitive Example Lead Azide Mercury Fulminate DDNP] --> L2[Secondary Explosives Moderate Sensitivity Example PETN RDX HMX Tetryl]
    L2 --> L3[Tertiary Explosives Least Sensitive Example ANFO Heavy ANFO Emulsion Bulk Slurries]
    L1 -->|Initiated by| M1[Heat Spark Impact Friction]
    L2 -->|Initiated by| M2[Shock Wave from Primary Booster]
    L3 -->|Initiated by| M3[Strong Shock from Booster Detonation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block is a *functional module*. The arrows indicate *energy/signal flow direction*, not physical placement. The triangles on the right show the *initiation dependency* chain — i.e., tertiary explosives cannot be initiated by the same stimulus as primary explosives, which is why the booster exists in the chain.
