## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Q: Differentiate between Primary and Secondary explosives with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Primary Explosives** are highly sensitive explosives that can be readily initiated by a small amount of heat, spark, friction, or impact, and they undergo a very rapid **deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT)**.

- Examples: Lead Azide Pb(N₃)₂, Mercury Fulminate Hg(ONC)₂, DDNP.
- Used in tiny quantities inside detonator caps only.

**Secondary Explosives** are much less sensitive, requiring a strong shock wave from a primary or booster to detonate. They are used as the main explosive charge.

- Examples: TNT, PETN, RDX, HMX.
- Used as the base charge of detonators, as boosters, and as bulk explosives.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "TNT is a primary explosive." This is wrong — TNT is secondary. Memorize: **Primary = cap contents (Lead Azide, Mercury Fulminate, DDNP)**. Secondary = bulk (TNT, PETN, RDX, HMX, ANFO, Emulsion).

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q]`

**Q: What is a booster and why is it essential for initiating ANFO?**

**Model Answer:**

A **booster** is a small, dense, high-explosive charge (typically **cast Pentolite 50/50 PETN/TNT** or **pressed RDX/Wax 95/5**, of mass 50–800 g) used to amplify the detonator's output into a stable shock wave capable of initiating the main explosive charge.

It is essential for ANFO because:
1. ANFO has a **large critical diameter (~25–50 mm in steel)** and is **not cap-sensitive**.
2. The detonator's output pressure is only ~1–2 GPa, while ANFO requires **>5 GPa** for reliable initiation.
3. A booster provides **C-J pressure of ~10–15 GPa**, which is sufficient to drive ANFO to stable detonation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice):** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of an **electric delay detonator**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of a **detonating cord** in a blasting circuit. Discuss its construction, properties, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of an Electric Delay Detonator:**

The electric delay detonator consists of the following components (top to bottom of the shell):

1. **Leg Wires (0.5 m copper, twin-core):** Carry the firing current from the blasting machine to the bridge wire.
2. **Sulfur–Aluminum Seal Plug:** Provides waterproofing and prevents gas leakage.
3. **Bridge Wire + Match Head:** A nichrome/platinum-iridium wire (resistance ~1 Ω) embedded in a pyrotechnic ignition head (lead thiocyanate + PbO₂ + Sb₂S₃).
4. **Delay Element:** A pressed column of pyrotechnic composition (typically Si/PbO₂/Sb₂S₃ or B/PbCrO₄) that burns at a controlled rate, providing a fixed time delay (25 ms increments from 0 to 1,000 ms).
5. **Primary Charge:** ~0.20 g of Lead Azide or DDNP.
6. **Base Charge:** ~0.50 g of PETN, pressed to ~1.5 g/cm³.
7. **Aluminum or Copper Shell:** Length ~50–80 mm, diameter ~7 mm.

**Working:** When current flows ($I \geq 0.45$ A for $\geq 50$ ms), the bridge wire heats per $Q = I^2Rt$. The match head ignites. The delay element burns for the specified milliseconds. The primary charge DDTs to the base charge, which detonates at ~7,000 m/s, sending a detonation wave into the booster or main charge.

**(b) Detonating Cord:**

**Construction:** A flexible cord with a textile / synthetic fiber outer sheath and a **PETN core** (5–100 g/m, typically 10 g/m for surface trunklines and 40 g/m for down-the-hole).

**Properties:**
- VOD = **6,000–7,000 m/s**.
- Tensile strength > 50 kg.
- Waterproof outer sheath.
- Detonation is unaffected by electrical interference.

**Applications:**
- **Surface trunkline delay system** connecting multiple holes.
- **Down-the-hole initiation** — cord lowered into the borehole to initiate bottom charge.
- **Deck loading** in deep holes with stemming columns.
- **Mud-capping and adobe blasting** for secondary blasting of boulders.
- **Underground blasting** where electric detonators are unsafe due to stray currents.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** With a block diagram, explain the **hierarchy of initiation** in a commercial blasting circuit from blasting machine to main charge. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **booster**? Derive the **Chapman–Jouguet pressure** for a typical Pentolite booster and comment on its adequacy to initiate ANFO. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchy of Initiation (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

The hierarchy proceeds as:
**Blasting Machine (energy source)** → **Initiator (electric/Nonel/electronic)** → **Bridge wire + Match Head (Tier 1)** → **Delay Element (pyrotechnic column)** → **Primary Explosive (Lead Azide)** → **Base Charge (PETN/RDX)** → **Booster (Cast Pentolite)** → **Main Charge (ANFO/Emulsion)**.

Each stage amplifies the energy/power level from milliwatts to gigawatts, ensuring the bulk charge receives a sufficiently strong and stable shock wave.

**(b) Booster and C-J Pressure Derivation:**

A booster is a dense secondary explosive charge (e.g., 150 g cast Pentolite, density 1.65 g/cm³, VOD 7,500 m/s) used to amplify the detonator output.

**Derivation:** From conservation of mass and momentum across a steady detonation front, with the C-J sonic condition:

$$
P_{cj} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\rho_{0}\, V_D^{\,2}}{2\,(\gamma \,+\, 1)}
$$

Substituting $\rho_0 = 1{,}650 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $V_D = 7{,}500 \;\text{m/s}$, $\gamma = 3$:

$$
P_{cj} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1{,}650 \times (7{,}500)^2}{2\,(3 \,+\, 1)} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}650 \times 5.625 \times 10^{7}}{8} \;\approx\; 1.16 \times 10^{10}\;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 11.6\;\text{GPa}
$$

**Adequacy:** ANFO requires an initiation shock pressure of **~5 GPa** for stable detonation. Since the booster provides **~11.6 GPa**, it is more than adequate (a safety factor of ~2.3×) to initiate ANFO reliably.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. Confusing **primary** vs. **secondary** explosives (loses 1–2 marks instantly).
> 2. Writing $Q = IR$ instead of $Q = I^2Rt$ in Joule's Law derivation.
> 3. Forgetting the unit of detonation pressure (**GPa**, not MPa).
> 4. Stating that ANFO is cap-sensitive (it is **NOT**; this is a high-marks-loss mistake).
> 5. Drawing the detonator diagram **without labelling the delay element** — the examiner specifically looks for this.
> 6. Writing "detonating cord contains TNT" (it contains **PETN**, not TNT).
> 7. Mixing up **detonation velocity** (VOD) values between detonating cord (~6,500 m/s) and detonator base charge (~7,000 m/s).
> 8. Not mentioning **safety fuse burn rate of 90 s/foot (100 s/m)** when asked about safety fuse — examiners deduct marks if this standard value is missing.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mechanical/Explosive Devices** are the *non-bulk control accessories* that initiate, propagate, delay, or amplify detonation.
- The **hierarchy** is: Blasting Machine → Initiator → Bridge Wire → Delay Element → Primary Explosive (Lead Azide) → Base Charge (PETN/RDX) → Booster → Main Charge.
- **Electric Detonator** uses $Q = I^2Rt$ (Joule heating); standard all-fire current is **0.45 A for 50 ms**.
- **Non-Electric (Nonel)** uses a shock tube; immune to RF/stray current — preferred for gassy mines.
- **Electronic Detonators** offer ± 0.1 ms accuracy — used for precision blasting.
- **Detonating Cord** has a **PETN core (5–100 g/m)** and VOD of **6,000–7,000 m/s**.
- **Booster** (Cast Pentolite, 50–800 g) provides C-J pressure of **~11.6 GPa**, easily initiating ANFO.
- **Safety Fuse** burns at **90 s/foot = 100 s/m** — provides retreat time for the operator.
- **Critical Diameter** of ANFO is **25–50 mm** — this is why boosters are mandatory.
- Always remember: **Primary = sensitive, cap contents; Secondary = bulk, requires booster**.
- The four C's of safe blasting: **Competence, Care, Caution, Compliance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification, Transportation and Storage of Explosives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification, Transportation and Storage of Explosives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Classification, Transportation and Storage of Explosives** refers to the systematic categorization of explosive substances and articles into defined hazard classes (as per UN/DOT and the Indian Explosives Rules, 2008), coupled with the regulated procedures governing their packaging, labeling, conveyance by approved modes of transport, and segregation in licensed storage magazines, to ensure the safety of life, property, and the environment throughout the explosive's life cycle.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, this is treated as a **Statutory Safety Framework** — i.e., the legal-engineering bridge that connects the *physics of explosion* (Modules 1–2) to *practical industrial application* (Module 5, blasting & tunneling).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library of Dangerous Books"

Imagine a **library of extremely dangerous books** — some can burst into flames if you merely shake them (primary explosives), some only when subjected to severe heat and pressure (secondary explosives), and some burn slowly giving off colored smoke (pyrotechnics). You wouldn't:

- **Shelve them randomly** (this is *Storage* — magazines with separation distances).
- **Send them through regular post** (this is *Transportation* — specialized carriers, licensed vehicles, placards).
- **Forget to label their hazard level** (this is *Classification* — UN Division 1.1, 1.2, 1.3 etc.).

The library's *catalog system* is **Classification**. The *security protocol for moving books between branches* is **Transportation**. The *fortified, isolated reading room with fire-proof walls* is **Storage**.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars — At a Glance

| Pillar | Governing Body | Core Concern | Key Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | UN Committee of Experts | Hazard identification | UN Recommendations on Transport of Dangerous Goods (Orange Book) |
| **Transportation** | IATA / IMDG / ADR / Indian Central Motor Vehicles Rules | Safe conveyance | Explosives Rules 2008 (India), PESO |
| **Storage** | PESO (Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation, India) | Safe segregation | Explosives Rules 2008, Schedule IV |

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants to Memorize (Board-Favorite):**
> - **UN Hazard Class 1** is exclusively reserved for *explosives* (1 of the 9 UN classes).
> - **6 Hazard Divisions** exist: 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6.
> - **13 Compatibility Groups** (A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, N, S) govern mixed storage.
> - **Licensing authority in India:** **PESO**, Nagpur (under DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce & Industry).
> - **Storage quantity trigger:** Any storage above the *small quantity exemption limit* (Schedule I, Rule 4 of ER 2008) requires a license.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Explosives — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 A. Classification by **Physical State**

1. **Solid Explosives** — TNT, RDX, PETN, ANFO (bulk), gunpowder, detonators.
2. **Liquid Explosives** — Nitroglycerin (NG), nitromethane, hydrogen peroxide (high concentration), isopropyl nitrate.
3. **Gaseous/Vapor Explosives** — Acetylene, propane, hydrogen, methane (in air at stoichiometric ratios).
4. **Gels/Slurries/Emulsions** — Water gels, emulsion explosives (modern bulk industrial type).

### 2.1.2 B. Classification by **Sensitivity** (Academically Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked** classification in KTU exams.

| Type | Initiation Required | Examples | Detonation Velocity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (Initiating) Explosives** | Very low — heat, spark, friction, impact | Lead Azide (PbN₆), Lead Styphnate, Mercury Fulminate, Diazodinitrophenol (DDNP) | ~4,500–5,500 m/s |
| **Secondary (High) Explosives** | Detonator or booster required | RDX (Cyclonite), HMX, PETN, TNT, Tetryl | ~6,000–9,100 m/s |
| **Tertiary (Blasting Agents)** | Strong booster, cannot be detonated by a No. 8 cap | ANFO, slurry, emulsion, black powder (deflagration) | ~2,000–5,500 m/s |

> **The "Why" behind the classification:** Sensitivity correlates inversely with *brisance* (shattering power). Primary explosives are *so sensitive* they cannot be used in bulk for blasting because the slightest stimulus during handling would cause mass detonation. Secondary explosives are *less sensitive* but pack far more energy — perfect for bulk use in mining.

### 2.1.3 C. Classification by **Velocity of Detonation (VOD)**

- **High Explosives** (VOD > 1,000 m/s, typically 4,000–9,000 m/s) — *detonating* type.
- **Low Explosives** (VOD < 1,000 m/s, typically 300–800 m/s) — *deflagrating* type.
  - e.g., Black powder, smokeless propellants, pyrotechnic compositions.

### 2.1.4 D. UN/DOT Classification (The Global Standard — Board Essential)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UN Hazard Class 1 — Explosives — Six Divisions:**

| Division | Hazard Description | Mass Explosion | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.1** | Mass explosion hazard | YES — entire load explodes virtually instantaneously | TNT, dynamite, RDX, ANFO |
| **1.2** | Projection hazard, but NOT mass explosion | NO | Detonating cord, rocket motors, aerial bombs |
| **1.3** | Mass fire hazard | NO (mostly burning) | Smokeless powder, propellants, flash powders |
| **1.4** | Minor explosion hazard | NO — limited to package | Small arms ammunition, signal flares, consumer fireworks |
| **1.5** | Very insensitive mass-explosive substance | YES (theoretically, but hard to initiate) | ANFO, blasting agents, emulsion explosives |
| **1.6** | Extremely insensitive detonating articles | NO individual mass explosion | Explosive bolts in Apollo spacecraft |

### 2.1.5 E. Compatibility Groups (Mixed-Storage Code, A–S)

Each explosive is assigned a **Compatibility Group Letter** indicating *which other groups* it can be stored with.

- **A** — Primary explosives (e.g., lead azide).
- **B** — Articles containing primary explosives (e.g., detonators).
- **D** — Secondary detonating explosives without sensitive component (e.g., dynamite, TNT).
- **G** — Pyrotechnic substances, flash powders.
- **L, N, S** — Special categories.

> **Rule of Thumb:** Group A can NEVER be stored with Group D (primary vs. secondary risk). Compatibility groups are *preserved* across all 3 life-cycle stages — manufacturing, transport, storage.

---

## 2.2 Transportation of Explosives — Structured Logic

### 2.2.1 The 5-Step Safe Transport Cycle

1. **Pre-shipment Risk Assessment** — classify material, identify UN number, assign packing group.
2. **Packaging & Marking** — UN-certified packaging, hazard labels (Class 1 diamond, **orange**, **black explosive symbol**, division number on bottom corner).
3. **Documentation** — Consignor's declaration, transport emergency card (TREM card), Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS).
4. **Approved Carrier** — licensed vehicle, trained driver with *Explosives Driver's Certificate*, specific color code (often red body or specific placards).
5. **Route & Time Compliance** — avoid populated zones, night transport discouraged, escort vehicles for large quantities.

### 2.2.2 Mode-of-Transport Matrix

| Mode | Governing Code (Global) | Governing Code (India) | Key Restriction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Road** | ADR (Europe), UN Model Reg. | Central Motor Vehicles Rules + ER 2008 Rule 196–201 | Max 200 kg of dynamite per vehicle (typical), driver endurance test |
| **Rail** | RID | Indian Railways Red Tariff Rules | Explosive vans attached at end of train, 2 attendants per wagon |
| **Sea** | IMDG Code | Merchant Shipping Rules | Stowage category A–E; segregation table |
| **Air** | IATA DGR | Aircraft Rules (DGCA) | **Cargo aircraft only** for most Class 1, max quantity per package strictly limited |

### 2.2.3 The UN Label (Recognize & Draw — Board Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE]
> A diamond-shaped label **100 mm × 100 mm** (small packages) or **250 mm × 250 mm** (large), **orange background**, with:
> - A black **bursting star symbol** (3-pointed, with 6 stubby rays) at the top half.
> - The **number "1"** in the bottom corner.
> - The **Division number** in the bottom corner (e.g., "1.1", "1.4").
> - The **Compatibility group letter** below the division (e.g., "1.4G" for small arms ammunition).

---

## 2.3 Storage of Explosives — Structured Logic

### 2.3.1 The Two Magazine Types

| Type | Purpose | Construction |
|---|---|---|
| **Permanent Magazine** | Long-term, large-quantity storage | Earth-covered (mounded) or above-ground concrete with **traversing/lightning protection** |
| **Temporary Magazine** | Day-storage near work site, < 200 kg | Light steel structure, located 200–500 m from working face |

### 2.3.2 The Concept of "Quantity-Distance" (Q-D) — **The Most Tested Concept**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantity-Distance (Q-D) Principle:**
> The minimum safe separation distance (D) between two explosive storage locations (or between a magazine and a protected building/road/railway) is a **function of the quantity of explosives (Q)** and the **type of barrier** (natural earth mound, sand-wall, or nothing).

The general relationship is:

$$D = k \cdot Q^{n}$$

where:
- $D$ = separation distance (m).
- $Q$ = net explosive quantity (kg, NEQ).
- $k$ = constant (depends on barrier type, division, occupancy).
- $n$ = exponent (**typically 1/3 for earth-covered magazines**, **1/2 for barricaded, 1/3 for unbarricaded**).

For an **unbarricaded** magazine (most conservative — what the Board will likely test):

$$D = k \cdot Q^{1/3}$$

For a **barricaded** magazine:

$$D = k \cdot Q^{1/3} \quad \text{(slightly lower } k \text{)}$$

The **$Q^{1/3}$ law** arises because the blast wave energy scales with the cube-root of the explosive charge at a given scaled distance:

$$Z = \frac{D}{Q^{1/3}}$$

where $Z$ is the **scaled distance** (m/kg$^{1/3}$). Critical $Z$ values:

- $Z \ge 1.0$ — minimal property damage.
- $Z \ge 3.0$ — very low damage.
- $Z \ge 8.0$ — practically no damage to heavy structures.

### 2.3.3 Indian Specifics (PESO, Schedule IV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Schedule IV of Explosives Rules, 2008 (India) specifies:**
> - **Single largest quantity** in a permanent magazine: **typically 5,000 kg** (Class 1.1) with a 15 m earth cover.
> - **Distance from inhabited buildings:** minimum **0.5 × Q$^{1/3}$ km** for barricaded; **1.0 × Q$^{1/3}$ km** for unbarricaded (illustrative).
> - **Distance from railways & public roads:** same formula.
> - **Distance between two magazines** (inter-magazine): less than above; subject to *sum-of-quantities rule* for mixed storage.
> - **Lightning protection:** mandatory earth-rod system with resistance < 10 Ω.
> - **Ventilation:** natural, with **lightning-conducting** ventilation openings (no metal ducts entering the magazine).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| UN Class | $\text{UN Class 1 — Explosives}$ | — | 1 of 9 UN hazard classes |
| Division | $1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6$ | — | Always paired with compatibility group |
| Scaled distance | $Z = D / Q^{1/3}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | Cube-root law for blast scaling |
| Inter-magazine distance (unbarricaded) | $D = k_u \cdot Q^{1/3}$ | m | $k_u$ from PESO table |
| Energy yield of TNT | $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4.184 \times 10^6$ | J/kg | Reference explosive |
| Trinitrotoluene (TNT) oxygen balance | $\text{OB}_{\text{TNT}} = -74\%$ | % | Negative — oxygen-deficient |
| NG oxygen balance | $\text{OB}_{\text{NG}} = +3.5\%$ | % | Near zero — ideal |
| Brisance (Sand crush test) | $\beta = \rho_{\text{exp}} \cdot V_{\text{det}}^2$ | Pa | Proportional to shattering effect |
| Kistiakowsky–Wilson rule | $p_{CJ} \approx 0.31 \cdot \rho_0 \cdot V_d^2$ | Pa | CJ pressure (related, used in module 2 — recall) |
| Detonation pressure | $p_{CJ} = \frac{\rho_0 V_d^2}{4}$ (approx) | Pa | Chapman–Jouguet |
| Brisance of lead block (Kast) | Brisance = $V_{\text{exp}} \cdot \rho_{\text{exp}} \cdot V_d^2$ | g·m²/s² | Kast formula |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook for Cube-Root Law:**
> $D \propto Q^{1/3}$ — *Why cube-root?* Because doubling the charge doubles the *radius* of the dangerous pressure zone (volume scales with r³, so r ∝ Q$^{1/3}$). This is the **Hopkinson-Cranz scaling law**, universally used in military and civil blasting.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mining & Quarrying:** Determines the *legal buffer* between an emulsion magazine and the nearest village.
- **Defense Storage:** Q-D values for ammunition depots are computed using the same formula — India's vast ammunition depots follow **Indian Army Technical Instructions (TIs)** which are direct applications of the $Q^{1/3}$ law.
- **Civil Demolition:** A *licensee* in India must store dynamite at a minimum Q-D from any public road, railway, or high-tension line.
- **Pyrotechnic Manufacturing:** The classification of consumer fireworks as UN 1.4G vs. display fireworks as 1.3G decides their storage quantity, vehicle, and route.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Cube-Root Scaling Law (Hopkinson-Cranz)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **gold standard** for 14-mark KTU questions. Showing this step-by-step demonstrates mastery of "Apply" and "Analyze" Bloom levels.

### Step 1 — Starting Premise (Cubic Similarity)

The blast wave is assumed to be a **self-similar phenomenon**. If we double every linear dimension, the energy must scale as the cube of the linear scale factor.

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^3$$

### Step 2 — Rearranging for Distance

$$\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^3 = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1}$$

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$R_2 = R_1 \cdot \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

### Step 3 — Defining Scaled Distance

A **reference charge $Q_1 = 1$ kg** is chosen. The distance from this reference is $R_1 = Z$ (the *scaled distance*).

$$R_2 = Z \cdot (Q_2)^{1/3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z = \frac{R_2}{Q_2^{1/3}}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Form

For a given $Z$ value that corresponds to a particular damage threshold:

$$\boxed{D = Z \cdot Q^{1/3}}$$

where $D$ is in metres, $Q$ in kg, and $Z$ in m/kg$^{1/3}$.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A mining magazine stores **5,000 kg** of ANFO. Compute the safe distance to a village using an unbarricaded $Z = 8$ m/kg$^{1/3}$.

**Solution (each step shown):**

$$D = Z \cdot Q^{1/3}$$

$$Q^{1/3} = (5000)^{1/3}$$

$$5000 = 5 \times 10^3$$

$$(5 \times 10^3)^{1/3} = 5^{1/3} \times (10^3)^{1/3}$$

$$5^{1/3} \approx 1.71$$

$$(10^3)^{1/3} = 10$$

$$\therefore Q^{1/3} = 1.71 \times 10 = 17.1 \text{ kg}^{1/3}$$

$$D = 8 \times 17.1 = 136.8 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 137 \text{ m}}$$

**Sanity Check:** If quantity is doubled to 10,000 kg:

$$D_2 = 8 \times (10000)^{1/3} = 8 \times 21.54 = 172.3 \text{ m}$$

The distance only increased by **26%** for a **100%** increase in charge — confirming the sub-linear cube-root relationship.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Compatibility Group Storage Logic

**Logical steps in determining mixed-storage safety:**

1. Identify the UN Division (1.1–1.6) of each substance in the magazine.
2. Identify the Compatibility Group (A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, N, S) of each.
3. Consult the **UN Mixed Storage Table** (typically a 13×13 matrix).
4. If the cell is **"X"** — *Permitted*. If **"O"** — *Forbidden*. If blank — *Conditional*.
5. Apply the **Sum-of-Quantities Rule** when permitted together:
   $$Q_{\text{eff}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \cdot Q_i$$
   where $\alpha_i$ is a weighting factor (typically 1.0 for Division 1.1, lower for 1.3, 1.4).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Storing an Unknown Explosive

| Step | Question | Possible Answers | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is mass explosion possible? | YES → 1.1; NO | Go to step 2 |
| 2 | Are projections the dominant hazard? | YES → 1.2; NO | Go to step 3 |
| 3 | Is mass fire the dominant hazard? | YES → 1.3; NO | Go to step 4 |
| 4 | Minor hazard only? | YES → 1.4; NO | Go to step 5 |
| 5 | Mass explosion but very insensitive? | YES → 1.5; NO | Go to step 6 |
| 6 | Articles only, not substances, extremely insensitive? | YES → 1.6 | End |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Calculating the Q-D for Two Magazines (Sum-of-Weights)

**Given:** Magazine A holds **2,000 kg** of ANFO (UN 1.5D, weight factor 1.0); Magazine B holds **500 kg** of emulsion (UN 1.1D, weight factor 1.0). The magazines are both unbarricaded. Use $Z = 8$ m/kg$^{1/3}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the effective quantity.

$$Q_{\text{eff}} = (1.0 \times 2000) + (1.0 \times 500) = 2500 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Compute separation distance.

$$D = 8 \times (2500)^{1/3}$$

$$(2500)^{1/3} = (2.5 \times 10^3)^{1/3} = 2.5^{1/3} \times 10$$

$$2.5^{1/3} \approx 1.357$$

$$Q^{1/3} = 13.57$$

$$D = 8 \times 13.57 = 108.6 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 109 \text{ m}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (UN System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Hazardous Material Arrives for Classification] --> A1{Is it an Explosive Substance or Article?}
    A1 -- NO --> A2[Other UN Class 2-9]
    A1 -- YES --> A3[UN Class 1 - Explosives]
    A3 --> A4{Does it have mass explosion potential?}
    A4 -- YES --> A5[Division 1.1 - Mass Explosion]
    A4 -- NO --> A6{Is projection the main hazard?}
    A6 -- YES --> A7[Division 1.2 - Projection Hazard]
    A6 -- NO --> A8{Is mass fire the main hazard?}
    A8 -- YES --> A9[Division 1.3 - Mass Fire]
    A8 -- NO --> A10{Hazard present but minor?}
    A10 -- YES --> A11[Division 1.4 - Minor Explosion]
    A10 -- NO --> A12{Extremely insensitive mass explosive?}
    A12 -- YES --> A13[Division 1.5 - Very Insensitive]
    A12 -- NO --> A14[Division 1.6 - Extremely Insensitive Articles]
    A5 --> A15[Assign Compatibility Group A-S]
    A7 --> A15
    A9 --> A15
    A11 --> A15
    A13 --> A15
    A14 --> A15
    A15 --> A16[Apply UN Number and Label]
```

## 4.2 Transportation Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Manufacturer] --> B1{Packaging and Marking}
    B1 --> B2[UN Certified Drum or Box]
    B2 --> B3[Hazard Diamond Label 1.1 to 1.6]
    B3 --> B4[Documentation - MSDS, TREM Card]
    B4 --> B5[Licensed Carrier with Placarded Vehicle]
    B5 --> B6{Traffic Mode}
    B6 -- Road --> B7[ADR Compliance - India: ER 2008]
    B6 -- Rail --> B8[RID Compliance - India: Red Tariff]
    B6 -- Sea --> B9[IMDG Code Compliance]
    B6 -- Air --> B10[IATA DGR - Cargo Aircraft Only]
    B7 --> B11[Receiver Magazine]
    B8 --> B11
    B9 --> B11
    B10 --> B11
    B11 --> B12[PESO License Verification]
    B12 --> B13[Storage in Compatible Magazine]
```

## 4.3 Magazine Layout — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C0[Earth-Covered Permanent Magazine Layout]
    C0 --> C1[Mound of Earth - 1.5 m Minimum]
    C0 --> C2[Concrete Roof Slab - 15 cm]
    C0 --> C3[Inner Chamber - Explosives Storage]
    C0 --> C4[Double Steel Door with Combination Lock]
    C0 --> C5[Ventilation - Lightning Protected]
    C0 --> C6[Earth Rod - Resistance below 10 Ohm]
    C0 --> C7[Sand Bucket and Water Hose]
    C0 --> C8[Warning Sign - EXPLOSIVES - NO SMOKING]
    C0 --> C9[Exclusion Zone of Distance D = Z Q1/3]
    C9 --> C10[Nearest Inhabited Building]
    C9 --> C11[Public Road and Rail Line]
```

## 4.4 Life-Cycle of an Explosive (Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Manufacture] --> D1[Classification UN]
    D1 --> D2[Packaging and Labeling]
    D2 --> D3[Storage in Magazine]
    D3 --> D4[Transportation to Site]
    D4 --> D5[Day Storage Near Face]
    D5 --> D6[Use in Blasting or Demolition]
    D6 --> D7[Post-Use Detritus - Disposal via PESO]
    D3 -.same classification.-> D4
    D4 -.same classification.-> D5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Primary and Secondary explosives. Give two examples of each.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Primary (Initiating) Explosives | Secondary (High) Explosives |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** | Extremely sensitive to heat, friction, spark, impact | Relatively insensitive — needs detonator/booster |
| **Brisance** | Low | High |
| **Use** | Initiate secondary explosives (in detonators, blasting caps) | Main charge in blasting, military warheads |
| **Detonation Velocity** | Lower (4,500–5,500 m/s) | Higher (6,000–9,100 m/s) |
| **Examples** | Lead azide (PbN₆), Mercury fulminate, Lead styphnate, DDNP | TNT, RDX, PETN, HMX, Dynamite |

---

### Q2. What is meant by UN Compatibility Group 'D'? Name two substances belonging to it.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**UN Compatibility Group D** consists of **secondary detonating explosive substances** that are *insensitive to initiation* and do **not contain** a primary explosive component. They can be mass-detonated and require a separate initiator.

**Examples:**
1. **TNT (Trinitrotoluene)** — a classic military and commercial secondary explosive.
2. **Dynamite (Nitroglycerin-based gel)** — a standard secondary explosive used in mining and construction.

Other members: ANFO, Composition B, Tetryl, PETN.

> **Key Rule:** Group D explosives **can NEVER be stored with Group A (primary explosives)** as the primary may sympathetically initiate the secondary.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **UN classification of explosives** with all six hazard divisions. Give at least one example for each division. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **Quantity-Distance (Q-D) principle** used in the storage of explosives. Derive the cube-root scaling law. A barricaded magazine stores **1,000 kg** of an explosive. If the scaled distance $Z = 6$ m/kg$^{1/3}$, calculate the safe separation distance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

The United Nations Committee of Experts on the Transport of Dangerous Goods has classified all explosives under **UN Class 1**, which is further divided into **six hazard divisions** based on the *type and severity of hazard* they present:

**Division 1.1 — Mass Explosion Hazard:** Substances and articles that present a *mass explosion hazard* — meaning the entire load virtually detonates simultaneously. **Example:** TNT, RDX, dynamite, ANFO.

**Division 1.2 — Projection Hazard (Non-Mass Explosion):** Items that do not mass-explode but produce fragments, shrapnel, or projections at significant velocities. **Example:** Detonating cord (without initiating charge), rocket motor assemblies, aerial bombs.

**Division 1.3 — Mass Fire Hazard:** Substances whose dominant hazard is a *mass fire* (large-volume burning, intense heat) without significant blast or projection. **Example:** Smokeless propellant, magnesium flash powder, most pyrotechnic flares.

**Division 1.4 — Minor Explosion Hazard:** Items that present *only a minor explosion hazard* confined to the package, with no significant fragments or blast outside. **Example:** Small arms ammunition (1.4S), consumer fireworks, signal flares.

**Division 1.5 — Very Insensitive Mass-Explosive Substances:** Substances that have a *mass explosion hazard* but are *so insensitive* that there is very low probability of accidental initiation. **Example:** ANFO (bulk), some emulsion explosives under specific conditions.

**Division 1.6 — Extremely Insensitive Articles:** Articles containing only *extremely insensitive detonating substances* and demonstrating a *negligible probability* of accidental initiation or propagation. **Example:** Explosive bolts used in spacecraft separation systems.

Each substance also receives a **Compatibility Group letter (A–S)** indicating which other groups it may be safely stored/transported with.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**The Quantity-Distance Principle (Q-D):**

The Q-D principle is the **cornerstone of explosive safety** in storage and siting. It states that the *minimum permissible separation distance* $D$ between a magazine (or explosive store) and a *target* (a building, road, railway, public area) is a function of:

- The **net explosive quantity (NEQ)**, denoted $Q$, in the magazine.
- The **type of protection/barrier** (earth mound, sand barricade, or none).
- The **occupancy class** of the target (inhabited building, public road, rail line, magazine, etc.).

The engineering formulation arises from the **Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root scaling law**.

**Derivation:**

Assume the blast wave is a *geometrically self-similar* phenomenon. If we have two charges $Q_1$ and $Q_2$, their *equivalent blast radii* $R_1$ and $R_2$ satisfy the cubic similarity relation:

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^3$$

(Volume, and hence the energy, scales as the cube of the linear dimension.)

Rearranging:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^{1/3}$$

Define a **reference charge** $Q_1 = 1$ kg at a reference distance $R_1 = Z$ (the *scaled distance*):

$$R_2 = Z \cdot Q_2^{1/3}$$

In engineering form:

$$\boxed{D = Z \cdot Q^{1/3}}$$

where $D$ is in metres, $Q$ in kg, and $Z$ in m/kg$^{1/3}$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $Q = 1{,}000$ kg
- $Z = 6$ m/kg$^{1/3}$

Substituting:

$$D = 6 \times (1000)^{1/3}$$

$$(1000)^{1/3} = 10$$

$$D = 6 \times 10 = 60 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D = 60 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Using $Q^{1/2}$ instead of $Q^{1/3}$ (square-root law applies only to *very close-in* cratering effects, not blast-distance separation).
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting to take the *cube root* and simply multiplying $Z \times Q$. The Board deducts **2 marks** for this error.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Not specifying the units of $Z$ in the final answer.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Confusing the "barricaded" vs. "unbarricaded" magazine in the question — the $Z$ value changes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Describe the **sensitivity-based classification of explosives** with examples. Why are primary explosives never used as the main charge in blasting? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **modes of transport for explosives** with reference to Indian regulations (Explosives Rules 2008). What is the role of PESO in the storage licensing process? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Sensitivity-Based Classification of Explosives:**

Explosives are classified by their **sensitivity to external stimuli** (impact, friction, heat, electrostatic discharge) into three principal categories:

**1. Primary (Initiating) Explosives:** These are the *most sensitive* explosives. They can be detonated by a simple spark, heat, friction, or a light mechanical impact. They have *low brisance* and a relatively *low detonation velocity* (4,500–5,500 m/s). They are used to *initiate* larger charges of secondary explosives. Examples: Lead azide (PbN₆), Mercury fulminate (Hg(ONC)₂), Lead styphnate, Diazodinitrophenol (DDNP).

**2. Secondary (High) Explosives:** These are *less sensitive* to external stimuli and require a *detonator or booster* to initiate. They possess *high brisance* and *high detonation velocity* (6,000–9,100 m/s). They are the workhorses of commercial and military blasting. Examples: TNT (Trinitrotoluene), RDX (Cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine), HMX, PETN, Dynamite (gelatinous NG-based), Composition B, Tetryl.

**3. Tertiary (Blasting Agents):** These are the *least sensitive*. They cannot be detonated by a standard No. 8 blasting cap and require a *strong booster charge*. They release energy by *deflagration-to-detonation transition* (DDT) under confinement. Examples: ANFO (Ammonium Nitrate + Fuel Oil), water-gel slurries, emulsion explosives.

**Why Primary Explosives Are Never Used as Main Charge in Blasting:**

1. **Extreme sensitivity** — The slightest unintended stimulus during handling, transport, or charging of a blast hole would cause *premature initiation*, endangering the entire blast crew.
2. **Low brisance** — They lack the *shattering energy* needed to fracture hard rock, which is the principal purpose of a commercial blast.
3. **Cost and availability** — They are *expensive* and *scarce* compared to secondary explosives.
4. **Stability issues** — Many primary explosives (e.g., mercury fulminate) *degrade* with time and temperature, becoming unreliable.
5. **Regulatory prohibition** — PESO and UN frameworks *forbid* the bulk storage of primary explosives for blasting.

Hence, primary explosives are used *only in detonators and blasting caps* in *milligram quantities* to trigger the much larger, safer secondary explosive charge.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Modes of Transport for Explosives (Indian Regulations — Explosives Rules 2008):**

The transport of explosives in India is governed by the **Explosives Rules, 2008 (ER 2008)** framed under the **Explosives Act, 1884** and administered by **PESO (Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation)**, headquartered in Nagpur.

**1. Road Transport:**
- Vehicle must be **specifically designed or adapted** — closed body, no exposed metal fittings, fire-resistant.
- Vehicle must display **hazard placards** on all four sides (Class 1, Division number).
- Driver must hold a valid **Explosives Driver's Certificate** issued by the licensing authority.
- A **competent attendant** (or two for certain quantities) must accompany the consignment.
- Maximum quantity per vehicle is regulated by Schedule (e.g., typically **200 kg of dynamite** per standard licensed truck).
- **No smoking, no open flames**; the vehicle must carry **fire extinguishers** (CO₂ or dry powder).
- Route restrictions: avoid **densely populated areas, schools, hospitals, places of worship**; specific timing constraints may apply.

**2. Rail Transport:**
- Explosives are carried in **special wagons** (typically marked 'EX') attached at the **end of the train formation**.
- Two **armed guards** (explosives-trained) accompany each wagon.
- Speed restrictions and shunting rules apply (no loose shunting near the explosives wagon).
- Governed by the **Indian Railways Red Tariff Rules**.

**3. Sea Transport:**
- Governed by the **IMDG (International Maritime Dangerous Goods) Code** as adopted by the Indian Merchant Shipping Rules.
- Stowage categories (A, B, C, D, E) determine which explosive may be stowed on which deck and at what separation from passenger spaces.
- Vessels carrying explosives must be **certified** for that purpose.

**4. Air Transport:**
- Governed by the **IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations (DGR)** and India's Aircraft Rules.
- **Only cargo aircraft** may carry most Class 1 goods; **passenger aircraft** are restricted to specific 1.4 items in small quantities.
- Strict **per-package quantity limits**; aircraft must be type-certified to carry explosives.

**Role of PESO in Storage Licensing:**

The **Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO)** is the *nodal agency* of the Government of India (under DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce & Industry) responsible for the *safety oversight* of explosives and petroleum.

PESO's role in the **storage licensing process** includes:

1. **Granting licenses** for manufacture, storage, transport, sale, and use of explosives under the Explosives Act 1884 and ER 2008.
2. **Site inspection** of proposed magazine locations for compliance with **Schedule IV** (construction standards) and the **Quantity-Distance** rules.
3. **Verification of technical competence** of the applicant and key personnel (e.g., the *Controller of Explosives*-certified officer on site).
4. **Periodic safety audits** of existing magazines (typically annual).
5. **Investigation of accidents** and enforcement of corrective actions.
6. **Approval of new explosive substances** before they are introduced into Indian commerce.
7. **Approval of packaging standards** (UN-certified drums, inner packs) for explosives.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Question B:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Writing only "ER 2008" without naming the **Explosives Act 1884** — the *parent act* — which the Board considers essential.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Conflating **PESO** with **DGMS (Directorate General of Mines Safety)**. PESO governs *explosives safety*; DGMS governs *mine safety* (a related but distinct body).
> - **Common mistake #3:** Stating that passenger aircraft carry all explosives — only **1.4S** and small quantities of 1.4G are allowed on passenger flights.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Forgetting to mention the **competent attendant** and **explosives driver certificate** in road transport — the Board deducts marks for incomplete regulatory description.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **UN Class 1** is the exclusive hazard class for explosives; **6 Divisions (1.1–1.6)** and **13 Compatibility Groups (A–S)** govern classification.
- ✅ **Primary explosives** = lead azide, mercury fulminate, DDNP, lead styphnate — used in *milligram quantities* in detonators.
- ✅ **Secondary explosives** = TNT, RDX, PETN, HMX, dynamite — main charge in blasting.
- ✅ **Tertiary (blasting agents)** = ANFO, slurries, emulsions — require a *strong booster*, not a No. 8 cap.
- ✅ **Cube-root scaling law:** $D = Z \cdot Q^{1/3}$ is the *fundamental equation* of explosive storage safety.
- ✅ **PESO** (Nagpur) is India's apex explosives safety regulator; **Explosives Rules 2008** is the current rulebook.
- ✅ **Indian Roads:** licensed vehicle, **placards on 4 sides**, **explosives-driver certificate**, **competent attendant**, route restrictions.
- ✅ **Indian Railways:** explosive wagon at **train end**, 2 armed guards, no loose shunting.
- ✅ **Air transport:** **cargo aircraft only** for most Class 1 items; passenger aircraft = 1.4S/1.4G only.
- ✅ **Earth-covered magazine** is the safest storage type; lightning protection (earth rod <10 Ω) is mandatory.
- ✅ **Compatibility Group D** (e.g., dynamite, TNT) **must NOT** be stored with Group A (e.g., lead azide) — sympathic initiation risk.
- ✅ **Schedule IV of ER 2008** specifies the construction, separation, and licensing of magazines.
- ✅ For 14-mark derivation questions, **always show the cubic similarity step** explicitly — it is worth at least 3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosives Classification
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Explosives Classification

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Explosives Classification** refers to the systematic categorization of energetic materials based on their physico-chemical properties, sensitivity, velocity of reaction, oxygen balance, application, and hazard potential. As per KTU Module 4, classification is the foundational framework used to determine the safe handling, storage, transportation, and end-use application of any explosive substance.

In the context of **KTU PESFT753**, classification is treated as a **pre-emptive safety design philosophy** — every explosive is assigned to a specific class so that its *storage compatibility*, *transport category*, *handling protocol*, and *demolition application* can be uniquely determined without ambiguity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Library System":**
> Imagine a massive library with millions of books. If the books were just dumped on the floor, finding one would take hours. So, the librarian **classifies** them — by language, genre, size, and risk-level (rare, fragile, hazardous). 
>
> Similarly, an explosives engineer faces **thousands of energetic compounds**. A gelignite stick, a black powder charge, a detonator cap, and a rocket propellant are all "explosives" — but they behave completely differently. **Classification** is the librarian's system that tells the engineer:
> *How fast does it react? How sensitive is it? What is it used for? How dangerous is it in storage?*

The classification system essentially answers **four master questions** for every explosive:

1. **How does it react?** (Deflagration vs Detonation — i.e., *speed of energy release*)
2. **How easily does it react?** (Primary vs Secondary sensitivity)
3. **What does it do when it reacts?** (Push, shatter, ignite, propel)
4. **How dangerous is it to humans around it?** (UN Hazard Division 1.1 – 1.6)

## 1.3 Key Metrics Highlighted in KTU 2024 Module 4

| Parameter | Standard Value / Boundary | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Velocity of Detonation (VOD)** | **1,500 – 9,000 m/s** | Differentiates low vs high explosives |
| **Velocity of Deflagration** | **< 1,000 m/s** | Subsonic combustion reaction |
| **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)** | Critical Run-up Distance | Defines safe-to-dangerous behavior |
| **Figure of Insensitivity (F of I)** | Ratio of heights (50% drop test) | Quantifies safety in handling |
| **Oxygen Balance (OB)** | ± % of oxygen excess/deficit | Determines fume class & efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Module 4 explicitly mandates that students must be able to **differentiate**, **tabulate**, and **justify** the classification of any given explosive into at least **five** independent classification frameworks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Explosives Classification

The KTU 2024 scheme for **PESFT753 — Module 4** recognises **seven canonical classification frameworks**. Every commercial/military explosive in the world can be slotted into all seven simultaneously.

### Pillar 1 — Classification by Physical State

- **Solid Explosives:** TNT, RDX, PETN, HMX, ANFO, Emulsions.
- **Liquid Explosives:** Nitroglycerine (NG), Nitromethane, Isopropyl nitrate.
- **Gaseous Explosives:** Acetylene-Oxygen, Methane-Air mixtures, CNG explosions.
- **Why it matters:** Liquid explosives have a *high vapour pressure*, increasing **inhalation toxicity** and **fume hazard**. Storage is in sealed, vented magazines.

### Pillar 2 — Classification by Velocity of Reaction (The Most Important)

This is the **KTU board-favourite** classification.

- **Low Explosives (LEs):** React by **deflagration** — subsonic combustion wave.
  - VOD typically **300 – 1,000 m/s** (less than the speed of sound in air, ~343 m/s, but often higher than speed of sound in the medium).
  - Example: **Black powder (Gunpowder)** — 6:1:1 ratio of KNO₃ : Charcoal : Sulphur.
  - Used as **propellants** in pyrotechnics and small-arms.
- **High Explosives (HEs):** React by **detonation** — supersonic shock wave driven by chemistry.
  - VOD **1,500 – 9,500 m/s**.
  - Example: **TNT, RDX, PETN, HMX, TATB**.
  - Used as **shattering / bursting charges** in mining, demolition, and warheads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:**
> Deflagration = **subsonic push** (like a pressure cooker releasing steam).
> Detonation = **supersonic slam** (like a hammer striking at 9,000 m/s).
> The transition DDT (Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition) is the most dangerous phenomenon in propellant storage.

### Pillar 3 — Classification by Sensitivity

- **Primary Explosives:** Extremely sensitive to heat, spark, friction, impact.
  - Initiate detonation from a small stimulus. 
  - Examples: **Lead Azide (Pb(N₃)₂), Mercury Fulminate (Hg(CNO)₂), Lead Styphnate, DDNP**.
  - Used in **detonator caps and blasting caps** to initiate secondary explosives.
- **Secondary Explosives:** Relatively insensitive; require a primary explosive or booster to detonate.
  - Examples: **TNT, RDX, PETN, HMX, TATB, NQ (Nitroguanidine)**.
  - Used as **main bursting charges**.
- **Tertiary Explosives (Insensitive Munitions):** So insensitive they require a strong booster; developed for storage safety.
  - Examples: **TATB, NTO-based compositions, Ammonium Nitrate (prilled)**.

### Pillar 4 — Classification by Chemical Nature

- **Nitro Compounds (Aromatic):** TNT, Picric Acid, TATB, DNAN.
- **Nitrate Esters (Aliphatic):** NG, EGDN, PETN, NC (Nitrocellulose).
- **Nitramines (Cyclic Aliphatic):** RDX, HMX, CL-20, NQ.
- **Chlorate / Perchlorate Mixtures:** Flash powders, pyrotechnics.
- **Fuel-Oxidiser Mixtures (Binary):** ANFO, Emulsions, Slurries, Water gels.
- **Metal-Aided:** ALANFO, DBX (aluminised compositions for blast enhancement).

### Pillar 5 — Classification by Application / Use

- **Initiating (Primary) Explosives:** Lead Azide, Mercury Fulminate.
- **Booster Explosives:** Tetryl, PETN, RDX/wax (95/5) — sensitised but stable.
- **Main Bursting Charges:** TNT, Composition B, Octol, HMX-based warhead fills.
- **Propellants:** Single-base (NC), Double-base (NC+NG), Triple-base (NC+NG+NG), Composite (AP + HTPB + Al).
- **Pyrotechnics:** Flash, smoke, incendiary, signal, delay compositions.

### Pillar 6 — Classification by Oxygen Balance (OB)

Oxygen balance is the **most mathematically rigorous classification** in KTU Module 4.

**Definition:** The percentage of oxygen remaining (excess) or required (deficit) when an explosive decomposes to its fully oxidised products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂, etc.).

The empirical formula for an explosive is written as:

$$C_{a} H_{b} N_{c} O_{d} \quad \text{with molecular weight } M$$

**The KTU Standard Formula:**

$$OB = \frac{\left[d - \left(2a + \frac{b}{2} + c_{\text{halogen-substituted}}\right)\right] \times 16}{M} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

- **OB > 0** → **Oxygen Positive** (e.g., NG, PETN, NG-based compositions). Produces NOₓ — *hydrogen-poor, fuel-deficient*.
- **OB = 0** → **Zero Oxygen Balance** (e.g., TNT has OB ≈ –74%; ideal **practical explosives** are tuned near zero). 
- **OB < 0** → **Oxygen Negative** (e.g., TNT, TATB). Generates CO, soot, toxic fumes — *fuel-rich*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In underground coal mining, the OB is *deliberately tuned* slightly positive so that the CO produced by an oxygen-negative charge is oxidised to CO₂ in the post-blast atmosphere. This defines a **"Permitted Explosive"** in Indian DGMS (Directorate General of Mines Safety) regulations.

### Pillar 7 — Classification by UN Hazard Division (Transport Code)

The **UN Class 1 — Explosives** is sub-divided into **six hazard divisions** (1.1 to 1.6):

| UN Div | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1.1** | Mass Explosion Hazard | Bulk TNT, ANFO in bulk |
| **1.2** | Projection Hazard (no mass explosion) | Rocket motors, flares |
| **1.3** | Mass Fire Hazard | Propellant charges, pyrotechnics |
| **1.4** | Minor Explosion / Fire Hazard | Small-arms ammunition, signal flares |
| **1.5** | Very Insensitive (Blasting Agent) | Bulk ANFO, Emulsion |
| **1.6** | Extremely Insensitive | Insensitive Munitions (IM) |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Typical Range / Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxygen Balance (General CₐHᵦNᵧOᵟ) | $OB = \dfrac{16}{M}\left[d - 2a - \dfrac{b}{2}\right] \times 100$ | % | TNT → **–74%**; NG → **+3.5%** |
| 2 | Detonation Velocity (Chapman-Jouguet) | $D = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1) Q}$ | m/s | RDX → **8,750 m/s** |
| 3 | Detonation Pressure | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{\rho_0 D^2}{\gamma + 1}$ | GPa | TNT → **21 GPa**; HMX → **42 GPa** |
| 4 | Heat of Detonation (Experimental) | $Q = \dfrac{(T_2 - T_1) (C_p + m_s c_s)}{m_e}$ | kJ/kg | TNT → **4,560 kJ/kg** |
| 5 | Figure of Insensitivity | $F = \dfrac{H_{50,\text{sample}}}{H_{50,\text{RDX}}}$ | dimensionless | TATB → **> 1.5** (safer than RDX) |
| 6 | TNT Equivalence | $E_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{blast}} \times Q_{\text{blast}}}{m_{\text{TNT}} \times Q_{\text{TNT}}}$ | unitless | ANFO → **0.82** |
| 7 | Trauzl Lead Block Test | $\Delta V = V_{\text{test}} - V_{\text{blank}}$ | cm³ | TNT → **285 cm³**; NG → **520 cm³** |
| 8 | Brisance (Brisance Index) | $B = \rho_0 \times D^2 / 2$ | arbitrary | Higher = more shattering |
| 9 | Critical Diameter | $d_c$ (minimum diameter for steady detonation) | mm | PETN → **0.5 mm**; ANFO → **80 mm** |
| 10 | Detonation Pressure (Alternate) | $P_{CJ} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_0 D^2$ (approx.) | Pa | Engineering shortcut |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Every formula in this table is used in **blasting engineering software** (e.g., O-Pitblast, JKSimBlast, WipFrag) to predict fragmentation size, ground vibration, fly-rock range, and air-blast overpressure. The OB governs **fume class selection** for underground blasts, while VOD is used in **detonation wave front tracking** for shaped-charge and warhead design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Oxygen Balance Calculation for TNT

The KTU board examiner considers the **OB calculation of TNT (C₇H₅N₃O₆)** the gold-standard derivation question. Let us solve it step-by-step with no shortcuts.

### Given
- **Compound:** 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene
- **Molecular formula:** $\text{C}_{7}\text{H}_{5}\text{N}_{3}\text{O}_{6}$
- **Molecular weight:** $M = 7(12) + 5(1) + 3(14) + 6(16) = 84 + 5 + 42 + 96 = 227$ g/mol

### Step 1 — Write the Idealised Decomposition Reaction

When TNT detonates under **ideal (oxygen-rich) stoichiometric conditions**, the carbon goes to CO₂, hydrogen to H₂O, and nitrogen to N₂:

$$\text{C}_{7}\text{H}_{5}\text{N}_{3}\text{O}_{6} \;\longrightarrow\; 7\text{CO}_{2} + \tfrac{5}{2}\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{N}_{2}$$

### Step 2 — Count Oxygen Atoms Required on the Right-Hand Side

Oxygen required for **complete oxidation** of the products:

$$O_{\text{required}} = 7(2) + \tfrac{5}{2}(1) = 14 + 2.5 = 16.5 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

### Step 3 — Compare with Oxygen Available in TNT

TNT itself contains:

$$O_{\text{available}} = 6 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Oxygen Deficit

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ deficit (in atoms)} = 6 - 16.5 = -10.5 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

In terms of **O₂ molecules** (since each O₂ has 2 atoms), the deficit is:

$$\Delta O_2 = \frac{-10.5}{2} = -5.25 \;\; \text{molecules of } O_2$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Mass of Oxygen Deficiency

$$\Delta m_{O_2} = -5.25 \times 32 \; \text{g/mol} = -168 \; \text{g}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the OB Formula Per 100 g of Explosive

$$OB = \frac{\Delta m_{O_2}}{M_{\text{TNT}}} \times 100 = \frac{-168}{227} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{OB_{\text{TNT}} = -74.0 \;\%}$$

This confirms the **textbook value of –74%**. The negative sign indicates that **TNT is fuel-rich and oxygen-deficient** — it will generate **carbon monoxide (CO)** and **soot** in its detonation products, which is why underground blasting with raw TNT is forbidden.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU examiners award full marks only when the student explicitly states (a) the sign convention, (b) the conversion from atomic oxygen to O₂, and (c) the per-100 g normalisation. Skipping any one of these three steps costs 2 marks.

## 3.2 Worked Example — OB of Nitroglycerine (NG)

### Given
- **Compound:** Nitroglycerine (1,2,3-trinitroxypropane)
- **Molecular formula:** $\text{C}_{3}\text{H}_{5}\text{N}_{3}\text{O}_{9}$
- **Molecular weight:** $M = 3(12) + 5(1) + 3(14) + 9(16) = 36 + 5 + 42 + 144 = 227$ g/mol

### Step 1 — Idealised Combustion Reaction

$$\text{C}_{3}\text{H}_{5}\text{N}_{3}\text{O}_{9} \;\longrightarrow\; 3\text{CO}_{2} + \tfrac{5}{2}\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{N}_{2}$$

### Step 2 — Oxygen Required

$$O_{\text{required}} = 3(2) + \tfrac{5}{2}(1) = 6 + 2.5 = 8.5 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

### Step 3 — Oxygen Available

$$O_{\text{available}} = 9 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

### Step 4 — Oxygen Excess

$$\Delta O = 9 - 8.5 = +0.5 \;\; \text{atoms of O}$$

In O₂ molecules:

$$\Delta O_2 = +\frac{0.5}{2} = +0.25 \;\; \text{molecules of } O_2$$

### Step 5 — Mass of Excess Oxygen

$$\Delta m_{O_2} = +0.25 \times 32 = +8 \;\; \text{g}$$

### Step 6 — Compute OB

$$OB_{\text{NG}} = \frac{+8}{227} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{OB_{\text{NG}} = +3.52 \;\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** NG is **slightly oxygen positive**. It will produce some NOₓ gases, but it has nearly perfect balance. This is why NG is the **workhorse ingredient** in commercial dynamites and double-base propellants — it donates its excess oxygen to *balance the oxygen deficit* of the absorbent fuel (wood pulp, sawdust, sulphur) in dynamite.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Explosives and their OB / VOD

| Explosive | Formula | M (g/mol) | OB (%) | VOD (m/s) | Class (Pillar 2) | Class (Pillar 3) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TNT | C₇H₅N₃O₆ | 227 | **–74.0** | 6,940 | High | Secondary |
| RDX | C₃H₆N₆O₆ | 222 | **–21.6** | 8,750 | High | Secondary |
| HMX | C₄H₈N₈O₈ | 296 | **–21.6** | 9,100 | High | Secondary |
| PETN | C₅H₈N₄O₁₂ | 316 | **–10.1** | 8,400 | High | Secondary (also used as booster) |
| NG | C₃H₅N₃O₉ | 227 | **+3.5** | 7,700 | High | Secondary (liquid) |
| TATB | C₆H₆N₆O₆ | 258 | **–18.6** | 7,800 | High | Tertiary (IM) |
| AN (Ammonium Nitrate) | NH₄NO₃ | 80 | **+20.0** | 5,300 (under confinement) | High (weak) | Tertiary |
| ANFO (94/6) | NH₄NO₃ + fuel oil | ~84 | **≈ 0** | 3,500 – 4,500 | High | Tertiary / Blasting Agent |
| Black Powder | KNO₃ + C + S | 0.84 mix ratio | **≈ –4** | 400 – 600 | **Low** (deflagration) | Primary (sensitive) |
| Lead Azide | Pb(N₃)₂ | 291 | **–5.5** | 5,180 | High | **Primary** |
| Mercury Fulminate | Hg(CNO)₂ | 284 | **–15.9** | 5,000 | High | **Primary** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice that the **lower the OB (more negative)**, the **less efficient** the explosive is energetically (because some of the energy is spent oxidising soot to CO in a post-flame reaction). That is why practical military explosives are *formulated* to be near-zero OB (e.g., Composition B — 60% RDX / 40% TNT — has OB ≈ –43%, a compromise between safety and energy).

## 3.4 Algorithm — Selecting the Right Class for a Given Explosive

```
INPUT: A given explosive compound
STEP 1 → Determine Physical State at 25°C
         (Solid? Liquid? Gas? Polymer? Gel?)
STEP 2 → Measure or Look-up VOD
         IF VOD < 1000 m/s → LOW EXPLOSIVE (Deflagration)
         IF VOD > 1500 m/s → HIGH EXPLOSIVE (Detonation)
STEP 3 → Determine Sensitivity
         IF drop test (H50) < 10 cm      → PRIMARY
         IF 10 ≤ H50 < 100 cm            → SECONDARY
         IF H50 > 100 cm or no F of I ≤ 1 → TERTIARY (IM)
STEP 4 → Compute OB
         IF OB > 0    → OXYGEN POSITIVE
         IF OB ≈ 0    → ZERO BALANCE (preferred for commercial)
         IF OB < 0    → OXYGEN NEGATIVE
STEP 5 → Classify by Application
         (Initiator / Booster / Main Charge / Propellant / Pyrotechnic)
STEP 6 → Assign UN Hazard Division (1.1 to 1.6)
STEP 7 → Identify Storage Compatibility Group
OUTPUT: A 7-tuple classification tag, e.g.,
        (Solid, High, Secondary, Nitro-Aromatic, OB –74%, Main Charge, UN 1.1D)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 VOD vs Sensitivity Mapping Diagram

This plot (described as a block diagram for a Mermaid-friendly equivalent) shows the **engineering trade-off** between VOD (energy) and sensitivity (safety).

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1[QUADRANT 1 HIGH VOD HIGH SENSITIVITY PRIMARY EXPLOSIVES]:::q1
    Q2[QUADRANT 2 HIGH VOD LOW SENSITIVITY IDEAL MILITARY COMPOSITIONS]:::q2
    Q3[QUADRANT 3 LOW VOD LOW SENSITIVITY SAFE PERMITTED EXPLOSIVES]:::q3
    Q4[QUADRANT 4 LOW VOD HIGH SENSITIVITY AVOID - DANGEROUS WASTE]:::q4
    AXISX[VOD INCREASES RIGHT]:::ax
    AXISY[SENSITIVITY INCREASES UP]:::ay

    AXISX --- AXISY
    AXISY --- Q1
    AXISX --- Q2
    AXISY --- Q3
    AXISX --- Q4

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Quadrant Plot:**
> The *ideal* military/commercial explosive lies in **Quadrant 2** (e.g., HMX, TATB, Composition B) — high energy yet insensitive enough to survive accidental impact. Quadrant 1 is occupied only by **primary explosives** (Lead Azide), where insensitivity would defeat the purpose.
