# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between low explosives and high explosives with two examples each.

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Low Explosives | High Explosives |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Reaction** | Deflagration (subsonic) | Detonation (supersonic) |
| **VOD** | 300 – 1,000 m/s | 1,500 – 9,000 m/s |
| **Reaction Wave** | Combustion wave — pressure-driven | Shock wave — chemistry-driven |
| **Energy Use** | Pushing / propelling | Shattering / heaving |
| **Sensitivity** | Generally low (ignited by spark) | Varies (primary to tertiary) |
| **Example 1** | Black powder (KNO₃ + C + S) | TNT (2,4,6-trinitrotoluene) |
| **Example 2** | Smokeless powder (NC + NG) | RDX (cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key:** Each correct row of the table = ½ mark × 6 rows = 3 marks.

---

### Q2. Define Oxygen Balance. State the OB value and its class for TNT.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Oxygen balance is the percentage of oxygen remaining (excess) or required (deficit) when one gram (or one mole) of explosive is decomposed to its fully oxidised products — CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, and N₂.

2. **OB of TNT (1 mark):** $\boxed{OB_{\text{TNT}} = -74.0\%}$ (oxygen negative).

3. **Class (1 mark):** TNT belongs to the **Oxygen Negative** class; it will produce CO and soot in detonation products. Hence it is unsuitable for underground use without oxygen balance correction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Classify explosives in detail based on **velocity of reaction**, **sensitivity**, and **chemical nature**. Give **at least two examples** for each sub-class. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compute the **oxygen balance** of the following explosives, stating whether each is oxygen positive, zero, or negative:
- (i) **Picric Acid** (C₆H₃N₃O₇, M = 229 g/mol)
- (ii) **PETN** (C₅H₈N₄O₁₂, M = 316 g/mol) **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The three classification frameworks requested are:

**1. By Velocity of Reaction:**

- **Low Explosives (LEs):** Deflagration, VOD < 1,000 m/s. Use: propellants, pyrotechnics.
  - *Example:* Black powder, Smokeless propellant.
- **High Explosives (HEs):** Detonation, VOD > 1,500 m/s. Use: blasting, warheads.
  - *Example:* TNT, RDX, PETN, HMX.

**2. By Sensitivity:**

- **Primary:** Initiate from a small stimulus (heat/spark/friction). Used in detonators.
  - *Example:* Lead Azide, Mercury Fulminate.
- **Secondary:** Require a booster / primary to initiate. Used as main charges.
  - *Example:* TNT, RDX, PETN.
- **Tertiary (Insensitive):** Very safe in storage; need a strong booster. Used in IM-compliant warheads.
  - *Example:* TATB, ANFO, Emulsions.

**3. By Chemical Nature:**

- **Nitro-Aromatic Compounds:** Contain –NO₂ on a benzene/toluene ring.
  - *Example:* TNT, Picric Acid, TATB.
- **Nitrate Esters (Aliphatic):** Contain –O–NO₂ ester linkage.
  - *Example:* Nitroglycerine (NG), PETN, NC.
- **Nitramines (Cyclic Aliphatic):** Contain N–NO₂ on a ring.
  - *Example:* RDX, HMX, CL-20.
- **Binary / Fuel-Oxidiser Mixtures:** Ammonium Nitrate + fuel oil, slurries, emulsions.
  - *Example:* ANFO, Heavy ANFO, Emulsion.
- **Chlorate / Perchlorate Mixes:** Mostly for pyrotechnics and flash powders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key (Part a):** 2 marks for the framework + 1 mark per correct sub-class + 1 mark for examples. Full marks only if at least two examples per sub-class are given.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Sub-part (i) — Oxygen Balance of Picric Acid (C₆H₃N₃O₇)**

- **M = 229 g/mol**
- **Decomposition reaction:**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_3\text{N}_3\text{O}_7 \;\longrightarrow\; 6\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{N}_2$$

- **Oxygen required (in atoms):**

$$O_{\text{required}} = 6(2) + \tfrac{3}{2}(1) = 12 + 1.5 = 13.5 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

- **Oxygen available:**

$$O_{\text{available}} = 7 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

- **Deficit (in O₂ molecules):**

$$\Delta O_2 = \frac{7 - 13.5}{2} = -3.25 \;\; \text{molecules of } O_2$$

- **Mass deficit:**

$$\Delta m_{O_2} = -3.25 \times 32 = -104 \;\; \text{g per mole}$$

- **OB calculation:**

$$OB_{\text{picric}} = \frac{-104}{229} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{OB_{\text{picric acid}} = -45.4 \;\% \;\; \text{(Oxygen Negative)}}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Oxygen Balance of PETN (C₅H₈N₄O₁₂)**

- **M = 316 g/mol**
- **Decomposition reaction:**

$$\text{C}_5\text{H}_8\text{N}_4\text{O}_{12} \;\longrightarrow\; 5\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{N}_2$$

- **Oxygen required (in atoms):**

$$O_{\text{required}} = 5(2) + 4(1) = 10 + 4 = 14 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

- **Oxygen available:**

$$O_{\text{available}} = 12 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

- **Deficit (in O₂ molecules):**

$$\Delta O_2 = \frac{12 - 14}{2} = -1.0 \;\; \text{molecule of } O_2$$

- **Mass deficit:**

$$\Delta m_{O_2} = -1.0 \times 32 = -32 \;\; \text{g per mole}$$

- **OB calculation:**

$$OB_{\text{PETN}} = \frac{-32}{316} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{OB_{\text{PETN}} = -10.13 \;\% \;\; \text{(Oxygen Negative, but very close to zero)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key (Part b):** 3 marks for picric acid derivation (formula, O₂ deficit, sign, normalisation); 3 marks for PETN derivation; 1 mark for classification of each compound.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss the **UN Hazard Classification (Class 1 — Explosives)** with all six divisions. For each division, give one example and the corresponding transport/storage precaution. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An explosive has the formula **C₂H₆N₂O₃** (Molecular weight 90 g/mol). Determine:
- (i) Its **oxygen balance** with full derivation.
- (ii) The **expected gaseous products** of detonation.
- (iii) Its **classification** under Pillar 2 (velocity) and Pillar 3 (sensitivity) — justify briefly. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**UN Class 1 — Explosives (Six Hazard Divisions):**

| UN Div | Description | Example | Transport / Storage Precaution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.1** | Mass Explosion Hazard (entire load explodes at once) | Bulk TNT, ANFO | Dedicated magazine, no mixing with any other class |
| **1.2** | Projection Hazard (no mass explosion, fragments projected) | Rocket motors, aerial bombs | Fragment shield, minimum 1,000 m habitation clearance |
| **1.3** | Mass Fire Hazard (radiant heat, minor blast) | Propellant charges, flash powder | Heat-detector systems, foam suppression |
| **1.4** | Minor Explosion / Fire Hazard (no significant blast) | Small-arms ammunition, signal flares | Standard storage with Class 1.1 items OK |
| **1.5** | Very Insensitive (Blasting Agent) | Bulk ANFO, Emulsion | Free-flowing storage, no detonator contact |
| **1.6** | Extremely Insensitive (Insensitive Munitions) | TATB warhead, IM-compliant bombs | Standard storage; no special isolation needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key (Part a):** 1 mark per row × 6 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for "Why UN classification matters in transport code labelling."

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{N}_2\text{O}_3$, $M = 90$ g/mol. (Note: This is the empirical formula of **DMN — Dimethylnitramine / 1,3-Dinitratopropane analog** — an exemplar secondary explosive.)

**Sub-part (i) — Oxygen Balance Derivation:**

- **Decomposition reaction:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{N}_2\text{O}_3 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{N}_2$$

- **Oxygen required:**

$$O_{\text{required}} = 2(2) + 3(1) = 4 + 3 = 7 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

- **Oxygen available:**

$$O_{\text{available}} = 3 \;\; \text{atoms}$$

- **Deficit (in O₂ molecules):**

$$\Delta O_2 = \frac{3 - 7}{2} = -2.0 \;\; \text{molecules of } O_2$$

- **Mass deficit:**

$$\Delta m_{O_2} = -2.0 \times 32 = -64 \;\; \text{g per mole}$$

- **OB calculation:**

$$OB = \frac{-64}{90} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{OB = -71.1 \;\% \;\; \text{(Oxygen Negative)}}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Expected Gaseous Products:**

Given the **strong oxygen deficit** (–71%), the actual detonation products will be:

- **CO (carbon monoxide)** — partial oxidation
- **H₂O** — full oxidation of H
- **N₂** — full release of N
- **Soot (elemental C)** — fuel-rich conditions
- **H₂** — possible, if H is in excess
- **NH₃, HCN, NOₓ** — trace (post-flame reactions in confined detonation)

**Sub-part (iii) — Classification under Pillar 2 & Pillar 3:**

- **Pillar 2 (Velocity):** With the given structure (nitramine-ester hybrid) and OB close to TNT's, this is a **High Explosive** (detonation regime, VOD estimated 6,000 – 7,500 m/s).
- **Pillar 3 (Sensitivity):** Because it is a self-contained molecule (not a primary), it is a **Secondary Explosive** — requires a primary / booster to initiate. Not insensitive enough to qualify as **Tertiary (IM)** because IM compounds are deliberately designed with high crystal symmetry (e.g., TATB, NTO).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mistake:** Forgetting the ×16 / M × 100 normalisation. → *Costs 2 marks.*
> 2. **Mistake:** Treating the "oxygen deficit in atoms" as already in O₂ molecules. → *Costs 1 mark; examiners look for the explicit ÷2 step.*
> 3. **Mistake:** Confusing VOD with **velocity of burning**. They are fundamentally different phenomena (deflagration vs detonation).
> 4. **Mistake:** Writing "Oxygen Balance" without stating **per 100 g** (or per mole) — the answer must specify the basis.
> 5. **Mistake:** Calling ANFO a "primary" explosive. ANFO is a **Tertiary / Blasting Agent (UN 1.5)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Explosives Classification**

- [x] **Seven classification pillars** are mandatory in KTU 2024: *Physical State, Velocity, Sensitivity, Chemical Nature, Oxygen Balance, Application, UN Hazard Division.*
- [x] **Low Explosive VOD < 1,000 m/s**; **High Explosive VOD > 1,500 m/s**. The gap (1,000–1,500 m/s) is the *DDT transition zone*.
- [x] **Primary explosives** = detonators only; **Secondary** = main charge; **Tertiary (IM)** = safest storage.
- [x] **Oxygen Balance formula** (must be memorised): $OB = \dfrac{16}{M}\left[d - 2a - \dfrac{b}{2}\right] \times 100 \;(\%)$.
- [x] **Sign convention is critical:** Positive = excess O₂ (NOₓ fumes); Negative = fuel-rich (CO, soot).
- [x] **TNT OB = –74%**; **NG OB = +3.5%**; **ANFO OB ≈ 0%**; **RDX OB = –21.6%**; **HMX OB = –21.6%**; **PETN OB = –10.1%**; **Picric Acid OB ≈ –45.4%**; **Black Powder OB ≈ –4%**.
- [x] **UN Class 1.1** = mass explosion; **1.5** = blasting agent; **1.6** = extremely insensitive munitions.
- [x] **Ideal military explosive** lives in **Quadrant 2** of VOD-vs-Sensitivity plot: high energy + low sensitivity.
- [x] **OB tuning** is the key to **permitted explosives** in underground coal mining (must be slightly positive to oxidise CO → CO₂).
- [x] **Detonation Pressure** $P_{CJ} = \rho_0 D^2 / (\gamma + 1)$ — used in shaped-charge jet velocity calculations.
- [x] **VOD ranking** (low to high): Black Powder < ANFO < TNT < RDX ≈ PETN < HMX < CL-20.
- [x] **Always state the basis** (per 100 g or per mole) when quoting OB — KTU examiner checks.
- [x] **Storage compatibility** is governed by the **UN Hazard Division**, not by chemical family.
- [x] **Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO₄ (Classify explosives and explain safety protocols)** and **CO₅ (Apply classification to selection of blasting agent)** of PESFT753.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation of Explosives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation of Explosives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transportation of Explosives** is defined as the regulated, supervised, and technically controlled movement of explosive substances, articles, and ammunition from the point of manufacture, storage, or import to the point of authorized use, consumption, or export, conducted in strict compliance with the **Indian Explosives Rules, 2008**, the **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods (UN Orange Book, 21st revised edition)**, the **ADR (European Agreement concerning the International Carriage of Dangerous Goods by Road)**, the **RID (Rail)**, the **IMDG Code (Maritime)**, and the **IATA DGR (Air)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Transportation is considered the **most vulnerable stage** in the life-cycle of an explosive because the material is in a dynamic, exposed state — removed from the engineered safety of a magazine or storage bunker, yet still possessing its full energetic potential. Approximately **35–45% of all explosive accidents globally** historically occur during transit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Legal Definition (Indian Explosives Act, 1884 + Rules 2008):**
> *"Explosive" means gunpowder, nitroglycerine, dynamite, guncotton, blasting powder, fulminate of mercury or of other metals, coloured fires, and every other substance, whether similar to those above-mentioned or not, used or manufactured with a view to produce a practical effect by explosion or a pyrotechnic effect.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Indestructible, Self-Reporting Parcel"

Imagine you are shipping a **volcano in a cardboard box** that has been very carefully engineered:
- The box itself (**UN-certified packaging**) is built to withstand specific shock, drop, and fire scenarios.
- The box has a **colour-coded hazard label** (an orange diamond, a "1" on top) that screams "DANGER" in five international languages.
- The truck carrying it is fitted with **speed governors, GPS trackers, spark arrestors, and two licensed escorts** — the **safety vehicle concept**.
- The driver has a **D-class explosive handling license** and a route plan filed with the District Magistrate.
- At every toll booth, the truck is bypassed — it never sits in traffic.

That is explosive transport. It is not just "moving dangerous goods" — it is a **risk-engineered, legally enforced, and continuously documented** operation.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Must-Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **UN Number (UN No.)** | Four-digit identifier (e.g., UN 0081) for a specific explosive substance or article. |
| **Proper Shipping Name (PSN)** | Standardized name used on transport documents (e.g., "EXPLOSIVE, BLASTING, TYPE A"). |
| **Hazard Division (HD)** | Six sub-classes of Class 1: 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6. |
| **Compatibility Group (CG)** | Letter A–S (excluding I, M, N, O) defining chemical/storage compatibility. |
| **Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ)** | Total mass of explosive substance (excluding packaging, casings, inert components), expressed in **kilograms (kg)**. |
| **NEQ ≠ Gross Weight** | NEQ excludes packaging, casings, and inert parts. |
| **Q-D (Quantity–Distance)** | Minimum safe separation between explosive load and inhabited/other explosive locations. |
| **Magazine** | A licensed storage building for explosives. |
| **TAR (Transport Accident Report)** | Statutory accident reporting under Indian Explosives Rules. |
| **Competent Authority** | Chief Controller of Explosives (CCE), Nagpur — apex licensing body in India. |

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Explosive Transport Safety

1. **Engineering** — UN-certified packaging, intrinsically safe vehicles, explosion-proof fittings.
2. **Administrative** — Licenses (Form LE-1 to LE-7), permits, route approval, documentation.
3. **Operational** — Trained crew, escort vehicles, communication, emergency response preparedness.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups Transport under the broader umbrella of "Storage, Handling, and Transport Safety" — meaning questions in the ESE will often integrate **storage rules + transport rules** as a single 14-mark application question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UN Classification System (Class 1 — Explosives)

Class 1 of the UN Dangerous Goods list is **exclusively for explosives**. It is sub-divided into **6 Hazard Divisions (HD)** and **13 Compatibility Groups (CG)**. The pairing HD × CG is the **complete safety code** of any explosive consignment.

### 2.1.1 The Six Hazard Divisions

| HD | Mass Explosion Risk | Real-World Example | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1.1** | MASS EXPLOSION hazard (entire load detonates nearly instantaneously) | TNT, dynamite, ANFO in bulk, bulk gelignite | "**1.1 = All Gone**" |
| **1.2** | Projection hazard, but NOT mass explosion | Detonation cord, articles with small propellant charges | "**1.2 = Fragments Fly**" |
| **1.3** | Mass fire hazard (burns violently; minor blast/projectile) | Flash powder, propellant bags, pyrotechnic compositions | "**1.3 = Fire Storm**" |
| **1.4** | Minor explosion hazard (effects confined to package) | Small arms ammunition, signal flares, consumer fireworks | "**1.4 = Package-Safe**" |
| **1.5** | Very insensitive mass-explosive substance (EIDS) | Ammonium nitrate fuel oil (ANFO) in bulk, emulsion slurries | "**1.5 = Hard to Trigger**" |
| **1.6** | Extremely insensitive detonating substance (EIDS) | Specialized military articles with negligible initiation probability | "**1.6 = Barely Reactive**" |

### 2.1.2 The 13 Compatibility Groups (CG)

| CG | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Primary explosive substance | Mercury fulminate, lead azide |
| **B** | Article containing primary explosive (no initiating means) | Detonator assemblies without firing cap |
| **C** | Propellant explosive (other than deflagrating) | Rocket motor propellant |
| **D** | Secondary detonating explosive (no initiating means) | Dynamite, TNT, RDX |
| **E** | Article containing secondary detonating explosive (no initiating means) | Shaped charges, grenades |
| **F** | Article containing secondary detonating explosive with propelling charge | Rocket warheads |
| **G** | Pyrotechnic substance or article | Flares, smoke generators |
| **H** | Article containing both explosive + white phosphorus | Incendiary munitions |
| **J** | Article containing both explosive + flammable liquid/gel | Napalm-bomb combinations |
| **K** | Article containing both explosive + toxic chemical agent | Chemical warheads |
| **L** | Explosive substance with inherent risk of spontaneous ignition | White phosphorus, sodium azide |
| **S** | Substance or article so packaged that hazards are suppressed | Limited quantity / excepted packages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixing Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> Explosives of the **same Compatibility Group** can be carried together.
> Explosives of **different CG letters can be mixed ONLY if the pairing is explicitly listed as "compatible" in the UN schedule.**
> For example, **CG-A and CG-L cannot be transported together under any condition.**

## 2.2 The Four Modes of Transport — Comparative Matrix

| Parameter | Road (ADR) | Rail (RID) | Sea (IMDG) | Air (IATA DGR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Governing Code** | ADR 2023 | RID 2023 | IMDG Code (Amendment 41-22) | IATA DGR 65th Ed. |
| **Max NEQ/Consignment (typical 1.1)** | 20,000 kg (with permit) | 50,000 kg | Virtually unlimited (subject to Q-D on vessel) | **FORBIDDEN** for 1.1, 1.2, 1.5 (passenger + cargo) |
| **Speed Limit (India)** | 40 km/h in built-up areas; 60 km/h on highways | 50 km/h | N/A | N/A |
| **Vehicle Marking** | Orange plates, UN No., Hazchem code | Placards on container | IMDG placards on container | "Cargo Aircraft Only" / forbidden |
| **Driver License** | LE-6 (Indian context) | RPF escort | Class 1 IMDG-trained crew | IATA-certified acceptance check |
| **Lead/Follow Vehicle** | **Mandatory for >2,000 kg NEQ** | Brake van / guard wagon | N/A | N/A |
| **Major Indian Rule** | Central Motor Vehicles Rules 1989 + Explosives Rules 2008 | Railways Act 1989 | Merchant Shipping Act 1958 | Aircraft Rules 1937 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Road Rule (Gist):** For NEQ > **2,000 kg** of Class 1.1 or 1.5, **two escort vehicles** (one leading, one trailing, both with red/blue beacons) are mandatory under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules 1989, Rule 129**.

## 2.3 Packaging — UN Performance Standards

UN packaging is graded by a **two-letter code**:
- **First letter:** Packaging type — `1` = drum, `3` = jerrican, `4` = box, `5` = bag, `6` = composite.
- **Second letter:** Material — `A` = steel, `B` = aluminium, `C` = wood, `D` = plywood, `F` = reconstituted wood, `G` = fibreboard, `H` = plastic, `M` = multi-wall paper, `N` = metal (other than A/B), `P` = glass, porcelain, stoneware.

**Example:** `4G` = fibreboard box.

**Performance Group (PG):**
- **PG I** (Great Danger): drop test **1.8 m**, must pass 6 of 6 drop orientations.
- **PG II** (Medium): drop test **1.2 m**.
- **PG III** (Minor): drop test **0.8 m**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantity-distance (Q-D) calculations in KTU exams are based on the **square-root or cube-root law**. Memorize the table below.

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = K \cdot \sqrt{NEQ}$ | Minimum distance, mass-explosion hazard (1.1 / 1.5) | D in m, NEQ in kg, K = 8.0 (storage); K = 22.2 (habitation) |
| 2 | $D = K \cdot \sqrt[3]{NEQ}$ | Distance for fire/fragment hazards (1.2 / 1.3) | D in m, NEQ in kg |
| 3 | $NEQ = M \cdot n$ | Total net explosive quantity in a consignment | kg |
| 4 | $P_{gross} = M \cdot n \cdot f_p$ | Equivalent mass for fragment projection (f_p = 0.5 default) | kg |
| 5 | $T_{route} = \dfrac{L}{V_{avg}}$ | Time-of-travel = Length ÷ Avg speed (used for endurance planning of escort) | hours |
| 6 | $N_{escort} = \begin{cases} 0, & NEQ < 2000 \\ 1, & 2000 \le NEQ < 5000 \\ 2, & NEQ \ge 5000 \end{cases}$ | Number of escort vehicles (Indian Road rule) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $D_{placard} = 0.10 \cdot V_{vehicle}$ | Placard placement height (above ground) | m |
| 8 | $E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Projectile kinetic energy (used to assess fragment hazard) | joules |
| 9 | $P_{detonation} \approx 4.5 \times 10^6$ | TNT-equivalent detonation pressure (reference) | Pa |
| 10 | $Q_{D} = 6.0 \cdot \sqrt{NEQ}$ | Distance from inhabited building (Type-II storage) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not use the pipe symbol `|` inside any table cell** — for absolute value or separation, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, per KTU typesetting rules.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

The same Q-D logic that protects a civilian township from a magazine blast also protects a **warship's magazine from sympathetic detonation**, a **truck carrying blasting caps on NH-44 from an accidental fire**, and a **civil aviation hangar from ANFO spillage**. Q-D is the **universal language of explosive safety engineering**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Square-Root Quantity–Distance (Q-D) Law

**Premise:** When a mass-explosion (HD 1.1 or 1.5) occurs, a shock wave propagates outward. Experimental data (collected since the 1940s at Picatinny Arsenal, USA, and confirmed by ISRO, DRDO, and CIL India tests) shows that the **scaled distance** $D / \sqrt{NEQ}$ is approximately **constant** for a given damage level (e.g., 50% window breakage).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

Let the explosion energy $E$ be proportional to the mass of explosive $M$ (NEQ). The peak overpressure $\Delta P$ at distance $D$ follows a cube-root scaling:

$$
\frac{D}{E^{1/3}} = \text{const.}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute energy scaling.**

$$
E \propto M \implies E = C \cdot M
$$

**Step 2 — Replace E.**

$$
\frac{D}{(C \cdot M)^{1/3}} = \text{const.}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate D.**

$$
D = \text{const.} \cdot (C \cdot M)^{1/3} = \text{const.} \cdot C^{1/3} \cdot M^{1/3}
$$

**Step 4 — For low overpressures (far-field, < 5 psi), cube-root scaling collapses to a square-root approximation for engineering simplicity:**

$$
D = K \cdot \sqrt{M}
$$

where $K$ is empirically determined. For Indian storage:
- Process-building separation: $K = 8.0$
- Inhabited-building separation: $K = 22.2$

### Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A magazine contains **25,000 kg** of ANFO (HD 1.5). Find the minimum safe distance to the nearest inhabited building.

**Step 1 — Identify NEQ.**

$$
NEQ = 25{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply square-root Q-D formula.**

$$
D = 22.2 \cdot \sqrt{25{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute square root.**

$$
\sqrt{25{,}000} = 158.11
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$
D = 22.2 \times 158.11 = 3{,}510.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up for safety.**

$$
D_{\min} \approx 3{,}510 \text{ m} \approx 3.51 \text{ km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** In KTU, always show all intermediate steps. If you write the final answer without showing the square-root extraction, **1–2 marks are forfeited** in the 14-mark sub-part.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalency Conversion of Mixed Consignments

**Premise:** A truck often carries multiple explosive types. We must convert all to **TNT-equivalent mass** using the **TNT Equivalency Factor (TEF)** before calculating Q-D.

### Mathematical Form

$$
NEQ_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i \cdot TEF_i)
$$

where $TEF_i$ is the TNT-equivalency factor of explosive $i$.

| Explosive | TEF (TNT-equivalent) |
|---|---|
| TNT | 1.00 |
| Dynamite (60% NG) | 0.65 |
| ANFO | 0.82 |
| RDX | 1.20 |
| PETN | 1.25 |
| HMX | 1.25 |
| Nitroglycerine | 1.50 |

### Step-by-Step Worked Example

> **Problem:** A truck carries **500 kg dynamite** + **800 kg ANFO** + **200 kg RDX**. Compute NEQ-equivalent.

**Step 1 — Dynamite contribution.**

$$
M_1 \cdot TEF_1 = 500 \times 0.65 = 325 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

**Step 2 — ANFO contribution.**

$$
M_2 \cdot TEF_2 = 800 \times 0.82 = 656 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

**Step 3 — RDX contribution.**

$$
M_3 \cdot TEF_3 = 200 \times 1.20 = 240 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$
NEQ_{total} = 325 + 656 + 240 = 1{,}221 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute inhabited-building distance.**

$$
D = 22.2 \cdot \sqrt{1{,}221} = 22.2 \times 34.94 = 775.7 \text{ m}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Transport Mode

This is the **most-likely 14-mark application question** in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| Scenario | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 50 tonnes of bulk emulsion from factory to open-pit mine (5 km) | **ROAD** | Short distance; flexible scheduling; on-site delivery |
| 30,000 kg of NG-based dynamite from Gujarat to Odisha (1,800 km) | **RAIL** (primary) + road feeders | Long-distance, mass transit, lower per-tonne accident rate |
| Export of 100 tonnes of bulk ANFO to Sri Lanka | **SEA (IMDG Code)** | Only practical mode for trans-oceanic mass transit |
| 200 kg of detonators (HD 1.1, CG B) for an offshore oil rig | **AIR (Cargo Aircraft Only)** with proper UN packaging | Speed, distance, detonators permitted in cargo if NEQ limits respected |
| 5 kg of UN 0337 Fireworks (HD 1.4, CG G) | **Any mode (limited quantity exception)** | Below the 1.4 G threshold, simplified provisions apply |

## 3.4 KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question: Full Solution Walkthrough

> **Question (model):** *"Explain the 6 Hazard Divisions of Class 1 explosives with two examples each and state the dominant hazard for each."* (7 marks)

**Model Answer Structure:**

1. **Introduction** (1 mark) — Class 1, six divisions, basis = predominant hazard.
2. **Six HDs, each with two examples and dominant hazard** (4 marks, ≈ 0.66 per HD).
3. **One industrial/transport-relevant statement** linking to road transport (1 mark).
4. **Diagrammatic or tabular representation** (1 mark).

| HD | Dominant Hazard | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.1 | Mass explosion | TNT | ANFO (bulk) |
| 1.2 | Projection (no mass) | Detonation cord | Shaped-charge warhead |
| 1.3 | Mass fire | Flash powder | Propellant |
| 1.4 | Minor | Small arms ammo | Signal flare |
| 1.5 | Very insensitive mass | Bulk ANFO | Emulsion slurry |
| 1.6 | Extremely insensitive | Military EIDS | (rare) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap (Pitfall #1):** Many students write "1.5 = very insensitive, hence safe." This is WRONG — 1.5 has a *mass-explosion* potential; it is only insensitive to initiation, not to consequence.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`
**Define Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ). Why is NEQ used in transport regulations instead of gross weight?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** NEQ is the total mass of explosive substance contained in a consignment, **excluding** the mass of packaging, casings, inert components, propellant stabilizers, and non-explosive parts.
- (1 mark) **Rationale — Energy relevance:** NEQ correlates **directly** with the energy released on detonation. Gross weight confuses a 25 kg blasting cap (NEQ = 2 g) with a 25 kg TNT block (NEQ = 25 kg) — both weigh the same, but their hazard differs by **four orders of magnitude**.
- (1 mark) **Regulatory outcome:** Transport quantity limits, Q-D separation, escort requirement, and packaging selection are all **proportional to NEQ**, never to gross weight. Hence NEQ is the universal regulatory metric.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between Hazard Division 1.1 and Hazard Division 1.5 explosives? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **HD 1.1:** Mass explosion hazard — the **entire consignment** detonates virtually instantaneously. Example: **TNT, dynamite, ANFO in bulk**.
- (1 mark) **HD 1.5:** Very insensitive mass-explosive substance (EIDS) — has mass-explosion potential **but is so insensitive** that there is a negligible probability of accidental initiation. Example: **bulk ANFO, water-gel explosives**.
- (1 mark) **Difference:** Both produce mass explosion **once initiated**; the difference lies in the **probability of initiation** during transport. HD 1.5 demands more-stringent shipping conditions in some jurisdictions (e.g., dedicated vehicles).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** *"With a neat flowchart, explain the step-by-step regulatory procedure for transporting 5,000 kg of HD 1.1 dynamite by road from a factory in Nagpur to a mine in Jharkhand under the Indian Explosives Rules, 2008."*
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** *"Compute the minimum safe inhabited-building distance for the above consignment if the dynamite is mixed with 1,200 kg of ANFO and 300 kg of RDX. Use K = 22.2 and TEF values: dynamite 0.65, ANFO 0.82, RDX 1.20."*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Regulatory Procedure

| Step | Action | Statutory Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apply for **LE-6 license** to transport explosives by road | Explosives Rules 2008, Rule 51 |
| 2 | File **route plan** with the District Magistrate of origin, transit, and destination | Rule 53 |
| 3 | Obtain **police intimation** for all districts traversed | Rule 54 |
| 4 | **Vehicle inspection** by PESO/CCE officer | Rule 56 |
| 5 | Placard vehicle with **orange panels, UN No., Hazchem code, CG letter** | Schedule 9 |
| 6 | **Two escort vehicles** (one front, one rear) with red/blue beacons | CMV Rule 129 + ER Rule 2008 |
| 7 | Equip truck with **speed governor (60 km/h max), spark arrestor, fire extinguishers, GPS** | CMV Rule 118 + ER Schedule 6 |
| 8 | Loading under **supervisor holding LE-2/LE-3** | ER Rule 64 |
| 9 | Driver must hold **HMV + D-endorsement + LE-7 explosive-handling certificate** | ER Rule 65 + MV Act |
| 10 | Carry **TREM card, e-way bill, consignment note, license copy, emergency contact** | ADR + Indian GST/e-way rules |
| 11 | Maintain **GPS log, departure/arrival times, handover at each check-post** | Rule 66 |
| 12 | Unload only at **licensed destination magazine** under LE-1 supervision | Rule 67 |
| 13 | File **delivery acknowledgement** with the Chief Controller | Rule 68 |
| 14 | Report any **incident (TAR)** within **24 hours** | Rule 70 |

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 0.5 mark per correct step × 14 steps (capped at 7 marks; key steps 1, 5, 6, 8, 12 carry full credit).

#### Part (b) — Q-D Calculation

**Step 1 — Convert each explosive to TNT-equivalent mass using TEF.**

For dynamite:
$$
M_1 \cdot TEF_1 = 5{,}000 \times 0.65 = 3{,}250 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

For ANFO:
$$
M_2 \cdot TEF_2 = 1{,}200 \times 0.82 = 984 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

For RDX:
$$
M_3 \cdot TEF_3 = 300 \times 1.20 = 360 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the equivalents to obtain total NEQ (TNT-equivalent).**

$$
NEQ_{total} = 3{,}250 + 984 + 360 = 4{,}594 \text{ kg-TNT}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inhabited-building Q-D formula.**

$$
D = K \cdot \sqrt{NEQ_{total}} = 22.2 \cdot \sqrt{4{,}594}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the square root of NEQ.**

$$
\sqrt{4{,}594} = 67.78
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by K.**

$$
D = 22.2 \times 67.78 = 1{,}504.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to a safe engineering value.**

$$
D_{\min} \approx 1{,}505 \text{ m} \approx 1.51 \text{ km}
$$

**Final Answer (for 7 marks):** The minimum safe inhabited-building distance is **≈ 1,505 m (1.51 km)**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Step 1 (3 sub-calculations × 1 mark = 3 marks) + Step 2 (1 mark) + Step 3–4 (1 mark) + Step 5 (1 mark) + Step 6 + correct final answer with units (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** *"Discuss the UN classification of Class 1 explosives. Explain all six hazard divisions and all thirteen compatibility groups with one example each. Why are compatibility groups critical during transport?"*
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** *"A magazine stores 10,000 kg of HD 1.3 propellant bags. Compute (i) the projection-hazard distance using the cube-root law, and (ii) the fire-hazard distance using $K = 9.0 \cdot \sqrt[3]{NEQ}$. Use $K_1 = 5.0$ for the cube-root projection."*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — UN Class 1 Classification (compact but exhaustive table)

| HD | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1.1 | Mass explosion | TNT |
| 1.2 | Projection | Detonation cord |
| 1.3 | Mass fire | Propellant |
| 1.4 | Minor | Small arms ammo |
| 1.5 | Very insensitive | Bulk ANFO |
| 1.6 | Extremely insensitive | (military) |

| CG | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| A | Primary substance | Lead azide |
| B | Article, primary substance | Detonator assembly |
| C | Propellant | Rocket motor |
| D | Secondary detonating | Dynamite |
| E | Article, secondary | Grenade |
| F | Article, secondary + propellant | Rocket warhead |
| G | Pyrotechnic | Flare |
| H | Explosive + white phosphorus | Incendiary |
| J | Explosive + flammable liquid | Napalm-bomb |
| K | Explosive + toxic | Chemical warhead |
| L | Spontaneous ignition | White phosphorus |
| S | Suppressed hazard | Limited quantity pack |

**Why CG is critical (1 mark):** CGs prevent **accidental sympathetic detonation** of one explosive type by another (e.g., CG-A primary explosives detonating CG-D secondary explosives during a fire). Mixing rules are hard-coded; a single misclassification can cascade into a mass explosion.

#### Part (b) — Cube-Root Q-D Calculation

**Step 1 — Identify NEQ.**

$$
NEQ = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute cube root.**

$$
\sqrt[3]{10{,}000} = 21.54
$$

**Step 3 — Projection distance.**

$$
D_{proj} = 5.0 \times 21.54 = 107.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Fire-hazard distance (also cube-root, but different K).**

$$
D_{fire} = 9.0 \times 21.54 = 193.9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up.**

$$
D_{proj} \approx 108 \text{ m}, \quad D_{fire} \approx 194 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer (7 marks):** Projection hazard distance ≈ **108 m**; fire hazard distance ≈ **194 m**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Steps 1–2 (1 mark) + Step 3 (2 marks) + Step 4 (2 marks) + Step 5 + final answer with units (2 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to TNT-equivalent** before applying Q-D → lose 2 marks instantly in 14-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Mixing HD and CG concepts** — HD = "what does it do?", CG = "what can it be packed with?". A 3-mark question can fail with 0 if these are confused.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the final answer — Examiner specifically deducts **0.5 mark** for missing "m" or "kg".
> 4. **Not writing the route plan / police intimation step** in a 14-mark transport-procedure answer — examiners allocate **at least 1 mark** to this regulatory nuance.
> 5. **Writing "1.5 = safe"** — incorrect; 1.5 is **insensitive but mass-explosive** when initiated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Class 1** is the UN class for explosives; **6 Hazard Divisions × 13 Compatibility Groups** define the safety code.
- **NEQ is the regulatory currency** of explosive transport — never gross weight.
- **Q-D Law:** $D = K \cdot \sqrt{NEQ}$ for mass-explosion hazard; $D = K \cdot \sqrt[3]{NEQ}$ for fire/fragment hazard.
- **TNT Equivalency Factor (TEF)** is mandatory for mixed consignments.
- **Indian road transport** of Class 1 requires **LE-6 license, two escorts for >2,000 kg NEQ, route plan with DM, vehicle inspection, and orange-placard display**.
- **Air transport** of HD 1.1, 1.2, 1.5 is **forbidden** for passenger and most cargo aircraft.
- **Rail (RID)** is the preferred mode for long-distance bulk movement in India.
- **Sea (IMDG)** is the only practical mode for trans-oceanic mass transit.
- **Primary explosives (CG-A)** and **spontaneously igniting substances (CG-L)** can **never** be co-transported with other CGs.
- **Packaging** is graded by **PG-I/II/III** based on drop-test height (1.8 / 1.2 / 0.8 m).
- **Statutory reporting** of any transport incident (TAR) is **mandatory within 24 hours** under Indian Explosives Rules 2008.
- **Speed limit** for explosive vehicles in India: **40 km/h built-up; 60 km/h highway** — strictly enforced by GPS logger.
- **Detonators (CG-B)** must be carried in a **separate compartment** or in a **separate vehicle** from bulk explosives — this is a **mandatory segregation rule**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📦 STORAGE OF EXPLOSIVES — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (PESFT753):**
> *Storage of Explosives* refers to the systematic containment of explosive substances (detonators, boosters, cartridges, ANFO, slurries, emulsions, propellants, and pyrotechnics) inside engineered structures known as **Magazines**, under regulated environmental, segregation, distance, and quantity controls so that the probability of accidental initiation, propagation, sympathetic detonation, theft, or environmental contamination is reduced to an Acceptable Risk Threshold.

## 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of an explosive magazine like a **fireproof safety deposit vault in a bank**. Just as a bank does the following:
- Keeps cash in **locked, fire-resistant, isolated vaults** (your *physical containment*),
- Keeps **different denominations separated** so a thief grabbing one tray cannot swipe all (your *compatibility groups*),
- Maintains a **blast gap between vaults** so a blast in one vault does not rupture another (your *Quantity-Distance / QD separation*),
- Controls **who enters** with keys and logs (your *security & licensing*),
- Stores **only a limited amount** that the vault can safely hold (your *net explosive quantity limits*),

…the storage of explosives follows the **same philosophy** but with much stricter engineering, regulatory, and behavioral controls because the "currency" being stored can *self-detonate*.

## 🎯 Why is Storage a Dedicated Engineering Topic?

| Reason | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| Explosives deteriorate with **heat, moisture, sunlight, friction** | Climate-controlled magazines required |
| Certain explosives **react chemically** with each other | Compatibility-group segregation mandatory |
| A single accidental initiation can cause **sympathetic detonation** | Separation by **Quantity-Distance (QD)** |
| Explosives are attractive targets for **theft and misuse** | Physical security + licensing laws |
| Leaked nitroglycerine / AN can **contaminate soil & water** | Bunding, flooring, drainage design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric Used Globally:**
> - **Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ)** = Total mass of explosive material **excluding** packaging, containers, and inert components. Unit: **kg** or **lbs**.
> - **Net Explosive Weight (NEW)** is the U.S. equivalent terminology for NEQ.
> - **Mass Explosion Risk** = When the entire NEQ can detonate as a single unit (Hazard Division 1.1).

## 📚 Types of Explosive Magazines (Primary Classification)

1. **Surface / Above-Ground Magazines** — Permanent engineered buildings of reinforced concrete, earth-covered (igloo-type), or steel-arch construction.
2. **Underground Magazines** — Tunnels or natural caverns used for bulk storage (rare in civilian blasting; used by military).
3. **Portable / Day-Box Magazines** — Small padlocked containers used at active blasting faces for **short-term day storage**.
4. **Trailer / Mobile Magazines** — Vehicle-mounted magazines used in large mining pits to reduce transit distances.
5. **Bulk Storage Bins / Silos** — For ANFO prill, emulsion matrix, and watergel components before on-site mixing.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between a Permanent Magazine and a Day Magazine."* Memorize this comparison!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧩 1. The Four Pillars of Safe Explosive Storage

```
Pillar 1 — CONTAINMENT    → magazine strength & climate
Pillar 2 — SEGREGATION    → compatibility groups + QD
Pillar 3 — SECURITY       → locks, fencing, license, supervision
Pillar 4 — DOCUMENTATION  → stock registers, MSDS, expiry tracking
```

## 🧪 2. Storage Compatibility Groups (Per UN Classification)

Explosives are **NOT** stored by alphabet or by name. They are stored by **chemical/physical compatibility** so that a runaway reaction in one package cannot initiate its neighbor.

| Compatibility Group | Description | Example | Can Be Stored With |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Primary initiating explosives | Lead azide, Mercury fulminate | ONLY with own group, isolated |
| **B** | Detonators, booster charges | Detonators (with their own safety fuse) | ONLY with own group |
| **C** | Mass-detonating propellants / explosives | Bulk TNT, dynamite, ANFO | D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, N, S |
| **D** | Secondary detonating substances | RDX, PETN, HMX | C, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, N, S |
| **E** | Blasting agents (mass-detonating) | Water gels, slurries | C, D, F, G, H, J, K, L, N, S |
| **F** | Low explosives, propellants | Smokeless powder, flash powder | C, D, E, G, H, J, K, L, N, S |
| **G** | Pyrotechnics | Flares, signals, fireworks | C, D, E, F, H, J, K, L, N, S |
| **H** | White phosphorus munitions | WP grenades | C, D, E, F, G, J, K, L, N, S |
| **J** | Liquid-detonating munitions | Liquid-fuel bombs | C, D, E, F, G, H, K, L, N, S |
| **K** | Storage-aid devices (inert) | Empty cartridges | C, D, E, F, G, H, J, L, N, S |
| **L** | Hazard Division 1.2 (fragments only) | Projectiles without mass-explosion | C, D, E, F, G, H, J, K, N, S |
| **N** | Hazard Division 1.6 (very low sensitivity) | Very insensitive substances | C, D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, S |
| **S** | Hazard Division 1.4 (no mass effect) | Small-arms ammunition, safety fuse | WITH EVERYTHING except A & B |

> [!WARNING]
> **Groups A and B must NEVER be stored together with any other group.** Detonators stored with cartridges can cause mass detonation of the entire magazine.

## 📏 3. Quantity-Distance (QD) Principles

The **single most tested concept** in KTU for this topic. The QD principle states:

> *"The greater the mass of explosive stored (NEQ), the greater the separation distance required from inhabited buildings, public roads, other magazines, and the magazine itself."*

### KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{W}$ (Cube-Root Law) | $D$ = distance, $W$ = NEQ | m, kg | Minimum separation between magazines |
| 2 | $D = k \cdot \sqrt{W}$ (Square-Root Law) | same | m, kg | Older US DoD rule for inhabited buildings |
| 3 | $\text{NEQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$ | sum of all explosive masses | kg | Total explosive quantity in magazine |
| 4 | $\text{Loading Density} = \dfrac{W}{V}$ | $W$ = NEQ, $V$ = magazine volume | kg/m³ | Magazine capacity utilization |
| 5 | $\text{Vent Area Ratio} = \dfrac{A_{\text{vent}}}{V_{\text{mag}}}$ | $A_{\text{vent}}$ = vent opening | m²/m³ | Required for deflagrating explosives |
| 6 | $D_{\text{ILD}} = 24.0 \cdot \sqrt[3]{W}$ | ILD = Intraline Distance | m, kg | Distance between two process buildings |
| 7 | $D_{\text{PHD}} = 18.0 \cdot \sqrt[3]{W}$ | PHD = Public Highway Distance | m, kg | Distance to public road |
| 8 | $D_{\text{IBD}} = 40.0 \cdot \sqrt[3]{W}$ | IBD = Inhabited Building Distance | m, kg | Distance to nearest inhabited building |
| 9 | $\text{Scaled Distance} = \dfrac{D}{\sqrt[3]{W}}$ | dimensionless | — | Used in blast-pressure analysis |

### 🔬 Engineering Use of Each Formula
- The **cube-root law** is the *industry standard* (Indian Explosives Rules 2008, US DoD 6055.09).
- $k$ varies with hazard division: typical values range from **8.0** (small magazines) to **45.0** (mass-explosion public safety).
- Scaled distance is reused in **blast overpressure prediction** (Kingery–Bulmash charts).

## 🏗️ 4. Magazine Construction — Engineering Specifications

| Element | Requirement |
|---|---|
| Walls | Reinforced concrete, **≥ 150 mm** thick, or earth-mounded |
| Roof | Lightweight frangible material (so it blows off, vents pressure) |
| Door | Steel, outward-opening, fitted with **two separate locks** |
| Flooring | Non-sparking (wood over concrete), no metal fasteners exposed |
| Ventilation | Cross-ventilation, vent area ≈ **1/150 of floor area** minimum |
| Lightning Protection | Air termination rod, down-conductor, earth resistance ≤ **10 Ω** |
| Temperature | Internal temp maintained ≤ **40 °C** for most explosives |
| Bonding | All metal fittings **bonded & earthed** to prevent static accumulation |
| Illumination | **No electrical fittings inside** the magazine; use battery LEDs outside through porthole |
| "No Smoking" | Mandatory sign, no naked flames within **30 m** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📘 Derivation 1 — The Cube-Root Distance Law

The cube-root law is the **cornerstone of explosive storage safety engineering**. It is derived from dimensional analysis and Hopkinson's scaling law for blast-wave propagation.

### 🧠 Why Cubic Root?

When an explosive charge of mass $W$ detonates, the energy released is:

$$
E \propto W \quad \text{(energy scales linearly with mass)}
$$

A blast wave of energy $E$ propagates spherically. The energy density (energy per unit surface area of the expanding sphere) at distance $D$ is:

$$
\varepsilon(D) = \frac{E}{4\pi D^{2}}
$$

Since we want a *constant* energy density $\varepsilon_{0}$ to produce the same damage level (e.g., eardrum rupture, window breakage, structural collapse) regardless of charge size:

$$
\frac{E}{4\pi D^{2}} = \varepsilon_{0}
$$

Because $E \propto W$, we have $E = cW$ for some explosive-specific constant $c$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{cW}{4\pi D^{2}} = \varepsilon_{0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad D^{2} = \frac{cW}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}
$$

Taking the cube root on both sides (this is where dimensional similarity and Hopkinson's third-power scaling enter):

$$
D = k \cdot W^{1/3}
$$

This is the **fundamental cube-root law**.

### ✅ Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Two surface magazines, each containing 5000 kg of NEQ (Hazard Division 1.1), are to be constructed. Determine the **Intraline Distance (ILD)** between them using the cube-root formula with $k = 24.0$.

**Solution:**

$$
D_{\text{ILD}} = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{W}
$$

$$
D_{\text{ILD}} = 24.0 \times \sqrt[3]{5000}
$$

$$
\sqrt[3]{5000} = (5000)^{1/3} = 17.0997\ldots
$$

$$
D_{\text{ILD}} = 24.0 \times 17.10
$$

$$
D_{\text{ILD}} = 410.41 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{ILD}} \approx 411 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always state **rounded to the nearest metre** because construction layouts use integer distances.

---

## 📘 Derivation 2 — Cumulative NEQ for Multiple Magazines

When several storage points exist, the **cumulative NEQ** must be considered for separation from public areas.

$$
W_{\text{cum}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i}
$$

### Worked Example

A site has three magazines:
- Magazine A: $W_A = 1200 \text{ kg}$
- Magazine B: $W_B = 800 \text{ kg}$
- Magazine C: $W_C = 2500 \text{ kg}$

**Total cumulative NEQ:**

$$
W_{\text{cum}} = 1200 + 800 + 2500
$$

$$
W_{\text{cum}} = 4500 \text{ kg}
$$

**Public Highway Distance (PHD), with $k = 18.0$:**

$$
D_{\text{PHD}} = 18.0 \times \sqrt[3]{4500}
$$

$$
\sqrt[3]{4500} = 16.5096
$$

$$
D_{\text{PHD}} = 18.0 \times 16.51
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{PHD}} \approx 297.2 \text{ m}}
$$

---

## 📘 Derivation 3 — Magazine Loading Density

To check whether a magazine is **overloaded** (which compromises ventilation and increases sympathetic detonation risk):

$$
\rho_{\text{loading}} = \frac{W_{\text{NEQ}}}{V_{\text{magazine}}}
$$

### Worked Example

A magazine has interior dimensions $6 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$ and stores 3000 kg of ANFO.

**Volume:**

$$
V = 6 \times 4 \times 3 = 72 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Loading Density:**

$$
\rho_{\text{loading}} = \frac{3000}{72}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{\text{loading}} = 41.67 \text{ kg/m}^{3}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Industry rule of thumb: Loading density **should not exceed 50 kg/m³** for ANFO in a permanent surface magazine, to ensure heat dissipation and venting.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table for Exam Revision

| Feature | Permanent Magazine | Day Magazine | Underground Magazine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Construction | Concrete / earth-mound | Steel / plywood cabinet | Rock tunnel, shotcrete-lined |
| Typical NEQ | 1000–10000 kg | ≤ 250 kg | 10000+ kg |
| Distance from blasting face | ≥ 100 m | ≤ 30 m (used and returned) | N/A |
| Occupancy | Unattended | Unattended | Unattended |
| Locking | Two locks, dual-key | Single lock + padlock | Multiple blast doors |
| Climatic control | Yes (vent + thermometer) | No | Naturally cool |
| Cost | High | Low | Very High |
| Use case | Long-term bulk storage | Day-shift carry-over | Bulk military / strategic reserve |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📈 Diagram 1 — Storage Decision & Safety Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Explosive Arrives at Site"] --> B{"Is It a Detonator or Booster?"}
    B -- "YES" --> C["Storage Compatibility Group A or B"]
    B -- "NO" --> D["Storage Compatibility Group C through S"]
    C --> E["Dedicated Detonator Magazine"]
    D --> F["Bulk Explosive Magazine"]
    E --> G{"NEQ less than magazine license limit?"}
    F --> G
    G -- "YES" --> H["Compute QD using cube root law D = k times W to power 1/3"]
    G -- "NO" --> I["STOP - Split NEQ into smaller magazines"]
    H --> J["Check separation from inhabited buildings IBD"]
    H --> K["Check separation from public roads PHD"]
    H --> L["Check separation from other magazines ILD"]
    J --> M["All distances satisfy Indian Explosives Rules 2008?"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M -- "YES" --> N["Grant Storage Approval and Log in Register"]
    M -- "NO" --> O["Revise layout or reduce NEQ"]
```
