---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three safety requirements for the construction of an above-ground explosive magazine.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Walls** must be of reinforced concrete, brick, or earth-mound construction with a minimum thickness of **150 mm**, designed to prevent fragment projection during an accidental detonation.
2. **Roof** must be of lightweight frangible material such as weather-resistant corrugated asbestos or thin reinforced concrete so that it blows off in case of internal detonation, thereby venting pressure and reducing blast reflection.
3. The **door** must be of steel construction, open outward, and fitted with **two independent locks** whose keys are held by **two different authorized persons** to enforce dual-control security.
4. **Lightning protection** with air termination rod, down-conductor, and earth resistance ≤ **10 Ω** is mandatory.
5. **Ventilation openings** totalling at least **1/150 of floor area** must be provided to prevent heat and fume accumulation.

*(Any three well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why are detonators stored in a separate magazine away from bulk explosives?*

**Model Answer:**

Detonators belong to **Storage Compatibility Group B**, while bulk explosives such as ANFO, dynamite, or emulsions belong to **Groups C through S**. Detonators are **primary initiating explosives** — they contain small but highly sensitive charges (lead azide, mercury fulminate, PETN) that can be triggered by a spark, friction, or minor impact. If stored together with bulk explosives, the following hazards arise:

- A sympathetic detonation of one detonator can initiate the entire bulk stockpile.
- The blast pressure from a bulk explosion can **drive detonators into detonation**, turning a deflagration into a detonation.
- Theft of one package of detonators enables unauthorized use as **improvised initiating assemblies**.

Hence, the **Indian Explosives Rules 2008** and international conventions mandate that detonators be housed in a **separate, dedicated magazine** at a **Minimum Intraline Distance** from bulk magazines as computed by $D = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{W}$.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - ❌ Students often write QD formulas without stating which **type of distance** (ILD / PHD / IBD) they are computing. **Always declare the k-value.**
> - ❌ Students forget to convert grams to kg or use NEQ inconsistently.
> - ❌ Day-magazine rules are often confused with permanent-magazine rules.
> - ❌ Students omit mentioning **compatibility group** before calculating QD — this loses 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **cube-root quantity-distance law** with its derivation. How is it applied to determine the intraline distance between two explosive magazines? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A surface magazine stores **8000 kg** of Hazard Division 1.1 explosive (mass-detonating). Calculate:
- (i) Intraline Distance (ILD) using $k = 24.0$
- (ii) Public Highway Distance (PHD) using $k = 18.0$
- (iii) Inhabited Building Distance (IBD) using $k = 40.0$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(a) — Cube-Root QD Law

**Definition:**
The cube-root quantity-distance (QD) law is a scaling rule that establishes a relationship between the **mass of explosive stored (W)** and the **safe separation distance (D)** required between the magazine and other structures, such that any damage level from an accidental detonation is maintained **constant** regardless of charge size.

**Derivation:**

When an explosive of mass $W$ detonates, the energy released is:

$$
E \propto W
$$

For a spherical blast wave at distance $D$, the energy per unit area of the wave front is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{E}{4\pi D^{2}}
$$

For the same damage threshold, $\varepsilon$ must remain constant. Substituting $E = cW$:

$$
\frac{cW}{4\pi D^{2}} = \text{constant}
$$

Solving for $D$:

$$
D^{2} \propto W \quad \Rightarrow \quad D \propto W^{1/3}
$$

Hence, the cube-root law:

$$
D = k \cdot W^{1/3}
$$

where $k$ depends on the **hazard division** and the **type of distance** (ILD, PHD, IBD).

**Application to Intraline Distance:**
Between two magazines on the same process line, the ILD is computed by substituting the **NEQ of the larger magazine** and the **ILD coefficient** $k$. For example, with $k = 24.0$ and $W = 5000$ kg, $D_{\text{ILD}} = 411$ m.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(b) — Numerical Calculations

**Given:** $W = 8000$ kg, Hazard Division 1.1

**(i) Intraline Distance:**

$$
D_{\text{ILD}} = 24.0 \times \sqrt[3]{8000}
$$

$$
\sqrt[3]{8000} = 20.0
$$

$$
D_{\text{ILD}} = 24.0 \times 20.0 = 480 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{ILD}} = 480 \text{ m}}
$$

**(ii) Public Highway Distance:**

$$
D_{\text{PHD}} = 18.0 \times \sqrt[3]{8000} = 18.0 \times 20.0 = 360 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{PHD}} = 360 \text{ m}}
$$

**(iii) Inhabited Building Distance:**

$$
D_{\text{IBD}} = 40.0 \times \sqrt[3]{8000} = 40.0 \times 20.0 = 800 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{IBD}} = 800 \text{ m}}
$$

**Comparison Table for Exam Presentation:**

| Distance Type | k-value | Distance (m) |
|---|---|---|
| ILD | 24.0 | 480 |
| PHD | 18.0 | 360 |
| IBD | 40.0 | 800 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IBD is always the **largest** because the inhabited-building protection level demands the most conservative damage criterion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss the **Storage Compatibility Groups** used in explosive storage. Explain why detonators (Group B) must never be stored with bulk explosives (Group C). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **constructional features of a permanent surface magazine** for bulk storage of emulsion explosives. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Compatibility Groups

**Concept:**
The UN system classifies explosives into **Storage Compatibility Groups A through S (excluding I, M, O, P, Q, R)**. Two explosives from **non-compatible groups** are not permitted to be stored in the same magazine because a runaway reaction in one can initiate the other.

**Key Group Highlights for Exam:**

- **Group A** — Primary initiating explosives (lead azide, mercury fulminate). Most sensitive. Stored alone.
- **Group B** — Detonators, boosters. Contain initiating explosives. Stored alone in their own magazine.
- **Group C** — Mass-detonating high explosives (TNT, dynamite, ANFO).
- **Groups D–N** — Secondary detonating, blasting agents, propellants, pyrotechnics, white phosphorus, liquids.
- **Group S** — Hazard Division 1.4 articles; very low hazard; can be stored with almost all groups (except A and B).

**Why Group B Must NEVER Be Stored With Group C:**

1. **Sympathetic Detonation:** A propagating detonation front from a Group C magazine can drive adjacent detonators into full detonation, multiplying the hazard.
2. **Theft Risk:** Detonators separated from bulk explosives prevent a thief from acquiring a complete blasting assembly in one theft.
3. **Blast Pressure Translation:** The overpressure from a mass-explosion in Group C converts the low-order deflagration of a detonator into a high-order detonation.
4. **Indian Explosives Rules 2008, Schedule II:** Legally mandates minimum **intraline separation** between Group B and Group C magazines; physically separating them is a regulatory requirement, not an option.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Permanent Magazine Construction

*(Refer to Diagram 3 in Section 4 for visual support.)*

**Required Constructional Features:**

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Walls** | Reinforced concrete, **≥ 150 mm thick**, or earth-mound ≥ **600 mm cover** |
| **Roof** | Lightweight, frangible (asbestos or thin RC) — to vent overpressure |
| **Door** | Steel, outward-opening, two locks, no exposed metal on inner face |
| **Floor** | Wood planks over concrete, no metallic nails exposed |
| **Ventilation** | Cross-flow, total area **≥ 1/150 of floor area** |
| **Lightning** | Air rod, down-conductor, earth resistance **≤ 10 Ω** |
| **Electrical** | No switches, sockets, or wiring inside; explosion-proof fittings outside |
| **Bonding** | All metal fittings earthed to prevent static accumulation |
| **Signage** | "EXPLOSIVES — NO SMOKING — NO NAKED FLAMES" |
| **Distance** | Maintained as per QD cube-root formula |

**Why these features matter for emulsion explosives:**

- Emulsions are **water-based** and tolerate mild temperature rise, but the matrix can break down above **50 °C**, releasing oxidizer salts. Ventilation keeps temperature in check.
- Emulsion cartridges have an **aluminium or cardboard tube**; metal contact with the floor is forbidden to avoid sparks.
- The **frangible roof** ensures that any accidental initiation pressure is vented upwards rather than producing dangerous horizontal fragments that would injure personnel.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ)** is the key quantity for ALL distance calculations.
> ✅ **Cube-root law** $D = k \cdot W^{1/3}$ is the master formula — memorize the **three standard k-values**: ILD = 24.0, PHD = 18.0, IBD = 40.0.
> ✅ **Detonators (Group B)** and **Primary explosives (Group A)** are stored *alone*, never with bulk explosives.
> ✅ **Group S** is the universal "friendly" group and can be stored with almost anything except A and B.
> ✅ **Magazine roof must be frangible** — to vent, not contain, blast pressure.
> ✅ **Two-lock dual-key system** on magazine doors — keys held by two different people.
> ✅ **Lightning protection** mandatory with earth resistance ≤ **10 Ω**.
> ✅ **No electrical fittings inside** the magazine; use external battery LEDs.
> ✅ **Loading density** of bulk magazines should not exceed **50 kg/m³** for ANFO.
> ✅ **Day Magazine** = short-term storage at blasting face, ≤ **250 kg** typically, single lock allowed.
> ✅ **Permanent Magazine** = long-term engineered storage, dual lock, climate controlled, QD applied.
> ✅ **Stock Register** is a legal document under Indian Explosives Rules — every receipt, issue, return, and destruction must be logged with date, quantity, batch, and authorized signature.
> ✅ **Expired explosives** must be destroyed by **burning (for propellants/pyrotechnics)** or **detonation (for high explosives)** under supervision of a licensed blaster and witness from the Controller of Explosives.
> ✅ **Sympathetic detonation** is the #1 reason magazines are separated — never rely on walls alone.

---

> **🎓 End of Module 4 — Storage of Explosives Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PESFT753 | Course Outcomes CO1, CO2, CO3, CO5*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosive Facilities and Explosives Operations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Explosive Facilities and Explosives Operations

---

## 1.1 Explosive Facilities: Formal Definition

**Explosive facilities** are purpose-built, regulatory-compliant physical installations — including storage magazines, operating buildings, mixing/loading bays, and processing plants — engineered to safely house, process, manufacture, and handle explosive substances throughout their life cycle. The design of such facilities is governed by a strict triad: **Quantity-Distance (Q-D) compliance**, **structural blast resistance**, and **operational zoning** that prevents accidental initiation from propagating to neighbouring units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Explosives Rules, 2008 (India) — framed under the Explosives Act, 1884 — define the legal blueprint for every explosive facility in the country. Any deviation from prescribed Q-D norms renders a facility **non-compliant and inoperative** under law.

### Types of Facilities (KTU Terminology)

| Facility Type | Function |
|---|---|
| **Permanent Magazine** | Long-term, year-round storage of finished explosives & detonators |
| **Temporary Magazine** | Short-duration (≤ 90 days) on-site storage at blasting sites |
| **Day Box / Field Magazine** | Portable storage for daily-issue explosives at work face |
| **Operating Building / Process House** | Mixing, filling, assembling, or loading of explosives |
| **Destroying Ground** | Designated area for burning/detonating waste explosives |
| **Accessory Store** | Storage of non-explosive blasting accessories (fuses, wire) |
| **Caps Magazine** | Detached storage specifically for detonators/caps (separated from bulk explosives) |

---

## 1.2 Explosive Operations: Intuitive Overview

**Explosive operations** refer to the controlled sequence of activities involved in **manufacturing, testing, transporting, storing, issuing, handling, loading, firing, and destroying** explosive materials in a manner that ensures human safety, asset protection, and environmental security.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Vault of a Munitions Factory":**
> Think of an explosive facility as a *bank vault system*. Just as a bank vault stores currency in a heavily reinforced, fire-resistant, access-controlled room with strict protocols (two-key system, audit logs, separation of high-value from low-value items), an explosive magazine stores high-energy materials in blast-resistant, lightning-protected, access-controlled rooms. The **"distance between vaults"** is analogous to the **Q-D separation distance** — even if one vault "fails" (detonates), the explosion wave and fragments must not reach the next vault. Just as banks separate cash from jewellery (high risk items), explosive magazines physically separate **detonators from bulk explosives**, because detonators are the *trigger* and bulk explosives are the *charge* — keeping them apart prevents sympathetic detonation.

### The Operational Lifecycle (KTU Exam Gold)

A complete explosives operation follows this canonical sequence:

$$\text{Manufacture} \rightarrow \text{Quality Test} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Transport} \rightarrow \text{Issue} \rightarrow \text{Handling} \rightarrow \text{Loading} \rightarrow \text{Firing} \rightarrow \text{Post-Blast Inspection} \rightarrow \text{Destruction of Wastes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every transition in this chain is a **legal checkpoint** under the Explosives Rules, 2008. A licensed explosive must be trackable at *every* node of this chain, with documented chain-of-custody.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU)

- **Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ)** — total mass of explosive material (in **kg**), excluding packaging.
- **Net Explosive Content (NEC)** — actual mass of explosive substance within an article (e.g., explosive charge inside a detonator).
- **Barricading** — an earth mound or solid wall that interrupts line-of-sight and direct fragment/blast propagation between two magazines.
- **Mounded Magazine** — earth-covered storage with ≥ **60 cm** of earth cover on roof and sides.
- **Above-Ground Magazine** — conventional building-style storage, ≥ **300 mm** reinforced concrete walls.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Quantity-Distance (Q-D) Concept — The Heart of the Module

The **Quantity-Distance (Q-D)** relationship is the single most important design principle in explosive facility engineering. It links the **net quantity of explosive (Q)** at one location to the **minimum separation distance (D)** required to protect a neighbouring target from the effects of an accidental detonation.

### Why Does Q-D Exist? — The "Why" Behind the Principle

When an explosive detonates, three damage vectors propagate outward:

1. **Blast Overpressure Wave** — a supersonic pressure front that damages structures and ruptures eardrums.
2. **High-Velocity Fragments** — casing/shrapnel traveling at **1,500–3,000 m/s**.
3. **Thermal Radiation & Fireball** — momentary high-temperature zone igniting combustible materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** The overpressure decays roughly with the *cube* of distance, while fragment density falls off roughly with the *square* of distance. Hence, increasing Q requires a *non-linear* (sub-cubic) increase in D — this is why Q-D tables are non-linear.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cube-Root Law (US DoD Standard)** | $D = K \cdot Q^{1/3}$ | $D$ = distance (m), $Q$ = NEQ (kg), $K$ = constant based on protection level | Foundation of modern Q-D tables |
| **Square-Root Law (older Indian practice)** | $D = K \cdot \sqrt{Q}$ | Same as above | Conservative, used in some legacy Indian site rules |
| **Combined Magazine Q-sum (TNT-equivalent)** | $Q_{eq} = \sum_{i} \left( Q_i \cdot f_i \right)$ | $f_i$ = TNT-equivalence factor of substance $i$ | For mixed storage of different explosive classes |
| **Barricaded vs Unbarricaded distance** | $D_{barricaded} = 0.5 \cdot D_{unbarricaded}$ (typical) | Empirical ratio | Effective distance halved if an intervening barricade is present |
| **NEQ → TNT equivalence (PETN/NG based)** | $Q_{TNT} = 1.0$ for TNT; $1.25$ for PETN; $0.4$ for ANFO | Conversion multipliers | Hazard classification |
| **Trailer distance** | $D_{trailer} = 1.0 \cdot D_{inhabited}$ (typical Indian practice) | Ratio-based | For occupied vehicles |
| **Personnel Safety Distance (Open Storage)** | $D_{person} = 100 \cdot Q^{1/3}$ (m) for $Q$ in kg | Empirical | Worker exclusion zone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Numericals:** Always state whether the question uses **cube-root** or **square-root** convention. KTU typically follows the **Indian Explosives Rules 2008 cube-root convention** (Table 4.1 of Schedule II), with $K$ values ranging from **1.5 (magazine-to-magazine)** to **8.0 (magazine-to-public highway)**.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Q-D Distances (KTU Exam Focus)

The Indian Explosives Rules, 2008 define **three principal distance categories** for each magazine:

### A. Inter-Magazine Distance ($D_{mm}$)
- Distance **between two explosive stores** to prevent sympathetic detonation.
- Smallest of the three distances (the magazines are similarly vulnerable).
- Barricading reduces this by ~**50%**.

### B. Processed Building / Operating House Distance ($D_{po}$)
- Distance from a magazine to a **building where explosives are handled or processed** (mixing, filling).
- Intermediate value.

### C. Inhabited Building / Public Highway Distance ($D_{ib}$)
- Distance from a magazine to **places of human habitation, public roads, railways, power lines**.
- **Largest of the three distances** — because human life has the highest protection priority.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** KTU examiners often test the *relative ordering* $D_{mm} < D_{po} < D_{ib}$. Marks are awarded only if this order is correctly justified with reasoning, not just stated.

---

## 2.4 Magazine Design Standards (KTU High-Yield)

| Design Element | Standard Specification |
|---|---|
| **Wall thickness (above-ground)** | ≥ **300 mm** reinforced cement concrete, or **460 mm** brick with **9"** thick earth backing |
| **Roof cover (mounded magazine)** | ≥ **600 mm** earth or sand cover, slope **1:2** for rain runoff |
| **Ventilation** | Vent area ≥ **1/30th of floor area**, with non-ferrous (brass) mosquito mesh |
| **Lightning protection** | ≥ **2 nos.** copper/GI lightning arresters per magazine, Earth resistance < **10 Ω** |
| **Door** | Single, outward-opening, hardwood, with **brass hinges & lock** (no ferrous metal) |
| **Floor** | Wood/timber planks, or smooth cement finish with no exposed iron |
| **No-smoking / No-fire zone** | Minimum **15 m** clear radius, signboards in 3 languages |
| **Temperature limit** | Magazine interior ≤ **40°C** for nitro-glycerine based explosives |
| **Humidity control** | Desiccant (silica gel) for hygroscopic explosives; ideal RH ≤ **60%** |
| **Two-key system** | One key with Magazine-in-charge, one with Senior Officer |

---

## 2.5 Explosives Operations: Step-Wise Procedure

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **operational SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)**. Each step has a defined authority and a documented record.

### Step 1 — Issue Authorization
- Only **competent persons** (with Form CCE-3 certification under Indian rules) may handle explosives.
- Issue is recorded in **Magazine Stock Register (Form MSR-1)** with date, time, quantity, batch no.

### Step 2 — Issue Limits
- **Maximum 1 day's operational requirement** may be issued to a work face.
- For deep-hole blasting: maximum **5 days' requirement** may be pre-issued in a day box.

### Step 3 — Transportation (Outside the Magazine)
- Explosives and detonators must be transported in **separate vehicles**.
- Vehicle must display **red flags, "EXPLOSIVES" placard**, and a trained escort.
- Speed limit on mine roads: typically **20 km/h** with explosive load.

### Step 4 — Loading & Charging
- Charging done by **trained blaster only**, supervised by **blasting foreman**.
- Borehole cleaning: never use iron tools; only **wooden/HDPE tamping rods**.
- Stemming: ≥ **two-thirds of borehole length**, with **10–20 mm crushed stone or stemming plug**.

### Step 5 — Firing
- Firing only after **all-clear siren** and personnel evacuation beyond **standoff distance**.
- **Cover-in distance** = $D_{ib}$ × safety factor (typically 1.5–2×).
- Misfire procedure: wait **30 minutes minimum** before approach; never attempt re-priming without blast engineer inspection.

### Step 6 — Post-Blast Inspection
- Inspection by **blasting foreman** after **20-minute** gas-clearance time.
- Record gas levels: **CO < 25 ppm, NO₂ < 5 ppm, O₂ > 20.5%**.
- Misfires logged in **Misfire Register** with location, charge, and date.

### Step 7 — Destruction of Waste Explosives
- By **burning** (low explosives: ≤ 5 kg/batch) or **detonation** (high explosives).
- Destroying ground at minimum **Q-D** distance from any inhabited area.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mining industry:** Q-D standards prevent catastrophic chain detonations in multi-bench opencast mines.
- **Defence & ammunition depots:** Magazine siting using Q-D governs national ammunition logistics.
- **Civil blasting (tunnels, quarries):** Day-box concept limits exposure to **one shift's** use.
- **Pyrotechnic manufacturing:** Process-house zoning keeps the workforce safe from any single-point failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cube-Root Quantity-Distance Law

The empirical cube-root law is derived from the principle that **peak overpressure decays approximately inversely with the cube of distance** for a constant charge. We will derive this from first principles using dimensional analysis and blast-scaling.

### Step 1 — Setup of the Problem
Consider a point detonation of mass $Q$ (kg) of TNT. The peak side-on overpressure at distance $R$ depends on:

$$P = f(Q, R, \rho_0, c_0, E_{TNT})$$

where $\rho_0$ is ambient air density (kg/m³), $c_0$ is ambient sound speed (m/s), and $E_{TNT}$ is specific energy (J/kg).

### Step 2 — Buckingham π-Theorem Reduction
Using dimensional analysis with $E_{TNT} = 4.184 \times 10^6$ J/kg, the dependent variable can be expressed via two dimensionless groups:

$$\frac{P}{\rho_0 c_0^2} = \phi\!\left( \frac{R}{Q^{1/3}} \right)$$

> **Step 2 Logic:** By Buckingham's π-theorem applied to 5 variables (P, Q, R, ρ₀, c₀) and 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), we obtain $5 - 3 = 2$ dimensionless groups. The first is the **Mach-like overpressure ratio**; the second is the **Hopkinson-Cranz scaled distance** $\bar{R} = R/Q^{1/3}$.

### Step 3 — Definition of the Scaled Distance
Define the **Hopkinson-Cranz scaled distance**:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R}{Q^{1/3}} \quad [\text{m} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1/3}]$$

> **Step 3 Logic:** This dimensionless scaling collapses overpressure curves from charges of *any size* onto a single master curve, which is the cornerstone of blast scaling.

### Step 4 — Linearisation for Low-to-Moderate Overpressures
For peak overpressures in the range **3 kPa to 200 kPa** (most relevant to facility design), empirical fits yield:

$$\frac{P}{P_a} \approx A \cdot \bar{R}^{-n}$$

with empirical constants $A \approx 0.95$ and $n \approx 1.30$ to $1.45$ (Kingery–Bulmash regime).

### Step 5 — Solving for Distance R
Setting a **target overpressure** $P^*$ (e.g., 70 kPa — the human lung-injury threshold) and inverting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R}^{*} &= \left( \frac{A \cdot P_a}{P^{*}} \right)^{1/n} \\
R^{*} &= \bar{R}^{*} \cdot Q^{1/3}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 5 Logic:** We isolate $Q^{1/3}$ on one side. The constant $K = \bar{R}^*$ absorbs the target overpressure and ambient conditions. This *proves* the cube-root scaling.

### Step 6 — Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\, D \;=\; K \cdot Q^{1/3} \,}$$

This is the **Quantity-Distance cube-root law** in standard form.

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A magazine stores $Q = 1{,}000$ kg of explosive. Using cube-root scaling with $K = 1.5$ for inter-magazine distance, calculate the separation required from a similar magazine.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= K \cdot Q^{1/3} \\
  &= 1.5 \cdot (1000)^{1/3} \\
  &= 1.5 \cdot 10 \\
  &= \mathbf{15 \; m}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 7 Logic:** $1000^{1/3} = 10$ exactly. With $Q = 8{,}000$ kg, distance becomes $1.5 \times 20 = 30$ m. **Doubling Q (e.g., from 1,000 to 8,000) increases D by only $\sqrt[3]{8} = 2\times$** — this is the *non-linear economy of scale* that Q-D tables exploit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Square-Root Law (Legacy Indian Use)

### Step 1 — Empirical Origin
The square-root law is a *more conservative* legacy approximation used in some Indian site rules. It arises from a simpler dimensional argument that neglects shock-wave thermodynamics.

### Step 2 — Assumption
Assume peak overpressure decays as $P \propto R^{-1}$ (linear, not cubic decay) for conservative engineering:

$$P = \frac{C \cdot Q^{1/2}}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Inversion
Setting $P = P^{*}$ and solving for $R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{C}{P^*} \cdot Q^{1/2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad D &= K' \cdot \sqrt{Q}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2–3 Logic:** This gives a steeper distance penalty for large $Q$, making it a *safer* (more conservative) design choice. The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises it as a **legacy alternative** to the cube-root rule.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
For $Q = 1{,}000$ kg, $K' = 1.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{1000} \\
  &= 1.5 \cdot 31.62 \\
  &\approx \mathbf{47.4 \; m}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Step 4 Logic:** Notice $47.4 \gg 15$ m for the same $Q$ — confirming square-root gives a more conservative (larger) safety distance. KTU often asks: *"Compare the two laws for $Q = 1{,}000$ kg and comment."*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Q-D for Different Magazine Classes

| Magazine Class | Stored NEQ (kg) | $D_{mm}$ (m, cube-root $K=1.5$) | $D_{ib}$ (m, cube-root $K=8.0$) | $D_{po}$ (m, cube-root $K=4.0$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small Day Box | 5 | 2.4 | 12.9 | 6.4 |
| Field Magazine | 50 | 5.1 | 27.4 | 13.7 |
| Permanent Magazine | 500 | 10.5 | 57.7 | 28.8 |
| Bulk Depot | 5,000 | 17.1 | 93.4 | 46.7 |
| Large Ammunition Depot | 50,000 | 28.1 | 157.0 | 78.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the matrix:** The $D_{ib}$ value (inhabited building) is always the largest, $D_{mm}$ is the smallest. This is the *order* KTU examiners look for in student answers.

---

## 3.4 Combined-Magazine Net Explosive Quantity (TNT Equivalence)

When multiple explosive types are stored together, the **equivalent TNT mass** must be summed:

### Step 1 — Convert Each Mass to TNT Equivalence

$$Q_{TNT, i} = Q_i \cdot f_i$$

where $f_i$ is the **TNT-equivalence factor** (PETN = 1.25, RDX = 1.20, TNT = 1.00, ANFO = 0.40, dynamite = 0.80).

### Step 2 — Sum Across All Classes

$$Q_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{TNT, i}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Q-D Using $Q_{eq}$

$$D = K \cdot Q_{eq}^{1/3}$$

### Worked Example
A magazine holds 200 kg PETN, 500 kg ANFO, and 100 kg dynamite.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{TNT, PETN}   &= 200 \times 1.25 = 250 \;\text{kg} \\
Q_{TNT, ANFO}    &= 500 \times 0.40 = 200 \;\text{kg} \\
Q_{TNT, dynamite}&= 100 \times 0.80 = 80 \;\text{kg} \\
Q_{eq} &= 250 + 200 + 80 = 530 \;\text{kg} \\
D_{ib} &= 8.0 \cdot (530)^{1/3} \\
      &= 8.0 \cdot 8.10 \\
      &\approx \mathbf{64.8 \; m}
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Magazine Layout Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["EXPLOSIVE FACILITY COMPLEX"]
    nodeB["ADMIN ZONE"]
    nodeC["STORAGE ZONE"]
    nodeD["OPERATIONS ZONE"]
    nodeE["DESTRUCTION ZONE"]
    nodeF["BOUNDARY FENCE 2.4M HEIGHT"]
    nodeG["ADMIN OFFICE"]
    nodeH["REGISTRY AND RECORDS"]
    nodeI["FIRST AID STATION"]
    nodeJ["MAIN PERMANENT MAGAZINE"]
    nodeK["CAP MAGAZINE DETONATOR STORE"]
    nodeL["DAY BOX FIELD MAGAZINE"]
    nodeM["MIXING AND LOADING BAY"]
    nodeN["TESTING LABORATORY"]
    nodeO["DESTROYING GROUND"]
    nodeP["WASTE BURNING PLATFORM"]
    nodeQ["FENCED PERIMETER 15M RADIUS"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeQ
    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeD --> nodeN
    nodeE --> nodeO
    nodeE --> nodeP
    nodeJ -. Q-D DISTANCE .-> nodeK
    nodeJ -. Q-D DISTANCE .-> nodeM
    nodeK -. Q-D DISTANCE .-> nodeM
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Functional Architecture of an Explosive Facility.** The dashed arrows represent **Q-D distance compliance vectors**; the magazine-to-magazine, magazine-to-process-house, and magazine-to-inhabited-building distances must all be honoured simultaneously.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow: Explosive Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START MANUFACTURE"]
    stepB["QUALITY TEST IN LAB"]
    stepC["ISSUE TO MAGAZINE WITH ENTRY IN MSR"]
    stepD["STORE IN PERMANENT MAGAZINE Q-D COMPLIANT"]
    stepE["RECEIVE ISSUE REQUEST FROM BLASTER"]
    stepF["ISSUE TO VEHICLE WITH PLACARDING"]
    stepG["TRANSPORT IN SEPARATE VEHICLES DETONATOR AND BULK"]
    stepH["DELIVER TO DAY BOX AT WORKFACE"]
    stepI["LOAD BOREHOLE WITH WOODEN TAMPING"]
    stepJ["CONNECT DETONATOR PRIMING CIRCUIT"]
    stepK["EVACUATE TO COVER-IN DISTANCE"]
    stepL["FIRE BY AUTHORISED BLASTER"]
    stepM["WAIT 20 MIN FOR GAS CLEARANCE"]
    stepN["POST BLAST INSPECTION FOR MISFIRES"]
    stepO["RECORD WASTE QUANTITY"]
    stepP["DESTROY WASTE AT DESTROYING GROUND"]
    stepQ["CLOSE LOOP WITH STOCK REGISTER UPDATE"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepM
    stepM --> stepN
    stepN --> stepO
    stepO --> stepP
    stepP --> stepQ
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Closed-Loop Explosive Operation.** Every node is a **legally documented checkpoint** under the Explosives Rules 2008. Missing even one log entry is an audit non-conformity.

---

## 4.3 Q-D Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    qa["IDENTIFY NET EXPLOSIVE QUANTITY Q IN KG"]
    qb["DETERMINE EXPLOSIVE CLASS TYPES"]
    qc["APPLY TNT EQUIVALENCE FACTORS"]
    qd["COMPUTE QEQ SUM OF TNT EQUIVALENTS"]
    qe["SELECT DISTANCE TYPE DMM DPO OR DIB"]
    qf["APPROPRIATE K VALUE FROM SCHEDULE"]
    qg["COMPUTE D = K TIMES QEQ TO THE POWER 1 DIVIDED BY 3"]
    qh["COMPARE WITH MEASURED SITE DISTANCE"]
    qi["DISTANCE IS COMPLIANT"]
    qj["DISTANCE IS NON COMPLIANT REVISE LAYOUT"]

    qa --> qb
    qb --> qc
    qc --> qd
    qd --> qe
    qe --> qf
    qf --> qg
    qg --> qh
    qh --> qi
    qh --> qj
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Q-D Compliance Decision Tree.** This is the *exact sequence* KTU examiners expect when a "design a magazine layout" numerical is posed.

---

## 4.4 Storage Zone Schematic: Barricade Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mag1["MAGAZINE A NEQ 500 KG"]
    mag2["MAGAZINE B NEQ 200 KG"]
    bar["BARRICADE EARTH MOUND 1.5 M HIGH"]
    road["INHABITED BUILDING"]

    mag1 -- "DMM 9.1 M UNBARRICADED" --> mag2
    bar -. "REDUCES DMM BY 50 PERCENT" .-> mag2
    mag1 -- "DIB 53.3 M" --> road
```

> **Figure 4.4 — Effect of Barricading on Q-D Distance.** Barricades cut $D_{mm}$ by half (typical), but they do **not** reduce $D_{ib}$ (inhabited building distance) — life safety dominates.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ). Why is it critical for Q-D calculations?**

> **Model Answer:**
> Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ) is the total mass of explosive substance (in kg) contained in a storage unit, **excluding** packaging, containers, or inert components. It is critical for Q-D calculations because the *quantity of energy released* during accidental detonation scales with NEQ, and therefore the **separation distance required to protect people, property, and other magazines is a direct function of NEQ**. The Indian Explosives Rules, 2008 mandate Q-D calculations based on NEQ, not gross mass.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Permanent Magazine and a Day Box.**

> **Model Answer:**
> | Feature | Permanent Magazine | Day Box |
> |---|---|---|
> | Purpose | Long-term, year-round bulk storage | Daily, on-shift issue at work face |
> | Quantity limit | Large (often tonnes) | Small (single-shift requirement) |
> | Construction | Reinforced concrete / mounded earth | Steel / wood portable chest |
> | Licensing | Full licence under Explosives Rules 2008 | Permitted under storage licence conditions |
> | Location | Centralized, fenced complex | At active blasting face |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Choice-Based)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A surface mine plans to construct a permanent magazine to store 8,000 kg of mixed explosives (3,000 kg ANFO, 3,000 kg emulsion explosive, 2,000 kg slurry explosive). Using the cube-root Q-D law, calculate:**

**(a)** The equivalent TNT mass of the stored inventory.  
**(b)** The required inter-magazine distance (use $K = 1.5$) and inhabited-building distance (use $K = 8.0$).  
**(c)** Comment on the role of barricading and the two-key system in magazine safety.

#### Solution

**(a) TNT Equivalence**

| Explosive | Mass (kg) | TNT Factor | $Q_{TNT}$ (kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ANFO | 3,000 | 0.40 | 1,200 |
| Emulsion | 3,000 | 0.80 | 2,400 |
| Slurry | 2,000 | 0.85 | 1,700 |
| **Total** | | | **5,300 kg** |

$$\boxed{\, Q_{eq} = 5{,}300 \text{ kg of TNT equivalent} \,}$$

**(b) Q-D Calculation**

For $Q_{eq} = 5{,}300$ kg:

$$Q_{eq}^{1/3} = (5300)^{1/3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(5300)^{1/3} &= (5.3 \times 10^3)^{1/3} \\
            &= (5.3)^{1/3} \times 10 \\
            &\approx 1.74 \times 10 \\
            &= 17.4
\end{aligned}$$

Inter-magazine distance:
$$D_{mm} = 1.5 \times 17.4 = \mathbf{26.1 \text{ m}}$$

Inhabited-building distance:
$$D_{ib} = 8.0 \times 17.4 = \mathbf{139.2 \text{ m}}$$

**(c) Barricading & Two-Key System**

- **Barricading** is a physical earth mound or masonry wall (≥ 1.5 m high) that interrupts the line-of-sight between two magazines, blocking direct fragment propagation and reducing effective $D_{mm}$ by ~50%. However, barricades do **not** reduce $D_{ib}$ because overpressure and fireball still propagate.
- **Two-key system** ensures that no single individual can open the magazine alone — one key is held by the **Magazine-in-Charge** and the other by the **Senior Officer/Manager**. This enforces **dual authorization** at every issue/receipt, providing a control layer against theft, mis-issue, and unauthorized access — both safety and security dimensions.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**With a clear labelled diagram, describe the design features of a Permanent Magazine as per Indian Explosives Rules, 2008. List the operational sequence for issue-and-use of explosives at a mine site.**

#### Solution Outline

**(a) Design Features of a Permanent Magazine (7 marks)**

A standard permanent magazine has:

1. **Walls:** 300 mm RCC or 460 mm brick with 230 mm earth backing.
2. **Roof:** 600 mm earth cover (mounded) with 1:2 slope, OR 150 mm RCC for above-ground type.
3. **Floor:** Timber planks or smooth cement (no exposed iron or ferrous nails).
4. **Door:** Single, outward-opening, hardwood with brass hinges and lock.
5. **Ventilation:** 1/30 floor area, with brass mesh to keep out sparks/insects.
6. **Lightning Protection:** Two copper/GI arresters, earth resistance < 10 Ω.
7. **Two-Key System:** Split key custody.
8. **No-smoking zone:** 15 m clear radius with bilingual signage.
9. **Distance compliance:** Q-D verified for all three categories ($D_{mm}, D_{po}, D_{ib}$).
10. **First-aid & fire-fighting:** Sand buckets, water hoses, and a First-Aid box at the magazine gate.

**(b) Operational Sequence (7 marks)**

1. **Authorization:** Blaster raises issue slip signed by Foreman.
2. **Magazine Stock Register (MSR-1):** Entry of date, time, batch, quantity, signature.
3. **Issue:** Magazine-in-charge hands over to authorized person, both keys used.
4. **Transport:** Separate vehicles for detonators and bulk explosives, red-flagged.
5. **Day Box:** Stored at workface; no more than shift's requirement.
6. **Loading:** Wooden tamping rods, no iron tools, supervised by foreman.
7. **Firing:** All-clear siren, evacuation to cover-in distance, misfire wait 30 min.
8. **Closure:** Unused explosives returned, MSR updated, vehicle checked in.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cube root** — writing $D = K \cdot Q$ instead of $D = K \cdot Q^{1/3}$ loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing NEQ with NEC** — NEQ includes *all* explosive mass; NEC is *only the charge inside a finished article*. Wrong selection = **2-mark loss**.
> 3. **Skipping the barricade effect** — barricades halve $D_{mm}$ only, **not** $D_{ib}$. Stating otherwise = **2-mark loss**.
> 4. **Omitting TNT equivalence** in mixed-storage questions = **2-mark loss** per sub-part.
> 5. **Wrong distance ordering** — if student writes $D_{ib} < D_{mm}$, full marks withheld for the ordering sub-question.
> 6. **Ignoring the two-key system** in any "magazine safety" answer = **1-mark deduction** — examiners look for it as a buzzword.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 25 Things You Must Remember for the KTU Exam on this Module:**

- [x] **NEQ** = total mass of explosive excluding packaging.
- [x] **NEC** = mass of explosive *inside* an article (e.g., detonator charge).
- [x] **Cube-root law** $D = K \cdot Q^{1/3}$ is the **default** for KTU 2024.
- [x] **Square-root law** $D = K \cdot \sqrt{Q}$ is a **legacy/conservative** alternative.
- [x] **Distance ordering:** $D_{mm} < D_{po} < D_{ib}$ — always.
- [x] **Barricades** halve $D_{mm}$ but **not** $D_{ib}$.
- [x] **TNT equivalence:** PETN = 1.25, RDX = 1.20, TNT = 1.00, ANFO = 0.40, Dynamite = 0.80, Emulsion ≈ 0.80, Slurry ≈ 0.85.
- [x] **Wall thickness (above-ground):** 300 mm RCC or 460 mm brick + 230 mm earth.
- [x] **Roof cover (mounded):** ≥ 600 mm earth, slope 1:2.
- [x] **Vent area:** ≥ 1/30 of floor area, brass mesh.
- [x] **Lightning protection:** ≥ 2 arresters, earth resistance < 10 Ω.
- [x] **Temperature limit:** ≤ 40°C for NG-based explosives.
- [x] **Two-key system:** one key each with Magazine-in-Charge and Senior Officer.
- [x] **No-smoking zone:** 15 m radius from magazine.
- [x] **Detonators and bulk explosives** are stored in **separate magazines**.
- [x] **Maximum issue:** one shift's requirement; up to 5 days for deep-hole work.
- [x] **Transport rule:** **separate vehicles** for caps and bulk; red-flagged.
- [x] **Tamping rule:** wooden/HDPE rods only; **no iron tools**.
- [x] **Stemming rule:** ≥ 2/3 of borehole length, with 10–20 mm crushed stone.
- [x] **Misfire wait:** ≥ 30 minutes before approach.
- [x] **Post-blast gas wait:** ≥ 20 minutes; CO < 25 ppm, NO₂ < 5 ppm, O₂ > 20.5%.
- [x] **Magazine Stock Register (MSR-1):** mandatory for every issue/receipt.
- [x] **Cover-in distance:** typically 1.5–2× $D_{ib}$ during firing.
- [x] **Destroying ground:** dedicated area with its own Q-D compliance.
- [x] **Licensing authority:** Chief Controller of Explosives (CCE), Nagpur — under Explosives Rules 2008.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosive Facilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT753 - EXPLOSIVES TECHNOLOGY AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Explosive Facilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Explosive Facilities

An **Explosive Facility** is any building, structure, magazine, operating area, or enclosure that is purpose-designed, constructed, and approved for the manufacture, processing, handling, testing, storage, or transportation of explosive materials (EMs) and explosive articles. These facilities must comply with stringent national and international safety codes (e.g., Indian Explosives Rules 2008, IS 4081, NATO AASTP-1, NFPA 495) and are engineered to prevent accidental initiation while mitigating the consequences of an unintended event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The course explicitly requires students to design and evaluate explosive facilities based on the **Quantity-Distance (Q-D)** principle, the **3-D Concept of Safety** (Deflagration, Detonation, Defense), and the hierarchy of engineering controls.

The key sub-categories that fall under "Explosive Facilities" are:

1. **Storage Magazines** – Earth-covered magazines, above-ground magazines, portable magazines, and bulk storage bins.
2. **Operating Buildings** – Mixing, pressing, pelletizing, drying, loading, assembling, and packing bays.
3. **Process Buildings** – Manufacturing plants for initiating explosives, propellants, and pyrotechnics.
4. **Laboratories and Test Facilities** – Small-scale testing rooms (impact, friction, electrostatic spark sensitivity).
5. **Firing Ranges and Burning Grounds** – Open-air destruction pads and proving grounds.
6. **Auxiliary Facilities** – Change houses, guard posts, sub-stations, fire-water pump houses, watchtowers, and access control zones.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Vault for the World's Most Temperamental Guests"**

Imagine you are hosting a thousand extremely temperamental guests at a resort. These guests are brilliant and useful (they can lift mountains, launch rockets, blast tunnels), but if two of them bump into each other too hard, they explode violently. Your job is to:

- Put each guest in their own **individually isolated, well-ventilated room** (storage magazine).
- Make sure the rooms are built from materials that **won't shatter into lethal fragments** (traverses and barricades).
- Keep the rooms **far enough apart** that one exploding guest cannot trigger another (Quantity-Distance separation).
- Install **fire alarms, lightning rods, anti-static flooring, and emergency exits** so the guests stay calm.
- Ensure that **unauthorised people never get close** (intrusion fences, guard posts).

That is exactly what an explosive facility is: a carefully engineered "resort" where dangerous reactive materials are kept isolated, ventilated, defended, and supervised.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Explosive Facilities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks to Memorise**

- **Net Explosive Quantity (NEQ)** — measured in **kg of TNT equivalent** for hazard classification.
- **Intraline Distance (ILD)** — separation between two operating buildings on the same facility.
- **Intermagazine Distance (IMD)** — separation between two storage magazines.
- **Public Traffic Route Distance (PTRD)** — separation from public roads and railways.
- **Inhabited Building Distance (IBD)** — separation from occupied structures.
- **Aboveground Magazine (AGM)** vs **Earth-Covered Magazine (ECM)** — the design reduction factor for Q-D is typically **0.5 ×** for ECMs.
- **Barricade Effectiveness Multiplier** — an efficient barricade typically reduces required distance by a factor of **0.5** to **0.75**.

## 1.4 The 3-D Concept of Safety

The **3-D Philosophy** is the cornerstone of explosive facility design:

| D | Term | Meaning |
|---|------|---------|
| D₁ | **Deflagration Prevention** | Eliminate ignition sources, prevent heat/spark accumulation. |
| D₂ | **Detonation Mitigation** | Limit mass detonation through separation, splinter control, and barricades. |
| D₃ | **Defence / Damage Limitation** | Protect personnel, public, and other facilities from blast, fragments, and thermal radiation. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Explosive Facilities

### 2.1.1 Storage Magazines

A **magazine** is a closed structure used for the permanent or temporary storage of explosive articles and substances. Two principal classes exist:

1. **Earth-Covered Magazine (ECM)** – A semi-buried, reinforced-concrete or corrugated-steel arch structure covered with a minimum **0.6 m to 1.0 m** of earth on the roof and sides. ECMs are vastly safer than AGMs because the earth cover absorbs blast energy and arrests fragments.

2. **Above-Ground Magazine (AGM)** – A reinforced-concrete or heavy-steel building constructed with frangible (blow-off type) roofs/walls designed to vent overpressure. AGMs require substantially **larger Q-D separations** than ECMs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A correctly designed ECM typically reduces the required IMD by a factor of **0.5** compared to an equivalent AGM holding the same NEQ.

### 2.1.2 Operating Buildings

Operating buildings are designed for **brief** occupancy (a few minutes to a few hours) and are equipped with:
- Lightweight frangible walls/roof (e.g., 50 mm asbestos-cement sheets, frangible aluminium, or 0.5 mm steel) that vent blast outward, not inward toward personnel.
- Earth or sand-bag traverses at doorways and operator positions.
- Anti-static conductive flooring (resistance **$10^5$ to $10^8$ Ω**).
- Intrinsically safe electrical fittings (Ex-proof category).

## 2.2 The Quantity-Distance (Q-D) Principle

The **Quantity-Distance (Q-D)** concept is the foundation of explosive facility siting. It establishes a relationship between the **mass of explosive (Q)** at a potential explosion site (PES) and the **minimum separation distance (D)** to a vulnerable target (exposed site, ES).

### 2.2.1 The "Square-Root Law"

For mass-explosive storage and operations, the standard KTU/NATO AASTP-1 formula is:

$$
D \;=\; k \, \sqrt{Q}
$$

where:

- $D$ = minimum separation distance (m)
- $Q$ = net explosive quantity (kg of TNT equivalent)
- $k$ = hazard / barrier factor (dimensionless, depends on magazine type and target vulnerability)

### 2.2.2 Standard Hazard Factors (k)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following values are widely used in KTU board examination problems and Indian Explosives Rules.

| Target Vulnerable Site | AGM (no barricade) | AGM (barricaded) | ECM (barricaded) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Other operating building (ILD) | $k = 2.4$ | $k = 1.8$ | $k = 1.2$ |
| Other magazine (IMD) | $k = 2.4$ | $k = 1.8$ | $k = 1.2$ |
| Inhabited building (IBD) | $k = 8.0$ | $k = 6.0$ | $k = 4.5$ |
| Public traffic route (PTRD) | $k = 8.0$ | $k = 6.0$ | $k = 4.5$ |

### 2.2.3 Cube-Root Law (used for blast-pressure calculations at long range)

When the explosion's primary damaging effect is **overpressure** at long range, the cube-root scaling is used:

$$
D_{p} \;=\; \frac{C}{\left(P_{so}\right)^{1/3}} \, \sqrt[3]{Q}
$$

where $P_{so}$ is the peak side-on overpressure (kPa) and $C$ is a yield factor (~444 kPa·m / kg$^{1/3}$ for TNT).

## 2.3 Construction Materials and Design Philosophy

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:** A well-designed explosive facility aims to **vent overpressure, capture fragments, and shield personnel** — not to be explosion-proof.

| Component | Recommended Construction | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Walls (manned buildings) | Reinforced concrete ≥ 300 mm, or frangible steel + earth barricade | Prevents lethal fragment projection |
| Roof | Frangible / lightweight (≤ 50 kg/m²) | Allows controlled upward venting |
| Floor | Anti-static, non-sparking, conductive (10⁵–10⁸ Ω) | Prevents static discharge |
| Door | Steel-clad, outward-opening, max 1 personnel door + 1 cargo | Blast relief, escape path |
| Lightning protection | Class II air terminal + down-conductor + earth grid ≤ 10 Ω | Prevents direct strike |
| Ventilation | Forced exhaust, 6–10 air changes/hour, anti-static ducts | Removes fumes, dust, vapour |
| Bonding & earthing | All metallic equipment bonded, resistance ≤ 1 Ω | Prevents spark hazards |

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Reference — Memorise These Equations**

| # | Formula | Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = k\sqrt{Q}$ | Q-D separation (mass-explosion) | m, kg |
| 2 | $D = C \cdot Q^{1/3}$ | Long-range blast (cube-root) | m, kg |
| 3 | $D_{ECM} = 0.5 \times D_{AGM}$ | ECM distance reduction | m |
| 4 | $D_{barricade} = 0.5 \times D_{open}$ | Effective barricade reduction | m |
| 5 | $V_{max} = \frac{R}{T}$ | Debris throw velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $R_{max} = V_0 \cdot \frac{T}{2}$ (vertical throw) | Max fragment range | m |
| 7 | $\Delta P = f(Q, R)$ (Kingery–Bulmash) | Peak overpressure | kPa |
| 8 | $Q_{TNT-equiv} = Q_{actual} \times \eta$ | TNT equivalence | kg |
| 9 | $L = 0.6 \times k \times \sqrt{Q}$ | Minimum earth-cover thickness on ECM | m |
| 10 | $I_{light} \geq 0.1 \cdot I_{max}$ (10% rule) | Lightning rolling-sphere protection | kA |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These principles are applied in:
- **Defence ordnance factories** (e.g., Indian Ordnance Factories Board).
- **Mining industry** — storage of bulk ANFO, emulsion explosives, detonators.
- **Civil blasting** — quarry magazines and explosive vans.
- **Space launch** — solid-propellant storage and integration facilities.
- **Pyrotechnic industry** — firework manufacturing units (Sivakasi cluster, India).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Square-Root Q-D Law from Buckingham π-Theorem

**Goal:** Show that for two geometrically similar explosion sources, the required distance scales as $\sqrt{Q}$.

**Step 1 — List the governing physical variables.**

For an explosion event, the minimum safe distance $D$ is assumed to depend on:

- $Q$ = net explosive mass (kg)
- $E_d$ = energy released per kg of explosive (J/kg)
- $\rho_a$ = ambient air density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (m/s²)

$$
D = f(Q, E_d, \rho_a, g)
$$

**Step 2 — Total variables = 4, fundamental dimensions = 3 ⇒ 1 dimensionless group.**

Using the Buckingham π-theorem, the number of dimensionless groups is $4 - 3 = 1$.

**Step 3 — Choose repeating variables.** Take $Q$, $E_d$, $\rho_a$ as repeating.

**Step 4 — Form the π-group.**

$$
\pi_1 = \frac{D \, \rho_a^{\alpha} \, E_d^{\beta}}{Q^{\gamma}}
$$

Solving for dimensional homogeneity yields $\alpha = 1/3$, $\beta = 1/3$, $\gamma = 1/3$:

$$
\pi_1 = \frac{D \cdot \rho_a^{1/3} \cdot E_d^{1/3}}{Q^{1/3}}
$$

Equating to a constant $C$:

$$
\frac{D \cdot \rho_a^{1/3} \cdot E_d^{1/3}}{Q^{1/3}} = C
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for D.**

$$
D = C \cdot \frac{Q^{1/3}}{\rho_a^{1/3} \cdot E_d^{1/3}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the mass-explosion limit.** When the **detonation energy per unit mass** is the controlling variable, dimensional analysis of the blast-similarity arguments (Hopkinson-Cranz scaling) reduces to:

$$
D = k \, \sqrt{Q}
$$

where $k$ absorbs the constants $\rho_a$ and $E_d$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cube-Root Scaling from Hopkinson's Law

**Goal:** For two charges of the same explosive with weights $W_1$ and $W_2$ producing the same peak overpressure at distances $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 1 — Self-similar blast wave assumption.** Detonation products and air behave as a self-similar polytropic gas; the scaled distance $Z = R / W^{1/3}$ is a dimensionless similarity parameter.

**Step 2 — Same peak overpressure ⇒ same Z.**

$$
Z_1 = Z_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{R_1}{W_1^{1/3}} = \frac{R_2}{W_2^{1/3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange.**

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}
$$

This is the famous **Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root scaling law**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Q-D Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):** A storage magazine is barricaded and holds $Q = 10\,000$ kg of mass-explosive material. Determine the **(a)** IMD to a neighbouring similar magazine, **(b)** IBD to the nearest inhabited building, and **(c)** distance to a public highway. Use ECM barricaded case: IMD k = 1.2, IBD k = 4.5, PTRD k = 4.5.

### 3.3.1 Solution to Part (a) — IMD

$$
D_{IMD} = k_{IMD} \cdot \sqrt{Q} = 1.2 \times \sqrt{10\,000}
$$

$$
D_{IMD} = 1.2 \times 100 = 120 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.3.2 Solution to Part (b) — IBD

$$
D_{IBD} = k_{IBD} \cdot \sqrt{Q} = 4.5 \times \sqrt{10\,000}
$$

$$
D_{IBD} = 4.5 \times 100 = 450 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.3.3 Solution to Part (c) — PTRD

$$
D_{PTRD} = k_{PTRD} \cdot \sqrt{Q} = 4.5 \times \sqrt{10\,000}
$$

$$
D_{PTRD} = 4.5 \times 100 = 450 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification via Cube-Root Consistency Check:** The cube-root formula should yield a comparable order of magnitude. For an overpressure of 7 kPa (typical lung-injury threshold), the cube-root distance is:
>
> $$
> R = C \cdot Q^{1/3} = 444 \times (10\,000)^{1/3} \approx 444 \times 21.5 \approx 9\,550 \text{ m}
> $$
>
> This is far larger because 7 kPa is a **personnel injury** threshold, not a structure-collapse threshold. The square-root $D = k\sqrt{Q}$ formula already incorporates the appropriate pressure level for a structure (≈ 21 kPa for inhabited buildings), which is why it gives a smaller, more practical distance.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Fragment Throw Calculation

**Problem:** A steel magazine door (mass $m = 25$ kg) is accelerated to a velocity $v_0 = 40$ m/s at $45^\circ$ angle. Determine the maximum range.

**Step 1 — Apply projectile range formula** (assuming vacuum for upper bound):

$$
R = \frac{v_0^2 \sin 2\theta}{g} = \frac{40^2 \times \sin 90^\circ}{9.81}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1600 \times 1}{9.81} \approx 163.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Realistic (drag-corrected) range.** A typical aerodynamic drag coefficient of $C_d \approx 1.0$ and cross-section $A = 1$ m² reduces the practical range to about **30 % of the vacuum range**:

$$
R_{real} \approx 0.3 \times 163.1 \approx 49 \text{ m}
$$

This is why **barricades (traverses)** are mandated at doorways — they physically intercept fragments. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Facility Type | Max NEQ | Wall Type | Roof Type | Distance Basis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Earth-Covered Magazine (ECM) | 250 000 kg | Reinforced conc. + earth | Earth-covered | IMD with $k=1.2$ |
| Above-Ground Magazine (AGM) | 50 000 kg | Reinforced conc. ≥ 300 mm | Frangible, ≤ 50 kg/m² | IMD with $k=2.4$ |
| Operating Building | 500 kg | Frangible + barricade | Frangible, ≤ 50 kg/m² | ILD with $k=1.8$ |
| Portable Magazine | 100 kg | Steel, double-walled | Steel | Local transport rules |
| Bulk ANFO Bin | 25 000 kg | Earth bund 1.5 m high | Open-top or shed | Local PT rules |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Explosive Facility Zone

The following Mermaid diagram represents the standard zonal layout of a Class A explosive storage installation (typical of Indian Ordnance Factories and major mine sites).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pubZone["PUBLIC ZONE"]:::pub
    guardGate["GUARD POST AND SECURITY GATE"]:::guard
    adminBlk["ADMIN BLOCK AND CHANGE HOUSE"]:::admin
    perimFence["PERIMETER FENCE WITH RAZOR WIRE"]:::perim
    fireStation["FIRE WATER PUMP HOUSE AND HYDRANTS"]:::fire
    watchTower["WATCH TOWER 1"]:::wt
    watchTower2["WATCH TOWER 2"]:::wt
    ecmA["ECM STORE A NETQ 5000 KG"]:::ecm
    ecmB["ECM STORE B NETQ 5000 KG"]:::ecm
    agm1["ABOVE GROUND MAGAZINE 1 NETQ 1000 KG"]:::agm
    opBlk["OPERATING BLOCK MIXING AND PRESSING"]:::op
    labBlk["TEST LABORATORY IMPACT AND FRICTION"]:::lab
    burnGr["BURNING GROUND"]:::burn
    destrPad["DESTRUCT PAD OPEN AIR"]:::burn
    electSub["EXPLOSIVE PROOF SUB STATION"]:::elec
    ltArr["LIGHTNING MAST ARRAY"]:::elec

    pubZone --> guardGate
    guardGate --> adminBlk
    adminBlk --> perimFence
    perimFence --> watchTower
    perimFence --> watchTower2
    perimFence --> fireStation
    perimFence --> ecmA
    perimFence --> ecmB
    perimFence --> agm1
    perimFence --> opBlk
    perimFence --> labBlk
    perimFence --> burnGr
    perimFence --> destrPad
    perimFence --> electSub
    perimFence --> ltArr

    classDef pub fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px
    classDef guard fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef admin fill:#e2eefd,stroke:#339,stroke-width:1px
    classDef perim fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px
    classDef fire fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#a64,stroke-width:1px
    classDef wt fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#639,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ecm fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#36c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef agm fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px
    classDef op fill:#b3ffd9,stroke:#396,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lab fill:#ffe680,stroke:#996,stroke-width:1px
    classDef burn fill:#ff9966,stroke:#933,stroke-width:1px
    classDef elec fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#369,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Safety Systems Inside a Magazine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    explosiveStock["EXPLOSIVE STOCK INSIDE MAGAZINE"]:::core
    tempCtrl["TEMPERATURE AND HUMIDITY CONTROL"]:::env
    ventFan["FORCED VENTILATION FAN EX PROOF"]:::env
    gasDetect["GAS AND VAPOUR DETECTOR"]:::env
    ltRod["LIGHTNING AIR TERMINAL CLASS II"]:::elec
    earthGrid["EARTHING GRID BELOW 10 OHM"]:::elec
    intrAlarm["INTRUSION AND PIR ALARM"]:::sec
    cctv["CCTV CAMERA WITH IR NIGHT VISION"]:::sec
    fireDetect["SMOKE AND HEAT DETECTOR"]:::fire
    deluge["DELUGE VALVE AND SPRINKLER"]:::fire
    barricade["EARTH TRAVERSE AND BARICADE"]:::def
    fireWall["BLAST WALL 300 MM RCC"]:::def
    accessDoor["EXPLOSION RELIEF DOOR"]:::def
    exLight["EXPLOSION PROOF LUMINAIRE"]:::elec

    explosiveStock --> tempCtrl
    explosiveStock --> ventFan
    explosiveStock --> gasDetect
    explosiveStock --> ltRod
    explosiveStock --> earthGrid
    explosiveStock --> intrAlarm
    explosiveStock --> cctv
    explosiveStock --> fireDetect
    explosiveStock --> deluge
    explosiveStock --> barricade
    explosiveStock --> fireWall
    explosiveStock --> accessDoor
    explosiveStock --> exLight

    classDef core fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:3px
    classDef env fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef elec fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#909,stroke-width:1px
    classDef sec fill:#ffe680,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1px
    classDef fire fill:#ff9966,stroke:#933,stroke-width:1px
    classDef def fill:#b3d9b3,stroke:#363,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Quantity-Distance Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```
